We are no longer defined by location, status, or institution. We are the sanctuary. The veil has been torn. God does not dwell in tents made by hands, nor in the stone structures of human pride. He has chosen instead to tabernacle in living stones (1 Peter 2:5), to 128

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: dwell in hearts made holy by the blood of Christ and the breath of the Spirit. As in Eden, where the first humans walked with God, now in Christ, God walks again, with and within His people. Where we go, His glory follows. Where we speak peace, His Spirit moves. Where we serve in humility and truth, His kingdom breaks through. We are not static monuments to God's past presence, we are moving temples of His current activity. Eden has gone mobile. This radically reshapes our vocation. Our lives are not private religious enclaves, nor are they passive containers for divine blessing. Rather, we are active conduits of God’s renewal. Every act of mercy, every pursuit of justice, every word of truth, every creation of beauty becomes temple work. These are not peripheral duties, they are the liturgies of our daily lives, the incense of our calling. Paul echoes this vision when he says, “Thanks be to God, who in Christ always leads us in triumphal procession, and through us spreads the fragrance of the knowledge of Him everywhere” (2 Corinthians 2:14). We are temples that exude the aroma of Eden, the scent of heaven on earth. We do not simply attend sacred space; we are sacred space, dispersed across the globe to make known the glory of the One who dwells within. In this vision, our ordinary lives are transfigured. Work is no longer secular. Relationships are no longer random. Meals, meetings, and moments become liturgical. The presence of God within us makes all of life holy ground. This is the high calling of every believer: to be a living Eden, to carry God’s presence wherever we go, and to join in the mission of the Spirit, healing, renewing, and reconciling all things in Christ. We are not waiting for Eden to return. In Christ, Eden walks again through us. 129

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: Conclusion: Living with Cosmic Purpose Genesis does not end with the loss of Eden; it begins the story of its restoration. The expulsion from the garden is not the final word but the opening chapter of redemption. From that moment on, Scripture tells the story of God’s relentless purpose to reclaim His world, restore His image-bearers, and dwell once again with His people. That story is not only past and future, it continues in us today. The world is not random, accidental, or meaningless. It is a temple waiting to be filled with glory (Habakkuk 2:14). Our lives are not incidental or expendable; they are priestly callings, woven into God’s great design to make His presence known. The Christian life, then, is not merely escape from sin or survival until heaven. It is active participation in God’s cosmic plan to restore all things in Christ (Ephesians 1:10). To live with this vision is to see with new eyes. Every moment becomes sacred, every calling priestly, every place holy ground. Work is no longer mere labor; it is service in the temple of creation. Relationships are no longer ordinary; they are opportunities to reflect the divine image. Worship is no longer confined to sanctuary walls; it overflows into vocation, justice, creativity, and love. The world is a temple, and our lives are liturgies. Every act of faithfulness. whether cultivating a garden, raising a child, conducting business with integrity, or proclaiming the gospel is an offering before the King. We live as image-bearers, carrying the presence of God into creation that is destined for glory. Genesis reminds us where it all began. Christ shows us where it is going. And the Spirit empowers us to live even now in the hope of new creation.

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: King. We live as image-bearers, carrying the presence of God into creation that is destined for glory. Genesis reminds us where it all began. Christ shows us where it is going. And the Spirit empowers us to live even now in the hope of new creation. To embrace this vision is to discover cosmic purpose: to walk as priests in God’s world, to see every breath as grace, and to join creation’s song until the day when heaven and earth are one. 130

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: Chapter 13 Behold Our God. The story of Genesis 1-2 is not merely an account of how things began. It is a revelation of who God is. The Bible does not begin with philosophy or speculation, nor with human striving or searching, but with a vision: “In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth.” From the first words, our gaze is lifted upward. The text is less concerned with explaining the mechanics of creation than with unveiling the majesty of the Creator. Genesis is a summons, a call to behold our God. Behold the Creator Before there was time, before there was matter, before there was light, there was God. Eternal, self-existent, uncreated. He is not one deity among many, nor a rival in a pantheon, but the One from whom, through whom, and to whom all things exist (Romans 11:36). He alone is without beginning and without end, the fountain of all reality, the Alpha who speaks and the Omega who fulfills. His act of creation is effortless. By His Word He summons the universe into being: “And God said… and it was so.” Galaxies blaze into light, oceans gather into place, the earth springs forth with seed and fruit, all in obedience to His voice. Creation is not the product of divine struggle, violence, or bloodshed, as the myths of the nations imagined. The God of Genesis does not carve the world out of a slain rival; He calls it forth out of nothing ( ex nihilo ) by sheer authority and joy. This is no act of tyranny, no assertion of power to silence challengers. It is the overflow of divine generosity. Creation is gift, not accident; artistry, not aftermath.

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: val; He calls it forth out of nothing ( ex nihilo ) by sheer authority and joy. This is no act of tyranny, no assertion of power to silence challengers. It is the overflow of divine generosity. Creation is gift, not accident; artistry, not aftermath. Each act of forming and filling is punctuated by delight: “God saw that it was good.” And when His work is complete, the refrain swells to its crescendo: “God saw all that He had made, and it was very good.” The universe is not reluctant or grudging, it is radiant with the goodness of the Creator. 132

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: To behold God as Creator is to see Him as the source of all things. It is to confess that nothing is random, nothing is meaningless, and nothing exists apart from His sustaining will. Our beginning is not in chaos but in design, not in conflict but in communion. We are dust, yes, but dust kissed with breath, matter infused with Spirit, earthly yet destined for glory. To truly behold Him as Creator is to stand in awe, to recognize that all of life is sacramental, every breath is grace, and every created thing, no matter how small is a theater of His glory. Behold the King Genesis 2 reveals that creation is not only made, it is ordered as a temple. The flowing rivers, the mention of gold and precious stones, the fertile garden planted by the hand of God, and the divine presence that walks in its midst all point beyond landscape to liturgy. Eden is not a mere orchard, but sacred architecture, a holy dwelling patterned for worship. The climax of this cosmic temple does not arrive on the sixth day with humanity, as we might expect, but on the seventh day with God’s rest. In the ancient Near East, divine “rest” was not about idleness. It was the language of enthronement. Once a temple was built, the deity was believed to “rest” in it, that is, to take up residence as king, ruling from the sacred space where heaven and earth intersected. So when Genesis declares that God rested, it does not picture a weary deity withdrawing from work. It reveals a sovereign Lord assuming His throne. His rest is not absence but presence, not disengagement but authority. The Sabbath is the coronation of creation, the moment when God takes His rightful seat as King of the cosmos. To behold God as King, then, is to confess that the world belongs to Him.

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: rest is not absence but presence, not disengagement but authority. The Sabbath is the coronation of creation, the moment when God takes His rightful seat as King of the cosmos. To behold God as King, then, is to confess that the world belongs to Him. The cosmos is not abandoned to chaos, not ruled by idols, not an arena of random chance. It is the ordered domain of His wise and benevolent reign. Every Sabbath stands as a cosmic declaration: the throne is not vacant, the 133

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: crown is not contested. The King reigns. And He reigns not with tyranny but with justice, not with caprice but with peace. To live under this reign is to see creation as sanctuary, time as holy, and life itself as a summons to worship. To behold Him as King is to bow in awe, to rest in His rule, and to join the chorus of creation that proclaims, “The Lord reigns, let the earth rejoice” (Psalm 97:1). Behold the Redeemer Genesis does not let us linger long in unbroken harmony. The garden that was sanctuary becomes a place of exile. The image, once radiant, is marred. The temple, meant for communion, becomes defiled. Humanity turns from the Creator to the serpent’s lie, seeking life apart from the very Breath who gave it. Dust, kissed with Spirit, chooses death, and so dust returns to dust. Yet even here, amid curse and consequence, a promise flickers. In the words of judgment comes the seed of hope: “The seed of the woman will crush the serpent’s head” (Genesis 3:15). From that moment forward, the Scriptures unfold not only the story of creation, but the story of redemption. It is the account of a God who will not abandon His world, a God who not only begins but brings to completion, who not only forms but transforms. In Christ, the Second Adam, this Redeemer is revealed. Where the first Adam succumbed in a garden, Christ resisted in Gethsemane. Where Adam grasped at life and found death, Christ laid down His life and brought life to many. The cross becomes the new Tree of Life, planted on a barren hill, its fruit offered for the healing of the nations. The resurrection becomes the dawn of new creation, the first day of a new week, the beginning of a cosmos made whole. What was forfeited in Eden is restored in Him, and more besides. 134

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: To behold God as Redeemer is to glimpse the depth of His love. He does not save from a distance. He enters the dust, assumes our weakness, bears our death, and raises us in His life. He comes not merely to repair but to re-create, not merely to forgive but to renew. Redemption is not a side note in the biblical story, it is the heartbeat of creation itself, pulsing through history until it culminates in new heavens and a new earth. Behold the Dwelling One The purpose of creation has always been communion. From the beginning, Eden was not merely a garden of delights; it was a sanctuary. Its rivers, its gold, its trees, and its presence marked it as the first temple, a sacred place where heaven and earth touched, and where God walked with His image-bearers in fellowship (Genesis 3:8). The design of creation itself pointed to a God who does not remain aloof, but who delights to dwell among His people. The story of Scripture unfolds as a movement of dwelling. From Eden to the tabernacle, God’s presence is localized in a tent pitched among His people in the wilderness. From the tabernacle to the temple, His glory fills a house of stone and cedar on Zion’s hill. From the temple to the incarnation, the Word becomes flesh and tabernacles among us (John 1:14). From the incarnation to Pentecost, the Spirit descends and makes the Church God’s living temple (1 Corinthians 3:16). And the story reaches its climax in Revelation, when the heavenly voice declares: “Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man” (Revelation 21:3). Jesus Christ is the fulfillment of this dwelling. He is the true Temple (John 2:19-21), not built with hands, but with flesh and blood. In Him, the fullness of God dwells bodily (Colossians 2:9).

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: dwelling place of God is with man” (Revelation 21:3). Jesus Christ is the fulfillment of this dwelling. He is the true Temple (John 2:19-21), not built with hands, but with flesh and blood. In Him, the fullness of God dwells bodily (Colossians 2:9). He is Emmanuel, God with us not distant, not detached, but present. From His pierced side flows the water of life (John 19:34), echoing the rivers of Eden and anticipating the river of the New Jerusalem (Revelation 22:1-2). The sanctuary is no longer 135

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: a place but a Person, and in Him, the presence of God is made accessible to all. To behold God as the Dwelling One is to realize that creation itself is destined not for abandonment but for embrace, not for escape but for union. The world is not disposable, a mere staging ground for salvation; it is sacramental, a holy space designed to host the presence of God. History moves toward this destiny, when every corner of creation resounds with His glory and every dwelling becomes His home. The final vision is not humanity whisked away to heaven, but heaven descending to earth. The New Jerusalem is a garden-city, a consummated Eden, where God dwells with His people forever. There, the Dwelling One fulfills the purpose announced in the beginning: God with us, and we with Him. Behold and Become Beholding is never passive. In Scripture, vision leads to transformation. As Paul writes, “We all, with unveiled face, beholding the glory of the Lord, are being transformed into the same image from one degree of glory to another” (2 Corinthians 3:18). To behold God is not simply to observe Him, it is to be changed by Him. To see His glory is to begin to reflect His glory, just as the moon reflects the radiance of the sun. This is the heart of vocation. Our lives are not secular routines punctuated by occasional worship; they are living liturgies. Work becomes worship when done under the gaze of God. Relationships become sacraments when seen as reflections of His covenant love. Ordinary days become holy ground when they are lived in awareness of His presence. As image-bearers, we are not called only to look at God but to mirror Him, to make visible in creation the character of the invisible Creator.

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: ns of His covenant love. Ordinary days become holy ground when they are lived in awareness of His presence. As image-bearers, we are not called only to look at God but to mirror Him, to make visible in creation the character of the invisible Creator. The more we behold, the more we become and the more we become, the more the world beholds Him in us. 136

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: In the end, Genesis does not merely call us to understand beginnings, it calls us to worship the Beginner. Creation is not primarily an argument to be defended, but a summons to be answered. Eden is not merely a memory of what was lost, but a promise of what will be restored. The story begins with “In the beginning, God” and ends with “Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man.” Between these bookends runs the thread of glory, weaving together creation, redemption, and new creation into one grand tapestry of divine purpose. To behold our God is to live in anticipation of that day when the earth will be filled with the knowledge of His glory as the waters cover the sea (Habakkuk 2:14). It is to live as priests in His temple, to breathe grace in a world of dust, and to carry light into the shadows until He comes. Behold Him, then. For in beholding, we become. And in becoming, we bear witness to the One who began all things, sustains all things, and will bring all things to their glorious end. Lift your eyes, then. Beyond the dust, beyond the toil, beyond the exile, there is a greater vision: Behold our God. He is Creator, King, Redeemer, and Dwelling One. He sits enthroned over creation, yet stoops to walk in the garden. He speaks light into darkness, yet breathes life into dust. He judges sin, yet carries it on His own shoulders. He fills His temple, yet makes His people His dwelling place. The story that began with “In the beginning, God” ends with “Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man.” To behold Him now is to live in anticipation of that day. To worship Him now is to join the song of creation that will echo forever: Behold our God. This is where the story begins, and this is where it ends. 137

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: Epilogue Back to the Beginning to See the End The Bible begins with a garden and ends with a garden-city. Genesis opens with a cosmos ordered by the voice of God, teeming with life, beauty, and divine presence. Revelation closes with a renewed creation, a radiant city where the tree of life once again bears fruit and the river of life flows from the throne of God and the Lamb. What was lost in Genesis is restored in Revelation, but not merely returned. It is consummated. Glorified. Transfigured. The story of Scripture is not cyclical, but telic, it moves with purpose, from Eden to New Creation, from dust to destiny. It does not loop back to where it began, but rather fulfills the intentions set forth in the beginning. The world that God called “very good” in Genesis is the world He makes “new” in Revelation (Revelation 21:5). This is not a replacement but a restoration, the marriage of heaven and earth, once torn by sin, now eternally reconciled in Christ. Revelation’s Edenic Restoration In Revelation 22, the echoes of Genesis resound unmistakably. The tree of life, long guarded by cherubim after the fall (Genesis 3:24), is now accessible to all who belong to the Lamb. The curse that stained creation is lifted: “No longer will there be anything accursed” (Revelation 22:3). God walks once more among His people, not hidden, but face to face: “They will see his face, and his name will be on their foreheads” (v. 4). Eden has become the New Jerusalem, a garden-city, where worship and vocation, priesthood and kingship, creation and Creator are united. The human story is not one of mere survival but of sacred return. This is the Gospel in its final movement: resurrection of the world, not just the soul; renewal of creation, not just escape from it.

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: ood and kingship, creation and Creator are united. The human story is not one of mere survival but of sacred return. This is the Gospel in its final movement: resurrection of the world, not just the soul; renewal of creation, not just escape from it. The Bible’s end is its beginning fulfilled. 138

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: Heaven and Earth Reunited in Christ At the heart of this restoration is the person of Christ. He is the Tree of Life, offering Himself for the healing of the nations. He is the river, quenching every thirst with the water of eternal life. He is the Bridegroom, coming for His Bride, the Church, to dwell with her forever. The union of heaven and earth is accomplished not by human ingenuity, but by divine incarnation. Christ is Jacob’s ladder (John 1:51), the one in whom angels ascend and descend, the mediator of a new and better covenant, the bridge between God and man. In Him, the boundaries are abolished: heaven touches earth, the divine indwells the human, the eternal enters time. This is the deepest mystery of our faith, that in Christ, the cosmic is made personal, and the personal is caught up into the cosmic. He is the Second Adam who did not fall to temptation but crushed the serpent. He is the Gardener who rose from the tomb to tend the new creation. In Him, we don’t just await Eden restored, we begin to live it now. Living Now with Genesis Eyes To see the world through the lens of Genesis is to live with hope, vision, and sacred responsibility. It is to realize that creation was always a temple, that our lives are always priestly, and that history is always moving toward communion with God. We do not merely wait for access to the Tree of Life, we have it now. Eternal life is not postponed until the end; it is present in Christ. He is the Tree of Life (John 6:51; Revelation 2:7), and all who abide in Him partake of that life. Through Him, we have already tasted the powers of the age to come (Hebrews 6:5). The Spirit within us is the firstfruits of the restored Eden, the down payment of the new creation. 139

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: We live now between the trees: the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, which once brought death, and the Tree of Life, which now brings eternal life through faith in Christ. But we are not lost. We are led by the Spirit of the same God who walked in Eden’s cool of day and who even now walks with us. And He will dwell forever with His people in the new heavens and new earth. To live with “Genesis eyes” is to see every place as potentially sacred, every person as bearing the image of God, and every moment as moving toward glory. It is to plant gardens even in exile (Jeremiah 29:5), to walk by faith even in wilderness, and to carry Eden in our hearts as we await the day when God makes His dwelling with man again. The beginning was not a myth. It was a mirror of the end. And the end is not a fantasy, it is the fulfillment of a divine promise. Behold, He is making all things new. 140

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: Appendix: Genesis and the Question of Science Introduction: Two Questions, One Story When we open Genesis with the words “In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth” (Gen. 1:1), we are confronted not only with a statement of origins but with a claim of sovereignty. The Bible’s first verse does not set out to satisfy scientific curiosity about mechanics; it establishes that the world has a beginning, that its beginning was willed, and that its beginning is anchored in the character of the Creator. Modern readers, however, often bring different questions to the text. “What about the Big Bang?” “What about cosmology, evolution, or multiverse theories?” These questions are not new. The Church Fathers wrestled with Greek eternalism, medieval theologians grappled with Aristotelian cosmology, and Reformation and post-Enlightenment thinkers contended with emerging astronomy and geology. In every age, believers have asked: How do the claims of science relate to the witness of Scripture? This appendix is not a comprehensive survey of every model. Rather, it seeks to set Genesis alongside the scientific accounts of beginnings, showing that while science asks the how , Genesis reveals the Who and why . Science describes mechanisms; Scripture discloses meaning. And when both are rightly understood, they are not enemies but different kinds of witnesses to the same reality. Genesis and the Beginning of Time The opening phrase of Scripture, “In the beginning” (Hebrew bereshit ), carries a weight far greater than a chronological marker. It announces that time itself had a starting point. In the ancient world, this was a revolutionary idea. Many cultures conceived of existence as cyclical, an endless loop of birth, decay, and renewal.

Many cultures conceived of existence as cyclical, an endless loop of birth, decay, and renewal. The Babylonian Enuma Elish depicts creation as arising from the violent conflict between primordial 141

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: deities, a battle destined to repeat as chaos always threatens order. 1 Similarly, Hindu cosmologies describe the universe as oscillating through unending cycles ( kalpas ) of creation and destruction. 2 In these accounts, time is not a purposeful story but an eternal wheel, often indifferent or hostile to human dignity. Against this backdrop, Genesis makes a bold and unparalleled claim: time is linear, purposeful, and moral. The narrative does not begin with conflict or chaos, but with a decisive act of divine will. The world has a definite beginning, it moves in a determined direction, and it will culminate in a divinely appointed telos. This linear conception of time distinguishes Israel’s worldview from its neighbors and lays the foundation for biblical eschatology. 3 Jewish and Christian thinkers through the centuries have meditated on the implications of this truth. The Jewish philosopher Philo of Alexandria (1st century CE), writing in the Hellenistic context, noted that Genesis affirms not merely a beginning of material things but the truth that time itself is a creation, dependent entirely on God. 4 Augustine of Hippo, reflecting deeply in Confessions (Book XI), argued that time itself began with creation: there was no “before” in which God was waiting, for God exists in eternity, outside of time’s flow. 5 His creative act, Augustine insists, gives rise to time’s very reality. Strikingly, modern physics offers a parallel insight. The Big Bang model, the dominant scientific account of cosmic origins, suggests not simply the 5 Augustine, Confessions , trans. Henry Chadwick (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1991), XI.13-14. 4 Philo, On the Creation of the World 26-27, in The Works of Philo , trans. C. D. Yonge (Peabody, MA: Hendrickson, 1993).

Henry Chadwick (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1991), XI.13-14. 4 Philo, On the Creation of the World 26-27, in The Works of Philo , trans. C. D. Yonge (Peabody, MA: Hendrickson, 1993). 3 Gerhard von Rad, Genesis: A Commentary , rev. ed., Old Testament Library (Philadelphia: Westminster Press, 1972), 49-50. 2 Wendy Doniger, Hindu Myths: A Sourcebook Translated from the Sanskrit (London: Penguin, 1975), 23-29. 1 Alexander Heidel, The Babylonian Genesis: The Story of the Creation (Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1963), 34-47. 142

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: beginning of matter and energy but the beginning of space-time itself. 6 Time, according to the model, is woven into the very fabric of the universe. To speak of a “before the Big Bang” is meaningless in physical terms, echoing Augustine’s point fifteen centuries earlier. Thus, Genesis 1:1 is not a primitive myth but a theological statement of breathtaking scope. It insists that the universe is not eternal, that history is not cyclical, and that time itself is a gift of God. The clock of history ticks because God willed it to start, and it moves toward a telos, an appointed end, which He also holds in His hands. As the Alpha and the Omega (Rev. 22:13), He stands at both the beginning and the end, encompassing all of history in His sovereign design. The Big Bang and the Word of God The Big Bang theory, now the dominant paradigm in cosmology, proposes that the universe expanded from an extremely hot, dense singularity approximately 13.8 billion years ago. 7 It is supported by converging lines of observational evidence: the detection of cosmic microwave background radiation, the observed redshift of galaxies (Hubble’s law), and the predicted abundance of light elements such as hydrogen and helium. 8 Far from being speculative, it is one of the most empirically grounded models in modern science. At first glance, this account may seem far removed from Genesis’ majestic simplicity: “In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth” (Gen. 1:1). Yet many Christian theologians, scientists, and philosophers have argued that the Big Bang does not rival Genesis but confirms its most fundamental claim: the universe had a beginning.

Yet many Christian theologians, scientists, and philosophers have argued that the Big Bang does not rival Genesis but confirms its most fundamental claim: the universe had a beginning. For centuries, secular 8 Steven Weinberg, The First Three Minutes: A Modern View of the Origin of the Universe (New York: Basic Books, 1977), 29-35. 7 P. J. E. Peebles, Principles of Physical Cosmology (Princeton: Princeton University Press, 1993), 56-59. 6 Stephen Hawking, A Brief History of Time (New York: Bantam, 1988), 8-9; John D. Barrow and Frank J. title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: philosophy preferred eternal models, whether Aristotle’s eternal cosmos, 9 or the steady-state theory of the 20th century, championed by Fred Hoyle, which posited that the universe had always existed and continually generated matter to maintain equilibrium. 10 But as evidence mounted, the steady-state theory collapsed, and cosmology was forced to concede what Genesis had always declared: the universe has not always existed; it came into being. The crucial difference, then, lies not in the data but in the interpretation. Science can describe how the expansion unfolded, but it cannot answer why there is something rather than nothing. The Big Bang begins with energy, quantum fields, or singularities, but it does not explain their origin, why they exist, or what their purpose is. Genesis gives the answer in seven words: “In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth.” As theologian Alister McGrath observes, the Big Bang is best understood as “the scientific echo of the biblical voice.” 11 It describes, in physical terms, the sudden eruption of time, space, and matter, but it cannot account for the transcendent will that brought it into being. John Polkinghorne, the Cambridge physicist-turned-priest, used to say that science explains the music but not the Composer. 12 The data tells us the melody, but only theology explains why there is music at all. Centuries before telescopes and satellites, Augustine reflected similarly. In his City of God , he wrote that God creates not out of necessity or compulsion but “out of the abundance of His goodness.” 13 Creation is not an accident, nor is it an overflow of divine need, but an act of gratuitous 13 Augustine, City of God , trans. Henry Bettenson (London: Penguin, 2003), XI.24.

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: love. Where science sees radiation and expansion, theology discerns generosity and purpose. Thus, when Genesis records “And God said, ‘Let there be light’” (Gen. 1:3), we encounter divine speech as the ultimate cause of existence. When cosmology speaks of a primordial burst of light and energy at the dawn of time, we may hear in it the faint echo of that eternal Word, the Logos, through whom all things came into being (John 1:3). The correspondence is not exact, but it is suggestive: science traces the afterglow, theology reveals the Speaker. The universe is intelligible because it was spoken into being, and it is meaningful because it was spoken with purpose. Alternative Cosmologies and Their Limits While the Big Bang model remains the most widely accepted description of cosmic origins, other cosmological theories have been proposed across history and into the modern era. These models, though intriguing, often arise from a desire to avoid the implications of a singular beginning, a moment that suggests dependence on a transcendent cause. Genesis, however, speaks with unmatched clarity: “In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth” (Gen. 1:1). Whatever mechanisms are described, the Bible insists that behind them all is divine authorship and intention. Steady-State Theory The Steady-State theory, most prominently advocated in the mid-20th century by Fred Hoyle, Hermann Bondi, and Thomas Gold, argued that the universe had no beginning. 14 Instead, it was thought to expand eternally, with new matter continually created to maintain an average density.

The philosophical appeal of this theory was obvious: it eliminated the need for 14 Fred Hoyle, Hermann Bondi, and Thomas Gold, “The Steady-State Theory of the Expanding Universe,” Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society 108, no. 3 (1948): 372-82. 145

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: a “moment of creation.” 15 If the universe has always existed, then one need not reckon with the question of why it exists. Yet the discovery of cosmic microwave background radiation in 1965 dealt a decisive blow to the model. 16 The evidence strongly indicated that the universe had a fiery, finite origin. Steady-State cosmology was largely abandoned, and with it the attempt to escape the idea of a beginning. Genesis, by contrast, had long declared the truth secular science sought to evade: matter is not eternal; it is created. The cosmos is not self-sustaining but contingent. Cyclical Universe Models Another alternative has been the cyclical or “oscillating” universe hypothesis. In this model, the universe undergoes endless cycles of expansion and contraction: a “Big Bang” followed by a “Big Crunch,” followed again by another expansion. 17 The appeal of this theory lies in its apparent alignment with certain ancient cosmologies that imagined eternal cycles of birth, decay, and renewal. But Genesis offers a strikingly different picture. The biblical worldview insists that time is linear, not circular. History is not an endless wheel grinding forward without goal; it is a purposeful narrative with a beginning, middle, and end. 18 The prophets proclaim an appointed “Day of the Lord,” and the New Testament speaks of a consummation in which Christ will return and the new creation will dawn. 19 Far from repeating eternally, the biblical cosmos moves toward a decisive fulfillment. 19 See Joel 2:31; 1 Thessalonians 5:2; Revelation 21:1-5. 18 Gerhard von Rad, Old Testament Theology: Volume I (New York: Harper & Row, 1962), 144-46. 17 Richard C. Tolman, Relativity, Thermodynamics, and Cosmology (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1934), 501-25. 16 Arno A. Penzias and Robert W.

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: Multiverse Theories In recent decades, some cosmologists have speculated that our universe may be only one among countless others, a “multiverse.” This idea arises from certain interpretations of quantum mechanics and inflationary cosmology. 20 While mathematically fascinating, multiverse theories remain untestable and, for many, philosophically unsatisfying. Even if other universes exist, they do not answer the ultimate question of why any universe exists. For the Christian, Colossians 1:16 provides the decisive perspective: “For by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible… all things were created through him and for him.” Whether we speak of one cosmos or many, all are encompassed within the scope of God’s creative will. The existence of multiple universes would not diminish the Creator; it would only expand the stage of His glory. In each of these cases; Steady-State, cyclical, or multiverse, Genesis does not need to compete with scientific speculation. It speaks from a higher vantage. Whatever the mechanics, however vast the cosmos, the truth remains: the universe exists because God willed it into being, and it moves purposefully toward His appointed end. The world is not eternal, not random, and not self-sustaining. It is creation, and creation implies a Creator. Creation ex Nihilo and Divine Speech One of the most striking claims of Genesis is that God creates not by struggle, violence, or craftsmanship with preexistent materials, but by His Word. The Hebrew verb bara (“to create”) is distinctive: it is never used of human making but is reserved for divine activity. 21 Unlike Mesopotamian 21 Claus Westermann, Genesis 1-11: A Commentary (Minneapolis: Augsburg, 1984), 102-3.

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: ls, but by His Word. The Hebrew verb bara (“to create”) is distinctive: it is never used of human making but is reserved for divine activity. 21 Unlike Mesopotamian 21 Claus Westermann, Genesis 1-11: A Commentary (Minneapolis: Augsburg, 1984), 102-3. 20 Andrei Linde, “The Inflationary Universe,” Reports on Progress in Physics 47, no. 8 (1984): 925-86. 147

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: or Egyptian cosmogonies, where gods fashion the cosmos out of the carcass of a slain deity or mold humans from clay mixed with divine blood, Genesis insists that God calls reality into being where nothing existed before. This doctrine, creatio ex nihilo (creation out of nothing), would become a cornerstone of Christian theology. The radical simplicity of the biblical claim is essential. As Gerhard von Rad observed, Genesis 1:1 “explodes with the unheard-of idea that the world is not the outcome of conflict among the gods, but the free, sovereign act of the One God.” 22 This act is accomplished not through force or battle but through speech: “And God said…” (Gen. 1:3). Divine speech is not mere poetry; it is effective cause. God’s Word does what it says. This became the settled conviction of the Church. Thomas Aquinas, in the Summa Theologica , insisted that only God possesses the power to create ex nihilo. 23 Human beings may reshape, rearrange, or repurpose matter, but only the Creator can bring something into existence from absolute nothingness. For Aquinas, creation was not simply the beginning of things but the continuous dependence of all things upon God’s Word for their being. Modern cosmology, with its Big Bang model, describes the unfolding of the universe from an initial singularity of matter-energy. Yet it cannot explain the origin of that singularity itself. Where did it come from? Why does it exist? Why is there something rather than nothing? Genesis declares that even that “singularity” is not eternal. It, too, is the result of God’s command. Science describes expansion; Scripture reveals intention. The psalmist affirms this when he declares: “By the word of the LORD the heavens were made, and by the breath of his mouth all their host” (Ps.

Fathers of the English Dominican Province (New York: Benziger Brothers, 1947), I, Q.45, Art. 1 22 Gerhard von Rad, Genesis: A Commentary , rev. ed., Old Testament Library (Philadelphia: Westminster Press, 1972), 49. 148

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: 33:6). Divine speech is paired with divine breath, the Word and the Spirit together bringing forth creation. The writer of Hebrews echoes this truth: “By faith we understand that the universe was created by the word of God, so that what is seen was not made out of things that are visible” (Heb. 11:3). The visible is not ultimate; the unseen God is. Patristic theologians like Irenaeus of Lyons saw creatio ex nihilo as crucial to safeguarding the Creator’s sovereignty. If matter were eternal, God would be merely a craftsman reshaping preexistent stuff. But if God calls all things into being by His Word, then everything is contingent upon Him, and nothing is beyond His rule. 24 Augustine similarly argued that God created both matter and time itself, not in time but with time, such that creation is wholly dependent on God’s will. 25 The theological and devotional implications are immense. If creation arises from divine speech, then creation is fundamentally intelligible and personal. The same Word that calls stars into existence is the Word that became flesh (John 1:14). The same breath that animates galaxies is the Spirit who gives new life to believers (John 20:22). To confess creatio ex nihilo is not merely to affirm an abstract doctrine of beginnings, it is to recognize that our very existence is grounded in the living Word of God, and that the universe itself is sacramental, bearing the imprint of His voice. Science as “How,” Genesis as “Who and Why” In wrestling with the relationship between Genesis and modern science, it is vital to avoid two opposite but equally distorting errors. The first is treating Genesis as if it were a scientific textbook, as though its purpose were to supply astronomical charts or geological models.

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: hip between Genesis and modern science, it is vital to avoid two opposite but equally distorting errors. The first is treating Genesis as if it were a scientific textbook, as though its purpose were to supply astronomical charts or geological models. The second is treating science as if it could answer ultimate theological questions about origin, meaning, and destiny. Both errors collapse distinct but 25 Augustine, Confessions , trans. Henry Chadwick (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1991), XI.13–14. 24 Thomas Aquinas, Summa Theologica , trans. Fathers of the English Dominican Province (New York: Benziger Brothers, 1947), I, Q.45, Art. 1 149

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: complementary ways of knowing into one another, resulting in confusion rather than clarity. Science excels in describing processes: how stars form from interstellar gas clouds, how planets orbit their suns according to gravitational laws, and how galaxies cluster under the fabric of cosmic expansion. Scripture, by contrast, reveals the Person behind the process, the One who speaks, wills, and sustains all things. The two are not contradictory; they are different in scope and intent. To confuse them is to diminish both. John Calvin anticipated this distinction when he observed that the Spirit “accommodated” the language of Genesis to the capacity of ordinary people. 26 Moses, Calvin argued, was not tasked with instructing Israel in astronomy, but with revealing the true God as Creator. 27 The purpose of Genesis is theological, not scientific. It declares who made the heavens and the earth, why creation exists, and how human beings are to live within it. As Calvin put it elsewhere, Scripture is the “spectacles” through which we behold the world rightly. 28 Genesis, then, answers questions that no telescope or laboratory can address: Who am I? Why am I here? What is the destiny of the world? Science can trace the chemical processes of DNA, but it cannot tell us the meaning of life. It can measure the lifespan of stars, but it cannot tell us why the heavens declare the glory of God (Ps. 19:1). It can describe entropy, but it cannot tell us why creation groans for redemption (Rom. 8:22). As the 20th-century theologian Karl Barth insisted, “Revelation does not give us information about the world in addition to what we already have, 28 Ibid., 1.6.1. 27 John Calvin, Institutes of the Christian Religion , trans.

As the 20th-century theologian Karl Barth insisted, “Revelation does not give us information about the world in addition to what we already have, 28 Ibid., 1.6.1. 27 John Calvin, Institutes of the Christian Religion , trans. Ford Lewis Battles (Philadelphia: Westminster Press, 1960), 1.14.1. 26 John Calvin, Commentary on Genesis , trans. John King (Grand Rapids: Baker, 1996), 78. 150

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: but it gives us the key to understand what the world means.” 29 Science is the study of mechanisms; Scripture is the unveiling of meaning. When the two are kept in their proper relation, they enrich one another: science describes the harmony, theology introduces us to the Composer. The Christological Center Genesis and cosmology alike find their true coherence not in abstract principles or impersonal forces but in the person of Jesus Christ. John’s prologue echoes Genesis with deliberate intent: “In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God… Through him all things were made; without him nothing was made that has been made” (John 1:1-3). Here the Logos is not a philosophical concept but the eternal Son, the divine Word who both is God and is with God, the One in whom creation has its origin. Paul makes the same point in Colossians: “For in him all things were created: things in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible, whether thrones or powers or rulers or authorities; all things have been created through him and for him. He is before all things, and in him all things hold together” (Col. 1:16-17). Creation is not merely through Christ but also for Christ, He is both the agent and the goal of all reality. He is the Alpha and the Omega, the beginning and the end (Rev. 22:13). From this vantage, cosmology finds its true foundation. The Big Bang may describe the unfolding of energy and space-time, but it cannot explain why the universe coheres in orderly law rather than disintegrating into chaos. Christian theology answers: the Logos is the One in whom all things hold together. He is not only the Beginner but also the Sustainer. 30 30 Karl Barth, Church Dogmatics III/1: The Doctrine of Creation , trans. G. W.

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: r than disintegrating into chaos. Christian theology answers: the Logos is the One in whom all things hold together. He is not only the Beginner but also the Sustainer. 30 30 Karl Barth, Church Dogmatics III/1: The Doctrine of Creation , trans. G. W. Bromiley (Edinburgh: T&T Clark, 1958), 11-12. 29 Karl Barth, Church Dogmatics I/1 , trans. G. W. Bromiley (Edinburgh: T&T Clark, 1936), 119. 151

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: Moreover, in His resurrection, Christ becomes the inaugurator of New Creation, the dawn of a cosmos renewed. As N. T. Wright argues, the resurrection is not merely the reversal of death but the launching of God’s new world within the old. 31 The first creation began with the words, “Let there be light” (Gen. 1:3). The new creation begins with the proclamation: “The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it” (John 1:5). In Christ, creation and redemption are inseparably bound: the Word who called light into being is the Light who overcomes death. Thus, Christian theology does not fear cosmology, it transcends it. Every scientific model, whether Big Bang or steady-state or multiverse, remains incomplete unless it finds its meaning in Christ. As John Polkinghorne insisted, “Science tells us how the world works, but theology tells us why the world is there at all and both find their ultimate ground in Christ.” 32 Without Him, cosmology is an unfinished sentence; with Him, it becomes a hymn of praise. Wonder and Worship At the end of the day, the purpose of Genesis is not to win a scientific debate but to summon us into worship. The opening chapters of the Bible are a call to behold the Creator with awe, not merely to analyze the mechanics of beginnings. As the psalmist declares: “The heavens declare the glory of God; the skies proclaim the work of his hands” (Ps. 19:1). This declaration holds whether through ancient eyes gazing up at constellations or through modern telescopes peering into galaxies billions of light years away. Science may trace the patterns, but Genesis reveals the Person behind them.

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: ” (Ps. 19:1). This declaration holds whether through ancient eyes gazing up at constellations or through modern telescopes peering into galaxies billions of light years away. Science may trace the patterns, but Genesis reveals the Person behind them. The physicist Stephen Hawking once asked: “What is it that breathes fire into the equations and makes a universe for them to describe?” 33 Genesis 33 Stephen Hawking, A Brief History of Time (New York: Bantam, 1988), 174. 32 John Polkinghorne, Theology in the Context of Science (London: SPCK, 2008), 45. 31 N. T. Wright, Surprised by Hope: Rethinking Heaven, the Resurrection, and the Mission of the Church (New York: HarperOne, 2008), 216-20. 152

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: answers simply: God. He is the One who breathes, who speaks, who gives. The biblical creation account does not explain the mathematics of beginnings but unveils the meaning : that all things exist by the will of the Creator. For the believer, then, cosmology is not a threat but an occasion for wonder. Whether creation came by instant command or unfolded through processes we only dimly understand, the universe exists because God willed it. The proper response is not endless speculation but humble adoration. As Karl Barth observed, creation is first and foremost “the external basis of the covenant,” a theater prepared for the revelation of God’s glory. 34 To stand before the cosmos is to stand in a temple, a sacred space ordered for worship. Thus, when we read, “In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth” (Gen. 1:1), and when we look at galaxies stretching across unfathomable distances, our posture should be the same: awe, gratitude, and trust. The world is not chaos. It is gift. It is temple. It is the theater of God’s glory. The book of Revelation completes this arc, declaring that the end of history is not dissolution but communion: “Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man” (Rev. 21:3). From Genesis to Revelation, the invitation is constant, behold our God . He is the Maker of heaven and earth, the One who spoke light into darkness, and the One in whom the story finds its fulfillment. 34 Karl Barth, Church Dogmatics III/1: The Doctrine of Creation , trans. G. W. Bromiley (Edinburgh: T&T Clark, 1958), 94. 153

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: Glossary Adamah Hebrew for “ground” or “soil.” Related to adam and used to underscore humanity’s earthly origin and mortality (Gen 2:7, 3:19). Adam Hebrew for “human” or the first man. Used both generically for humanity and specifically for the historical figure in Genesis. Ancient Near East (ANE) Geographic-cultural setting of the Old Testament world, including Mesopotamia, Egypt, Canaan, and surrounding regions. Anthropology, biblical The doctrine of humanity drawn from Scripture, focused on creation in God’s image, vocation, fall, and redemption. Apodosis The main clause of a conditional sentence. Useful when parsing covenant or promise formulas. Apsu Primordial fresh waters deity in Babylonian myth, featured in the Enuma Elish . Atrahasis Epic Old Babylonian narrative describing creation of humans from clay mixed with divine blood to relieve the gods of labor, and a subsequent flood. Bdellium A resin or precious substance listed with gold and onyx in Eden’s description, signaling luxury and sacred adornment (Gen 2:12). 158

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: Behold Scriptural invitation to see with faith-filled attention. In your book, a cue for doxological vision, not merely observation. Bereshit Hebrew title of Genesis, “In the beginning.” Signals a purposeful start to time and history. Canon The recognized collection of inspired biblical books used by the Church for faith and practice. Christology The study of the person and work of Christ. In your book, the lens for reading creation, temple, Sabbath, and image-bearing. Cosmic temple A view that creation is structured as God’s temple, climaxing in the seventh-day rest as enthronement. Covenant A solemn, relational bond initiated by God with promises, obligations, and signs, guiding biblical history and identity. Creatio ex nihilo “Creation out of nothing.” The classical Christian teaching that God brought all things into existence by His Word, not from preexistent matter. Devarim Hebrew title of Deuteronomy, “Words.” Emphasizes covenant speech shaping God’s people. Dominion Authorized stewardship given to humanity to reflect God’s wise rule in creation, never exploitation. 159

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: Doxology Glory-language or praise to God. In your project, the goal of theology is worship. Eden The garden sanctuary where God dwelt with His image-bearers. Prototype of temple presence and human vocation. Eisegesis Reading meaning into a text from one’s own assumptions, the opposite of exegesis. Ekphrasis Vivid description. Helpful in reading temple and garden imagery as theological art. Elohim A Hebrew title for God, stressing His majesty and sovereignty in Genesis 1. Enuma Elish Babylonian creation epic where Marduk slays Tiamat and fashions the cosmos from her carcass. Contrasts with Genesis’ peaceful creation by speech. Eschatology The doctrine of last things. In your book, new creation fulfillment of temple, Sabbath, and image-bearing. Ezer “Helper,” a word used of God’s aid to His people and of the woman in Gen 2:18. Connotes strength and indispensable partnership. Ezer kenegdo “Helper corresponding to him.” Signals equality, complementarity, and mutuality in the creation of woman. 160

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: Exegesis Drawing meaning out of a text by careful attention to language, context, and canon. Genesis First book of the Bible, origin story of creation, image, Sabbath, and temple patterns that the rest of Scripture develops. Gethsemane Garden of Jesus’ agony. Functions in your arc as a garden-to-garden hinge toward new creation. Glory The weight and radiance of God’s self-revelation. Creation is the theater of glory, the Church reflects it, new creation overflows with it. Hendiadys A single idea expressed by two terms joined with “and,” often used to intensify meaning. Hevel “Vapor” or “breath” in Ecclesiastes, describing fleetingness, not necessarily meaninglessness. Hermeneutics The art and theory of interpretation. Your work models a canonical, Christ-centered hermeneutic. Imago Dei Latin for “image of God.” Humanity’s identity and vocation to represent God, reflect His character, and extend His rule. Incarnation The Word made flesh in Jesus Christ. God’s dwelling with humanity in personal, embodied presence. 161

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: Inspired Scripture The Bible as God-breathed, authoritative for faith and life. Guides all doctrine and devotion. JPS The Jewish Publication Society, producer of respected Jewish translations and commentaries. Kabash Hebrew “to subdue.” In Genesis, the call to order and cultivate creation, never to exploit. Kenosis Self-emptying of the Son in taking human form. Highlights humility and service, not loss of deity. Kingu Figure in Enuma Elish whose blood is mixed with clay to create humans. Emblem of utilitarian anthropology that Genesis rejects. LXX Septuagint, the ancient Greek translation of the Hebrew Bible, influential in early Christian interpretation. Logos “Word.” In John 1, the eternal Son through whom all things were made and who became flesh. Ma’at Egyptian concept of cosmic order, truth, and balance. Offers a foil to biblical wisdom grounded in God’s character. Merism Two extremes used to express totality. “Heavens and earth” means “everything.” 162

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: Mimesis Imitation or representation. In biblical theology, humans image God by reflecting His character in action. Mishkan Hebrew for “tabernacle,” the portable dwelling of God’s presence among Israel. New Creation The renewed heavens and earth inaugurated in Christ’s resurrection and consummated at His return. New Jerusalem The garden-city of Revelation 21-22 where God dwells with His people forever. Nicene faith Creedal confession of the Church regarding the Trinity and the person of Christ. Pneumatology The doctrine of the Holy Spirit. Central to breath, life, temple, and new creation in your book. Priest-king A combined role reflected in Adam’s vocation, Israel’s calling, and Christ’s fulfillment. Prolepsis A foretaste of the future in the present. The resurrection is proleptic new creation. Protology The doctrine of first things. Genesis 1-2 as the pattern and promise of all God’s works. 163

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: Radah Hebrew “to rule.” In Genesis, a regal stewardship patterned on God’s just and caring rule. Revelation, general and special General is God’s self-disclosure in creation. Special is God’s speech in Scripture and the Son. Ruach Hebrew for breath, wind, Spirit. The life-giving presence of God, from creation to new creation. Sabbath The seventh day sanctified by God. Sign of creation’s completion, divine enthronement, and human rest. Sanctification The Spirit’s work of conforming believers to Christ’s image in life and character. Sarcina A Latin term for burden or load. Useful metaphorically when contrasting slavery and sonship. Sensus plenior “Fuller sense.” The canonical meaning of a text as it unfolds in Christ. Shamar Hebrew “to keep, guard.” Adam’s priestly charge to protect sacred space, later used for Levitical duty. Shekinah Post-biblical term for God’s dwelling glory. Useful as shorthand for manifest presence. 164

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: Shemot Hebrew title of Exodus, “Names.” Focuses on identity, deliverance, and covenant. Sonship Adoptive status and vocation granted by grace in Christ. Opposed to slavery to sin. Tabernacle Portable temple that housed God’s presence. Anticipates the incarnation and the Church as God’s dwelling. Teleology Purpose or design. Scripture presents history as moving from creation to new creation under God’s plan. Temple Permanent house of God’s presence in Jerusalem. A microcosm of creation, fulfilled in Christ and His Church. Theophany Visible manifestation of God’s presence, often with glory-cloud imagery. Theosis Patristic term for participation in the divine life by grace. Resonates with transformation into Christ’s image. Tiamat Chaos goddess in Babylonian myth. Her defeat and dismemberment stand opposite Genesis’ peaceful creation. Torah Instruction or law. The first five books, foundational for covenant life and worship. 165

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: Tree of Life Symbol of divine life and unending fellowship. Lost after the fall, regained in Christ and restored in Revelation. Trinity One God in three Persons, Father, Son, and Spirit. Foundation for understanding creation, redemption, and dwelling. Typology God-ordained patterns that prefigure fulfillment in Christ. Eden, temple, priesthood, and Sabbath are major types. Vayikra Hebrew title of Leviticus, “And He called.” Focuses on holiness, sacrifice, and proximity to God. Vice-regent A delegated ruler who represents the king. Humanity’s royal vocation under God. Wisdom literature Biblical books that teach skill for godly living. Proverbs, Job, Ecclesiastes, and others. Word of God God’s personal self-communication. In Scripture, speech that creates, covenants, judges, and saves. In Christ, the Word made flesh. YHWH The covenant name of God revealed to Moses, often rendered “the LORD.” Sign of God’s self-giving presence and faithfulness. Zion The hill of the temple in Jerusalem. Symbol of God’s reign, presence, and salvation. 166

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: Zoharic echoes Later Jewish mystical literature that sometimes echoes temple and Eden motifs. Use with care in Christian theology. 167

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: About the Book. A Revelation from Genesis: Seeing the Whole Bible in Genesis 1 and 2 is a theological, devotional, and scholarly journey into the first pages of Scripture. More than an introduction to creation, Genesis 1 and 2 provide the foundation for understanding the whole story of the Bible. Dr. Daniel Folarin guides readers through these opening chapters with clarity and depth, showing how Genesis was written not in a cultural vacuum but in conversation with the myths and cosmologies of the ancient Near East. Where surrounding nations spoke of violent gods, endless cycles, or humanity as divine laborers, Genesis offers a radically different vision: a sovereign God who creates by His Word, a world designed as His temple, and humanity dignified as His image-bearers. Drawing on biblical scholarship, historical theology, and pastoral insight, the book explores themes such as: ● Creation and Order : God’s speech as the source of light, life, and meaning. ● Human Vocation : The image of God as royal-priestly calling. ● Temple Theology : The cosmos as the dwelling place of God. ● Christological Fulfillment : How Genesis finds its true center in Christ, the Word through whom all things were made and the Redeemer who inaugurates New Creation. ● Practical Application : What Genesis reveals about our identity, purpose, and destiny today. This book is both an academic exploration and a devotional invitation. It bridges the ancient and the modern, the scholarly and the spiritual, calling readers to see Genesis not merely as history, but as revelation—a 168

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: revelation that points to Christ, centers our lives in God’s purpose, and fills us with awe at the Creator who made the heavens and the earth. A Revelation from Genesis is for pastors, students, theologians, and believers who long to go beyond debates about science and myth, and instead encounter Genesis as the living Word of God. 169

title: A Revelation from Genesis
text: About the Author Dr. Daniel Folarin is a pastor, theologian, and author with a passion for bringing biblical depth into everyday faith. As the founding pastor of Great Haven Assembly and a mentor to leaders across nations, he combines pastoral warmth with scholarly rigor, guiding people into a deeper understanding of Scripture and a richer walk with God. Trained in divinity and theology, Dr. Folarin’s ministry spans preaching, teaching, and writing. He is the author of multiple works in theology and Christian living, including his Soteria series and other volumes that explore biblical doctrine, Christian identity, and victorious faith. His writings are marked by clarity, devotion, and a commitment to showing how the Bible speaks freshly to the modern world. Known for his ability to bridge scholarship and spirituality, Dr. Folarin draws from biblical studies, ancient Near Eastern backgrounds, church history, and contemporary theology, always with a Christ-centered focus. His calling is to equip the Church with what he calls biblical intelligence ; wisdom that forms resilient disciples and builds lives anchored in God’s Word. Above all, he is a servant of Christ, devoted to seeing people grow in faith, hope, and love until the whole earth is filled with the knowledge of the glory of the Lord. He lives in Calgary, Alberta, with his beloved wife, Tolulade, and their three beautiful children: Charis, Eirene, and Doxa. 170

Or, as our Lord Himself prayed: "The glory that you have given me I have given to them, that they may be one even as we are one, I in them and you in me, that they may become perfectly one, so that the world may know that you sent me and loved them even as you loved me" (Jn 17:21-23, RSV). ~ I would like to express a special thanks to the Lilly Fellows Program at Valparaiso University; a grant from this fine organiza­ tion helped underwrite this project. She read the whole manuscript several times and provided invaluable advice and criticism. [ 1] THE PROMISE OF THE IMAGE JOHN BEHR T HAT human beings are important is something we take for granted, and not only because we are human beings (or so we think-more on this later). It is a theme that has provoked reflection since time immemorial, and not only for us human beings: the question of the Psalmist- "What is man that thou art mindful of him?" (Ps 8:4, 144:3; cf. Jb 7:17; Heb 2:6)­ presumes that no less than God himself recognizes. our worth! However, over recent decades, the subject of human dignity has become very controversial, es­ pecially as it has come to be utilized as a mainstay in arguments about bioethics. "Dignity;' Harvard Uni­ versity's Steven Pinker argues, is a "squishy, subjec­ tive notion, hardly up to the heavyweight moral de- . mands assigned to it:' It is a vague concept employed 15

title: Behr-Promise_of_Image
text: 16 JOHN BEHR uncritically, he further claims, by those who wish to advance an "obstructionist bioethics" in a neutral moral language that in re­ ality rests upon prior religious convictions. Moreover, he insists, not only does it not add anything to the discussion, but it is in fact potentially harmful, as the perception of "dignity" resides in the eye of the beholder: "Every sashed and be-medaled despot re­ viewing -his troops from a lofty platform seeks to command re­ spect through ostentatious displays of dignity:'1 Now, what from a Christian perspective marks out the dignity of human beings is that, unlike the rest of creation (and even the angels), they alone are created in the image and likeness of God. This statement, of course, requires a lot of unpacking, both in re­ spect of its content and also, and perhaps more-importantly, in re­ gard to the hermeneutic by which we can make the statement. Its content has been explicated in a variety of ways over the centuries. But it is striking that the typically twentieth-century manner of restating its content-that it is as persons that human beings are in the image of God-resorts to the very same point upon which Pinker and others would attempt to rest their moral arguments. As Pinker puts it, "Even when breaches of dignity lead to an iden­ tifiable harm, it's ultimately autonomy and respect for persons that give us the grounds for condemning if' 2 '~utonomy and respect for persons:' Such language may well seem to be far less indebted to or based upon a Christian heri~ tage: we are all "persons" regardless of our race, creed, or status. It would seem to be an eminently humanistic claim.

title: Behr-Promise_of_Image
text: g if' 2 '~utonomy and respect for persons:' Such language may well seem to be far less indebted to or based upon a Christian heri~ tage: we are all "persons" regardless of our race, creed, or status. It would seem to be an eminently humanistic claim. It is the first of the "self-evident" truths proclaimed by the Declaration of In­ dependence (1776), that "all men are created equal, that they are 1. Steven Pinker, "The Stupidity of Dignity;' The New Republic, May 28, 2008, 28-31. 2. Ibid. 1 l 1 THE PROMISE OF THE IMAGE 17 endowed by their Creator with certain unalienable Rights, that among these are Life, Liberty and the pursuit of Happiness:' And it is universally applied by the United Nations' Declaration of Hu­ man Rights (1948), again as the first item: '~ll human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights. They are endowed with reason and conscience and should act towards one another in a_ spirit of brotherhood:' But are these truths in fact self-evident? They are not empiri­ cally verifiable, and in fact they fly in the face of our own daily ob­ servation. Yet despite the great inequality into which human be­ ings are born-in disparate conditions, economic, social, physical, and intellectual-we would nevertheless surely still want to say , that there is something about every human being as a person that is absolute, equal, and irreplaceable. But because this conviction is not an empirical conclusion, nor even empirically verifiable, it is an a priori assumption, or, in other words, a statement of faith.

title: Behr-Promise_of_Image
text: thing about every human being as a person that is absolute, equal, and irreplaceable. But because this conviction is not an empirical conclusion, nor even empirically verifiable, it is an a priori assumption, or, in other words, a statement of faith. HUMAN NATURE AND THE "cHRISTIAN REVOLUTION" David Bentley Hart has recently argued a spirited case that this absolute value placed upon each human being as a person is not, as the fashionable enemies of Christianity are wont to as­ sume, the result of an enlightened, civilized society breaking free from the bondage of religion in the name of reason, so that if the value ascribed to the person is an a priori, it is at least one of rea­ son. Hart argues that, quite to the contrary, the very notion of the person is in fact a result of the revolution that is Christianity. He gives the example, for instance, of Peter in the Gospels, in whom, as Erich Auerbach noted, we can see "the image of a man in the highest and deepest and most tragic sense" compared to the por­ traiture of the great classical writers. Yet that he is nothing but a

title: Behr-Promise_of_Image
text: 18 JOHN BEHR Galilean peasant is not only not good taste but an act of rebellion, in which "we see something beginning to emerge from darkness into full visibility, arguably for the first time in our history: the human person as such, invested with an intrinsic and inviolable dignity, and possessed of infinite value:' 3 The very fact that we ha­ bitually and unthinkingly speak of all human beings as "persons" is a testimony to the impact that the Christian revolution has had, for to "have a person:' strictly speaking, was a right which Roman law bestowed only upon citizens-slaves were human beings lack­ ing personhood (non habens personam). For Christians in the ancient world, the Gospel was literally a message of liberation in a manner we can barely begin to com­ prehend today. Christ had triumphed over the powers of this world, all the things to which human beings had subjected them­ selves but which Christ had shown to be nothing: the elemental spirits of the universe; things which have no power over us. but to which we give subservience; things which are not but which hold us in thrall (such as, today, our "market forces")-his triumph has tamed the fearful world in which humans had formerly lived. That God created the world ex nihilo emphasized the absolute tran­ scendence of the Creator, who in reverse was now experienced as immanent within creation; and creation itself was understood as a gratuitous expression of divine love, a place of beauty and wonder, whose diversity reflected the multifaceted splendor of God's own wisdom, and thus a subject worthy of our inquisitiveness. And that the drama of salvation is enacted within this world, working backward to the beginning and forward to the eschaton, gives the time of creation a meaning and an orientation.

title: Behr-Promise_of_Image

And that the drama of salvation is enacted within this world, working backward to the beginning and forward to the eschaton, gives the time of creation a meaning and an orientation. It is within this new world created by the Christian revolution, 3· David Bentley Hart, Atheist Delusions: The Christian Revolution and its Fash­ ionable Enemies (New Haven, Conn.: Yale University Press, 2009), 167. THE PROMISE OF THE IMAGE 19 Hart argues, that our notion of "person" emerges, particularly in the context of the debates about the person and nature of the In­ carnate Son of God. As Hart writes, The rather extraordinary inference to be drawn from this doctrine [of Chalcedon] is that personality is somehow transcendent of nature. A person is not merely a fragment of some larger cosmic or spiritual cat­ egory, a more perfect or more defective expression of some abstract set of attributes, in light of which his or her value, significance, legitimacy, or proper place is to be judged. This man or that woman is not merely a specimen Of the general set of the human; rather, his or her human na­ ture is only one manifestation and one part of what he or she is or might be. And personality is an irreducible mystery, somehow prior to and more spacious than everything that would limit or define it, capable of exceeding even its own nature in order to embrace another, ever more glorious nature. This immense dignity-this infinite capacity-inheres in every person, no matter what circumstances might for now seem to limit him or her to one destiny or another.

title: Behr-Promise_of_Image
text: exceeding even its own nature in order to embrace another, ever more glorious nature. This immense dignity-this infinite capacity-inheres in every person, no matter what circumstances might for now seem to limit him or her to one destiny or another. No previous Western vision of the human being remotely resembles this one, and no other so fruitfully succeeded in embracing at once the entire range of finite human nature, in all the intricacy of its inner and outer dimensions, while simultane­ ously affirming the transcendent possibility and strange grandeur pres­ ent within each person. 4 The result of all the intense theological reflection in the controver­ sies that beset the church from the fourth to the eighth centuries, over matters which Edward Gibbon famously dismissed as turn­ ing upon an iota, was a "coherent concept of the human as such, endowed with infinite dignity in all its individual 'moments: full of powers and mysteries to be fathomed and esteemed ... an un­ imaginably exalted picture of the human person-made in the di­ vine image and destined to partake of the divine nature-without 4· Ibid., 211.

title: Behr-Promise_of_Image
text: 20 JOHN BEHR thereby diminishing or denigrating the concrete reality of human nature, spiritual, intellectual, or carnal:'s Something profound hap­ pened, resulting in a new, and radically different, way of looking at the world and understanding ourselves. Hart is clear that this was not an immediate result, nor that every supposedly "Christian'' society lived up to this reality. But, as he points out: It required an extraordinary moment of awakening in a few privileged souls, and then centuries of the relentless and total immersion of culture in the Christian story, to make even the best of us conscious of (or at least able to believe in) the moral claim of all other persons upon us, the splendor and irreducible dignity of the divine humanity within them, that depth within each of them that potentially touches upon the eter­ nal. In the light of Christianity's absolute law of charity, we came to see what we formerly could not: the autistic or Down syndrome or otherwise disabled child, for instance, for whom the world can remain a perpetual perplexity, which can too often cause pain but perhaps only vaguely and fleetingly charm or delight; the derelict or wretched or broken man or woman who has wasted his or her life away; the homeless, the ll:tlerly impoverished, the diseased, the mentally ill, the physically disabled; ex­ iles, refugees, fugitives; even criminals and reprobates. To reject, turn away from, or kill any or all of them would be, in a very real sense, the most purely practical of impulses. To be able, however, to see in them not only something of worth but indeed something potentially godlike, to be cherished and adored, is the rarest and most ennoblingly unreal­ istic capacity ever bred within human souls.

To look on the child whom our ancient ancestors would have seen as somehow unwholesome or as a worJ:hless burden, and would have abandoned to fate, and to see in him or her instead a person worthy of all affection-resplendent with divine glory, ominous with an absolute demand upon our consciences, evoking our love and our reverence-is to be set free from mere elemental exis- 5. Ibid., 213. i .l, THE PROMISE OF THE IMAGE 21 tence, and from those natural limitations that pre-Christian persons took to be the very definition of reality. 6 This is indeed a most remarkable and inspiring vision. What es­ pecially strikes one is the way Hart focuses o~ examples which are weak and broken, on the instances where we would rather turn our faces (rather like the disciples at the Passion), preferring in­ stead our idea and ideals of what constitutes human dignity and divine existence. But a vision which reverses the terms, as it were, by a divine exchange- to see divine strength in human weakness, eternal life in death, and the very Logos of God in flesh-is always going to appear a folly and a scandal to human thought. It will necessarily be a fragile vision, one that is all too easily forgotten.

title: Behr-Promise_of_Image
text: ange- to see divine strength in human weakness, eternal life in death, and the very Logos of God in flesh-is always going to appear a folly and a scandal to human thought. It will necessarily be a fragile vision, one that is all too easily forgotten. And so, Hart concludes with a troubling question: How long can our gentler ethical prejudices-many of which seem to me to be melting away with fair rapidity-persist once the faith that gave them their rationale and meaning has withered away? Love endures all things perhaps, as the apostle says, and is eternal; but as a cultural reality, even love requires a reason for its preeminence among the virtues, and the mere habit of solicitude for others will not necessarily survive when that reason is no longer found. If, as I have argued ... the "human'' as we now understand it is the positive invention of Christianity, might it not also be the case that a culture that has become truly post-Christian will also, ultimately, become posthuman?7 This may not necessarily be so, but there doesn't appear to be much cause for thinking otherwise. Having abandoned the notion of "dignity;' even Pinker resorts to a "respect for persons;' without giving any real reason for this. Hart's typically sharp posing of the question does indeed give us pause for thought. But is it really the case that the "personal" 6. Ibid., 214. 7· Ibid., 215.

title: Behr-Promise_of_Image
text: 22 JOHN BEHR dimension of human existence, as we understand it today, is really the fruit of the Christian revolution? And is this indeed the best way to think of human dignity? Is being "human" to be equated with being a "person;' as this has come to be understood today? DIGNITY AS PERSONHOOD: THEOLOGICAL RESERVATIONS It is unquestionable that the primary category in terms of which we understand ourselves today is as "persons:' And it is also clear that how we understand this- "endowed with infinite dignity in all its individual 'moments; full of powers and myster­ ies to be fathomed and esteemed;' as Hart puts it-differs from previous generations, betraying the fact that the term "person'' has its own history and evolution: human self-understanding, the hu­ man experience of self, of being a person, has changed throughout the ages, as it changes throughout the life span of a single human being (a version of Ernst Haeckel's recapitulation theory, that "on­ togeny recapitulates phylogeny"). As Charles Taylor notes, "There is some truth in the idea that people always are selves, that they distinguish inside from outside in all cultures:' But he elaborates, "The really difficult thing is distinguishing the human universals from the historical constellations, and not eliding the second into the first so that our particular way seems somehow inescapable for humans as such, as we are always tempted to do:·s Unlike a statement of anatomy (that we have a head attached to a body, for instance), the articulation of our "personhood" is necessarily self-interpretative and self-referential, and necessarily specific, bound to a particular age-of the person concerned or the period of human history with which we are concerned-and 8.
