In psychology and spiritual formation alike, we understand that past behavior is often the best indicator of future behavior, unless intentional repentance and transformation have occurred (Luke 3:8). Theologian and marriage researcher Dr. John Gottman argues that relationships begin to unravel when “the four horsemen” (criticism, contempt, defensiveness, and stonewalling) become habitual. Similarly, in Christian thought, the apostle Paul warns that we should not be “unequally yoked” (2 Corinthians 6:14), not merely as a spiritual principle but as a protective boundary. Some red flags signal immaturity that time and growth can heal. Others signal dangerous ground , not because people can’t change, but because marriage is not the place to experiment with what hasn't yet changed . Here are key red flags to prayerfully pay attention to: 189

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: a. Patterned Dishonesty Truth is the foundation of trust. When dishonesty becomes habitual, whether through lies, omissions, or strategic silence, it erodes the covenantal fabric of marriage. “A faithful man shall abound with blessings: but he that maketh haste to be rich shall not be innocent.” - Proverbs 28:20, KJV Watch for: ● Inconsistent stories that subtly shift over time ● Withholding significant details (especially about past relationships, finances, or family) ● Justifying deception as “necessary” or “for your own good” Remember: If truth is negotiable before marriage, integrity will likely be unstable within it. b. Emotional Manipulation or Control Power without love becomes coercion. If someone uses fear, guilt, or silence to shape your behavior, they are not building a partnership, they’re engineering control. Dr. Diane Langberg, a leading voice in trauma-informed Christian counseling, warns that “coercive control violates the image of God in another by removing their agency under the guise of love.” That’s not romance, it’s dysfunction. Watch for: ● Making you feel responsible for their anger, sadness, or behavior 190

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: ● Threatening to leave, withhold affection, or spiritualize guilt to control your decisions ● Systematically isolating you from family, friends, or spiritual mentors The presence of love must never mean the absence of freedom. c. Lack of Accountability Maturity is not perfection, it’s the willingness to own imperfection and grow through it. When someone resists accountability, what they're really resisting is growth. Watch for: ● Defensiveness when lovingly confronted ● Resisting spiritual authority, mentorship, or feedback ● A pattern of blame-shifting: it’s always “someone else’s fault” In Christian formation, sanctification happens in community (James 5:16; Proverbs 27:6). If someone consistently lives above correction, they are not ready for covenant. “He that being often reproved hardeneth his neck, shall suddenly be destroyed, and that without remedy.” - Proverbs 29:1, KJV d. Inconsistent Faith and Values Shared spiritual convictions are not an accessory to marriage, they are an anchor. The Bible never asks for perfection but it does command alignment (Amos 3:3). 191

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: If your potential spouse's walk with God is sporadic, performance-based, or lukewarm, the issue isn’t judgment, it’s disparity . Faith is what will sustain your union when feelings fade, challenges arise, or life changes. Watch for: ● Irregular spiritual rhythms: Only praying, serving, or attending church when it’s convenient or visible ● Pressuring you to compromise on convictions or spiritual boundaries ● Disinterest in Scripture, prayer, accountability, or fellowship Don’t confuse spiritual language for spiritual life. Fruit, not just fluency, reveals depth (Matthew 7:16). Discernment Key: Red flags don’t always scream, they often whisper . They hide in “It’s not a big deal,” “They just had a bad day,” or “They’ll change after we marry.” But red flags are not your enemy, they are mercy in disguise. They are the Holy Spirit’s gentle caution saying: Don’t ignore the signs now and expect peace later. Wisdom is the ability to interpret warning signs as God's kindness, not just inconvenience. 2. Positive Signs to Value and Nurture (Green Flags) Green flags are the markers of a healthy foundation , the qualities and behaviors that demonstrate not just capability but readiness for partnership. 192

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: They don't signal perfection but rather a trajectory of growth and mutual commitment. In the same way a gardener watches for signs of healthy soil before planting seeds, discerning the green flags in a potential spouse helps you identify whether the environment is conducive to a lasting relationship. These indicators are often humble and unassuming, yet they are critical in building the kingdom-centered marriage you envision. a. Emotional Honesty and Humility True humility is not just about saying “sorry” when you’ve been caught, it’s about admitting that you’re still learning and growing together. Emotional honesty is the bridge between two hearts; it’s the vulnerability to say, “I was wrong,” or “I don’t have it all figured out, but I’m willing to grow.” Theologian Timothy Keller notes, "Humility is not thinking less of yourself, but thinking of yourself less." This creates a marriage dynamic where neither partner is threatened by the other’s strengths or weaknesses but can instead walk humbly together in mutual growth. Green Light: ● They admit mistakes without shifting blame (Psalm 51:17) ● They seek feedback and accept correction gracefully (Proverbs 12:1) ● They understand that vulnerability is strength in seed form (Philippians 2:3-4) 193

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: b. Shared Spiritual Direction While it’s true that couples may differ on secondary matters, a shared spiritual direction is vital for a long-lasting marriage. A unified commitment to Christ creates a common purpose and a foundation to stand on when life challenges arise. Your spiritual life doesn’t need to be an identical twin, but it must be rooted in the same soil of faith. Dr. Gary Thomas, in his book Sacred Marriage , highlights that a couple’s spiritual intimacy often determines the quality of their emotional and relational intimacy. Shared devotion to God is a lifeline that sustains joy even in hard seasons. Green Light: ● They actively pursue God, not just show up for church attendance (Matthew 6:33) ● They seek spiritual growth with you, showing a willingness to be sharpened (Proverbs 27:17) ● They value prayer, the Word, and godly counsel (Colossians 3:16) c. Respectful Communication Communication is the oil that keeps the gears of marriage moving smoothly. However, respectful communication , which involves listening with empathy and speaking with grace, is often the unsung hero of successful relationships. It’s easy to assume that “good communication” is just talking, but true communication involves listening in love, even when it’s uncomfortable. The Apostle James teaches us, “Let every man be swift to hear, slow to speak, slow to wrath” (James 1:19). Healthy communication isn’t about 194

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: winning the argument, it’s about building understanding and maintaining respect for one another as equal partners. Green Light: ● They don’t weaponize silence, anger, or withdrawal to control the conversation (Ephesians 4:26-27) ● They make space for your voice and seek to understand before responding (Proverbs 18:13) ● They express disagreement with respect , not contempt (Ephesians 4:15) d. Consistent Character Over Time Time is the great revealer of character. A charming personality or persuasive speech may win you over for a season, but the fruit of the Spirit (Galatians 5:22-23) will stand the test of time. Look for consistent patterns, not fleeting moments of good behavior. Character is not about what a person is for a short period but about who they are in every season, public or private. As author and philosopher C.S. Lewis put it, "Integrity is doing the right thing, even when no one is watching." Green Light: ● They are the same person in public and private, showing consistency (Luke 16:10) ● Their lifestyle aligns with their words, revealing integrity (Matthew 7:20) 195

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: ● People who know them trust their character, even when it's hard to see (Proverbs 22:1) Reflection Prompt: Don’t just ask, “Do I love them?” Instead, ask, “Can I build with them?” A strong marriage isn’t about perfect compatibility, it’s about creating a vision together and building that vision with patience, integrity, and respect for one another’s weaknesses. Green flags are not just signs of individual virtue, but markers of a relationship built on mutual understanding, love, and the grace to grow together. Keep your eyes open for these green flags, and be ready to nurture them with prayer, patience, and purpose. 3. When to Pause or Walk Away Discernment isn’t always about rushing forward with a definitive “yes.” Sometimes, it’s about recognizing that the right decision may involve pausing to gain clarity or even walking away from a situation that isn’t healthy. These moments can be painful, but they are part of a divine wisdom that honors wholeness over hasty decisions . In relationships, discernment requires courage to say "not now" or "not ever", and that’s okay. The cost of rushing into marriage without discernment is often a lifelong struggle with preventable pain. It’s better to walk away in wisdom than to settle for a future filled with regret. As Proverbs 4:7 says, “Wisdom is the principal thing; therefore get wisdom: and with all thy getting, get understanding.” 196

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: Pause When: Sometimes, the absence of peace or the difficulty of a situation is not a clear signal to walk away. It may just be a moment to pause, reflect, and seek deeper clarity. Pausing allows you to stop, re-examine, and process. a. There is potential, but unresolved trauma, instability, or immaturity still dominates the relationship. Potential is a wonderful thing, but it needs time to mature. Relationships can have incredible potential, but if one or both partners are carrying unresolved trauma or dealing with instability , growth may be hindered. Trauma may manifest in ways that are subtle yet profound, creating reactions that cloud discernment or suppress emotional availability. Pause and evaluate: ● Is healing taking place, or is the trauma being avoided? ● Are you both open to personal growth, or is emotional immaturity continuing to surface? ● Can you envision a future where these wounds are healed, or is this just a recurring cycle? Counsel from trusted mentors and wise individuals who can provide an unbiased perspective can be incredibly valuable in these moments. b. You see growth, but it’s still too early to tell if it’s consistent. Change is often hard to measure in the short term. It’s easy to get excited when you see a glimpse of growth, but growth requires consistency over time. If a person is showing positive change, but 197

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: you’re unsure whether this change is sustainable, it might be time to pause and observe a bit longer. Pause and evaluate: ● Is there consistent effort to improve, or do the changes seem temporary? ● Are old patterns of behavior reemerging? ● Are both partners committed to lasting transformation? Growth is a journey, and while it’s good to see progress, it’s important to test its roots and fruit before fully committing. c. Godly counsel urges you to wait and examine more closely. There are times when our emotions, desires, and even well-meaning intentions cloud our judgment. In these moments, godly counsel from spiritually mature individuals can offer clarity and wisdom that you may not see in the heat of the moment. Pause and evaluate: ● Have you sought counsel from trusted leaders who know you well? ● Are they recommending you pause or take more time to discern? ● Do their insights resonate with your spirit or bring peace to your decision-making? Sometimes God speaks through those we trust, guiding us with wisdom we might not have on our own. 198

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: Walk Away When: There are moments when discernment calls for a decisive “no.” This isn’t about walking away from discomfort or challenges, but about recognizing when certain red flags are non-negotiable, especially when they compromise your safety, well-being, or faith. Walking away requires not just discernment but courage, courage to protect your future and trust that God has a better plan. a. Red flags are denied, minimized, or spiritualized. A critical red flag in any relationship is the refusal to acknowledge or take responsibility for problematic behavior. If red flags are denied , minimized , or spiritualized (e.g., “This is just my cross to bear” or “God will change them”), it shows a lack of honesty and accountability. This pattern signals that real issues are being avoided or rationalized. Walk away and evaluate: ● Are red flags being minimized or ignored for the sake of convenience? ● Is there a lack of honest self-examination or remorse for hurtful actions? ● Is the behavior being justified by “spiritual” terms without real repentance or change? God calls us to peace, not to tolerate toxic behaviors masked in spiritual rhetoric (Ephesians 4:26-27). b. You feel less like yourself and more like someone they want to control. In healthy relationships, both partners should encourage each other to be their truest selves. If you begin to feel controlled, 199

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: manipulated, or pressured to become someone else, it’s a red flag. True love respects individuality, allowing space for each person to thrive and flourish. Walk away and evaluate: ● Are you being pressured to conform to someone else’s expectations? ● Do you feel isolated from your core identity or passions? ● Are your boundaries being constantly crossed or disregarded? Healthy relationships foster growth, not control. Love allows both partners to become more of who they truly are in Christ. c. You sense God’s peace lifting, and pressure replacing prayer. One of the most telling signs that it’s time to walk away is the absence of God’s peace. Peace isn’t the absence of conflict, but the presence of clarity and confidence in the decision-making process. If you sense the peace of God lifting and pressure starting to cloud your judgment, it’s time to reevaluate. When pressure replaces prayer, your clarity may be compromised, and your emotional state may become the driving force rather than the Holy Spirit. Walk away and evaluate: ● Is there a sense of growing pressure rather than peace? ● Are you feeling more anxious or overwhelmed than confident? ● Are you being led by fear or spiritual peace in your decision-making? 200

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: As Isaiah 26:3 promises, “You will keep him in perfect peace, whose mind is stayed on You.” If peace is absent, don’t ignore the absence, it may be God’s way of guiding you. Reflection: Peace is not always the absence of conflict, but the presence of clarity. If peace is missing, don’t override it. The decision to pause or walk away may be difficult, but it is always the wisest course when it comes to protecting your future and aligning with God’s plan. God’s will is always the best path, even if it requires difficult decisions along the way. Discernment is a Gift from God Discernment is more than just a skill, it’s a gift from God, one that helps us navigate relationships with wisdom and grace. It doesn’t make us cynical or overly suspicious; rather, it sharpens our ability to recognize what’s truly in front of us, both the good and the bad. While love is a powerful force, love that is rooted in discernment is what makes relationships sustainable. Without discernment, love can become blind, but when paired with wisdom, it becomes a force that nurtures growth and truth. Discernment keeps us from being swept away by surface-level emotions or the desire to rush forward without understanding the full picture. When we trust the Holy Spirit, He becomes our guide, illuminating the areas that our eyes might miss and our hearts might ignore. He gives us the courage to take bold steps when necessary, to pause, pursue, or even walk away, always with peace. The peace of God isn't the absence of difficulty; it’s the presence of confidence that we are aligned with His will. 201

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: As Proverbs 3:5-6 encourages us: “Trust in the Lord with all thine heart; and lean not unto thine own understanding. In all thy ways acknowledge Him, and He shall direct thy paths.” Discernment isn’t about finding perfection, it’s about finding peace in the direction God is leading us. And when we follow His guidance, we can rest in the assurance that we’re making decisions that honor Him and lead to His best for us. 202

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: Chapter 13 Dating with Intention: From Friendship to Engagement Cultivating Emotional Intimacy Dating is often seen as a time to explore compatibility and attraction, but at its core, it’s about cultivating emotional intimacy. Emotional intimacy isn’t a fleeting sensation; it’s the slow, deliberate building of trust, understanding, and connection. It’s the bedrock that supports the relationship long after the excitement of infatuation fades. Without it, a relationship may struggle to sustain itself through life’s inevitable challenges. At the heart of emotional intimacy is the ability to share your authentic self, your hopes, dreams, fears, and vulnerabilities, with another person, and for them to reciprocate. When both individuals feel emotionally safe enough to be real, without fear of judgment, the relationship has the potential to thrive. Emotional intimacy fosters the kind of connection that goes beyond superficial attraction, enabling both partners to grow closer and form a bond that can withstand the trials of time. Key Elements of Emotional Intimacy Emotional intimacy is not just about sharing feelings; it’s about creating a space where both partners can be vulnerable, heard, and trusted. It is the foundation of a relationship that goes beyond surface-level attraction and compatibility. Here are three key elements to cultivate emotional intimacy, ensuring your connection is strong, resilient, and lasting. 1. Vulnerability: Opening the Door to Authentic Connection Vulnerability is the act of showing up as your true self, without pretense or defenses. Emotional intimacy thrives when both partners are willing to share their inner worlds, their fears, dreams, insecurities, and past wounds. 203

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: It’s not just about sharing the easy or positive parts of your life, but also the messy and imperfect ones. This kind of openness fosters trust and mutual understanding. Brené Brown, in her groundbreaking work on vulnerability ( Daring Greatly ), argues that vulnerability is the heart of connection: “Vulnerability is not winning or losing; it's having the courage to show up and be seen when we have no control over the outcome.” When you let each other into your vulnerabilities, it invites deeper emotional connection and builds a foundation of trust. Practical Tip : During your conversations, go beyond surface-level talk. Ask questions that allow your partner to share deeper parts of themselves, such as, “What’s something you’ve been afraid to share with someone?” or “What’s one of the most difficult experiences you’ve had growing up?” These questions open doors to deeper emotional intimacy and understanding. 2. Active Listening: Understanding the Heart Behind the Words Listening is one of the most powerful ways to cultivate emotional intimacy. But it’s not enough to just hear words, active listening involves understanding the emotions and motivations that drive those words. It means being fully present in the moment, without distractions, and giving your partner the space to express their true feelings. Psychologist and communication expert Dr. John Gottman, known for his work on relationship dynamics, emphasizes the importance of active listening in creating lasting emotional bonds. In his book The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work , Gottman notes that successful couples often practice what he calls “empathic listening,” which is not just about hearing words but also about responding with empathy and validation.

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: l bonds. In his book The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work , Gottman notes that successful couples often practice what he calls “empathic listening,” which is not just about hearing words but also about responding with empathy and validation. This strengthens the emotional connection and reinforces trust. 204

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: Example : When your partner shares something challenging, instead of jumping into problem-solving mode, focus on listening and offering support. For example, you might say, “I hear you, and I’m so glad you trust me enough to share that. How can I support you in this?” This approach shows empathy, validating their feelings without rushing to offer solutions. Practical Tip : During conversations, resist the urge to interrupt or immediately provide advice. Instead, focus on truly understanding your partner’s emotions. Reflect back what you hear by saying, “It sounds like you’re feeling [emotion].” This shows you’re listening actively and validating their experience. 3. Building Trust: The Foundation of Emotional Intimacy Trust is the bedrock of emotional intimacy. It’s built through consistent, honest communication and respect. Without trust, it’s impossible for emotional intimacy to flourish. Trust is developed over time through actions that demonstrate reliability, honesty, and care. As you build trust, the connection between you and your partner deepens, allowing both of you to feel emotionally safe and supported. Trust is not just about major commitments; it’s also about the little things, keeping your word, respecting boundaries, and being reliable. In The Speed of Trust , Stephen M.R. Covey states that “trust is the one thing that changes everything.” When trust is present, everything else in the relationship flows more easily. Practical Tip : Keep your promises, whether big or small. If you say you’ll do something, follow through. Trust isn’t built in a single moment, it’s the accumulation of consistent, reliable actions. When you make promises, ensure they align with your abilities and intentions.

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: promises, whether big or small. If you say you’ll do something, follow through. Trust isn’t built in a single moment, it’s the accumulation of consistent, reliable actions. When you make promises, ensure they align with your abilities and intentions. Over time, these small acts of reliability will strengthen the emotional bond between you and your partner. 205

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: A Strong Relationship Built on Emotional Intimacy Cultivating emotional intimacy is not something that happens overnight, it takes intentionality, patience, and effort. By embracing vulnerability, practicing active listening, and building trust, you create a foundation where both partners can thrive emotionally. This depth of connection strengthens the relationship, helping it endure and grow through the inevitable challenges life will bring. When emotional intimacy is nurtured, the relationship moves beyond surface-level interactions and becomes a partnership rooted in deep understanding, mutual support, and trust. It’s not always easy, but it’s always worth it. Relationships that are built on these principles have the potential to withstand time and trials, making them strong enough to endure the beautiful journey of life together. Practical Tools for Intentional Courtship Courtship is more than just a relationship with the goal of "seeing how things go." It is a deliberate, prayerful journey designed to explore compatibility, deepen emotional intimacy, and discern if the relationship is leading toward marriage. Intentional courtship requires both partners to approach the relationship with focus, transparency, and trust. Here are some practical tools to guide this journey. 206

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: 1. Set Clear Intentions: Aligning on the Purpose of Your Relationship One of the fundamental elements of intentional courtship is clarity about the purpose. Are you both dating for marriage? Is this a journey towards lifelong commitment? Both partners must be on the same page to avoid confusion and ensure that they are making decisions with shared goals in mind. When intentions are set clearly, it allows for the relationship to grow within a defined context, avoiding misunderstandings that might emerge from unspoken assumptions. A relationship without clear direction can become a source of frustration and may lead to one partner feeling misled or unsure. Practical Tip : Have an open, honest conversation early in the courtship about your expectations and goals. Ask yourself, “What do I want in a future partner, and how does this relationship align with my desires for marriage?” Share these thoughts with your partner, and make sure you are both on the same page. This helps build transparency from the outset. 2. Define Boundaries: Protecting Your Hearts and Relationship Boundaries are crucial in any relationship, especially in courtship. Setting clear emotional, physical, and relational boundaries helps maintain mutual respect and ensures both individuals feel valued. Healthy boundaries protect both partners and provide a safe space where they can grow and learn about each other. When boundaries are respected, the relationship flourishes. It allows both partners to navigate the journey of courtship without feeling pressure or discomfort. Communication about boundaries should be ongoing, as they may need to be adjusted based on the progression of the relationship and each person’s comfort level. 207

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: Example : "I believe in setting physical boundaries as a way of showing respect for each other and for our future together. Let’s talk about what we both feel comfortable with and ensure that we are honoring one another." Setting these boundaries early on fosters trust and helps safeguard the relationship from unnecessary tension. 3. Engage in Meaningful Conversations: Getting to the Heart of the Relationship Courtship should not just be a time for small talk or shared activities, it’s a season to have meaningful conversations about life’s most important topics. These include values, goals, faith, and even vulnerabilities. It’s within these deep conversations that true compatibility is revealed. Discussing significant matters, like life aspirations, family dynamics, and handling conflict, lays a strong foundation for mutual understanding. These conversations also help you discern if you and your partner have shared values and if your life goals are aligned. Relationships that focus only on surface-level topics often miss the opportunity to explore the deeper compatibility that will be crucial for a lasting marriage. Practical Tip : Ask intentional questions, such as, “How do you envision your future? What role do you think faith should play in a marriage? How do you handle conflict when it arises?” These types of questions help foster deeper intimacy and understanding, and they encourage both partners to reflect on important aspects of their future together. 208

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: 4. Invite Trusted Mentors Into Your Journey: Building a Community of Accountability Courtship is not meant to be a solitary endeavor. While the relationship between you and your partner is the heart of the journey, it’s important to invite trusted mentors and accountability into the process. This can include family members, spiritual leaders, older couples, or friends who are invested in your well-being and growth. Mentors offer wisdom, perspective, and guidance that can prevent emotional impulsivity and help you avoid blind spots. They can also act as a sounding board, providing feedback when you are uncertain or when tensions arise. Involving others in your journey keeps the courtship grounded in humility and community. Practical Tip : “We’ve been dating for a few months now, and we’d love to have you both over for dinner so you can share your thoughts on our relationship. We value your guidance and would appreciate any advice you have on this stage of our journey.” Inviting mentors into the relationship is an act of wisdom that brings clarity and support, helping you avoid making decisions in isolation. 5. Prayer and Discernment: Seeking God’s Will Together Courtship is not just about personal preferences and practical compatibility, it is ultimately about seeking God’s will for your life and the potential for a lifelong commitment. Prayer together brings God into the relationship, providing clarity, wisdom, and peace as you make decisions about the future. Courtship is a time to discern whether you and your partner are equally yoked spiritually and whether God is leading you toward marriage. Prayer should be a regular practice, not only as a couple but also individually, as you seek God’s guidance for the relationship. Trusting the Holy Spirit to 209

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: lead you both gives the relationship an eternal perspective, helping you focus on long-term unity rather than temporary feelings. Practical Tip : Set aside time each week to pray together for clarity, guidance, and strength. Also, commit to praying separately for discernment, asking God to reveal His will for the relationship. This strengthens your spiritual bond and keeps you aligned with God’s purpose for your journey. Courtship with Purpose Intentional courtship is more than just a stage between dating and marriage, it’s a season for deepening your connection, understanding each other’s hearts, and discerning God’s will for your future together. By setting clear intentions, defining boundaries, engaging in meaningful conversations, inviting mentors into your journey, and seeking God’s guidance through prayer, you create a foundation that will help lead you to a strong, lasting marriage. Courtship, when done with purpose and intentionality, becomes a sacred time of growth, learning, and alignment. It’s not just about finding the right person but becoming the right person who is ready to build a future together. Transitioning from Love to Commitment While love serves as the emotional foundation of any relationship, it is commitment that secures its future. Love, in its initial stages, is often filled with excitement, spontaneity, and passion. However, commitment is what ensures that love endures beyond the initial emotions and transforms into a lasting partnership. As a relationship deepens, moving from love to commitment is a critical transition, one that requires intentionality, mutual respect, and shared values. 210

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: 1. Recognizing Love's Depth: Love as a Decision, Not Just a Feeling At the beginning of a relationship, love is often characterized by infatuation, intense emotions, butterflies, and a desire to spend as much time as possible with your partner. While these feelings are beautiful, true love matures over time. It becomes more than just an emotional reaction, it becomes a conscious choice to commit to someone every single day, even when feelings fluctuate. The depth of love is seen in the willingness to choose your partner over and over again, even when challenges arise or when the emotions that once felt so intense may soften. It’s about deciding to stay, to grow together, and to weather the storms of life as a team. Practical Tip : Take a moment to list the qualities you love about your partner, both the obvious and the subtle traits. Reflect on the depth of your affection, the qualities that may not always be visible but contribute to the unique bond you share. By consciously acknowledging these qualities, you solidify the emotional foundation of your relationship. 2. Moving Beyond Infatuation: Commitment as a Deliberate Choice Infatuation is often an overwhelming feeling of attraction, and it can be confusing, especially in the early stages of a relationship. It tends to be fleeting and can cloud judgment. Commitment, on the other hand, is the resolve to choose your partner regardless of the ebbs and flows of feelings. It is the decision to move beyond temporary emotions and build something stable and lasting. While love may bring you joy and excitement, commitment is the daily choice to invest in your relationship, even when it’s not easy. It’s about being there for each other in good times and bad, and building a future 211

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: based on mutual trust and respect. True commitment means sticking together through the inevitable challenges, setbacks, and changes that life brings. Practical Tip : Share with your partner stories of past relationships, perhaps about challenges or setbacks that occurred, and discuss how you both might handle similar situations now that you're committed to one another. This reflection allows you to demonstrate growth and maturity in how you handle conflict, disappointment, and other obstacles. 3. Commitment in Action: Demonstrating Love Through Consistent Effort Commitment is more than just a promise, it is demonstrated daily through actions. Love may bring you together, but commitment keeps you together by requiring both partners to actively work toward building a shared future. It’s about being consistent, following through on promises, and always prioritizing the well-being of the relationship. When difficulties arise, showing commitment means choosing to work through them, communicate openly, and respect each other’s perspectives. It involves the discipline of selflessness, patience, and compromise, all of which are necessary to build a stable foundation for the future. Example : “I know we’ve had disagreements, and we may not always see eye to eye, but I’m committed to working through those moments with you because I see a future with you. Let’s grow from these experiences and continue to strengthen our bond.” This mindset shows the strength of commitment and helps both partners navigate through conflict with love and respect. 4. The Proposal: A Natural Expression of Deep Commitment 212

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: The proposal is not just a momentary gesture, it is the culmination of a long journey of intentional courtship, building trust, and nurturing emotional intimacy. By the time you arrive at the point of proposing, you and your partner should both feel secure in your shared values, life goals, and vision for the future. The proposal becomes the outward expression of a commitment that has already been deepened over time through mutual respect, trust, and love. A proposal is not merely a question; it is a promise. It’s a promise to continue growing together, to face challenges side by side, and to honor one another for the rest of your lives. By making this commitment, you’re both stepping into a covenant that will require continued effort, love, and faithfulness. Practical Tip : As you prepare for the proposal, have open and candid conversations about what marriage will look like for you. Discuss your shared vision for the future, your roles within the marriage, and how you plan to grow individually and as a couple. This will ensure that you both have realistic expectations and are aligned in your commitment to each other. From Love to Lifelong Commitment Transitioning from love to commitment is not always a smooth process; it requires patience, intentionality, and an ongoing willingness to invest in the relationship. Love may bring two people together, but commitment ensures that the bond grows deeper and stronger over time. Commitment is the unwavering foundation that allows love to thrive through life’s challenges, making it sustainable and fruitful for years to come. This transition involves recognizing the depth of your love, moving beyond fleeting infatuation, consistently demonstrating commitment through actions, and preparing for the future together.

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: nges, making it sustainable and fruitful for years to come. This transition involves recognizing the depth of your love, moving beyond fleeting infatuation, consistently demonstrating commitment through actions, and preparing for the future together. A proposal, when 213

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: the time is right, is simply the final step in a journey that has been built on mutual respect, trust, and a shared desire for lifelong partnership. When both partners choose commitment, they create a secure, loving environment where their relationship can continue to grow and flourish, ultimately leading to a marriage that stands the test of time. Cultivating a Lasting Relationship Dating with intention is more than just a series of dates, it’s a purposeful journey that prepares two people for a lifetime of love, respect, and partnership. Through intentional dating, you are laying the foundation for a relationship rooted in emotional intimacy, shared faith, and a commitment to grow together. This intentionality allows both individuals to move forward with a clear understanding of each other's values, beliefs, and goals, creating a strong and lasting bond. As you transition from friendship to engagement, it’s essential to recognize that marriage is not just about a momentary celebration or an exciting proposal, it is about the enduring choice to love, grow, and partner with one another for the long haul. The engagement period should serve as a time to deepen your connection, resolve any remaining uncertainties, and solidify your commitment to each other’s future. Marriage is a sacred covenant, one that demands more than just affection; it requires a willingness to build something enduring. It’s a journey of ongoing growth, mutual support, and the understanding that both partners will evolve over time. While love is the initial spark, commitment is the steady flame that sustains and strengthens the relationship through life’s challenges.

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS

While love is the initial spark, commitment is the steady flame that sustains and strengthens the relationship through life’s challenges. By cultivating emotional intimacy, being purposeful in your courtship, and embracing the commitment necessary for a lasting relationship, you are investing in a marriage that will withstand the tests of time. It's not about 214

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: creating perfection, there will be challenges, moments of doubt, and obstacles along the way, but about choosing to grow together, building on a foundation of trust, respect, and shared values. The key is in your willingness to enter into the covenant of marriage with the understanding that it is not a destination but a lifelong journey. The intention to love, the courage to commit, and the faith to build together will carry your relationship through seasons of joy, difficulty, growth, and transformation. Ultimately, a lasting relationship is a shared vision for the future, rooted in faith, cultivated with care, and strengthened by the love you both invest in one another. As you embark on the path to marriage, remember that this journey requires both partners to continuously choose each other, not just in the moments of joy, but especially in the moments of challenge. Together, you will build a legacy of love, honor, and commitment that will stand the test of time. 215

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: Chapter 14 Building a Sound Relationship House Creating a healthy and enduring marriage is not just about falling in love; it’s about building a strong foundation that can weather the inevitable challenges and changes that life will bring. Dr. John Gottman offers insightful frameworks for building and maintaining relationships that last. In this chapter, we will integrate Gottman’s principles, trust, commitment, shared meaning, love maps, admiration, and emotional bids, to construct a "sound relationship house," a metaphor for a healthy, sustainable marriage. Trust, Commitment, and Shared Meaning: Building the Pillars of a Strong Marriage At the heart of any enduring marriage are trust, commitment, and shared meaning. These pillars form the foundation of a relationship that not only survives the inevitable challenges but also thrives over time. Drawing from the work of Dr. John Gottman, we will explore these essential components and how they contribute to a relationship’s long-term health. Trust: The Cornerstone of Emotional Security Trust is the bedrock of any successful marriage. It is the emotional safety net that allows both partners to be vulnerable, express their needs, and share their deepest desires. According to Gottman, trust is not built in grand, sweeping gestures but in the small, everyday moments, acts of kindness, dependability, and emotional availability. Trust is like the air we breathe in a relationship: often invisible, but when it's absent, it is acutely felt. 216

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: How Trust is Built: ● Reliability: Consistently following through on promises and being there for each other when needed builds trust. These daily demonstrations of dependability foster emotional security. ● Emotional Availability: Sharing your emotional world with your partner and being present when they need support strengthens trust. Vulnerability is the glue that binds trust in a relationship, allowing both individuals to feel safe in expressing their thoughts and feelings. ● Small Acts of Kindness: Whether it’s a gentle touch, a compliment, or an understanding word, these seemingly small acts have a profound impact on trust. They signal to your partner that you care, which in turn strengthens the emotional bond. What Happens When Trust is Broken? While trust can be damaged, it can be rebuilt, but it requires time, patience, and a conscious effort from both partners. Gottman refers to this process as "repairing the ruptures." When trust is broken, it’s not just a matter of apologizing; it’s about showing through consistent actions that you are committed to restoring emotional safety. Practical Tip: Start by acknowledging past hurts, and engage in open, non-defensive communication about the issue at hand. When your partner expresses their feelings, listen actively, and be willing to take responsibility for your actions. The rebuilding of trust happens in small, consistent steps. Commitment: The Daily Choice to Choose Each Other Commitment in a marriage is much more than simply staying together. It is an intentional decision to prioritize your partner, especially in the face of adversity. Gottman emphasizes that commitment is not passive; it is active, 217

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: requiring both partners to choose each other daily. It’s about showing up for your partner, even when life becomes difficult, and resolving conflicts constructively. Commitment in Action: ● Resilience in the Face of Conflict: Disagreements and challenges are inevitable in any relationship. Commitment is about approaching these conflicts as opportunities for growth, rather than as threats to the relationship. Partners who are committed are willing to work through issues rather than walking away when things get tough. ● Long-Term Perspective: Commitment also involves having a long-term view of the relationship. In healthy marriages, both partners are committed to working through the ups and downs, recognizing that challenges are temporary, but the bond they share can be everlasting. ● Sacrifices for the Relationship: True commitment often involves making sacrifices, whether it's in terms of time, resources, or personal preferences. The willingness to put the needs of the marriage before individual desires demonstrates a deep level of commitment to each other. Practical Tip: Create shared rituals and traditions that reaffirm your commitment to one another. Whether it’s a weekly date night, a daily check-in, or a yearly getaway, these acts remind both partners that they are invested in each other’s well-being and the relationship. 218

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: Shared Meaning: Building a Life Together In a successful marriage, shared meaning refers to the creation of a unique culture and set of values within the relationship. Partners who build a life together, not just live side by side, have a deeper connection that transcends the day-to-day routine. Shared meaning involves aligning your individual goals with those of the relationship, creating a partnership where both personal and joint aspirations are honored. Creating Shared Meaning: ● Aligning Values and Goals: While every individual is unique, it’s essential for couples to understand and align their core values and long-term goals. When both partners share a similar vision for the future, whether it’s regarding family, career, or faith, they are more likely to work in harmony to achieve those goals. ● Rituals and Traditions: Shared meaning can be cultivated through shared rituals and traditions that build connection and reinforce the partnership. These rituals are symbolic and reflect the values and commitments of both partners. ● Cohesion through Life’s Milestones: Building shared meaning also involves navigating life’s milestones, such as having children, purchasing a home, or pursuing shared career goals, together. These experiences strengthen the bond between partners as they build a common narrative and create shared memories. Practical Tip: Regularly discuss your individual goals and desires, then find ways to integrate them into a shared vision for your future. Whether it’s working toward a financial goal, traveling to a new place, or even supporting each other in career changes, these shared pursuits create lasting meaning in the relationship. 219

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: The Importance of Ongoing Conversations Building trust, commitment, and shared meaning is not a one-time effort; it’s a lifelong process. The work of cultivating a lasting relationship is ongoing. Regular communication is key to maintaining these pillars and ensuring that both partners remain aligned. Practical Tip: Schedule regular "vision-setting" conversations where you discuss the long-term direction of your relationship. Share your goals, fears, and dreams, and ensure that both of you are still committed to the vision you’ve built together. These conversations keep the relationship dynamic and evolving in a healthy direction. A Blueprint for a Lasting Relationship Trust, commitment, and shared meaning are the bedrock on which a strong, lasting marriage is built. By understanding and nurturing these essential components, couples can create a relationship that not only survives but thrives over time. As Gottman suggests, the foundation of a healthy relationship is not just love, but a continual investment in one another’s well-being and the shared future they are creating together. When trust is strong, commitment is unwavering, and both partners share a common vision for the future, the marriage becomes an unshakable partnership. Through consistent effort, open communication, and shared meaning, couples can build a relationship that will stand the test of time, one that’s built not just on love, but on a deep, mutual understanding of what it means to be in this journey together. Love Maps: Getting to Know Your Partner Deeply In the journey of building a deep, lasting relationship, one of the most essential tools is what Dr. John Gottman refers to as "Love Maps." These 220

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: are the intimate, mental representations we create about our partner’s inner world, their thoughts, feelings, dreams, and preferences. In essence, a Love Map is the map to understanding the emotional terrain of your partner’s life, and it plays a pivotal role in fostering emotional intimacy and connection. Gottman’s research highlights that couples who know each other deeply, who have well-developed Love Maps, are better equipped to navigate the inevitable challenges of marriage. Why? Because when you understand your partner’s world on a deeper level, you can anticipate their needs, emotional responses, and reactions. Love Maps allow partners to respond thoughtfully, with empathy and understanding, instead of reacting in frustration or confusion. The Importance of Love Maps in Emotional Intimacy Emotional intimacy is built on the foundation of deep knowledge and understanding. It’s not enough to simply “get along” or share surface-level interests. True emotional intimacy requires partners to know each other’s core, to be aware of their fears, hopes, and even the smallest quirks that make them unique. Gottman’s studies show that couples who regularly update and revisit their Love Maps are significantly more successful at managing conflict, communicating effectively, and providing emotional support. When you understand your partner’s world, you are more capable of showing up for them when they need you most. How to Build Your Love Map: Practical Steps Building a Love Map is not a one-time task, it’s a lifelong endeavor. It involves ongoing conversations, curiosity, and openness. Here are some practical ways to create and maintain a strong Love Map: 221

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: a. Ask Thoughtful, Open-Ended Questions To truly understand your partner, you need to engage in deeper conversations about their past, present, and future. Asking open-ended questions that allow them to share more than just “yes” or “no” responses is key. Instead of asking questions like “How was your day?” (which might yield a brief reply), you can ask, “What’s something unexpected that happened today?” or “What part of your day brought you the most joy?” These types of questions prompt your partner to share the nuances of their emotional experiences, helping you gain insight into their world. Examples of Questions to Ask: ○ "What is a childhood memory that still brings you joy?" ○ "What do you wish people understood about you?" ○ "What are your biggest hopes for the future?" ○ "What is something that helps you feel loved and supported when you’re feeling overwhelmed?" b. Be Curious About Their World Curiosity is the key to discovering and updating your Love Map. It’s important to be genuinely interested in your partner’s life, not just the “big” moments, but the little things that shape their daily experience. Engage in regular check-ins about what’s going on in their life, whether it’s about their work, friends, or emotional well-being. This shows respect and communicates that you care about the details of their world. Practical Tip: Schedule time to talk about each other’s day or 222

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: week. It doesn’t always have to be a formal conversation. A casual check-in, like "How's the new project going?" or "Is there something that has been on your mind lately?" helps to keep your understanding of each other’s inner world fresh and current. c. Continually Update Your Love Map Just like a real map, a Love Map needs to be regularly updated. People change over time, and as they evolve, so should your understanding of them. Take time to stay involved in your partner’s life and show an active interest in their dreams, challenges, and aspirations. The more you ask and listen, the more you will learn about how they feel, what they need, and what’s important to them. Practical Tip: Check in about things like career changes, personal goals, or family dynamics. Even something as simple as asking, “What’s something that’s been on your mind lately?” can offer deep insights into your partner’s emotional state. d. Share Your Own Inner World Building a Love Map isn’t a one-way street. While you learn more about your partner, you must also be open and vulnerable about your own feelings, dreams, and experiences. The best relationships thrive on mutual vulnerability, where both partners feel safe enough to express their true selves without fear of judgment. Sharing your own experiences and emotional world allows your partner to build a Love Map of you as well, deepening the emotional bond. Practical Tip: Share something meaningful about yourself during conversations. For example, you could say, "Lately, I’ve been thinking a lot about how much I value our time together. I’ve realized it’s one of the things that makes me feel connected and 223

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: supported." This opens the door for deeper emotional intimacy and helps your partner understand the intricacies of your emotional landscape. e. Be Present and Listen Actively It’s important to approach conversations with an attitude of active listening. This means listening not just to the words being said, but also to the emotions behind them. By staying engaged in the moment, offering empathy, and validating your partner’s feelings, you create an environment where your partner feels truly heard and understood. Practical Tip: When your partner shares something, try to avoid offering solutions or interrupting. Instead, focus on validating their feelings. A response like, “That sounds tough. How do you feel about it?” can make a world of difference in showing support and understanding. Example: Applying Love Maps in Daily Life Consider this real-life example: "I know that you’ve been really stressed at work lately. How’s the new project going?" or "What’s something that brings you peace when you're feeling overwhelmed?" These types of questions show that you are attuned to your partner’s emotional state and that you care enough to be involved in their life. By asking meaningful questions and taking the time to listen attentively, you are not only strengthening your Love Map but also reinforcing the trust and emotional intimacy that sustain a healthy relationship. 224

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: The Power of Love Maps Love Maps are essential for nurturing emotional intimacy and fostering a deeper connection with your partner. When you know your partner’s inner world, what makes them tick, what makes them feel loved, and what causes them stress, you are better equipped to respond with empathy, patience, and support. By continuously learning about each other’s thoughts, feelings, and needs, you ensure that your relationship remains dynamic, grounded in mutual understanding, and prepared for the inevitable changes life brings. A well-developed Love Map is the key to emotional closeness, and as Gottman suggests, it’s a vital ingredient in building a lasting, fulfilling relationship. The more you invest in learning about each other, the more you create a bond that will not only withstand challenges but flourish over time. In the end, Love Maps are not just about knowing facts, they are about understanding the heart of your partner, and building a life together based on shared emotional intimacy. Admiration: Cultivating Positive Regard In the journey of building a strong, loving relationship, one of the key elements is Admiration, a vital component that Dr. John Gottman highlights in his research. Admiration goes beyond simply noticing the good things about your partner; it involves deeply appreciating their strengths, qualities, and the positive contributions they make to the relationship. By cultivating admiration, couples create an emotional climate filled with warmth, respect, and positivity, which forms the foundation for deeper intimacy. 225

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: Why Admiration Matters Admiration is essential because it helps partners feel valued, seen, and respected. It fosters a sense of mutual appreciation and builds a positive emotional connection. Couples who admire each other are more likely to feel supported and loved, which strengthens their bond, especially during times of stress or conflict. When we focus on the positive aspects of our partner, we are more likely to respond to each other with kindness and understanding rather than frustration or negativity. How to Cultivate Admiration Here are several practical ways to actively cultivate admiration in your relationship: a. Acknowledge Positive Qualities Regularly One of the easiest ways to admire your partner is to regularly acknowledge their strengths, character, and the wonderful traits they bring to the relationship. It could be something as simple as complimenting their kindness, humor, or the way they handle challenges with grace. When we focus on what we admire in our partner, we foster a positive atmosphere where both individuals feel cherished. Practical Tip: Compliment your partner daily on something you genuinely admire. Whether it's how they make you laugh, their hard work, or their thoughtful nature, expressing your admiration can strengthen your emotional connection. 226

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: b. Practice Appreciation for Both Big and Small Acts Often, we reserve our expressions of admiration for grand gestures or milestones. However, true admiration is rooted in recognizing the small, everyday acts of care, kindness, and effort your partner puts into the relationship. It’s easy to overlook these little things, but when we consciously choose to appreciate them, it reinforces the bond and fosters a deeper sense of gratitude. Practical Tip: Take note of the small things your partner does, whether it’s making your favorite meal, helping you with chores, or offering emotional support. These moments may seem insignificant, but expressing gratitude for them helps your partner feel appreciated on a daily basis. c. Start Each Day with a Mental Note of Admiration Start each day by intentionally focusing on something you admire about your partner. This practice not only helps set a positive tone for your interactions, but it also trains your mind to focus on the good in your partner. When you make it a habit to mentally acknowledge their qualities, it becomes second nature to express your admiration throughout the day. Practical Tip: Begin each morning by thinking of at least one thing you admire about your partner. It could be their work ethic, the way they love you, or their ability to stay calm under pressure. At the end of the day, share this thought with them as a reminder of your love and respect. d. Express Admiration Verbally and Physically Words of affirmation can go a long way in showing admiration. Let your partner know that you notice their efforts and that you are grateful for them. Compliments, kind words, and affectionate expressions can significantly boost their self-esteem and reinforce your emotional bond. In 227

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: addition, physical gestures like a warm hug, a smile, or a gentle touch can communicate admiration in a non-verbal way. Practical Tip: Try saying things like, "I really appreciate how dedicated you are to your work," or "Your kindness to others inspires me every day." Simple, heartfelt statements can make a big impact on how your partner feels valued and loved. e. Admire Your Partner's Growth and Efforts Admiration is not only about recognizing what your partner already does but also about acknowledging their growth and the efforts they make to improve themselves. Whether they’re working on a personal goal, learning new skills, or navigating difficult situations, expressing admiration for their progress reinforces their sense of accomplishment and encourages them to continue striving for the best. Practical Tip: If your partner is working hard on personal development or facing a challenge, let them know you admire their effort and perseverance. For example, "I’m proud of how you’ve handled the changes at work. You’ve shown so much resilience." Example of Applying Admiration in Daily Life Imagine you notice that your partner has been working extra hours on a project. Instead of simply recognizing their hard work in passing, take a moment to express your admiration. You might say something like, "I really admire how dedicated you are to this project. I know it’s not easy, but you’re doing an amazing job. I’m so proud of you." By acknowledging their efforts and showing appreciation, you not only boost their confidence but also reinforce your emotional connection. 228

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: The Power of Admiration Admiration is not a passive feeling, it’s an active choice to focus on the best qualities of your partner and express appreciation for them. By regularly acknowledging their strengths, contributions, and efforts, you cultivate a positive emotional climate in your relationship. Admiration fosters mutual respect, strengthens emotional intimacy, and creates a foundation of trust and affection. As Dr. Gottman suggests, admiration is a powerful tool for building a lasting, fulfilling relationship. When you actively cultivate positive regard and appreciation for your partner, you enhance both the emotional depth and the overall quality of your connection. In the end, admiration is not just about recognizing what’s good about your partner, it’s about celebrating who they are and the love you share together. Emotional Bids: Responding to Each Other’s Needs In any relationship, emotional connection is vital for creating a sense of security, trust, and intimacy. Dr. John Gottman introduces the concept of emotional bids , which are the subtle requests or gestures that partners make to connect emotionally. These bids can take various forms: a simple touch, a glance, a question, or even an expression of vulnerability. How partners respond to these bids plays a crucial role in the strength and health of their relationship. What Are Emotional Bids? Emotional bids are any attempts made by one partner to gain emotional support, attention, or connection from the other. These bids may be verbal or nonverbal and may include things like: ● A partner seeking eye contact. 229

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: ● A lighthearted comment or joke. ● A request for help or attention. ● A touch or gesture to initiate closeness. The key to emotional bids is that they represent a partner's attempt to share a moment of vulnerability or emotional need, and they are often subtle. Sometimes they are easily overlooked, but responding to them appropriately can build a stronger emotional bond. Why Responding to Emotional Bids Is Important How you respond to emotional bids can significantly influence the relationship’s overall emotional climate. Gottman’s research shows that couples who respond to emotional bids in a positive way, turning toward each other, tend to have stronger, more satisfying relationships. On the other hand, partners who consistently fail to respond, or who dismiss these bids, often experience emotional distance and disconnection over time. How to Respond to Emotional Bids Responding to emotional bids requires attentiveness, empathy, and a willingness to engage in moments of emotional connection. Here’s how you can positively respond to your partner’s bids: a. Recognize the Bid Emotional bids are not always obvious, and sometimes they come in the form of subtle cues. Your partner might share a fleeting comment like, "I'm feeling so overwhelmed today," or might simply look at you for attention. Recognizing these bids is the first step in responding effectively. These cues are usually an attempt to seek connection, comfort, or reassurance, and recognizing them shows your awareness of your partner's emotional needs. 230

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: Practical Tip: Pay attention to the tone of voice, body language, or facial expressions. Even a slight change in behavior, such as your partner seeking eye contact or leaning in closer, may indicate a desire for emotional connection. b. Respond with Empathy and Attentiveness Once you’ve recognized an emotional bid, the next step is to respond with empathy and attentiveness. This doesn’t always require a grand gesture, it can be something as simple as eye contact, a warm smile, or a few thoughtful words. Empathy means showing that you understand and care about your partner’s feelings, while attentiveness ensures that they feel heard and valued. Practical Tip: Try to mirror your partner’s emotional tone when responding. If they seem sad, offer a comforting response. If they are excited, share in their enthusiasm. Acknowledging their emotional state with a matching reaction builds a deeper connection. c. Avoid Dismissing or Ignoring the Bid Dismissing or ignoring emotional bids can create emotional distance. When you fail to respond to a partner’s emotional needs, whether intentionally or unintentionally, it sends a message that their feelings don’t matter. Over time, repeated dismissal of emotional bids can lead to resentment, hurt feelings, and a lack of emotional connection. Practical Tip: If you’re busy or distracted when your partner makes an emotional bid, acknowledge it even if you can’t give full attention right away. You could say, “I see that you need me right now. Let me just finish this, and I’ll be with you in a moment.” 231

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: This validates the bid, showing your partner that you are aware of their needs. d. Respond Positively and with Care A positive, caring response makes a significant difference. When your partner feels heard and valued, it strengthens the emotional bond between you. Whether the bid is for emotional support, a light-hearted comment, or simply seeking attention, the way you respond can deepen your connection. This kind of emotional engagement builds a sense of safety and reassurance in the relationship. Practical Tip: If your partner shares a small vulnerability, such as saying, "I'm feeling really tired today," offer a supportive response like, “I’m sorry to hear that. Is there anything I can do to help you feel better?” This simple yet thoughtful response shows empathy, validates their feelings, and opens the door for further emotional closeness. Example of a Positive Response to an Emotional Bid Let’s say your partner says, “I’ve had such a tough day. I’m really feeling drained.” A dismissive response might be: "You always feel tired. It’s no big deal." But a positive response might be: "I’m really sorry to hear that. Do you want to talk about it, or would it help if I made you a cup of tea?" In this example, the second response acknowledges your partner’s feelings and offers both empathy and support. By responding in this way, you show that their emotions are valid and that you care about their well-being. 232

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: The Power of Turning Toward Each Other Gottman’s research highlights the concept of turning toward your partner as opposed to turning away. When one partner makes an emotional bid, turning toward them means responding in a way that acknowledges their need for emotional connection. This could be through offering empathy, attention, or support. In contrast, turning away (ignoring or dismissing the bid) creates emotional distance and erodes intimacy over time. The act of turning toward each other reinforces the sense of partnership and helps build a strong emotional bond, making it easier to navigate challenges and conflicts in the future. Strengthening Emotional Connection Through Bids Emotional bids are small yet significant cues that invite emotional connection. By recognizing, responding positively, and turning toward your partner’s emotional needs, you build a foundation of trust, empathy, and mutual respect. Responding to emotional bids is an ongoing practice that, over time, deepens the emotional intimacy of your relationship and fosters a supportive, loving atmosphere. Remember: Emotional bids, though often subtle, provide a powerful opportunity to strengthen your connection. When you respond with care and attentiveness, you show your partner that you value them and their emotional well-being, which ultimately enhances the quality of your relationship. Marriage as a Team Project Dr. John Gottman’s view of marriage as a team project reshapes how couples approach their relationship. Rather than seeing marriage as a series of individual endeavors or as a competition, Gottman encourages partners to see it as a partnership, where the goal is mutual growth, shared success, 233

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: and a united vision for life. This approach highlights the importance of working together, making decisions as a team, and fostering respect and collaboration in every area of the relationship. Why Marriage is a Team Project In any successful team, every member contributes toward a common goal. Likewise, in marriage, both partners must contribute to creating a healthy, fulfilling relationship. By embracing the concept of teamwork, couples move beyond the idea of “I” and instead focus on “we.” This shared perspective allows both partners to support each other’s growth and share the burdens of life together. Key Elements of a Team Marriage a. Open and Regular Communication A team thrives when all members are on the same page. In marriage, this means communicating openly and regularly about your needs, desires, dreams, and challenges. Each partner should feel comfortable sharing their thoughts and feelings, knowing that they will be heard and respected. This dialogue fosters understanding and ensures that both partners are actively working toward shared goals. Practical Tip: Consider having regular “relationship check-ins” where you both sit down and discuss how you’re feeling, any challenges you’re facing, and what you both need from each other moving forward. This can be a monthly or weekly ritual to stay aligned and avoid misunderstandings. b. Shared Responsibilities Just as in a work project, a successful marriage requires that both partners share responsibilities. This can apply to household tasks, emotional support, finances, child-rearing, and more. Working as a 234

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: team means recognizing each other’s strengths and dividing responsibilities in a way that is fair and balanced. It’s not about who does more, but rather about supporting each other to ensure that no one feels overwhelmed or unsupported. Practical Tip: Identify areas where you can divide tasks based on your individual strengths and preferences. For example, one partner may enjoy cooking, while the other might prefer organizing finances. Sharing responsibilities reduces stress and allows you to focus on building a life together. c. Mutual Respect and Support A strong team is built on trust, respect, and mutual support. In marriage, respect means valuing each other’s opinions, listening attentively, and validating each other’s feelings and perspectives. This support is not only emotional but also practical, as both partners should be there for one another, whether it’s during times of stress, celebration, or growth. Practical Tip: Always express appreciation for the things your partner does, whether big or small. Acknowledge their efforts and make sure they feel valued. Simple gestures of kindness and gratitude can strengthen the foundation of respect. d. Embrace Challenges as Opportunities for Growth Every team faces obstacles, and marriage is no different. What matters is how you face and overcome these challenges together. Instead of viewing challenges as setbacks or reasons for blame, see them as opportunities to grow stronger as a couple. Working through difficulties as a team enhances trust and builds resilience. When you solve problems together, you become more united in your approach to future challenges. 235

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: Practical Tip: Approach disagreements or challenges as opportunities to collaborate rather than fight. Instead of focusing on what’s wrong, discuss how you can work together to find solutions. Whether it's a financial setback or a disagreement on parenting, collaborate to create a plan that works for both of you. e. Making Decisions Together In a team, decisions are made collectively. In marriage, this means discussing significant decisions together, whether they’re about finances, career choices, where to live, or how to raise children. Each partner’s opinion and voice should be heard and considered when making decisions, so both feel equally invested in the outcome. Practical Tip: Practice decision-making by discussing the potential impact of major decisions before making them. This way, both partners can share their thoughts, and the decision-making process feels collaborative rather than unilateral. Practical Tips for Strengthening Your Team Dynamic ● Schedule Regular “Relationship Check-Ins”: These discussions can be short but are essential in ensuring you’re both on the same page. During these check-ins, you can share your emotional needs, reflect on your relationship’s progress, and discuss any upcoming challenges or goals. ● Celebrate Successes Together: Just as teams celebrate victories, couples should take time to acknowledge their shared accomplishments, whether it’s a milestone in the relationship, personal growth, or achieving a common goal. Celebrating success 236

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: fosters positive reinforcement and keeps both partners motivated to continue working together. ● Support Each Other’s Dreams: A true team supports each member’s individual dreams and aspirations. If one partner is pursuing a personal or professional goal, the other should offer encouragement and support. By helping each other achieve personal goals, you grow closer as a couple and help one another thrive. Marriage as a Shared Journey When viewed as a team project, marriage becomes a journey of collaboration, growth, and mutual support. It’s not about one partner being in charge or controlling the outcome; rather, it’s about working together as equals to create a life full of meaning, joy, and fulfillment. Marriage is not a one-person endeavor but a partnership that requires both individuals to invest their time, energy, and care into creating something beautiful together. By embracing communication, shared responsibilities, mutual respect, and a problem-solving mindset, couples can build a solid foundation of trust and intimacy. Approach marriage as a team project, and you’ll find that the challenges you face become opportunities for growth, while your shared successes strengthen the bond you have. Remember, the effort you put into your relationship will shape the future of your marriage. When both partners are equally committed to working together, the journey becomes far more rewarding. Building the Sound Relationship House The Sound Relationship House is a powerful metaphor that Dr. John Gottman uses to describe the emotional framework needed for a successful, lasting marriage. Just like any physical house, the emotional 237

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: house of a marriage requires a solid foundation, carefully constructed walls, and a protective roof. When each element is carefully tended to, the marriage can stand strong and endure life’s inevitable challenges. However, just as a house requires regular maintenance and attention, so too does a marriage require consistent effort, communication, and nurturing to thrive. By integrating the principles that Gottman outlines, such as trust, admiration, emotional bids, love maps, and commitment, couples can create the emotional infrastructure necessary for long-term success. These principles form the foundation of your relationship and act as building blocks for intimacy, teamwork, and shared meaning. The Key Pillars of a Strong Marriage 1. Trust : Trust is the cornerstone of any lasting relationship. It ensures that partners feel safe, valued, and supported. Without trust, the relationship lacks security, and the emotional walls cannot be built solidly. 2. Admiration and Respect : Admiration fosters an environment where each partner is seen for who they truly are, not just for their actions. Respect is the glue that binds the emotional connection, and admiration strengthens the bond. 3. Commitment : A commitment to the relationship ensures that both partners are willing to put in the work, even during challenging times. A shared commitment to making things work is what helps marriages endure and grow stronger. 4. Love Maps : Understanding your partner on a deep level, knowing their hopes, dreams, fears, and preferences, is the emotional 238

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: blueprint for a relationship. Love maps create emotional intimacy and understanding, helping partners stay connected over time. 5. Emotional Bids : These small, everyday efforts to connect emotionally are the “bricks” that build the emotional infrastructure of your relationship. Responding to these bids with care and attention strengthens your emotional connection. Creating a Marriage That Endures The strength of your relationship isn’t based on perfection, it’s based on the continuous, thoughtful actions that reflect love, respect, and commitment. Dr. Gottman’s research shows that healthy marriages are those where partners are consistently mindful of each other’s needs and show a willingness to work together to solve problems. This consistency, more than any single act of grand romantic gesture, builds a solid emotional foundation. Incorporating these principles into your relationship doesn’t mean that conflict or challenges will disappear. In fact, disagreements will inevitably arise, but the tools you’ve built together, through communication, shared values, mutual respect, and emotional connection, will allow you to navigate difficulties in a way that strengthens, rather than weakens, your bond. Building a lasting, fulfilling marriage is a journey, not a destination. It requires constant effort, reflection, and a willingness to grow as individuals and as a couple. As you continue to nurture your relationship, keep in mind that every effort, no matter how small, contributes to a foundation that will hold your marriage steady through life’s ups and downs. Remember that a healthy marriage isn’t about being perfect, but about being committed to the journey of mutual growth, understanding, and love. 239

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: When both partners are devoted to creating a relationship that is based on trust, respect, admiration, and emotional intimacy, the results are profound. In conclusion, the Sound Relationship House offers a blueprint for creating a strong and enduring partnership. By focusing on the foundational elements that Gottman emphasizes, couples can transform their relationship into one that is deeply fulfilling, emotionally connected, and resilient to life’s challenges. Keep in mind that every step you take toward understanding, supporting, and choosing each other builds a lasting, joyful marriage.
