As you continue to strengthen your relationship, you are not only creating a home for yourselves but also crafting a legacy of love, commitment, and shared meaning for the future. 240

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: PART FIVE: TOOLKIT FOR THE JOURNEY AHEAD 241

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: Chapter 15 Premarital Checklists and Deep-Dive Questions Before you commit to a lifetime of love, it's crucial to have open and honest conversations with your partner. Premarital discussions serve as a way to explore expectations, understand each other’s values, and ensure compatibility. It’s about setting a strong foundation, not just for today, but for a lifetime of growth together. Here is a comprehensive premarital checklist and deep-dive questions that help you explore important aspects of marriage: 1. Decision-Making Frameworks: Understanding the Heart of Marital Choices Decision-making is a fundamental aspect of any marriage. It is not just about the what , the things you choose to do, but the how , how you arrive at those choices together. In marriage, every decision, whether big or small, influences the trajectory of your shared life. A healthy decision-making process can strengthen your relationship, while a dysfunctional one can create tension, misunderstandings, or resentment. It's therefore crucial to have conversations about how you make decisions and to establish a framework that promotes mutual respect, understanding, and growth. This is where decision-making frameworks come in. These are the strategies or models that guide how you, as a couple, approach decisions. Understanding your partner’s decision-making style and how that style complements (or challenges) your own is essential for marital harmony. To build a strong, collaborative marriage, both partners must have a clear sense of their roles in the decision-making process and a shared vision for how decisions will be made. 242

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: Key Areas for Discussion: a. How Do You Make Decisions? Understanding the individual ways in which both partners approach decisions is crucial. In some cultures and personal experiences, people may grow accustomed to making decisions quickly and decisively (often driven by intuition or past experiences), while others may prefer to take a more reflective, methodical approach (which might involve consulting others or considering long-term consequences). Both approaches are valid, but they require understanding and accommodation in a relationship. Key Questions: ● Are you more impulsive or reflective when making decisions? ○ According to Daniel Kahneman, author of Thinking, Fast and Slow , humans typically make decisions using two systems: System 1 (quick, intuitive, and automatic) and System 2 (slower, more deliberate, and logical). Recognizing which system you lean on can help explain differences in decision-making approaches. ● How do you feel about the decision-making process in our marriage? Do you prefer to think things through together, or do you feel that some decisions should be made by one person? By discussing this, you can create a safe space to share how each partner naturally approaches decisions and how you can harmonize those differences. b. Joint vs. Individual Decision-Making: Finding a Balance Not all decisions need to be made together. In fact, expecting every decision to be a joint venture can lead to frustration or a sense of 243

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: indecision. Some choices are better suited to being made individually, especially when they concern personal preferences or areas of expertise. Key Questions: ● What kinds of decisions do you feel should be made jointly, and which ones should be made individually? ○ John Gottman, a leading relationship researcher, has argued that shared decision-making fosters a sense of partnership, while allowing space for individual autonomy can preserve personal identity. Deciding on roles, who handles what, within the marriage will help maintain mutual respect and understanding. ● For instance, would you prefer to make decisions about your career changes individually, or would you want to make these decisions together? What about day-to-day decisions like household chores or leisure activities? Having clarity on which decisions will be collaborative and which will be individual can reduce tension and help each partner feel empowered and respected. c. Financial Decisions: Transparency and Collaboration Financial management is a central area where decision-making can significantly impact the marriage. How a couple handles money often reflects their broader approach to teamwork, communication, and trust. Discussing your views on finances early on is crucial for avoiding misunderstandings and building financial stability together. Key Questions: ● How do you view financial decision-making in marriage? 244

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: ○ According to Dave Ramsey, a financial expert, couples should have joint financial goals, even if they have separate accounts. Financial harmony is about clarity in communication, openness about goals, and agreed-upon budgets. ● How will we manage our finances? Will we have joint or separate accounts? ○ Consideration: Some couples prefer to keep separate accounts for personal expenses, while others prefer fully joint accounts to simplify things. Whatever approach you choose, it’s vital to discuss it openly. ● How will we handle budgeting, saving, and big purchases (like buying a house or planning for children)? ○ Financial behavior research shows that couples with clear financial agreements and regular financial check-ins are more likely to avoid financial strain in marriage. The discussion should involve how each partner views saving, spending, and long-term financial goals. d. Sharing Major Life Decisions Big life decisions like buying a house, making career changes, or deciding when to have children are pivotal moments in any marriage. These decisions can be stressful, and the way they are approached, whether collaboratively or individually, can affect the emotional climate of the marriage. Shared decision-making in these areas requires a deep level of communication, trust, and mutual respect. 245

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: Key Questions: ● How do you feel about sharing responsibility for major life decisions, like purchasing a home or changing careers? ○ According to Fawn Weaver, author of The Marriage Minded Woman , shared decision-making around major milestones not only brings about unity but also provides a platform for the couple to align on long-term goals, such as family planning and career aspirations. ● How involved should both of us be when it comes to decisions such as when to have children, where to live, or significant career moves? ○ Consideration: The process of decision-making for life-altering decisions should involve regular conversations, understanding each partner’s hopes and fears, and being willing to compromise when necessary. Be sure to make decisions based on shared values, but also recognize that flexibility is key. Practical Tips for Strengthening Your Decision-Making Framework: - Create a Shared Vision: Discuss the long-term vision for your life together. This will provide a framework for making day-to-day decisions, knowing that they are working toward a larger purpose. - Set Boundaries and Roles: Discuss what areas of life you each want to take charge of, ensuring that each partner feels confident and competent in those areas. - Develop Communication Skills: Improve how you communicate around decision-making by using active 246

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: listening , empathy , and respect . Regularly check in with each other to ensure that you’re both on the same page. - Be Open to Flexibility: Understand that decisions won’t always go according to plan. Being adaptable and willing to revisit decisions when necessary is part of a healthy partnership. Decision-making is the bedrock of a successful partnership. By understanding how you and your partner approach decisions, you can avoid unnecessary conflicts, build deeper understanding, and create a collaborative, supportive environment. By embracing each other’s decision-making styles and setting a clear framework for your marriage, you will ensure that every choice, big or small, builds toward a shared, fulfilling life together. 2. Compatibility and Shared Values: Building a Unified Foundation While many relationships thrive on shared hobbies or common interests, true compatibility extends far beyond these surface-level connections. It is rooted in aligning on core values that reflect your worldview and life goals. When couples share fundamental values, they build a stronger foundation for their marriage, leading to greater understanding, fewer conflicts, and more fulfilling relationships. This goes beyond agreeing on preferences, it's about cultivating a shared vision for your future, navigating differences with respect, and understanding how each partner’s values shape their decisions and behaviors. This compatibility isn’t about finding someone identical to you. It’s about recognizing and respecting how your values, beliefs, and visions for life complement and challenge each other. 247

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: Key Areas for Discussion: a. Core Life Values: Aligning or Navigating Differences? To understand the depth of your compatibility, you need to start with what matters most to you, your core values . These are the beliefs and principles that guide your decisions and shape your identity. It is important to assess whether you and your partner share these values or how you can respect and navigate differences. Key Questions: ● What are your core values in life? Consider what is non-negotiable for you in your marriage, and what values define your choices and actions. These might include values like honesty, loyalty, faith, family, career, health, and community. ○ According to John W. T. Mason , author of A Theory of Marriage , shared values create a stable and meaningful foundation for long-term relationships. If partners share values like faith, work ethic, or family priorities, they can overcome surface-level disagreements more easily. ● How do you view religion, family, career, and community? Do these values align, or are there areas where they differ? How will we navigate those differences, especially in areas that are central to your identity (like religion or career)? ○ Consideration: For example, if one partner views family as a top priority while the other emphasizes career, a marriage can be strained unless both parties discuss and align their expectations on time, priorities, and balance. 248

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: b. The Language of Love: Understanding How You Give and Receive Affection Love is often expressed in many forms. One of the most powerful ways to understand each other’s needs and deepen emotional intimacy is by exploring how you both express and experience love. Dr. Gary Chapman’s concept of the Five Love Languages helps partners better understand how affection is communicated and received. Key Questions: ● What does love look like to you? This question delves into how each partner defines love and affection. Do you feel most loved when your partner speaks affirming words, shows acts of service, gives thoughtful gifts, spends quality time, or engages in physical touch? ○ In The 5 Love Languages , Chapman explains that recognizing your partner's love language (and your own) is one of the most effective ways to ensure emotional fulfillment in your marriage. ● How do you like to give and receive love? Whether it's through physical closeness, verbal affirmations, or acts of service, understanding these preferences will help avoid feelings of neglect or miscommunication. ○ Consideration: For example, if one partner values "quality time" but the other prefers "acts of service," each might misinterpret the other’s gestures as lack of love unless they understand these differences. c. Envisioning Family Life: Tradition, Legacy, and Boundaries 249

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: Family life is one of the most significant aspects of a marital relationship. Understanding how you both envision family life and which traditions or practices you'd like to bring into your marriage is essential. Whether it’s celebrating holidays or creating new traditions, the way you approach family can significantly impact your marriage dynamics. Key Questions: ● How do you envision your family life? Discuss your dreams for the family you want to build together, including how you want to organize your home, your parenting philosophy, and your approach to work-life balance. ○ E. Mavis Hetherington, a family psychologist, emphasizes that couples with shared family goals, such as raising children in a particular faith, creating a nurturing environment, or fostering specific traditions, tend to experience greater marital satisfaction. ● What traditions or practices from your own family would you want to bring into our relationship? Are there certain values, practices, or ways of doing things that you want to continue in your new life together? ○ Consideration: You may want to discuss if there are family traditions that one partner might find emotionally burdensome, or if there are customs that are important to you both that should be carried forward in the marriage. ● Are there any family traditions or practices that you would not want to bring into our relationship? It’s important to express concerns about traditions that may not align with your values, like certain cultural expectations or behaviors. 250

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: d. Physical Intimacy: Expectations, Boundaries, and Communication Physical intimacy is a vital component of many marriages, and discussing expectations in this area early on is essential. Understanding each other’s preferences, boundaries, and concerns around intimacy can strengthen the emotional and physical bond of the marriage. Key Questions: ● What are your expectations for physical intimacy in marriage? Discuss how often you’d like to be intimate, how you express affection physically, and any other aspects of physical connection that are important to you. ○ According to relationship researchers such as Dr. Laura Berman, couples who communicate openly about their sexual and physical intimacy needs are more likely to experience greater satisfaction and deeper emotional bonds. ● Are there boundaries, preferences, or concerns we need to discuss openly? Having a candid conversation about comfort levels, desires, and preferences around intimacy helps avoid misunderstandings and ensures that both partners feel safe and respected. ○ Consideration: Respecting boundaries and mutual preferences in intimacy sets a foundation for trust. Some couples find it helpful to create "rules of engagement" for intimacy, where both partners can express their feelings and preferences in a supportive environment. 251

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: Practical Tips for Strengthening Compatibility and Shared Values: - Create a Shared Vision: Regularly revisit your goals, values, and the life you want to build together. Ensure that both of you are aligned, not only on the major decisions but also on how you want your family to grow and what kind of legacy you want to leave. - Regular Communication on Love Languages: As life and circumstances evolve, so might your love language preferences. Check in periodically with your partner about how you both like to express love and affection, and adjust accordingly. - Respect Each Other’s Family Traditions: Each of you brings different family backgrounds to the marriage. Have ongoing conversations about which traditions matter most and create new ones together that blend both backgrounds. - Discuss Boundaries in Intimacy: Keep the lines of communication open when it comes to physical intimacy. Respect your partner’s feelings and encourage a healthy, ongoing dialogue about your needs and expectations. Compatibility in marriage is about understanding and aligning on the core values that matter most to both partners. It’s about fostering a shared vision for your future, being open to how your partner expresses love, respecting family traditions, and communicating openly about intimacy. By discussing these essential topics thoughtfully and with respect, you lay the groundwork for a marriage that is strong, fulfilling, and resilient to the challenges of life. By building a life together with shared values, you create a solid foundation on which your relationship can thrive. 252

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: 3. Compromise: Navigating Differences for a Stronger Marriage Compromise is essential for any marriage to thrive. It’s about finding middle ground, respecting each other’s needs, and working together to navigate challenges in a way that honors both partners’ values. However, healthy compromise is not simply about giving in or conceding at all costs. It's about understanding which areas are worth negotiating and which ones are non-negotiable, those core principles that are integral to your identity and sense of well-being. In marriage, compromise isn't just about managing differences; it’s about strengthening your connection by fostering mutual respect, open communication, and a willingness to adapt. Effective compromise helps couples build trust, enhance emotional intimacy, and cultivate a sense of partnership. Key Areas for Discussion: a. Identifying Areas of Compromise: What Are Your Non-Negotiables? Before you can compromise effectively, you need to understand what matters most to you, and where you’re willing to be flexible. There are certain areas in a relationship where one partner may be willing to make adjustments, but other areas may be non-negotiable . Identifying these can help you navigate potential conflicts without feeling resentful or misunderstood. Key Questions: ● Where are you willing to compromise, and where do you draw the line? Consider which aspects of life or marriage are flexible for 253

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: you and which are absolute necessities. For example, faith , children , and work-life balance are common non-negotiables for many people. It's essential to understand each other’s limits so that no one feels pressured into accepting something that undermines their values. ○ John Gottman , a renowned relationship expert, suggests that couples who communicate about what is truly important to them and are transparent about their non-negotiables experience less friction over time. Couples can find peace when both partners understand where flexibility is possible and where they must stand firm. ● Are there non-negotiable areas that we should be aware of? For example, do you both view faith and religion as integral to your lives, or are there values like honesty, trust, or financial stability that are paramount for both of you? b. Handling Conflict: Conflict Resolution Styles Every couple will encounter disagreements and challenges, but the way they handle conflict can make or break the relationship. Understanding how each partner approaches conflict is crucial for finding constructive solutions and maintaining emotional harmony. Key Questions: ● How do you handle conflict? Are you quick to resolve issues, or do you need time to reflect before engaging in a conversation? Understanding each other’s conflict resolution style will help you manage tension in a healthy way. ○ In their book The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work , John Gottman and Nan Silver emphasize that couples 254

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: who respect each other’s conflict resolution styles, whether that involves taking space or addressing issues immediately, are more likely to manage differences without escalating conflict. ● When we disagree, do you prefer to resolve things quickly, or do you need time to cool off before talking about it? Some partners may feel the need to immediately address issues, while others may need time to process their emotions. Both approaches are valid, but understanding this difference helps prevent misunderstandings and tension. ● What’s your preferred method of conflict resolution? Do you prefer to talk things out calmly, seek a third-party mediator, or take some space before resuming the conversation? Creating a framework for resolving conflicts will allow you to address issues constructively. c. The Art of Apologizing: Cultivating Genuine Apologies Apologizing is an essential skill for maintaining harmony in any relationship. However, not all apologies are equal. A sincere apology acknowledges hurt, takes responsibility, and seeks to repair the emotional bond. Understanding how each partner apologizes and what makes an apology feel genuine is key to resolving conflict and healing after misunderstandings. Key Questions: ● What is your approach to apologizing? How do you express regret when you’ve hurt someone? Is it through words, actions, or both? Some partners prefer direct verbal apologies, while others 255

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: may express regret through actions, such as acts of kindness or giving space. ○ According to Dr. Gary Chapman, author of The 5 Apology Languages , there are different ways people apologize. Some need a genuine acknowledgment of their feelings, while others require the promise of behavioral change. Understanding your partner’s apology language can transform how you handle conflict. ● What makes an apology feel genuine to you? Some individuals may want to hear specific words, such as “I’m sorry” or “I was wrong,” while others might seek an explanation or a commitment to change. Understanding these nuances will help ensure your apologies are effective. d. Time Apart vs. Immediate Resolution: Balancing Space and Connection During conflict, emotions can run high, and partners may need space to cool off or reflect. On the other hand, some couples prefer to resolve their disagreements immediately, as postponing the conversation might lead to unresolved tension. The key is finding the right balance between taking space and working through issues immediately. Key Questions: ● How do you feel about taking time apart when you argue? Is it necessary for you to have space to cool down, or do you prefer to stay engaged and resolve the conflict right away? It’s important to discuss whether taking a break during a disagreement is productive or if it leads to further disconnection. 256

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: ○ Dr. John Gottman suggests that when partners are emotionally flooded during a conflict, taking a break can actually be beneficial. However, it’s important to establish an understanding that taking time apart isn’t avoidance, it’s simply a tool to regain emotional composure. ● Do you prefer to work through things immediately? Some couples feel that leaving issues unresolved creates anxiety and tension, while others might feel overwhelmed by prolonged conflict. Understanding each other’s preference for timing during conflict can help avoid unnecessary stress or feelings of neglect. Practical Tips for Effective Compromise and Conflict Resolution: - Identify and Communicate Non-Negotiables Early On: Make sure you both understand what areas of your life and relationship are non-negotiable. Being upfront about these will help prevent frustration and resentment in the future. - Establish a Conflict Resolution Plan: Agree on a strategy for dealing with disagreements, whether that means taking a time-out or resolving things right away. Knowing how you handle conflict ahead of time can save energy and reduce the emotional toll of arguments. - Be Genuine in Your Apologies: Make sure your apologies are heartfelt and specific. If you’ve hurt your partner, take full responsibility for your actions, express your regret, and demonstrate your commitment to positive change. - Respect the Need for Space: If either partner needs space to process emotions, respect that request. Conversely, if you need to resolve the issue immediately, communicate this desire without 257

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: pressuring your partner. Compromise is a fundamental element of a successful marriage. By understanding what you’re willing to compromise on and where you draw the line, you set clear expectations and foster mutual respect. Effective conflict resolution, heartfelt apologies, and the ability to balance space with connection during disagreements are all crucial components of a healthy marriage. Compromise doesn’t mean sacrificing your values, it’s about understanding your partner’s needs, respecting differences, and finding solutions that work for both of you. Through open communication, trust, and flexibility, couples can navigate challenges and build a lasting, harmonious relationship. 4. Covenant: Understanding Marriage as a Sacred Commitment A covenant is more than just a contract; it is a deeply sacred and lifelong commitment. In marriage, this idea of a covenant goes beyond love and companionship, it is a pledge to stay committed to each other through every season of life, both the joyous and the challenging. Understanding the weight of this commitment can help guide couples through difficulties and foster a deeper, more resilient connection. When partners approach marriage as a covenant, they frame their relationship as a sacred bond that requires intentionality, responsibility, and dedication. A covenant in marriage implies a choice that transcends temporary feelings, external circumstances, or personal challenges. It acknowledges the reality that marriage is not always easy, but it is always worth fighting for. It is grounded in mutual respect, love, and a shared vision for life together. Key Areas for Discussion: 258

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: a. Defining the Covenant: What Does it Mean to You? In marriage, the concept of a covenant emphasizes a lifetime commitment , rooted in shared values and deep affection. Understanding how both partners view the idea of a covenant will shape how they approach their relationship and guide how they treat each other throughout their lives together. Key Questions: ● What does the word ‘covenant’ mean to you in the context of marriage? How does this word shape your understanding of commitment? For some, covenant may represent a sacred promise made before God, while for others, it might signify a solemn, binding agreement with deep personal meaning. Regardless of the specific interpretation, understanding what this commitment looks like will help guide the relationship through various seasons of life. ○ Dr. Gary Chapman, author of The 5 Love Languages , notes that a covenant perspective in marriage is one that views the relationship as something worth nurturing and protecting. It is not conditional on circumstances but rooted in the decision to remain loyal and committed. b. Purpose of Marriage: What’s the Vision? Every couple brings their own beliefs and expectations about the purpose of marriage into the relationship. Some may see it as a partnership based on companionship, others as a shared journey of growth, or as a foundation for building a family. Discussing what each partner views as the ultimate purpose of marriage can help align both partners’ expectations and guide their actions. 259

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: Key Questions: ● What do you believe is the purpose of marriage? Is it about companionship , love , mutual growth , family , or something else entirely? Understanding your partner’s vision for marriage will help you both pursue that vision together, aligning your actions, decisions, and long-term goals. ○ According to Tim Keller, in his book The Meaning of Marriage , marriage is not simply about finding happiness or fulfillment; it is about serving one another selflessly, fostering mutual growth, and reflecting the covenant love that God has for His people. He emphasizes that both partners should cultivate a sense of purpose beyond personal satisfaction, focusing instead on the relationship as a reflection of commitment and mutual sacrifice. c. Navigating Trials and Tough Seasons: Staying Committed In marriage, difficult seasons are inevitable. Whether it’s dealing with financial struggles, health issues, or emotional challenges, it’s important to have a shared understanding of how you will face these hardships together. A covenantal approach to marriage encourages resilience through tough times and reminds each partner of their commitment to each other, even when life feels difficult. Key Questions: ● How do you handle trials or tough seasons? Are you committed to staying together through the hard times, or do you believe there’s a point where separation is necessary? Having a conversation about what commitment looks like during challenging times will help 260

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: both partners remain grounded in their shared responsibility to each other. ○ John Gottman, in The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work , asserts that couples who view their marriage as a lifelong commitment are better equipped to weather the storms of life. They are not as quick to resort to separation when faced with difficulties. Instead, they focus on staying together and resolving conflicts by keeping the long-term perspective of their commitment in mind. d. The Role of Faith in Marriage: Spiritual Growth and Shared Values Faith can be a central pillar in many marriages, guiding the couple's relationship and offering a framework for love, grace, and forgiveness. Understanding the role of spirituality in marriage and how both partners can nurture their faith together is vital for fostering a deep, abiding connection. Key Questions: ● How do you feel about the role of faith in our marriage? Is spiritual growth and shared religious values important to you? If so, how would we nurture that together? For some couples, prayer, church attendance, and spiritual conversations are vital to maintaining emotional intimacy and a shared sense of purpose. ○ In his book Sacred Marriage , Gary Thomas highlights that marriage is a journey of spiritual growth and reflection of God's love for His people. Couples who make space for their faith in their relationship often develop deeper emotional intimacy, as their commitment is rooted in a 261

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: higher purpose beyond the immediate challenges or joys of the relationship. Practical Tips for Nurturing the Covenant: - Define Your Shared Vision: Clarify the purpose of your marriage and ensure that both partners are aligned on the fundamental reasons you are entering this commitment. This helps both of you stay motivated and focused on your relationship’s long-term success. - Commit to Each Other in the Tough Times: Recognize that difficulties are a part of life. Discuss your strategies for staying united through challenges and ensure that both partners understand the importance of weathering storms together. - Faith as a Foundation: Make faith an intentional part of your relationship. Regularly engage in spiritual practices like prayer, Bible study, or shared worship to nurture your spiritual connection and keep God at the center of your marriage. - Reaffirm Your Covenant Regularly: Just as vows are made on the wedding day, it’s important to keep reaffirming your commitment to each other. Revisit the covenant you’ve made, celebrate milestones, and reinforce your loyalty and love regularly. Marriage as a covenant is about more than just emotional connection or legal contracts; it’s a sacred promise that requires intentional effort, responsibility, and a shared commitment to each other. By understanding and embracing this commitment, couples can approach their relationship with a sense of responsibility, unity, and purpose. When both partners approach marriage as a covenant, they create a foundation of trust, mutual respect, and enduring love, qualities that can sustain them through any trial or challenge they may face. A covenant marriage is a journey of growth, 262

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: love, and resilience, one that is worth nurturing and cherishing for a lifetime. 5. Practical Areas of Discussion: Building a Life Together While big-picture conversations about commitment, faith, and values are crucial, it’s equally important for couples to address the day-to-day realities of living together. These practical areas of discussion ensure that both partners understand each other’s expectations and preferences when it comes to creating a shared life. By having honest, open conversations about how you envision your future together, you can avoid unnecessary conflicts and build a partnership that supports both individuals’ needs and goals. Here are key areas where practical discussions are essential, along with thoughtful questions to guide the conversation. Key Areas for Discussion: a. Children: Family Planning and Parenting Styles One of the most significant decisions in marriage is the choice to have children, how many to have, and how to raise them. Understanding your partner’s feelings about parenthood, and discussing your collective vision for raising children, can help prevent misunderstandings in the future. This conversation should also explore ideas on discipline, education, and the overall family dynamic. Key Questions: ● How do you feel about children? Do you want children, and if so, how many? What age gap would you prefer? It’s important to talk about your vision for family size, but also to be open about timing. 263

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: Some people envision having children soon after marriage, while others may want to wait. ● What are your views on parenting styles? How do you think we should handle discipline, education, and the overall well-being of our children? Will we adopt a more authoritative approach, or would we lean towards an authoritative or permissive style? How will we align on how we guide and support our children? ○ John Gottman , in The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work , emphasizes that couples who are aligned on parenting philosophies tend to have more harmonious relationships and better outcomes for their children. Clarity on parenting can reduce stress and create a stable, loving environment for kids. b. Careers and Work-Life Balance: Setting Boundaries and Expectations Work-life balance is a significant consideration in most marriages, especially as careers evolve over time. Partners should have an honest discussion about their career aspirations, the level of importance each person places on their career, and how both partners envision managing their work and family responsibilities. Key Questions: ● What is your view on careers and work-life balance? How important is your career to you? Are you seeking personal fulfillment, financial stability, or something else from your work? ● Do you envision one partner being a stay-at-home parent? Or do you both want to work full-time? How do you see us managing work-life harmony? This may involve practical conversations about 264

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: job flexibility, parenting, household management, and how each partner can contribute to the family dynamic while pursuing their career goals. ○ Sheryl Sandberg, in her book Lean In , discusses how career ambitions should be balanced with family expectations. She encourages couples to have candid discussions about both partners' goals to create a supportive environment where each person can pursue their ambitions without neglecting the marriage or children. c. Household Responsibilities: Sharing the Load Understanding how to share household responsibilities effectively can prevent frustration and foster teamwork in the home. It’s important to recognize that every partner has preferences, and it’s essential to discuss which household tasks each partner enjoys, dislikes, and is willing to handle. Establishing clear roles, expectations, and boundaries around chores can lead to smoother day-to-day interactions. Key Questions: ● What are your views on household responsibilities? How do you envision sharing household chores? Are there specific tasks you prefer or dislike doing? Are there certain household tasks that each of us should take ownership of? ● How will we divide responsibilities in our home? Will we set regular check-ins to ensure we’re both on the same page, or will we have an ongoing, flexible system for sharing chores? d. In-Laws and Extended Family: Setting Boundaries In-laws and extended family members can significantly impact a marriage, either positively or negatively. Understanding your partner’s expectations 265

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: for family involvement and setting boundaries with extended family is crucial for maintaining a balanced relationship. Discussing these dynamics early on will allow both partners to create a united front when it comes to family involvement. Key Questions: ● How do you feel about in-laws and extended family involvement? What level of involvement do you envision with our extended families? Will weDr. John Gottman in his research notes that shared responsibility, whether it be for chores, childcare, or finances, plays a vital role in reducing relationship tension. Couples who divide responsibilities fairly often report higher satisfaction and are better equipped to handle stress. have frequent visits, or do you prefer more boundaries in place? ● How will we handle family holidays, vacations, and special occasions? What role do you think our families should play in our daily lives, and how will we protect our time as a couple and family? ● How will we maintain healthy boundaries with our families? How do we navigate disagreements or issues that may arise with relatives without letting them affect our marriage? ○ Dr. Les Parrott, in Saving Your Marriage Before It Starts , advises that couples should establish boundaries with their families early in their marriage. Healthy boundaries help avoid conflicts and protect the marriage from external pressures. Setting those limits ensures that both partners feel supported, respected, and able to nurture their relationship. 266

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: Practical Tips for Managing Day-to-Day Life Together: - Establish Clear Family Roles: Understand the distribution of household tasks, parenting duties, and emotional labor. Consider each partner’s strengths and preferences to ensure an equitable, harmonious home life. - Create Shared Financial Goals: Plan your work-life balance and career paths in line with your financial aspirations. Be transparent about money management, whether you have joint or separate accounts, and how you will manage household expenses. - Maintain Healthy Family Boundaries: Set regular times to discuss family dynamics, boundaries, and expectations. Keep an open dialogue about the role of in-laws, ensuring that both partners feel respected and heard. - Flexibility in Career and Family: Be prepared to adapt to changing circumstances in both careers and family life. Whether one partner transitions to a stay-at-home role or both continue to work full-time, agree on the rhythm that will balance both professional and personal lives effectively. These practical discussions offer a valuable opportunity to evaluate how both partners envision their day-to-day life together. Aligning on fundamental aspects like family planning, career goals, household responsibilities, and extended family dynamics creates a strong foundation for living harmoniously. Open, honest conversations about these areas will help both partners navigate their relationship with clarity and intention, building a life that reflects both individual needs and shared goals. When both partners approach these practical aspects thoughtfully, they cultivate a life of mutual respect, love, and support. 267

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: 6. Growth and Change: Evolving Together in Marriage Marriage is not just about surviving day-to-day life together, but growing together as individuals and as a couple. Over the course of a marriage, both partners will experience personal growth, and the relationship itself will evolve. It’s crucial to foster an environment where both partners feel supported in their individual pursuits, while also working toward shared goals. Growth is a vital aspect of marriage, and navigating this process thoughtfully ensures the relationship remains fulfilling and resilient, no matter what life brings. Here are key areas to address when discussing growth and change in marriage, along with thoughtful questions to guide these important conversations. Key Areas for Discussion: a. Personal Growth: Supporting Each Other’s Development As people, we are always evolving. In a marriage, it’s essential for both partners to feel supported in their personal growth, whether it’s in their careers, hobbies, education, or emotional well-being. Understanding how each partner envisions their personal development helps set the foundation for mutual encouragement and a deep sense of fulfillment. Key Questions: ● How do you see yourself growing as a person over the next five to ten years? What are your personal goals or dreams, and how can I support you in achieving them? This could include everything from career aspirations to personal well-being, education, or hobbies. The goal is to understand how your partner envisions their 268

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: future and how you can be a part of their growth. ● What are your aspirations for self-improvement or personal fulfillment? Are there areas of your life that you would like to work on? Whether it’s emotional health, developing a new skill, or building better habits, discussing these goals can help you support each other’s growth. ○ Harville Hendrix in Getting the Love You Want discusses how important it is for couples to support each other’s growth. When partners encourage individual growth, they feel more fulfilled and connected, which ultimately strengthens the marriage. b. Navigating Change in the Relationship Marriage is an evolving journey, and as life circumstances change, whether due to career shifts, raising children, aging, or personal growth, so too will the relationship. It’s important to discuss how you both approach change , whether it’s changes in your goals, identity, or the relationship itself. By addressing these changes early on, you create a framework for navigating them together. Key Questions: ● How do you feel about change in the relationship? As we grow, things will inevitably shift in our marriage. How do we handle these changes, whether they are internal, such as personal growth, or external, such as new life circumstances (e.g., moving to a new city, having children, etc.)? 269

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: ● How do we handle shifts in our individual and collective goals? As we change over time, it’s important to have a system in place for recalibrating our shared goals. Are we open to adjusting our plans or aspirations as we grow individually and as a couple? ○ According to Dr. Sue Johnson, in Hold Me Tight , successful couples don’t resist change, they embrace it. She notes that couples who can adapt together, adjusting their relationship and goals as life evolves, are more likely to experience long-term satisfaction and connection. c. Envisioning the Future Together: Keeping Intimacy Alive As you look toward the future, it’s vital to discuss how you both envision your marriage evolving in the long term. This includes maintaining intimacy, managing long-term goals, and ensuring that you stay connected as you age together. A healthy relationship isn’t just about surviving life’s challenges, it’s about thriving and nurturing the emotional bond over time. Key Questions: ● What do you want for our marriage in the future? How do you envision our life together in 10, 20, or even 30 years? What kind of experiences do you hope we will share? ● How do you want to maintain the intimacy and connection we build today as we grow older together? What actions or rituals can we adopt now to ensure we continue to nurture our relationship in the years to come? Whether it’s through communication, physical affection, or shared adventures, discussing these ideas helps maintain a strong, intimate bond. ● How can we support each other’s evolving needs and desires over the years? Marriage isn’t static, and it’s important to be 270

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: flexible as the relationship grows. Discuss how you can ensure each partner feels valued, loved, and supported as needs change over time. ○ In The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work , Dr. John Gottman emphasizes the importance of fostering shared meaning and rituals of connection. Couples who continually invest in their emotional bond, creating new ways to stay connected and engaged, report a higher quality of marriage even as they age. Practical Tips for Growing Together: - Create Regular Check-Ins: Set aside time regularly (e.g., monthly or quarterly) to talk about your personal growth goals, relationship changes, and future aspirations. These check-ins will help you stay aligned and proactive about any shifts. - Celebrate Milestones: Whether it’s a career achievement, personal growth, or a shared goal accomplished together, celebrate these moments. Acknowledging both individual and collective milestones can help strengthen your bond. - Be Open to Change: Recognize that both you and your partner will change over time. Be open to discussing those changes and adjusting your expectations and goals as necessary. - Maintain Emotional and Physical Intimacy: As you evolve, it’s important to prioritize the intimacy in your relationship. Whether through regular date nights, maintaining physical affection, or finding new ways to connect emotionally, nurturing intimacy is key to maintaining a thriving marriage. 271

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: Growth and change are fundamental aspects of a successful marriage. It’s essential for both partners to feel supported in their individual pursuits while also nurturing the relationship’s collective development. By discussing your goals, aspirations, and the ways you can adapt together, you lay the groundwork for a marriage that thrives over time. A relationship that embraces growth, both as individuals and as a couple, will not only survive but will flourish, bringing deeper intimacy, understanding, and joy as you evolve together. The Power of Preparation Premarital conversations are far more than a checklist, they are an opportunity to build a solid foundation for a lifelong partnership. These discussions enable both partners to share their deepest desires, fears, values, and expectations, creating a space where each person feels heard, understood, and valued. In doing so, you establish a mutual respect that serves as the cornerstone of a healthy, thriving marriage. As you prepare for marriage, it’s important to approach it not just as an event, but as a covenant, a sacred commitment to grow and evolve together. Marriage is a partnership, one that thrives on communication, understanding, and the willingness to compromise while also honoring each other’s individual growth. It’s not about perfection; it’s about consistently choosing one another, even through life’s inevitable challenges. In addition, approaching marriage as a journey of shared growth allows both partners to support each other in their personal and collective development. Just as individuals evolve over time, so too should the relationship, with both partners fostering a continuous, mutual investment in each other’s well-being. 272

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: By investing in deep, honest, and sometimes difficult conversations before the wedding, you lay the groundwork for a marriage that isn’t just about surviving together, it’s about thriving together. It’s about creating a strong, resilient bond that can withstand life’s trials, while also celebrating its joys and triumphs. When you and your partner enter marriage with a clear understanding of one another’s needs and values, you create a relationship that is built to last, a relationship that is fulfilling, joyful, and deeply meaningful for years to come. 273

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: Chapter 15 Resources for Further Growth: Building a Healthy Community Around Your Relationship Creating and maintaining a thriving marriage doesn’t happen in isolation. Building a healthy community around your relationship is crucial for both emotional support and growth. Whether it’s through mentors, trusted friends, or faith-based communities, surrounding yourself with the right people can provide invaluable perspectives, guidance, and encouragement. Here are some practical ways to build and nurture that support network: 1. Marriage Counseling and Premarital Education ● Premarital Counseling : Seeking professional counseling before marriage is an excellent way to ensure that both partners are equipped with the tools to communicate effectively and navigate challenges. It provides a space for deep, focused conversations with a trained counselor who can offer guidance on resolving conflicts, understanding each other’s needs, and building a strong foundation. ● Marriage Enrichment Programs : Attending programs that are specifically designed to strengthen marriages can help couples refine their communication, deepen their connection, and gain insights on sustaining intimacy. Many churches, community centers, and organizations offer courses designed to enhance marital skills. ● Books & Resources : Consider reading books on marriage and relationships. Some highly recommended ones include: ○ The 5 Love Languages by Gary Chapman ○ The Meaning of Marriage by Timothy Keller 274

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: ○ Hold Me Tight by Dr. Sue Johnson ○ The Seven Principles for Making Marriage Work by Dr. John Gottman 2. Mentorship and Couples Groups ● Mentorship : Seek out couples who have been married for a long time and have demonstrated a strong, thriving relationship. These couples can provide wisdom, support, and a model of lasting love. A mentor couple can walk alongside you, offering advice and helping you navigate challenges with a seasoned perspective. ● Couples Groups : Join a small group or community that prioritizes relationship-building. This could be a faith-based marriage group, a friendship group with other couples, or even a professional network. Being part of a group that shares similar values can provide both emotional and practical support. 3. Spiritual and Faith-Based Support ● Faith Communities : A church, synagogue, mosque, or other religious community can be an essential resource for nurturing marriage. Many faith communities offer pre-marriage counseling, marriage enrichment seminars, and ongoing support for couples throughout their marriage. Partners who share a strong spiritual connection often find their relationship bolstered by a common faith. ● Spiritual Counselors : Pastors, priests, rabbis, and other spiritual leaders can offer insight based on religious teachings. They can guide couples in maintaining the spiritual health of their marriage and can often recommend relevant scripture or principles to strengthen relationships. 275

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: ● Faith-Based Books : Exploring books that address marriage from a faith perspective can provide both practical advice and spiritual guidance. Some books to consider include: ○ Sacred Marriage by Gary Thomas ○ The Meaning of Marriage by Timothy Keller ○ What Did You Expect? by Paul David Tripp 4. Friendships and Support Networks ● Trustworthy Friendships : Friendships that offer emotional support and wisdom can help couples navigate challenging times. Close friends who understand your relationship dynamics and respect your privacy can provide perspective and encouragement. ● Couple Friendships : Having friendships with other couples allows you to learn from each other, share experiences, and support one another. It’s a way of seeing the highs and lows of other relationships, which can help normalize struggles and offer helpful tips for growth. 5. Online Communities and Resources ● Online Forums and Groups : There are many online communities focused on marriage, offering both support and resources. Websites like Reddit have relationship advice forums, and platforms like Facebook and Instagram host groups that are designed for couples. ● Marriage Blogs & Podcasts : Many bloggers and podcasters offer practical advice for couples, covering everything from communication and conflict resolution to intimacy and parenting. Some popular podcasts include: 276

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: ○ The Marriage Podcast for Smart People ○ The Loving Well Podcast ○ Marriage365 Podcast 6. Self-Help Tools for Personal Growth ● Individual Therapy : Sometimes, personal growth and healing are necessary to build a healthy marriage. Individual therapy or counseling can help each partner address their own emotional health, past traumas, or unresolved issues, which ultimately supports a stronger relationship. ● Books on Personal Development : Books that focus on individual growth can also contribute to a healthier marriage. Some recommended titles: ○ The Gifts of Imperfection by Brené Brown ○ Daring Greatly by Brené Brown ○ The Seven Habits of Highly Effective People by Stephen R. Covey 7. Financial Resources ● Financial Counseling : One of the biggest stressors in marriage can be financial disagreements. Engaging in financial counseling or taking courses on personal finance for couples can help manage money and reduce stress about budgeting, saving, and investing. ● Books on Financial Management : ○ Smart Couples Finish Rich by David Bach 277

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: ○ The Total Money Makeover by Dave Ramsey Building a Strong Foundation: The Importance of Community Creating and nurturing a strong, supportive community around your relationship is one of the best ways to ensure its health and longevity. It’s not just about having resources or experts to turn to in times of crisis, it’s about surrounding yourself with people who encourage you to grow, challenge you to be better, and celebrate your successes together. By continuously learning, investing in your marriage, and seeking support from others, you build a relationship that can withstand life’s challenges and grow stronger with each passing year. Remember, you don’t have to walk this journey alone. Reach out to mentors, community groups, spiritual leaders, and trusted friends to create a support network that strengthens your bond, deepens your connection, and helps you thrive as a couple. The strength of your marriage is not just built on the two of you, it’s fortified by the communities that surround you. 278

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: About the Book Ready for Forever: Emotional and Mental Preparedness for a Thriving Marriage is more than just a marriage guide, it’s a roadmap for couples who are committed to building a strong, lasting, and flourishing relationship from the very beginning. Drawing from years of reflection, personal experience, and teaching, this book delves into the emotional and mental foundation essential for a successful marriage, beyond traditional counseling advice. Marriage is one of life’s most profound commitments, yet many couples overlook the emotional and mental preparation required to navigate it successfully. This book underscores the importance of being spiritually, emotionally, and mentally equipped before entering into a lifelong covenant. It encourages readers to reflect on their readiness, to align on key values, and to establish a resilient partnership that can withstand life’s inevitable challenges. Through practical guidance, reflective exercises, and real-life examples, Ready for Forever prompts meaningful conversations between couples and within individuals themselves. Topics include cultivating emotional intelligence, conflict resolution, building trust, embracing the dynamics of marriage, and understanding how to grow together as a couple over time. Written with clarity, compassion, and practical wisdom, this book invites couples to lay a solid foundation for their relationship long before the wedding day. Whether you're engaged, preparing for marriage, or in the early stages of married life, Ready for Forever offers the tools, insights, and encouragement needed to nurture a relationship grounded in love, respect, and unwavering commitment. 281

title: EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL PREPAREDNESS
text: This resource will empower you to embrace marriage with a strong emotional and mental foundation, ensuring that your commitment to each other stands the test of time, ready for forever. 282

title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: Taken from Jesus Through Middle Eastern Eyes by Kenneth E. Bailey. Copyright © 2008 by Kenneth E. Bailey. Published by InterVarsity Press, Downers Grove, IL. www.ivpress.com

title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: 1 The Story of Jesus’ Birth LUKE 2:1-20 THE TRADITIONAL EVENTS OF THE C HRISTMAS STORY are well- known to all Christians. The birth of Jesus includes three wise men bearing gifts, shepherds in the ﬁelds in mid-winter, a baby born in a stable and “no room in the inn.” These aspects of the account are ﬁrmly ﬁxed in the popular mind. The ques- tion becomes: Is there a critical distinction to be made between the text and the traditional understanding of it? Have the centuries added meanings to our under- standing of the text that are not there? 1 A diamond ring is admired and worn with pride, but with the passing of time, it needs to be taken to a jeweler to be cleaned to restore its original brilliance. The more the ring is worn, the greater the need for occasional cleaning. The more fa- miliar we are with a biblical story, the more difﬁcult it is to view it outside of the way it has always been understood. And the longer imprecision in the tradition re- mains unchallenged, the deeper it becomes embedded in Christian consciousness. The birth story of Jesus is such a story. The traditional understanding of the account in Luke 2:1-18 contains a num- ber of critical ﬂaws. These include: 1. Joseph was returning to the village of his origin. In the Middle East, historical memories are long, and the extended family, with its connection to its village of origin, is important. In such a world a man like Joseph could have appeared in Bethlehem, and told people, “I am Joseph, son of Heli, son of Matthat, the son of Levi” and most homes in town would be open to him. 2. Joseph was a “royal.” That is, he was from the family of King David. The fam- ily of David was so famous in Bethlehem that local folk apparently called the 1 For a technical discussion of this text see Kenneth E.

title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: 26 J ESUS T HROUGH M IDDLE E ASTERN E YES town the “City of David” (as often happens). The ofﬁcial name of the village was Bethlehem. Everyone knew that the Hebrew Scriptures referred to Jeru- salem as the “City of David.” Yet locally, many apparently called Bethlehem the “City of David” (Lk 2:4). Being of that famous family, Joseph would have been welcome anywhere in town. 3. In every culture a woman about to give birth is given special attention. Simple rural communities the world over always assist one of their own women in childbirth regardless of the circumstances. Are we to imagine that Bethlehem was an exception? Was there no sense of honor in Bethlehem? Surely the com- munity would have sensed its responsibility to help Joseph ﬁnd adequate shel- ter for Mary and provide the care she needed. To turn away a descendent of David in the “City of David” would be an unspeakable shame on the entire village. 4. Mary had relatives in a nearby village. A few months prior to the birth of Jesus, Mary had visited her cousin Elizabeth “in the hill country of Judea” and was welcomed by her. Bethlehem was located in the center of Judea. By the time, therefore, that Mary and Joseph arrived in Bethlehem they were but a short distance from the home of Zechariah and Elizabeth. If Joseph had failed to ﬁnd shelter in Bethlehem he would naturally have turned to Zechariah and Elizabeth. But did he have time for those few extra miles? 5. Joseph had time to make adequate arrangements. Luke 2:4 says that Joseph and Mary “went up from Galilee to Judea,” and verse 6 states, “while they were there, the days were accomplished that she should be delivered” ( KJV, italics added).

title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: a miles? 5. Joseph had time to make adequate arrangements. Luke 2:4 says that Joseph and Mary “went up from Galilee to Judea,” and verse 6 states, “while they were there, the days were accomplished that she should be delivered” ( KJV, italics added). 2 The average Christian thinks that Jesus was born the same night the holy family arrived—hence Joseph’s haste and willingness to accept any shel- ter, even the shelter of a stable. Traditional Christmas pageants reinforce this idea year after year. In the text, the time spent in Bethlehem before the birth is not speciﬁed. But it was surely long enough to ﬁnd adequate shelter or to turn to Mary’s family. This late-night-arrival-imminent-birth myth is so deeply engrained in the popular Christian mind that it is important to inquire into its origin. Where did this idea come from? 2 Some modern translations hide the fact that a number of days passed in Bethlehem before Jesus was born. The original text (along with the King James Version) is precise. JesusThruMidEastrn.book Page 26 Wednesday, April 29, 2015 2:19 PM

title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: The Story of Jesus’ Birth 27 A CHRISTIAN NOVEL The source of this misinterpretation stems from approximately two hundred years after the birth of Jesus, when an anonymous Christian wrote an expanded account of the birth of Jesus that has survived and is called The Protevangelium of James. 3 James had nothing to do with it. The author was not a Jew and did not understand Palestinian geography or Jewish tradition. 4 In that period many wrote books claiming famous people as the authors. Scholars date this particular “novel” to around the year A.D. 200, and it is full of imaginative details. Jerome, the famous Latin scholar, attacked it as did many of the popes. 5 It was composed in Greek but translated into Latin, Syriac, Arme- nian, Georgian, Ethiopic, Coptic and old Slavonic. The author had clearly read the Gospel stories, but he (or she) was unfamiliar with the geography of the Holy Land. In the novel, for example, the author describes the road between Jerusalem and Bethlehem as a desert. It is not a desert but rather rich farm land. 6 In the novel, as they approach Bethlehem, Mary says to Joseph, “Joseph, take me down from the ass, for the child within me presses me, to come forth.” 7 Responding to this request, Joseph leaves Mary in a cave and rushes off to Bethlehem to ﬁnd a midwife. After seeing fanciful visions on the way, Joseph returns with the midwife (the baby has already been born) to be faced with a dark cloud and then a bright light overshadowing the cave. A woman by the name of Salome appears out of no- where and meets the midwife who tells her that a virgin has given birth and is still a virgin. Salome expresses doubt at this marvel and her hand turns leprous as a re- sult. After an examination, Mary’s claim is vindicated.

title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: Salome appears out of no- where and meets the midwife who tells her that a virgin has given birth and is still a virgin. Salome expresses doubt at this marvel and her hand turns leprous as a re- sult. After an examination, Mary’s claim is vindicated. Then an angel suddenly “stands” before Salome and tells her to touch the child. She does so and the dis- eased hand is miraculously healed—and the novel spins on from there. Authors of popular novels usually have good imaginations. An important part of this novel’s story line is that Jesus was born even before his parents arrived in Bethlehem. This novel is the earliest known reference to the notion that Jesus was born the night Mary and Joseph arrived in or near Bethlehem. The average Christian, who has never heard of this book, is nonetheless unconsciously inﬂuenced by it. 8 The novel 3 Oscar Cullman, “Infancy Gospels,” in New Testament Apocrypha, ed. Wilhelm Schneemelcher (Phil- adelphia: Westminster Press, 1963), 1:370-88. 4 Ibid., p. 372. 5 Ibid., p. 373. 6 I lived on that road for ten years, and at that time it ran through ﬂourishing olive orchards. 7 The Protevangelium of James 17:3, in New Testament Apocrypha, ed. Wilhelm Schneemelcher (Phila- delphia: Westminster Press, 1963). 8 Curiously, Codex Bezae (5th-6th century A.D.) changes the text to read “as they arrived she brought forth . . .” This change in the Greek text afﬁrms the idea that Jesus was born just as they arrived. title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: 28 J ESUS T HROUGH M IDDLE E ASTERN E YES is a fanciful expansion of the Gospel account, not the Gospel story itself. To summarize the problems in the traditional interpretation of Luke 2:1-7, Jo- seph was returning to his home village where he could easily ﬁnd shelter. Because he was a descendent of King David nearly all doors in the village were open to him. Mary had relatives nearby and could have turned to them but did not. There was plenty of time to arrange suitable housing. How could a Jewish town fail to help a young Jewish mother about to give birth? In the light of these cultural and historical realities, how are we to understand the text? Two questions arise: Where was the manger, and What was the “inn”? In answer to both questions, it is evident that the story of the birth of Jesus (in Luke) is authentic to the geography and history of the Holy Land. The text records that Mary and Joseph “went up” from Nazareth to Bethlehem. Bethlehem is built on a ridge which is considerably higher than Nazareth. 9 Second, the title “City of David” was probably a local name to which Luke adds “which is called Bethlehem” for the beneﬁt of nonlocal readers. Third, the text informs the reader that Joseph was “of the house and lineage of David.” In the Middle East, “the house of so-and-so” means “the family of so-and-so.” Greek readers of this account could have visualized a building when they read “house of David.” Luke may have added the term lineage to be sure his readers understood him. He did not change the text, which was ap- parently already ﬁxed in the tradition when he received it (Lk 1:2). But he was free to add a few explanatory notes. Fourth, Luke mentions that the child was wrapped with swaddling cloths.

title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: ers understood him. He did not change the text, which was ap- parently already ﬁxed in the tradition when he received it (Lk 1:2). But he was free to add a few explanatory notes. Fourth, Luke mentions that the child was wrapped with swaddling cloths. This ancient custom is referred to in Ezekiel 16:4 and is still practiced among village people in Syria and Palestine. Finally, a Davidic Christology surfaces in the account. These ﬁve points emphasize that the story was composed by a messianic Jew at a very early stage in the life of the church. For the Western mind the word manger invokes the words stable or barn. But in traditional Middle Eastern villages this is not the case. In the parable of the rich fool (Lk 12:13-21) there is mention of “storehouses” but not barns. People of great wealth would naturally have had separate quarters for animals. 10 But simple village homes in Palestine often had but two rooms. One was exclusively for guests. That room could be attached to the end of the house or be a “prophet’s chamber” on the roof, as in the story of Elijah (1 Kings 17:19). The main room was a “family room” where the entire family cooked, ate, slept and lived. The end of the room next to the door, was either a few feet lower than the rest of the ﬂoor or blocked off with 9 Nazareth is 1,600 feet above sea level, while Bethlehem is built on a ridge and is 2,250 feet high. 10 Yizhar Hirschfeld with M. F. Vamosh, “A Country Gentleman’s Estate: Unearthing the Splendors of Ramat Hanadiv,” Biblical Archaeology Review 31, no 2 (2005): 18-31. title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: The Story of Jesus’ Birth 29 heavy timbers. Each night into that designated area, the family cow, donkey and a few sheep would be driven. And every morning those same animals were taken out and tied up in the courtyard of the house. The animal stall would then be cleaned for the day. Such simple homes can be traced from the time of David up to the middle of the twentieth century. I have seen them both in Upper Galilee and in Bethlehem. Figure 1.1 illustrates such a house from the side. The roof is ﬂat and can have a guest room built on it, or a guest room can be attached to the end of the house. The door on the lower level serves as an entrance for people and animals. The farmer wants the animals in the house each night be- cause they provide heat in winter and are safe from theft. The same house viewed from above is illustrated in ﬁgure 1.2. Figure 1.1. Typical village home in Palestine viewed from the side Figure 1.2. Typical village home in Palestine viewed from above JesusThruMidEastrn.book Page 29 Wednesday, April 29, 2015 2:19 PM

title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: 30 J ESUS T HROUGH M IDDLE E ASTERN E YES The elongated circles represent mangers dug out of the lower end of the living room. The “family living room” has a slight slope in the direction of the animal stall, which aids in sweeping and washing. Dirt and water naturally move downhill into the space for the animals and can be swept out the door. If the family cow is hungry during the night, she can stand up and eat from mangers cut out of the ﬂoor of the living room. Mangers for sheep can be of wood and placed on the ﬂoor of the lower level. This style of traditional home ﬁts naturally into the birth story of Jesus. But such homes are also implicit in Old Testament stories. In 1 Samuel 28, Saul was a guest in the house of the medium of Endor when the king refused to eat. The medium then took a fatted calf that was “in the house” (v. 24), killed it, and pre- pared a meal for the king and his servants. She did not fetch a calf from the ﬁeld or the barn, but from within the house. The story of Jephthah in Judges 11:29-40 assumes the same kind of one-room home. On his way to war, Jephthah makes a vow that if God will grant him victory on his return home he will sacriﬁce the ﬁrst thing that comes out of his house. Jephthah wins his battle but as he returns home, tragically, and to his horror, his daughter is the ﬁrst to step out of the house. Most likely he returned early in the morning and fully expected one of the animals to come bounding out of the room in which they had been cramped together all night. The text is not relating the story of a brutal butcher. The reader is obliged to assume that it never crossed his mind that a member of his family would step out ﬁrst. Only with this assumption does the story make any sense.

title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: amped together all night. The text is not relating the story of a brutal butcher. The reader is obliged to assume that it never crossed his mind that a member of his family would step out ﬁrst. Only with this assumption does the story make any sense. Had his home housed only human beings, he would never have made such a vow. If only people lived in the house, who was he planning to murder and why? The story is a tragedy because he expected an animal. These same simple homes also appear in the New Testament. In Matthew 5:14-15, Jesus says, “No one after lighting a lamp puts it under a bushel, but on a stand, and it gives light to all in the house.” Obviously, Jesus is assuming a typical village home with one room. If a single lamp sheds light on everybody in the house, that house can only have one room. Another example of the same assumption appears in Luke 13:10-17 where on the sabbath Jesus healed a woman who “was bent over and could not fully straighten herself.” Jesus called to her and said, “Woman, you are freed [lit. untied] from your inﬁrmity.” The head of the synagogue was angry because Jesus had “worked” on the sabbath. Jesus responded, “You hypocrites! Does not each of you on the sabbath untie his ox or his ass from the manger, and lead it away to water JesusThruMidEastrn.book Page 30 Wednesday, April 29, 2015 2:19 PM

title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: The Story of Jesus’ Birth 31 it?” (v. 15). His point being: Today, on the sabbath you untied an animal. I “untied” a woman. How can you blame me? The text reports that “all his adversaries were put to shame” (v. 17). Clearly, Jesus knew that every night his opponents had at least an ox or an ass in their houses . That morning everyone in the room had taken animals out of houses and tied them up outside. The ruler of the synagogue did not reply, “Oh, I never touch the animals on the sabbath.” It is unthinkable to leave animals in the house during the day, and there were no stables. One of the earliest and most care- fully translated Arabic versions of the New Testament was made, probably in Pal- estine, in the ninth century. Only eight copies have survived. This great version (translated from the Greek) records this verse as: “does not every one of you untie his ox or his donkey from the manger in the house and take it outside and water it?” 11 No Greek manuscript has the words “in the house” in this text. But this ninth-century Arabic-speaking Christian translator understood the text correctly. Doesn’t everybody have a manger in the house? In his world, simple Middle East- ern villagers always did! The one-room village home with mangers has been noted by modern scholars as well. William Thompson, an Arabic-speaking Presbyterian missionary scholar of the mid-nineteenth century observed village homes in Bethlehem and wrote, “It is my impression that the birth actually took place in an ordinary house of some common peasant, and that the baby was laid in one of the mangers, such as are still found in the dwellings of farmers in this region.” 12 The Anglican scholar E. F. F. Bishop, who lived in Jerusalem from 1922 to 1950, wrote: Perhaps . . .

title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: use of some common peasant, and that the baby was laid in one of the mangers, such as are still found in the dwellings of farmers in this region.” 12 The Anglican scholar E. F. F. Bishop, who lived in Jerusalem from 1922 to 1950, wrote: Perhaps . . . recourse was had to one of the Bethlehem houses with the lower section provided for the animals, with mangers “hollowed in stone,” the dais being reserved for the family. Such a manger being immovable ﬁlled with crushed straw, would do duty for a cradle. 13 For more than a hundred years scholars resident in the Middle East have un- derstood Luke 2:7 as referring to a family room with mangers cut into the ﬂoor at one end. If this interpretation is pursued, there remains the question of the iden- tity of “the inn.” What precisely was it that was full? If Joseph and Mary were taken into a private home and at birth Jesus was placed in a manger in that home, how is the word inn in Luke 2:7 to be under- 11 Vatican Arabic MSS 95, Folio 71, italics added. 12 William Thompson, The Land and the Book (New York: Harper & Brothers, 1871): 2:503. 13 E. F. F. Bishop, Jesus of Palestine (London: Lutterworth, 1955), p. 42. JesusThruMidEastrn.book Page 31 Wednesday, April 29, 2015 2:19 PM

title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: 32 J ESUS T HROUGH M IDDLE E ASTERN E YES stood? Most English translations state that after the child was born, he was laid in a manger “because there was no room for them in the inn.” This sounds as if they were rejected by the people of Bethlehem. Was that really the case? There is a trap in traditional language. “No room in the inn” has taken on the meaning of “the inn had a number of rooms and all were occupied.” The “no va- cancy sign” was already “switched on” when Joseph and Mary arrived in Bethle- hem. But the Greek word does not refer to “a room in an inn” but rather to “space” (topos) as in “There is no space on my desk for my new computer.” It is important to keep this correction in mind as we turn to the word we have been told was an “inn.” The Greek word in Luke 2:7 that is commonly translated “inn” is katalyma. This is not the ordinary word for a commercial inn. In the parable of the good Sa- maritan (Lk 10:25-37) the Samaritan takes the wounded man to an inn. The Greek word in that text is pandocheion. The ﬁrst part of this word means “all.” The second part, as a verb, means “to receive.” The pandocheion is the place that receives all, namely a commercial inn. This common Greek term for an inn was so widely known across the Middle East that over the centuries it was absorbed as a Greek loan word into Armenian, Coptic, Arabic and Turkish with the same meaning—a commercial inn. If Luke expected his readers to think Joseph was turned away from an “inn” he would have used the word pandocheion, which clearly meant a commercial inn. But in Luke 2:7 it is a katalyma that is crowded. What then does this word mean? Literally, a katalyma is simply “a place to stay” and can refer to many types of shelters.

title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: he would have used the word pandocheion, which clearly meant a commercial inn. But in Luke 2:7 it is a katalyma that is crowded. What then does this word mean? Literally, a katalyma is simply “a place to stay” and can refer to many types of shelters. The three that are options for this story are inn (the English translation tradition), house (the Arabic biblical tradition of more than one thousand years), and guest room (Luke’s choice). Indeed, Luke used this key term on one other oc- casion in his Gospel, where it is deﬁned in the text itself. In Luke 22 Jesus tells his disciples: Behold, when you have entered the city, a man carrying a jar of water will meet you; follow him into the house which he enters, and tell the householder, ‘The Teacher says to you, Where is the guest room [katalyma] where I am to eat the passover with my disciples?’ And he will show you a large upper room furnished; there make ready. (Lk 22:10-12) Here, the key word, katalyma, is deﬁned; it is “an upper room,” which is clearly a guest room in a private home. This precise meaning makes perfect sense when ap- plied to the birth story. In Luke 2:7 Luke tells his readers that Jesus was placed in a manger (in the family room) because in that home the guest room was already full.
