title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: The Story of Jesus’ Birth 33 If at the end of Luke’s Gospel, the word katalyma means a guest room attached to a private home (22:11), why would it not have the same meaning near the be- ginning of his Gospel? The family room, with an attached guest room, would have looked something like the diagram below: This option for katalyma was chosen by Alfred Plummer in his inﬂuential commentary published in the late nineteenth century. Plummer writes, “It is a lit- tle doubtful whether the familiar translation ‘in the inn’ is correct. . . . It is possible that Joseph had relied upon the hospitality of some friend in Bethlehem, whose ‘guest-chamber,’ however, was already full when he and Mary arrived.” 14 I. Howard Marshall makes the same observation but does not expand on its signiﬁcance. 15 Fitzmyer calls the katalyma a “lodge,” which for him is a “public caravansary or khan.” 16 I am convinced that Plummer was right. If so, why was this understanding not adopted by the church, either in the East or the West? In the West the church has not noticed the problems I have already listed. When the traditional understanding of the story, therefore, is “not broken,” it would seem that the best course to follow is “don’t ﬁx it.” But once the problems with the traditional view of the text are clariﬁed, they cry out for solutions. On the other side, in the East, the dominant Christian presence is the venerated Ortho- dox Church in its various branches. What of its traditions? 14 Alfred Plummer, Gospel According to S. title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: 34 J ESUS T HROUGH M IDDLE E ASTERN E YES Christianity in the Middle East has traditionally focused on the birth having taken place in a cave. Many simple homes in traditional villages in the Holy Land begin in caves and are then expanded. The tradition of the cave can be traced to Justin Martyr, writing in the middle of the second century. What I have already suggested is in harmony with this tradition. The Eastern tradition has always maintained that Mary was alone when the child was born. In worship even the al- tar is hidden from the eyes of the faithful, and the event of the elements becoming the body and blood of Jesus (in the Eucharist) takes place out of sight. How much more should the “Word that became ﬂesh” take place without witnesses? Father Matta al-Miskin, a twentieth-century Coptic Orthodox scholar and monk who wrote six weighty commentaries in Arabic on the four Gospels, reﬂects with won- der on Saint Mary alone in the cave. He writes: My heart goes out to this solitary mother. How did she endure labor pains alone? How did she receive her child with her own hands? How did she wrap him while her strength was totally exhausted? What did she have to eat or drink? O women of the world, witness this mother of the Savior. How much did she suffer and how much does she deserve honor, . . . along with our tenderness and love? 17 This genuine and touching piety is naturally not interested in considering birth in a private home with all the care and support that other women would have given. Therefore, among Christians, East and West, there have been understandable rea- sons why a new understanding of this text has been neglected.

title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: sted in considering birth in a private home with all the care and support that other women would have given. Therefore, among Christians, East and West, there have been understandable rea- sons why a new understanding of this text has been neglected. To summarize, a part of what Luke tells us about the birth of Jesus is that the holy family traveled to Bethlehem, where they were received into a private home. The child was born, wrapped and (literally) “put to bed” (anaklino4) in the living room in the manger that was either built into the ﬂoor or made of wood and moved into the family living space. Why weren’t they invited into the family guest room, the reader might naturally ask? The answer is that the guest room was al- ready occupied by other guests. The host family graciously accepted Mary and Jo- seph into the family room of their house. The family room would, naturally, be cleared of men for the birth of the child, and the village midwife and other women would have assisted at the birth. After the child was born and wrapped, Mary put her newborn to bed in a manger ﬁlled 17 Matta al-Miskin, al-Injil, bi-Hasab Bisharat al-Qiddis Luqa (Cairo: Dayr al-Qiddis Anba Maqar, 1998), p. 128 (my translation). JesusThruMidEastrn.book Page 34 Wednesday, April 29, 2015 2:19 PM

title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: The Story of Jesus’ Birth 35 with fresh straw and covered him with a blanket. When Jesus engaged in ministry as an adult “The common people heard him gladly” (Mk 12:37 KJV). That same acceptance was evident at his birth. What then of the shepherds? The story of the shepherds reinforces the picture I have presented. Shepherds in ﬁrst century Palestine were poor, and rabbinic traditions label them as un- clean. 18 This may seem peculiar because Psalm 23 opens with “The L ORD is my shepherd.” It is not clear how such a lofty metaphor evolved into an unclean pro- fession. The main point seems to be that ﬂocks ate private property. 19 Five lists of “proscribed trades” are recorded in rabbinic literature and shepherds appear in three out of the ﬁve. 20 These lists hail from post-New Testament times but could reﬂect developing ideas alive at the time of Jesus. In any case, they were lowly, un- educated types. In Luke 2:8-14 the ﬁrst people to hear the message of the birth of Jesus were a group of shepherds who were close to the bottom of the social scale in their society. The shepherds heard and were afraid. Initially, they were probably frightened by the sight of the angels, but later they were asked to visit the child! From their point of view, if the child was truly the Messiah, the parents would reject the shepherds if they tried to visit him! How could shepherds be convinced to expect a welcome? The angels anticipated this anxiety and told the shepherds they would ﬁnd the baby wrapped (which was what peasants, like shepherds, did with their newly born children). Furthermore, they were told that he was lying in a manger! That is, they would ﬁnd the Christ child in an ordinary peasant home such as theirs.

title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: would ﬁnd the baby wrapped (which was what peasants, like shepherds, did with their newly born children). Furthermore, they were told that he was lying in a manger! That is, they would ﬁnd the Christ child in an ordinary peasant home such as theirs. He was not in a governor’s mansion or a wealthy merchant’s guest room but in a simple two- room home like theirs. This was really good news. Perhaps they would not be told, “Unclean shepherds—be gone!” This was their sign, a sign for lowly shepherds. With this special sign of encouragement, the shepherds proceeded to Bethle- hem in spite of their “low degree” (Lk 1:52). On arrival they reported their story and everyone was amazed. Then they left “praising God for all that they had heard and seen.” The word all obviously included the quality of the hospitality that they witnessed on arrival. Clearly, they found the holy family in perfectly adequate ac- commodations, not in a dirty stable. If, on arrival, they had found a smelly stable, a frightened young mother and a desperate Joseph, they would have said, “This is outrageous! Come home with us! Our women will take care of you!” Within ﬁve 18 Joachim Jeremias, “Despised Trades and Jewish Slaves,” in Jerusalem in the Time of Jesus (Philadelphia: Fortress, 1969), pp. 303-4. 19 J. D. M. title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: 36 J ESUS T HROUGH M IDDLE E ASTERN E YES minutes the shepherds would have moved the little family to their own homes. The honor of the entire village would rest on their shoulders and they would have sensed their responsibility to do their duty. The fact that they walked out, without moving the young family, means that the shepherds felt they could not offer better hospitality than what had already been extended to them. Middle Eastern people have a tremendous capacity for showing honor to guests. This appears as early as the story of Abraham and his guests (Gen 18:1-8) and continues to the present. The shepherds left the holy family while praising God for the birth of the Messiah and for the quality of the hospitality in the home in which he was born. This is the capstone to the story of the shepherds. The child was born for the likes of the shepherds—the poor, the lowly, the rejected. He also came for the rich and the wise who later appear with gold, frankincense and myrrh. Matthew informs his readers that the wise men entered the house where they saw Mary and the child (Mt 2:1-12). The story in Matthew conﬁrms the sugges- tion that Luke’s account describes a birth in a private home. With this understanding in mind, all the cultural problems I have noted are solved. Joseph was not obliged to seek a commercial inn. He does not appear as an inept and inadequate husband who cannot arrange for Mary’s needs. Like- wise, Joseph did not anger his wife’s relatives by failing to turn to them in a cri- sis. The child was born in the normal surroundings of a peasant home sometime after they arrived in Bethlehem, and there was no heartless innkeeper with whom to deal.

title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: ise, Joseph did not anger his wife’s relatives by failing to turn to them in a cri- sis. The child was born in the normal surroundings of a peasant home sometime after they arrived in Bethlehem, and there was no heartless innkeeper with whom to deal. A member of the house of David was not humiliated by rejection as he returned to the village of his family’s origins. The people of Bethlehem offered the best they had and preserved their honor as a community. The shep- herds were not hardhearted oafs without the presence of mind to help a needy family of strangers. Our Christmas crèche sets remain as they are because “ox and ass before him bow, / for he is in the manger now.” But that manger was in a warm and friendly home, not in a cold and lonely stable. Looking at the story in this light strips away layers of interpretive mythology that have built up around it. Jesus was born in a simple two-room village home such as the Middle East has known for at least three thousand years. Yes, we must rewrite our Christmas plays, but in rewriting them, the story is enriched, not cheapened. 21 21 Cf. Kenneth E. Bailey, Open Hearts in Bethlehem (Louisville: Westminster/John Knox, 2005). This is a Christmas musical constructed around the ideas presented here. JesusThruMidEastrn.book Page 36 Wednesday, April 29, 2015 2:19 PM

title: JESUS THROUGH MIDDLE EASTERN EYE
text: The Story of Jesus’ Birth 37 SUMMARY : THE STORY OF JESUS’ BIRTH 1. Jesus’ incarnation was complete. At his birth the holy family was welcomed into a peasant home. These people did their best and it was enough. At his birth the common people sheltered him. The wise men came to the house. When Jesus was an adult, the common people heard him gladly. 2. The shepherds were welcome at the manger. The unclean were judged to be clean. The outcasts became honored guests. The song of angels was sung to the simplest of all. I know that in an increasingly secular world “Merry Christmas” competes with “Happy Holidays.” I long to turn the traditional “Merry Christmas” the other di- rection and introduce a new greeting for Christmas morning. Greeting: The Savior is born. Response: He is born in a manger. O that we might greet each other in this manner. JesusThruMidEastrn.book Page 37 Wednesday, April 29, 2015 2:19 PM

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: INTRODUCTION Welcome to the Foundational Believers’ Class. This program is not just a course, it is a journey. A journey that will lead you into a deeper relationship with Christ, a stronger foundation in your faith, and a life that reflects His character in all you do. The path you are about to embark on is one that seeks to establish you in the core truths of the Christian faith, nurturing spiritual maturity, character formation, and faithful service. This is not merely an opportunity to gain knowledge, but to build your life on the solid foundation of Christ’s Word and His purposes for your life. Over the course of this year, you will be guided through systematic biblical instruction that will strengthen your understanding of God’s Word, empower you to walk in victory over sin, and equip you to serve effectively in His Kingdom. You will learn to embrace your identity in Christ and understand your vital role in the body of Christ. This journey will also challenge you to grow in your spiritual disciplines, overcome the works of the flesh, and develop a lifestyle that reflects Christ’s love, humility, and power. At the heart of this program is the goal of shaping you into a true disciple of Jesus, a believer whose life radiates His love and truth. By the end of this course, we trust that you will be firmly rooted in sound doctrine, empowered by the Holy Spirit, and ready to serve the local church and beyond, living with joy, purpose, and conviction. As you begin this journey, know that it is a privilege to walk with you as you grow in faith, obedience, and service. You are laying the groundwork for a lifelong journey of discipleship, where you will continuously grow in Christ, live for His glory, and fulfill His calling on your life.

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum

You are laying the groundwork for a lifelong journey of discipleship, where you will continuously grow in Christ, live for His glory, and fulfill His calling on your life. May this class be a turning point in your spiritual walk, and may you leave this year stronger, more equipped, and more passionate about serving God and others. Dr. Daniel Folarin Founder/Senior Pastor Great Haven Ministries Inc. 3

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class - Week 1 TOPIC: THE GOSPEL AND SALVATION - WHAT IT MEANS TO BE SAVED INTRODUCTION Salvation is the foundation of the Christian faith. God’s plan is to rescue humanity from sin and restore fellowship with Him through the preaching of the gospel (Romans 10:13-17). As the Scriptures declare in Romans 1:16-17, the gospel is the power of God unto salvation for everyone who believes. In this lesson, we will explore the meaning of the gospel and salvation, how they are received, and their significance for the believer. 1. UNDERSTANDING THE GOSPEL The word gospel means "good news." The good news is that Jesus Christ came to save mankind from sin and reconcile us to God. The gospel is the central message of Christianity, yet many misunderstand it. The gospel is not just the beginning of the Christian life but the foundation of everything. Key Scriptures: ● John 3:16 - "For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life." ● Romans 5:8 - "But God commendeth his love toward us, in that, while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us." Key Points: 1. The gospel is centered on Jesus Christ - His birth, death, resurrection, and ascension. 2. Sin separated man from God, but Jesus’ sacrifice provided a way back. 10

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 3. Salvation is a gift from God, not earned by good works (Ephesians 2:8-9). The Finality of the Gospel of Christ ● The gospel of Christ defines humanity through its lens. ● The work of salvation is entirely the work of God. ● Faith in Christ is receiving from, not giving to Christ. ● The wisdom of the gospel does not rely on personalities but on Christ’s finished work. 2. THE GOSPEL: NOT RELIGION, NOT IRRELIGION The gospel stands apart from both legalism (religion) and relativism (irreligion). Key Differences: ● Religion (Legalism): "I obey; therefore, I am accepted." ● Irreligion (Secularism): "I do what I want; there is no ultimate truth." ● The Gospel: "I am accepted through Jesus Christ; therefore, I obey." Key Scripture: Ephesians 2:8-9 - "For by grace are ye saved through faith; and that not of yourselves: it is the gift of God: Not of works, lest any man should boast." The gospel is not about trying to earn God’s favor through good works, nor is it about rejecting moral responsibility. It is about grace, God’s unmerited favor through Christ. 3. WHAT IT MEANS TO BE SAVED To be saved means to be delivered from sin and its consequences, to be made new in Christ, and to have eternal life with God. 11

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Key Scriptures: ● Romans 10:9-10 - "That if thou shalt confess with thy mouth the Lord Jesus, and shalt believe in thine heart that God hath raised him from the dead, thou shalt be saved." ● 2 Corinthians 5:17 - "Therefore if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature: old things are passed away; behold, all things are become new." Key Points: 1. Salvation involves confessing Jesus as Lord and believing in His resurrection. 2. It is a spiritual rebirth (John 3:3-5). 3. It grants access to God’s kingdom and eternal life. 4. HOW TO RECEIVE SALVATION Receiving salvation is a simple but profound act of faith. Steps to Salvation: 1. Hear the Gospel - Faith comes by hearing (Romans 10:17). 2. Repent of Sin - Acknowledge sin and turn away from it (Acts 3:19). 3. Believe in Jesus - Trust that He is the Son of God who died and rose again. 4. Confess Jesus as Lord - Openly declare your faith in Him (Romans 10:9-10). 5. THE BENEFITS OF SALVATION ● Colossians 1:13 - "Who hath delivered us from the power of darkness, and hath translated us into the kingdom of his dear Son." ● John 10:10 - "I am come that they might have life, and that they might have it more abundantly." Salvation brings many blessings, including: 1. Eternal Life - Assurance of heaven with God (John 5:24). 12

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 2. Victory Over Sin - Freedom from the bondage of sin (Romans 6:14). 3. Peace with God - Restored relationship with the Father (Romans 5:1). 4. Indwelling of the Holy Spirit - Guidance and empowerment for righteous living (Acts 2:38). 5. Membership in God's Family - The believer becomes a child of God, and then it qualifies you for membership in the body of Christ (John 1:12, 1 Cor. 12:13). 6. LIFE AFTER SALVATION - THE BELIEVER’S WALK Salvation is not the end but the beginning of a new life in Christ. Key Responsibilities of a Saved Person: 1. Grow Spiritually - Study God’s Word and pray daily (2 Timothy 2:15). 2. Fellowship with Other Believers - Be part of a local church (Hebrews 10:25). 3. Live a Holy Life - Walk in righteousness and obedience (1 Peter 1:15-16). 4. Share the Gospel - Tell others about Jesus (Mark 16:15). CONCLUSION Salvation is the greatest gift from God to humanity. It is by grace through faith in Jesus Christ. If you have not yet received this gift, today is the day of salvation (2 Corinthians 6:2). Those who are saved must keep growing in the knowledge of God and walking in the light of His Word. ASSIGNMENT 1. Memorize Romans 10:9-10 and John 3:16 . 2. Write a short testimony of how you received salvation. 3. Share the gospel with at least one person this week. 13

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class - Week 2 TOPIC: The Gospel is Not Just the ABCs But the A to Z Key Scripture: Colossians 2:6-7 "As ye have therefore received Christ Jesus the Lord, so walk ye in him." Introduction: Many people think that the gospel is just the entry point into the Christian life, but as Timothy Keller emphasized, the gospel is not just the beginning; it is the foundation of the entire Christian life. It shapes everything about us, from how we see ourselves to how we interact with the world. In this lesson, we will explore how the gospel impacts every aspect of the believer's life, and how it shapes our salvation, sanctification, mission, and relationships. 1. The Gospel Shapes Everything The gospel is the power of God unto salvation (Romans 1:16). When we believe the gospel, we receive salvation by faith, not by our works. It’s the beginning of a new life in Christ. Romans 10:9-10 - "That if thou shalt confess with thy mouth the Lord Jesus, and shalt believe in thine heart that God hath raised him from the dead, thou shalt be saved." Salvation is God’s gift , not an exchange. The unsaved person has nothing to give; they are dead in sin. But salvation is about receiving God's life through Christ’s resurrection. Sanctification: The Gospel Changes Us Sanctification is the ongoing transformation into the image of Christ. The gospel is not just a past event; it is the present power that shapes our character. 2 Corinthians 3:18 - "But we all, with open face beholding as in a 14

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: glass the glory of the Lord, are changed into the same image from glory to glory, even as by the Spirit of the Lord." The gospel continually shapes us, transforming us into the likeness of Christ. The gospel is not just for the beginning of the Christian journey , but for the whole journey. Mission: The Gospel Compels Us The gospel compels us to share it. It is the good news that the world desperately needs. We are not saved to stay passive but to be active participants in God's mission . Matthew 28:19-20 - "Go ye therefore, and teach all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost..." The gospel gives us a mission to bring the lost to Christ, to make disciples, and to teach others about the truth of the resurrection. Social Transformation: The Gospel Renews Culture and Society The gospel is the power that can renew culture and transform society . It calls us to work for justice, mercy, and righteousness in our world. Luke 4:18-19 - "The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he hath anointed me to preach the gospel to the poor..." The gospel has social implications, it demands that we love our neighbors, care for the poor, and work for justice. It renews society , bringing God's kingdom to bear on every area of life. 2. The Gospel Changes Our Identity The gospel doesn’t just save us; it transforms our identity . Instead of defining ourselves by our achievements, our relationships, or our performance, we find our true identity in Christ. Gospel Identity vs. Other Identities ● Performance-Based Identity: "I am valuable because of what I do." 15

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● People-Pleasing Identity: "I am valuable because others approve of me." ● Gospel Identity: "I am valuable because God loves me in Christ." Galatians 2:20 - "I am crucified with Christ: nevertheless I live; yet not I, but Christ liveth in me." When we embrace gospel identity , we find our worth in what Christ has done for us, not in what we do for ourselves or what others think of us. 3. The Gospel Transforms Relationships The gospel is the power of reconciliation . It reconciles us to God, and it also reconciles us to one another. The gospel teaches us forgiveness, humility, and sacrificial love. How the Gospel Transforms Relationships: ● Forgiveness: Because we are forgiven, we forgive others (Ephesians 4:32). ● Humility: Because we are saved by grace, we don’t feel superior to others (Philippians 2:3-4). ● Love: Because Christ loved us first, we love others sacrificially (1 John 4:19). 2 Corinthians 5:18 - "And all things are of God, who hath reconciled us to himself by Jesus Christ, and hath given to us the ministry of reconciliation." The gospel reconciles us to God, and this reconciliation enables us to live in peace and unity with others. We live by grace , not by superiority or judgment. 4. The Gospel Calls Us to Serve and Renew the World The gospel is not just about personal salvation but about restoring all of creation . As believers, we are called to serve the world. Gospel-Centered Mission: ● Care for the poor and marginalized (James 1:27) 16

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Work for justice and righteousness (Micah 6:8) ● Share the gospel with the world (Matthew 5:14-16) Luke 4:18 - "The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he hath anointed me to preach the gospel to the poor..." Conclusion: Living in the Gospel The gospel is not just something we believe ; it is something we live . It shapes our identity , our relationships , and our mission in the world. Practical Application: 1. Preach the gospel to yourself daily : Remember that you are saved by grace, not works. 2. Let the gospel shape your identity : Rest in Christ’s love rather than worldly achievements. 3. Apply the gospel to your relationships : Live with humility, forgiveness, and love. 4. Share the gospel with others : Be bold in proclaiming the good news. 5. Live on mission : Seek justice, serve the needy, and be a light in your community. Romans 1:16 - "For I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ: for it is the power of God unto salvation to everyone that believeth." Questions: 1. How does the gospel shape your view of yourself? 2. In what ways has the gospel changed your relationships? 3. How can you better live out the gospel in your daily life? Final Thought: The gospel is not about what we do for God but what He has done for us in Jesus Christ. Rest in His grace, and let it transform every aspect of your life. 17

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 3 The Assurance of Salvation: Can a Believer Be Sure of Their Salvation? (1 John 5:11-13) Introduction: Have you ever doubted your salvation? The uncertainty about our standing with God can be a heavy burden to bear. Maybe you’ve felt that any unconfessed sin could cost you your eternity with God. Maybe you’ve believed that no matter what you do, if Jesus returns, you’re going to hell. This fear and uncertainty can rob you of peace and joy in your Christian walk. But today, we will explore what the Bible says about the assurance of salvation and how we can have confidence in our eternal security in Christ. 1. The Gift of Salvation Is Secure Salvation is not something we earn by our works, and it is not something we can lose through our failures. The Bible teaches that salvation is a gift from God, and He is the one who holds it. Jesus said in John 10:27-28: “My sheep listen to My voice, and I know them, and they follow Me. I give them eternal life, and they will never perish. No one will snatch them out of My hand.” This is a powerful promise! Jesus is saying that once we belong to Him, nothing and no one can take us away from Him. If it were up to us to keep our salvation, we would certainly fail, but it is God who holds us securely in His hand. 2. The Assurance of Eternal Life God’s Word assures us that those who have placed their faith in Jesus Christ are given eternal life. The Apostle John writes in 1 John 5:11-13: “And this is the record, that God hath given to us eternal life, and this life is in His Son. He that hath the Son hath life; and he that hath not the Son of God hath not life. These things have I written unto you that believe on the name of the Son of God; that ye may 18

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: know that ye have eternal life, and that ye may believe on the name of the Son of God.” Notice that John doesn’t just say we can “hope” for eternal life, but that we know we have eternal life if we have the Son of God. This is a strong assurance that salvation is not uncertain. It is a guarantee, not based on our performance, but on the finished work of Christ. 3. Our Salvation Is Sealed by the Holy Spirit The moment we believe in Jesus, we are sealed with the Holy Spirit as a guarantee of our inheritance. Ephesians 1:13 says: “In whom ye also trusted, after that ye heard the word of truth, the gospel of your salvation: in whom also after that ye believed, ye were sealed with that holy Spirit of promise.” The Holy Spirit is God’s seal upon our hearts, confirming that we are His and that our salvation is secure. This is God’s promise to us, and He is faithful to keep it. 4. Nothing Can Separate Us from God’s Love Romans 8:38-39 reminds us that nothing in all creation can separate us from the love of God that is in Christ Jesus: “For I am persuaded, that neither death, nor life, nor angels, nor principalities, nor powers, nor things present, nor things to come, nor height, nor depth, nor any other creature, shall be able to separate us from the love of God, which is in Christ Jesus our Lord.” This verse reinforces the idea that our salvation is secure. Once we are in Christ, nothing can take us away from His love. Not even our own failures or mistakes can separate us from Him. 19

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 5. The Role of Faith and Not Works It’s important to note that salvation is not about our works but about our faith in the finished work of Christ. In John 5:24, Jesus says: “Verily, verily, I say unto you, He that heareth My word, and believeth on Him that sent Me, hath everlasting life, and shall not come into condemnation; but is passed from death unto life.” Our salvation is based on believing in Jesus Christ and accepting His sacrifice for our sins. It is not about how good we are or how many good deeds we do, but about faith in what Jesus has already done for us. 6. Can a Believer Go on Sinning? The assurance of salvation doesn’t give us a license to sin. The Holy Spirit that dwells in us helps us to live righteous lives. While we are saved by grace through faith, we are also called to live according to God’s standards. The Bible teaches that those who are born of God do not continue in sin (1 John 3:9). This is not about being perfect but about growing in holiness through the power of the Holy Spirit. As believers, we should develop a righteousness consciousness that empowers us to live for God rather than a sin consciousness that keeps us trapped in sinful habits. Practical Application: ● Rest in the Assurance of Salvation : Know that your salvation is secure in Christ, and nothing can take you out of His hand. ● Live by Faith : Your salvation is not based on your performance but on your trust in Jesus and His finished work on the cross. ● Focus on Righteousness : Let the Holy Spirit empower you to live a life of holiness, not out of fear of losing your salvation but out of gratitude for what Christ has done for you. 20

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Conclusion: The assurance of salvation is not a matter of wishful thinking but a firm foundation based on God’s promises. We are saved by grace through faith, and God Himself secures our salvation. As believers, we can live confidently, knowing that we are safe in the hands of Jesus. Let this assurance lead you to a life of peace, joy, and purpose as you grow in your relationship with God and fulfill His mission on earth. Reflection Questions: 1. Do you have peace in knowing that your salvation is secure in Christ? 2. How can you remind yourself of this truth daily? 3. How does the assurance of salvation affect the way you live your life? 4. What steps can you take to focus more on righteousness and less on sin? Prayer: “Father, thank You for the assurance of my salvation through Jesus Christ. I rest in Your promises, knowing that nothing can separate me from Your love. Help me to live out my salvation with joy and purpose, empowered by the Holy Spirit. In Jesus' name, Amen.” 21

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class - Week 4 TOPIC: The Forgiveness of Sins Introduction One of the most important truths in the Christian faith is the forgiveness of sins. Many believers struggle with whether they need to repeatedly ask for forgiveness in every prayer or if their sins have already been forgiven in Christ. Understanding the finished work of Christ Jesus will help us walk in the confidence and boldness God intends for us. The Basis of Our Forgiveness: The Blood of Jesus The forgiveness of sins is not based on our efforts, emotions, or repeated confessions. It is based entirely on the sacrifice of Jesus Christ. Ephesians 1:7 - “In whom we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of sins, according to the riches of his grace.” Colossians 1:14 - “In whom we have redemption through his blood, even the forgiveness of sins.” Matthew 26:28 - Jesus said, “For this is my blood of the new testament, which is shed for many for the remission of sins.” These scriptures show that the forgiveness of sins is not a future event but a past and completed act through Christ’s shed blood. The believer is already forgiven. Forgiveness is a Completed Work The Bible teaches that forgiveness was accomplished once and for all by Christ’s remission of our sins. This means that upon salvation, all our sins, past, present, and future, are forgiven. Ephesians 4:32 - “And be ye kind one to another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, even as God for Christ’s sake hath forgiven you.” 22

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: The phrase “hath forgiven” indicates a completed action. A believer is not in a continuous cycle of forgiveness but has already received it in Christ. Hebrews 10:12-14 - “But this man, after he had offered one sacrifice for sins forever, sat down on the right hand of God… For by one offering he hath perfected forever them that are sanctified.” Jesus’s sacrifice was once for all time, meaning we do not need repeated forgiveness rituals. How Should a Believer Approach Sin? Although our sins are forgiven in Christ, believers are not called to live in sin. The Bible encourages a lifestyle of righteousness and repentance when we fall short. 1 John 1:9 - “If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins, and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness.” This verse does not suggest that we need to continually seek new forgiveness. Rather, it emphasizes having a repentant heart and acknowledging when we fall short. 1 John 2:1-2 - “My little children, these things write I unto you, that ye sin not. And if any man sin, we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous.” The goal is not to sin, but if we do, we already have an advocate in Jesus. Romans 6:1-2 - “What shall we say then? Shall we continue in sin, that grace may abound? God forbid. How shall we, that are dead to sin, live any longer therein?” Forgiveness is not a license to sin, but an empowerment to live righteously. Do We Need to Ask for Forgiveness Every Time We Pray? Many believers have been taught that they must ask for forgiveness before every prayer or else God will not hear them. However, this idea is inconsistent with the Gospel. 23

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Hebrews 4:16 - “Let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy, and find grace to help in time of need.” We are called to approach God with boldness, not guilt or fear of rejection. John 16:27 - “For the Father himself loveth you, because ye have loved me, and have believed that I came out from God.” God’s love for us is not dependent on constant pleas for forgiveness but on our faith in Christ. 1 John 3:20 - “For if our heart condemn us, God is greater than our heart, and knoweth all things.” Even when we feel unworthy, God’s assurance is greater than our feelings. Instead of repeatedly asking for forgiveness as though our sins are not yet forgiven, we should: 1. Acknowledge our sins immediately when we fall (repentance should be instant, not stored for prayer time). 2. Thank God for His forgiveness and embrace His grace. 3. Move forward in righteousness rather than wallowing in guilt. What About Answered Prayers? Some people believe that their prayers will not be answered if they have unconfessed sins. However, the Bible teaches that God answers prayers based on His grace, our faith, and His will, not our perfection. Matthew 7:11 - “If ye then, being evil, know how to give good gifts unto your children, how much more shall your Father which is in heaven give good things to them that ask him?” A father does not stop feeding his child because the child made a mistake. 24

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: John 14:13-14 - “And whatsoever ye shall ask in my name, that will I do, that the Father may be glorified in the Son. If ye shall ask anything in my name, I will do it.” Our prayers are answered because of Jesus’ name and His righteousness, not because we asked for forgiveness beforehand. Final Exhortation: Living in the Assurance of Forgiveness Since our sins are already forgiven in Christ: ● We do not need to repeatedly ask for forgiveness in fear. ● We should confess our sins and repent immediately when we fall. ● We should approach God with boldness, knowing we are forgiven. ● We must grow in righteousness and avoid habitual sin. Key Takeaways: - Forgiveness is a finished work of Christ. - Believers do not need to ask for forgiveness every time they pray. - Confessing sin is about repentance and acknowledgment, not securing fresh forgiveness. - We approach God with boldness, not fear. - God answers prayers based on His love, not our perfection. Reflection Questions: 1. Do you approach God with confidence or with fear of rejection? 2. How can understanding forgiveness change your prayer life? 3. What should you do when you sin? 4. How can you balance living in grace and pursuing righteousness? Closing Prayer : Heavenly Father, thank You for the complete forgiveness I have in Christ. I receive Your grace with boldness, knowing that my sins have been fully dealt with by Jesus’ sacrifice. Help me to live righteously, not out of fear but out of love for You. Thank You for hearing my prayers and drawing me closer to You. In Jesus’ name, Amen. 25

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 5 TOPIC: Baptism (Water & Holy Spirit): Its significance and practice (Matthew 28:19, Acts 2:38) Introduction Baptism is a significant TOPIC in Christian doctrine. Understanding its biblical context is essential for a proper grasp of its meaning and role in the New Testament. This lesson will explore the different aspects of baptism, particularly the baptism of John and the baptism of Jesus, distinguishing between water baptism and spiritual baptism. It will also establish that baptism does not always refer to water baptism and that spiritual baptism is the true baptism for believers. The Role of John the Baptis t The word “Baptist” in John the Baptist’s name identifies his ministry of baptizing. However, John was primarily a prophet. Matthew 21:26 - “But if we shall say, Of men; we fear the people; for all hold John as a prophet.” Luke 7:28 - “For I say unto you, Among those that are born of women there is not a greater prophet than John the Baptist.” The primary role of an Old Testament prophet was to point to Christ, and John did this through his baptism. John’s Baptism and Its Purpose The baptism of John had three key assignments: - Ceremonial - It demonstrated the repentance of those who believed his message. - Figurative - It was symbolic of the coming Messiah. - Prophetic - It was a sign to confirm Jesus as the Christ (Mark 1:7-8, John 1:31-33). John’s baptism was known as the baptism of repentance (Matthew 3:1, Mark 1:4, Acts 10:37, Acts 13:24). 26

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: The Baptism of Jesus Jesus’ baptism was/is different from John’s baptism. Matthew 3:11 - “I indeed baptize you with water unto repentance: but he that cometh after me is mightier than I, whose shoes I am not worthy to bear: he shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost, and with fire.” Jesus never baptized anyone in water (John 3:22, John 4:2). Baptism Does Not Always Mean Water Baptism The Greek word baptizo means “to immerse” or “to place into,” and its meaning depends on the context. Baptism in the Bible does not always refer to water baptism but often to spiritual realities: Romans 6:3 - “Know ye not, that so many of us as were baptized into Jesus Christ were baptized into his death?” Galatians 3:27 - “For as many of you as have been baptized into Christ have put on Christ.” 1 Corinthians 12:13 - “For by one Spirit are we all baptized into one body.” In these passages, baptism refers to being placed into Christ by faith, not into water. Matthew 28:19-20 Reexamined “Go ye therefore, and teach all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost.” However, “baptizing them” here does not necessarily mean water baptism. The word baptism (Greek: baptizo) means “to immerse” or “to place into.” In the early church, baptism was understood in multiple ways, including the baptism of the Holy Spirit (Acts 1:5, Acts 2:38). The early church did not view baptism as a strict water ritual but rather as an identification with Christ. For example: - The Apostle Paul placed greater emphasis on faith in Christ rather than on water baptism (1 Corinthians 1:14-17). 27

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: - The Quakers and early Anabaptists later rejected water baptism altogether, emphasizing the inward work of the Spirit. - In Acts 10:44-48, the Gentiles received the Holy Spirit before they were ever baptized in water, proving that water baptism was not a prerequisite for salvation. - Some of the earliest Christian writings (such as the Didache) mention baptism, but its focus was more on discipleship and teaching rather than a required water ritual. Thus, the command in Matthew 28:19-20 refers to immersing new disciples into the truth and authority of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, aligning them with Christ through faith. This is consistent with spiritual baptism, which places believers into Christ (Romans 6:3-4, Colossians 2:12). Water Baptism Is Not Part of the Gospel 1 Corinthians 1:17 - “For Christ sent me not to baptize, but to preach the gospel.” 1 Corinthians 15:3-4 defines the Gospel as Christ’s death, burial, and resurrection. The True Baptism: Baptism of the Holy Spirit Spiritual baptism places believers into Christ. 1 Corinthians 12:13 - “For by one Spirit are we all baptized into one body.” Romans 6:3 speaks of being baptized into Christ, referring to spiritual baptism. Galatians 3:27 emphasizes that being baptized into Christ is about faith, not water baptism. Paul emphasizes one baptism: Ephesians 4:5 - “One Lord, one faith, one baptism.” Acts 19:1-5 - Paul distinguishes between John’s baptism and the baptism into Christ. Problem Passages Explained 28

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Mark 16:15-16 - “He that believeth and is baptized shall be saved; but he that believeth not shall be damned.” While this verse mentions baptism, it is important to understand that Jesus’ own ministry never involved baptizing with water (John 4:2). Instead, John the Baptist clarified that Jesus would baptize with the Holy Spirit (Matthew 3:11). Thus, when Jesus speaks about baptism here, He refers to spiritual baptism, the baptism of the Holy Spirit, which is essential for salvation. Water baptism was a symbolic act of repentance, but spiritual baptism is what actually saves a person, as it unites them with Christ (1 Corinthians 12:13). The emphasis in Mark 16:16 is on belief in the gospel, not the external act of water baptism. So, Mark 16:15-16 points to the baptism of the Holy Spirit as the true means of salvation, with belief in Christ being the key to entering God’s kingdom. John 3:5 - “Except a man be born of water and of the Spirit, he cannot enter into the kingdom of God.” “Born of water” refers to natural birth by context here, as the next verse talks about being born of the flesh- natural birth- and being born of the Spirit (Spiritual birth; “born of the Spirit” refers to salvation. Acts 2:38 - “Repent, and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the remission of sins.” “For” in Greek can mean “because of” rather than “in order to obtain.” Peter himself clarifies in Acts 10:43-48 that salvation comes by faith, and baptism follows as a public testimony. Acts 22:16 - “Arise, and be baptized, and wash away thy sins, calling on the name of the Lord.” A proper translation would emphasize that sins are washed away by calling on the Lord, not by baptism.

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: y faith, and baptism follows as a public testimony. Acts 22:16 - “Arise, and be baptized, and wash away thy sins, calling on the name of the Lord.” A proper translation would emphasize that sins are washed away by calling on the Lord, not by baptism. Titus 3:5 - “Not by works of righteousness which we have done, but according to his mercy he saved us, by the washing of regeneration, and renewing of the Holy Ghost.” 29

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: The “washing of regeneration” refers to spiritual cleansing by the Holy Spirit at salvation, not water baptism. Conclusion In the New Covenant, water baptism is not required for salvation. The Bible teaches salvation by grace through faith alone (Ephesians 2:8-9), and the true baptism for believers is the baptism of the Holy Spirit, which unites them with Christ (1 Corinthians 12:13, Romans 6:3-4). While John's water baptism symbolized the coming of Christ, Jesus baptizes with the Holy Spirit (Matthew 3:11, Acts 1:5). The baptism of the Holy Spirit is the defining mark of a believer’s salvation and union with Christ (Romans 6:3-4, Colossians 2:12). Therefore, water baptism is not required in the New Covenant. 30

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: TERM 1: FOUNDATIONS OF THE FAITH (Weeks 1-15) Module 2: Christian Growth & Identity in Christ (Weeks 6-11) 31

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: WEEK 6 TOPIC: WHO WE ARE IN CHRIST - OUR NEW IDENTITY Scriptures: 2 Corinthians 5:17, Ephesians 2:10 Introduction Understanding our identity in Christ is crucial to living a victorious Christian life. As believers, our old identity, shaped by sin, was put to death at the cross. In Christ, we have received a new identity, one that is rooted in His righteousness, purpose, and divine calling. This lesson will explore what it means to have a new identity in Christ and how this identity shapes our lives and actions. We Are New Creations in Christ (2 Corinthians 5:17) The moment we accepted Jesus Christ as Lord and Savior, we became new creations. The old self , defined by sin and separation from God, has passed away. In its place, we have become something entirely new, with a new nature that is aligned with God’s will and purpose. 2 Corinthians 5:17 - “Therefore if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature: old things are passed away; behold, all things are become new.” Implication: - Our past mistakes, sins, and failures no longer define us. We are not bound by our old identity but are now free to live according to God’s purpose for our lives. - This new identity is not just a change in behavior, but a complete transformation of our spirit, mind, and emotions, rooted in our union with Christ. We Are Created for Good Works (Ephesians 2:10) As believers, we are not only made new, but we are also created in Christ Jesus for good works . Our new identity comes with a divine purpose, God has prepared us to live out His will on earth. Our works are not a means to earn salvation but a response to the grace we have received. 32

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Ephesians 2:10 - “For we are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus unto good works, which God hath before ordained that we should walk in them.” Implication: - Our identity in Christ includes being God’s workmanship , uniquely designed and equipped for specific purposes. We are meant to reflect God’s goodness and share His love with others through the good works He has prepared for us. - These good works are not just religious activities but include every aspect of our daily lives, relationships, work, and service to others, all can be expressions of our new identity. Our New Identity Transforms How We Live Having a new identity in Christ is not just a theological concept, it has practical implications for how we live our daily lives. It means: - Living with purpose : Knowing that God has a specific plan for our lives empowers us to live with intention and passion. - Living with power : Our new identity means that we are no longer slaves to sin but have the power to live victoriously over temptation and challenges. - Living with peace : Because we are accepted by God and made righteous in Christ, we can live in peace with Him and with others. Who We Are in Christ - A Summary - We are new creations in Christ, free from the power of sin and death (2 Corinthians 5:17). - We are God’s workmanship , created for good works that reflect His love and purpose (Ephesians 2:10). - Our new identity transforms how we live, giving us purpose, power, and peace. 33

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Conclusion In Christ, we are not the same as we once were. We have a new identity, one that is defined by who God says we are, not by our past or our failures. As we grow in our understanding of this identity, we begin to live out the purpose God has ordained for us. This week, let us meditate on our new identity in Christ and embrace the truth that we are no longer defined by our past, but by our relationship with Him. Reflection Questions: 1. How does understanding that you are a new creation in Christ change the way you view yourself? 2. What good works do you believe God has prepared for you to walk in? 3. In what areas of your life do you need to live out your new identity more fully? 34

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: WEEK 7 TOPIC: THE ROLE OF THE HOLY SPIRIT - THE HOLY SPIRIT IN THE BELIEVER’S LIFE Scriptures: John 14:26, Galatians 5:22-23 Introduction As believers, we are not left to live out our Christian life by our own strength. The Holy Spirit is sent to be our Helper, Guide, and Empowerer. He plays an essential role in every believer’s life, from our salvation experience to daily living. In this lesson, we will explore the vital role of the Holy Spirit in the believer's life and how He empowers us to live according to God's will. The Holy Spirit as Our Helper (John 14:26) Jesus, knowing that He would soon leave His disciples, promised to send the Holy Spirit to help them. The Holy Spirit is often referred to as the "Helper" (or "Comforter" in some translations), sent to guide, teach, and remind believers of God's truth. ● John 14:26 - “But the Comforter, which is the Holy Ghost, whom the Father will send in my name, he shall teach you all things, and bring all things to your remembrance, whatsoever I have said unto you.” Implication: ● The Holy Spirit teaches us God’s Word and helps us understand it. He reminds us of God’s promises and guidance when we need them. ● The Holy Spirit empowers us to live out God's commands and strengthens our faith through His presence. The Holy Spirit as the Source of Spiritual Growth (Galatians 5:22-23) The Holy Spirit not only helps us understand God’s Word, but He also produces godly character within us. As we yield to the Holy Spirit, He 35

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: cultivates the fruit of the Spirit in our lives, transforming our attitudes, actions, and desires to reflect Christ. ● Galatians 5:22-23 - “But the fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, longsuffering, gentleness, goodness, faith, meekness, temperance: against such there is no law.” Implication: ● The fruit of the Spirit is the evidence of the Holy Spirit’s work in a believer’s life. These traits, love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, and self-control, are not produced by human effort but by the Holy Spirit working in us. ● As we grow in our relationship with God and yield to the Holy Spirit, these traits become more evident in our lives, showing the world who we are in Christ. The Holy Spirit as Empowerment for Service The Holy Spirit empowers us for effective service in God's kingdom. He equips believers with spiritual gifts and abilities to build up the church and serve others. It is the Holy Spirit who enables believers to do the work of the ministry, whether it's evangelism, healing, teaching, or helping others. ● Acts 1:8 - “But ye shall receive power, after that the Holy Ghost is come upon you: and ye shall be witnesses unto me both in Jerusalem, and in all Judaea, and in Samaria, and unto the uttermost part of the earth.” Implication: ● The Holy Spirit equips believers with the power to witness to others about Christ. He empowers us to live boldly and to share the gospel with conviction. ● The Holy Spirit also gives us spiritual gifts for building up the body of Christ, enabling us to serve effectively within the church. The Holy Spirit's Role in Conviction and Guidance 36

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: One of the vital roles of the Holy Spirit is convicting the world of sin and guiding believers into all truth. He leads us in the way we should go, aligning our lives with God's will. ● John 16:8 - “And when he is come, he will reprove the world of sin, and of righteousness, and of judgment.” ● Romans 8:14 - “For as many as are led by the Spirit of God, they are the sons of God.” Implication: ● The Holy Spirit convicts us when we sin, pointing us to repentance and restoration. He guides us in our daily decisions, helping us align our lives with God's will and purpose. The Holy Spirit and Assurance of Salvation The Holy Spirit also bears witness with our spirit that we are children of God. He provides believers with the assurance that we belong to God and that our salvation is secure in Him. ● Romans 8:16 - “The Spirit itself beareth witness with our spirit, that we are the children of God.” Implication: ● The presence of the Holy Spirit in our lives is a confirmation of our new identity as God’s children. He reminds us of God's love and assures us of our salvation, even in times of doubt or struggle. Conclusion The Holy Spirit is an essential part of the believer’s life, guiding, empowering, and transforming us from the inside out. He teaches us God's Word, produces godly character, equips us for service, convicts us of sin, and assures us of our salvation. As we yield to the Holy Spirit and walk in His power, we can live out the life that God has called us to live and fulfill His purpose for our lives. 37

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Reflection Questions: 1. How has the Holy Spirit been a Helper in your life, especially in understanding God’s Word? 2. Which fruit of the Spirit do you feel the Holy Spirit is working on in your life right now? 3. How can you open yourself more to the Holy Spirit’s guidance in your daily decisions and service to God? 38

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: WEEK 8 TOPIC: THE WORD OF GOD - IMPORTANCE OF BIBLE STUDY Scriptures: 2 Timothy 3:16-17, Joshua 1:8 Introduction The Bible is not just a book; it is the living Word of God, inspired by the Holy Spirit and filled with divine wisdom for every believer. As Christians, Bible study is essential for spiritual growth, transformation, and guidance. In this lesson, we will explore why studying the Word of God is vital for our Christian walk and how it equips us to live according to God’s will. The Bible is God’s Inspired Word (2 Timothy 3:16-17) 2 Timothy 3:16-17 - “All scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness: that the man of God may be perfect, thoroughly furnished unto all good works.” Key Points: - Inspiration: The Bible is inspired by God. This means that the words in the Bible are not merely human ideas but are the direct communication from God Himself. - Profitability: The Word of God is profitable for teaching, correction, rebuke, and training in righteousness. It provides everything we need to live a godly life and grow in faith. - Equipping for Good Works: Through the Bible, believers are equipped and prepared to do good works that glorify God and reflect His love and righteousness. Implication: - Bible study is crucial because it helps us understand God’s will, corrects our mistakes, teaches us righteousness, and equips us to serve God and others effectively. Without consistent study of the Word, we lack the spiritual foundation necessary to live according to God’s will. 39

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: The Word of God as a Guide for Life (Joshua 1:8) Joshua 1:8 - “This book of the law shall not depart out of thy mouth; but thou shalt meditate therein day and night, that thou mayest observe to do according to all that is written therein: for then thou shalt make thy way prosperous, and then thou shalt have good success.” Key Points: - Meditation on God’s Word: God commanded Joshua to meditate on His Word day and night. Meditation means to think deeply, reflect, and apply the Word of God in every area of life. - Obedience to the Word: Studying the Bible is not enough; we are called to obey what it says. By meditating on the Word, we are better equipped to follow God’s instructions and live righteously. - Prosperity and Success: God promises that when we meditate on and obey His Word, it will lead to prosperity and success. This doesn’t necessarily mean financial gain, but it includes success in living a godly, fruitful life that fulfills God's purposes. Implication: - Bible study is not only about knowledge; it’s about transformation. When we study God’s Word and obey it, we will experience the fruit of a successful, blessed life, one that pleases God and impacts others. The Word of God as a Source of Strength and Comfort Psalm 119:11 - “Thy word have I hid in mine heart, that I might not sin against thee.” Psalm 119:105 - “Thy word is a lamp unto my feet, and a light unto my path.” 40

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Key Points: - The Word of God provides guidance, strength, and comfort in times of trial or difficulty. It serves as a lamp to light our path and protect us from falling into sin. - By hiding God’s Word in our hearts, we are able to overcome temptation and stay strong in our faith, even in the midst of challenges. Implication: - In moments of weakness or doubt, the Word of God is our source of strength and comfort. It helps us navigate the difficulties of life and ensures that we stay aligned with God’s will. The Word of God in the Life of Jesus Matthew 4:4 - “But he answered and said, It is written, Man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that proceedeth out of the mouth of God.” Key Points: - Jesus Himself relied on the Word of God when tempted by the devil in the wilderness. He responded to every temptation by quoting Scripture, showing us the power of God’s Word in overcoming temptation. - Jesus demonstrated that the Word of God is vital for spiritual survival. Just as our bodies need food, our souls need the Word of God. Implication: - If Jesus, the Son of God, relied on the Word for strength, guidance, and victory, how much more should we, as believers, immerse ourselves in Scripture? Bible study is a powerful tool for resisting temptation and living victoriously. 41

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Conclusion The Bible is the foundation of our faith, and studying it is crucial for our spiritual growth, understanding, and success in life. Through Bible study, we are equipped to live according to God’s will, grow in godliness, and be transformed into the image of Christ. As we meditate on the Word and apply it to our lives, we experience the prosperity and success that come from living in alignment with God's purpose. Therefore, consistent Bible study is not just a recommendation but a necessity for every believer. Reflection Questions: 1. How has the Word of God impacted your life and helped you grow spiritually? 2. In what ways can you make Bible study a daily habit and priority in your life? 3. How can you apply God’s Word to the challenges you are currently facing? 42

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: WEEK 9 TOPIC: Pauline Prayers Introduction The Apostle Paul, in his epistles, recorded several powerful prayers that reveal his deep concern for the spiritual growth of believers. These prayers are rich in scriptural truth, emphasizing the wisdom, revelation, strength, love, and the fullness of God. Studying and applying Pauline prayers can transform our prayer lives and strengthen our walk with God. Characteristics of Pauline Prayers 1. Christ-Centered Focus - Paul's prayers emphasize the supremacy and work of Christ in the believer’s life. 2. Spiritual Growth and Maturity - His prayers focus on wisdom, knowledge, and understanding of God's will. 3. Empowerment by the Holy Spirit - Paul intercedes for believers to be strengthened with divine power. 4. Thanksgiving and Praise - He often begins prayers by giving thanks for believers and acknowledging God's faithfulness. 5. Intercession for the Church - Paul consistently prays for the corporate body, not just individuals. Key Pauline Prayers 1. Prayer for Wisdom and Revelation (Ephesians 1:15-23) Main Themes: - Spirit of wisdom and revelation ● Enlightenment of the heart ● Knowledge of God’s calling and power Paul prays that believers may know: ● The hope of God's calling ● The riches of His glorious inheritance ● The immeasurable power available to them 43

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 2. Prayer for Strength and Love (Ephesians 3:14-21) Main Themes: ● Strengthened with might by the Spirit ● Christ dwelling in hearts through faith ● Rooted and grounded in love ● Experiencing the fullness of God Paul desires that believers be empowered to grasp the breadth, length, depth, and height of Christ’s love, leading to spiritual fullness. 3. Prayer for Discernment and Holiness (Philippians 1:9-11) Main Themes: ● Abounding love with knowledge and discernment ● Approving things that are excellent ● Being filled with the fruits of righteousness This prayer highlights the role of spiritual discernment in Christian maturity, ensuring believers live pure and blameless lives. 4. Prayer for Fruitfulness and Strength (Colossians 1:9-14) Main Themes: ● Filled with the knowledge of God's will ● Walking worthy of the Lord ● Strengthened with all might ● Giving thanks for redemption and inheritance Paul prays that believers walk in a manner pleasing to God , bearing fruit in every good work and increasing in knowledge. 5. Prayer for Encouragement and Establishment (2 Thessalonians 2:16-17, 3:1-5) Main Themes: ● Comfort and strength in every good work ● Establishment in faith ● Direction into the love of God and patience of Christ 44

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: This prayer emphasizes encouragement, stability, and steadfastness in the midst of trials. Application of Pauline Prayers 1. Pray These Prayers Over Yourself and Others - Personalize them by inserting names or situations. 2. Align Your Prayer Life with Scriptural Priorities - Focus on spiritual growth, wisdom, love, and empowerment , rather than just material needs. 3. Use Pauline Prayers for Church Intercession - These prayers are powerful tools for praying for our church community. 4. Meditate on the Theological Truths - Each prayer contains profound truths about God's will, Christ’s work, and the believer’s position in Him. 5. Develop a Habit of Thanksgiving - Paul’s prayers are often filled with gratitude, setting an example for us to follow. Conclusion Pauline prayers are a divine blueprint for effective prayer and spiritual growth. They shift the believer’s focus from temporary concerns to eternal realities. By studying, praying, and living out these prayers, we align ourselves with God’s purposes and experience the transformative power of the Holy Spirit in our daily lives. 45

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: WEEK 10 TOPIC: The Local Church & Fellowship - Why We Need the Church Key Scriptures: Hebrews 10:25, Acts 2:42-47 Introduction The Christian life is not meant to be lived in isolation. From the earliest days of the Church, believers have gathered together in fellowship, worship, and encouragement. The local church is God’s design for nurturing spiritual growth, fostering accountability, and fulfilling the Great Commission. In this lesson, we will explore why the local church is essential and how believers are called to be active participants in it. 1. The Local Church as God’s Design ● Jesus established the Church (Matthew 16:18) and called believers to function as one body (1 Corinthians 12:12-27). ● The Church is described as the body of Christ, meaning every member has a role to play in its health and function. ● Believers are called to live in unity and grow together in faith (Ephesians 4:11-16). 2. The Purpose of Fellowship in the Local Church Acts 2:42-47 provides a model of Christian fellowship. The early believers were devoted to: 1. Teaching and Doctrine - Learning the Word of God and growing in faith. 2. Fellowship - Building strong relationships with other believers. 3. Breaking of Bread - Sharing in the Lord’s Supper and communal meals. 4. Prayer - Strengthening their faith and dependence on God. Through these practices, they experienced spiritual growth, miracles, and unity. 46

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 3. Why We Need the Local Church a. Spiritual Growth and Maturity ● The local church provides teaching, correction, and encouragement (2 Timothy 3:16-17). ● Pastors and leaders help believers mature in their faith (Ephesians 4:11-12). b. Fellowship and Support ● Christianity is not an individual journey; believers need one another (Ecclesiastes 4:9-12). ● The local church provides a community where we bear each other's burdens (Galatians 6:2). c. Worship and Communion ● Corporate worship strengthens our faith (Psalm 22:3). ● Communion reminds us of Christ’s sacrifice and our unity as His body (1 Corinthians 11:23-26). d. Accountability and Discipleship ● The church helps believers stay accountable in their walk with God (James 5:16). ● Through discipleship, believers are trained and equipped for ministry (Matthew 28:19-20). e. Evangelism and Mission ● The Church is the vehicle for spreading the Gospel (Matthew 28:19-20). ● Together, believers can make a greater impact in reaching the lost. 4. The Danger of Neglecting Church Fellowship Hebrews 10:25 warns against forsaking the assembly of believers. When we isolate ourselves, we risk: ● Spiritual stagnation - Lack of encouragement and growth. ● Vulnerability to sin - No accountability. ● Lack of support - Facing life’s struggles alone. 47

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Application: How to Engage in the Local Church 1. Attend Regularly - Make church fellowship a priority in your life. 2. Serve Actively - Find a ministry to contribute to. 3. Build Relationships - Engage in small groups, prayer meetings, and church activities. 4. Support the Church - Give your time, resources, and prayers to the church’s mission. 5. Invite Others - Encourage others to be part of the fellowship. Conclusion The local church is an essential part of a believer’s life. It is a place of growth, fellowship, worship, and mission. Just as the early church thrived in unity, devotion, and love, every believer today is called to actively participate in and support the body of Christ. By committing to the local church, we align ourselves with God’s design and experience the fullness of Christian life. 48

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: WEEK 11 TOPIC: Handling Conflicts in the Church - Biblical Conflict Resolution & Extending Grace to Others Introduction Conflict is inevitable in any community, including the church. As believers, we are called to handle disputes in a manner that reflects the love, humility, and wisdom of Christ. The Bible provides clear principles for resolving conflicts and extending grace to others when they fall. This lesson will explore biblical conflict resolution, forgiveness, and maintaining unity within the body of Christ. Understanding Conflict in the Church Conflict can arise due to various reasons, such as differences in opinion, misunderstandings, pride, or even sin. The goal is not to avoid conflict at all costs but to handle it in a Christlike way. Common Causes of Conflict in the Church: 1. Miscommunication - Failure to communicate clearly can lead to misunderstandings. 2. Doctrinal Differences - Differing interpretations of Scripture may cause division. 3. Personality Clashes - Differences in background and temperament may create tension. 4. Offenses and Sins - When believers wrong one another, conflict arises. 5. Pride and Selfishness - An unwillingness to yield or consider others breeds division. Biblical Principles for Resolving Conflict The Bible offers a roadmap for handling conflict in a godly way: 1. Address Conflict with Love and Truth (Ephesians 4:15) 49

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Speak the truth in love. ● Avoid gossip and slander. ● Confront with a heart of restoration, not condemnation. 2. Follow the Biblical Steps of Conflict Resolution (Matthew 18:15-17) Jesus outlined a clear process for resolving disputes: 1. Go privately to the person - Seek reconciliation one-on-one. 2. Take witnesses if needed - If unresolved, involve mature believers. 3. Bring it to the church - As a last resort, church leadership may intervene. 4. Let them go if unrepentant - When reconciliation is impossible, maintain love but establish boundaries. 3. Pursue Peace and Unity (Romans 12:18, Hebrews 12:14) ● Strive to live at peace with all. ● Avoid unnecessary quarrels and strife. ● Seek reconciliation over division. 4. Extend Forgiveness and Grace (Colossians 3:13, Matthew 6:14-15) ● Just as Christ forgave us, we must forgive others. ● True forgiveness means releasing resentment and seeking restoration. ● Forgiveness is not condoning sin but allowing God to heal hearts. Having Grace When Others Fall In every church community, believers may stumble and fall. Our response should reflect the grace and mercy of Christ. 1. Restore Gently (Galatians 6:1-2) ● Approach fallen believers with humility. ● Help restore them without pride or condemnation. ● Bear one another’s burdens in love. 50

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 2. Avoid a Judgmental Attitude (James 4:11-12) ● We are called to correct with love, not to condemn. ● Recognize that we all need God’s grace daily. 3. Encourage Repentance and Accountability (2 Corinthians 7:10) ● Call for genuine repentance. ● Provide support and accountability to prevent future struggles. 4. Maintain Fellowship and Unity (1 Corinthians 1:10, John 17:21) ● A healthy church culture promotes forgiveness and reconciliation. ● Unity glorifies God and strengthens the church’s witness. Conclusion Conflict in the church is an opportunity to grow in grace, humility, and love. By applying biblical principles, seeking peace, and extending grace to others, we reflect Christ’s character and preserve the unity of the body. As believers, let us commit to handling conflicts with wisdom, forgiveness, and a heart for reconciliation, ensuring that our fellowship remains a testimony of God’s love to the world. 51

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: TERM 1: FOUNDATIONS OF THE FAITH (Weeks 1-15) Module 3: Christian Living (Weeks 12-15) 52

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: WEEK 12 TOPIC: Victory Over Sin - Overcoming Temptation Key Scriptures: Romans 6:1-14, Galatians 5:16 Introduction As believers, we are called to live victoriously over sin. While temptation is a universal experience, God has given us the power to overcome it through the work of Christ and the indwelling of the Holy Spirit. Understanding our position in Christ and walking in the Spirit are essential keys to overcoming temptation and living a life that pleases God. 1. Understanding Sin and Temptation ● The Nature of Sin: Sin is rebellion against God and manifests in disobedience to His will (1 John 3:4). ● Temptation is Not Sin: Being tempted is not a sin, but yielding to it leads to sin (James 1:14-15). ● Common to All: No believer is exempt from temptation, but God provides a way of escape (1 Corinthians 10:13). 2. Our Victory Through Christ ● Dead to Sin, Alive in Christ (Romans 6:1-14): ○ Through Christ’s death and resurrection, we are no longer slaves to sin. ○ We are to reckon ourselves dead to sin but alive unto God. ○ Sin shall not have dominion over us because we are under grace. ● Crucifying the Flesh (Galatians 5:16-24): ○ Walking in the Spirit enables us to resist the desires of the flesh. ○ The works of the flesh are contrasted with the fruit of the Spirit. 53

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ○ Those who belong to Christ have crucified the flesh with its passions and desires. 3. Practical Steps to Overcome Temptation 1. Renew Your Mind with God’s Word (Romans 12:2): The Word of God transforms our thinking and helps us resist sinful desires. 2. Be Led by the Spirit (Galatians 5:16): The Holy Spirit empowers us to walk in holiness. 3. Avoid Triggers and Flee from Sin (2 Timothy 2:22): We must stay away from situations and influences that lead to temptation. 4. Pray for Strength (Matthew 26:41): Jesus taught that prayer keeps us from falling into temptation. 5. Use the Armor of God (Ephesians 6:10-18): Spiritual warfare requires putting on the full armor of God to stand against the enemy. 6. Rely on God’s Grace (2 Corinthians 12:9): God’s grace strengthens us in our weaknesses. 7. Surround Yourself with Godly Influence (Hebrews 10:24-25): Fellowship with believers strengthens our faith and accountability. 4. The Power of Confession and Restoration ● If a believer falls into sin, confession and repentance restore fellowship with God (1 John 1:9). ● God’s grace is sufficient to restore and strengthen us for continued victory. Conclusion Victory over sin is not about striving in our own strength but relying on the finished work of Christ and the power of the Holy Spirit. As we renew our minds, walk in the Spirit, and apply God’s Word, we will overcome temptation and live a life that glorifies God. 54

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Reflection Questions: 1. What are some areas where you struggle with temptation? 2. How can you apply the principles from this lesson to gain victory? 3. What scriptures can you meditate on to strengthen your resolve against sin? Memory Verse: Romans 6:14 - "For sin shall not have dominion over you: for ye are not under the law, but under grace." 55

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: WEEK 13 Faith & Spiritual Warfare - The Believer’s Authority (Ephesians 6:10-18) Introduction As believers, we are engaged in a spiritual battle. The forces of darkness constantly seek to oppose God's purposes in our lives. However, God has equipped us with everything we need to stand firm and walk in victory. Ephesians 6:10-18 outlines the full armor of God, which enables us to resist the enemy and exercise our authority in Christ. 1. Understanding Spiritual Warfare Spiritual warfare is the battle between the kingdom of God and the forces of darkness (Ephesians 6:12). It is not a physical battle but a spiritual one that requires spiritual weapons. ● The Nature of the Battle: ○ Not against flesh and blood (Ephesians 6:12) ○ A battle of faith and truth against deception and sin ○ The enemy uses fear, doubt, temptation, and distractions to weaken believers ● The Victory of Christ: ○ Jesus has already won the victory (Colossians 2:15, 1 John 4:4) ○ We enforce His victory through faith, prayer, and obedience ○ The authority given to us in Christ (Luke 10:19) 2. The Armor of God (Ephesians 6:13-18) To stand strong in spiritual warfare, we must put on the full armor of God: 1. Belt of Truth - Living in the truth of God’s Word keeps us from deception. 2. Breastplate of Righteousness - Our righteousness in Christ protects our hearts from condemnation. 56

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 3. Shoes of the Gospel of Peace - Walking in the peace of God keeps us stable in the face of trials. 4. Shield of Faith - Faith extinguishes the fiery darts of the enemy, such as doubt and fear. 5. Helmet of Salvation - Protects our minds from lies and discouragement. 6. Sword of the Spirit - The Word of God is our offensive weapon against the enemy. 7. Prayer and Supplication - Constant communication with God empowers us in battle. 3. Exercising Our Authority in Christ ● Know Your Identity in Christ - We are seated with Christ in heavenly places (Ephesians 2:6). ● Speak and Stand on God’s Word - Jesus used Scripture to defeat Satan (Matthew 4:1-11). ● Resist the Devil - Submit to God, resist the devil, and he will flee (James 4:7). ● Walk in Obedience and Holiness - Sin gives the enemy a foothold, but holiness keeps us strong. ● Be Watchful and Disciplined - Stay alert in prayer and discernment (1 Peter 5:8-9). 4. Practical Steps for Victory ● Daily Devotion - Spend time in prayer and the Word to strengthen your faith. ● Guard Your Thoughts - Take every thought captive (2 Corinthians 10:4-5). ● Speak Life - Use your words to declare God’s promises over your life. ● Stay in Fellowship - Surround yourself with believers who will encourage and sharpen you. 57

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Engage in Worship - Worship is a powerful weapon against spiritual attacks. Conclusion Victory in spiritual warfare comes through faith, obedience, and reliance on God’s power. By putting on the full armor of God and exercising our authority in Christ, we can stand strong against every attack of the enemy and walk in the victory that Jesus has secured for us. We are more than conquerors in Christ (Romans 8:37). 58

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: WEEK 14 TOPIC: Stewardship of Resources: Tithes, Offerings, and Giving A Biblical Perspective on New Covenant Giving Introduction Stewardship in the kingdom of God is a fundamental principle that reflects our acknowledgment of God’s ownership over all things. As believers, we are called to manage our resources in a way that honors God, serves the church, and meets the needs of others. While the Old Testament provided clear instructions on tithing, the New Testament does not mandate a fixed percentage but emphasizes the heart, attitude, and purpose of giving. This manual explores the biblical teaching on tithes, offerings, and giving, focusing on how believers should steward their resources under the New Covenant. 1. The Principle of Stewardship A. God Owns Everything “The earth is the Lord’s, and the fulness thereof; the world, and they that dwell therein.” (Psalm 24:1) “For all things come of thee, and of thine own have we given thee.” (1 Chronicles 29:14b) Believers are not owners but stewards of the resources God has entrusted to them. Stewardship requires faithfulness, accountability, and the recognition that everything we have comes from God. B. The Purpose of Wealth and Resources • To meet personal and family needs (1 Timothy 5:8) • To support the work of the ministry (1 Corinthians 9:13-14) • To help those in need (Galatians 6:10, Acts 4:34-35) 59

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 2. Tithing in the Old Testament vs. Giving in the New Covenant A.
