The Old Testament Command on Tithing “And all the tithe of the land, whether of the seed of the land, or of the fruit of the tree, is the Lord’s: it is holy unto the Lord.” (Leviticus 27:30) “Bring ye all the tithes into the storehouse, that there may be meat in mine house, and prove me now herewith, saith the Lord of hosts, if I will not open you the windows of heaven, and pour you out a blessing, that there shall not be room enough to receive it.” (Malachi 3:10) In the Old Testament, tithing was part of the Mosaic Law, requiring Israelites to give a tenth of their produce, livestock, and income to support the Levites, the temple, and the needy. B. The New Testament Perspective on Giving 1. Giving is Commanded, But Amount is Not Mandated The New Testament does not command believers to give a specific percentage. Instead, it emphasizes giving as an act of worship and gratitude. “Every man according as he purposeth in his heart, so let him give; not grudgingly, or of necessity: for God loveth a cheerful giver.” (2 Corinthians 9:7) 2. Giving is a Response to God’s Blessing Giving is an acknowledgment that God has blessed us. It is a natural response to His provision. “Upon the first day of the week let every one of you lay by him in store, as God hath prospered him, that there be no gatherings when I come.” (1 Corinthians 16:2) 60

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 3. Sacrificial and Generous Giving is Encouraged “But this I say, He which soweth sparingly shall reap also sparingly; and he which soweth bountifully shall reap also bountifully.” (2 Corinthians 9:6) “They first gave their own selves to the Lord, and unto us by the will of God.” (2 Corinthians 8:5) 4. Needs in the Church Must be Met The early church responded to needs in the body of Christ through generous giving. “Neither was there any among them that lacked: for as many as were possessors of lands or houses sold them, and brought the prices of the things that were sold, and laid them down at the apostles’ feet: and distribution was made unto every man according as he had need.” (Acts 4:34-35) 3. Offerings and Special Giving in the Church A. Freewill Offerings Offerings in the church should be freewill, from the heart, and not coerced. “Let him give; not grudgingly, or of necessity.” (2 Corinthians 9:7) B. Giving to Church Needs There are moments when specific needs arise in the church, and believers are encouraged to meet those needs. “And let us consider one another to provoke unto love and to good works.” (Hebrews 10:24) C. Giving to the Poor and Missions Helping those in need is a crucial part of biblical giving. 61

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: “He that hath pity upon the poor lendeth unto the Lord; and that which he hath given will he pay him again.” (Proverbs 19:17) 4. The Heart and Attitude of Giving A. Giving Should Be Cheerful “God loveth a cheerful giver.” (2 Corinthians 9:7) B. Giving Should Be Generous “Freely ye have received, freely give.” (Matthew 10:8) C. Giving Should Be Done in Faith “Honour the Lord with thy substance, and with the firstfruits of all thine increase.” (Proverbs 3:9) D. Giving Should Not Be Manipulated The New Testament does not teach forced giving but emphasizes voluntary generosity. “That thy alms may be in secret: and thy Father which seeth in secret himself shall reward thee openly.” (Matthew 6:4) 5. The Blessing of Giving 1. God Supplies the Giver’s Needs “But my God shall supply all your need according to his riches in glory by Christ Jesus.” (Philippians 4:19) 2. Giving Opens Doors for More Grace “Give, and it shall be given unto you; good measure, pressed down, and shaken together, and running over, shall men give into your bosom.” (Luke 6:38) 3. Giving Strengthens the Church and Expands the Gospel “Not because I desire a gift: but I desire fruit that may abound to your account.” (Philippians 4:17) 62

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Conclusion: Living as Faithful Stewards Stewardship in the New Testament is not about percentages but about faithfulness, gratitude, and obedience to the leading of the Holy Spirit. Giving is a response to God’s blessings, a way to support the work of the ministry, and an expression of love for others. As believers, we must give cheerfully, sacrificially, and with the right heart, trusting that God will always provide for our needs. May we be found faithful stewards of all that God has entrusted to us. Amen. 63

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: WEEK 15 TOPIC: Living as a Witness - Evangelism and Discipleship Key Scriptures: Matthew 28:19-20, Romans 1:16 Introduction As believers, we are called not only to live out our faith but also to share it with others. Evangelism and discipleship are fundamental aspects of the Great Commission given by Jesus. We are witnesses of Christ's love and saving power, and we are charged with spreading the gospel to the world. 1. Understanding Evangelism Evangelism is the act of proclaiming the good news of Jesus Christ. It is not reserved for pastors or missionaries alone; every believer is called to share their faith. A. The Mandate for Evangelism Matthew 28:19-20 - Jesus commands us to go and make disciples of all nations. Romans 1:16 - The gospel is the power of God unto salvation for everyone who believes. Acts 1:8 - We are empowered by the Holy Spirit to be witnesses. B. The Message of Evangelism ● God loves the world and desires that none perish (John 3:16, 2 Peter 3:9). ● Sin separates humanity from God (Romans 3:23, Isaiah 59:2). ● Jesus is the only way to salvation (John 14:6, Acts 4:12). ● Salvation is received by grace through faith (Ephesians 2:8-9, Romans 10:9-10). 64

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: C. The Methods of Evangelism ● Personal Testimony: Sharing your story of salvation (John 4:29, Revelation 12:11). ● Lifestyle Evangelism: Living in a way that reflects Christ (Matthew 5:16, 1 Peter 3:15). ● Direct Evangelism: Engaging in conversations, preaching, and distributing gospel materials. ● Media and Technology: Utilizing social media, podcasts, and videos to spread the gospel. 2. The Call to Discipleship Evangelism does not end at conversion; new believers must be nurtured in their faith. Discipleship is the process of helping others grow in their relationship with Christ. A. The Importance of Discipleship ● Jesus modeled discipleship by training His followers (Mark 3:14, Luke 6:40). ● The early church was devoted to learning and fellowship (Acts 2:42). ● Mature believers are called to teach and equip others (2 Timothy 2:2, Hebrews 5:12-14). B. The Process of Discipleship ● Teaching Sound Doctrine: Helping believers understand the Word (2 Timothy 3:16-17). ● Encouraging Spiritual Growth: Prayer, fasting, and studying Scripture (Colossians 2:6-7). ● Providing Accountability: Building relationships that foster growth (Hebrews 10:24-25). ● Training for Ministry: Encouraging service and leadership in the church (Ephesians 4:11-12). 65

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 3. Overcoming Barriers to Evangelism and Discipleship Many believers struggle with fear, inadequacy, or lack of knowledge when it comes to sharing their faith and discipling others. Here are ways to overcome these challenges: ● Fear of Rejection: Remember that salvation is God’s work (John 6:44, 1 Corinthians 3:6-7). ● Lack of Knowledge: Study Scripture and rely on the Holy Spirit (John 14:26, 2 Timothy 2:15). ● Busyness: Make evangelism and discipleship a priority in daily life (Colossians 4:5-6). ● Spiritual Warfare: Engage in prayer and put on the armor of God (Ephesians 6:10-18). 4. The Reward of Evangelism and Discipleship ● Joy in Heaven: There is great rejoicing over one sinner who repents (Luke 15:7). ● Spiritual Growth: Evangelism strengthens our own faith (Philemon 1:6). ● Eternal Impact: Making disciples has lasting rewards (Daniel 12:3, 1 Corinthians 3:12-14). Conclusion Living as a witness is a vital part of the Christian walk. Every believer is called to share the gospel and disciple others. Through the power of the Holy Spirit, we can boldly proclaim Christ, nurture new believers, and fulfill the Great Commission. Let us be faithful stewards of this calling, knowing that our labor in the Lord is never in vain (1 Corinthians 15:58). Reflection Questions: 1. What fears or challenges do you face in sharing your faith? 2. How can you be more intentional in discipling others? 66

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 3. Who in your life needs to hear the gospel or be mentored in their faith? Action Steps: ● Identify one person to share the gospel with this week. ● Commit to mentoring a new believer. ● Pray for boldness and opportunities to witness. By following the biblical principles of evangelism and discipleship, we align ourselves with God’s heart for the lost and fulfill our purpose as His witnesses in the world. 67

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: TERM 2: UNDERSTANDING THE BIBLE (Weeks 16-28) Module 4: The Bible - God’s Inspired Word (Weeks 16-17) 68

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: WEEK 16 TOPIC: The Origin and Authority of the Bible How the Bible Came to Be Key Scriptures: 2 Timothy 3:16-17, 2 Peter 1:20-21 Introduction The Bible is the inspired Word of God and the final authority for faith and practice. It is unique among all writings in its divine origin, historical reliability, and transformative power. Understanding how the Bible came to be helps us appreciate its authority and trustworthiness in our lives as believers. 1. The Divine Inspiration of Scripture (2 Timothy 3:16-17) - “All scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness: That the man of God may be perfect, thoroughly furnished unto all good works.” ● The term “inspiration” means “God-breathed” (Greek: theopneustos ), signifying that the Bible is not merely human literature but divinely revealed. ● Every part of Scripture is inspired, from Genesis to Revelation, and serves a purpose in teaching, correcting, and equipping believers. (2 Peter 1:20-21) - “Knowing this first, that no prophecy of the scripture is of any private interpretation. For the prophecy came not in old time by the will of man: but holy men of God spake as they were moved by the Holy Ghost.” 69

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● The Bible was written by human authors, but they were moved by the Holy Spirit, ensuring the message was God’s and not their own opinions. ● This process is known as verbal plenary inspiration, meaning every word and every part of Scripture is fully inspired by God. 2. The Formation of the Bible The Bible consists of 66 books (39 in the Old Testament, 27 in the New Testament), written over a period of about 1,500 years by more than 40 authors from different backgrounds and time periods, yet maintaining perfect unity. A. The Old Testament ● The Old Testament was written in Hebrew (with some portions in Aramaic) and was recognized as Scripture by the Jewish people. ● Jesus and the apostles affirmed the Old Testament as the authoritative Word of God (Luke 24:27, 44). ● It includes the Law (Torah), Prophets, and Writings, all pointing to the coming Messiah. B. The New Testament ● The New Testament was written in Greek and consists of Gospels, Epistles, and Revelation. ● The early church recognized the writings of the apostles as divinely inspired and authoritative. ● By the 4th century, church councils affirmed the 27 books of the New Testament as Scripture, though they were already widely accepted by the early believers. 3. The Authority of the Bible 70

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: The Bible’s authority rests on its divine authorship, its historical accuracy, and its spiritual power. A. The Bible Is Infallible and Inerrant ● Infallible means it is completely trustworthy in all matters of faith and practice. ● Inerrant means it is without error in its original manuscripts. B. The Bible Is the Final Authority ● It supersedes human traditions, opinions, and experiences (Isaiah 40:8, Matthew 24:35). ● It is the standard by which all teachings and doctrines must be tested (Acts 17:11). ● It provides guidance for Christian living, equipping believers for every good work (Psalm 119:105). C. The Bible’s Power and Preservation ● The Bible has survived countless attempts to be destroyed or discredited throughout history (e.g., persecution under Roman emperors, skepticism in modern times), yet it remains the most widely read and distributed book in the world. ● God has preserved His Word so that His truth remains available for every generation (Matthew 5:18). 4. How We Should Respond to the Bible ● Read and Study It Regularly - Joshua 1:8 commands us to meditate on God’s Word day and night. ● Apply It in Daily Life - James 1:22 exhorts believers to be doers of the Word, not just hearers. ● Defend It with Boldness - 1 Peter 3:15 urges us to always be ready to give an answer for our faith. 71

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Trust in Its Power - Hebrews 4:12 describes the Word as living and active, able to transform hearts and minds. Conclusion The Bible is not just a religious book; it is God’s revelation to mankind. Understanding its divine origin and authority strengthens our confidence in its teachings and our commitment to live by its truths. As believers, we must cherish, study, and proclaim the Word of God, knowing that it is the foundation of our faith and the key to spiritual growth. 72

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 17 TOPIC: Bible Structure and Canonization Introduction The Bible is the inspired Word of God, consisting of two major divisions: the Old Testament and the New Testament. Understanding how these books were structured and selected into the biblical canon helps believers appreciate the authority and reliability of Scripture. 1. The Structure of the Bible The Bible consists of 66 books divided into two main sections: A. The Old Testament (39 Books) The Old Testament was primarily written in Hebrew (with small portions in Aramaic) and contains books that were recognized as Scripture by the Jewish people before the coming of Christ. It is traditionally divided into: 1. The Law (Torah/Pentateuch) - 5 Books ○ Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, Deuteronomy 2. The Historical Books - 12 Books ○ Joshua, Judges, Ruth, 1 & 2 Samuel, 1 & 2 Kings, 1 & 2 Chronicles, Ezra, Nehemiah, Esther 3. The Poetic and Wisdom Books - 5 Books ○ Job, Psalms, Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, Song of Solomon 4. The Major Prophets - 5 Books ○ Isaiah, Jeremiah, Lamentations, Ezekiel, Daniel 5. The Minor Prophets - 12 Books ○ Hosea, Joel, Amos, Obadiah, Jonah, Micah, Nahum, Habakkuk, Zephaniah, Haggai, Zechariah, Malachi B. The New Testament (27 Books) Written in Greek, the New Testament contains the revelation of Jesus Christ and the foundation of Christian doctrine. It is divided into: 73

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 1. The Gospels - 4 Books ○ Matthew, Mark, Luke, John 2. The Historical Book - 1 Book ○ Acts 3. The Pauline Epistles (Letters) - 13 Books ○ Romans, 1 & 2 Corinthians, Galatians, Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, 1 & 2 Thessalonians, 1 & 2 Timothy, Titus, Philemon 4. The General Epistles - 8 Books ○ Hebrews, James, 1 & 2 Peter, 1, 2, & 3 John, Jude 5. The Prophetic Book - 1 Book ○ Revelation 2. The Canonization of Scripture The term "canon" means a rule or standard. The biblical canon refers to the collection of books recognized as inspired by God and authoritative for faith and practice. A. Canonization of the Old Testament ● The Jewish community recognized the 39 books of the Old Testament as authoritative long before the time of Christ. ● Jesus and the apostles referred to these writings as Scripture (Luke 24:44, Matthew 5:17-18). ● The Council of Jamnia (AD 90) reaffirmed the Old Testament books but did not introduce new ones. B. Canonization of the New Testament The early church carefully evaluated writings before recognizing them as Scripture. The criteria used included: 1. Apostolic Authority - Was the book written by an apostle or a close associate of an apostle? 74

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 2. Orthodoxy (Agreement with Christian Doctrine) - Did the book align with the teachings of Jesus and the apostles? 3. Widespread Usage - Was the book widely accepted and used by the early church? 4. Divine Inspiration - Did the book exhibit evidence of being inspired by the Holy Spirit? By AD 367 , Athanasius (Bishop of Alexandria) listed the 27 New Testament books as Scripture, which was later affirmed by the Councils of Hippo (AD 393) and Carthage (AD 397). 3. The Apocrypha and Deuterocanonical Books The Apocrypha refers to a collection of Jewish writings included in some versions of the Old Testament (e.g., the Roman Catholic Bible) but not in the Hebrew Bible or Protestant Bible. These books were written between the Old and New Testament periods but were not universally accepted as inspired Scripture. Reasons why the Apocrypha is not included in the Protestant Bible: ● The Jewish community did not recognize it as Scripture. ● Jesus and the apostles never quoted from it. ● The early church fathers had differing opinions about its authority. ● It contains historical and doctrinal inconsistencies. 4. The Authority of the Bible The Bible is authoritative because it is the inspired Word of God: 2 Timothy 3:16-17 - "All scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness." 2 Peter 1:20-21 - "Knowing this first, that no prophecy of the scripture is of any private interpretation. For the prophecy came not 75

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: in old time by the will of man: but holy men of God spake as they were moved by the Holy Ghost." 5. Why Understanding the Canon is Important ● It Strengthens Our Faith - Knowing the Bible’s structure and history affirms its reliability. ● It Guards Against False Teachings - Understanding how books were selected helps distinguish Scripture from other religious writings. ● It Equips Us for Ministry - Recognizing the authority of God’s Word enables us to confidently share and defend the faith. Conclusion The Bible is not a random collection of writings but a divinely inspired and carefully preserved revelation of God. Understanding its structure and the process of canonization assures us of its authority and reliability. As believers, we must study, meditate on, and apply the Scriptures in our daily lives, knowing that they are the ultimate guide for faith and practice. Reflection Questions 1. Why is it important to understand how the books of the Bible were selected? 2. How does knowing the structure of the Bible help you in your study of Scripture? 3. How can you grow in your confidence in the authority of the Bible? Memory Verse: "All scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness." - 2 Timothy 3:16 76

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: TERM 2: UNDERSTANDING THE BIBLE (Weeks 16-28) Module 5: Literary Divisions of the Bible (Weeks 18-23) 77

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 18 The Law (Pentateuch) - Genesis to Deuteronomy (God’s Covenant and Commandments) Introduction The first five books of the Bible, known as the Pentateuch or Torah , form the foundation of Scripture and lay the groundwork for understanding God's relationship with humanity. These books - Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy - contain the story of creation, God's covenant with His people, the giving of the Law, and Israel's journey to the Promised Land. Studying the Pentateuch helps us grasp God's covenant, His commandments, and His plan for redemption . It also teaches us about obedience, faith, and God's unchanging nature. 1. Overview of the Pentateuch Each book of the Pentateuch contributes uniquely to the story of God's people: ● Genesis : The book of beginnings - creation, the fall, the flood, the covenant with Abraham, and the formation of Israel. ● Exodus : The story of Israel's deliverance from Egypt, the giving of the Law at Mount Sinai, and God's instructions for worship. ● Leviticus : Laws concerning holiness, sacrifices, the priesthood, and how Israel is to live as a holy nation. ● Numbers : The journey of Israel through the wilderness, their struggles, and God's guidance. ● Deuteronomy : A repetition of God's law and Moses' final instructions before Israel enters the Promised Land. 2. Understanding God’s Covenant A key theme of the Pentateuch is God's covenant with His people. The covenant establishes: 78

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● God's promise to Abraham (Genesis 12:1-3, 15:5-6) to make him a great nation. ● God's deliverance of Israel from slavery and His calling of them as His chosen people (Exodus 6:6-7). ● The Mosaic Covenant (Exodus 19-24), where God gives the Law at Mount Sinai, setting Israel apart as His people. The covenant demonstrates God’s faithfulness and His expectation of obedience and worship from His people. 3. The Commandments and the Law The Law given through Moses was meant to: ● Define God’s standard of righteousness (Leviticus 19:2). ● Show Israel how to live as a holy nation (Deuteronomy 6:4-5). ● Establish moral, ceremonial, and civil laws to govern the people. ● Point toward the need for redemption , ultimately fulfilled in Christ (Galatians 3:24). Key aspects of the Law: ● The Ten Commandments (Exodus 20:1-17) - God's moral law for Israel and a reflection of His holy nature. ● Sacrificial Laws (Leviticus) - Pointing to redemption and ultimately fulfilled in Christ (Hebrews 10:1-10). ● Instructions for Worship and the Priesthood (Leviticus 8-10) - Establishing the tabernacle, priests, and sacrifices. 4. Relevance of the Pentateuch Today Though Christians are not under the Old Testament Law in the same way Israel was (Romans 6:14), the Pentateuch remains relevant because: ● It reveals God’s character - His holiness, justice, love, and mercy. ● It lays the foundation for understanding Jesus as the fulfillment of the Law (Matthew 5:17). ● It teaches obedience and faith - principles still applicable today. 79

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● It points to God’s redemptive plan , which is fully realized in Christ. Conclusion The Pentateuch is essential for understanding the Bible as a whole. It introduces God's covenant, His laws, and His dealings with humanity, all of which point forward to Christ. Studying these books helps us develop a greater appreciation for God's plan of redemption and our role as His people. Key Scriptures for Meditation: ● Genesis 12:1-3 - God's covenant with Abraham. ● Exodus 20:1-17 - The Ten Commandments. ● Deuteronomy 6:4-5 - Loving God with all our heart, soul, and might. ● Galatians 3:24 - The Law as a tutor leading us to Christ. 80

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 19 TOPIC: The Historical Books - Joshua to Esther (God’s Dealings with Israel’s History) Introduction The Historical Books of the Bible (Joshua to Esther) cover the history of Israel from the conquest of Canaan to the return from exile. These books reveal God’s faithfulness, Israel’s cycles of obedience and rebellion, and His divine sovereignty in shaping history. Understanding these books helps believers appreciate God’s covenant relationship with His people and the lessons applicable to our spiritual journey today. Overview of the Historical Books The Historical Books include twelve books that document Israel's national history: 1. Joshua - Conquest and division of the Promised Land 2. Judges - Israel's cycle of sin, oppression, repentance, and deliverance 3. Ruth - Redemption through Boaz, the kinsman-redeemer 4. 1 & 2 Samuel - Transition from judges to monarchy; reigns of Saul and David 5. 1 & 2 Kings - The kingdom's rise, division, and eventual downfall 6. 1 & 2 Chronicles - Retelling of Israel's history with a focus on the temple and worship 7. Ezra - The return from exile and the rebuilding of the temple 8. Nehemiah - Rebuilding of Jerusalem’s walls and spiritual renewal 9. Esther - God’s providential protection of His people in exile Each book reveals aspects of God’s dealings with Israel, highlighting faithfulness, judgment, mercy, and restoration. Themes and Lessons from the Historical Books 1. God’s Sovereignty in Human Affairs 81

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Joshua's victories show God's power in fulfilling His promises (Joshua 1:3-9). ● The rise and fall of kings demonstrate God’s control over leadership (1 Samuel 16:1, Daniel 2:21). ● Esther’s story reveals God’s unseen hand in delivering His people (Esther 4:14). 2. The Consequences of Obedience and Disobedience ● Blessings followed Israel’s obedience, as seen in Joshua’s conquest (Joshua 24:14-15). ● Disobedience led to oppression during the period of the judges (Judges 2:11-23). ● Idolatry and injustice resulted in Israel’s exile (2 Kings 17:7-23). 3. God’s Faithfulness to His Covenant ● Despite Israel’s failures, God remained faithful to His promises (1 Kings 8:56). ● The return from exile fulfilled God’s promise of restoration (Jeremiah 29:10, Ezra 1:1-4). ● The preservation of the Jewish people in Persia (Esther) reflects God's enduring faithfulness. 4. God Uses Ordinary People for His Divine Plan ● Ruth, a Moabite, becomes an ancestor of Jesus through Boaz (Ruth 4:13-17). ● Nehemiah, a cupbearer, leads the rebuilding of Jerusalem’s walls (Nehemiah 2:4-5). ● Esther, an orphan, is used to deliver the Jews (Esther 4:16). Application for Believers Today 1. Trust in God’s Sovereignty - Just as He guided Israel’s history, He directs our lives. 2. Obedience Brings Blessing - Walking in God’s ways leads to spiritual growth and protection. 3. Remain Faithful in Adversity - Like Esther, stand firm in faith even when God seems silent. 82

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 4. God’s Purpose in Trials - Challenges refine us and prepare us for greater things, as seen in Nehemiah’s leadership. 5. God Uses Ordinary People - No matter your background, God has a role for you in His plan. Conclusion The Historical Books provide valuable lessons about God’s dealings with His people. They remind us of His justice, mercy, and faithfulness. By studying these books, we learn how to trust, obey, and live for God in all circumstances. As believers, we are called to learn from Israel’s history and apply these truths to our walk with Christ. 83

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 20 TOPIC: Wisdom & Poetic Books - Psalms, Proverbs, Ecclesiastes (Poetry, Wisdom, Worship) Introduction The Wisdom and Poetic Books of the Bible, Psalms, Proverbs, and Ecclesiastes, offer rich insight into worship, wisdom for daily living, and reflections on the purpose of life. These books stand out in Scripture for their poetic style and depth of spiritual truths, shaping both personal devotion and theological understanding. Overview of the Wisdom & Poetic Books 1. The Book of Psalms - The Book of Worship ● The Book of Psalms is a collection of 150 songs, prayers, and poems that express the full range of human emotions in worship before God. ● It is divided into five sections, mirroring the five books of Moses (Genesis to Deuteronomy). ● The main authors include David, Asaph, the sons of Korah, Solomon, Moses, Ethan, and anonymous contributors. ● Themes include thanksgiving, repentance, lament, praise, and prophecy about the Messiah. ● Psalms were used in Israel’s temple worship and are frequently quoted in the New Testament. 2. The Book of Proverbs - The Book of Wisdom ● Written primarily by Solomon, with contributions from Agur and King Lemuel. ● Proverbs contains 31 chapters, offering practical wisdom for godly living. ● The book emphasizes the fear of the Lord as the foundation of wisdom (Proverbs 1:7). 84

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● It teaches on key life TOPICs such as relationships, work ethic, speech, discipline, and integrity. ● Proverbs are short, memorable sayings designed to shape daily conduct. 3. The Book of Ecclesiastes - The Book of Reflection ● Traditionally attributed to Solomon, Ecclesiastes reflects on the meaning of life and the pursuit of true fulfillment. ● It reveals the emptiness of life apart from God and the futility of chasing after worldly pleasures. ● The key phrase "Vanity of vanities! All is vanity" (Ecclesiastes 1:2) expresses the book’s central theme: life without God is meaningless. ● The book concludes with a powerful truth: "Fear God and keep His commandments, for this is the whole duty of man" (Ecclesiastes 12:13). How to Read the Psalms and Proverbs in a Month Reading Psalms and Proverbs systematically helps believers gain wisdom and deepen their worship life. Psalms Reading Plan ● Read five chapters of Psalms each day. ● Add 30 to the previous number to stay on track. Example: ○ Day 1: Psalms 1, 31, 61, 91, 121 ○ Day 2: Psalms 2, 32, 62, 92, 122 ○ Day 3: Psalms 3, 33, 63, 93, 123 ○ Continue this pattern until you complete all 150 Psalms in 30 days. Proverbs Reading Plan ● Read one chapter of Proverbs per day. ● Since Proverbs has 31 chapters, reading one chapter daily allows you to finish it in a month. 85

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● This ensures daily exposure to godly wisdom applicable to life’s situations. Assignment 1. Follow the Psalms and Proverbs reading plan for the next 30 days. 2. Write down five key lessons from Proverbs that you can apply to your life. 3. Watch the teaching series on Song of Solomon on our YouTube page: Great Haven Ministries 4. Journal your reflections on a Psalm or Proverb that speaks to your current season of life. Conclusion The Wisdom and Poetic Books serve as a guide for worship, wisdom, and reflection on life’s purpose. As we engage with Psalms in worship, Proverbs in practical living, and Ecclesiastes in reflection, we grow in understanding our relationship with God. May this study encourage you to cultivate a lifestyle of devotion, wisdom, and worship in Christ! 86

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 21 TOPIC: The Major & Minor Prophets - Isaiah to Malachi (Prophecy, Messianic Promises) Introduction The prophetic books of the Bible serve as God's voice to His people, calling them to repentance, warning of judgment, and proclaiming the coming of the Messiah. These books are traditionally divided into Major Prophets and Minor Prophets , not because of their importance but because of their length and scope. While the Major Prophets contain more extensive writings, the Minor Prophets still hold powerful and profound messages from God. Understanding the Prophetic Books The prophetic books (Isaiah to Malachi) cover several centuries of Israel's history, from the time of the divided kingdom to the post-exilic period. They were written by prophets whom God raised up to speak on His behalf. These books contain three key themes : 1. Judgment - Prophets warned of impending judgment due to Israel’s sin and unfaithfulness. 2. Hope & Restoration - They pointed toward God's mercy, the coming Messiah, and the restoration of His people. 3. Messianic Prophecy - Many of their prophecies found fulfillment in Jesus Christ. The Major Prophets The Major Prophets include Isaiah, Jeremiah, Lamentations, Ezekiel, and Daniel . These books are longer and contain extensive visions, prophecies, and theological insights. 1. Isaiah (66 Chapters) - The Miniature Bible ● Often called the "Fifth Gospel" because of its strong Messianic focus. 87

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Isaiah has 66 chapters , mirroring the 66 books of the Bible: the first 39 chapters align with the Old Testament’s themes of judgment, while the last 27 chapters reflect New Testament themes of grace and salvation. ● Isaiah 53 is one of the clearest prophecies about Jesus’ suffering and redemptive work. 2. Jeremiah (52 Chapters) - The Weeping Prophet ● Known for his deep sorrow over Israel's rebellion and impending exile. ● Introduced the concept of the New Covenant (Jeremiah 31:31-34), which was fulfilled in Christ. ● He prophesied Israel’s captivity in Babylon for 70 years (Jeremiah 25:11-12). 3. Lamentations (5 Chapters) - A Cry of Mourning ● Written by Jeremiah as a poetic lament over Jerusalem’s destruction. ● Each chapter is an acrostic poem except the final chapter. ● Lamentations 3:22-23 speaks of God’s mercies being new every morning . 4. Ezekiel (48 Chapters) - The Prophet of Visions ● Had incredible visions (e.g., the valley of dry bones in Ezekiel 37). ● Introduced the concept of a new heart and new spirit (Ezekiel 36:26-27). ● He saw God’s glory depart from the temple due to Israel’s sin and later promised its return. 5. Daniel (12 Chapters) - The Prophet of Kingdoms ● Contains apocalyptic visions and narratives about faithful living in exile. 88

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Prophesied about world empires and the coming of the Messianic Kingdom (Daniel 2, 7, 9). ● The 70 weeks prophecy (Daniel 9:24-27) foretells Christ’s coming and the end times. The Minor Prophets The 12 Minor Prophets are called "minor" because their books are shorter, not because they are less significant. Their messages are equally powerful and essential to biblical prophecy. 1. Hosea - The Prophet of God’s Love ● Hosea’s life mirrors God’s faithful love to an unfaithful Israel. ● Hosea 6:6: "For I desire mercy, not sacrifice." 2. Joel - The Prophet of the Day of the Lord ● Prophesied about Pentecost (Joel 2:28-29, fulfilled in Acts 2). 3. Amos - The Prophet of Justice ● Strongly condemned social injustice and religious hypocrisy. 4. Obadiah - The Prophet Against Edom ● The shortest Old Testament book, pronouncing judgment on Edom. 5. Jonah - The Reluctant Prophet ● A story of God’s mercy to Nineveh and a lesson in obedience. ● Jesus referenced Jonah as a sign of His resurrection (Matthew 12:40). 6. Micah - The Prophet of the Messiah’s Birth ● Prophesied that Christ would be born in Bethlehem (Micah 5:2). 7. Nahum - The Prophet of Nineveh’s Doom ● A sequel to Jonah, foretelling Nineveh’s destruction. 89

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 8. Habakkuk - The Prophet Who Questioned God ● “The just shall live by faith” (Habakkuk 2:4), quoted in Romans, Galatians, and Hebrews. 9. Zephaniah - The Prophet of the Day of the Lord ● Prophesied about God’s future judgment and restoration . 10. Haggai - The Prophet of Rebuilding ● Encouraged Israel to rebuild the temple after exile. 11. Zechariah - The Prophet of the Coming King ● Prophesied Jesus' triumphal entry (Zechariah 9:9). ● Contains more Messianic prophecies than any Minor Prophet. 12. Malachi - The Last Prophet ● Warned about spiritual apathy and foretold John the Baptist (Malachi 3:1). ● Closes the Old Testament, setting the stage for Christ. Devotional Reflection The prophets were not just messengers of judgment; they were voices of hope, pointing to Jesus Christ. Their prophecies show God’s justice, mercy, and faithfulness . In every warning of judgment, there was a promise of restoration through the coming Messiah. ● Jesus is the fulfillment of prophecy. Every Major and Minor Prophet, in some way, pointed to Christ. ● God speaks through His Word today. Just as He used prophets, He still speaks through Scripture. ● Prophecy builds our faith. The accuracy of biblical prophecy affirms the trustworthiness of God’s Word. Assignment 1. Read Isaiah 53 and write a reflection on how it points to Jesus. 90

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 2. Study Joel 2:28-32 and Acts 2:16-21. How does Peter confirm the fulfillment of Joel’s prophecy at Pentecost? Conclusion The prophetic books reveal God’s heart for His people , His call to holiness, His plan of redemption, and His promise of restoration. As believers, studying the Major and Minor Prophets strengthens our understanding of God’s character and deepens our faith in Jesus, the ultimate fulfillment of prophecy. 91

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 22 TOPIC: The Gospels & Acts - The Life of Jesus and the Early Church (Matthew to John, Acts) Introduction The Gospels and the Book of Acts are foundational to our understanding of Jesus Christ and the birth of His Church. The Gospels (Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John) present four accounts of the life, ministry, death, and resurrection of Jesus. The Book of Acts continues the narrative by detailing the birth and expansion of the early Church through the work of the Holy Spirit. Studying these books gives us a clearer picture of Jesus’ mission, His teachings, His sacrifice, and the power of the Holy Spirit working through His followers. Our faith is rooted in Christ, and these books help us to grow in our knowledge and relationship with Him. The Four Gospels: Four Perspectives, One Story Each Gospel presents a unique perspective of Jesus’ life, yet they all harmonize to give us a complete picture of His mission. 1. The Gospel of Matthew - Jesus as the Promised King ● Written by Matthew (Levi), a tax collector turned disciple. ● Emphasizes Jesus as the Messianic King and the fulfillment of Old Testament prophecies. ● Contains the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5-7), which teaches Kingdom principles. ● Written primarily to a Jewish audience to prove that Jesus is the long-awaited Messiah. 2. The Gospel of Mark - Jesus as the Servant ● The shortest Gospel, likely written by John Mark, who worked closely with Peter. 92

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Emphasizes Jesus as a servant who came to give His life as a ransom for many (Mark 10:45). ● Focuses on action and miracles , making it a fast-paced narrative. ● Written primarily for a Roman audience unfamiliar with Jewish traditions. 3. The Gospel of Luke - Jesus as the Son of Man ● Written by Luke, a physician and companion of Paul. ● Presents Jesus as the perfect Son of Man , emphasizing His compassion and humanity. ● Contains the most detailed account of Jesus’ birth (Luke 1-2). ● Highlights Jesus’ ministry to the poor, outcasts, and Gentiles. ● Written for a Greek audience and emphasizes historical accuracy. 4. The Gospel of John - Jesus as the Son of God ● Written by John, the beloved disciple. ● Emphasizes Jesus’ divinity and eternal existence (John 1:1-14). ● Contains seven “I AM” statements that reveal Jesus’ divine nature (e.g., “I am the Bread of Life” - John 6:35). ● Focuses on belief and salvation, with John 3:16 being a central verse. The Gospels give us a complete picture of Jesus, King, Servant, Son of Man, and Son of God, so that we may believe and have eternal life (John 20:31). The Book of Acts - The Birth of the Church The Book of Acts, written by Luke, serves as a bridge between the Gospels and the Epistles. It details the work of the Holy Spirit in establishing the Church and spreading the Gospel from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth (Acts 1:8). Key Themes in Acts: 93

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 1. The Great Commission Fulfilled - The disciples carry out Jesus’ command to make disciples of all nations (Matthew 28:19-20). 2. The Power of the Holy Spirit - Acts 2 records the Pentecost , when the Holy Spirit descended upon the believers, empowering them for ministry. 3. The Spread of the Gospel - The apostles, especially Peter and Paul, take the message of Christ beyond Jewish boundaries to the Gentile world. 4. Persecution and Growth - Despite opposition, the Church grows rapidly, proving that the Gospel cannot be stopped. 5. Paul’s Missionary Journeys - Acts follows Paul’s journeys as he plants churches and writes letters that later become much of the New Testament. Acts shows us that the Gospel is not just history, it is a living, powerful movement that continues through us today . Devotional Reflection ● Have you encountered Jesus in a personal way through the Gospels? ● Are you living in the power of the Holy Spirit like the early Church? ● Are you actively sharing the Gospel and making disciples as Jesus commanded? Reading the Gospels and Acts with an open heart will transform your faith and make you an effective witness for Christ. Assignment 1. Read one Gospel over the next week (choose Matthew, Mark, Luke, or John). 2. Write a short reflection on how Jesus’ teachings and actions have impacted your faith. 94

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: By immersing ourselves in the life of Jesus and the early Church, we are equipped to live as bold witnesses for Christ in our own time. Conclusion The Gospels and Acts lay the foundation for our faith. Jesus came to redeem us, and through the power of the Holy Spirit, we are now His hands and feet in the world. Let us take His message to the nations, just as the early Church did, knowing that the same Spirit who empowered them empowers us today. Let’s go forth, living in the reality of Jesus Christ and the power of the Holy Spirit, continuing the story of Acts in our generation. Amen! 95

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 23 TOPIC: The Epistles (Pauline & General) - Apostolic Teachings (Romans to Jude) Introduction The Epistles are letters written by the apostles to early churches and believers, providing doctrinal instruction, encouragement, correction, and practical guidance for Christian living. They form the theological foundation of the New Testament, explaining the significance of Jesus’ life, death, and resurrection, and instructing believers on how to live in light of the Gospel. The Epistles can be divided into two main categories: 1. Pauline Epistles (Romans to Philemon) - Letters written by Apostle Paul. 2. General Epistles (Hebrews to Jude) - Letters written by other apostles and early church leaders. Understanding these writings is crucial to growing in faith, living a godly life, and being equipped to disciple others. 1. The Pauline Epistles (Romans - Philemon) Paul wrote 13 (or 14, if including Hebrews) epistles to churches and individuals, addressing theology, Christian conduct, and church leadership. His letters can be divided into three categories: A. Doctrinal Epistles (Romans, 1 & 2 Corinthians, Galatians) ● Romans: A comprehensive explanation of salvation by grace through faith, justification, and the righteousness of God. ● 1 Corinthians: Addresses divisions in the church, spiritual gifts, love, and resurrection. ● 2 Corinthians: Focuses on Paul’s ministry, suffering, and encouragement for believers. 96

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Galatians: Defends salvation by grace through faith, opposing legalism and emphasizing freedom in Christ. B. Prison Epistles (Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, Philemon) ● Ephesians: Teaches about the believer’s identity in Christ, spiritual blessings, and unity in the body of Christ. ● Philippians: A letter of joy, gratitude, and encouragement to remain faithful despite trials. ● Colossians: Exalts Christ as supreme and warns against false teachings. ● Philemon: A personal letter advocating forgiveness and reconciliation. C. Pastoral Epistles (1 & 2 Timothy, Titus) ● 1 & 2 Timothy, Titus: Instructions for church leadership, sound doctrine, and godly living. 2. The General Epistles (Hebrews - Jude) These letters were written to a broader audience, addressing faith, endurance, and warnings against false teachings. ● Hebrews: Highlights Jesus as the fulfillment of the Old Testament, our High Priest, and the New Covenant. ● James: Stresses practical Christian living, faith demonstrated by works, and perseverance. ● 1 & 2 Peter: Encourages suffering believers, warns against false teachers, and speaks of Christ’s return. ● 1, 2, & 3 John: Focuses on love, truth, and assurance of salvation. ● Jude: Warns against apostasy and contending for the faith. Devotional Reflection 1. Are you living according to the teachings of the Epistles? The letters of Paul and other apostles call us to holiness, faith, and love. 97

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 2. Do you understand your identity in Christ? The Epistles reveal our position as saved, sanctified, and seated in heavenly places with Christ (Ephesians 2:6). 3. Are you growing in sound doctrine? False teachings existed in the early church and still exist today. Staying rooted in Scripture protects us. Assignment 1. Choose one Epistle and read it this week. 2. Write down three key takeaways from your reading and share them with someone. Conclusion The Epistles provide guidance for every believer. They teach us how to live, endure trials, avoid deception, and fulfill the Great Commission. Let us apply these truths in our lives, standing firm in sound doctrine and spreading the message of Christ to the world. Amen! 98

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: TERM 2: UNDERSTANDING THE BIBLE (Weeks 16-28) Module 6: Biblical Interpretation & Application (Weeks 24-28) 99

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 24 Week 24: Prophetic Books & Apocalyptic Literature - Revelation, Daniel, Ezekiel (Understanding Prophecy and End Times) Introduction Prophecy is a significant part of Scripture, revealing God’s divine plan for His people, His judgment against sin, and His promises of restoration. Apocalyptic literature, a unique subset of prophecy, conveys divine revelations about future events through vivid imagery, symbols, and visions. The books of Daniel, Ezekiel, and Revelation contain some of the most profound prophetic and apocalyptic messages in the Bible. As a bible student , it is important to approach these texts with an understanding that many prophecies have already been fulfilled , particularly those concerning Israel and the destruction of the Temple in AD 70. However, some aspects remain in anticipation of Christ’s final return and the renewal of all things. 1. The Book of Daniel - God’s Sovereignty Over Kingdoms Key Passages: Daniel 2, 7, 9, 12 The book of Daniel is a mixture of historical narrative and apocalyptic prophecy. It was written during the Babylonian exile and reveals God's sovereignty over history and nations. Themes in Daniel: ● God’s Sovereignty: The rise and fall of kingdoms are under His control (Daniel 2:21). ● The Kingdom of God: Daniel 2 prophesies about a divine kingdom that will replace all human empires, which is fulfilled in Christ. ● The Son of Man: Daniel 7:13-14 speaks of “one like the Son of Man” given dominion, Jesus explicitly applies this to Himself (Matthew 26:64). 100

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Fulfilled Prophecy: Many of Daniel’s visions were fulfilled in the rise and fall of empires (Babylon, Medo-Persia, Greece, and Rome), and some were fulfilled in Christ’s first coming and the destruction of Jerusalem. ● The 70 Weeks (Daniel 9): This prophecy is often linked to the Messiah and the events leading up to the destruction of the Second Temple in AD 70. Devotional Reflection: ● Do you trust God’s sovereignty over history, including the challenges you face? ● Are you living with the confidence that Christ’s kingdom is already advancing? 2. The Book of Ezekiel - God’s Judgment and Restoration Key Passages: Ezekiel 1, 10, 37, 40-48 Ezekiel was a prophet during the Babylonian exile, and his visions conveyed God’s judgment on Israel for their rebellion and His promise of restoration. Themes in Ezekiel: ● God’s Glory Departing and Returning: Ezekiel 10 describes God’s glory departing the temple due to Israel’s sin, but chapters 40-48 speak of restoration. ● The Valley of Dry Bones (Ezekiel 37): A vision of Israel’s spiritual revival and return, fulfilled in part by their return from exile and ultimately in the new covenant through Christ. ● The New Temple (Ezekiel 40-48): A highly symbolic vision representing God’s restored presence among His people, ultimately fulfilled in Jesus as the true temple (John 2:19) and the Church as His dwelling place (Ephesians 2:20-22). Devotional Reflection: 101

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Have you allowed God to restore areas of spiritual dryness in your life? ● Are you living as a temple of the Holy Spirit, reflecting His glory? 3. The Book of Revelation - The Victory of Christ Key Passages: Revelation 1, 5, 13, 19-22 Revelation is not just about the future but is a letter of encouragement to the persecuted Church. It reveals Jesus as the victorious King and the fulfillment of God’s promises. Themes in Revelation: ● The Victory of Christ: Jesus is the Lamb who was slain but now reigns (Revelation 5). ● Symbolism and Imagery: Revelation is filled with symbols, many drawn from the Old Testament. ● The Beast and the Great Tribulation: Much of Revelation describes events surrounding the fall of Jerusalem in AD 70 , including persecution and divine judgment. ● The New Heaven and New Earth: Revelation 21-22 speaks of the final renewal when God dwells with His people. A More Balanced Perspective on Revelation: ● Much of Revelation was fulfilled in the first century , particularly in the fall of Jerusalem (Revelation 1:1, "things that must shortly come to pass"). ● However, the final chapters (21-22) look ahead to the complete renewal of creation at the return of Christ. Devotional Reflection: ● Are you living with the assurance that Christ is victorious? ● Does your life reflect the reality that His kingdom is advancing? 102

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Conclusion The prophetic books and apocalyptic literature reveal God’s sovereignty, faithfulness, and ultimate plan for redemption . While much of biblical prophecy has been fulfilled, we continue to live in the reality of Christ’s victory and look forward to the full manifestation of His reign. Assignment: 1. Read Daniel 7, Ezekiel 37, and Revelation 21. 2. Write down key insights about God’s kingdom and how they apply to your life today. 3. Listen to Pastor’s teachings on Eschatology Let us walk in the confidence that Jesus is King, and His kingdom is unshakable! 103

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 25 TOPIC: How to Rightly Divide the Word - Context, Exegesis vs. Eisegesis (2 Timothy 2:15) Introduction In 2 Timothy 2:15, the Apostle Paul urges believers to "rightly divide the word of truth." This is a call to approach Scripture with diligence, care, and reverence, ensuring that we understand and interpret it accurately. The study of God’s Word is not a casual or haphazard endeavor; it requires intentionality, humility, and a commitment to sound methods of interpretation. In this lesson, we will explore the importance of rightly dividing the Word of truth , focusing on the key aspects of context , exegesis , and eisegesis . Our aim is to equip you with tools for responsible biblical interpretation, so that you may handle Scripture with integrity and apply it correctly in your life. 1. The Importance of Context One of the first and most essential principles in interpreting the Bible is the importance of context . Context is key to understanding the meaning of any passage of Scripture. Without proper context, we can easily misinterpret what God is saying, leading to confusion or misapplication. A. Immediate Context The immediate context refers to the verses immediately surrounding the passage you are studying. A verse should never be taken in isolation; it must be understood in light of the verses before and after it. Always ask the questions: What is the immediate context? What is the flow of thought in the passage? For example, consider Philippians 4:13: "I can do all things through Christ who strengthens me." While this verse is often quoted to encourage personal achievement, we must remember that Paul was speaking in the 104

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: context of contentment in all circumstances, not necessarily success in every endeavor. The surrounding verses show that Paul is talking about endurance through hardship and the strength Christ provides to endure. B. Literary Context In addition to the immediate context, we also need to consider the literary context . Is the passage a historical narrative, a poetic song, a prophetic utterance, or a letter of instruction? Different literary genres in the Bible require different interpretative approaches. Understanding whether you are reading a parable, a prophecy, or a letter will shape your understanding. For example, when reading the Psalms, we must recognize that they are poetic and often contain figures of speech. In contrast, when reading historical narratives like the Book of Acts, the focus is on literal events and real people. C. Historical and Cultural Context We must also take into account the historical and cultural context of the time when the text was written. The Bible was written in specific historical and cultural settings, and understanding those can illuminate the meaning of certain passages. For instance, knowing the customs of ancient Israel helps us understand rituals in Levitical law, or understanding the Greco-Roman world helps explain many of Paul’s writings in the New Testament. 2. Exegesis vs. Eisegesis To rightly divide the Word of truth, we must understand the difference between exegesis and eisegesis , two approaches to interpreting Scripture. A. Exegesis - Drawing Out the Meaning Exegesis is the correct method of interpretation. It comes from the Greek word exēgeisthai , which means “to lead out” or “to explain.” In exegesis, the interpreter seeks to draw out the meaning of the text as it was 105

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: originally intended by the author, guided by the Holy Spirit. It involves careful study, including examining the context, language, grammar, and culture of the time. Key elements of exegesis include: ● Understanding the author’s intent : What did the author intend to communicate to the original audience? What are the key themes and messages of the passage? ● Looking at the original languages : Understanding the Hebrew, Greek, or Aramaic words can help clarify nuances in meaning. ● Consulting historical and cultural context : Understanding the time and culture in which the text was written is crucial for proper interpretation. Exegesis enables us to hear the Word of God as it was intended to be heard, with our focus on the meaning that God has given. B. Eisegesis - Reading Into the Text On the other hand, eisegesis is the wrong approach to interpreting the Bible. It comes from the Greek word eis meaning “into,” and it refers to reading our own ideas and biases into the text rather than drawing the meaning out of it. When we practice eisegesis , we approach Scripture with preconceived notions or agendas and force the text to fit our own desires or perspectives. This can lead to misinterpretations that distort the truth of God’s Word. For example, if we read our cultural preferences or personal desires into a passage, we may end up interpreting it in a way that suits our own preferences rather than allowing God’s message to speak directly to us. Eisegesis is dangerous because it distorts the true meaning of Scripture and leads to false teachings. 106

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 3. The Importance of Rightly Dividing the Word To rightly divide the Word of truth means to handle the Scriptures accurately and faithfully. We are responsible before God to interpret His Word correctly and to apply it in our lives. When we divide the Word properly: ● We avoid false doctrine and misleading teachings. ● We gain a clearer understanding of God’s will for our lives. ● We are better equipped to live out the teachings of Scripture. ● We fulfill our calling to be faithful stewards of the Word of God. As believers, it is essential that we engage in careful study of Scripture, striving to rightly divide the Word through prayer, humility, and reliance on the Holy Spirit. Devotional Reflection As you read and study the Bible, remember that it is not just a book, it is the living Word of God . Approach it with reverence, asking the Holy Spirit to guide you into all truth. When you come across difficult passages, take time to understand the context, and always seek to draw out the meaning rather than impose your own interpretation. Let us pray that God will give us wisdom to handle His Word faithfully, and that we will grow in our understanding of Him as we study His Word. Assignment 1. Read 2 Timothy 2:15 and reflect on what it means to "rightly divide the Word of truth." 2. Choose a passage you have found challenging and apply the principles of context , exegesis , and eisegesis . Write a brief reflection on what you learned from the passage after applying these principles. By the end of this study, may we all grow in our ability to handle God's Word rightly and live out His truth in our daily lives. 107

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Conclusion In conclusion, rightly dividing the Word of God requires diligence, humility, and a commitment to accuracy. As we seek to understand the Scriptures, let us avoid the temptation of reading into the text what is not there. Instead, let us seek to understand what God is saying to us through careful study, prayer, and guidance from the Holy Spirit. When we rightly divide the Word, we honor God and grow in our faith. 108

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 26 TOPIC: Types & Shadows in Scripture - Understanding Biblical Symbolism (Hebrews 10:1, Colossians 2:16-17) Introduction In the Christian faith, the Old Testament is not just a collection of ancient history or laws; it is full of deep, prophetic meanings that point to Jesus Christ. The Bible is rich with types and shadows , symbols and representations that find their ultimate fulfillment in the person and work of Jesus Christ. In the New Testament, we see that many events, people, and practices from the Old Testament were types or shadows that foreshadowed the reality of Christ's coming, death, and resurrection. In this lesson, we will explore what types and shadows are, and how understanding these biblical symbols enhances our comprehension of Scripture and deepens our relationship with Christ. 1. What are Types & Shadows? The terms types and shadows refer to figures, symbols, or representations found in the Old Testament that serve as a preview of greater, more significant realities revealed in the New Testament, especially in the life and work of Jesus Christ. These Old Testament figures or institutions were not the final or complete expression of God's plan, but rather they pointed to a fuller revelation of God's redemptive work in Christ. A. Types A type is a person, event, object, or institution in the Old Testament that serves as a symbol or representation of something greater that is revealed in the New Testament. It is a foreshadowing that finds its fulfillment in the New Testament in Christ. For example, Adam is a type of Christ. In Romans 5:14, Paul writes that Adam is a "type" of the One to come (Jesus Christ). While Adam’s 109

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: disobedience led to sin and death, Christ's obedience brought righteousness and life. Thus, Adam as a "type" foreshadows the work of Christ. B. Shadows A shadow is a lesser or incomplete representation of a more complete reality. Shadows can point to something to come, but they are not the full substance themselves. In Hebrews 10:1, the writer refers to the Old Testament sacrifices as shadows of the good things to come in Christ. Shadows are typically symbols that are temporary , incomplete , and preparatory . They reveal the outlines of something much more substantial, and they serve to direct our attention to the reality that would come in Christ. 2. Types & Shadows in the Old Testament Pointing to Christ Let’s consider some specific examples of types and shadows in the Old Testament that point to Christ: A. The Tabernacle (A Type of Christ's Work) The Tabernacle in the Old Testament is a type of Christ. The Tabernacle was the dwelling place of God's presence among His people. Its structure, sacrifices, and rituals all pointed forward to Jesus Christ, who is the ultimate fulfillment of God's dwelling among His people. ● The Bread of the Presence (Exodus 25:30) is a shadow of Jesus, the true Bread of Life (John 6:35). ● The sacrifices in the Tabernacle foreshadow the ultimate sacrifice of Jesus Christ on the cross, which would atone for the sins of humanity once for all (Hebrews 9:11-14). B. The Sacrificial System (A Shadow of Christ’s Sacrifice) The Old Testament sacrificial system is a shadow of Christ's ultimate sacrifice on the cross. The blood of animals offered for sin in the Old Testament could never fully cleanse people from sin, but it pointed to the greater sacrifice of Jesus' blood (Hebrews 10:1-4).

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: sacrificial system is a shadow of Christ's ultimate sacrifice on the cross. The blood of animals offered for sin in the Old Testament could never fully cleanse people from sin, but it pointed to the greater sacrifice of Jesus' blood (Hebrews 10:1-4). ● The Passover Lamb (Exodus 12) is a clear type of Christ, who is referred to as the Lamb of God (John 1:29), whose blood cleanses 110

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: us from sin. C. The Priesthood (A Shadow of Christ’s Mediatorship) The Levitical Priesthood was a shadow of Christ's eternal priesthood. The priests in the Old Testament mediated between God and the people through sacrifices, but Jesus, as our High Priest, made the ultimate sacrifice for sin and now intercedes for us before the Father (Hebrews 7:23-27). 3. Understanding the Fulfillment in Christ In the New Testament, we see that all the types and shadows of the Old Testament find their fulfillment in Jesus Christ . In Colossians 2:16-17 , Paul explains: “Let no one judge you in food or in drink, or regarding a festival or a new moon or sabbaths, which are a shadow of things to come, but the substance is of Christ.” Here, Paul makes it clear that the Old Testament observances, such as dietary laws, festivals, and the Sabbath, were shadows of the greater reality that is found in Christ. These rituals pointed to Christ, but they were never the final solution. Jesus is the substance or fulfillment of all that the Old Testament shadows represented. In Hebrews 10:1 , the writer emphasizes that the Old Testament law was a shadow of the good things to come, but it was not the reality itself. The law pointed forward to the work of Christ , who is the substance of the law's promises. 4. The Devotional Significance of Types and Shadows Understanding types and shadows in Scripture is not just a theological exercise, it has profound devotional implications for our daily lives. A. Drawing Near to God Through Christ 111

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: When we see the fulfillment of the Old Testament shadows in Christ, we are reminded of the nearness of God through Jesus. Christ is the true Tabernacle , the Lamb of God , the Great High Priest , and the final sacrifice . Through Him, we can draw near to God without the need for external rituals. We have direct access to the Father, and that access is made possible because Christ is the fulfillment of all the Old Testament types and shadows. B. Growing in Faith and Assurance Recognizing how the Old Testament points forward to Christ strengthens our faith in God’s redemptive plan. The types and shadows show us that God's plan has been unfolding over centuries, and every promise finds its “Yes” in Jesus Christ (2 Corinthians 1:20). When we see Jesus as the fulfillment of these ancient symbols, our faith grows stronger, knowing that God is faithful to His Word. C. Living in the Reality of Christ's Fulfillment As believers, we live in the reality of what the types and shadows only foreshadowed . We don’t need to offer sacrifices, follow strict ceremonial laws, or observe old feasts to maintain our relationship with God. Christ has fulfilled all of that, and we are invited to live in the freedom that He provides. This reality should lead to worship, gratitude, and a deeper relationship with God. Devotional Reflection As you meditate on the types and shadows in Scripture, let your heart be drawn closer to Christ. Remember, these symbols were given to point you to Him, the One who is the true fulfillment of everything the Old Testament foreshadowed. Let this understanding fill you with awe and wonder at God's plan of salvation, which spans all of history and finds its fulfillment in the person of Jesus Christ. 112

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Conclusion In conclusion, the study of types and shadows in Scripture enriches our understanding of the Bible and brings us closer to the heart of God’s redemptive plan. As we recognize how Old Testament symbols find their ultimate fulfillment in Christ, we are led into deeper worship, stronger faith, and greater appreciation for the work of salvation. Assignment 1. Study the story of Joseph in Genesis. Identify the ways in which he is a type of Christ. How do his life and actions point to Jesus? 2. Reflect on Hebrews 10:1 and Colossians 2:16-17. What Old Testament practices or symbols have you been focusing on that find their fulfillment in Christ? How does this change your perspective on those practices? 3. Journal about how understanding the types and shadows of Scripture deepens your relationship with Christ. May your understanding of Christ's fulfillment of the Old Testament types and shadows lead you into deeper intimacy with Him, and may your life reflect His grace and truth to the world around you. 113

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 27 TOPIC: The Covenants of God - Old vs. New Covenant (Jeremiah 31:31-34, Hebrews 8:6-13) Introduction A covenant is a divine agreement between God and His people, establishing the terms of their relationship. The Bible speaks of multiple covenants, but the Old and New Covenants stand as the foundation of God's redemptive plan. Understanding these covenants is key to rightly interpreting Scripture and applying it to our lives. 1. The Old Covenant - The Law Given to Israel Key Passages: Exodus 19-24, Deuteronomy 28, Jeremiah 31:31-32 The Old Covenant was established at Mount Sinai when God gave the Law to Israel through Moses. It was a covenant of obedience: blessings followed obedience, and curses followed disobedience (Deuteronomy 28). Characteristics of the Old Covenant: ● Based on the Law (613 commandments, including the Ten Commandments). ● Required sacrifices for atonement (Leviticus 16). ● Had a mediator, Moses (Exodus 24:8). ● Written on stone tablets (Exodus 31:18). ● Conditional - dependent on Israel’s obedience (Deuteronomy 28:1-2). The problem with the Old Covenant was not the Law itself but Israel’s inability to keep it (Hebrews 8:7-8). It pointed to the need for something greater, a covenant written on the heart. 2. The New Covenant - A Better Covenant Key Passages: Jeremiah 31:31-34, Hebrews 8:6-13, Luke 22:20 114

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: The New Covenant was prophesied in the Old Testament and fulfilled in Christ. It is a covenant of grace, mediated by Jesus, bringing an internal transformation rather than mere external adherence to laws. Characteristics of the New Covenant: ● Established through Jesus’ blood (Luke 22:20). ● The law is written on the heart, not on tablets of stone (Jeremiah 31:33). ● Provides full forgiveness of sins (Hebrews 8:12, 10:17-18). ● Has a perfect mediator, Jesus (Hebrews 8:6). ● Unconditional - based on God's promise, not human effort (Ephesians 2:8-9). The New Covenant fulfills the Old, not by abolishing the moral principles of God’s law, but by bringing about true righteousness through the Holy Spirit (Romans 8:3-4). 3. Comparing the Old and New Covenants Aspect Old Covenant New Covenant Mediator Moses Jesus Christ (Hebrews 8:6) Law Written On Stone tablets Human hearts (Jeremiah 31:33) Sacrifices Repeated animal sacrifices Christ's once-for-all sacrifice (Hebrews 10:10-12) Access to God Through priests Direct access through Christ (Hebrews 4:16) Forgiveness Temporary, through animal sacrifices Permanent, through Christ’s blood (Hebrews 10:17-18) 115

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 4. Application - Living in the New Covenant As believers, we must walk in the reality of the New Covenant, understanding that our righteousness is in Christ, not in works of the law. However, this does not mean we live lawlessly, but rather, we obey God from a transformed heart (Titus 2:11-12). Devotional Reflection: ● Are you living in the freedom and grace of the New Covenant? ● Do you still relate to God based on performance, or do you rest in Christ’s finished work? Conclusion The Old Covenant served its purpose in revealing sin and pointing to Christ, but the New Covenant brings the fullness of salvation and an unbreakable relationship with God. As believers, we are called to live in the reality of the New Covenant, walking in grace, truth, and the power of the Spirit. Assignment: 1. Read Hebrews 8-10 and reflect on how the New Covenant surpasses the Old. 2. Write a personal response on how the New Covenant impacts your daily life. 3. Share insights with your study group. In Christ, we have a better covenant built on better promises! (Hebrews 8:6). 116

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 28 TOPIC: Applying Scripture to Daily Life - Living by the Word (Psalm 119:105, James 1:22) Introduction The Bible is not just a book to be read; it is the living Word of God meant to guide, shape, and transform our lives. Psalm 119:105 declares, "Thy word is a lamp unto my feet, and a light unto my path." This means that God’s Word provides direction for every step we take in life. James 1:22 reminds us, "But be ye doers of the word, and not hearers only, deceiving your own selves." It is not enough to hear the Word; we must actively apply it to our daily lives. The Power of God’s Word in Our Lives 1. God’s Word Transforms Us ○ Romans 12:2 tells us not to be conformed to this world but to be transformed by the renewing of our minds. The Word of God reshapes our thoughts, desires, and actions. ○ Meditating on Scripture allows us to develop Christ-like character (2 Corinthians 3:18). 2. God’s Word Directs Our Steps ○ The Bible serves as a spiritual GPS, guiding us in decisions, relationships, and daily challenges. ○ When faced with uncertainty, Scripture provides wisdom (Proverbs 3:5-6). 3. God’s Word Guards Our Hearts ○ Psalm 119:11 says, "Thy word have I hid in mine heart, that I might not sin against thee." Storing Scripture in our hearts equips us to resist temptation. 117

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ○ Jesus overcame temptation by speaking the Word (Matthew 4:1-11). 4. God’s Word Brings Fruitfulness ○ A life built on Scripture produces spiritual growth (John 15:7-8). ○ The Word nurtures our faith, making us effective witnesses of Christ (2 Timothy 3:16-17). Practical Ways to Apply Scripture Daily 1. Read and Meditate on the Word Daily ○ Set aside time each day to read the Bible and reflect on its meaning. ○ Start with a verse, a chapter, or a structured Bible reading plan. 2. Pray the Scriptures ○ Turn Bible verses into personal prayers. For example, praying Psalm 23 can bring comfort and assurance. ○ Declare God’s promises over your life (Philippians 4:6-7). 3. Obey the Word in Your Actions ○ Don’t just read or memorize Scripture, put it into practice. ○ If the Word says to forgive, then forgive (Colossians 3:13). If it says to love, then love (1 Corinthians 13:4-7). 4. Share the Word with Others ○ Encourage someone with a Scripture verse. ○ Teach and disciple others in the truth of God's Word (Matthew 28:19-20). 5. Apply Scripture in Difficult Times 118

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ○ When facing trials, find strength in God's promises (Isaiah 41:10, Romans 8:28). ○ Let Scripture be your source of encouragement and guidance. Devotional Reflection Take a moment to reflect: Is God’s Word the foundation of your daily life? Are you merely hearing the Word, or are you living by it? Ask the Holy Spirit to help you apply Scripture in every aspect of your life. Commit to making the Bible not just a book you read, but a life you live. Assignment 1. Read Psalm 119:1-16 and write down three ways you can apply the Word to your daily life. 2. Memorize James 1:22 and share how it challenges you to be a doer of the Word. Conclusion Living by the Word means more than just reading it, it means allowing it to shape our thoughts, decisions, and actions. As we embrace God’s Word as our daily guide, we will grow in faith, wisdom, and effectiveness as followers of Christ. May we be not just hearers, but doers of the Word! 119

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: TERM 3: DOCTRINE & SPIRITUAL DISCIPLINES (Weeks 29-41) Module 7: Key Christian Doctrines (Weeks 29-35) 120

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 29 TOPIC: The Trinity - Understanding the Godhead (Matthew 28:19, 2 Corinthians 13:14) Introduction The doctrine of the Trinity is one of the most profound and foundational teachings of the Christian faith. It defines the nature of God as three distinct Persons, Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, yet one in essence. Matthew 28:19 affirms this truth: "Go ye therefore, and teach all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost." Likewise, 2 Corinthians 13:14 expresses the relational dynamic within the Godhead: "The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the communion of the Holy Ghost, be with you all." Understanding the Trinity is essential for our faith because it shapes how we relate to God, worship Him, and understand His work in salvation. I. The Biblical Basis for the Trinity Though the word "Trinity" is not found in Scripture, the concept is deeply embedded in biblical revelation. 1. The Father is God - Jesus referred to the Father as God (John 6:27, 1 Peter 1:2). 2. The Son is God - Jesus is called God (John 1:1, Hebrews 1:8). 3. The Holy Spirit is God - He is identified as God and possesses divine attributes (Acts 5:3-4, 1 Corinthians 2:10-11). 121

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 4. The Three are Distinct Yet One - At Jesus’ baptism (Matthew 3:16-17), all three Persons of the Trinity are present and active. II. Understanding the Oneness and Distinction in the Godhead A. One in Essence, Three in Person ● The Trinity is not three gods (polytheism) nor one God acting in different modes (modalism). The Father, Son, and Spirit are distinct yet share the same divine nature. ● Analogy: Water exists as ice, liquid, and vapor but remains H ₂ O. Though imperfect, this analogy helps illustrate the unity and distinction within the Godhead. B. The Roles of Each Person in Redemption ● The Father planned salvation and sent the Son (John 3:16, Ephesians 1:3-6). ● The Son accomplished salvation through His life, death, and resurrection (John 10:11, 1 John 2:2). ● The Holy Spirit applies salvation by regenerating, indwelling, and sanctifying believers (John 14:26, Titus 3:5). III. The Trinity in Christian Living 1. Prayer and Worship - We pray to the Father, through the Son, by the Holy Spirit (Ephesians 2:18). 2. Christian Fellowship - The unity in the Godhead models our unity in the body of Christ (John 17:21). 3. Empowerment for Ministry - The Spirit empowers us to live as witnesses of Christ (Acts 1:8). 4. Security in Salvation - The Father secures, the Son intercedes, and the Spirit seals us (Romans 8:33-39, Ephesians 1:13-14). IV. Devotional Reflection 122

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: The Trinity reveals God’s deep love and commitment to His people. Each Person of the Godhead is actively involved in our salvation and sanctification. Take a moment to reflect: ● Do you recognize the Father’s love for you? ● Have you placed full trust in the finished work of Christ? ● Are you walking in the power of the Holy Spirit? The Trinity is not just a theological concept; it is the reality of our relationship with God. Assignment 1. Read John 14:16-26 and identify the roles of each Person of the Trinity in our lives. 2. Memorize 2 Corinthians 13:14 and reflect on how you have experienced the grace, love, and fellowship of the Trinity. Conclusion The doctrine of the Trinity is at the heart of the Christian faith. As we grow in our understanding of God’s triune nature, we grow in intimacy with Him. Let us worship and serve God with the knowledge that He is Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, three in one, and one in three. Amen. 123

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 30 The Nature of Jesus Christ - Fully God, Fully Man (John 1:1-14, Colossians 2:9) Introduction One of the most profound truths of the Christian faith is the dual nature of Jesus Christ, He is fully God and fully man. This is not a contradiction but a divine mystery that holds essential significance for our salvation, relationship with God, and understanding of Christ’s present work in heaven. John 1:1 declares, "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God." Then verse 14 confirms, "And the Word was made flesh, and dwelt among us…" Likewise, Colossians 2:9 states, "For in him dwelleth all the fulness of the Godhead bodily." These passages affirm both His divine and human natures. Jesus Christ as Fully Man During His Earthly Ministry 1. His Birth in the Flesh ○ Jesus was born of a virgin, fulfilling Isaiah’s prophecy (Isaiah 7:14; Matthew 1:23). ○ He experienced human limitations, hunger (Matthew 4:2), weariness (John 4:6), and sorrow (John 11:35). 2. His Human Emotions and Temptations ○ Jesus felt deep emotions: compassion (Matthew 9:36), joy (Luke 10:21), and distress (Luke 22:44). ○ He was tempted in all ways yet without sin (Hebrews 4:15). 3. His Dependence on the Father and the Spirit ○ Jesus operated within human limitations, relying on the Holy Spirit (Luke 4:1, 18). 124

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ○ He prayed constantly, modeling dependence on God (Mark 1:35; Luke 5:16). 4. His Death as a True Human Sacrifice ○ His humanity enabled Him to suffer and die as our atoning sacrifice (Philippians 2:8; Hebrews 2:14-17). ○ His physical death on the cross was necessary for our redemption (1 Peter 3:18). Jesus Christ as Fully God During His Earthly Ministry 1. His Divine Authority ○ Jesus forgave sins, a prerogative of God alone (Mark 2:5-7). ○ He commanded nature, calming the storm (Mark 4:39) and walking on water (Matthew 14:25). 2. His Power Over Death and Demons ○ He raised the dead, demonstrating divine power (John 11:43-44). ○ Demons recognized His divine authority (Mark 1:24). 3. His Self-Identification as God ○ He declared, "Before Abraham was, I am" (John 8:58), claiming eternal existence. ○ Thomas worshiped Him as "My Lord and my God" (John 20:28), and Jesus accepted it. The Exalted Christ - His Present Role in Heaven 1. His Exaltation and Lordship ○ After His resurrection, Jesus ascended to heaven and was seated at the right hand of the Father (Acts 1:9-11; Ephesians 1:20-21). 125

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
