text: ○ Philippians 2:9-11 declares that He has been given a name above all names, and every knee shall bow before Him. 2. His Role as High Priest ○ Jesus intercedes for believers before the Father (Hebrews 7:25; Romans 8:34). ○ As our High Priest, He provides mercy and grace in times of need (Hebrews 4:14-16). 3. His Ongoing Work in the Church ○ Christ, as the head of the Church, directs and empowers His body (Colossians 1:18). 4. His Eternal Kingship ○ Jesus reigns now and will consummate His rule upon His return (Revelation 19:11-16). ○ His kingdom is everlasting (Daniel 7:14; 1 Corinthians 15:24-28). Devotional Reflection The dual nature of Christ is not just theology, it is the foundation of our faith. As fully man, He understands our struggles, and as fully God, He has the power to save, sustain, and lead us. Because He lived as we live, He is a compassionate High Priest. Because He reigns as God, He is worthy of our worship and obedience. Ask yourself: Do I relate to Jesus as my understanding Savior? Do I honor Him as my reigning King? Assignment 1. Read Philippians 2:5-11 and write down three ways Christ’s humility and exaltation impact your faith. 126

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 2. Spend time in prayer, thanking Jesus for both His humanity and deity. Conclusion Jesus Christ is fully God and fully man, this is the mystery and beauty of our faith. He walked among us, suffered for us, and now reigns for us. As we grow in our understanding of His nature, let us worship Him with awe and follow Him with unwavering devotion. May our lives reflect the truth of who He is! “Jesus Christ the same yesterday, and to day, and for ever.” - Hebrews 13:8 127

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 31 TOPIC:The Role of the Holy Spirit - Conviction, Empowerment, Gifts (Acts 1:8, 1 Corinthians 12:4-11) Introduction The Holy Spirit is the active presence of God in the believer’s life, working to convict, empower, and distribute spiritual gifts for the edification of the Church. Jesus promised His disciples the gift of the Holy Spirit to guide them into all truth and to equip them for the mission of spreading the Gospel (John 16:7-13). Understanding the role of the Holy Spirit is essential for living a victorious Christian life. 1. The Holy Spirit Convicts John 16:8 - "And when he is come, he will reprove the world of sin, and of righteousness, and of judgment." One of the primary roles of the Holy Spirit is to bring conviction: ● Conviction of Sin - The Holy Spirit makes sinners aware of their need for salvation. ● Conviction of Righteousness - He reveals the righteousness found in Christ and calls believers to live holy lives. ● Conviction of Judgment - He reminds the world of the certainty of judgment and the authority of Christ over Satan. The conviction of the Holy Spirit leads to true repentance, drawing people closer to God and away from sin. 2. The Holy Spirit Empowers Acts 1:8 - "But ye shall receive power, after that the Holy Ghost is come upon you: and ye shall be witnesses unto me both in Jerusalem, and in all Judaea, and in Samaria, and unto the uttermost part of the earth." 128

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: The Holy Spirit provides supernatural power for: ● Bold Witnessing - The disciples, once fearful, became bold preachers of the Gospel after receiving the Holy Spirit (Acts 2:1-4, Acts 4:31). ● Overcoming Sin and Flesh - The Spirit enables believers to live holy and victorious lives (Galatians 5:16-17). ● Spiritual Understanding - He illuminates the Scriptures, helping believers understand God’s Word (John 14:26). The empowerment of the Holy Spirit is not for personal gain but for advancing God’s kingdom on earth. 3. The Holy Spirit Distributes Spiritual Gifts 1 Corinthians 12:4-7 - "Now there are diversities of gifts, but the same Spirit. And there are differences of administrations, but the same Lord. And there are diversities of operations, but it is the same God which worketh all in all." The Holy Spirit gives various gifts to believers for the benefit of the Church: ● Revelation Gifts - Word of Wisdom, Word of Knowledge, Discerning of Spirits. ● Power Gifts - Faith, Gifts of Healing, Working of Miracles. ● Utterance Gifts - Prophecy, Divers Kinds of Tongues, Interpretation of Tongues. These gifts are not given based on merit but according to the will of the Holy Spirit (1 Corinthians 12:11). Every believer has a unique gift meant to edify and strengthen the body of Christ. Devotional Reflection 129

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Are you allowing the Holy Spirit to convict, empower, and work through you? The Holy Spirit is not a distant force but an intimate guide, helper, and comforter in your walk with Christ. Surrendering to His leading will bring transformation, boldness, and fruitfulness in your life. Assignment 1. Read Acts 2 and write down how the Holy Spirit transformed the early church. Conclusion The Holy Spirit’s role is indispensable in the life of a believer. He convicts the world of sin, empowers us for service, and equips us with spiritual gifts. As we yield to His leading, we will experience transformation and be effective witnesses for Christ. Let us walk daily in the power and presence of the Holy Spirit! 130

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 32 TOPIC: The Church - Universal & Local - The Body of Christ (1 Corinthians 12:12-27) Introduction The Church is not a building, denomination, or institution; it is the body of Christ. The Bible presents the Church in two main ways: the Universal Church, which includes all believers across time and space, and the Local Church, which is a specific gathering of believers in a particular place. Understanding the nature and function of the Church helps believers to walk in unity, purpose, and love. The Universal Church: The Body of Christ 1. What is the Universal Church? ○ The Universal Church consists of all people who have been born again by faith in Jesus Christ (Ephesians 2:19-22). ○ It transcends culture, nationality, and denominational lines (Galatians 3:28). ○ It is described as the “Bride of Christ” (Revelation 19:7-9). 2. Christ as the Head of the Church ○ Christ is the foundation and head of the Church (Colossians 1:18). ○ He purchased the Church with His blood (Acts 20:28). ○ The Church exists to glorify Christ and fulfill His mission (Matthew 28:19-20). 3. The Church as One Body with Many Members ○ Paul describes the Church as a body with different parts functioning together (1 Corinthians 12:12-27). ○ Every believer has a role to play (Romans 12:4-5). 131

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ○ Unity in diversity is the strength of the Church (Ephesians 4:3-6). The Local Church: A Place of Fellowship and Growth 1. What is the Local Church? ○ A local church is a gathering of believers in a specific community (Acts 2:42-47). ○ It provides discipleship, fellowship, worship, and ministry. ○ The local church functions under spiritual leadership (Hebrews 13:17). 2. The Importance of Belonging to a Local Church ○ Encourages spiritual growth (Ephesians 4:11-16). ○ Provides accountability and pastoral care (Hebrews 10:24-25). ○ Equips believers for service and ministry (Ephesians 4:12). 3. Responsibilities of Members in a Local Church ○ Commitment to sound doctrine (Acts 2:42). ○ Participation in corporate worship and prayer (Hebrews 10:25). ○ Serving in love and humility (Galatians 5:13). ○ Financial stewardship and giving (2 Corinthians 9:7). Devotional Reflection The Church is not a spectator event; it is a living, breathing body of believers working together under the lordship of Jesus Christ. Are you actively engaged in your local church? Do you recognize your role in the body of Christ? The Church is God's chosen vessel to reveal His wisdom and love to the world (Ephesians 3:10). Commit yourself today to being a faithful and active part of His body. Assignment 132

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 1. Read 1 Corinthians 12 and reflect on what part of the body you identify with. 2. Write down three ways you can serve more effectively in your local church. 3. Pray for the unity and growth of both the Universal and Local Church. Conclusion The Church is God's instrument for His purposes on earth. The Universal Church represents the entire body of believers, while the Local Church provides a community for worship, teaching, and spiritual growth. As members of Christ’s body, we are called to function in love, unity, and service. May we commit ourselves fully to the Church, for it is the hope of the world, reflecting the glory of Christ! 133

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 33 TOPIC: The Second Coming & Eternal Judgment - Understanding the Last Things (1 Thessalonians 4:16-17, Revelation 20:11-15) Introduction The doctrine of the Second Coming of Jesus Christ and Eternal Judgment is central to Christian faith and eschatology. It brings hope, assurance, and a call to live righteously in anticipation of the future fulfillment of God’s promises. Understanding these events through a biblical lens helps believers to live with an eternal perspective, grounded in the reality of both future glory and future judgment. As we explore this TOPIC, we will discuss the return of Christ, the final judgment, and the distinction between the Judgment Seat of Christ for believers and the White Throne Judgment for unbelievers. As bible students, we recognize that some aspects of biblical prophecy have already been fulfilled in the past, yet the future hope of Christ's return and the eternal judgment remain as certain events that will unfold. The Second Coming of Christ - The Hope of the Believer The second coming of Christ is the moment when Jesus returns in glory to establish His eternal Kingdom. This is the consummation of God's redemptive plan for humanity. In 1 Thessalonians 4:16-17 , Paul describes this event as the "coming of the Lord" in which He will descend from heaven with a shout, with the voice of the archangel, and with the trumpet of God. At that moment, the dead in Christ will rise first, and those who are alive and remain will be caught up together with them in the clouds to meet the Lord in the air. This promise of Christ’s return is not just a future event, it is a living hope that sustains believers through the trials and challenges of life. It reminds us that our present struggles are not the end of the story.

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: uds to meet the Lord in the air. This promise of Christ’s return is not just a future event, it is a living hope that sustains believers through the trials and challenges of life. It reminds us that our present struggles are not the end of the story. As we wait for His 134

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: return, we are called to remain faithful, expectant, and diligent in our walk with God. The White Throne Judgment - A Judgment for Unbelievers In Revelation 20:11-15 , we are given a sobering picture of the White Throne Judgment, where all the dead are judged according to their works. This judgment is not for believers but for unbelievers, those who have rejected Christ and His offer of salvation. John describes the scene where the dead, both great and small, stand before God, and books are opened. The ultimate book, the Book of Life , is also opened. Anyone whose name is not found in the Book of Life is cast into the lake of fire, which is the second death. The Book of Life represents Christ Himself, as He is the source of eternal life. Therefore, those whose names are not found in the Book of Life are those who have not been reconciled to God through Jesus Christ. It’s not merely a list of names but a representation of one's relationship with Christ. Since those who reject Christ are already condemned (John 3:18), this judgment simply confirms their decision to reject the grace and salvation offered through Jesus. Key Truths to Remember: ● The White Throne Judgment is not for believers; believers will not stand before this judgment. ● It is for the unsaved, those who have rejected Christ's salvation. ● Being excluded from the Book of Life signifies rejection of Christ, and such individuals face eternal separation from God. The Judgment Seat of Christ - A Judgment for Believers While the White Throne Judgment is for unbelievers, believers will face a different kind of judgment known as the Judgment Seat of Christ (also called the Bema Seat ). This is where believers will give an account of their lives before Christ, not for the purpose of condemnation, but for the 135

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: purpose of reward. 2 Corinthians 5:10 tells us that we must all appear before the Judgment Seat of Christ so that each may receive what is due for the things done while in the body, whether good or bad. The Judgment Seat of Christ is not about determining salvation, that has already been settled by Christ’s sacrifice on the cross, but about rewarding believers for their faithfulness in service. This judgment will be based on how we have lived out our calling, our obedience to God’s Word, and our service in the Kingdom. 1 Corinthians 3:12-15 explains that the quality of each person's work will be revealed by fire, and if what they have built survives, they will receive a reward. If not, they will suffer loss, though they themselves will be saved. Key Truths to Remember: ● The Judgment Seat of Christ is for believers and will take place after the rapture. ● It is a judgment of works, not salvation. ● Believers will be rewarded according to their faithfulness in service to Christ and His Kingdom. The Eternal Implications of the Judgment Both judgments, whether the White Throne Judgment or the Judgment Seat of Christ, remind us of the eternal significance of our decisions today. The White Throne Judgment warns of the eternal consequences of rejecting Christ, leading to eternal separation from God. It is a call for evangelism and an urgency to share the gospel, for we know that those who do not accept Christ are already condemned. On the other hand, the Judgment Seat of Christ encourages believers to live lives that honor God, knowing that we will give an account of our lives. It motivates us to serve faithfully, not out of fear, but with the joy of knowing that we will be rewarded for our obedience. 136

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Devotional Reflection As you reflect on these truths, consider the eternal implications of your choices: ● Are you living with a sense of urgency to share the gospel, knowing that those who do not know Christ are headed for the White Throne Judgment? ● Are you living with the hope of the Judgment Seat of Christ, knowing that you will be rewarded for your faithfulness? ● How does the knowledge of Christ’s return and the eternal judgment shape your daily walk with God? Let us live each day with the awareness that our actions have eternal significance. May we be found faithful, eagerly awaiting the return of our Savior. Assignment 1. Read Revelation 20:11-15 and write down your understanding of the White Throne Judgment. 2. Read 2 Corinthians 5:10 and reflect on the Judgment Seat of Christ. What motivates you to live a life of faithful service? 3. Memorize 1 Thessalonians 4:16-17 and share with a fellow believer the hope of the Second Coming of Christ. 4. Listen to Pastor’s teachings on Eschatology Conclusion The Second Coming of Christ and the eternal judgment are profound truths that shape how we live today. For the believer, it is a reminder to live with a sense of responsibility, knowing that our works will be judged and rewarded. For the unbeliever, it is a call to repentance and faith in Christ, for the White Throne Judgment is a fearful reality. Let us live in light of eternity, keeping our eyes fixed on Jesus, our hope of glory. 137

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 34 TOPIC: Understanding Grace & Works - The Balance (Ephesians 2:8-10, James 2:17) Introduction The relationship between grace and works is one of the most significant and often misunderstood aspects of the Christian faith. On one hand, we are saved by grace alone, not by anything we can do (Ephesians 2:8-9). On the other hand, true faith always results in good works, as we are created in Christ Jesus to do them (Ephesians 2:10; James 2:17). The tension between these two truths, grace and works, can sometimes confuse believers, but understanding the balance is key to living out the Christian faith effectively. Understanding Grace 1. Grace is God’s Unmerited Favor ○ Ephesians 2:8-9 makes it clear that salvation is a gift from God. It is not earned or deserved by human effort. Grace is God’s unmerited favor towards us, offered freely, without any strings attached. ○ Grace is what enables us to stand before God. It is through grace that we are saved, and it is through grace that we are transformed into new creations in Christ (2 Corinthians 5:17). 2. Grace and Salvation ○ The Bible teaches that salvation is by grace alone, through faith alone, in Christ alone. Ephesians 2:8 says, "For by grace are ye saved through faith; and that not of yourselves: it is the gift of God." 138

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ○ Grace frees us from the penalty of sin, but it also empowers us to live a godly life. Grace is not just about the initial act of salvation but also about God's ongoing work in our lives, shaping us to reflect Christ. 3. Grace is Empowering ○ Titus 2:11-12 reminds us that the grace of God teaches us to live righteous and godly lives. Grace is not a license to sin, but the power to live in a way that honors God. ○ The Apostle Paul talks about grace enabling him to do the work of ministry: "But by the grace of God I am what I am" (1 Corinthians 15:10). Grace is the strength to fulfill God’s calling on our lives. Understanding Works 1. Works Cannot Save Us ○ Ephesians 2:9 clearly tells us that salvation is not by works: "Not of works, lest any man should boast." This emphasizes that no human effort, no matter how good, can earn salvation. ○ Romans 3:20 and Titus 3:5 affirm this, works, no matter how moral or righteous they seem, cannot justify us before God. 2. Works are the Evidence of True Faith ○ While works cannot save us, true faith produces good works. James 2:17 says, "Even so faith, if it hath not works, is dead, being alone." Genuine faith is demonstrated through action. Without works, faith is incomplete. ○ Jesus Himself taught that a tree is known by its fruit (Matthew 7:17-20). In the same way, our lives should reflect the reality of the grace we’ve received, leading to acts of 139

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: service, love, and obedience. 3. Works are a Response to Grace ○ Good works are a natural outflow of the grace we’ve received. In Ephesians 2:10 , we are reminded that we are God’s workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared beforehand that we should walk in them. ○ Our works are not a means to earn God’s favor, but they are a way to show our gratitude for the grace we have received. We serve because we’ve been served; we love because we’ve been loved. The Balance Between Grace and Works 1. Grace First, Then Works ○ Grace comes first. We are saved by grace, and then, as a result of that grace, we are empowered to do good works. Ephesians 2:10 makes this clear: we were created in Christ Jesus for good works, not by good works. ○ James 2:22 shows us how Abraham's faith and works were interconnected. His faith was demonstrated by his actions, but his faith preceded his works. In other words, we don't work to get saved; we work because we are saved. 2. Grace Leads to Transformation ○ Grace doesn’t leave us where we are. It changes us. It leads us to live differently, in a way that reflects Christ. As Philippians 2:13 says, "For it is God which worketh in you both to will and to do of his good pleasure." The transformation produced by grace is visible through our works. 3. Works Must Be Done in the Right Heart 140

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ○ Matthew 6:1-4 reminds us that works should be done with the right heart, not for self-glory, but for God’s glory. The motivation behind our works matters. If we are doing good deeds to be seen by others, we miss the point. ○ Our works must flow out of a heart of love for God and others. They should reflect the love, grace, and mercy that we have received. Practical Application 1. Live Out Your Faith ○ In practical terms, apply the grace you’ve received by loving others, serving those in need, and living a life of integrity and holiness. Your works should be a reflection of the grace that has changed your heart. ○ Take time to examine your own life: Are there areas where you’re trying to work for God’s approval, rather than working out of gratitude for the grace He’s already given? 2. Be Active in Good Works ○ Let your faith be active. Involve yourself in the service of others. Reach out to the hurting, give generously, and be involved in your local church’s mission. ○ Remember that your good works are a testimony of God’s love and grace to the world around you. Devotional Reflection Consider how grace and works are intertwined in your own life. Are you relying on grace alone for salvation, or are you also relying on your own works? Remember, works don’t save us, but they demonstrate the life-changing power of grace in our lives. Let us, therefore, be doers of the Word, demonstrating our faith through love and service. 141

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Assignment 1. Reflect on your life and identify one area where you can live out the good works God has prepared for you. 2. Write down a prayer of thanksgiving, thanking God for His grace, and asking Him to help you demonstrate that grace in your actions. 3. Share with a fellow believer how grace has transformed your life and the role of good works in expressing your faith. Conclusion Understanding the balance between grace and works is essential for the Christian life. Grace is the foundation; it is the starting point of our salvation and transformation. Works are the natural outcome of that grace, a demonstration of our faith in action. Let us rest in the grace of God while living out our faith through good works. As we do, we bring glory to God and fulfill His purposes for our lives. 142

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: TERM 3: DOCTRINE & SPIRITUAL DISCIPLINES (Weeks 29-41) Module 8: Christian Disciplines (Weeks 36-41) 143

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 35 TOPIC: Spiritual Gifts and Calling Discovering and Operating in Gifts Introduction Spiritual gifts are divine abilities given by the Holy Spirit to every believer for the edification of the body of Christ. Understanding these gifts helps believers function effectively in their calling and contribute to the mission of the Church. This study will explore: 1. The Nature of Spiritual Gifts (Romans 12:6-8, 1 Corinthians 12:4-11) 2. The Distribution and Operation of Gifts 3. Discovering and Functioning in Spiritual Gifts 4. The Purpose of Spiritual Gifts in the Church 1. The Nature of Spiritual Gifts Key Texts: Romans 12:6-8; 1 Corinthians 12:4-11 1. Spiritual gifts are given to every believer 1 Corinthians 12:7 - "But the manifestation of the Spirit is given to every man to profit withal." Every believer possesses spiritual gifts for the benefit of the body of Christ. 2. There are different kinds of gifts, but the same Spirit 1 Corinthians 12:4-6 - "Now there are diversities of gifts, but the same Spirit. And there are differences of administrations, but the same Lord." 144

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Though believers have different functions, all gifts come from the same Holy Spirit. 3. Gifts function by grace and faith Romans 12:6 - "Having then gifts differing according to the grace that is given to us, whether prophecy, let us prophesy according to the proportion of faith." Gifts operate based on grace and require faith to function effectively. 2. The Distribution and Operation of Gifts Key Text: 1 Corinthians 12:8-11 A. Every Believer Has Access to Spiritual Gifts ● The Holy Spirit dwells in all believers, meaning every believer has access to the gifts. 1 Corinthians 12:7 - "The manifestation of the Spirit is given to every man to profit withal." B. Do All Believers Have All Gifts? 1 Corinthians 12:8-10 lists gifts such as wisdom, knowledge, faith, healing, miracles, prophecy, discernment, tongues, and interpretation. "To one is given… to another…" This does not mean some gifts are exclusive to certain individuals, but that the Spirit distributes gifts based on the needs of the body at a given time. 1 Corinthians 12:11 - "But all these worketh that one and the selfsame Spirit, dividing to every man severally as he will." ● Balanced View: - Every believer has the gifts of the Spirit. 145

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: - The Holy Spirit determines when and how gifts manifest. - Believers may operate more frequently in certain gifts, but no gift is permanently exclusive to an individual. However, those in the fivefold ministry require specific gifts to effectively fulfill their calling. 3. Discovering and Functioning in Spiritual Gifts A. Desire and Yielding to the Holy Spirit 1 Corinthians 14:1 - "Follow after charity, and desire spiritual gifts, but rather that ye may prophesy." Believers are encouraged to desire spiritual gifts, especially those that edify the Church. 2 Timothy 1:6 - "Wherefore I put thee in remembrance that thou stir up the gift of God, which is in thee by the putting on of my hands." Gifts must be stirred up through faith and practice. B. Identifying Spiritual Gifts ● By Function in the Body ○ Romans 12:6-8 outlines gifts such as teaching, exhortation, giving, leadership, and mercy. ○ A believer can identify their gifts by recognizing how God uses them to serve others. ● By the Leading of the Holy Spirit ○ The Spirit often reveals gifts during times of prayer, service, and fellowship. 4. The Purpose of Spiritual Gifts in the Church 146

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Key Text: 1 Corinthians 14:26 1. Gifts Are for Edification 1 Corinthians 12:7 - "The manifestation of the Spirit is given to every man to profit withal." Gifts benefit the entire church, not just the individual. 2. Gifts Function in Church Gatherings 1 Corinthians 14:26 - "How is it then, brethren? when ye come together, every one of you hath a psalm, hath a doctrine, hath a tongue, hath a revelation, hath an interpretation. Let all things be done unto edifying." Gifts manifest as needed to build up the body. Conclusion: ● Spiritual gifts are given to every believer. ● The Spirit determines how and when gifts operate. ● Every believer has access to all gifts, but some may function more frequently in certain gifts. ● Gifts are for the edification of the Church and should be stirred up by faith. Application Questions: 1. What gifts do you recognize operating in your life? 2. How can you stir up the gifts God has given you? 3. How can you use your gifts to edify the body of Christ? Memory Verse: 1 Corinthians 12:7 - "But the manifestation of the Spirit is given to every man to profit withal." 147

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Prayer: Lord, help me to recognize and stir up the gifts You have placed in me. Let me be a vessel for Your work and a blessing to the Church. Amen. 148

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 36 The Power of Fasting - Spiritual Benefits of Fasting Key Texts: Isaiah 58:6-8, Matthew 6:16-18 Introduction Fasting is a powerful spiritual discipline that brings believers into deeper intimacy with God. It is not merely abstaining from food but a deliberate act of seeking God with sincerity and humility. Throughout Scripture, fasting is presented as a means to experience spiritual breakthroughs, divine direction, and increased intimacy with God. This study will explore: 1. The Biblical Purpose of Fasting 2. The Spiritual Benefits of Fasting 3. The Proper Attitude in Fasting 4. How to Fast Effectively 1. The Biblical Purpose of Fasting Key Text: Isaiah 58:6 - "Is not this the fast that I have chosen? to loose the bands of wickedness, to undo the heavy burdens, and to let the oppressed go free, and that ye break every yoke?" Fasting serves several divine purposes: ● Spiritual Renewal: It brings a believer into a state of spiritual sensitivity and deeper connection with God. ● Deliverance: It breaks chains of oppression and spiritual bondage. ● Repentance and Humility: It demonstrates humility before God and genuine repentance. ● Divine Intervention: It opens doors for God’s supernatural help in challenging situations (Esther 4:16, 2 Chronicles 20:3). 149

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Empowerment for Ministry: Jesus fasted before starting His public ministry (Luke 4:1-14). 2. The Spiritual Benefits of Fasting Key Text: Isaiah 58:8 - "Then shall thy light break forth as the morning, and thine health shall spring forth speedily: and thy righteousness shall go before thee; the glory of the Lord shall be thy rereward." A. Greater Spiritual Clarity and Sensitivity ● Fasting tunes out distractions, allowing believers to hear God's voice more clearly. ● Daniel received divine revelations during a period of fasting (Daniel 9:3, 10:2-3). B. Victory Over the Flesh ● It disciplines the body, helping believers overcome fleshly desires (Galatians 5:16-17). ● Jesus overcame temptation after fasting for 40 days (Matthew 4:1-11). C. Breakthroughs in Prayer ● Certain strongholds are broken through fasting and prayer (Matthew 17:21). ● Many biblical figures fasted for divine intervention (Ezra 8:21-23, Nehemiah 1:4). D. Healing and Restoration ● Isaiah 58:8 speaks of fasting bringing health and healing. ● It brings spiritual renewal and refreshment. E. Increased Anointing and Power 150

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Fasting enhances spiritual authority and power, as seen in Jesus’ ministry (Luke 4:14). ● The early church fasted before making major decisions (Acts 13:2-3). 3. The Proper Attitude in Fasting Key Text: Matthew 6:16-18 - "Moreover when ye fast, be not, as the hypocrites, of a sad countenance: for they disfigure their faces, that they may appear unto men to fast. Verily I say unto you, They have their reward." A. Fasting Should Be Done in Secrecy and Humility ● It should not be for public recognition but as an act of devotion to God. ● Jesus emphasized that fasting should not be performed for show (Matthew 6:17-18). B. Accompanied by Prayer and the Word ● Fasting without prayer is mere hunger strike. ● Engaging the Word of God during fasting deepens spiritual growth. C. With the Right Motives ● Fasting should be done with the right intent, not out of obligation or religious routine (Zechariah 7:5-6). ● It should be a time of true repentance and alignment with God’s will. 4. How to Fast Effectively A. Prepare Spiritually and Physically ● Set a clear purpose for fasting. ● Reduce distractions and dedicate time to seeking God. B. Choose the Right Type of Fast 151

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Absolute Fast: No food or water (Esther 4:16, Acts 9:9). ● Normal Fast: Only water (Matthew 4:2). ● Partial Fast: Abstaining from certain foods (Daniel 10:3). ● Intermittent Fast: Specific fasting periods (Luke 18:12). C. Engage in Prayer and Worship ● Set specific prayer times during the fast. ● Meditate on the Word and listen for God’s direction. D. Break the Fast Wisely ● Avoid overeating when concluding a fast. ● Reintroduce food gradually to avoid digestive issues. Conclusion: Fasting is a powerful spiritual discipline that brings believers into deeper alignment with God's will. When done with the right motives, it leads to spiritual breakthroughs, personal renewal, and greater sensitivity to the Holy Spirit. Reflection Questions: 1. What is my motive for fasting? 2. How can I incorporate fasting into my spiritual walk? 3. What areas in my life need divine intervention that fasting can address? Memory Verse: Isaiah 58:6 - "Is not this the fast that I have chosen? to loose the bands of wickedness, to undo the heavy burdens, and to let the oppressed go free, and that ye break every yoke?" 152

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Prayer: Lord, grant me the grace to seek You through fasting and prayer. Help me to fast with the right heart, to grow in spiritual strength, and to experience Your power in my life. Amen. 153

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 37 TOPIC: Discerning God’s Voice - Hearing and Obeying God Key Texts: John 10:27, Romans 8:14 Introduction One of the greatest privileges of being a believer is the ability to hear and be led by God. Jesus declared, "My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me." (John 10:27). However, many struggle with discerning God's voice amidst the noise of life. This study will explore how believers can recognize, understand, and obey God’s leading. This lesson will cover: 1. The Nature of God’s Voice 2. Ways God Speaks to His People 3. Developing Sensitivity to God’s Voice 4. Obedience: The Key to Walking in God’s Will 1. The Nature of God’s Voice A. God Speaks to His Children John 10:27 - "My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me." Romans 8:14 - "For as many as are led by the Spirit of God, they are the sons of God." God desires to communicate with His children. Every believer has access to His voice. B. God’s Voice is Clear and Consistent ● God does not speak in confusion or contradiction (1 Corinthians 14:33). 154

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● His voice aligns with His written Word (Psalm 119:105). C. God’s Voice Leads to Peace Colossians 3:15 - "Let the peace of God rule in your hearts." ● When God speaks, He brings clarity, not fear or anxiety. 2. Ways God Speaks to His People A. Through the Written Word (The Bible) ● The Bible is the primary and most reliable way God speaks (2 Timothy 3:16-17). ● God will never speak contrary to His Word. B. Through the Inner Witness of the Holy Spirit Romans 8:16 - "The Spirit itself beareth witness with our spirit, that we are the children of God." This is an inward knowing or prompting from the Holy Spirit. C. Through the Still Small Voice 1 Kings 19:12 - "And after the earthquake a fire; but the Lord was not in the fire: and after the fire a still small voice." God’s voice is often gentle and requires stillness to perceive. D. Through Prophetic Messages ● God can use prophecy, dreams, and visions (Joel 2:28, Acts 2:17). ● All prophetic revelations must align with the Word of God (1 Thessalonians 5:20-21). 155

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: E. Through Circumstances and Open Doors ● God sometimes directs through circumstances, opportunities, or closed doors (Acts 16:6-7). ● However, circumstances alone should not be the sole basis for discerning God’s will. F. Through Godly Counsel Proverbs 11:14 - "Where no counsel is, the people fall: but in the multitude of counsellors there is safety." ● Wise and mature believers can provide confirmation and guidance. 3. Developing Sensitivity to God’s Voice A. Spend Time in Prayer and Fellowship with God Jeremiah 33:3 - "Call unto me, and I will answer thee, and shew thee great and mighty things, which thou knowest not." ● A consistent prayer life strengthens spiritual discernment. B. Meditate on God’s Word Psalm 119:11 - "Thy word have I hid in mine heart, that I might not sin against thee." ● God’s voice is best recognized when we are filled with His Word. C. Cultivate a Heart of Obedience John 7:17 - "If any man will do his will, he shall know of the doctrine." 156

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: A willingness to obey sharpens our ability to discern God’s direction. D. Practice Spiritual Stillness Psalm 46:10 - "Be still, and know that I am God." In a noisy world, taking time to be still before God enhances clarity. E. Develop Spiritual Maturity Hebrews 5:14 - "But strong meat belongeth to them that are of full age, even those who by reason of use have their senses exercised to discern both good and evil." Spiritual growth enhances the ability to distinguish between God's voice and personal thoughts. 4. Obedience: The Key to Walking in God’s Will A. Obedience Unlocks More Revelation Psalm 25:9 - "The meek will he guide in judgment: and the meek will he teach his way." Those who obey God’s voice will receive greater direction. B. Delayed Obedience is Disobedience ● Jonah 1:1-3 - Jonah initially refused to obey God’s voice, leading to consequences. ● Obedience should be immediate and complete. C. Obedience Brings Blessings 157

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Isaiah 1:19 - "If ye be willing and obedient, ye shall eat the good of the land." God rewards those who walk in His ways. D. Obedience Requires Faith Hebrews 11:8 - "By faith Abraham, when he was called to go out into a place which he should after receive for an inheritance, obeyed." ● Sometimes, obedience requires trusting God beyond what we understand. Conclusion ● God desires every believer to hear and follow His voice. ● He speaks through His Word, the Holy Spirit, and other divine means. ● Sensitivity to God’s voice requires prayer, meditation, and obedience. ● Obeying God’s voice leads to a life of divine guidance and fulfillment. Application Questions: 1. In what ways has God spoken to you before? How did you recognize it? 2. What steps can you take to grow in discerning God’s voice? 3. How can you become more obedient to God’s instructions? Memory Verse: John 10:27 - "My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me." 158

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Prayer: Lord, open my ears to hear Your voice clearly. Help me to walk in obedience and follow Your leading daily. In Jesus' name, Amen. 159

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 38 TOPIC: Walking in Love & Forgiveness - Christ-like Character Key Texts: John 13:34-35, Ephesians 4:32 Introduction Love and forgiveness are central to the Christian faith. Jesus commanded His followers to love one another as He has loved them. Walking in love and forgiveness is not just an instruction but a way of life that reflects the character of Christ in us. In this study, we will explore what it means to walk in love, the necessity of forgiveness, and how these attributes impact our relationships and spiritual growth. 1. The Command to Walk in Lov e Key Text: John 13:34-35 A. Love is a Commandment, Not a Suggestion Jesus said, "A new commandment I give unto you, That ye love one another; as I have loved you, that ye also love one another." (John 13:34) ● Love is the distinguishing mark of a true disciple of Christ. (John 13:35) ● The standard of love is not based on human emotions but on Christ’s example of selfless and sacrificial love. B. The Nature of Christ-like Love ● Unconditional Love: Love that is not based on merit but is freely given. (Romans 5:8) ● Sacrificial Love: Jesus laid down His life for us; we must also be willing to love sacrificially. (1 John 3:16) ● Active Love: Love is demonstrated through action, not just words. (1 John 3:18) 160

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 2. The Necessity of Forgiveness Key Text: Ephesians 4:32 A. God Commands Us to Forgive "And be ye kind one to another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, even as God for Christ's sake hath forgiven you." (Ephesians 4:32) ● We are to forgive others just as God has forgiven us. (Colossians 3:13) B. Why Forgiveness is Essential ● Unforgiveness Hinders Our Prayers: Jesus taught that if we do not forgive, our Heavenly Father will not forgive us. (Mark 11:25-26) ● Forgiveness Brings Freedom: Unforgiveness keeps us in bondage, while forgiving releases us from emotional and spiritual burdens. (Matthew 18:21-35) ● Forgiveness is an Act of Obedience: We forgive because God commands it, not because the offender deserves it. C. How to Walk in Forgiveness ● Acknowledge the Hurt but Choose to Release It: Holding onto offense only harms you. ● Pray for Those Who Hurt You: (Luke 6:27-28) ● Depend on the Holy Spirit for Strength to Forgive: (Philippians 4:13) 3. The Impact of Walking in Love and Forgiveness A. Restores Relationships ● Love and forgiveness mend broken relationships and foster unity in the body of Christ. (Colossians 3:14) B. Reflects God’s Character 161

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● When we love and forgive, we reveal the nature of God to the world. (1 John 4:7-8) C. Brings Peace and Joy ● Love and forgiveness free us from bitterness, leading to inner peace. (Philippians 4:7) Conclusion: ● Love and forgiveness are at the heart of Christ-like character. ● As followers of Christ, we must choose love over offense and forgiveness over resentment. ● Walking in love and forgiveness glorifies God and strengthens our faith. Application Questions: 1. Are there people you need to forgive? 2. How can you demonstrate Christ-like love to those around you? 3. What steps will you take this week to walk in love and forgiveness? Memory Verse: Ephesians 4:32 - "And be ye kind one to another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, even as God for Christ's sake hath forgiven you." Prayer: Lord, help me to love as You have loved me and forgive as You have forgiven me. Fill my heart with Your compassion and let my life reflect Your character. In Jesus’ name, Amen. 162

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 39 TOPIC: The Ministry of Helps - Serving in the Local Church Key Texts: 1 Corinthians 12:28, Romans 12:7 Introduction The Ministry of Helps is a vital part of the local church, ensuring the smooth operation of its functions and the edification of the body of Christ. It is not a lesser calling but an essential ministry ordained by God. Every believer is called to serve, and through serving, we demonstrate Christ-like humility and love. This study will cover: 1. Understanding the Ministry of Helps 2. Biblical Examples of the Ministry of Helps 3. Attitudes for Effective Service 4. The Rewards of Serving in the Local Church 1. Understanding the Ministry of Helps Key Text: 1 Corinthians 12:28 ● The Ministry of Helps is a divine assignment within the church to assist in various functions, ensuring order and effectiveness in ministry. 1 Corinthians 12:28 - "And God hath set some in the church, first apostles, secondarily prophets, thirdly teachers, after that miracles, then gifts of healings, helps, governments, diversities of tongues." ● The word "helps" refers to supportive roles that enable the church to fulfill its mission effectively. 163

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Romans 12:7 - "Or ministry, let us wait on our ministering: or he that teacheth, on teaching." ● Serving is an act of worship and obedience to God. 2. Biblical Examples of the Ministry of Helps A. The Levites (Numbers 1:50-53, 1 Chronicles 23:28-32) ● They assisted the priests in the tabernacle, setting up, dismantling, and maintaining God’s house. B. The Deacons (Acts 6:1-7) ● Chosen to serve tables and assist the apostles, allowing them to focus on prayer and the Word. ● Stephen and Philip were among them, showing that service is not separate from spiritual impact. C. Jesus’ Example (John 13:12-17) ● Jesus washed the disciples' feet, teaching that true leadership is service. 3. Attitudes for Effective Service A. Serve Willingly and Cheerfully Psalm 100:2 - "Serve the Lord with gladness: come before his presence with singing." God loves a cheerful servant, not one who serves grudgingly. B. Serve with Excellence and Faithfulness 164

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Colossians 3:23 - "And whatsoever ye do, do it heartily, as to the Lord, and not unto men." Excellence in service glorifies God and sets a standard for others. C. Serve in Humility Philippians 2:3 - "Let nothing be done through strife or vainglory; but in lowliness of mind let each esteem other better than themselves." No task is beneath us; service should be done with a heart of humility. 4. The Rewards of Serving in the Local Church A. Spiritual Growth ● Serving allows for practical application of faith and spiritual maturity. B. Divine Promotion Matthew 25:21 - "His lord said unto him, Well done, thou good and faithful servant: thou hast been faithful over a few things, I will make thee ruler over many things." Faithful service leads to greater responsibilities and blessings. C. Kingdom Impact ● Serving helps advance the gospel and build a strong church community. Conclusion ● The Ministry of Helps is a calling from God and essential for the local church. ● Every believer should embrace serving as a privilege, not a burden. 165

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● True service should be done with joy, humility, and excellence. Application Questions 1. How are you currently serving in your local church? 2. What areas of ministry can you contribute to more effectively? 3. How can you develop a better attitude towards serving? Memory Verse: 1 Corinthians 12:28 - "And God hath set some in the church, first apostles, secondarily prophets, thirdly teachers, after that miracles, then gifts of healings, helps, governments, diversities of tongues." Prayer: Lord, give me a heart of service and humility. Help me to serve faithfully in Your house, knowing that my labor is not in vain. May my service bring glory to Your name and edify Your Church. Amen. 166

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 40 TOPIC: Christian Family & Marriage Biblical Principles for Family Life Key Text: Ephesians 5:21-33 Introduction The family is God’s foundational institution for society, designed to reflect His love and order. Christian marriage is a covenant that mirrors Christ’s relationship with the Church. Understanding and applying biblical principles ensures that our families are built on God’s wisdom and thrive in His purpose. This study will explore: 1. The Foundation of a Christian Marriage 2. Roles and Responsibilities in Marriage 3. Love and Submission in Marriage 4. Raising a Godly Family 1. The Foundation of a Christian Marriage A. Marriage is a Covenant, Not a Contract Genesis 2:24 - "Therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother, and shall cleave unto his wife: and they shall be one flesh." Marriage is a lifelong commitment ordained by God. B. Christ at the Center of Marriage Colossians 3:17 - "And whatsoever ye do in word or deed, do all in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God and the Father by him." 167

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: A Christ-centered marriage thrives in love, faith, and purpose. 2. Roles and Responsibilities in Marriage A. The Husband’s Role Ephesians 5:25 - "Husbands, love your wives, even as Christ also loved the church, and gave himself for it." Husbands are called to love, lead, and provide spiritual covering. B. The Wife’s Role Ephesians 5:22-24 - "Wives, submit yourselves unto your own husbands, as unto the Lord." Submission is a willing response to godly leadership, not oppression. C. Mutual Honor and Respect Ephesians 5:21 - "Submitting yourselves one to another in the fear of God." Marriage thrives on love, sacrifice, and partnership. 3. Love and Submission in Marriage A. Christ-like Love in Marriage ● 1 Corinthians 13:4-7 describes love as patient, kind, selfless, and enduring. ● Love in marriage is demonstrated through actions, not just words. B. Submission with Understanding 1 Peter 3:7 - "Likewise, ye husbands, dwell with them according to knowledge, giving honour unto the wife..." 168

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Husbands and wives must understand and support each other’s needs. C. Forgiveness and Grace Colossians 3:13 - "Forbearing one another, and forgiving one another... even as Christ forgave you, so also do ye." ● No marriage is perfect; grace and forgiveness sustain it. 4. Raising a Godly Family A. Teaching Children God’s Ways Proverbs 22:6 - "Train up a child in the way he should go: and when he is old, he will not depart from it." ● Parents are spiritual stewards of their children. B. The Role of Fathers and Mothers Ephesians 6:4 - "Fathers, provoke not your children to wrath: but bring them up in the nurture and admonition of the Lord." ● Biblical parenting is based on love, discipline, and instruction. C. Family as a Ministry Joshua 24:15 - "As for me and my house, we will serve the Lord." ● A godly home is a place of worship, discipleship, and peace. Conclusion: ● Marriage is a reflection of Christ’s love for the Church. ● Husbands and wives must honor their God-given roles. ● Love, submission, and forgiveness sustain a godly marriage. ● Raising children in God’s ways secures their future in Christ. 169

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Application Questions: 1. How can you apply biblical principles to strengthen your marriage or future marriage? 2. In what ways can husbands and wives better love and submit to each other? 3. How can Christian parents be intentional about raising godly children? Memory Verse: Ephesians 5:33 - "Nevertheless let every one of you in particular so love his wife even as himself; and the wife see that she reverence her husband." Prayer: Heavenly Father, help us build strong marriages and families that glorify You. May our love reflect Christ’s love, and may our homes be places of peace and righteousness. Amen. 170

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 41 TOPIC: Christian Parenting - Raising Godly Children Key Texts: Proverbs 22:6, Deuteronomy 6:6-7 Introduction Christian parenting is a divine mandate to nurture, instruct, and model godly living for children. The family is God’s primary institution for discipleship, and parents are the first spiritual leaders in their children’s lives. Raising godly children requires intentionality, consistency, and reliance on the wisdom of God. This study will explore: 1. The Biblical Mandate for Parenting 2. Principles for Raising Godly Children 3. Challenges in Christian Parenting and How to Overcome Them 4. The Impact of Godly Parenting 1. The Biblical Mandate for Parenting Key Text: Proverbs 22:6 - “Train up a child in the way he should go: and when he is old, he will not depart from it.” ● Parenting is a command from God : The responsibility of raising children in the way of the Lord is not an option but a divine assignment. ● Parental instruction is lifelong : Deuteronomy 6:6-7 emphasizes that parents must diligently teach their children God’s Word at all times. ● Discipline and instruction must be rooted in love : Ephesians 6:4 warns parents not to provoke their children but to bring them up in the nurture and admonition of the Lord. 2. Principles for Raising Godly Children A. Teach and Model God’s Word 171

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Children learn more from what they see than from what they hear. Psalm 78:4 - "We will not hide them from their children, shewing to the generation to come the praises of the LORD." ● Have regular family devotionals and ensure Scripture is the foundation of your home. B. Discipline with Love and Wisdom ● Discipline should not be about punishment but correction and instruction in righteousness ( Hebrews 12:11 ). ● Avoid extremes, neglecting discipline or being overly harsh can have lasting negative effects ( Colossians 3:21 ). C. Cultivate a Loving and Secure Environment ● A Christ-centered home is one where love, respect, and honor are practiced. John 13:34-35 - "By this shall all men know that ye are my disciples, if ye have love one to another." D. Pray for and with Your Children ● Covering children in prayer helps shape their destiny and protect them from evil influences. Job 1:5 - Job interceded for his children daily, setting an example for parents. E. Encourage Responsibility and Accountability ● Teaching children accountability prepares them for spiritual and practical success. 172

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Give them age-appropriate responsibilities and help them understand the value of hard work ( Proverbs 13:4 ). 3. Challenges in Christian Parenting and How to Overcome Them A. Cultural and Technological Distractions ● Solution: Set boundaries for media consumption and encourage meaningful engagement with Scripture. B. Rebellion and Disobedience ● Solution: Continue loving discipline, prayer, and trust in God’s promises ( Isaiah 54:13 ). C. Parental Failures and Regrets ● Solution: Repent where necessary and trust God’s ability to restore and transform ( Joel 2:25 ). 4. The Impact of Godly Parenting John Wilmot’s Quote: "Before I got married, I had six theories about raising children; now, I have six children and no theories." ● This humorous yet profound quote highlights the reality that parenting is not about rigid theories but about wisdom, grace, and learning along the way. Karen Hughes' Story: Karen Hughes, a former White House counselor, once shared how she prioritized her child’s upbringing over her high-profile political career. She stepped down from her role to ensure she was present in her son's life. This demonstrates that no success outside the home can compensate for failure in raising children. Conclusion: 173

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Christian parenting is a sacred duty that requires diligence, love, and spiritual discipline. ● Teaching, modeling, and disciplining in love produce children who walk in the ways of the Lord. ● Challenges in parenting can be overcome through prayer, wisdom, and reliance on God’s grace. Application Questions: 1. How intentional am I in teaching my children God’s Word? 2. What areas of parenting do I need to improve on? 3. How can I create a more Christ-centered environment in my home? Memory Verse: Proverbs 22:6 - "Train up a child in the way he should go: and when he is old, he will not depart from it." Prayer: Lord, grant me the wisdom, patience, and love to raise my children according to Your Word. Help me to model Christ in my home and instill godly values in their hearts. Amen. 174

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: TERM 4: WORKERS IN TRAINING & MINISTRY DEVELOPMENT (Weeks 42-52) Module 9: The Heart of a Christian Worker (Weeks 42-47) 175

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 42 TOPIC: The Character of a Christian Worker - Humility, Faithfulness Introduction The effectiveness of a Christian worker is not just in their skills or service but in the Christ-like character they exhibit. Two essential virtues that define a godly worker are humility and faithfulness . The Bible sets clear standards for those who serve in the household of faith. Without these virtues, even the most gifted worker may fall into error. This lesson explores the importance of humility and faithfulness in Christian service. 1. The Necessity of Humility in Christian Service Key Texts: Titus 1:7-9, Philippians 2:3-7 A. Definition of Humility ● Humility is a modest view of one’s importance, acknowledging that all abilities and opportunities come from God. Philippians 2:3 - "Let nothing be done through strife or vainglory; but in lowliness of mind let each esteem other better than themselves." B. Jesus as the Ultimate Example of Humility ● Philippians 2:6-7 - Jesus, though God, took on the form of a servant and humbled Himself. ● A Christian worker must reflect Christ’s servant-hearted nature. C. Dangers of Pride in Service Proverbs 16:18 - "Pride goeth before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall." ● Pride leads to self-reliance and makes one resistant to correction and accountability. 176

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: D. Practicing Humility in Ministry ● Acknowledge that your role is to serve and glorify God, not yourself. ● Be teachable and open to correction (Proverbs 12:1). ● Give credit to others and work with a team mindset (1 Corinthians 3:6-7). 2. Faithfulness: The Mark of a True Servant Key Texts: 1 Timothy 3:8-10, Matthew 25:21 A. Definition of Faithfulness ● Faithfulness means being reliable, trustworthy, and consistent in one’s commitments. 1 Corinthians 4:2 - "Moreover it is required in stewards, that a man be found faithful." B. Faithfulness in Small Things Luke 16:10 - "He that is faithful in that which is least is faithful also in much." ● The path to greater responsibilities in the Kingdom starts with being diligent in small tasks. C. Faithfulness in Doctrine and Conduct Titus 1:9 - "Holding fast the faithful word as he hath been taught, that he may be able by sound doctrine both to exhort and to convince the gainsayers." ● A Christian worker must be steadfast in the truth and live a life that reflects biblical values. D. Faithfulness in Trials and Challenges 177

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Faithfulness is tested in difficult times. Those who remain steadfast are rewarded (James 1:12). Matthew 25:21 - "Well done, thou good and faithful servant: thou hast been faithful over a few things, I will make thee ruler over many things." E. Practical Ways to Demonstrate Faithfulness ● Keep commitments and honor your word. ● Serve diligently even when no one is watching. ● Be punctual, prepared, and responsible in ministry duties. ● Stay devoted to prayer and the Word. 3. Combining Humility and Faithfulness in Christian Service A. Humility makes service pure, while faithfulness ensures consistency. B. A Christian worker must be humble enough to serve and faithful enough to endure. C. Both virtues help build a strong and lasting ministry. Conclusion A Christian worker must exemplify humility, recognizing that their strength and calling come from God. At the same time, faithfulness ensures that they remain consistent and trustworthy in their service. As believers, our ultimate goal is to hear the Lord say, "Well done, thou good and faithful servant." (Matthew 25:21) Application Questions: 1. In what areas of service do you need to cultivate more humility? 2. How can you demonstrate faithfulness in your current responsibilities? 3. What practical steps will you take to grow in these virtues? Memory Verse: 178

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 1 Corinthians 4:2 - "Moreover it is required in stewards, that a man be found faithful." 179

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 43 TOPIC: The Spirit-Filled Worker - Serving in God’s Power Key Texts: Acts 6:3-5 Introduction In the Christian walk, service is not just about performing tasks or fulfilling duties. It is about serving in the power of the Holy Spirit. The work we do in God's kingdom must be done through His strength, wisdom, and empowerment, rather than our own. A spirit-filled worker is one who is equipped by the Holy Spirit to carry out their tasks effectively and faithfully. In this study, we will explore: 1. The Importance of Being Filled with the Spirit for Service 2. Characteristics of the Spirit-Filled Worker 3. The Power of the Spirit in Ministry 4. Serving with Excellence in the Power of the Holy Spirit 1. The Importance of Being Filled with the Spirit for Service Key Text: Acts 6:3 The apostles were tasked with overseeing the early church’s ministry, but the needs of the people, particularly the distribution of food, required more workers. They chose seven men who were full of the Holy Spirit and wisdom to handle this responsibility. This illustrates the principle that spiritual service requires the empowerment of the Holy Spirit. Acts 6:3 - "Wherefore, brethren, look ye out among you seven men of honest report, full of the Holy Ghost and wisdom, whom we may appoint over this business." This passage shows that being filled with the Holy Spirit is essential for effective service in the kingdom of God. The Holy Spirit equips us to do 180

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: the work of the ministry and ensures that we serve with the right attitude and power. Application: ● Every believer is called to serve, but to serve effectively, we must be filled with the Holy Spirit. ● Our service must be empowered by the Spirit, not driven by human effort alone. 2. Characteristics of the Spirit-Filled Worker Key Text: Acts 6:3 The apostles instructed the church to select men who possessed the following qualities: A. Honesty and Integrity ● A spirit-filled worker is trustworthy and reliable in every aspect of their service. They uphold integrity and are dependable. ● Acts 6:3 - "Of honest report" highlights that they were known for their good character and reputation. B. Full of the Holy Spirit ● This is the foundational requirement for effective service. A worker must be filled with the Holy Spirit to be effective in God’s work. ● Ephesians 5:18 - "Be filled with the Spirit" is a continual action that empowers us for every aspect of life, including service. C. Wisdom ● The spirit-filled worker must be wise, applying godly insight to navigate the challenges of ministry. Wisdom allows one to handle situations in a way that brings glory to God. 181

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● James 1:5 - "If any of you lack wisdom, let him ask of God, that giveth to all men liberally, and upbraideth not; and it shall be given him." 3. The Power of the Spirit in Ministry Key Text: Acts 6:5-6 The appointment of these men to serve was done with prayer and the laying on of hands. This represents the transfer of spiritual authority and empowerment for ministry. The Holy Spirit is the source of all ministry power. Without His empowerment, our service is ineffective, but when we serve in His strength, we see God's power manifest in our work. ● Acts 6:5-6 - "And the saying pleased the whole multitude: and they chose Stephen, a man full of faith and of the Holy Ghost... whom they set before the apostles: and when they had prayed, they laid their hands on them." A. Faith and the Holy Spirit ● The Spirit-filled worker is not only empowered by the Spirit but also walks in faith. Faith is essential in ministry, as it enables us to trust God for outcomes beyond our natural abilities. B. God's Power in Service ● When filled with the Holy Spirit, our service becomes a vehicle for God's power. The Spirit enables us to perform tasks with supernatural effectiveness. Application: ● A Spirit-filled worker does not rely on human strength but on the power of God. ● Service in the Spirit involves trusting God to empower us for the tasks He has set before us. 4. Serving with Excellence in the Power of the Holy Spirit 182

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Key Text: Acts 6:3, Colossians 3:23-24 A spirit-filled worker serves with excellence and diligence, no matter how small or large the task may seem. Whether we serve in leadership or in the background, we do so with all our heart as unto the Lord. ● Colossians 3:23-24 - "And whatsoever ye do, do it heartily, as to the Lord, and not unto men; Knowing that of the Lord ye shall receive the reward of the inheritance: for ye serve the Lord Christ." Serving with excellence in the power of the Holy Spirit means giving our best for God's glory, knowing that He will reward our faithfulness. Conclusion: The Spirit-Filled Worker The Spirit-filled worker serves in God's power, relying on the Holy Spirit for guidance, wisdom, and strength. The Holy Spirit enables believers to fulfill their calling with effectiveness and excellence. Whether in leadership or serving behind the scenes, every believer is called to serve, empowered by the Holy Spirit. Application Questions: 1. How can you ensure that you are continually filled with the Holy Spirit for service? 2. What are the specific qualities of a Spirit-filled worker that you can apply in your own life? 3. How can you serve with excellence, knowing that you are serving the Lord and not just people? Memory Verse: Acts 6:3 - "Wherefore, brethren, look ye out among you seven men of honest report, full of the Holy Ghost and wisdom, whom we may appoint over this business." Prayer: 183

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Father, I thank You for the gift of the Holy Spirit. I ask that You fill me afresh with Your Spirit and empower me to serve You with wisdom, integrity, and faith. Help me to walk in Your strength and to serve with excellence in every area of my life. In Jesus' name, Amen. 184

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 44 TOPIC: Christian Leadership - Servant Leadership Key Texts: Mark 10:42-45 Introduction Christian leadership is distinct from secular leadership in that it is built upon the foundation of servanthood rather than dominance or authority. In Mark 10:42-45, Jesus teaches His disciples that the essence of true leadership is found in serving others. This lesson is a crucial one, especially in a world where leadership often means power, control, and position. Jesus, the greatest leader, turned that concept upside down by modeling leadership through service and humility. In this lesson, we will explore: 1. The Definition of Servant Leadership 2. Characteristics of a Servant Leader 3. The Model of Jesus’ Leadership 4. The Impact of Servant Leadership 1. The Definition of Servant Leadership Key Text: Mark 10:42-43 "But Jesus called them to him, and saith unto them, Ye know that they which are accounted to rule over the Gentiles exercise lordship over them; and their great ones exercise authority upon them. But so shall it not be among you: but whosoever will be great among you shall be your minister." Servant leadership is the model in which leaders prioritize the needs of others over their own. Rather than seeking personal gain or power, a servant leader seeks to serve, empower, and elevate those they lead. ● Servant Leadership focuses on the growth and well-being of people and the communities to which they belong. 185

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● A servant leader uses their influence to serve others, rather than seeking to be served. ● Jesus’ definition of leadership is not about asserting control or dominating others, but about becoming a servant to all. Application: ● Leadership in the Christian context is about serving, not lording over others. ● In our leadership roles, we must focus on meeting the needs of others and empowering them to succeed. 2. Characteristics of a Servant Leader Key Text: Mark 10:44 "And whosoever of you will be the chiefest, shall be servant of all." Jesus gives us a clear directive here: the greatest among you is the one who serves. The characteristics of a servant leader include: A. Humility ● A servant leader does not seek status or honor, but humbly serves others. ● Humility is at the core of Christian leadership. Jesus Himself, though the Son of God, demonstrated unparalleled humility by serving His disciples. B. Selflessness ● A servant leader puts the needs of others before their own. ● A true servant leader doesn’t consider their own advancement but focuses on the good of those they lead. C. Sacrificial Love ● The heart of servanthood is sacrificial love. 186

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Jesus exemplified this ultimate sacrifice by giving His life for the redemption of humanity. Leadership in Christ’s kingdom requires sacrificial love, putting others first even at great personal cost. D. Empowering Others ● A servant leader equips others to grow, take responsibility, and succeed. ● They are not threatened by the success of those they lead, but rather, they rejoice in seeing others grow and flourish. 3. The Model of Jesus’ Leadership Key Text: Mark 10:45 "For even the Son of man came not to be ministered unto, but to minister, and to give his life a ransom for many." Jesus is the ultimate model of servant leadership. He did not come to be served but to serve. His life exemplified selflessness, humility, and sacrifice. Throughout His ministry, Jesus served by healing the sick, teaching, and ultimately laying down His life for others. A. Jesus Was the Ultimate Servant Leader ● He washed His disciples' feet, a task reserved for the lowliest servant in the house (John 13:1-17). ● He lived a life of service and sacrifice, never seeking glory for Himself but always pointing to the Father. B. Jesus’ Leadership Was Marked by Compassion ● Jesus demonstrated deep compassion for those He served, whether it was the hungry crowds or the outcast sinners. ● Compassion for others is a vital component of servant leadership. Application: 187

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● As leaders, we must be willing to serve others, even in ways that may be uncomfortable or seem beneath us. ● Leadership is not about position, but about sacrificial service and love. 4. The Impact of Servant Leadership Key Text: Mark 10:45 (Continued) "…and to give his life a ransom for many." A. Transformation of Others ● Servant leadership has the power to transform lives. When leaders serve others, they help develop the leadership potential in others. ● Jesus’ model of leadership created a movement that changed the world. B. Building Community ● Servant leadership fosters unity, love, and mutual respect. When leaders prioritize others, they create an environment where everyone thrives. ● By serving others, leaders build a community of trust, support, and mutual care. C. Advancing the Kingdom ● Servant leadership is central to advancing the kingdom of God. Jesus came to serve, and through His servanthood, He redeemed humanity. ● As leaders, we follow in His footsteps, and our service points others to Christ and advances His purposes on earth. Conclusion: The Call to Servant Leadership True leadership in the Christian context is rooted in servanthood. Jesus’ teaching in Mark 10:42-45 challenges us to lay aside any desire for power or position and to focus on serving others with humility, love, and sacrifice. 188

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: When we serve in this way, we reflect the heart of Christ and build up the body of Christ. Application Questions: 1. What are some ways you can demonstrate servant leadership in your life and ministry? 2. How can you put the needs of others ahead of your own in your leadership role? 3. In what areas can you grow in humility and sacrificial love as a leader? Memory Verse: Mark 10:44 - "And whosoever of you will be the chiefest, shall be servant of all." Prayer: Lord, thank You for the example of servant leadership that Jesus set for us. Help me to walk in humility, selflessness, and sacrificial love as I serve others. Teach me to lead as You led - with a heart to serve, empower, and build up others. May my leadership bring glory to You and bless those I lead. In Jesus' name, Amen. Believers' Class: Week 45 TOPIC: Ministry Ethics & Accountability - Integrity in Service Key Texts: 1 Corinthians 4:1-2 Introduction In every area of life, ethics and accountability are crucial. They form the bedrock of trust and credibility. For those involved in ministry, these principles are even more important. Ministry is a sacred calling, and as such, it requires the highest standard of ethics, especially in the areas of integrity, responsibility, and accountability . In 1 Corinthians 4:1-2, Paul 189

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: emphasizes that ministry is about being faithful stewards of the mysteries of God, with an emphasis on integrity in our service. 1. Understanding Ministry Ethics Key Text: 1 Corinthians 4:1 "Let a man so account of us, as of the ministers of Christ, and stewards of the mysteries of God." Paul, in this verse, defines the role of ministers as stewards of God’s mysteries. The term "steward" indicates that ministers are entrusted with something precious , the gospel of Jesus Christ and the responsibilities of serving God's people. Ethical behavior in ministry means that we handle the calling and the gifts God has entrusted to us with honor, transparency, and respect. Ministry ethics are the moral principles that guide a believer’s actions and decisions in service to God. They include: ● Honesty and Truthfulness : Ministers must communicate the truth in love and avoid deceit in any form. ● Respect for Others : Treating others with dignity and recognizing their value as individuals created in God's image. ● Justice and Fairness : Leaders must act justly in their dealings, making decisions based on fairness and righteousness. ● Confidentiality : Keeping sensitive information shared in trust, respecting others' privacy. Application: ● As stewards of the gospel, we must handle our responsibilities with integrity and ensure that our actions reflect the truth of God's Word. ● Our ministry should always reflect honesty, transparency, and ethical behavior in every aspect, whether we are leading, counseling, or serving. 190

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 2. The Call to Accountability Key Text: 1 Corinthians 4:2 "Moreover it is required in stewards, that a man be found faithful." Ministers are not only stewards but also accountable to God . Paul states that one of the primary requirements of a steward is faithfulness. Accountability means that we are responsible for how we carry out our service and how we manage the resources and opportunities given to us. We will one day give an account to God for the way we have handled the ministry He has entrusted to us. Accountability in ministry involves: ● Answering to God : We are ultimately responsible to God for how we serve in ministry. ● Answering to Others : Church leaders and fellow ministers provide oversight and support to one another, helping to ensure that each person remains faithful to their calling. ● Transparency in Actions : Ministers are called to live above reproach, maintaining openness about their actions, decisions, and ministry operations. Application: ● Understanding that we are accountable to God should drive us to remain faithful in all aspects of our ministry. ● Accountability to others is also vital , we are not isolated but part of a community that helps us grow and stay on track. 3. Integrity in Service Key Text: 1 Corinthians 4:1-2 "Let a man so account of us, as of the ministers of Christ, and stewards of the mysteries of God. Moreover it is required in stewards, that a man be found faithful." 191

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: The word integrity comes from the Latin "integritas," meaning wholeness or completeness. A minister with integrity is someone whose actions, words, and decisions are consistent with their faith and principles, and who strives to live out the calling of God in every aspect of life. Integrity involves: ● Consistency in Character : Living in accordance with biblical principles both in public and in private. ● Trustworthiness : Being someone who others can rely on to act in the best interest of God’s work. ● Honoring Commitments : Being true to the promises and commitments made to the church and to God. Ministers must reflect the values of honesty, trustworthiness, and moral uprightness. Integrity is foundational in building the credibility of the ministry, allowing others to trust in the leadership and the Word of God they share. Application: ● Reflect on areas where integrity is being challenged in your ministry and seek God's help to strengthen your character. ● Strive for transparency and honesty in all ministry activities, keeping your actions aligned with the Word of God. 4. Why Integrity & Accountability Matter in Ministry Ministry is a sacred responsibility, and the integrity of those in leadership plays a significant role in the health and success of the church. When a minister fails to live with integrity, the reputation of the church and the effectiveness of the ministry are compromised. Integrity ensures that the ministry remains faithful to its purpose, which is to honor God and spread His truth. Accountability protects the church from abuse, error, and immorality. Together, integrity and accountability promote: ● Trust in Leadership : Leaders must model the ethical behavior that others are expected to follow. 192

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Spiritual Growth : Ministers who lead with integrity help others grow in their faith and trust in the gospel. ● Healthy Church Environment : A church founded on integrity and accountability fosters an atmosphere of love, respect, and spiritual maturity. Application: ● Reflect on the significance of integrity and accountability in your ministry. How does your behavior influence the church and its witness to the world? ● Commit to walking in integrity, understanding that your actions speak louder than words. 5. Practical Steps to Cultivate Integrity & Accountability ● Daily Prayer and Reflection : Ask God to search your heart and reveal areas where you need to align more closely with His Word. ● Seek Mentorship and Oversight : Embrace accountability by submitting yourself to spiritual oversight and guidance from mature leaders. ● Transparency in Relationships : Cultivate open communication with your fellow ministers and church members, being honest about your struggles and areas for growth. ● Commit to Ethical Practices : Be intentional about adhering to ethical principles in every aspect of ministry, whether it's financial management, counseling, or personal conduct. Conclusion As ministers of Christ, we are called to be faithful stewards of the mysteries of God. Our integrity and accountability are essential to maintaining a credible and effective ministry. Through our faithful service, we reflect the truth of the gospel and build trust within the community we serve. By embracing ministry ethics and accountability, we honor God and ensure that our lives and ministries bear lasting fruit. 193

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Application Questions : 1. What specific areas of your life or ministry need a deeper commitment to integrity and accountability? 2. How can you foster an environment of integrity and accountability in your church or ministry team? 3. Reflecting on 1 Corinthians 4:1-2, how can you be more faithful in your stewardship of God's work? Memory Verse: 1 Corinthians 4:2 - "Moreover it is required in stewards, that a man be found faithful." Believers' Class: Week 46 TOPIC: Faithfulness in Little Things - The Pathway to Promotion Key Text: Luke 16:10 Introduction Faithfulness is often seen as a quality reserved for handling large tasks or significant responsibilities, but Jesus teaches us in Luke 16:10 that it is actually the small, seemingly insignificant things that reveal our true character. How we handle the "little things" in life determines our capacity to manage greater responsibilities. God is watching how we steward the small things, and it is through this faithfulness that He promotes us to higher levels of influence and service. 1. Understanding the Principle of Faithfulness Key Text: Luke 16:10 "He that is faithful in that which is least is faithful also in much: and he that is unjust in the least is unjust also in much." 194

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: In this verse, Jesus lays down a fundamental principle of faithfulness: How we handle small things determines how we will handle larger things .
