The Lord evaluates us based on our integrity and reliability in the smallest matters, and it is through this testing that He prepares us for greater responsibilities. ● Faithfulness in small things : This refers to doing what we are called to do, no matter how small or unimportant it may seem to others. It involves the consistent, dependable performance of duties, even when no one is watching. ● Promotion through faithfulness : God does not promote us based on our abilities or desires, but on our ability to be faithful with what we have. When we prove ourselves faithful in small things, God will trust us with more. Application: ● Reflect on your current responsibilities. Are there areas where you are not being faithful in small things? ● Consider how your current faithfulness in little tasks is preparing you for bigger ones. 2. The Importance of Faithfulness in God’s Kingdom In God's Kingdom, promotion does not work as the world defines it. The world often values ambition, competition, and self-promotion. However, God’s ways are different. Jesus teaches that promotion comes through humility, service, and faithfulness . It is not about trying to climb the ladder of success by force, but about faithfully serving in the areas where God has placed us. ● Stewardship : Faithfulness is first and foremost about stewardship. God has entrusted us with time, gifts, and resources. How we handle them is a direct reflection of our trustworthiness. ● Trustworthy Character : God's promotion is based on our character . A person who cannot be trusted with small things 195

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: cannot be trusted with larger responsibilities. Application: ● Examine how you manage the time, resources, and relationships God has entrusted to you. ● Are you faithful in taking care of the things God has placed in your care, no matter how small they may seem? 3. Jesus’ Example of Faithfulness in Little Things Jesus Himself demonstrated perfect faithfulness in the small things. Although He was the King of Kings, He humbled Himself and served in the most menial tasks. Consider the following: ● Washing the Disciples’ Feet (John 13:1-17) : Jesus, the King, demonstrated His love by serving His disciples in the most humble way possible. This act of service showed that faithfulness is demonstrated through actions of humility, not seeking status or recognition. ● Faithfulness in His Ministry : Jesus stayed faithful to His mission despite the challenges, even when the crowds did not understand or appreciate His message. His faithfulness to God’s will led to the ultimate promotion - the seat at the right hand of the Father. Application: ● Consider the areas where you may be reluctant to serve humbly or where you may feel "too good" to do certain tasks. ● How can you emulate Christ’s example of faithfulness, especially in the small and unnoticed areas of life? 4. The Test of Faithfulness Faithfulness is tested in many ways: ● Consistency : How consistent are you in your daily duties and responsibilities? Being faithful means being reliable, even when you feel tired or discouraged. 196

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Attitude : Do you do the small tasks with a cheerful heart, or do you complain about doing the “little things”? Your attitude matters just as much as your actions. ● Quality of Work : Even the smallest task should be done with excellence. Whether it’s a task at church, at work, or at home, we are called to give our best to God in everything, no matter how insignificant it may seem. Application: ● Take a moment to evaluate your attitude toward the small tasks in your life. Are you performing them with excellence and joy? ● How do you respond when no one is looking? Faithfulness in the little things is about doing things as if doing them unto the Lord (Colossians 3:23). 5. Rewards for Faithfulness in Little Things God promises to reward those who are faithful with the little things. The rewards include: ● Greater Responsibility : God promises that those who are faithful in small things will be given more responsibility. As we handle smaller tasks with integrity, God entrusts us with greater opportunities. ● Divine Promotion : Just as God promoted David from shepherding sheep to ruling Israel, He will promote those who prove faithful in the small, unseen tasks. God’s promotion is not about position, but about the influence and responsibility to make an eternal impact. ● Spiritual Growth : Faithfulness in the small things contributes to our spiritual maturity. As we prove ourselves faithful, God refines our character and builds our faith. Application: ● Are you trusting God to reward your faithfulness in the small things? 197

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Pray for a heart of humility and trust in God’s timing for promotion. Remember, He sees every act of faithfulness, no matter how small. 6. Faithfulness in the Small Things Leads to Eternal Rewards While faithfulness may not always bring immediate recognition or earthly rewards, the ultimate reward for faithfulness is eternal. Jesus speaks about a future reward where we will hear, "Well done, good and faithful servant" (Matthew 25:21). These are the words we long to hear from the Lord. ● Eternal Impact : Your faithfulness in the little things today has eternal significance. God sees every sacrifice, every act of service, and every moment of integrity. ● Heavenly Promotion : The greatest reward for faithfulness is not necessarily promotion on earth, but promotion in the Kingdom of God, where we will be entrusted with eternal responsibilities. Application: ● Keep in mind the eternal perspective as you serve faithfully in the small things. Your actions today are building up treasures in heaven (Matthew 6:19-21). Conclusion Faithfulness in the little things is the pathway to promotion in the Kingdom of God. Jesus’ words in Luke 16:10 remind us that our true character is revealed in how we handle small, everyday tasks. As we remain faithful in these seemingly insignificant areas, God will promote us to greater responsibilities, not for our glory, but for His. Let us strive to serve with integrity, humility, and excellence, knowing that God sees and rewards every act of faithfulness. Application Questions: 1. In what areas of your life do you feel tempted to neglect the small things? 198

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 2. How can you demonstrate faithfulness in the “little things” at home, work, or church? 3. What is your attitude toward serving in humble or unnoticed tasks? How can you change this perspective? Memory Verse: Luke 16:10 - "He that is faithful in that which is least is faithful also in much: and he that is unjust in the least is unjust also in much." Prayer: Father, thank You for teaching me the value of faithfulness in the little things. Help me to serve with humility, integrity, and excellence in every area of my life. I trust that You see my faithfulness, and I believe that You will promote me in Your perfect timing. May I always do all things as unto You, knowing that every small act of service matters in Your Kingdom. In Jesus’ name, Amen. 199

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 47 Overcoming Burnout in Ministry - Spiritual Renewal Key Text: Isaiah 40:31 Introduction Ministry can be a rewarding yet demanding endeavor, often leading to exhaustion and burnout. Serving God and others with sincerity and dedication is important, but without proper spiritual renewal, a minister can easily become weary and overwhelmed. In Isaiah 40:31, God promises a divine solution to burnout, spiritual renewal through His power and presence. This promise assures us that when we rely on God, He will renew our strength and help us to continue serving with vigor and passion. In this session, we will explore how to overcome burnout and experience spiritual renewal in ministry. 1. Understanding Burnout in Ministry What is burnout? Burnout is a state of emotional, physical, and spiritual exhaustion caused by prolonged stress, overwork, and lack of rest. In ministry, burnout can be particularly detrimental as it affects both the minister's spiritual life and their ability to serve others effectively. Common causes of burnout in ministry include: ● Excessive workload : Too many responsibilities and demands can overwhelm a minister. ● Lack of rest : Neglecting to take time off or recharge can lead to exhaustion. ● Isolation : Ministers can sometimes feel alone in their work, leading to feelings of isolation or loneliness. ● Unresolved emotional stress : Unaddressed personal or ministry-related issues can take a toll on emotional and spiritual well-being. 200

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Unrealistic expectations : Ministers may set high expectations for themselves or feel pressure from others, leading to frustration and burnout. The impact of burnout : ● Decreased spiritual vitality ● Emotional and physical exhaustion ● Loss of passion for ministry ● Spiritual dryness ● Difficulty hearing God's voice ● Neglect of personal well-being Application: ● Reflect on the causes of burnout in your own life. Are you experiencing any of these factors? ● Consider how burnout has affected your ability to serve and grow spiritually. 2. God’s Promise for Renewal: Isaiah 40:31 Key Text: Isaiah 40:31 "But they that wait upon the Lord shall renew their strength; they shall mount up with wings as eagles; they shall run, and not be weary; and they shall walk, and not faint." This verse provides a powerful promise for those who are feeling spiritually drained, emotionally exhausted, or physically worn out. It emphasizes the importance of waiting upon the Lord to receive His strength and renewal. ● "Wait upon the Lord" : Waiting here implies more than just a passive activity; it is an active waiting, involving trust, prayer, worship, and seeking God’s presence. It’s about positioning 201

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ourselves to receive strength from God rather than relying on our own abilities. ● "Renew their strength" : God promises to renew the strength of those who wait on Him. This renewal is not just physical but spiritual, God restores our passion, energy, and focus for ministry. ● "Mount up with wings as eagles" : The eagle symbolizes strength, grace, and perseverance. Those who rely on God’s renewal will rise above their challenges and continue their work with strength and dignity. ● "Run and not be weary, walk and not faint" : God promises not only to renew our strength but to sustain us for the long haul. We will not grow weary or faint in the race if we lean on His strength. Application: ● How does Isaiah 40:31 speak to you in your current season of ministry? ● Are you actively waiting upon the Lord to renew your strength, or are you trying to press on in your own strength? 3. Practical Steps to Overcome Burnout and Experience Spiritual Renewal a) Prioritize Rest and Sabbath Rest is a biblical principle that God designed for our well-being. In the story of creation, God Himself rested on the seventh day, setting an example for us. In ministry, it’s easy to overlook the need for rest, but it is vital for long-term spiritual health and effectiveness. ● Rest in God’s presence : Take regular breaks, both physically and spiritually. Schedule intentional times of rest and reflection in your calendar. ● Sabbath rest : Establish a weekly day of rest, where you disconnect from work and ministry responsibilities to focus solely on God, relaxation, and rejuvenation. 202

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Application: ● When was the last time you took a complete rest from ministry work? ● How can you create a rhythm of rest in your life and ministry? b) Stay Connected to God Through Prayer and Worship Consistent prayer and worship are key to spiritual renewal. When we spend time with God in prayer, we invite His presence into our lives, and He replenishes our spirit. Worship helps refocus our hearts on God’s greatness and sovereignty, reminding us that our strength comes from Him. ● Daily prayer : Make time each day to seek God through prayer. Talk to Him about your burdens, and listen for His voice. ● Worship : Engage in both private and corporate worship. Worship stirs our hearts and draws us closer to God. Application: ● How is your prayer life? Do you set aside time to speak with God and listen to Him? ● Consider ways you can deepen your worship experience, both privately and in the congregation. c) Seek Community and Support Ministers are not meant to serve in isolation. Fellowship with other believers provides encouragement, support, and accountability. Burnout is often worsened by isolation, but when we surround ourselves with others who can pray for us and support us, it lightens the load. ● Accountability : Find trusted mentors, colleagues, or friends who can hold you accountable in your spiritual walk and ministry. ● Support networks : Join a group of fellow ministers or believers who understand the challenges of ministry and can offer emotional and spiritual support. 203

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Application: ● Who are you leaning on for support and accountability in your ministry? ● How can you build or strengthen your support network? d) Delegate and Share the Load In ministry, it’s easy to take on too many responsibilities, believing that everything depends on us. However, burnout can be alleviated when we learn to delegate tasks and share responsibilities with others. Trust others to step up, and give them opportunities to serve and grow. ● Empower others : Encourage and empower others to serve in areas of ministry. You do not need to do everything yourself. ● Delegate effectively : Learn to identify what can be delegated and train others to take on roles. Application: ● Are you holding onto too many tasks in ministry? ● How can you begin delegating and involving others in ministry? 4. The Power of Spiritual Renewal Spiritual renewal is not a one-time event but a continuous process that strengthens us for the long journey. As we make the choice to wait on God, rest in His presence, and seek His strength, He will consistently renew us, empowering us to overcome burnout and continue serving Him faithfully. The renewed strength is not only for our benefit but also for the benefit of those we serve. A spiritually renewed minister can serve with vitality, passion, and effectiveness, making an eternal impact on the lives of others. Application: ● How can you encourage others in ministry who may be facing burnout? 204

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● What changes can you make today to begin experiencing God’s spiritual renewal? 5. Trusting in God’s Timing for Renewal God's renewal does not always come on our timetable. Sometimes we need to trust that He will renew us at the right time. His promise in Isaiah 40:31 is a reminder that we do not need to rely on our own strength but on His. Trust God’s process : Trust that God will meet you at your point of need. Even if you feel weary and exhausted, remember that He is faithful to renew your strength in His perfect timing. Application: ● Trust that God sees your fatigue and will renew you. ● Reflect on how God has renewed you in the past, and trust Him for future renewal. Conclusion Burnout in ministry is a real challenge, but it is not an insurmountable one. God has provided a way for us to overcome burnout and experience spiritual renewal through waiting on Him. As we prioritize rest, prayer, worship, community, and delegation, we open ourselves to God’s refreshing power. Let us be faithful in waiting on the Lord, trusting that He will renew our strength, and help us continue running the race of ministry with vigor and passion. Application Questions: 1. Are you currently experiencing burnout in your ministry? What are the specific causes? 2. How can you begin to prioritize rest and spiritual renewal in your life? 3. In what areas of your life can you delegate tasks to reduce stress and share the workload? 205

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 4. How can you trust God for renewal even when you feel like you are running on empty? Memory Verse: Isaiah 40:31 - "But they that wait upon the Lord shall renew their strength; they shall mount up with wings as eagles; they shall run, and not be weary; and they shall walk, and not faint." Prayer: Father, I come before You acknowledging my weariness and the challenges of ministry. I trust in Your promise to renew my strength. I ask for Your spiritual renewal to refresh me and fill me with the power to continue serving You faithfully. Help me to rest in Your presence, trust in Your timing, and rely on Your strength. In Jesus’ name, Amen. 206

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: TERM 4: WORKERS IN TRAINING & MINISTRY DEVELOPMENT (Weeks 42-52) Module 10: Practical Ministry & Evangelism (Weeks 48-52) 207

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 48 Preaching & Teaching Effectively - Preparing Sermons/Bible Studies Key Text: 2 Timothy 4:2, 2 Timothy 2:15 Introduction Preaching and teaching are foundational to the growth of the church and the spiritual maturity of believers. Effective communication of God’s Word requires careful preparation, a clear understanding of the Scripture, and a reliance on the Holy Spirit. The preparation of a sermon or Bible study goes beyond simply delivering information; it is about transforming lives through the power of the gospel. In this session, we will explore the essential steps in preparing effective sermons and Bible studies, focusing on how to convey biblical truths clearly and practically. 1. The Call to Preach and Teach Key Text: 2 Timothy 4:2 - "Preach the word; be instant in season, out of season; reprove, rebuke, exhort with all longsuffering and doctrine." Preaching and teaching are not optional but essential aspects of the Great Commission. Paul’s charge to Timothy emphasizes the urgency of preaching the Word at all times, whether convenient or inconvenient. The preacher must be faithful to the Word of God, deliver it with clarity, and apply it to the lives of listeners. Application: ● How do you view the call to preach and teach? ● How can you ensure that your ministry is grounded in the Word of God? 2. Preparing a Biblical Sermon/Bible Study a) Begin with Prayer and Seek God’s Guidance The foundation of any effective sermon or Bible study is prayer. You must seek God’s guidance, asking for clarity, wisdom, and revelation as you prepare. The Holy Spirit is the true teacher, and without His help, all efforts would be in vain. 208

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Pray for insight : Ask God to open your heart and mind to the Scriptures. Pray for the Holy Spirit to illuminate the passage you are studying and to guide your understanding. ● Pray for your audience : Ask God to prepare the hearts of those who will hear the message, that they may receive it with understanding and apply it in their lives. Application: ● Before preparing a sermon or Bible study, take time to pray and invite the Holy Spirit into the process. ● How can you make prayer a priority in your sermon preparation? b) Choose a Passage and Focus on Context Selecting the right passage is critical to preparing a biblical sermon or study. Ensure that the passage you choose aligns with the needs of your congregation and the message God wants to communicate. After selecting a passage, study it thoroughly. ● Context is crucial : Understand the historical and cultural background of the passage. Why was it written? What was the author’s intent? What was the audience’s context? ● Interpretation : Use proper hermeneutical methods to interpret the Scripture. Make sure to interpret the passage within its immediate context and the broader context of the Bible. Application: ● Consider a passage you plan to preach or teach on. How will you ensure that you understand the context and interpret it correctly? ● Do you rely on resources like commentaries or concordances to deepen your understanding? c) Define the Main Message (Theme) Every sermon or Bible study must have a clear central message or theme. The theme should be the focus of the entire message, and every point 209

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: should contribute to it. A clear theme helps the listener grasp the main takeaway from the teaching. ● What is God saying? : Identify the central truth in the passage. This is the message God wants to communicate to His people. ● Be clear and concise : Avoid tangential ideas that distract from the main theme. Stay focused on the purpose of the message. Application: ● What is the central message of the last sermon you prepared? ● How can you ensure that each teaching is clear and focused? d) Develop the Outline and Structure Once you have a clear theme, begin to outline the sermon or Bible study. A well-organized structure will make your message more understandable and impactful. Good structure is essential for effective communication. ● Introduction : Start with an engaging introduction that captures attention. It should present the problem or need that the Scripture addresses. ● Body : Break the sermon into several main points, each supporting the central theme. Use Scripture to back up each point, and make sure each point flows logically from one to the next. ● Conclusion : Wrap up the message by summarizing the main points and offering a clear application for the audience. Challenge them to respond to the message. Application: ● How do you structure your sermons and Bible studies? ● Consider your last sermon: did it have a clear introduction, body, and conclusion? e) Use Illustrations and Practical Applications 210

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Illustrations make abstract truths more tangible and help the audience connect emotionally with the message. Use relevant stories, real-life examples, and analogies to illustrate biblical truths. ● Illustrations from Scripture : Often, the Bible itself provides powerful illustrations. ● Practical application : Make sure that the teaching is applicable to everyday life. Show how the biblical truth can be lived out by your audience. Application: ● How can you use illustrations and stories in your sermons to make them more engaging and relatable? ● How can you ensure that your message has a practical application for your audience? f) Allow Time for Reflection and Response Effective preaching and teaching lead to transformation. After delivering the message, encourage your audience to reflect on what they’ve heard and to respond. This could be through a time of prayer, an altar call, or a call to action. ● Invitation to respond : Give your audience an opportunity to respond to the Word. This can include a challenge, a prayer, or a moment of introspection. ● Follow-up : Offer resources or next steps for those who want to dive deeper into the TOPIC. Application: ● How can you create opportunities for reflection and response in your teaching? ● How do you encourage your audience to apply what they’ve learned? 211

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 3. The Role of the Holy Spirit in Preaching and Teaching Key Text: 2 Timothy 4:2 - "Preach the word; be instant in season, out of season; reprove, rebuke, exhort with all longsuffering and doctrine." The Holy Spirit plays a vital role in preaching and teaching. He is the One who empowers, inspires, and applies God’s Word to the hearts of listeners. While our preparation is important, it is the Holy Spirit who convicts, encourages, and empowers the message. ● Preach with the power of the Holy Spirit : Rely on the Spirit for wisdom, understanding, and boldness in delivering the message. ● Allow the Holy Spirit to work : Trust that the Holy Spirit will apply the message in the hearts of your listeners in a way that leads to transformation. Application: ● Do you rely on the Holy Spirit during your sermon preparation and delivery? ● How can you invite the Holy Spirit to guide you in both the preparation and delivery of your messages? 4. Preaching and Teaching with Integrity Preaching and teaching are sacred tasks, and it is important to approach them with integrity. The preacher or teacher should always aim to present the truth faithfully, without distortion or compromise. This requires careful study, humility, and reliance on God’s guidance. ● Be faithful to the Word : Never compromise the message for personal gain, popularity, or convenience. ● Live the message : As a preacher or teacher, your life should reflect the message you preach. Integrity in teaching includes walking in obedience to God’s Word yourself. 212

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Application: ● Are you committed to preaching and teaching with integrity? ● How can you ensure that your life aligns with the truths you teach? 5. Improving Preaching and Teaching Skills The process of preparing and delivering effective sermons and Bible studies requires ongoing growth and learning. Here are some ways to improve your skills: ● Feedback : Seek feedback from trusted mentors and peers who can help you grow. ● Practice : Regularly practice preaching or teaching, even in informal settings. ● Study : Continuously study the Word and deepen your understanding of Scripture. Application: ● What steps are you taking to improve your preaching and teaching skills? ● How can you practice and grow in delivering clear, impactful messages? Conclusion Preaching and teaching are powerful tools for sharing God’s Word and transforming lives. Effective sermon and Bible study preparation is crucial for conveying God’s message clearly and practically. By relying on the Holy Spirit, studying the Word diligently, and applying biblical truths to life, you can deliver messages that inspire and challenge others to live out their faith. Always remember that the ultimate goal of preaching and teaching is not just to inform, but to transform the lives of your hearers. Application Questions: 1. How do you currently prepare for preaching or teaching? What steps can you improve in your process? 213

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 2. What is the central message of your last sermon or Bible study? How did you ensure it was clear and impactful? 3. How can you integrate more practical applications into your messages? 4. How can you continually improve your preaching and teaching skills? Memory Verse: 2 Timothy 4:2 - "Preach the word; be instant in season, out of season; reprove, rebuke, exhort with all longsuffering and doctrine." Prayer: Father, I thank You for the privilege of preaching and teaching Your Word. Help me to prepare with diligence, humility, and reliance on the Holy Spirit. May my messages be clear, impactful, and faithful to Your truth. I ask for wisdom, creativity, and anointing as I share Your Word with others. In Jesus' name, Amen. 214

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 49 TOPIC: Counseling & Pastoral Care - Supporting Believers Key Texts: Galatians 6:2, 2 Corinthians 1:3-4 Introduction As ministers of the gospel, one of our key responsibilities is to offer pastoral care and counseling to believers in need. Counseling and pastoral care are essential aspects of the shepherd’s role, helping individuals navigate life’s challenges, strengthen their faith, and heal from emotional, spiritual, and relational wounds. Effective counseling is not about offering quick fixes but providing a compassionate, biblically-grounded approach that guides individuals toward spiritual restoration and growth. This session will discuss the importance of counseling and pastoral care, biblical principles for supporting believers, and practical steps for becoming a compassionate and effective caregiver. 1. The Importance of Counseling and Pastoral Care Key Text: Galatians 6:2 - "Bear ye one another's burdens, and so fulfill the law of Christ." Pastoral care involves walking alongside believers in their journey of faith, offering encouragement, comfort, and guidance through the challenges of life. The Bible calls us to bear each other's burdens and offer support, just as Christ has done for us. Counseling within the church community is meant to be a healing process where individuals can experience the love, grace, and truth of God in the midst of their struggles. Application: ● How can you create an environment where believers feel safe to share their burdens? ● Do you actively seek out opportunities to bear the burdens of others in your ministry? 215

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 2. Biblical Foundation for Counseling and Pastoral Care a) The Role of Compassion and Empathy Key Text: 2 Corinthians 1:3-4 - "Blessed be God, even the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of mercies, and the God of all comfort; who comforteth us in all our tribulation, that we may be able to comfort them which are in any trouble, by the comfort wherewith we ourselves are comforted of God." As counselors and caregivers, it is important to model the compassion and empathy of Christ. Jesus was deeply compassionate toward those in need, and we are called to follow His example. When we show empathy, we allow those seeking counsel to feel heard, valued, and understood. Our own experiences of God’s comfort should inspire us to offer comfort to others, offering the same grace and mercy we have received. Application: ● How can you demonstrate Christ’s compassion in your interactions with others? ● Reflect on a time when you experienced God's comfort. How did it shape your understanding of counseling others? b) Encouraging Healing and Restoration Key Text: Psalm 34:18 - "The Lord is nigh unto them that are of a broken heart; and saveth such as be of a contrite spirit." A primary goal of counseling is to encourage healing in brokenness, whether emotional, relational, or spiritual. God’s heart is near to the brokenhearted, and as His representatives, we are called to offer healing and restoration. Counseling should never be about simply offering advice; it must be about helping individuals to encounter God’s presence and experience His healing power. Application: ● How can you help individuals move from brokenness to healing in Christ? 216

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● What steps can you take to create an environment where healing can take place? 3. Key Principles for Effective Counseling and Pastoral Car e a) Active Listening and Communication One of the most important aspects of counseling is the ability to listen actively. Active listening involves being fully present, giving your undivided attention to the person, and understanding their emotions and concerns. Through active listening, you can gain insight into the person’s situation and offer more appropriate guidance. ● Ask open-ended questions : Encourage the person to share their feelings and thoughts by asking open-ended questions that invite deeper conversation. ● Avoid interrupting : Let the person speak without interruption, showing respect for their thoughts and feelings. Application: ● Do you practice active listening in your counseling? ● How can you develop better communication skills to better support believers? b) Prayer and Spiritual Support Key Text: James 5:14-15 - "Is any sick among you? let him call for the elders of the church; and let them pray over him, anointing him with oil in the name of the Lord: And the prayer of faith shall save the sick, and the Lord shall raise him up..." Prayer is an essential component of pastoral care. Praying for those who are struggling spiritually, emotionally, or physically invites God into the situation. Prayer can bring comfort, hope, and a sense of peace to those facing trials. 217

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Pray with and for the individual : Lead the person in prayer, inviting God to heal, restore, and provide wisdom. ● Pray according to Scripture : Use God’s Word to strengthen the person’s faith and provide biblical solutions to their struggles. Application: ● How can you integrate prayer into your counseling sessions? ● Reflect on how prayer has impacted your life and ministry. How can you help others experience the power of prayer? c) Providing Biblical Guidance and Wisdom Key Text: 2 Timothy 3:16-17 - "All scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness: That the man of God may be perfect, thoroughly furnished unto all good works." The Bible is the ultimate source of wisdom and guidance. As counselors, we are called to use Scripture to help individuals navigate their challenges. Scripture provides not only comfort but also the principles needed to overcome life’s difficulties. It is important to offer biblical counsel, ensuring that individuals are aligned with God’s will and His Word. ● Use Scripture effectively : Select Scriptures that speak to the person’s situation and offer them as a source of hope, correction, and instruction. ● Encourage practical application : Help the person understand how to apply biblical principles in their daily lives. Application: ● How can you ensure that the guidance you provide is based on biblical truth? ● Reflect on a situation where you applied Scripture to counsel someone. How did it impact them? 218

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 4. Practical Steps in Providing Pastoral Care and Counseling a) Create a Safe and Confidential Environment Believers seeking counsel need to know that their struggles are being handled with care and confidentiality. A safe environment is one where they can open up without fear of judgment or gossip. ● Maintain confidentiality : Keep the information shared in counseling sessions confidential unless there is a need for intervention (e.g., safety concerns). ● Establish trust : Build a relationship of trust where individuals feel comfortable sharing their struggles. Application: ● How can you establish a safe and confidential space for counseling in your ministry? ● Have you encountered any challenges in maintaining confidentiality, and how can you address them? b) Use Discretion When Giving Counsel Not all situations require the same response. It is important to discern the best course of action in each situation, whether that involves offering prayer, recommending professional help, or providing biblical counsel. ● Know when to refer : If the situation requires specialized counseling, such as mental health support, be willing to refer individuals to professional counselors. ● Be sensitive : Be aware of the person’s emotional state and needs, adjusting your approach accordingly. Application: ● How do you determine when to offer counsel and when to refer someone for professional help? ● Reflect on how you can be more discerning in your approach to pastoral care. 219

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 5. Avoiding Burnout in Pastoral Counseling Counseling can be emotionally and spiritually draining, especially when dealing with intense issues or difficult situations. It is important for counselors to maintain their own spiritual and emotional well-being to serve effectively. ● Take time for self-care : Regularly engage in personal reflection, prayer, and rest. ● Seek support : Connect with other ministers for encouragement and accountability. ● Maintain healthy boundaries : Avoid becoming too emotionally involved in the person’s struggles. Application: ● How do you maintain your own well-being while counseling others? ● What steps can you take to prevent burnout in your ministry? Conclusion Counseling and pastoral care are vital aspects of ministry, requiring empathy, wisdom, and a deep reliance on God’s guidance. As counselors, our role is not to solve problems but to walk alongside believers, pointing them to the healing power of Christ. By offering biblical counsel, prayer, and practical support, we can help others navigate their struggles and grow in their faith. Remember, pastoral care is a reflection of God’s love for His people, and our role is to embody that love as we support and guide others. Application Questions: 1. How can you demonstrate Christ’s love and compassion in your counseling ministry? 2. In what areas of counseling do you feel you need more training or experience? 3. How can you create a safe and confidential environment for those seeking pastoral care? 220

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: 4. What steps can you take to ensure that your own emotional and spiritual well-being is maintained as you care for others? Memory Verse: Galatians 6:2 - "Bear ye one another's burdens, and so fulfill the law of Christ." Prayer: Heavenly Father, I thank You for the privilege of supporting others through counseling and pastoral care. Help me to show compassion and empathy to those in need, and guide me to offer counsel that is rooted in Your Word. I pray for wisdom, discernment, and strength as I care for Your people. May I reflect Your love and grace in every interaction. In Jesus' name, Amen. 221

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 50 TOPIC: Missionary Work & Outreach - Understanding the Great Commission Key Texts: Matthew 28:18-20, Acts 1:8, Mark 16:15 Introduction The Great Commission is one of the most central and urgent calls given by Jesus to His followers. It is a mandate to go into all the world, preach the gospel, and make disciples of all nations. This week, we will dive into the biblical understanding of missionary work and outreach, exploring its importance, scope, and practical application. As believers, we are called not only to be witnesses of the gospel but also to actively participate in spreading the message of salvation to the ends of the earth. This teaching will explore the heart of missionary work and how every Christian can engage in this divine mission. 1. The Great Commission - A Clear Command Key Text: Matthew 28:18-20 - "And Jesus came and spake unto them, saying, All power is given unto me in heaven and in earth. Go ye therefore, and teach all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost: Teaching them to observe all things whatsoever I have commanded you: and, lo, I am with you alway, even unto the end of the world." The Great Commission is the command Jesus gave to His disciples after His resurrection. He emphasized that all authority in heaven and on earth had been given to Him, and therefore, He had the right and power to send His followers on this mission. The task is clear: Go, teach, baptize, and make disciples. ● Go : The first step is action. The commission requires movement, both physically and spiritually. This could mean going to distant lands or simply going next door. 222

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Teach : It’s not just about preaching; it’s about making disciples, teaching them to live out the teachings of Christ. ● Baptize : Baptism is the outward sign of an inward change. It signifies the beginning of a new life in Christ. Teach them to obey : Discipleship involves more than just knowledge; it involves obedience to Jesus’ teachings. Application: ● How can you actively participate in fulfilling the Great Commission in your daily life? ● Reflect on how the Great Commission influences your understanding of your mission in life. 2. The Scope of the Great Commission Key Texts: Acts 1:8 - "But ye shall receive power, after that the Holy Ghost is come upon you: and ye shall be witnesses unto me both in Jerusalem, and in all Judaea, and in Samaria, and unto the uttermost part of the earth." Mark 16:15 - "And he said unto them, Go ye into all the world, and preach the gospel to every creature." The Great Commission’s scope is global. Jesus’ command is not limited to a specific group of people or geographical area. It is all-encompassing and extends to all corners of the earth, to every nation, tribe, and language. The Commission also outlines a gradual plan, beginning in Jerusalem, extending to Judea, Samaria, and the ends of the earth. This suggests a model for outreach that starts locally and expands globally. ● Jerusalem : Start where you are. Outreach begins in our immediate surroundings, our communities, and those who are close to us. ● Judea and Samaria : After reaching our local communities, we are called to reach out to those who are culturally different or may have been overlooked. 223

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● The Ends of the Earth : Ultimately, the goal is to reach people from every nation and culture around the world. This calls for missionary work beyond our immediate context, possibly even beyond our borders. Application: ● How can you contribute to reaching your local community with the gospel? ● What role can you play in global outreach, even if you cannot go abroad? 3. The Role of the Holy Spirit in Missionary Work Key Text: Acts 1:8 - "But ye shall receive power, after that the Holy Ghost is come upon you: and ye shall be witnesses unto me both in Jerusalem, and in all Judaea, and in Samaria, and unto the uttermost part of the earth." The empowerment of the Holy Spirit is central to missionary work. Jesus promised His followers that they would receive power when the Holy Spirit came upon them. This power enables them to be effective witnesses, not by their own strength but through the empowerment and guidance of the Holy Spirit. The Holy Spirit equips believers with the boldness, wisdom, and understanding needed to engage in outreach and make disciples. ● Empowerment for service : The Holy Spirit empowers us to speak boldly, perform signs and wonders, and carry out the work of God’s kingdom. ● Guidance and direction : The Spirit leads believers to the right people and places, aligning their mission with God's will. 224

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Application: ● How have you experienced the empowerment of the Holy Spirit in your own life and outreach efforts? ● Are there specific ways you need to rely more on the Holy Spirit for guidance and strength in your mission? 4. The Importance of Discipleship in Missionary Work Key Text: Matthew 28:19 - "Go ye therefore, and teach all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost." Missionary work is not just about evangelizing; it is about making disciples, individuals who follow Christ and live according to His teachings. Discipleship is an ongoing process of learning, growing, and obeying Christ. Missionary work should always involve teaching, training, and equipping believers to live out the gospel in their lives. ● Teaching and instruction : As part of the Great Commission, we are called to teach others everything Jesus has commanded. This involves more than just preaching; it’s about guiding people into a deeper relationship with Christ. ● Nurturing new believers : Discipleship involves nurturing new believers and helping them mature in their faith, helping them to stand firm in their walk with Christ. Application: ● Are you actively discipling someone in your life? ● How can you create a culture of discipleship in your church or ministry? 5. Overcoming Challenges in Missionary Work and Outreach Missionary work can come with its own set of challenges, such as resistance, cultural barriers, lack of resources, or personal doubts. However, these challenges do not negate the importance of fulfilling the 225

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Great Commission. We can find strength in prayer, relying on the Holy Spirit, and trusting in God’s provision. ● Cultural barriers : Missionaries must be sensitive to the culture they are reaching. Understanding the culture helps in communicating the gospel effectively. ● Opposition : Jesus warned that His followers would face persecution, but He also assured them that He would be with them. ● Resources : Missionary work often requires resources, whether financial, human, or material. Trusting God to provide for these needs is essential. Application: ● What challenges might you face in engaging in outreach, and how can you overcome them? ● How can you support missionaries through prayer, finances, and encouragement? 6. Personal Application and Call to Action The Call to All Believers The Great Commission is not just for a select few. It is for every believer. Whether we go into full-time ministry, serve in our local church, or witness in our workplaces and homes, we are all called to participate in God’s mission. As we embrace the call to missions and outreach, we reflect the heart of Christ who desires that none should perish but that all would come to repentance. ● Personal responsibility : Every believer is responsible for fulfilling the Great Commission, whether by going, sending, or supporting missions. ● Active participation : Missions and outreach are not optional but integral to the Christian faith. 226

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Application: ● How will you engage in the Great Commission in your everyday life? ● Are there areas in your life where you need to step out in faith to reach others with the gospel? Conclusion The Great Commission is God’s global mission, and He has invited us to participate in it. Whether locally or globally, every believer has a part to play. As we go into all the world, we must rely on the power of the Holy Spirit to empower, guide, and sustain us. Missionary work and outreach are central to God’s kingdom agenda, and it is our privilege to be a part of this eternal mission. Let us be faithful in our responsibility to make disciples, teach the gospel, and bring others to know the saving grace of Jesus Christ. Application Questions: 1. How can you practically engage in outreach and missions in your current context? 2. What steps can you take to support global missions and missionaries? 3. How can you better disciple new believers in your church or ministry? 4. Reflect on the role of the Holy Spirit in missionary work. How can you rely on Him more? Memory Verse: Matthew 28:19 - "Go ye therefore, and teach all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost." Prayer: Heavenly Father, thank You for the privilege of being part of Your global mission. Help me to take the Great Commission seriously and to actively 227

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: participate in sharing the gospel with those around me. Empower me by Your Holy Spirit to be bold, compassionate, and effective in reaching others for Christ. Strengthen the work of missionaries around the world, and provide all that is needed to advance Your kingdom. In Jesus’ name, Amen. 228

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 51 TOPIC: Apologetics - Defending the Faith Key Text: 1 Peter 3:15 - "But sanctify the Lord God in your hearts: and be ready always to give an answer to every man that asketh you a reason of the hope that is in you with meekness and fear." Introduction: The Call to Defend the Faith In a world filled with differing beliefs, ideologies, and philosophies, the ability to defend our faith is essential. As Christians, we are called not only to live out our faith but to be ready to give a reason for the hope we have in Christ. Apologetics is the defense of the Christian faith through reasoned arguments, biblical truth, and a firm commitment to the gospel. Peter, in his first epistle, encourages believers to "be ready always to give an answer" to those who inquire about our faith. This call to apologetics is not just for scholars or theologians but for every Christian who is committed to representing Christ in the world. 1. The Importance of Apologetics in the Christian Life Key Text: 1 Peter 3:15 - "But sanctify the Lord God in your hearts: and be ready always to give an answer to every man that asketh you a reason of the hope that is in you with meekness and fear." The first part of Peter’s command is crucial: "Sanctify the Lord God in your hearts." This means setting apart Christ as Lord in our lives, acknowledging His supremacy and the truth of His Word. Apologetics is not merely about winning arguments; it is about pointing others to Christ, the source of our hope. As we recognize Christ’s sovereignty in our lives, we become more confident in defending the faith. 229

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Being ready to defend our faith comes from an understanding of Scripture, a relationship with Christ, and a reliance on the Holy Spirit. We defend our faith not with arrogance or pride but with meekness, understanding that our purpose is to bring others into a relationship with Jesus, not to prove ourselves right. Application: ● Have you set apart Christ as Lord in your heart? ● How can you deepen your understanding of Scripture to be ready to answer questions about your faith? 2. Apologetics: Defending the Hope Within You Key Text: 1 Peter 3:15 - "Be ready always to give an answer to every man that asketh you a reason of the hope that is in you." As Christians, we are called to give an account for the hope that we possess. The hope within us is the confident expectation of eternal life, salvation, and the promises of God. This hope is not just a vague feeling of optimism but is rooted in the person and work of Jesus Christ. Our defense of the faith is not about defending abstract doctrines; it is about defending the hope that Christ has given us. Why is this important? The world is filled with skepticism, confusion, and despair. People are looking for hope, and we, as believers, are called to share the reason for our hope in Jesus Christ. When we are asked about our faith, we should be ready to explain how Christ has transformed our lives and how His promises bring us assurance, peace, and joy. Application: ● Reflect on the hope you have in Christ. What specific ways has He transformed your life? 230

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● Can you articulate the hope of the gospel clearly to others? How can you grow in this ability? 3. Apologetics with Meekness and Fear Key Text: 1 Peter 3:15 - "With meekness and fear." When defending the faith, Peter instructs us to do so with meekness and fear . These two virtues are essential in our approach to apologetics. Meekness is not weakness but strength under control. It is the humility that recognizes that, while we may have the truth, we are not superior to others. Our goal is not to win arguments but to share the love of Christ. Fear here refers to a reverence for God. As we defend the faith, we must remember that it is God who gives the increase. We are simply vessels. Our apologetics should not be driven by a desire to prove others wrong but by a desire to honor God and bring others to the knowledge of His truth. Application: ● Are you approaching apologetics with humility and respect for others, even those who disagree with you? ● How can you ensure that your defense of the faith is motivated by love and a desire to honor God? 4. The Role of Scripture in Apologetics Key Texts: 2 Timothy 3:16 - "All scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness." Scripture is the foundation of our apologetic defense. God’s Word is alive and powerful, able to pierce the hearts of those who hear it. When we engage in apologetics, we should rely on Scripture to support our arguments, not on human wisdom or persuasive speech alone. The Bible is authoritative, and its truth stands firm. As we defend our faith, we must use Scripture to point others to the person of Christ and the truths of the gospel. Application: 231

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: ● How often do you rely on Scripture in your conversations with others about faith? ● How can you better familiarize yourself with key passages that support the truth of the gospel? 5. Apologetics in Action - Practical Steps for Defending the Faith ● Know the gospel : Before we can defend our faith, we must first know what we believe and why we believe it. Take time to study the core doctrines of the Christian faith, such as the nature of God, the person and work of Jesus, salvation, and the authority of Scripture. ● Understand common objections : Be aware of common challenges and objections to the Christian faith, such as questions about the existence of God, the problem of evil, and the reliability of the Bible. Equip yourself with biblical answers to these objections. ● Be patient and gentle : Apologetics is not about arguing to win. It’s about explaining the truth with patience and gentleness. Our goal should always be to point others to Christ, not to win an intellectual battle. ● Live out the gospel : Our lives are the most powerful apologetic. When people see the love, peace, and joy of Christ in our lives, they will be more likely to listen to what we have to say. Application: ● How can you deepen your understanding of the gospel to be ready to defend it? ● What are some common objections or questions you’ve encountered that you could prepare answers for? 6. Trusting God for the Outcome At the end of the day, we must trust that God is the one who opens hearts. Our responsibility is to present the truth with clarity and love, but it is the 232

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Holy Spirit who convicts and draws people to Christ. Therefore, while we are called to be faithful in defending our faith, we must also rest in God’s sovereignty and trust Him with the results. Application: ● How can you cultivate trust in God’s sovereignty in your conversations about the gospel? ● Are you willing to trust God with the outcome, even if someone does not respond immediately? Conclusion: A Call to Boldness and Love Defending the faith is not just for theologians or apologists; it is for every believer. We are all called to be ready to give an answer for the hope within us. As we do so, we must remember to approach others with meekness, humility, and reverence for God. Apologetics is not about winning arguments but about pointing people to the truth of the gospel and the hope found in Christ. Let us commit to being faithful witnesses of the gospel, ready to defend our faith with love, truth, and the power of the Holy Spirit. Memory Verse: 1 Peter 3:15 - "But sanctify the Lord God in your hearts: and be ready always to give an answer to every man that asketh you a reason of the hope that is in you with meekness and fear." Prayer: Heavenly Father, thank You for the hope we have in Christ. Help me to always be ready to give a reason for the hope within me with humility and love. Empower me by Your Holy Spirit to defend the faith effectively, pointing others to the truth of the gospel. Give me wisdom and courage as I engage with those who have questions or doubts. May my life reflect the gospel, and may Your truth shine through me. In Jesus’ name, Amen. 233

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Application Questions: 1. How can you prepare yourself to be ready to defend the hope within you? 2. What areas of your understanding of the faith do you need to strengthen for better apologetics? 3. How can you practice humility and meekness in your defense of the faith? 234

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: Believers' Class: Week 52 Commitment & Graduation ● Recap of key lessons ● Personal testimonies ● Commissioning & charge As you stand at the threshold of this new chapter in your Christian journey, I charge you today with a call that is higher than any earthly pursuit, a call to faithfully follow Christ and make His love known to the world. You have spent weeks, months, and perhaps years growing in the knowledge of God’s Word, maturing in your faith, and committing to His service. This is not the end but the beginning of a life of continual growth, service, and dedication to the Kingdom of God. Remember, what you have learned is not just for personal edification but for the building of the body of Christ. It is to be shared, lived out, and reflected in every area of your life. 1. The Call to Obedience: You are called to be faithful to God’s Word. As Jesus said in John 14:15, “If you love Me, keep My commandments.” This love is not just a feeling, it is a life that is in alignment with the truth of God’s Word. Let your obedience be a living testimony to the world of the power of God’s grace in your life. 2. The Call to Service: Christ, our perfect example, came not to be served, but to serve (Mark 10:45). You are called to the ministry of service, whether in the local church, your family, your community, or the world. Service is not just about doing things for others but about reflecting the heart of Christ in every act of kindness, humility, and love. 3. The Call to Perseverance: The path you’ve chosen is not always easy, but it is always worth it. You will face challenges, obstacles, and trials, but 235

title: Logos Believers’ Class Curriculum
text: take heart, for He who began a good work in you will be faithful to complete it (Philippians 1:6). Let perseverance be your companion as you run the race set before you. 4. The Call to Empowerment: You do not walk this journey alone. The Holy Spirit, the very power of God, is with you. He empowers you to live righteously, to speak boldly, and to serve with compassion. Be open to His guidance, for He will lead you into all truth and equip you for every good work (John 16:13). As you move forward, may your life be marked by the love of Christ, the grace of His calling, and the power of His Spirit. May your service be fruitful, and may you remain steadfast in your commitment to Him. And as you go, remember that you are never alone, God is with you, and the Church stands with you. Now, go forth with courage, knowing that you are a light in the darkness, a voice calling others to the grace of God, and a vessel for His purposes. Let the charge you have received today be the foundation of your service, your calling, and your walk with Christ. In the name of Jesus, Amen. Founder/Senior Pastor Great Haven Ministries Inc. Dr. Daniel Folarin 236

title: lostworldofgenesissamplechapter
text: Pro P osition 1 Genesis 1 is Ancient Cosmology s o what are the cultural ideas behind Genesis 1? o ur first proposition is that Genesis 1 is ancient cosmology. That is, it does not attempt to describe cosmology in modern terms or ad- dress modern questions. The israelites received no revelation to update or modify their “scientific” understanding of the cosmos. They did not know that stars were suns; they did not know that the earth was spherical and moving through space; they did not know that the sun was much further away than the moon, or even further than the birds flying in the air. They believed that the sky was material (not vaporous), solid enough to support the residence of deity as well as to hold back waters. in these ways, and many others, they thought about the cosmos in much the same way that anyone in the ancient world thought, and not at all like anyone thinks today. 1 And God did not think it important to revise their thinking. s ome Christians approach the text of Genesis as if it has mod- ern science embedded in it or it dictates what modern science should look like. This approach to the text of Genesis 1 is called “concordism,” as it seeks to give a modern scientific explanation Lost World Genesis REDO.indd 16 5/1/09 2:25:31 PM SAMPLE—DO NOT COPY

title: lostworldofgenesissamplechapter
text: Proposition 1 17 for the details in the text. This represents one attempt to “trans - late” the culture and text for the modern reader. The problem is, we cannot translate their cosmology to our cosmology, nor should we. if we accept Genesis 1 as ancient cosmology, then we need to interpret it as ancient cosmology rather than translate it into mod- ern cosmology. if we try to turn it into modern cosmology, we are making the text say something that it never said. it is not just a case of adding meaning (as more information has become avail - able) it is a case of changing meaning. s ince we view the text as authoritative, it is a dangerous thing to change the meaning of the text into something it never intended to say. Another problem with concordism is that it assumes that the text should be understood in reference to current scientific con - sensus, which would mean that it would neither correspond to last century’s scientific consensus nor to that which may develop in the next century. if God were intent on making his revelation correspond to science, we have to ask which science. We are well aware that science is dynamic rather than static. By its very nature science is in a constant state of flux. if we were to say that God’s revelation corresponds to “true science” we adopt an idea contrary to the very nature of science. What is accepted as true today, may not be accepted as true tomorrow, because what science provides is the best explanation of the data at the time. This “best explana- tion” is accepted by consensus, and often with a few detractors. s cience moves forward as ideas are tested and new ones replace old ones.

title: lostworldofgenesissamplechapter
text: 18 The Lost World of Genesis o ne s ince God did not deem it necessary to communicate a differ - ent way of imagining the world to israel but was content for them to retain the native ancient cosmic geography, we can conclude that it was not God’s purpose to reveal the details of cosmic geog- raphy (defined as the way one thinks about the shape of the cos - mos). The shape of the earth, the nature of the sky, the locations of sun, moon and stars, are simply not of significance, and God could communicate what he desired regardless of one’s cosmic ge- ography. Concordism tries to figure out how there could have been waters above the sky (Gen 1:7), whereas the view proposed here maintains that this terminology is simply describing cosmic geography in israelite terms to make a totally different point. (s ee the next proposition for details.) if cosmic geography is culturally descriptive rather than re - vealed truth, it takes its place among many other biblical examples of culturally relative notions. f or example, in the ancient world people believed that the seat of intelligence, emotion and person- hood was in the internal organs, particularly the heart, but also the liver, kidneys and intestines. Many Bible translations use the e nglish word “mind” when the h ebrew text refers to the entrails, showing the ways in which language and culture are interrelated. in modern language we still refer to the heart metaphorically as the seat of emotion. in the ancient world this was not metaphor, but physiology. Yet we must notice that when God wanted to talk to the israelites about their intellect, emotions and will, he did not revise their ideas of physiology and feel compelled to reveal the function of the brain.

text: 20 The Lost World of Genesis o ne tions that make up this book, one of the issues raised in the list above should be addressed immediately. That is, there is no concept of a “natural” world in ancient n ear e astern thinking. The dichot- omy between natural and supernatural is a relatively recent one. d eity pervaded the ancient world. nothing happened indepen- dently of deity. The gods did not “intervene” because that would assume that there was a world of events outside of them that they could step into and out of. The israelites, along with everyone else in the ancient world, believed instead that every event was the act of deity—that every plant that grew, every baby born, every drop of rain and every climatic disaster was an act of God. n o “natural” laws governed the cosmos; deity ran the cosmos or was inherent in it. There were no “miracles” (in the sense of events deviating from that which was “natural”), there were only signs of the deity’s ac- tivity (sometimes favorable, sometimes not). The idea that deity got things running then just stood back or engaged himself else - where (deism) would have been laughable in the ancient world be- cause it was not even conceivable. As suggested by r ichard Bube, if God were to unplug himself in that way from the cosmos, we and everything else in the cosmos would simply cease to exist. 3 There is nothing “natural” about the world in biblical theology, nor should there be in ours. This does not suggest that God micro - manages the world, 4 only that he is thoroughly involved in the operations and functions of the world.

The cate- gories of “natural” and “supernatural” have no meaning to them, let alone any interest (despite the fact that in our modern world such questions take center stage in the discussion). text: Proposition 1 21 into being without God or what “natural” processes he might have used. n otice that even the biblical text merges these perspectives when Genesis 1:24 says, “Let the earth bring forth living crea - tures” but then follows up with the conclusion in the very next verse, “s o God made the animals.” 5 All of these issues are modern issues imposed on the text and not the issues in the culture of the ancient world. We cannot expect the text to address them, nor can we configure the information of the text to force it to comply with the questions we long to have answered. We must take the text on its own terms—it is not written to us. Much to our dismay then, we will find that the text is impervious to many of the questions that consume us in today’s dialogues. Though we long for the Bible to weigh in on these issues and give us biblical perspectives or an- swers, we dare not impose such an obligation on the text. God has chosen the agenda of the text, and we must be content with the wisdom of those choices. if we attempt to commandeer the text to address our issues, we distort it in the process. As we begin our study of Genesis 1 then, we must be aware of the danger that lurks when we impose our own cultural ideas on the text without thinking. The Bible’s message must not be sub- jected to cultural imperialism. its message transcends the culture in which it originated, but the form in which the message was imbedded was fully permeated by the ancient culture. This was God’s design and we ignore it at our peril. s ound interpretation proceeds from the belief that the divine and human authors were competent communicators and that we can therefore comprehend their communication.

title: PARABLES
text: 2 THE STRUCTURE OF THE DIALOGUE ETERNAL LIFE AND GOOD DEEDS A lawyer, trying to test Jesus said to him, Lawyer: "How can I save myself by my own efforts?" (Question #1) Jesus: "Well - for a start, what does the law say?" (Question #2) Lawyer: "The law says to love God and the neighbor." (Answer #2) Jesus: That's a good answer - do that and you're in. (Answer #1) ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- So the lawyer, still hoping to save himself asked, Lawyer: "Ok, so - just who is my neighbor? (Question #3) Jesus: Let me tell you a story. Once, a redneck was beaten up out in the country. Two white guys cruised by, took one l ook and kept on moving. Then an African American guy passed by. F eeling sorry for the wounded man, he stopped, patched him up as best he could and took him to the hospital in the next town, which was entirely white. Now - who do you think became a neighbor to the redneck? (Question #4) Lawyer: "The guy who helped him out." (Answer #4) Jesus: "There's your answer - become a neighbor. Go live like him." (Answer #3) ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- BEN SIRACH 12:1-7 If you do a good turn, know for whom you are doing it, and your good deeds will not go to waste. Do good to a devout man, and you will receive a reward, if not from him, then certainly from the Most High. No good will come to a man who persists in evil, Or who refuses to give alms. Give to a devout man, do not go to the help of a sinner. Do good to a humble man, give nothing to a godless one. Refuse him bread, do not give him any, it might make him stronger than you are; then you would be repaid evil twice over for all the good you had done him.

title: PARABLES
text: 3 The Theological and Ethical Content of the Parable of the Good Samaritan (in its dialogue setting) 1. Eternal life - a gracious gift: The lawyer is given a standard he cannot meet. In the process he has the opportunity to discovers that he cannot earn eternal life for it comes to him as a free gift. 2. Becoming a neighbor : The lawyers’ question, “Who is my neighbor?” is the wrong question. Here he is challenged to ask, “To whom must I become a neighbor?” The parable answers, “Your neighbor is anyone in need regardless of their language, religion or ethnicity.” 3. Transforming Anger into Grace: The Jew hated the Samaritans. This Samaritan had inevitably suffered from those attitudes. To be willing to reach out to this “enemy of his people” the Samaritan was obliged to reprocess his anger into grace. 4. The limits of the law: Compassion reaches beyond the requirements of any law. The priest and the Levite cannot discover their duty solely by examining their code books. 5. Racism: Jesus attacks the religious and racial attitudes his community. He could have located the parable in Samaria with a good Jew rescuing a wounded Samaritan. Instead, it is a hated Samaritan who rescues (presumably) a wounded Jew. 6. Jesus the teacher: Jesus’ skills as a the teacher emerge. He does not answer the man’s questions. Rather he raises other questions and allows the lawyer to answer his own queries. 7. Christology: After the failure of the listeners’ religious leaders, the agent of salvation breaks in from outside. Jesus is talking about himself. 8 The Cross: The good Samaritan offers a costly demonstration of unexpected love. He risks his life by transporting a wounded Jew into a Jewish town and spending the night there. The wounded man neither can nor will ever be the same again.

title: PARABLES
text: 7 The Parable of the Good Woman and the Lost Coin reflects 8 out of the 9 dramatic elements found in the parallel parable of the Good Shepherd and the Lost Sheep. A . The Theological Cluster of the Parable of the Lost Sheep (Luke 15:1-7) 1. Failed Leadership. The parable contains criticism of leaders who lose their sheep and do nothing but complain about others who go after them. 2. Freely Offered Grace. The lost sheep does not earn the right to rescue. It is a gift. 3. Incarnation and Atonement. The shepherd goes out to the sheep (incarnation) and pays a high price to find and restore it to his home (atonement). 4. Sin. Humankind is depicted as unable to find its own way home. This applies both to the one and to the 99 who are last seen while yet “in the wilderness.” They are not safely in the village, but rather are also in need of guidance in order to arrive home. 5. Joy. With his friends, the shepherd rejoices at the success his saving act. 6. Repentance. Repentance is defined as acceptance of being found. The sheep is lost and helpless and yet is a symbol of repentance. Repentance becomes a combination of the shepherd’s act of rescue and the sheep’s acceptance of that act. The 99 “(think they) need no repentance” (like the older son in 15:25-32), but such a need is unmistakable. 7. The Individual and the Community. David tells of a single (lost) sheep. Jeremiah and Ezekial turn Psalm 23 into a tale of a lost flock (Israel) which God restores to his land. Jesus de-Zionizes the story and in the process he brings together a concern for the individual (the one) and the community (the 99). David’s emphasis on the individual and Jeremiah/Ezekiel’s concern for the community, are united by Jesus in a single story.

title: PARABLES
text: de-Zionizes the story and in the process he brings together a concern for the individual (the one) and the community (the 99). David’s emphasis on the individual and Jeremiah/Ezekiel’s concern for the community, are united by Jesus in a single story. In the process restoration/return to God is what matters, not return to the land. 8. Christology. Jesus is the good shepherd, a unique agent of God who restores the lost sinner to God. (The joy in the home of the shepherd is connected in the parable to joy in heaven.) This shepherd must personally make the costly demonstration of love/holiness in order to restore the helpless sheep. Three Old Testament texts stand behind the parable; Psalm 23, Jeremiah 23:1-4 and Ez. 34. This Old Testament background makes clear that the shepherd is more than merely an agent. He is the one who fulfills the promises of David, Jeremiah and Ezekial that God himself will come to his people and seek out his lost sheep. B. Themes Unique to the Parable of the Lost Coin (Luke 15:8-10) 9. The Unchanged Value of the Coin. When found, the sheep may be sick or injured. But the coin is of undiminished worth while lost and when found has lost nothing of its previous value. 10. The Worth of Women. In this parable Jesus affirms, "I am like this woman! I search for the lost. You should do likewise." Jesus elevates the worth of all women by his choice of imagery.

And the father said, “Rejoiced with me HE JOINS for I have found my two lo st sons!” The Family Points of Cultural Misconception of the Parable of the Compassionate Father 1. The request: The prodigal's request is an unthinkable breach of traditional culture. (He wants his father to die.) 2. His father's gift: The father reprocesses anger into grace and thereby deeply violates the code of an Oriental patriarch. (As is shown five times in the parable). 3. A hurried sale: The prodigal triggers the anger of the community. (He must leave town quickly.) 4. The kezaza ceremony: A threat hanging over the prodigal as he leaves town. (He must not lose the money.) 5. Expensive living: Not riotous (KJ), nor loose (RSV), nor dissolute (NRSV), nor wild (NIV). (The Greek word asotos is literally a + sozo, i.e. without saving. No hints of immorality) 6. Search for employment: The prodigal must find a paying job so he can repurchase the land. (The kezaza ceremony now threatens.) 7. Inauthentic Repentance: He projects A self-serving plan: (Augustinian or Pelagian? Complications with the lost sheep and coin.) 8. Turning point: The costly demonstration of unexpected love. (Grace not law. Love is offered before the confession.) 9. A father who behaves like a mother. (The father is defined by Hosea 11, not by Middle Eastern culture.) 10. Authentic Repentance: The Prodigal Revises His speech – he is not interrupted. (Like the lost sheep, he accepts to be found). 11. Christology: Incarnation and atonement meet. title: PARABLES
text: 11 14. Father's response: The Father once again reprocessed anger into grace. At the same time the father urges the older son dealing with the prodigal in the same way. 15. Older son's final reaction: An unfinished theological symphony (participation theater). The Parable of the Two Lost Sons - The Theological Cluster 1. Sin: The parable exhibits two types of sin. One is the sin of the law-breaker and the other the sin of the law-keeper. Each centers on a broken relationship. One breaks that relationship while failing to fulfill the expectations of the family and society. The second breaks his relationship while fulfilling those same expectations. 2. Freedom: God grants ultimate freedom to humankind, which is the freedom to reject His love. Humankind is free to choose its own way even if that way causes infinite pain to the loving heart of God. 3. Repentance: Two types of repentance are dramatically illustrated. The first: earn your acceptance as a servant/craftsman. The second: accept the costly gift of being found as a son. 4. Grace: A freely offered love that at great cost reprocess anger into grace for each son. 5. Joy: For the father, joy in finding. For the son, joy in being found and restored to community. 6. Fatherhood: The image of God as a compassionate father is given its finest definition in all of Scripture. That definition includes the offer of costly love to law-breakers and to law-keepers. 7. Sonship: Each son returns to the father either defining (the older son) or intending to define (the prodigal) his relationship to the father as that of a servant before a master. The father will not accept. The father offers costly love to each, out of his determination to have sons responding to love rather than servants obeying commands. 8.

title: PARABLES
text: (the prodigal) his relationship to the father as that of a servant before a master. The father will not accept. The father offers costly love to each, out of his determination to have sons responding to love rather than servants obeying commands. 8. Christology: The father twice takes upon himself the form of a suffering servant who in each case offers a costly demonstration of unexpected love. The woman and the shepherd do some of the same on a lesser scale. There is dramatic "self-emptying" in each case. The third parable embodies an implied one-to-one relationship between the actions of Jesus and the actions of the father in that each welcomes sinners into table fellowship. This unity of action affirms some form of a unity of person. 9. Family/community: The father offers costly love to his sons in order to restore them to fellowship in the context of a family/community. The family is Jesus' metaphor for the Church. 10. Incarnation and Atonement: The father empties himself and goes down and out to meet the sons where they are (incarnation). In the process he demonstrates costly redeeming love (atonement). Because of who he is, these acts generate incalculable atoning power. Some of the deepest levels of the meaning of both the incarnation and the cross are clearly exposed. 11. Eucharist: The son(s?) who partake of this banquet in the parable are sitting and eating with the one who offered costly love to win them into fellowship with himself. The heart of the Eucharist is thus clearly affirmed. The mood of the banquet/Eucharist is that of a celebration, not a funeral. The price paid by the shepherd, the woman and the father is not forgotten at the banquets that conclude each parable.

But the atmosphere at the banquet is that of joy at the success of the costly efforts of finding the lost. 12. Eschatology: The messianic banquet has begun. All who accept the father's costly love are welcome as his guests. Table fellowship with Jesus is a proleptic celebration of the Messianic banquet of the end times. The parable of the great banquet in Luke 14:15-24 precedes this parable. Luke (or his source) presents the reader with the former parable
