Then those who were reclining with him began to say to themselves, "Who is this who also forgives sins?", And he said to the woman, "Your faith has saved you, go in peace." The Parable (Mas hal) of the Creditor and the Two Debtors in its Setting (Nimshal) Luke 7:41-50 1. A CREDITOR has two DEBTORS: [God is the creditor] a. One debtor owes 500 denarii [like the woman] b. The other owes 50 denarii [like Simon] 2. The DEBTORS cannot pay 3. The CREDITOR forgives both [Jesus is the creditor] a. The 500 denarii debtor and [He forgives the woman] b. The 50 denarii debtor. [Does he forgive Simon?] 4. The DEBTORS respond with love for the CREDITOR: [Jesus is the creditor] a. The debtor who was forgiven 500 - loves much. [like the woman] b. The debtor who was forgiven 50 - loves little. [like Simon] ----------------------------------------------------------------------- Conclusion: Jesus is saying, "I have forgiven/mediated the forgiveness of this woman's sins. She has offered much love to me. This is appropriate because I am like the creditor in the parable." In the beginning of parable (#1) the creditor is clearly God. In #3 the creditor is clearly Jesus. (In the story Jesus says to the woman, "Your sins have been forgiven." The Pharisees then ask, "Who is this, who even forgives sins?") So in #3 Jesus (at least) presents himself as the mediator of the forgiveness of God. In #4, the creditor is again Jesus. Jesus accepts the grateful love of the woman and blames Simon because he has failed to serve as she served..

Jesus accepts the grateful love of the woman and blames Simon because he has failed to serve as she served.. Thus Jesus is (at least) saying, "I am the unique representative of God to whom thanks for forgiveness received is appropriately offered." We are indebted to God for our sins ("Forgive us our debts [Matt 6:12"]). In some profound sense Jesus is here identifying himself with God. This is a parabolic form of John 10:30, "I and the Father are one." ----------------------------------------------------------------------- Notes on Debts and Sins in the New Testament 1. The Aramaic word khoba means both debts and sins. 2. The Lord's Prayer in Matthew uses the word debts. Luke has debts and sins. 3. In two parallel passages in Luke this same pair of words appears. Luke 13:2 has the word sinners and Luke 13:4 reads debtors (RSV: offenders). The Aramaic word khoba is clearly behind each of these words.

title: PARABLES
text: 14 4. In Matt 18:23-35 a servant is forgiven a large debt by his master and then fails to forgive a fellow servant a small debt. The unforgiving servant is punished. Jesus responds, "So will also my Heavenly father do to every one of you, if you do not forgive your brother from your heart." The remission of a debt is used as a parable about the forgiveness of sin. 5. Col. 2:13-14 uses the symbolism of the canceling of a bond to explain what has happened on the cross. That is, a certificate of indebtedness, a bond = sins canceled by the cross. The Theological Themes th at Appear in Luke 7:36-50 1. Forgiveness and its Effect: The more forgiveness the believer receives from Jesus the more costly love he/she offers to Jesus. 2. Sin: Types of sinners are clarified. Law-keepers and law-breakers are sinners. 3. The Cross: Jesus offers a costly demonstration of unexpected love to the woman. In the process he is exposing one of the deepest levels of his saving ministry which climaxes on a cross. 4. Anger and Grace: Jesus’ hurt (and anger) is expressed in his attack of Simon. This anger is reprocessed into grace for the woman and for Simon. Even Simon “is forgiven little.” (Simon is dealt with very gently. He is not accused of being a great sinner like the woman.) 5. Forgiveness, Faith, Obedience, Salvation and Peace: These five great themes are linked in a single story. The woman is forgiven and saved (through grace) by a faith that is obedience. The result of the process of the interaction of these four themes is peace. 6. Women: The position of women is elevated. Jesus is willing to get hurt to publicly reach out in costly love to this unknown immoral woman. 7. Prophethood: Prophethood is clarified. Simon thinks a prophet is one who avoids contact with sinners.

title: PARABLES
text: ce. 6. Women: The position of women is elevated. Jesus is willing to get hurt to publicly reach out in costly love to this unknown immoral woman. 7. Prophethood: Prophethood is clarified. Simon thinks a prophet is one who avoids contact with sinners. For Jesus, a prophet is one who offers costly love to sinners. 8. Discipleship and the Suffering of Jesus: Through her costly obedience, the woman enters into the Messianic suffering of God in Christ. In the process she becomes a model for discipleship. 9. Christology: Jesus (like the creditor in the parable) accepts thanks for forgiveness received. Jesus is at least saying, "I am the unique representative of God to whom such thanks is appropriately offered." By her actions the woman affirms that the Divine presence of God (Shekinah) is now among his people in the person of Jesus. 10. A decision is inevitable. After such a scene, the participant/reader must accept that the divine presence of God is indeed in Jesus or reject that reality and be offended by him. There is no middle ground. ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- Rhetorical Features in this "Apostolic Homily" 1. The passage falls into seven stanzas/scenes. (Isaiah 40-66 alone has over twenty such texts. It is a common feature of prophetic literature.)

text: 15 2. These seven stanzas are inverted. (That is, the pattern of the material is A-B-C-D-C-B-A.) I have chosen to call this rhetorical form the Prophetic Rhetorical Template. 3. The "encased parable." The metaphor in the center is "encased" in a series of semantic envelopes. The first envelope is a conversation (#3 & #5). The second is the actions of the women (#2 & #6). The third is the introduction and the conclusion (#1 & #7). 4. The six lines in #2 exhibit inverted parallelism. 5. There is a straight line progression of six sets of Hebrew parallelisms in #6. title: PARABLES
text: 17 Theological Cluster of the Parable of the Rich Fool 1. A naked cry for justice, unqualified by any self-criticism, is not heeded by Jesus 2. In case of a broken personal relationship Jesus refuses to answer a cry for justice when the answer contributes to a finalizing of brokenness of that relationship. He did not come as a divider. 3. Jesus’ parables often reflect a profound concern for the poor. But for him justice includes a concern for needs and not simply earnings (cf. Matt. 20:1-16). Here a self-centered cry for justice is understood by Jesus to be a sickness. He refuses to answer the cry but rather addresses himself to the healing of the sickness that produced the cry. 4. Material possessions are gifts from God. At times God grants unearned surpluses of material things. For every human being life itself is a loan. The rich man in the parable assumed to own both his goods and his soul. The parable presents him as mistaken in both cases. 5. The person who thinks security and the good life are to be found in material things is stupid. 6. The abundant life is to be found in “treasuring up for God” rather than for self. 7. James talks of the rich man who will “fade away in the midst of his life-style (1:11). Jesus gives a parabolic picture of precisely this same phenomenon. This fool’s wealth destroyed his capacity to maintain any abiding human relationships. He has no one with whom to share his soul, and worst of all he does not even know he has a problem.

title: PARABLES
text: 19 Theology of the Great Banquet (Luke 14:15-24) 1. Christology. Jesus is God's unique agent calling for participation in the Messianic banquet of salvation promised in Isaiah 25:6-9. 2. Excuses. The excuses people offer for refusing to respond to the invitation to join the banquet are stupid and insulting. 3. Suffering, Anger and its Transformation into Costly Grace. Anger at those excuses/insults is reprocessed into grace. By inviting outcasts and outsiders, that grace takes the form of a costly demonstration of unexpected love. 4. The Banquet and its Timing. The Messianic banquet promised by Isaiah (Is. 25:6-9) is inaugurated in the table fellowship of Jesus (realized eschatology). But the parable is left open- ended. Not all the guests have assembled. The parable breaks off with the house only partially full. Thus the banquet looks to the future (futuristic eschatology). When the faithful sit down in the kingdom with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (Luke 13:28-29) the Messianic banquet will come to its fullest expression. The banquet of the end times is both now and not yet. 5. The Eucharist. The Eucharist is an extension of the table fellowship the first disciples enjoyed with Jesus. Therefore, the theology of this parable looks forward to the Eucharist. 6. The Unworthy Recipients. The invitation is extended to people who cannot compensate the host for his grace. These outcasts may be from within or from without the community. 7. Special Pleading/Evangelism. Grace is unbelievable. Special pleading is necessary to convince the undeserving that they are really invited. 8. Mission. A centrifugal force of mission appears in the parable. The servant, with his master's invitation, is told to extend the invitation to outsiders within and beyond the city.

title: PARABLES
text: ecessary to convince the undeserving that they are really invited. 8. Mission. A centrifugal force of mission appears in the parable. The servant, with his master's invitation, is told to extend the invitation to outsiders within and beyond the city. If God's salvation is to reach to the ends of the earth (Is. 49:6) someone must take that message and present it (Luke 14:23). 9. Judgment. There is a self-imposed concept of judgment. Those who choose to reject the invitation shut themselves off from fellowship with the host and his guests. 10. Presumption. A warning is addressed to the presumptuous in the believing community. God can get along without them. If they fail to respond to His invitation, He will proceed with outsiders. 11. Urgency. Time runs out on the invitation. The initial guests do not receive a second invitation. 12. Response required. Guests are invited but the fact of that invitation is not enough. The guests must respond and enter the banquet. Participation from a distance is not possible.

title: PARABLES
text: 22 THE INTERPRETATION OF THE PARABLE OF THE SOWER Luke 8:9-15 PATH the soil the ones (hoi) along the road those hearing, their hearts FUSION seed the word the plant ------------ ROCKY GROUND the soil the ones (hoi) on the rocky ground they hear, receive, have no root, believe, fall away the seed the word FUSION the plant for a while (no root) THORNS the soil those who hear "what fell among the thorns they are those who hear ..." the seed what fell among thorns FUSION the plant they are choked, do not mature GOOD SOIL the soil what is in the good earth the seed the word FUSION the plant hearing the word, hold it fast, bring forth fruit with patience CONCLUSION All through the interpretation there is a fusion of symbols. This is clear in the third scene with, "What fell among the thorns, they are those who hear." The seed is what fell among the thorns, but the seeds are not "those who hear." Often this text is seen as "confused." We would suggest that rather there is a deliberate fusion of symbols. The hearer (soil) hears the word (seed), and a new reality (plant) is born. This plant may or may not bear fruit. But the interaction of these three is so complete that there is an authentic "trinity" of three in one. This fusion is significant. In Matthew (13:18-23) the above mentioned fusion appears gradually. Here in Luke the fusion starts at the opening line of the interpretation. The text in Mark (4:13-20) is the longest. Mark is closer to Matthew than Luke. Luke is the shortest and can be seen as the most primitive. Matthew may be trying to bring order to the apparent confusion of symbols.

title: PARABLES
text: 23 The Parable of the Sower - the Theological Cluster (Luke 8:4-15; Mark 4:3-20; Matt 13:3-9, 18-23) THE ORIGINAL HEARERS: The right hearing of the gentle word of the kingdom bears fruit. THE THEOLOGICAL CLUSTER: 1. Realized Eschatology. The kingdom is already among them. "...the kingdom of God is already exercising its power on earth among different types of men." R. Brown, The Semitic Background of the Term "Mystery" in the New Testament (Philadelphia: Fortress, 1968) p. 33-34. Thus the kingdom is here and now, not focused on ”going to heaven when we die.” Our prayer is, “Thy kingdom come,…on earth…” 2. Patience. The kingdom is a seed sown and cultivated, growing quietly. It is not an apocalyptic [or political] revolutionary disruption. There is no instantly productive discipleship. 3. Gentleness. The word of the kingdom does not force itself upon the hearer, but rather the seed must be taken into soft, deep, clean soil in order to germinate, grow to maturity and produce fruit. 4. Encouragement for the sower. When there is no fruit it is not the fault of the seed or necessarily the fault of the sower. 5. Preparedness/impediments. If the soil is not plowed, to thin, or full of weeds there will be no harvest. There is a clarification of the various types of impediments to right hearing. 6. Assurance. There is an assurance of a great harvest in spite of these impediments. 7. Grace. There is grace illustrated in the actions of the sower who sows liberally even in potentially unfruitful places. 8. Harvest. Fruit-bearing is an essential mark of the kingdom. The plant is not enough. The sower is not satisfied with a green field blossoming before him. His goal is to produce a crop.. 9. Judgment. The seeds on the rocky ground expose the nature of the ground underneath. 10.

title: PARABLES
text: an essential mark of the kingdom. The plant is not enough. The sower is not satisfied with a green field blossoming before him. His goal is to produce a crop.. 9. Judgment. The seeds on the rocky ground expose the nature of the ground underneath. 10. Proclamation. The kingdom is the place where the word is proclaimed/heard/obeyed. The seed is scattered and received into the soil. Mk has "Listen!" Mk and Mt have "he who has ears to hear let him hear." 11. Humility. The kingdom is not a great cedar of Lebanon (cf. Ez. 17:1-24). Rather it is a lowly wheat/barley field. 12. Fusion. A fusion takes place between the seed, the soil and the plant. Even so the word of God, its reception, and the developing response, must fuse to produce results.

title: PARABLES
text: 27 Contrasts and Comparisons Between Isaiah 28:14-18 and Luke 6:46-49 COMPARISONS 1. Each parable has two houses. 2. The water/storm symbol appears in both. 3. The foundation is a critical issue in each parable. 4. In both, people are called to "hear the word." 5. Each is best understood as addressed to the individual and to the nation. a. Isaiah calls on the people to hear the wo rd in light of the coming of Sennacherib and the Assyrians (+ - 702 B.C.). b. Jesus is best understood to be calling th e nation to a new foundation in light of the coming conflict with Rome. CONTRASTS 1. In Isaiah 28 the house that falls is a full shelter. The second structure is only a promised future foundation. In Luke 6 both buildings are fully built in the present. 2. Isaiah 28 calls on the reader to "hear the word of the Lord." Jesus calls on his listeners to "hear and do my word." 3. Isaiah criticizes faith in the wrong thing - in Egypt and its gods. Jesus criticizes those who "hear and do not do my words." "The parables which deal with the impending crises were each uttered in a particular concrete situation, a fact which is essential for their understanding. It is not their purpose to propound moral precepts, but to shock into realization of its danger a nation rushing upon its own destruction, and more especially its leaders, the theologians and priests. But above all they are a call to repentance." J. Jeremias, The Parables of Jesus (London: SCM, 1963), p. 169. ------------------------------------------------------------------- The Theological Cluster of the Parable of the Two Builders [Luke 6:46-49] 1. The foundation on the rock is the person and words of Jesus. 2. The reader is called on to hear and do the words of Jesus. 3.

title: PARABLES
text: ----------------------------------------------- The Theological Cluster of the Parable of the Two Builders [Luke 6:46-49] 1. The foundation on the rock is the person and words of Jesus. 2. The reader is called on to hear and do the words of Jesus. 3. Hearing and doing are compared to the energy of digging through hard clay to the rock and laying a foundation on it. 4. The house is secure on a foundation which is invisible when life is calm. 5. The storm hits both houses. Faith in Jesus is not magical protection from the storms of life. 6. The parable is a warning and a promise to the community. It tells of: a. The shallowness of their foundation (the temple and its institutions). b. The coming storm and its disastrous effects (given their current foundation). c. The offer of a new foundation that can survive the storm. 7. Christology. The Christology of the passage appears on two levels. a. The word of the Lord (Isaiah) is parallel to the words and person of Jesus. b. Jesus is the new foundation promised in Isaiah (not the temple).

title: PARABLES
text: 31 The Story of Zacchaeus: The Theological Cluster (Luke 19:1-10) 1. Hostility into Grace: Jesus here appears as one who is willing to transform his own inevitable hostility against the betrayers of his people into grace. 2. “By His stripes we are healed.” The crowd is not able to make the above transformation. They are angry at Zacchaeus whom they call, “a sinner.” Jesus observes their intense hostility against Zacchaeus and transfers it to himself. 3. Costly Love and the Cross: Jesus demonstrates costly love to Zacchaeus and that love becomes a life changing force in the life of Zacchaeus. In the process Jesus enacts an important aspect of the theology of his own cross. 4. Repentance: Zacchaeus accepts to be found and thereby demonstrates the authentic repentance that Jesus set forth in the parable of the good shepherd. 5. Righteousness: By entering the house of Zacchaeus for the night, Jesus grants to Zacchaeus the gift of a new status of acceptance in his sight that at the core of the Biblical understanding of Righteousness. 6. A Response to Grace received: Zacchaeus responds to the costly love offered to him by pledging himself to demonstrate costly love to the community. The gift of grace he receives creates the energy and will to offer grace to others. 7. A Journey of Faith: Jesus restores Zacchaeus to the family of Abraham who went out responding to the call of God, not knowing where he was going. 8. Salvation: Jesus declares that “Salvation has come” to the house of Zacchaeus. This is a “Divine passive.” Jesus is the agent who brings salvation to Zacchaeus and he does so at great cost. All of this takes place before Zacchaeus carries out the restitution he promises.

title: PARABLES
text: ares that “Salvation has come” to the house of Zacchaeus. This is a “Divine passive.” Jesus is the agent who brings salvation to Zacchaeus and he does so at great cost. All of this takes place before Zacchaeus carries out the restitution he promises. Salvation is clearly more than a single decision, it is also a process that effects all of life. 9. The Ministry of Jesus: In his inauguration Jesus declared his ministry to involve, proclamation, justice advocacy and compassion (Lk 4:16-30). All three elements are present in this story. The message of life changing costly love is demonstrated. Financial oppression on the community is significantly lifted by changing the heart of the oppressor, and Zacchaeus is the recipient of compassion on many levels.

title: PARABLES
text: 33 THE THREE EAGER DISCIPLES (Luke 9:57-62) The First Dialogue The original listener: Discipleship to the Son of man has its price. Are you willing to pay this price? The Theological cluster: 1. Would-be disciples of Jesus at times fail to seriously consider the cost. 2. The Son of man does not come as a powerful conqueror but as a suffering rejected servant. 3. Disciples are rejected by Jesus until they accept to pay the price. 4. Some disciples seek Jesus out. The Second Dialogue The original listener: Jesus says to him, "My authority is higher than that of your own father." Theological cluster: 1. Jesus accepts no authority higher than His own. 2. The cultural demands of our community are not acceptable excuses for our failure in discipleship (irrespective of how honored and sacred those demands are). 3. "Follow me" = "Proclaim the Kingdom of God." 4. Some disciples are sought out by Jesus. The Third Dialogue The original listener: Jesus says to him, "You have made the initial response of obedience. Do not turn back to old loyalties." Theological Cluster: 1. The call of the Kingdom must replace all other loyalties. 2. The wavering disciple is unfit for the kingdom. 3. Following Jesus = taking up of a strenuous, creative task (i.e. like putting one's hand to a plow). 4. Value in the Kingdom = following Jesus (Christology)

title: PARABLES
text: 34 Lecture 12b: THE FRIEND AT MIDNIGHT Luke 11:5-8 And he said to them: (what will not happen) Can any one of you imagine having a friend and going to him at midnight and saying to him, 1. "Friend, REQUEST lend me three loaves, 2. for a friend of mine APPEAL TO has arrived on a journey DUTY and I have nothing to set before him." (reason) 3. And will he an swer from within, "Don't bother me, DUTY the door is now closed REFUSED and my children are in bed with me. (reason) 4. I cannot get up REQUEST REFUSED? and give you anything!?" I tell you: (what will happen) 5. though he will not give him anything, NOT GIVE - ANYTHING having arisen ARISING 6. because of being his friend, FOR FRIENDSHIP but because of his avoidance of shame BUT FOR HONOR 7. he will get up WILL ARISE and give him whatever he wants. GIVE - EVERYTHING Notes: The problem here is the Greek word HHPKLPH which in Greek usage all through the NT period and beyond means "shameless." This negative meaning destroys any potential theological content in the parable. It is our view that with the passing centuries, meaning "spilled" into the passage from the following verses and "persistence" as a new meaning was attached to the above Greek word. Then the new meaning of "persistence" was attributed to the host (outside the door), while the entire final sentence is talking about the man asleep. The original root of this key word is HPK, which means "shame." It is our suggestion that the original translators of the material from Aramaic into Greek intended "shame with a negative," meaning "blameless." That is, "shame with a negative" for them meant "avoidance of shame." The sleeper in the parable wants to preserve his own integrity/personal honor. The neighbors are listening to the conversation.

title: PARABLES
text: tended "shame with a negative," meaning "blameless." That is, "shame with a negative" for them meant "avoidance of shame." The sleeper in the parable wants to preserve his own integrity/personal honor. The neighbors are listening to the conversation. If he fails to respond positively to a request for anything as simple as bread and offers thin ridiculous excuses he will be blamed/shamed by the entire community. title: PARABLES
text: 35 THE GENEROUS FATHER'S GIFTS TO HIS SON Luke 11:9-13 And to you I say, 1. ask, and it shall be given to you, ASK seek, and you shall find, Seek knock, and it shall be opened to you. Knock 2. For everyone who asks receives, ASK and the one who seeks finds, Seek and to the knocker it is opened. Knock And will any one of you, 3. if the son asks the father for bread, will he give him a stone? PARABLES OF: 4. Or a fish, instead of a fish, Bread, Fish, Egg will he give him an eel (a snake)? (Ask & Give) 5. Or if he asks an egg, will he give him a scorpion? if therefore, 6. you being evil, GIVER you know good gifts Gift to give your children, Receiver 7. how much more the Father GIVER out of heaven shall give Holy Spirit Gift to those asking him. Receiver ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ COMMENT: The main point of #1 and #2 is, "Do not be afraid, you will receive." The thrust of #6 and #7 is, "Do not be afraid, the gift will be good." Each brief parable in the center articulates both themes. The verbs give and ask occur at the beginning of #1 and dominate the parables in the center. They appear again in the last lines of #7. As in Luke 15, Jesus is here defining his "Father in Heaven." That Father is not domineering, authoritative, determined to force his will on all. Rather the Father here presented as a generous, caring, responsible Father who gives only good gifts to his children. The wording in Matthew is almost identical to Luke. The one significant difference is that the Father (in Matthew) gives good gifts to his children. Here he offers Holy Spirit. Most early manuscripts of Luke include all three parables in the center of this poem (Cf.

title: PARABLES
text: 36 Lecture #13: THE PHARISEE AND THE TAX COLLECTOR Luke 18:9-14 And he said to certain people who considered in/by themselves that they were righteous and despised others, this parable: (introduction) 1. " Two men went up into the temple to pray, TWO GO UP one a Pharisee and the other a tax collector. Pharisee & Tax Collector 2. The Pharisee stood by himself PHARISEE thus praying, (his manner) 3. 'God, I thank you because I am not like other men, extortioners, unjust, adulterers, even like this tax collector. (his prayer) I fast twice a week. I give tithes of all that I possess.' 4. But the tax collector, standing afar off, TAX COLLECTOR would not even lift up his eyes to heaven, (his manner) but beat upon his chest saying, 5. 'God, make an atonement for me, a sinner!' (his prayer) 6. I tell you, he went down to his house TWO GO DOWN made righteous, rather than that one. Tax Collector & Pharisee For every one who exalts himself will be humbled, and he who humbles himself will be exalted." (conclusion) COMMENT: The verb "to pray" has traditionally been understood to mean private devotions. It can also mean public worship. Two people go and come at the same time. A place of public worship is mentioned. The twice daily atonement sacrifice, of a lamb, for the sins of Israel is most naturally the setting for the parable. In the ritual of this service there was a place for individual prayers (cf. Luke 1:9-10). The phrase "by himself" refers more appropriately to standing than praying. In Luke 16:3 the steward talks "to himself" (en heauto). In Luke 18:4 the judge talks "to himself" (en heauto). Here we have pros heauton which most naturally refers to something other than a soliloquy. The order of the words in the text is reflected in the translation given above.

title: PARABLES
text: to himself" (en heauto). In Luke 18:4 the judge talks "to himself" (en heauto). Here we have pros heauton which most naturally refers to something other than a soliloquy. The order of the words in the text is reflected in the translation given above. Thus, in each case, the reader is informed about how they stood. One stood aside proudly aside while the other stood humbly afar off. Verse 13 contains the word hilasthete (make an atonement). The more common word eleeson (have mercy) occurs in 18:39. The appearance of the weighty theological word hilasthomai in 18:13 must be intentional and significant. The most natural explanation appears to be that the two men are watching the atonement sacrifice in the temple. The tax collector longs that it might be for him. The conclusion to the parable (#7) makes clear that it was for him and for him alone.

title: PARABLES
text: 38 Points of Comparison between Isaiah 66:1-6 and the Parable of th e Pharisee and the Publican Luke 18:9-14 Similarities 1. Each is set in the temple. 2. Each deals with a person who is shut out. 3. In each, the person who is shut out, is the hero of the story. 4. Each contains arrogant people who shut out others and consider this rejection of others a service to God. Considerable self-righteous ness is thus condemned in each. 5. Each mentions sacrifices in the temple. 6. Each has a pious man in awe/fear before God. (One trembles, the other beats his chest.) 7. Each has an individual or persons who choose that in which he delights rather than choosing that which pleases God. 8. In each the sacrifice by itself is not enough without a contrite spirit which in both cases is praised. 9. There is a judgment in each. Contrasts 1. The language of Isaiah is extremely harsh. The parable is hard hitting but softer by comparison. 2. In Isaiah, the sacrifices are not under attack but instead the inadequacies of the one who makes them (#3). The reason for this attack is specified in stanza #4. Thus, the system is not under attack but rather the failure to hear and answer God, and with that failure, the apparent assumption that the sacrifices are enough and that the latter is unnecessary. Up to this point the two texts are similar. The difference between them is that in Isaiah the entire system is under discussion and tthe parable mentions only the atonement sacrifice. 3. Judgment has been noted in each account. Yet the theme is dealt with differently in both texts. Judgment in Isaiah is bold and harsh. The Pharisee is judged in the parable in that he goes down to his house unjustified. Judgment results from his own actions; not from an act of God.

Conclusion The parable is a brilliant "update" by Jesus of Nazareth of the theological content of the above prophetic homily through selection, expansion and conversion into a parable.

title: PARABLES
text: 39 The Theological Cluster of the Parable of the Pharisee and the Tax Collector (Luke 18:9-14) 1. Righteousness: A right relationship with God (righteousness) is a gift from God that comes through atonement and cannot be achieved through close observation of law. 2. Atonement: God’s offered grace through sacrifice (costly love) is worthless to the worshiper who is arrogant, judgmental and self satisfied. 3. A pattern for prayer: Informing God in a self-congratulatory manner of one’s ‘virtues’ is not prayer. A humble yearning for God’s unearned grace is an authentic part of genuine prayer. 4. The perversion of perceptions: Self-righteousness distorts the visions of self, God and the world. 5. The recipient of the Sacraments: Only those who sense their unworthiness in the presence of God’s offered grace can appropriately approach God’s holiness to receive His grace. 6. The critical linkage of Word and Sacrament: As in Isaiah 66:1-6, hearing the voice of God and obedience to that voice (word) is deeply linked to the sacraments. John 6:40 and 54 have the same message. Whenever “word” and “sacrament” are separated and one or the other is neglected, serious deterioration results. 7. Jesus and Anger. Here Jesus presents a repentant Tax Collector as the hero of a parable. By so doing Jesus demonstrates his willingness to reprocesses his own anger against collaborators (and other sinners) into grace.

title: PARABLES
text: 40 Pilate, the Galileans and the Tower Luke 13:1-5 There were some present at that very time who told him of the Galileans Whose blood Pilate had mingled with their sacrifices. And he answered them, 1. Do you think that those Galileans worse sinners they were POLITICAL that all the other Galileans because they suffered thus? Oppression 2. I tell you, No! But unless you repent REPENT you will all likewise perish. 3. Or those eighteen upon whom the tower fell (in Siloam and killed them). Do you think worse debtors they were HUMAN than all the others who dwelt in Jerusalem? Error 4. I tell you, No! But unless you repent REPENT you will all likewise perish. --------------------------------------------------------------- Pilot, the Galileans and the Tower: The Theological Cluster 1. Sin is defined both by evil acts and by duties left unperformed. 2. There is no one-to-one relationship between sin and suffering. Easy theological judgments about reasons for natural and political disasters must be rejected. 3. Nay intense political movement must look deep within its own soul to repent of its own evil, lest it destroy itself and the very people it seeks to serve 4. The compassion of Jesus reaches out equally to all who suffer, not only to those who are politically oppressed. 1 1 Kenneth E. Bailey, Through Peasant Eyes, p. 74-80.

title: PARABLES
text: 41 The Barren Fig Tree (Lk 13:6-9) 1. A man had a fig tree planted in his vineyard PLANT And he came seeking fruit on it Seek Fruit And he found none. No Fruit 2. And he said to the vinedresser, “Behold! These three years THREE YEARS I have come seeking fruit on this fig tree Seek Fruit And I find none No Fruit 3. Dig it out DIG IT OUT Why should it exhaust the ground?” Save the Soil 4. But he answering said to him, “Master? Forgive it this year also ONE YEAR until I dig around it Help Fruit Bearing and spread manure. Help Fruit Bearing 5. And if it bears fruit in the future – FIND FRUIT - ? And if not, No Fruit Dig it out. Dig Out --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- The Parable of the Barren Fig Tree: The Theological Cluster 1. The spiritual leaders of the household of faith are planted in “God’s vineyard” and are expected to produce fruit for Him. 2. When that leadership is fruitless it not only fails in its own obedience but also sterilizes the community around it. God cares for the community and will not tolerate this situation indefinitely. 3. Mercy is extended to unfruitful leadership in the form of an offer of forgivness and renewing grace. 4. Only in the grace of God, freely offered to the fruitless leaders, is renewal possible. God acts to forgive and renew. These acts come from beyond the leaders, who cannot renew themselves. 5. God’s offer of mercy must evoke a response from within or renewal will not take place and judgment becomes inevitable. 2 2 Revised from, Kenneth E. Bailey, Through Peasant Eyes, p. 80-87.

title: PARABLES
text: 42 Lecture 15a: The Farmer and His Servant (Lk 17:7-10) 1. Can you imagine having a servant, SERVANT plowing or keeping sheep, Fulfilling Orders who on coming in from the field Orders Fulfilled you say to him, Reward? ‘Come at once and recline to eat? 2. Will he not rather say to him, ‘Prepare for me something and I shall dine, and gird yourself and serve me, SERVE MASTER till I eat and drink; Then Yourself and afterward you shall eat and drink’? 3. Does the servant have special merit SERVANT because he did what was commanded? Fulfilling Orders So you, also, when you have done what was commanded Orders Fulfilled Say, ‘Nothing is owing us servants,3 Reward? We have only done our duty.’ ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- The Theological Cluster of the “Parable of the Obedient Servant.” 1. The believer is a servant/slave who is expected to obey and know his/her place as a servant. 2. Grace/salvation is a gift, not a reward for services rendered. 3. The servant of God labors to fulfill a duty. He does not develop a claim on God nor serve to receive rewards. 4. God is the master of the believer. Yet, at the same time, the believer’s servanthood is appropriately fulfilled in obedience to God’s unique agent/Son Jesus who should be served with diligence and loyalty.4 3 My translation. 4 For a full discussion of this parable see: K. E. Bailey, Through Peasant Eyes, 1980, pp. 114-126.

title: PARABLES
text: 44 The Widow and the Unjust Judge (the Theological Cluster) 1. Prayer conquers fear. 2. Persistence in prayer is appropriate to piety. 3. In prayer the believer addresses a loving Father (not a capricious judge). 4. God must, and indeed does, put His anger aside to hear the prayers of the faithful, for their cry for vind ication does not make them holy. 5. God is at work in history and will accomplish His purposes and vindicate His elect. 6. A woman is used as an example for the faithful to emulate. Ben Sirach tells a similar parable. Only he starts with a woman and then shifts to a male figure. Jesus starts with a woman and th en makes application to the elect in general. The sexist bias of Ben Sir ach is missing. Thus the status of women in the believing community is enhanced by the way the parable is told. 7. The cry for vindication requires self-examination lest the faithful themselves fail to keep faith.

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Introduction The story of redemption is, at its core, a story of rest. However, this rest is not merely the cessation of activity or a fleeting moment of peace; it is the profound, eternal peace found only in the fulfillment of God’s redemptive plan through Soteria . In the first volume of this series, Soteria: The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption , we explored the unshakeable gift of sonship and the power of redemption. Now, in this second volume, we turn our focus to the next chapter of this divine narrative, the pursuit of rest. Here, we will examine how Soteria, the unbreakable gift of salvation, ultimately culminates in the perfect rest that God has promised His people. In the first volume, we unpacked the transformative power of sonship, the glorious truth that, through Christ, we are adopted into God’s family, redeemed from sin and death, and made heirs to His eternal promises. This gift of sonship is the foundation of our salvation, and it is through this very salvation that we are invited into a rest that transcends all earthly striving. Soteria, as we discovered, is not only about the forgiveness of sin; it is about being restored to our rightful place in God’s Kingdom, where we can walk in the fullness of life He has always intended for us. However, Soteria is not merely a past event, it carries profound implications for both our present and our future. It is the foundation upon which Rest is built. The unbreakable gift of sonship leads us not just to a future hope but into a present experience of peace, with the ultimate rest that God has promised awaiting us in the new heavens and new earth. 1

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: From the very beginning of creation, God set the pattern for rest - not out of necessity, but as a sign of the perfect peace that comes from His completed work (Genesis 2:2). Yet, humanity’s fall disrupted that rest. The story of salvation, then, becomes the story of God’s relentless work to restore His creation to that original peace. The rest promised to Israel in the land of Canaan was but a shadow of the true rest that would come in Christ, a rest entered not through physical means, but by faith in the finished work of Christ on the cross. As we explore the twin themes of Soteria and Rest, we will see that salvation is not just about being saved from sin; it is about being brought into the eternal rest that God has prepared for His people. This rest is not only a future reality, it is a present experience for all who are in Christ. It is rooted in the completion of Christ’s work on the cross and flows from the assurance of our sonship and our place in God’s eternal Kingdom. It is the peace that transcends life’s struggles and empowers us to endure with joy and perseverance. Through Scripture, we see the deep connection between salvation and rest. From God’s promises to Abraham and Israel, through the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus, to the final hope of new creation in Revelation, rest is a key part of God’s redemptive plan. The writer of Hebrews reminds us that while Israel’s journey to the promised land was a type of rest, the true rest is only found in Christ (Hebrews 4:9). Christ’s completed work on the cross has made it possible for us to enter into God’s rest, not just in the future, but now. 2

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: In this second volume of the Soteria series, we will explore how Soteria leads to Rest. We will see that the unbreakable gift of sonship is the key to entering into the ultimate peace God has prepared for His people. It is my prayer that, as you engage with this book, you will experience a deeper, more profound rest in your own life, both now and in the age to come. As we continue this journey, let us remember that in Christ, Soteria and Rest are inseparable. The gift of sonship and the promise of rest are not just theological truths to be acknowledged, they are realities to be lived. May you find peace in the sufficiency of Christ’s work, and may His unbreakable gift of salvation establish you into the eternal rest that He alone can give. 3

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Preface Since the inception of our church in Canada in 2020, I have made it a point to dedicate time each year to teaching on the topic of Rest . It has become a central theme in the life of our congregation, a truth I believe is essential for every believer to grasp and experience. The subject of rest, however, is not a simple one. It is not a concept that can be fully exhausted in a single sermon, a single year, or even a single lifetime. It is an inexhaustible topic that speaks to the very core of God’s redemptive plan for humanity. As we journey through the demands of modern life, we often find ourselves weary physically, mentally, and spiritually. Yet, the Bible presents rest not as an optional escape but as a vital part of God’s design for us. It is a rest that transcends mere physical sleep, reaching into the deepest parts of our souls and inviting us into the peace that only God can give. This is the kind of rest that we see from the very beginning of creation and that God continues to offer through the finished work of Jesus Christ. Over the years, I have seen how this teaching on rest has impacted the lives of those in our congregation, bringing freedom from anxiety, healing from exhaustion, and a deeper connection with God’s eternal peace. Each year, as we revisit this theme, new layers of understanding unfold, and the richness of God’s promise of rest becomes even more profound. As the church continues to grow, it is clear to me that rest is not just a seasonal topic, but an enduring truth that must continue to be explored in fresh ways, as it remains central to our Christian walk. 4

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: This volume, Soteria and Rest: The Story of Redemption from Eden to Eternity , builds upon the foundation we have laid over these years. It is my prayer that this book will serve as both a reflection on the rest that we already have in Christ and an invitation to enter into the deeper rest that is still to come. As you journey through these pages, may you be reminded of the unbreakable gift of sonship and redemption, and may you experience the rest that only Christ can provide, a rest that is available now and that will be fully realized in the age to come. As you read, I invite you to open your heart to the inexhaustible truth of God’s rest, one that invites us to lay down our burdens and find our peace in Him. 5

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Chapter 1 In the Beginning - God’s Purpose Was Rest “And on the seventh day God ended his work which he had made; and he rested on the seventh day from all his work which he had made.” - Genesis 2:2 (KJV) In the Genesis creation account, the seventh day marks not the end of something, but the beginning of everything. While man was created on the sixth day, and indeed this is significant, the divine narrative reaches its peak in the seventh day, the day of God’s rest. This rest, often misunderstood, is not an indication that God was weary, for He is the omnipotent One who “fainteth not, neither is weary” (Isa. 40:28). Rather, rest in Genesis 2 signifies enthronement, dominion, and satisfaction. It is a theological statement of order and presence of God taking up residence in the world He has created. John H. Walton’s contribution to this understanding is monumental. In his work The Lost World of Genesis One , Walton contends that the creation narrative mirrors an ancient Near Eastern temple inauguration. According to him, the cosmos is being ordered, not merely fabricated, and that the goal of this ordering is to make creation a suitable dwelling place for God. The seventh day, then, is when God takes His place in the cosmic temple to begin His rule. “Rest,” in this context, is what a deity does in His temple, it 6

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: is not inactivity, but the active presence of God among His people in a stabilized, ordered cosmos. Genesis 2 supports this claim profoundly. Eden is not just a garden; it is portrayed as sacred space. The language of divine presence - God walking in the garden (Genesis 3:8) - recalls temple imagery. Later Scriptures, like Ezekiel 28, confirm Eden was understood as a mountain sanctuary, a garden-temple where heaven and earth overlapped. Adam, placed within this holy realm, is more than a gardener, he is a priest. The Hebrew verbs used to describe his role, abad (to work) and shamar (to keep), are the same used for the duties of the Levites in maintaining the tabernacle (cf. Numbers 3:7-8). In other words, Adam’s role was liturgical and representative. He was called to maintain sacred order, to minister before the Lord, and to extend Eden’s borders until the whole earth became the dwelling place of God. The theological implications are staggering. Man was created not merely to survive, but to abide in the rest of God. He was not intended to work toward rest, but to work from rest. His identity and assignment were to flow from communion with the Divine. The Sabbath was not man’s reward, it was man’s context. Before Adam ever worked the ground, he woke up into a world where the first full day of his existence was a day of rest. Theologically, this sets the pattern of grace before works, presence before performance, delight before duty. As Geerhardus Vos rightly observed, “The goal of creation was not merely life, but communion with God; not merely existence, but Sabbath rest.” 7

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: But this rest was violated. Sin entered the sacred space, and Eden - God’s temple - was defiled. Adam and Eve chose autonomy over intimacy, independence over abiding. The result was exile, a tragic reversal of the Sabbath. They were driven out of the garden, away from the tree of life and the presence of the Lord. Cherubim were stationed at the entrance, bearing flaming swords, not as decorations, but as guards of holy space. The divine rest was lost, not in God Himself, but in human experience. Man was plunged into toil, pain, and mortality. Rest was replaced by restlessness. Yet even in the judgment, God spoke of redemption. The promise of the Seed (Genesis 3:15) pointed to a restoration not merely of innocence, but of access of rest. The entire biblical narrative unfolds from this tension: the lost rest of Eden and God’s relentless mission to restore it. From patriarchs to prophets, from tabernacle to temple, the question resounds: How will God dwell with man again? When God called Israel out of Egypt, He brought them not just to freedom, but toward rest. The Sabbath was reintroduced not just as a day, but as a declaration. In Exodus 20, it points back to creation; in Deuteronomy 5, it points back to redemption. In both, the Sabbath speaks of identity, Israel is not a nation of slaves, but a covenant people. The weekly rhythm of rest becomes a sign that their lives are ordered by the sufficiency of God, not the scarcity of Egypt. As Abraham Heschel put it, “The Sabbath is a temple in time.” It is a portable Eden, a reminder that life begins not with hustle, but with godliness. 8

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Yet the Sabbath also anticipated something greater. The rest it offered was only a shadow of a deeper, eternal rest that could only be found in the Messiah. Jesus came as the fulfillment of the Sabbath, the true Temple, and the second Adam. In Him, the garden is restored. He is both the Priest and the Place of God’s presence. His life was marked by perfect communion with the Father; His death tore the veil; His resurrection inaugurated the new creation. When He cried “It is finished” (John 19:30), He echoed the rest of Genesis 2 - not the end of effort, but the completion of purpose. The writer of Hebrews captures this tension with deep theological beauty: “There remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God. For he that is entered into his rest, he also hath ceased from his own works, as God did from his” (Hebrews 4:9-10). This rest is both already and not yet. It is available now, in Christ, through faith. Yet it will find its consummation in the resurrection, when Eden is not merely remembered but restored - in the new heavens and the new earth, where God will dwell with His people forever. The call of this theology is urgent. To enter God’s rest is not to abandon effort, but to abandon self-sufficiency. It is to live not from striving but from surrender. To cease from our works, not from activity, but from the soul-crushing belief that our identity and approval come from performance. Christ invites us, “Come unto me, all ye that labour and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest” (Matt. 11:28). This is more than a moment of peace, it is a Person. In Him, rest is restored. In Him, we return to Eden. In Him, we become the temple. 9

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Thus, the beginning of creation and the beginning of redemption are joined by one divine purpose: that God may dwell with us. Rest is not a pause in God’s plan, it is the plan. It is not the end of the story, it is the meaning of the story. And so, in this journey through Soteria and Rest, we must first see that salvation is not a rescue from the world, but a restoration of communion, a reentry into the seventh day. The door that was once guarded by flaming cherubim is now opened by pierced hands. We are called to return, to abide, to rest, not just on the Sabbath, but forever. Welcome back to the beginning. Welcome to the place of rest. 10

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Chapter 2 The Great Loss - What Really Happened in Eden “Therefore the LORD God sent him forth from the garden of Eden, to till the ground from whence he was taken. So he drove out the man…” - Genesis 3:23-24 (KJV) To truly appreciate the significance of salvation and the promised rest in Christ, it is imperative to understand the depth of humanity’s original loss. The fall of man, as recorded in Genesis 3, is not merely an account of moral disobedience; it is theologically a cosmic fracture, an event that disrupted the order of creation and exiled humanity from divine presence. It must be interpreted not only as a transgression of command but also as a forfeiture of the very environment in which man was created to thrive: the presence of God. The garden of Eden was not simply a location of pristine nature or unspoiled resources. In both ancient Near Eastern context and biblical theology, Eden functions as the archetypal temple. This is supported by the imagery and geography described in Genesis 2, the presence of a river flowing out to water the garden, the trees of life and knowledge at its center, the precious stones, and especially the eastward orientation and the guarding cherubim at its entrance (Gen. 3:24), all of which are replicated in later tabernacle and temple structures (cf. Exodus 25-27; 1 Kings 6-8; Ezekiel 28). 11

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Eden was the original sanctuary. Its sanctity was not intrinsic to its flora but was derived from the presence of Yahweh Elohim who walked there (Gen. 3:8). Thus, the garden was the locus of God’s rest, the space where the Creator ceased from His work, not due to fatigue, but because the goal of creation had been reached: communion with humanity in a shared sacred space. The telos of creation was not simply man, but man in fellowship with God in a consecrated cosmos. The loss, therefore, is not best understood in terms of expulsion from a place, but rather in terms of alienation from divine presence. The act of disobedience, eating the fruit from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, was a theological crisis. It was a declaration of self-rule and autonomy, a rejection of God’s authority and sufficiency. Scholars such as Augustine characterized this act as curvatus in se - the soul curved in on itself. The prohibition was not arbitrary; it functioned as a covenantal test of trust. The tree represented the prerogative of moral autonomy, and to eat of it was to claim sovereignty over moral discernment. The serpent’s temptation “Ye shall be as gods, knowing good and evil” (Gen. 3:5) was a direct challenge to divine exclusivity. In succumbing, man did not merely break a rule; he severed a relationship. The immediate aftermath underscores this shift. “And the eyes of them both were opened, and they knew that they were naked” (Gen. 3:7). Nakedness here is not merely physical but relational and theological. It is a loss of innocence, a rupture of transparency, and an emergence of shame. When they heard the sound of God walking in the garden, they hid 12

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: themselves (Gen. 3:8). What was once a joy, God’s nearness, now evoked dread. The relational axis had shifted. The covenant had been fractured. The question God posed “Where art thou?” is not geographical but existential. It signifies the dislocation of man from his Creator. The divine presence that once enveloped man is now perceived as a threat. The garments of fig leaves sewn together signify a premature and inadequate attempt to manage shame. They also reveal that the human condition, post-fall, instinctively seeks to cover rather than confess. The consequences of this transgression unfold in layers. To the serpent, enmity is pronounced. To the woman, pain in childbirth and relational tension. To the man, toil and futility. But at the center of this pronouncement is a deeper theological truth: the ground is cursed for man’s sake, and the rhythms of rest are disrupted. Genesis 3:19 concludes, “In the sweat of thy face shalt thou eat bread, till thou return unto the ground…” The imagery is of a reversal, man, who came from dust and was given breath, now returns to dust without rest. The expulsion from Eden is described in emphatic terms: “So he drove out the man; and he placed at the east of the garden of Eden Cherubims, and a flaming sword...” (Gen. 3:24). The verb “drove out” ( garash in Hebrew) carries connotations of forceful eviction, as used in later contexts for the dispossession of nations. It indicates not just removal but disinheritance. Access to the tree of life is now sealed. The cherubim and flaming sword are not symbolic of judgment alone, they are liturgical guardians of sacred space, recurring in temple imagery (cf. Exodus 25:18-22; 1 Kings 13

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: 6:23-28). Thus, Eden becomes the first Holy of Holies that man can no longer enter. What is lost in Eden, therefore, is not only geographical access, but ontological harmony. Man is alienated from God, from creation, from the other, and from self. This alienation becomes the backdrop against which all of redemptive history unfolds. The temple/tabernacle system in Israel’s religious life is a mediated reintroduction to Eden’s presence, complete with cherubim, sacrifice, and restricted access. But these are shadows, not substance. This fall inaugurates a condition of spiritual death, alienation from the source of life. Paul, in Romans 5, traces this condition to Adam as the representative head of humanity: “By one man sin entered into the world, and death by sin...” (Rom. 5:12). The disorder introduced is not confined to human relationships but extends to creation itself. Romans 8:20-22 describes the entire cosmos groaning under the weight of this fall, subjected to futility, awaiting the manifestation of the sons of God. And yet, embedded within the judgment narrative of Genesis 3 is a forward-looking promise, what the Church Fathers later called the Protoevangelium - the first gospel. “And I will put enmity between thee and the woman… it shall bruise thy head, and thou shalt bruise his heel” (Gen. 3:15). Though obscure in its immediate context, this passage later emerges in messianic expectation. The bruised heel and crushed head suggest a conflict that ends in victory, though at a cost. The Seed of the 14

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: woman, an anomaly in patriarchal genealogies, will one day restore what has been lost. The shedding of blood to clothe Adam and Eve (Gen. 3:21) introduces yet another theological motif: atonement through substitution. The fig leaves are replaced with “coats of skins,” implying the death of an animal. Though understated, this act is a divine provision that foreshadows the sacrificial system and ultimately the cross of Christ. God does not abandon man to his own coverings. He provides. In summary, what happened in Eden was not merely a moral lapse, but a theological catastrophe. It was the disruption of divine rest, the loss of God’s presence, and the inauguration of exile. The remainder of Scripture unfolds as the narrative of divine recovery. God’s redemptive plan is not simply to rescue man from sin but to bring man back into His rest, the rest first modeled in creation, disrupted in Eden, and pursued throughout covenant history until it is fully realized in Christ. The doctrine of rest, therefore, cannot be divorced from the doctrine of the fall. To speak of salvation is to speak of restoration, not only of status, but of space. The heavenly sanctuary, of which Eden was a prototype and the tabernacle a shadow, finds its fulfillment in the Incarnate Word, in whom God once again “tabernacled” among us (John 1:14). Christ is the new temple, the true access point to the presence of God. And it is in Him that the exiles return, not only forgiven, but home. 15

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Chapter 3 Shadows of Redemption - Promise in the Patriarchs “And in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed; because thou hast obeyed my voice.” - Genesis 22:18 (KJV) The fall of man in Eden initiated a rupture so profound that only a divine initiative could bring reconciliation. Yet, as early as Genesis 3:15, we encountered the first whisper of hope, the Protoevangelium , a veiled promise that the seed of the woman would ultimately overcome the adversary. This notion of “seed” (Hebrew: zera ) becomes the golden thread woven throughout redemptive history, gradually clarified in covenantal stages. Nowhere is this thread more clearly defined in its embryonic form than in the call and covenant of Abraham. Here, in the person of one man, we encounter a shadow of redemption that will cast its light across generations, nations, and eternity. Abraham’s narrative is not merely historical; it is paradigmatic. In him, we see the early contours of Soteria , God’s plan to save, restore, and bless the nations. He is not a private recipient of divine favor but the covenantal prototype through whom God inaugurates His mission to redeem the world. 16

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: The Call: A New Beginning (Genesis 12) Genesis 12 introduces a decisive turning point in the biblical narrative, marking the formal beginning of what theologians often refer to as the patriarchal period of redemptive history. After the sweeping universality of Genesis 1-11, where creation, fall, judgment, and dispersion are portrayed on a global scale, God narrows His redemptive focus by calling one man, Abram (later Abraham), through whom He intends to accomplish His universal purposes. This narrowing is not an abandonment of the nations, but a strategy through which blessing would flow to all nations (cf. Gen. 12:3). What seems like a move toward particularity is, in fact, the divine means of restoring universality. The call of Abram functions as a theological hinge. Whereas Genesis 3-11 highlights the rupture caused by sin, alienation from God, social disintegration, judgment by flood, and the confusion of languages, Genesis 12 marks a divine intervention to initiate the reversal of this downward spiral. God’s words to Abram in Genesis 12:1-3 are both imperative and promissory, calling Abram out of his land and simultaneously assuring him of divine purpose and blessing. “Now the LORD had said unto Abram, Get thee out of thy country, and from thy kindred, and from thy father’s house, unto a land that I will shew thee” (Gen. 12:1, KJV). This calling is emblematic of what theologians describe as the divine summons, a call that originates in the will of God, not human initiative. It 17

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: is unilateral in its inception but bilateral in its outworking, requiring human response in the form of trust and obedience. Abram’s journey from Ur to Canaan is thus not only a geographical relocation, but a paradigmatic act of faith. The writer of Hebrews captures this when he says, “By faith Abraham…obeyed; and he went out, not knowing whither he went” (Heb. 11:8, KJV). Abram becomes the archetype of faith, one who leaves the known in pursuit of the unseen based solely on divine promise. At the heart of God’s promise to Abram is a triadic blessing, land, seed, and universal blessing (Gen. 12:1-3). These three themes are not arbitrary but are rich in theological meaning and are deeply tethered to the creation narrative: ● Land points back to Eden, where humanity was originally placed to dwell in communion with God. The promise of a land is a step toward restoring that dwelling space where God and His people can coexist in peace. ● Seed (or offspring) echoes the creational command to “be fruitful and multiply” and invokes the protoevangelium of Genesis 3:15, where the seed of the woman would one day crush the serpent’s head. This aspect of the promise ultimately points forward to Christ, the true seed (cf. Gal. 3:16). ● Blessing recalls God’s original benediction over creation (Gen. 1:28) and reverses the curse that has pervaded human experience 18

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: since the Fall. In Abraham, the world will once again experience the favor and peace of God. In this way, the Abrahamic covenant is not a novel departure from earlier themes, but a reinstatement and escalation of God’s original intent. It is a reorientation of history, placing one man and his descendants at the center of God’s unfolding drama of redemption. Abraham’s life becomes the foundation upon which the rest of salvation history is built, from Isaac and Jacob, through Moses and David, to the coming of Christ, who is the fulfillment of every covenantal promise (cf. 2 Cor. 1:20). Additionally, the covenant with Abraham is marked by both promise and process. While the promises are declarative, their fulfillment unfolds over time and through various crises. Abraham does not receive the land in his lifetime, nor does he see the full extent of the blessing to the nations. Yet, the Scriptures present him as a man who looked beyond the temporal toward the eschatological city “whose builder and maker is God” (Heb. 11:10). This narrative also reinforces a crucial biblical principle: God’s redemptive agenda is relational and covenantal. He does not merely act on behalf of humanity from a distance; He enters into binding relationships that progressively unfold through history. The covenant with Abraham serves as the genesis of Israel’s identity and the launchpad for redemptive history, ultimately pointing toward the new covenant mediated by Jesus Christ. 19

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: In conclusion, Genesis 12 is not merely about the journey of an ancient patriarch, it is the divine answer to the chaos of Genesis 3-11, the reassertion of God’s intention to bless and dwell with humanity. Abraham’s call is a new beginning, not just for Israel, but for all of humanity. It marks the inauguration of a redemptive trajectory that moves from Eden lost to Eden restored, from exile to inheritance, from curse to blessing, from restlessness to rest. The Covenant Formalized: Genesis 15 Genesis 15 is one of the most theologically significant chapters in the Hebrew Bible. It marks a climactic moment in the Abrahamic narrative, not only reaffirming God's promises but formally ratifying them through covenantal ritual. Whereas Genesis 12 introduced the divine initiative to bless all nations through Abraham, Genesis 15 clarifies how that promise would be established and secured, not through human strength or qualification, but through divine fidelity, covenant grace, and justifying faith. Divine Assurance in the Face of Delay The chapter opens with God’s comforting words to Abram: “Fear not, Abram: I am thy shield, and thy exceeding great reward” (Gen. 15:1, KJV). Despite his military victory in Genesis 14 and his refusal to enrich himself through worldly alliances, Abram remains in a state of existential tension. The promise of offspring and inheritance still lingers unfulfilled. His response reveals this inner disquiet: 20

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: “Lord GOD, what wilt thou give me, seeing I go childless, and the steward of my house is this Eliezer of Damascus?” (v. 2). Abram's question is not one of unbelief but of clarification, a longing for understanding in the face of a promise deferred. God's answer is not merely verbal reassurance but a revelatory gesture: “Look now toward heaven, and tell the stars, if thou be able to number them: and he said unto him, So shall thy seed be” (v. 5). While many interpret this as an invitation to count the stars numerically, a closer reading, especially of the Hebrew verb sāphar ( סָפַר ,) opens a richer possibility. The word can mean "to count," but also "to declare," "to recount," or "to tell a story" (cf. Psalm 19:1-4: “The heavens declare the glory of God…” ). From this perspective, God may be inviting Abram to recount the testimony inscribed in the heavens, interpreting the stars not merely as a numeric metaphor but as a medium of redemptive proclamation. The heavens, in biblical thought, are not silent. They are revelatory. Thus, Abram is being called to behold the redemptive storyline embedded in creation and to trust that his seed will play a central role in that cosmic narrative. Justifying Faith: The Heart of Covenant It is against this backdrop that one of Scripture’s most profound theological assertions is made: “And he believed in the LORD; and he counted it to him for righteousness” (Gen. 15:6). 21

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: This verse becomes the bedrock for Paul’s doctrine of justification by faith (Rom. 4:3; Gal. 3:6). Here, hundreds of years before the giving of the Law, before the rite of circumcision (Gen. 17), and before Sinai, righteousness is reckoned, not by works, rituals, or ethnicity, but by trust in the revealed character and promise of God. This anticipates the gospel in its essence: salvation is grounded in grace, accessed through faith, and accomplished by God alone. Abram does not earn righteousness; he receives it. He does not fulfill a law; he believes a promise. His act of faith becomes the prototype of covenantal relationship throughout redemptive history. In Pauline theology, this moment is not merely about Abram’s personal justification, but serves as the archetype for how God justifies the ungodly who believe (cf. Romans 4:5). The Covenant Ritual: Grace in Blood and Smoke In verses 9-21, the narrative shifts to a covenant-cutting ceremony. Abram is commanded to prepare specific animals: a heifer, a goat, a ram, a turtledove, and a pigeon. He divides the larger animals in half, arranging them opposite each other. This reflects an established Ancient Near Eastern treaty ritual, known as the blood path covenant (cf. Jer. 34:18-20), where two parties would walk between the slaughtered pieces to invoke a curse upon themselves should they violate the covenant. However, what follows is utterly unexpected and theologically stunning. Abram, rather than walking the path, falls into a “deep sleep” ( tardemah in 22

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Hebrew, v. 12). This term, used earlier in Genesis 2:21 when God caused Adam to sleep before creating Eve, signals a moment of divine action independent of human effort. Abram becomes a passive observer, a silent witness to the mystery of God’s unilateral grace. In the midst of that darkness and dread, a smoking furnace and a burning lamp pass between the pieces (v. 17). These symbols of divine presence, evoking the pillar of cloud and fire in the Exodus, indicate that God alone binds Himself to the covenant. In essence, God is saying: If this covenant fails, let the curse fall on Me . This is a radical act of divine self-obligation. God assumes full responsibility for the fulfillment of His word. Christ the Fulfillment: Covenant and Cross The significance of this moment cannot be overstated. Genesis 15 presents a covenant secured not by mutual obligation but by divine initiative. It sets the stage for the New Testament fulfillment in Christ. Just as God alone passed through the sacrificial pieces, Christ alone bore the covenantal curse, “Christ hath redeemed us from the curse of the law, being made a curse for us” (Gal. 3:13). The sacrificial drama of Genesis 15 anticipates the crucifixion, where the Son of God, representing both the divine party and the human recipient, offers Himself as the mediator of a better covenant. The blood spilled in Genesis 15 foreshadows the blood of the cross, where justice and mercy 23

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: meet, and the promises of God find their Yes and Amen in Christ (2 Cor. 1:20). The Apostle Paul’s reflection in Romans 4 affirms this connection. Just as Abraham was justified by faith before the Law, so believers, Jew and Gentile alike, are justified by faith apart from works. The Abrahamic covenant, ratified by God alone, becomes the foundational model for the New Covenant in Christ. The Gospel Pattern Revealed Genesis 15 is more than a historical or cultural artifact. It is a theological window into the very heart of redemptive history. It reveals the pattern that echoes from Eden to Calvary: ● Divine initiative (promise), ● Human response (faith), ● Covenantal ratification (blood), and ● Unilateral grace (God walks the path). It affirms that the God who made the heavens has inscribed His redemptive plan in both creation and covenant, calling us not to achieve but to believe. It is not a story of human qualification, but of divine condescension. In this narrative, we are reminded that faith is not a leap in the dark, but a trust in what God has revealed, both in the heavens and in the covenant, ultimately fulfilled in Christ. 24

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: The Sign of the Covenant: Genesis 17 Genesis 17 marks a decisive moment in the Abrahamic narrative, an epochal encounter in which God deepens, personalizes, and formalizes the covenant with Abraham. Coming on the heels of the events in Genesis 15, where God unilaterally pledged Himself to the covenant through a blood-path ritual, Genesis 17 introduces the covenant sign and unfolds its communal and eschatological dimensions. This chapter not only reshapes Abraham's identity and expectations but situates his story more explicitly within the framework of divine election, covenantal responsibility, and Messianic hope. A Renewed Revelation: “I Am El Shaddai” The chapter opens with divine self-disclosure: “And when Abram was ninety years old and nine, the LORD appeared to Abram, and said unto him, I am the Almighty God [El Shaddai]; walk before me, and be thou perfect” (Gen. 17:1, KJV). This introduction is theologically loaded. The name El Shaddai first used here highlights God's all-sufficiency and sovereign power, especially in the face of Abraham's frailty, aging body, and delayed promise. The command to “walk before me and be perfect” (Heb. tāmîm ) calls Abraham to covenantal fidelity. This is not a demand for moral perfection in the abstract, but a wholehearted devotion consistent with his new role as the father of nations. In covenantal language, “blamelessness” is the 25

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: expected response to divine grace (cf. Deut. 18:13; Job 1:1), not its prerequisite. Covenant Reaffirmed and Renamed In verse 2, God reaffirms His promise: “I will make my covenant between me and thee, and will multiply thee exceedingly.” This echoes earlier promises (Gen. 12, 15) but now comes with a deeper personal transformation. Abram is renamed Abraham, “father of a multitude” signifying a new identity that matches God’s expansive purpose: not merely a biological father, but the patriarch of many nations (Gen. 17:5). Name changes in Scripture often accompany divine commissioning (cf. Jacob to Israel, Simon to Peter). In this light, Abraham’s renaming is both a prophetic declaration and a theological shift: he is no longer simply the recipient of blessing, but the vehicle of blessing to the world. His identity becomes inseparable from his redemptive vocation. The Covenant Sign: Circumcision The centerpiece of Genesis 17 is the institution of circumcision as the covenant sign: “This is my covenant, which ye shall keep… Every man child among you shall be circumcised” (v. 10). Circumcision, the physical cutting away of the foreskin, serves both symbolic and theological functions: 1. It is a sign of belonging to a people uniquely set apart for God. 26

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: 2. It is a mark in the flesh, underscoring that the covenant is not merely verbal or spiritual, but embodied. 3. It points forward to a deeper reality: the need for an inner transformation, a circumcision of the heart (cf. Deut. 10:16; 30:6; Jer. 4:4). In Pauline theology, circumcision becomes a type or shadow that finds fulfillment in the regenerate heart (Rom. 2:28-29). As Paul writes, “he is a Jew, which is one inwardly; and circumcision is that of the heart, in the spirit…” In other words, the outward ritual anticipates an inward work of grace. This is not a contradiction but a progression: the sign points forward to its spiritual substance. Covenant Universality and Eternality A crucial feature of Genesis 17 is the phrase “an everlasting covenant” (v. 7), used to describe the nature of God's commitment to Abraham and his seed. The Hebrew term ʿolām often denotes not merely infinite duration but enduring purpose within God’s salvific plan. This “everlasting” covenant is not static or merely ethnic. Its scope is cosmic and eschatological. Indeed, while the chapter deals with land, lineage, and literal posterity, it also prefigures a larger redemptive agenda. Paul interprets this in Galatians 3:16: “Now to Abraham and his seed were the promises made. He saith not, And to seeds, as of many; but as of one, And to thy seed, which is Christ.” 27

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: In this light, the Abrahamic covenant is Messianic at its core. The “everlasting” promise finds its ultimate fulfillment not merely in a geopolitical nation but in a person, Jesus the Christ, who is both the seed of Abraham and the Savior of the world (cf. Matt. 1:1; Luke 1:72-73). The physical descendants are part of the story, but the climax of the covenant is spiritual and universal: “If ye be Christ’s, then are ye Abraham’s seed, and heirs according to the promise” (Gal. 3:29). The Pattern of Redemptive Signs Circumcision as a covenant sign fits a broader biblical pattern: God’s covenants are often accompanied by visible signs (e.g., the rainbow in Genesis 9, the Sabbath in Exodus 31, baptism in the New Covenant). These signs serve as visible, communal reminders of invisible, divine promises. Yet, signs are always subordinate to the substance. Circumcision without covenant faithfulness becomes empty ritual (cf. Jer. 9:25-26). This is precisely the critique the prophets and apostles bring. The sign must correspond to the inward reality; otherwise, it becomes a liability rather than a legacy. Sarah’s Role and the Reversal of Barrenness An often-overlooked element of Genesis 17 is the transformation of Sarai to Sarah (v. 15), and the promise that she too will be the mother of nations. In ancient patriarchal contexts, covenantal focus often rested solely on the male. But here, God explicitly includes Sarah in the covenantal promise, 28

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: even naming the future son, Isaac, meaning “laughter” signifying the reversal of barrenness and the joy of promise. This inclusion reinforces that the covenant is not merely transactional or genealogical, but relational and miraculous. The child of promise will be born not through natural capacity but divine enablement, foreshadowing the pattern of supernatural birth that climaxes in the virgin birth of Christ. A Covenant Marked in Flesh, Fulfilled in Christ Genesis 17 is not merely about ancient rituals or ethnic identity; it is a theological turning point that reveals how God's redemptive plan is marked, remembered, and fulfilled. It shows us that: ● God binds Himself to His people with visible signs, rooted in grace but requiring a response of faith. ● Covenant identity involves transformation of name, heart, and destiny. ● The Abrahamic covenant ultimately points to Christ, who embodies and fulfills its promises as the true Seed. ● The sign of circumcision anticipates the circumcision of the heart, the internal work of grace accomplished by the Spirit under the New Covenant. The chapter invites us not merely to admire Abraham's faith or Israel's history but to trace the unfolding gospel logic of Scripture: God’s covenant 29

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: is everlasting not because it is locked in antiquity, but because it finds eternal fulfillment in Christ. As the greater Seed of Abraham, He brings the nations into covenant blessing, not through the cutting of flesh, but through the cutting away of sin, the new birth, and the seal of the Spirit. The Seed and the Nations At the heart of the Abrahamic covenant is a singular, potent term that threads its way through the entire biblical narrative: “seed” the Hebrew word zeraʿ . This term is loaded, carrying both collective and singular significance. On one level, it refers to Abraham’s natural descendants, a multitude as vast as the stars of heaven and the sand on the seashore (Gen. 15:5; 22:17). On another, more profound level, it foreshadows a singular, messianic figure who will bring the divine promise to its climactic fulfillment. The word's grammatical ambiguity allows for this dual reading, and the biblical writers, especially the apostles, lean heavily into this layered meaning. In Genesis 22:18, after the dramatic near-sacrifice of Isaac, a pivotal moment of faith and divine provision, God declares, “And in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed; because thou hast obeyed my voice.” Here, the covenant language shifts from being national to international in scope. What was once perceived as a promise to a family line becomes a redemptive promise to all nations. Importantly, the phrase “in thy seed” ( zeraʿ ) carries an inclusive vision of global blessing. This is not merely 30

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
