The warning comes immediately after: “Today if ye will hear his voice, harden not your hearts…” (Heb. 3:7-8) This echoes Psalm 95 and reminds the readers of Israel’s rebellion. Though they had seen God’s mighty acts, their hearts turned from Him. They had miracles without maturity, deliverance without devotion. Unbelief, not disobedience alone, was the root problem. Hebrews 3:12 warns: “Take heed, brethren, lest there be in any of you an evil heart of unbelief, in departing from the living God.” Notice the phrase: departing from the living God . The danger is not theoretical unbelief but relational departure. To disbelieve God is to distance oneself from Him. And the result is forfeited rest. The implication is sobering: rest is available, but not automatic. It must be entered into by faith, and unbelief can keep a believer from experiencing the fullness of salvation. 146

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: The Gospel of Rest in Hebrews 4 Hebrews 4 begins with an urgent plea: “Let us therefore fear, lest, a promise being left us of entering into his rest, any of you should seem to come short of it.” (v. 1) The Gospel, we are told, was preached to them as it is to us (v. 2). But the word did not profit them, not being mixed with faith. The implication is that the same gospel promise, rest in God, is still offered, and still requires faith to access. Then comes a key theological declaration: “For we which have believed do enter into rest…” (v. 3) This is stunning. Rest is not a distant hope but a present reality. Believers, those who trust in Christ have already entered into divine rest. It is both now and not yet, but its essence is available today. Hebrews 4 goes on to demonstrate that God’s rest began at creation: “And God did rest the seventh day from all his works.” (v. 4) This is not because God was tired, but because His work was finished. Divine rest is completion, not fatigue. The rest we enter is the same rest God entered after creation. In Christ, our salvation is finished (John 19:30), and we are invited into that same repose. 147

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Rest Is a Person, Not a Place The climactic point comes in verse 10: “For he that is entered into his rest, he also hath ceased from his own works, as God did from his.” This verse connects divine rest to cessation from works. It echoes Paul’s teaching in Romans 4:5 - “to him that worketh not, but believeth...” The believer’s rest is not laziness, but liberation from self-justification. You are not striving to earn acceptance - you are resting in Christ’s acceptance. Jesus Himself declares in Matthew 11:28: “Come unto me, all ye that labour and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest.” He does not merely give rest from trouble, He gives rest in Himself. Jesus is the Sabbath. He is the greater Joshua who brings us not to a land but to life eternal. Rest is no longer Saturday. It is no longer Canaan. It is Christ. The Living Word and the Great High Priest Hebrews 4 does not end with rest but with response. Verses 12-16 transition into two essential truths. First, the Word of God: 148

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: “For the word of God is quick, and powerful, and sharper than any twoedged sword…” (v. 12) The Word discerns our hearts, revealing whether we are truly living in faith or drifting in unbelief. Rest is not passive. It involves submission to God’s Word - letting it examine, expose, and heal. Second, the High Priest: “Seeing then that we have a great high priest… let us hold fast our profession… let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of grace…” (vv. 14-16) The final invitation is not to Canaan but to the throne. We draw near not with fear, but with boldness, because our High Priest understands, intercedes, and invites. Rest is not the absence of activity. It is the presence of assurance, assurance rooted in the finished work of Christ and mediated by our heavenly High Priest. Living from Rest To live in the rest that Christ provides is to embody the truth of the gospel in our daily rhythms. This rest is not mere cessation from activity, nor a passive disengagement from life, it is a spiritual posture grounded in the finished work of Christ. 149

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Hebrews 4:9-10 tells us, “There remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God. For he that is entered into his rest, he also hath ceased from his own works, as God did from his.” This is not just eschatological (about heaven); it is deeply existential and immediate. Living from rest means aligning our entire being spirit, soul, and body, with the sufficiency of Christ. Ceasing from Self-Righteousness Living from rest means we no longer strive to earn what has already been freely given. The cross declares, “It is finished” (John 19:30), and the resurrection affirms that the sacrifice was enough (Rom. 4:25). We do not labor to secure God’s love; we labor because we are loved. “Not by works of righteousness which we have done, but according to his mercy he saved us…” - Titus 3:5 This rest challenges the modern Christian tendency toward performance-based spirituality, measuring our worth by spiritual activity, moral behavior, or public fruitfulness. In Christ, we are not laboring for identity; we labor from identity. Rejecting Spiritual Anxiety Many believers oscillate between spiritual elation and despair, depending on their sense of worthiness. But gospel rest reorients us: we are accepted not on the basis of merit but on the basis of union. Ephesians 1:6 says we are “accepted in the Beloved” a permanent status grounded in Christ’s righteousness, not our fluctuating devotion. 150

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Rest, then, becomes an antidote to spiritual anxiety. We are not spiritual orphans trying to impress a distant deity; we are sons and daughters already brought near (Eph. 2:13). Our assurance flows not from how well we hold onto God, but from how securely He holds us (John 10:28-29). Embracing Rhythms of Grace To live from rest is not to abandon diligence, excellence, or obedience but to practice them as expressions of grace, not burdens of guilt. Even in seasons of activity, we can live from rest when we are grounded in God’s sufficiency. This means integrating rhythms of Sabbath, prayer, silence, and contemplation, not as legalistic duties, but as means of staying rooted in the gospel. Jesus Himself said, “Come unto me… and I will give you rest” (Matt. 11:28-29). The rest He offers is not merely a moment of relief but a lifelong way of being. In this sense, rest is more than downtime, it is a discipleship framework. We no longer hustle for approval or scramble for spiritual status. We move at the pace of grace, attentive to the Spirit, resting even while laboring. Empowered Obedience This gospel rest is not opposed to Christian obedience, it is its power source. As Paul writes in Philippians 2:12-13, we are to “work out [our] own salvation with fear and trembling,” but also know that “it is God which worketh in [us] both to will and to do.” This paradox, divine 151

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: empowerment within human responsibility is made possible when we live from rest. We no longer serve out of fear, but out of love. We pursue holiness not to earn God's presence, but because His presence now dwells in us. We forgive not because we must, but because we’ve been forgiven. We love because He first loved us (1 John 4:19). Rest as Resistance To live from rest is, in many ways, an act of spiritual resistance in a world driven by anxiety, performance, and achievement. It is a prophetic declaration that Christ’s work is enough and that we are no longer slaves, but sons. We do not rest because we are lazy, we rest because we believe. And in believing, we become fruitful. We work from rest, not for rest. The “Not Yet” Dimension Though we enter rest now, there is a future consummation. Hebrews 4 speaks of a “Sabbath rest” (v. 9, Greek: sabbatismos ) that remains. This points to the eternal state, the final redemption of all things, the restoration of Eden, the rest that remains for the people of God. Revelation 14:13 confirms this: “Blessed are the dead which die in the Lord… they may rest from their labours…” 152

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Heaven is not an eternal church service. It is eternal communion. The rest we now experience by faith will then be experienced in fullness, unbroken, unending, unveiled. Conclusion To be saved is to enter rest. Not the rest of idleness, but the Sabbath-rest of faith, a soul anchored in the finished work of Christ. Salvation is not earned by merit, nor sustained by effort. It is received by believing, and lived by trusting. “For we which have believed do enter into rest…” -Hebrews 4:3 (KJV) To believe is to cease from striving. It is to lay down the tools of self-justification, to stop building altars of personal merit, and to trust the cross where the final sacrifice was offered. In Christ, the believer finds not only pardon but peace, not only rescue, but repose. To walk with Jesus, then, is to live in rhythm with divine repose. He does not call us into exhaustion but into alignment. “Take my yoke upon you... and you shall find rest for your souls” (Matt. 11:29). This is not theoretical. It is tangible. It is now. The invitation still resounds: “Today if ye will hear His voice, harden not your hearts” - Hebrews 4:7 153

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: This “today” is not merely a date on the calendar. It is a kairos moment, a sacred now, a holy summons into eternal life. It is God’s urgent whisper: cease from your works, enter Mine. Let go of your ladder to heaven and behold the cross where heaven came down. Will you believe? Will you lay aside the burden of performance and pick up the light yoke of grace? Will you enter His rest? Let the striving cease. Let the soul exhale. Let faith rise. Let grace reign. Enter in. 154

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Chapter 10 Resting Places - The Believer as God’s Tabernacle The story of redemption has always involved God desiring to dwell among His people. From Eden’s garden where He walked with Adam in the cool of the day, to the portable tabernacle in the wilderness, to Solomon’s glorious temple, and finally to the person of Jesus Christ, the trajectory of Scripture has pointed to one unchanging truth: God’s heart is to be with man. Yet the ultimate fulfillment of this longing is not in places made by human hands, but in people redeemed by divine love. The New Testament unearths this profound mystery, we, the believers in Christ, have become the very temple of the living God. We are His resting place. The Tabernacle and the Temple: Shadows of the True Dwelling In the unfolding drama of redemptive history, the tabernacle and the temple serve not as ultimate ends, but as types and shadows, temporary pointers to a greater, eternal reality in Christ. These sacred structures, central to Old Testament worship, were God-ordained means by which He could dwell among His covenant people, yet they were never meant to be the final expression of His presence. “And let them make me a sanctuary; that I may dwell among them.” - Exodus 25:8 (KJV) 155

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: The tabernacle, a portable sanctuary constructed under Moses’ leadership, was intricately designed according to the divine pattern shown on the mount. Every element- the ark of the covenant, the mercy seat, the lampstand, the altar , was a sacramental symbol, filled with Christological significance. It was heaven’s architecture temporarily pitched on earth. Later, Solomon’s temple rose in splendor, a fixed and glorious habitation for God’s name. But even this magnificent edifice was not the culmination of divine dwelling. The author of Hebrews makes this clear: “They serve unto the example and shadow of heavenly things” (Hebrews 8:5). The temple, like the tabernacle before it, was a copy, not the consummation. Through the prophet Isaiah, God begins to shift the paradigm: “The heaven is my throne, and the earth is my footstool: where is the house that ye build unto me? and where is the place of my rest?... but to this man will I look, even to him that is poor and of a contrite spirit, and trembleth at my word.” - Isaiah 66:1-2 (KJV) This is not a rejection of sacred space, but a redefinition of it. God is not confined to man-made sanctuaries. The true temple is not of stone but of spirit. Not a location, but a people. We do not go to temples, we are the temple. We do not await a house for God, we are that house (Hebrews 3:6). Let us then live with reverence, carry His presence with purity, and worship Him not in formality, but in intimacy. 156

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Christ: The True Temple and the Rest-Giver The climax of God's progressive self-revelation is found in the person of Jesus Christ. The long-anticipated dwelling of God with His people is no longer mediated through rituals, rooms, or relics, but through a person. John 1:14 proclaims with unmistakable clarity: “And the Word was made flesh, and dwelt among us...” (John 1:14, KJV) The Greek word rendered “dwelt” is eskenōsen literally, "He pitched His tent" or "tabernacled among us." This is not mere poetry. It is theological precision. Jesus is not a symbol of divine presence, He is the substance of it. What was once experienced through types and shadows in the tabernacle and temple has now been made manifest in Him. “In Him dwelleth all the fulness of the Godhead bodily.” (Colossians 2:9, KJV) When Jesus declared, “Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up” (John 2:19), the religious leaders were bewildered. They thought He spoke of the physical temple in Jerusalem. But John interprets: “He spake of the temple of His body” (v. 21). Jesus is the true temple, the new and living meeting place between heaven and earth, between God and humanity. In His incarnation, Jesus fulfilled the dwelling of God. In His crucifixion, He fulfilled the sacrifice. In His resurrection, He became the cornerstone of 157

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: a new spiritual temple: a people filled with His Spirit. As such, Jesus not only restores humanity’s relationship with God, He reconfigures the very concept of sacred space. It is no longer a place, it is a person. The Giver of Rest But there is more. Christ is not only the true temple; He is also the true Rest-Giver. “Come unto me, all ye that labour and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest.” (Matthew 11:28, KJV) This rest is not merely cessation from toil, it is relational repose. It is the soul’s settled confidence in the finished work of the Cross. Through His death and resurrection, Christ made it possible for the Holy Spirit to indwell the believer (John 14:17; Romans 8:9). And where the Spirit dwells, God rests. “This is the rest wherewith ye may cause the weary to rest; and this is the refreshing…” (Isaiah 28:12, KJV) The implication is staggering: the believer becomes the dwelling place, even the resting place of God. No longer in tents or temples, but in hearts renewed by grace. 158

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: “In whom ye also are builded together for an habitation of God through the Spirit.” (Ephesians 2:22, KJV) Thus, to be in Christ is to become God’s home and His Sabbath rest. We rest in Him, and He rests in us. It is a mutual indwelling. And from this union flows peace, assurance, and obedience, not as burdens, but as the fruit of grace. The Believer as God’s Dwelling Place Paul writes with clarity and boldness in 1 Corinthians 3:16: "Know ye not that ye are the temple of God, and that the Spirit of God dwelleth in you?" Again in Ephesians 2:21-22: "In whom all the building fitly framed together groweth unto an holy temple in the Lord: In whom ye also are builded together for an habitation of God through the Spirit." No longer do we seek rest in external locations. The believer is the resting place. God has found in us His habitation, not because we are worthy, but because Christ has made us holy by His blood. We are living sanctuaries, sacred spaces where the Eternal now resides. This truth redefines everything. Worship is not confined to church buildings, because we carry His presence. Prayer is not limited to sacred hours, because God rests within us. The believer becomes a mobile temple, releasing the fragrance of Christ wherever they go. We are carriers of divine presence and peace. 159

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: The Mystery of Union and Indwelling This indwelling presence is not a metaphor, it is our reality in Christ. The Holy Spirit, the Third Person of the Trinity, does not visit us on occasion; He abides. Jesus promised, "I will pray the Father, and he shall give you another Comforter, that he may abide with you forever" (John 14:16). To abide means to remain, to dwell, to rest. God has rested in us. And in this mutual abiding, we too are invited to rest in Him. This is the mystery and majesty of union with Christ. Our identity is now bound to His. We are seated with Him in heavenly places (Ephesians 2:6), and He dwells in us on earth. Living from the Place of Rest Understanding that we are God’s tabernacle changes how we live. We no longer strive for acceptance; we live from it. We do not labor to earn favor; we walk in the favor already given. The fruit of the Spirit becomes natural in our lives not through effort, but through abiding. Hebrews 4:10 says, "For he that is entered into his rest, he also hath ceased from his own works, as God did from his." This is not inactivity, but Spirit-empowered activity. It is the difference between striving and flowing. Ministry, parenting, service, and worship become expressions of rest, not burdens of performance. To live from the place of rest means to carry an awareness of God’s presence and approval. It means allowing His peace to rule in our hearts 160

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: (Colossians 3:15) and His word to dwell in us richly (Colossians 3:16). It is a life marked by stillness amid chaos, joy amid pressure, and love amid brokenness. The Ongoing Sanctification of the Temple Though we are made temples by the Spirit’s indwelling, sanctification is ongoing. Just as the physical temple required cleansing and rededication, so too our lives must continually be yielded to the Spirit. Paul admonishes in 2 Corinthians 7:1, "Let us cleanse ourselves from all filthiness of the flesh and spirit, perfecting holiness in the fear of God." This cleansing is not to attain God’s presence but to honor it. The more we recognize ourselves as His temple, the more intentional we become about maintaining the sacredness of our hearts and minds. We don’t tolerate bitterness, lust, pride, or greed in God’s house - why should we tolerate them in our bodies? A People of His Presence God’s desire was never just for a resting place but a resting people . Peter affirms this in 1 Peter 2:5: "Ye also, as lively stones, are built up a spiritual house, an holy priesthood..." The believer is not only a personal temple but also part of a collective dwelling. The Church becomes the habitation of God’s glory on the earth. As we gather in unity, love, and truth, God’s rest is magnified among us. 161

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: We are not merely individuals carrying His presence - we are a body, a holy nation, called to live in communion with God and each other. The Church is God’s tabernacle in the earth today. Our worship, service, and fellowship become spaces where God dwells richly. Conclusion The believer as God’s tabernacle is not a future hope; it is a present reality. Christ has made the way. The Spirit has come. The Father has found His rest in us. And we are invited to find our rest in Him. Let this truth anchor your identity, shape your relationships, and energize your purpose. You are not alone. You are not empty. You are not striving for what you already possess. You are God’s temple, a sacred space, a living sanctuary, a resting place of the Most High. Rest in Him, for He rests in you. 162

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Chapter 11 Eternal Rest - The Hope Beyond Time Throughout Scripture, the motif of rest is never merely a cessation of labor or activity. Rather, it culminates in the idea of shalom - wholeness, peace, and the fullness of divine presence. From the seventh day of creation to the call to faith in Hebrews, and from the tabernacle in the wilderness to the incarnation of Christ, God’s aim has consistently been to dwell among His people and bring them into His rest. This rest is not merely existential but eschatological. It reaches its apex not in time, but beyond it, in the eternal communion of God and His redeemed people in the New Creation. This chapter explores the eschatological dimensions of soteria and rest, focusing on the climactic vision of Revelation 21-22. It examines how the hope of eternal rest is grounded in the already-accomplished work of Christ and yet awaits full consummation. It demonstrates how God’s original purpose in creation, to dwell with humanity in rest, is fully realized in the eternal state. The Story That Leads to Glory The story of Scripture is not circular but teleological. It moves with purpose, toward an end that fulfills the beginning. Genesis opens with God creating the heavens and the earth and placing man in a garden-temple to dwell with Him. Revelation concludes with God creating a new heavens 163

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: and a new earth and placing His redeemed people in a garden-city where He dwells with them forever. In Genesis 2, God rests on the seventh day, not because He is weary, but because His cosmic temple is complete. This divine rest becomes the goal for all creation, a participation in the order, delight, and presence of God. But the fall disrupts this plan. Man is exiled from Eden, and with him, creation groans under the weight of corruption (Rom. 8:20-22). The rest that was meant to be communal and cosmic becomes fragmented and elusive. Yet, even in exile, the promise remains. The rest that was lost in Adam is pursued through covenants, prophets, kings, and ultimately through Christ, the last Adam. His work, as declared in John 19:30 “It is finished” is the foundation for the new creation. The goal of redemption is not merely moral renewal, but spatial and ontological reconciliation. God does not just forgive sin; He restores dwelling. Revelation 21-22: The Climax of Redemptive History Revelation 21:1-5 announces the arrival of the new heavens and new earth, declaring that “the former things have passed away.” The sea, often symbolic of chaos and evil, is no more. The new Jerusalem comes down from heaven as a bride adorned for her husband. And at the heart of the vision is this declaration: “Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man. He will dwell with them, and they will be his people…” (Rev. 21:3). 164

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Here is the telos of soteria, the unbroken fellowship of God and man, not mediated by sacrifices, temples, or even sacraments, but immediate and eternal. The voice from the throne echoes the prophetic longings of Isaiah 65 and 66, where God promises to create a new heavens and earth, where the sound of weeping shall be no more. This eternal rest is not inactivity. Revelation 22:3-5 portrays a scene of worship, reign, and service: “His servants will worship Him… they will reign forever and ever.” This is the restoration of the human vocation given in Genesis 1:26-28, to image God and exercise dominion. But now, it is done in the fullness of glory, without sin, death, or decay. Temple Imagery Fulfilled John’s description of the New Jerusalem is striking in what it lacks: “I saw no temple in the city, for its temple is the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb” (Rev. 21:22). The temple, throughout redemptive history, was both a blessing and a limitation. It signified God’s desire to dwell with His people, but it also reminded them of the separation caused by sin. In the eternal state, the symbols give way to substance. There is no need for a temple because God is fully present. The entire city becomes His sanctuary. Furthermore, the river of life flowing from the throne (Rev. 22:1) and the tree of life bearing fruit for the healing of nations (Rev. 22:2) echo Edenic imagery. What was once guarded by cherubim with flaming swords (Gen. 3:24) is now freely accessible. The curse is reversed. The exile is over. 165

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: The Hope That Anchors This vision of eternal rest is not given merely to satisfy eschatological curiosity but to anchor present faithfulness. The church, living between the “already” of Christ’s resurrection and the “not yet” of His return, is called to persevere in hope. Revelation was written to a suffering church, persecuted, marginalized, and tempted. The promise of eternal rest is pastoral before it is prophetic. It assures believers that their labor is not in vain and that their longing is not misplaced. The Apostle Paul affirms this in Romans 8:18-23, where he speaks of creation groaning with eager expectation for the revealing of the sons of God. The Spirit within us is a foretaste, a down payment, of the glory to come. Likewise, in 2 Corinthians 4:17, Paul declares, “For this light momentary affliction is preparing for us an eternal weight of glory beyond all comparison.” The hope of eternal rest empowers endurance in temporal trials. Implications Soteriology and Eschatology Are Inseparable : Salvation is not complete until it culminates in glorification. Any doctrine of salvation that stops at justification or sanctification is incomplete. As Herman Bavinck writes, “Grace restores nature, and glory crowns it.” Creation and Redemption Are Integrated : The end of the story is not disembodied bliss but renewed creation. The physicality of the new earth affirms the goodness of creation and the comprehensiveness of redemption. 166

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Presence Is the Point : From Eden to Sinai, from Calvary to New Jerusalem, the heart of God’s redemptive work is to dwell with His people. Holiness is not an end in itself; it is the condition for communion. The Church Lives Eschatologically : The church is the community of the age to come, called to live in the light of the future. As citizens of heaven (Phil. 3:20), we anticipate the consummation by living in its moral and missional implications now. The Final Rest “They shall see His face…” (Rev. 22:4). These five words encapsulate the climax of human longing and divine intention. To see God’s face, unmediated, unveiled, uninterrupted, is the highest good. The beatific vision, long anticipated by mystics and theologians, becomes the eternal reality of every believer. In this rest, there is no more striving, no more sorrow, and no more sin. The Lamb who was slain leads us to living fountains of water (Rev. 7:17), and God Himself wipes away every tear from our eyes. The journey of soteria ends not in a place, but in a Person, and in that Person, the fullness of joy and rest are eternally secured. This is the hope beyond time. This is the final and eternal rest of the redeemed. 167

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Chapter 12 Living in the Rest That Soteria Brings Embracing a Life of Graceful Rhythm Salvation ( soteria ) is not a one-time event confined to a moment of confession or conversion. It is a divine reality that invades time and transforms the totality of human existence past, present, and future. To be saved is to be brought into the rest of God, not merely in eternity, but also in the now. It is to live in the completeness of Christ’s work, where striving gives way to trusting, and hurry is replaced with holy rhythm. Jesus said in Matthew 11:28-30 (KJV), “Come unto me, all ye that labour and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest… and ye shall find rest unto your souls.” This is not just rest from burdens but rest in identity, in purpose, and in calling. It is the kind of rest that transforms work, not by removing it, but by reordering it. We close this book not merely with thoughts about heaven but with truths about how to live now . To live in the rest that soteria brings is to walk in the rhythms of grace, to labor from love and not for approval, to build with wisdom and not weariness. This is the believer’s call: to be fruitful, faithful, and free - in Christ . 168

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Rest Is Not a Break - It Is a Rhythm In a world obsessed with burnout and hustle, we have wrongly defined rest as “time off.” But biblical rest is not simply vacation, leisure, or escape. It is rhythm . It is alignment with the divine order, walking in step with the Creator’s cadence. The problem is not that we work too hard, but that we work disordered, fragmented, and disconnected from grace. People often say, “I need a break,” or “I just need a vacation,” but the reality is: vacation doesn’t heal what poor rhythms create. You don’t heal from chaos with a break. You heal from chaos with order. And divine order is found in embracing the rhythms of God’s rest. This is why God gave the Sabbath, not as a legalistic burden, but as a weekly reminder that He is God and we are not. Even in the New Covenant, rest is not merely a doctrine, it is a discipline. Hebrews 4:11 urges, “Let us labour therefore to enter into that rest...” That labor is not about earning grace; it’s about organizing life to cooperate with grace. Assessing the “Time-On”: Not Just Taking Time Off We live in a culture where people crawl toward weekends and vacations, hoping for recovery. But the real issue is not with the time off , it’s with the time on . How are you spending your active, productive time? ● Are you working from rest or working for identity? 169

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: ● Are your days reactive or rhythm-driven? ● Is your calendar filled with distractions or with purpose? Paul admonishes the believers in Ephesians 5:15-17 to “walk circumspectly… redeeming the time.” He wasn’t calling them to hurry, but to intentionality. Rest doesn’t start on Saturday. It starts on Monday - with how you live, lead, and labor. If your “time-on” is driven by guilt, pressure, and performance, your “time-off” will never be enough. You’ll come back from a vacation tired, not because the trip wasn’t restful, but because your soul is out of sync. The Productivity-Thrive Cycle: Living from Rest, Not toward It There is a divine productivity model in Scripture, a thrive cycle, not built on human effort but on grace-fueled rhythms: The Cycle Includes: 1. Revelation (Hearing from God) 2. Alignment (Adjusting life to what you’ve heard) 3. Execution (Acting in obedience and excellence) 4. Reflection (Reviewing and realigning your walk) 5. Restoration (Receiving renewed strength) Many believers never enter God’s rest because they skip steps, they execute without alignment, or they reflect only when there’s a breakdown. 170

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: But God’s rhythm is not a hamster wheel. It is a divine spiral, where each season of obedience leads to deeper rest, not deeper exhaustion. The Sacred Calendar: Managing Your Life with God in View Time management is not just a business skill, it’s a spiritual discipline. God commanded Israel to observe feasts, Sabbaths, and years of Jubilee. These weren’t just ceremonial, they were pedagogical. They taught Israel to live in rhythm with God. You don’t just need goals; you need seasons, rhythms, and sacred pauses. Rest doesn’t mean doing nothing. It means doing what matters most without hurry. In this light, rest is prophetic . It is a declaration that we are not slaves to Egypt (productivity for its own sake), but sons of God who live by faith in His provision. Setting a Kingdom Culture of Self-Examination To live in God’s rest is to set a culture in your own life of constant self-examination and spiritual alignment. Ask: ● What is driving me? ● Who am I trying to please? ● Am I doing this because it’s urgent or because it’s kingdom? 171

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: ● What is bearing fruit, and what is just noise? Jesus never ran, yet He finished everything the Father gave Him to do. He never apologized for time alone. He never let demands dictate His devotion. And He died with these words: “It is finished.” That is the power of a life lived in rhythm with God. Living in rest requires constant realignment, because drift is natural, but rhythm is intentional. Assess Your Bandwidth, Loyalty, and Productivity Here are three dimensions every believer must examine regularly: a. Bandwidth ● Your soul has limits. ● Don’t overcommit in the name of service and under-nourish your spirit. ● Even good things can become burdens if not in season. b. Loyalty ● Who are you really loyal to? ● The fear of man often masquerades as ministry. ● Jesus said, “I do always those things that please Him.” (John 8:29) c. Productivity 172

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: ● Are you fruitful, or just busy? ● John 15 teaches that productivity in the kingdom comes from abiding, not hustling. ● Fruitfulness is not measured by numbers alone, but by alignment with the Vine. Extending Soteria: Rest as Witness In a fractured world, rest becomes our apologetic. Not a passive posture, but an active testimony. A non-anxious presence in an anxious age. As Paul exhorted the Philippians: “Do all things without murmurings and disputings: That ye may be blameless and harmless, the sons of God… in the midst of a crooked and perverse nation, among whom ye shine as lights in the world.” (Philippians 2:14-15, KJV) The believer’s rest is a protest against the tyranny of performance and the idol of busyness. It says to the world: There is another way to live. There is a deeper yoke, and it is easy. There is a better burden, and it is light. Living as Embodied Invitations To extend soteria is not merely to proclaim a message, it is to embody a reality. We do not only preach rest, we become rest to others. We live unhurried but fruitful lives. We carry peace into rooms and offer calm in 173

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: conflict. We demonstrate that God's presence is not confined to sacred spaces but is alive in Spirit-filled people. “Let your moderation be known unto all men. The Lord is at hand.” (Philippians 4:5, KJV) We are not mere messengers of Christ; we are manifestations of His indwelling rest. Our lives become sacraments of serenity, visible signs of an invisible grace. The anxious are drawn not just to our words, but to our way , the way of Christ. A Commission of Rest Thus, the Great Commission is also a great invitation, not only to go and preach, but to go and invite weary souls into divine rest. In this, our witness becomes holistic: intellectual and emotional, theological and relational. “For he that is entered into his rest, he also hath ceased from his own works, as God did from his.” (Hebrews 4:10, KJV) This is how soteria extends not just through pulpits, but through people. Not just through declaration, but demonstration. We live restfully so others might rest. We cease striving so others might stop running. We are settled so others might be saved. 174

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: What is your life saying to the hurried and harassed world around you? Is it a sermon of struggle, or a sanctuary of rest? Conclusion Living in the rest that soteria brings is not a technique, it’s a trust. It’s a faith-filled rhythm that flows from knowing your salvation is complete. The Lord is your Shepherd, you shall not want. It’s choosing to believe that: ● You are not what you do. ● You don’t have to prove your worth. ● The most productive thing you can do is sometimes to stop, listen, and worship. So don’t crawl toward vacation. Don’t live your life on the edge of burnout. Don’t adopt the world’s obsession with doing more to be more. Instead, take Jesus at His word: “Ye shall find rest unto your souls.” Rest is not where your week ends. It’s where your life begins. 175

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Epilogue: Rest for the Journey As we conclude this exploration of soteria , salvation and the rest that it brings, we are reminded that our journey does not end with an intellectual understanding but with a personal transformation. Rest is not merely a concept; it is a way of life , deeply embedded in the rhythms of our daily existence. The message of rest of God’s presence, provision, and ultimate peace is not just theological but profoundly practical . It is the heartbeat of our daily walk with Christ. As we leave this reflection, consider the words of Jesus in Matthew 11:28-30, where He calls us to come to Him, offering a rest that is holistic, encompassing the body, mind, and spirit. It is a rest that does not call us to cease from activity but to cease from striving , the kind of striving that comes from insecurity, fear, and performance. In this rest, we are invited to live fully in the present, trusting that Christ’s finished work is enough. The invitation is not to escape our world, but to live in it differently. The true test of our faith is not how much we know but how we live out the rest that Christ offers us. Are we allowing His peace to govern our hearts? Are we learning to walk in the rhythms He has established? Are we setting aside the frantic pace of modern life to listen to the still, small voice of the Holy Spirit? 176

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: This rest begins with a choice, a decision to live in alignment with God’s purposes, a decision to surrender our lives to His will and His timing. It requires a constant process of self-examination and realignment. It calls us to assess how we spend our time, the nature of our productivity, and the priorities that govern our decisions. In essence, it is about living in a rhythm of grace that sustains, refreshes, and reorients us toward God’s kingdom. But this rest also has a missional dimension. When we live in the rest of Christ, we become witnesses of His grace to a weary and restless world. People are watching our lives, how we navigate the demands of work, relationships, and personal ambitions. The world is longing for rest, and we, as bearers of the gospel, have the unique opportunity to invite them into a rest that is eternal, secure, and life-giving. In the final analysis, the rest that soteria brings is not something we earn or achieve, it is something we receive. It is a gift of grace, a divine invitation to live in the fullness of God’s peace. It is a rest that transcends circumstances, a rest that holds us steady in the storms of life, and a rest that points us to the ultimate fulfillment of all things when Christ returns to make all things new. So, as we close this journey, let us be reminded that true rest is not found in an end-of-the-week break, but in a continual walk with Jesus, the Lord of our Sabbath. 177

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: May you walk in His rest, live in His grace, and extend His peace to the world around you. For in the rhythm of rest, we find not only solace but strength to carry on the work of His kingdom. 178

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Appendices A. Overview of the Pentateuch’s Redemptive Structure The Pentateuch, comprising Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy, is not merely the historical prologue of Israel’s national story, it is the theological foundation upon which the entire edifice of Scripture is built. These five books provide a sweeping narrative of creation, fall, covenant, and divine intervention, forming the contours of God's unfolding redemptive plan. Genesis opens with the majestic account of creation, presenting a world designed in harmony with its Creator, followed by the tragic intrusion of sin through Adam’s disobedience. The fall introduces alienation, judgment, and the necessity of divine intervention. Yet even within this judgment, there is the first glimmer of redemptive hope, the protoevangelium of Genesis 3:15, which hints at a future seed who will crush the serpent’s head. This verse becomes a theological seedbed, quietly growing through every subsequent covenant and narrative. As the narrative progresses, we see God’s redemptive purpose preserved through the lineage of Seth, Noah, and ultimately Abraham. With Abraham, God initiates a covenant of promise, rooted in grace and faith, that will have implications for all nations. His descendants, the Israelites, become the corporate recipients of God’s covenantal dealings, not by merit but by divine election and mercy. This covenant is reaffirmed to Isaac and Jacob and becomes the central motif of God's interaction with His people. 179

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Exodus continues this redemptive arc by portraying Israel's deliverance from Egyptian bondage, a historical event that serves as both a literal act of salvation and a typological foreshadowing of the greater exodus to come through Christ. The Passover, with its slain lamb and blood-marked doorposts, anticipates the substitutionary atonement of Jesus, the true Lamb of God. The giving of the Law at Sinai, while often viewed as legalistic, must be seen within the context of grace, as it follows divine deliverance. The Law was never the means of salvation, but a covenantal structure meant to shape the identity of a redeemed people. Leviticus presents the sacrificial system and priesthood, emphasizing God's holiness and the need for atonement. The repeated sacrifices, though unable to cleanse the conscience permanently, establish the categories of substitution, blood, and access that would find their fulfillment in the once-for-all sacrifice of Christ. The tabernacle, with its intricate design and rituals, becomes a mobile theology of redemption, God dwelling among His people, yet separated by sin and requiring mediation. Numbers illustrates both the faithfulness of God and the failure of man. The wilderness wanderings are marked by rebellion, unbelief, and divine discipline, but also by provision, guidance, and mercy. The tension between judgment and grace becomes palpable, underscoring the insufficiency of the old generation and the necessity of divine enablement for entrance into the promised rest. Deuteronomy functions as both a covenant renewal and a prophetic anticipation. As Moses addresses the new generation on the verge of 180

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: entering Canaan, he reiterates the Law and calls them to covenantal faithfulness. Yet even here, he foresees their future apostasy and the need for a circumcised heart, pointing forward to the work of the Spirit in the new covenant. The book ends not with closure but with expectation, as the people prepare to enter the land, and Moses dies without entering, a poignant reminder that the Law, though holy and good, cannot bring us into the ultimate rest. Throughout the Pentateuch, we encounter divine promises, human failure, gracious provision, and typological structures that all converge in the person and work of Christ. From Eden to Sinai, from sacrifice to sanctuary, from covenant to conquest, the Pentateuch is not a closed story but an open invitation to anticipate the fuller revelation of redemption. It shows us that humanity’s deepest need is not merely deliverance from slavery, but restoration to communion with God. The rest longed for by Israel is only realized in Christ, who fulfills the Law, embodies the tabernacle, and grants eternal Sabbath to those who believe. 181

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: B. Key Scriptures on Rest and Salvation The Bible is replete with references to both rest and salvation , two profound realities that are deeply woven into the fabric of God’s redemptive plan. These themes are not isolated concepts but are intrinsically tied together, forming a biblical pattern that begins in Genesis and culminates in Revelation. In the Scriptures, rest is not only the cessation of physical labor but a symbol of covenant relationship, spiritual renewal, and eternal security in God. Likewise, salvation is not merely deliverance from sin but entrance into God’s rest, His presence, His promises, and His peace. Below are key Scriptures that illuminate these dual themes, offering insight into the present and future dimensions of redemptive rest. 1. Genesis 2:2-3 - Divine Rest at Creation “And on the seventh day God ended his work which he had made; and he rested on the seventh day from all his work which he had made.” ● God’s rest on the seventh day after creation was not due to fatigue, but a declaration of completion and delight. This establishes a divine pattern of rest rooted in satisfaction and purpose. It becomes a type of the rest promised to humanity through salvation, a finished work, not a human effort. 2. Exodus 16:29 - The Manna and the Sabbath 182

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: “See, for that the Lord hath given you the sabbath, therefore he giveth you on the sixth day the bread of two days.” ● God instituted the Sabbath to teach Israel dependence on divine provision. Rest was a covenantal gift, not a burden, teaching the people to cease striving and trust God. It prophetically prefigures the gospel truth that salvation is not earned but received. 3. Exodus 33:14 - God’s Presence as Rest “And he said, My presence shall go with thee, and I will give thee rest.” ● God equated His presence with rest. True rest is not found in a location or a ritual but in communion with the Lord. This points forward to Christ, in whom God’s presence dwells bodily (Colossians 2:9), and in whom we find rest. 4. Deuteronomy 12:10 - Rest in the Promised Land “But when ye go over Jordan… and he giveth you rest from all your enemies round about, so that ye dwell in safety.” ● The conquest of Canaan is framed as the inheritance of rest. Yet, Hebrews 4 tells us that Joshua did not give them the ultimate rest, indicating that the land was a shadow of a greater spiritual reality. 5. Psalm 23:1-3 - Restored Soul and Divine Shepherding 183

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: “He maketh me to lie down in green pastures: he leadeth me beside the still waters. He restoreth my soul…” ● Rest is shown here as personal, restorative, and deeply spiritual. The Good Shepherd brings the soul into stillness, healing, and righteousness, core aspects of salvation. 6. Psalm 95:10-11 - Warning Against Missing God’s Rest “…So I sware in my wrath, They shall not enter into my rest.” ● A tragic reminder of how unbelief can forfeit divine rest. This passage is used in Hebrews 3-4 to teach that the promise of rest remains open, but must be entered by faith and obedience. 7. Isaiah 30:15 - Rest in Quietness and Confidence “In returning and rest shall ye be saved; in quietness and in confidence shall be your strength…” ● This verse tightly connects salvation with rest. It reveals that God’s path to deliverance is not through frantic striving but through returning (repentance), quiet trust, and restful confidence in Him. 8. Isaiah 11:10 - Christ as the Resting Place “And in that day there shall be a root of Jesse… and his rest shall be glorious.” 184

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: ● A messianic prophecy identifying Christ as the One who brings glorious rest to both Jew and Gentile. It anticipates the spiritual Sabbath believers enter in Christ. 9. Matthew 11:28-30 - Christ’s Personal Invitation “Come unto me, all ye that labour and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest.” ● Jesus speaks directly to weary souls, offering a yoke of grace instead of religion. His rest is peace with God (justification), peace of God (sanctification), and peace forever (glorification). 10. Mark 6:31 - Jesus Calling the Disciples to Rest “Come ye yourselves apart into a desert place, and rest a while…” ● This moment reflects not only physical rest but Jesus' heart for replenishment of His disciples. It speaks to the rhythm of work and worship, service and stillness, central to spiritual health. 11. Hebrews 4:9-11 - A Remaining Rest for God’s People “There remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God.” ● This “sabbatismos” (Sabbath-rest) is the believer’s inheritance in Christ. It is a finished work we enter by faith. The call to “labour to enter” underscores the diligence required to persevere in faith, not works. 185

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: 12. Revelation 14:13 - Eternal Rest of the Saints “Blessed are the dead which die in the Lord… that they may rest from their labours; and their works do follow them.” ● The final picture of rest is eternal. In heaven, rest is complete, not from purpose, but from toil and pain. Salvation culminates in perfect, unbroken communion with God. 13. Revelation 21:3-4 - Rest from Sorrow and Death “…There shall be no more death, neither sorrow, nor crying, neither shall there be any more pain…” ● The new heaven and new earth offer a rest where every tear is wiped away. The redeemed will rest in God’s presence forever, no more curse, no more striving, no more night. Summary These verses, among countless others, form a sacred fabric of truth that presents rest and salvation as interrelated gifts of God’s redemptive plan. From the rest God modeled at creation, to the Sabbath command in Israel, to the rest offered by Christ, and finally to the eternal rest of the saints, we see a divine storyline where God calls man out of striving and into grace. Rest is not laziness. It is not escapism. It is the fruit of redemption, the state of peace, wholeness, and joy that comes from union with God. 186

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Salvation is the means; rest is one of its ends. And in the Person of Jesus Christ, both are found in full. 187

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: C. Timeline of Redemptive History: From Eden to Eternity The story of the Bible is ultimately the story of redemption, a divine plan unfolding from creation to consummation. From the Sabbath rest of Eden to the eternal rest of the New Creation, the biblical narrative traces God's relentless pursuit of restoring what was lost through sin. At the heart of this redemptive journey is God’s invitation for humanity to enter into His rest, not merely as inactivity, but as covenantal wholeness, freedom from striving, and eternal communion with Him.This timeline serves as both a narrative guide and a theological roadmap, helping us see the interconnected moments where God’s rest is revealed, forfeited, foreshadowed, fulfilled, and ultimately restored. 188 Period Key Events & Scriptures Redemptive Significance Theme of Rest 1. Creation (Genesis 1-2) God creates the heavens and earth in six days and rests on the seventh day. God’s perfect creation reflects His order, goodness, and completeness. Rest is modeled by God (Genesis 2:2-3); the Sabbath is sanctified as a divine rhythm. 2. The Fall (Genesis 3) Adam and Eve disobey God, leading to sin, death, and exile from Eden. Humanity loses fellowship with God, bringing toil, sorrow, and spiritual unrest. Rest is disrupted. The curse brings labor, pain, and spiritual distance.

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: 189 3. The Promise to Abraham (Genesis 12, 15, 17) God calls Abram and promises to bless all nations through his seed. The covenant begins the redemptive lineage that leads to Christ. God promises a land of rest and a seed (Messiah) to restore peace. 4. The Exodus & Mosaic Covenant (Exodus 12-20) God delivers Israel from Egypt and gives the Law at Sinai. A nation is formed through deliverance and covenant. Sabbath laws are introduced. Sabbath rest is commanded to reflect God's rest and remind of deliverance (Deut. 5:15). 5. The Wilderness & Conquest (Numbers - Joshua) Israel wanders in the wilderness due to unbelief; Joshua leads them into Canaan. The promised land is entered, but full rest is not realized. Canaan represents rest , but true rest remains unfulfilled (Hebrews 4:8). 6. The Monarchy & the Prophets (1 Samuel - Malachi) Kings reign, prophets warn and encourage repentance and hope. God promises a future King and a New Covenant that brings lasting peace. Rest is longed for , often violated through idolatry and injustice (Isaiah 11:10). 7. The Incarnation & Earthly Ministry of Christ (Gospels) Jesus is born, lives a sinless life, teaches, heals, and calls to repentance. The Word becomes flesh, bringing the kingdom near and revealing the Father. Jesus offers true rest (Matthew 11:28-30); He becomes the new Sabbath.

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Expanded Redemptive Timeline Narrative Summary 190 8. The Crucifixion and Resurrection (John 19 - 20) Jesus dies on the cross and rises on the third day. The atonement is completed; the curse of sin is broken. Rest is secured. “It is finished” (John 19:30) marks the work of redemption complete. 9. Pentecost and the Church Age (Acts - Present) The Holy Spirit is poured out; the Church is birthed and expands globally. Believers become temples of the Spirit, living in the finished work of Christ. Spiritual rest is accessible now (Hebrews 4:9-11); we rest in grace and live by faith. 10. The Consummation (Second Coming) (1 Thess. 4, Rev. 19) Christ returns in glory to judge the world and reward the saints. Evil is vanquished, justice is established, and God's people are glorified. Rest is vindicated. No more striving or persecution. God's rest becomes visible glory. 11. The New Creation (Revelation 21-22) A new heaven and new earth are revealed; the dwelling of God is with man. Redemption is fully realized. God’s people live forever in His presence. Eternal rest is restored. No more curse, no more tears, no more night. The Lamb is the light.

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Rest was established in Eden, symbolizing communion with God and completion. 1. Sin disrupted that rest, bringing alienation and toil. 2. God promised a redeemer through Abraham, pointing to a restored relationship. 3. The Law introduced the Sabbath, teaching Israel dependence and covenant identity. 4. The land of Canaan was a shadow, not the substance, of the rest to come. 5. Jesus fulfilled the Law, accomplished redemption, and invited all to come and rest. 6. The Church now lives from that rest, proclaiming salvation to the nations. 7. Eternal rest awaits, when the New Jerusalem descends and God's people see His face. Rest is not merely a pause in human activity, it is the essence of salvation. In Christ, the rest of God is no longer a day, a place, or a practice. It is a Person. The redemptive timeline reveals that all of human history is heading toward a cosmic Sabbath, where God and redeemed humanity dwell together in uninterrupted peace. 191

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Glossary of Theological Terms A guide to understanding key concepts used throughout this book Atonement In the Old Testament, atonement refers to the ritual practice by which the sins of individuals or the nation were covered (Hebrew: kaphar ) through the shedding of animal blood, particularly on the Day of Atonement (Yom Kippur). This sacrificial system, repeated annually, symbolized both the seriousness of sin and the need for substitutionary death, but it provided only a temporary covering. Covenant A binding and solemn agreement between God and humanity. Major biblical covenants include the Noahic, Abrahamic, Mosaic, Davidic, and New Covenant, each revealing layers of God’s unfolding redemptive plan. Creation The act of God bringing the universe into existence from nothing (ex nihilo). The creation account in Genesis lays the foundation for understanding rest, order, and purpose in God's design. Eden A broader region described in the early chapters of Genesis, within which the Garden of Eden was planted eastward (Genesis 2:8). Eden represents the original setting of divine communion, abundance, and rest. The Garden within Eden was where God placed Adam and Eve to cultivate and enjoy unhindered fellowship with Him. The eventual expulsion from the Garden 192

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: due to sin points forward to humanity’s need for redemption and the restoration of intimate relationship with God. Election God’s sovereign choice in selecting individuals or nations for a specific purpose in His redemptive plan. In the context of salvation, it reflects God’s initiative in calling and saving people. Eschatology The study of the last things or end times, including events such as the return of Christ, final judgment, resurrection of the dead, and the establishment of the new heavens and new earth. Exodus The historical event of Israel’s deliverance from Egypt, which serves as a powerful type and foreshadowing of spiritual salvation in Christ. Also tied to the theme of entering rest. Faith A heartfelt trust in God and His promises, especially the redemptive work of Christ. It is by faith that we enter the rest of God and receive salvation. Garden of Eden The specific, divinely planted garden located eastward within the larger region called Eden (Genesis 2:8). It was a sacred space where Adam and Eve dwelled, representing the original environment of divine fellowship, provision, and rest. The Garden served as the earthly dwelling place of 193

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: God with man before the fall, and its imagery continues throughout Scripture as a type or shadow of restored communion with God, ultimately fulfilled in Christ and the New Creation. Grace God’s unmerited favor toward humanity. Salvation is by grace through faith, not by human effort, and it is central to understanding rest as a gift, not a reward. Justification A legal declaration by God that a sinner is righteous by virtue of faith in Jesus Christ. It is the beginning of the believer’s experience of salvation ( Soteria ). Messiah The “Anointed One” prophesied in the Old Testament, fulfilled in Jesus Christ. He is the one who brings salvation, establishes the New Covenant, and restores rest to God’s people. New Covenant The covenant inaugurated by Jesus’ death and resurrection, characterized by the internalization of God’s law, the indwelling of the Holy Spirit, and a direct relationship with God. Pentateuch The first five books of the Bible (Genesis to Deuteronomy). These form the foundation of God’s redemptive narrative, containing the creation story, the fall, the covenants, and the law, all of which point to Christ. 194

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Redemption A New Testament term that conveys the idea of being bought back or liberated through the payment of a ransom. It speaks of the deliverance from the power, penalty, and presence of sin through the sacrificial death of Jesus Christ. Rooted in the Greek word apolutrōsis , redemption emphasizes the legal and liberating nature of Christ’s work, where He, through His blood, pays the price to free humanity from bondage to sin, Satan, and death. It not only involves the forgiveness of sins but also the restoration of believers to their rightful place as sons and daughters in the family of God, enjoying full inheritance in Christ. Rest More than physical pause, rest in Scripture represents peace with God, freedom from the burden of sin, and eternal fellowship with Him. The Sabbath, the Promised Land, and Christ all embody layers of this divine rest. Sabbath Rest A concept rooted in the creation account (Genesis 2) and later instituted in the Mosaic Law (Exodus 20), representing both physical rest and spiritual renewal. It also foreshadows the ultimate rest believers will experience in Christ, a rest that is both a present reality and a future promise. Salvation Deliverance from sin and its consequences through the finished work of 195

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: Jesus Christ. It includes justification, sanctification, and glorification, restoring humanity to right relationship with God. Sanctification The ongoing work of the Holy Spirit in the life of a believer, making them more like Christ in thought, word, and deed. It is both a present process and a future completion. Soteria The Greek word for "salvation," which encompasses not only deliverance from sin but also the restoration of the whole person to God’s original design. It includes redemption, forgiveness, and the eternal inheritance promised to believers. Typology The study of how events, persons, and institutions in the Old Testament serve as “types” or foreshadowings of realities fulfilled in the New Testament, particularly in Christ. The Law Given to Israel through Moses, the Law reveals God’s holiness and humanity’s need for grace. Though it cannot save, it points toward the need for a Savior who fulfills it perfectly. The Promised Land The land of Canaan, promised to Abraham and his descendants, representing not just physical territory but the concept of divine inheritance, rest, and fulfillment. 196

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: The Resurrection The bodily rising of Jesus from the dead, signifying the defeat of sin and death and the beginning of new creation life for all who believe. The New Creation A future reality wherein God will dwell with redeemed humanity in a renewed heaven and earth, free from sin, sorrow, and death. It is the final destination of salvation and the ultimate fulfillment of rest. 197

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)

title: SOTERIA AND REST (2)
text: About the Book: Soteria and Rest is the second volume in the Soteria series, exploring the beautiful connection between God’s plan of salvation and the divine rest He promises His people. Volume one is Soteria: The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption . From creation to new creation, this book traces the unfolding story of redemption, how rest was lost through sin and restored in Christ. Beginning in Eden, we see God's original design for rest and fellowship. As the narrative moves through the covenants, the exodus, and the giving of the law, it becomes clear that God's desire has always been to dwell among His people and restore what was broken. In Jesus Christ, this promise is fulfilled. His finished work brings the rest humanity longed for, a rest that is both a present reality and a future hope. This book offers theological insight and personal reflection, showing how believers today can live in the rest Christ secured. Whether you're new to the faith or have walked with Christ for years, Soteria and Rest will help you see how salvation and rest are deeply intertwined and how they shape our lives now and forever. 229

text: About the Book This book is a journey, one that invites you to think deeply, study carefully, and ultimately worship. SOTERIA is about salvation, but not just as something that happens to us. It’s about salvation as the eternal plan of God, settled before time began, and revealed fully in Christ. I wrote this book with a conviction that the cross wasn’t God’s reaction to the fall, it was always part of His eternal purpose. According to Titus 1:2 and Romans 1:2, eternal life was promised before the world began. That means salvation is not a “Plan B.” It’s not God scrambling to fix what went wrong. It’s the unfolding of His love, wisdom, and grace, right on time. Now, I don’t believe God caused the fall, but I do believe He was fully aware of it and more than able to redeem us from it. That’s how I understand His sovereignty: not as micromanagement, but as the supreme ability to bring about His will despite the failures of men. This book explores key themes like the eternal nature of salvation, the centrality of the cross, the right foundation for understanding redemption, and how our view of God shapes everything else. While it touches on theological terms like infralapsarianism and election, it stays rooted in the truth of God’s Word and the simplicity of the gospel. I pray that as you read, your heart will be stirred, your mind renewed, and your love for Jesus grows stronger. This book is not just about doctrine, it’s about devotion. Because to understand salvation is to stand in awe of the God who saved us. 3

text: Preface Salvation, or Soteria , has been at the core of my teachings for as long as I can remember. Since the inception of the Life Conferences (2018), I have found myself returning to this foundational subject. The reason is simple: the more I teach about salvation, the more I see its depth and significance and the more I understand why it must be taught. Salvation is not just the beginning of the Christian journey; it is the foundation and framework for everything we believe and practice as followers of Christ. It is not the ABC of the faith, it is the A to Z. Every aspect of theology, whether it is Christology (the study of Christ), pneumatology (the study of the Holy Spirit), eschatology (the study of end times), or ecclesiology (the study of the church), finds its grounding in a proper understanding of Soteria . If we misunderstand salvation, we risk distorting our view of God, His purposes, and His plan for humanity. I believe salvation must be taught, re-taught, and understood deeply. Over the years, I have encountered many believers with questions about their faith, doubts about their standing with God, and confusion about the work of Christ. More often than not, these questions stem from an incomplete or flawed understanding of salvation. When we do not get Soteria right, our theology in other areas unravels. Conversely, when our understanding of salvation is clear and biblical, it strengthens every other area of our faith. The goal of this book is to lay out the doctrine of salvation in a way that is thorough, accessible, and rooted in Scripture. text: Introduction Salvation, known as Soteria in theological terms, lies at the very heart of God’s eternal purpose for humanity. It is not simply an event or a doctrine to be understood, but a journey into the depths of divine love, justice, and mercy. At its core, salvation speaks to the very character of God, His unchanging nature, His eternal promises, and His unrelenting desire to bring humanity into eternal fellowship with Him. To grasp the fullness of salvation, we must begin at its origin. Too often, discussions about salvation focus primarily on the end result, eternal life, the forgiveness of sins, or the promise of heaven. While these outcomes are certainly glorious, they cannot be fully appreciated without understanding the foundation upon which they rest. Just as a majestic structure draws its stability from the foundation beneath it, our understanding of salvation’s conclusion depends entirely on our understanding of its beginning. The Scriptures consistently point us to this truth. Titus 1:2 declares that God “promised eternal life before the world began.” This statement is profound. It tells us that salvation is not a reaction to human sin or a hastily devised plan to fix a broken world. Instead, salvation has always been central to God’s eternal purpose. Before the foundations of the earth were laid, before humanity was formed, and even before the fall, God had already prepared the way for redemption. This eternal nature of salvation dispels common misconceptions. Some have misunderstood the cross of Christ, viewing it as a response to human rebellion, a tragic necessity rather than a deliberate act of divine love. Worse still, some critics of Christianity have caricatured the crucifixion as 8

title: Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption
source_file: /Users/macuser/Downloads/Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption.pdf
text: a form of “divine child abuse,” where an angry God inflicts punishment on an innocent Son. Nothing could be further from the truth. The sacrifice of Jesus was not an afterthought, nor was it imposed on Him unwillingly. Jesus Himself declared, “I lay down my life, that I might take it again. No man taketh it from me, but I lay it down of myself” (John 10:17-18, KJV). His willing sacrifice was the ultimate expression of God’s justice and mercy, planned and foreordained before the foundation of the world. The cross is not evidence of God’s wrath but of His love, a love so profound that He would step into humanity’s brokenness to reconcile us to Himself. Reflecting on my own journey, I recall a moment when this truth deeply impacted me. For years, I had understood the cross primarily as a necessary act in response to sin. But as I began to grasp that the cross was always part of God’s eternal plan, it shifted my perspective entirely. I realized that the cross was not merely a reaction to human sin, but the unfolding of a grand, divine narrative, a love story that began before time itself. Theologically, this understanding invites us to reflect on the balance between God’s sovereignty and human free will. While God’s sovereign plan for redemption was established before the foundation of the world, it is essential to recognize that the fall was not caused by God. Instead, God, in His infinite wisdom, allowed humanity the freedom to choose, knowing that through this freedom, His redemptive plan would unfold. This view acknowledges that the integrity of God’s character will not override the will of man. The fall of humanity was not a necessary part of 9

title: Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption
source_file: /Users/macuser/Downloads/Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption.pdf
text: God’s plan, but in His sovereign wisdom, He permitted it, knowing that He would ultimately use it as a stage for displaying His glory through both judgment and mercy. In this light, salvation is not a mere reaction to the fall but the outworking of God’s eternal purpose to redeem and restore mankind to Himself. This understanding resonates with aspects of the doctrine traditionally referred to as infralapsarianism , but with a necessary distinction. In this view, God’s decree for salvation was made before the fall of humanity, not as a reaction, and certainly not because the fall was causally decreed by God, but because God, in His infinite foreknowledge and sovereign wisdom, knew humanity would fall. Thus, He lovingly and purposefully predetermined the means of salvation through Jesus Christ before the foundation of the world (Titus 1:2; Romans 1:2). The fall, therefore, is not understood as a necessary or orchestrated part of God’s decree, but as a foreseen reality within which God would reveal His glory through redemption. It affirms that God did not cause the fall but had, from eternity, a redemptive plan already in place, anchored in His unchanging love and eternal purpose. This book seeks to delve deeply into the doctrine of Soteria , exploring its eternal origins, its profound implications, and its transformative power. Salvation is not a “Plan B” but the centerpiece of God’s design for creation. 10
