text: PART ONE: THE FOUNDATION OF SALVATION Chapter 1: The Weight of Salvation Historical Context of Salvation: A Theological Journey Salvation, in Christian theology, is not a fleeting idea or merely a response to sin, but a profound, eternal plan of God that unfolds throughout the pages of Scripture and the history of humanity. To understand the weight of salvation , we must trace its development across the centuries, examining how salvation has been perceived, articulated, and applied by the church throughout history. This provides the historical foundation upon which modern Christian doctrine stands today. 1. Salvation in the Old Testament: Promise and Anticipation In the Old Testament, salvation is primarily God’s deliverance of His people from oppression, sin, and death. From the beginning of the Biblical narrative, salvation was always understood as a divine intervention in human history. The Exodus event (Exodus 12-15) is a prime example, where God delivered Israel from Egyptian bondage. The Passover became the central symbol of this deliverance, pointing to a sacrificial substitution where the blood of the lamb covered the Israelites, sparing them from death. Throughout the Old Testament, salvation was presented through various types and shadows , particularly through the Levitical sacrificial system . The blood of animals was seen as atoning for the sins of the people, but it was ultimately a temporary solution, pointing to a greater, eternal sacrifice to come. This anticipation was fulfilled in the Messiah , who 17

title: Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption
source_file: /Users/macuser/Downloads/Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption.pdf
text: would be both king and suffering servant , according to prophecies such as Isaiah 53 . The covenant promises made to Abraham (Genesis 12:1-3) were foundational in understanding salvation as God’s covenantal work. Salvation was not only individual but also corporate, God’s people as a whole would be redeemed, and through them, the entire world would be blessed. The promise of the Messiah was embedded in these covenants, ensuring that God’s plan for salvation was both personal and global . 2. Salvation in the Early Church: The Fulfillment of God’s Promise When the New Testament church emerged, salvation came to be understood through the lens of Jesus Christ . Jesus’ death and resurrection became the fulfillment of God’s redemptive plan that had been set in motion from the beginning of time. The Apostle Paul, in particular, played a pivotal role in articulating salvation’s profound theological depth. He emphasized that salvation was not merely about deliverance from physical oppression , as in the Exodus, but was primarily about deliverance from sin and eternal death . The earliest Christians began to reflect on the historical reality of Jesus’ death and resurrection. These events were seen as the climax of God’s long-awaited salvation plan. For the early church, salvation was understood through the lens of atonement . This is best reflected in passages like Romans 3:21-26 , where Paul explains that justification (the act of being made right with God) is made possible through the redeeming sacrifice of Christ , who bore the penalty for sin that humanity deserved. 18

title: Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption
source_file: /Users/macuser/Downloads/Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption.pdf
text: During this period, the church began to develop formalized doctrines of atonement and redemptio n particularly through the Council of Nicaea (325 AD) and the Council of Chalcedon (451 AD) , which established the orthodox belief in the full humanity and divinity of Christ . These councils underscored that Christ, as fully God and fully man, was the only one capable of reconciling humanity to God , bridging the gap that sin had created. 3. The Medieval Church: A Shift Toward Legalism and Scholasticism During the medieval period, the doctrine of salvation experienced notable shifts, largely influenced by the rise of scholastic theology , a method that emphasized rational argument and systematic thinking. One of the key figures of this era was Anselm of Canterbury (1033–1109) , who introduced a more legal and honor-based understanding of salvation through his work Cur Deus Homo (“Why God Became Man”). Anselm argued that sin was a violation of God’s honor, creating a debt humanity could not repay. In his view, God’s justice required satisfaction, not simply forgiveness. Thus, the incarnation and death of Christ were necessary, Jesus, as both God and man, offered Himself as the perfect substitute to satisfy divine honor and restore the balance. This concept became known as the Satisfaction Theory of Atonement . While Anselm’s framework emphasized God’s mercy in sending Christ, it reflected the feudal legal culture of his time and introduced a more transactional tone to salvation. At the same time, the medieval church began to increasingly tie salvation to sacramental participation and ecclesiastical authority . The rise of 19

title: Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption
source_file: /Users/macuser/Downloads/Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption.pdf
text: practices like indulgences and the growing emphasis on works made salvation seem contingent not only on grace but also on obedience to the Church’s sacramental system, rites like baptism , penance , and Eucharist became seen as essential pathways to God’s favor. Nevertheless, many theologians of the period, including Thomas Aquinas , maintained that salvation was ultimately a gift of God’s grace . They affirmed that it was by grace through faith that one could be saved. However, they increasingly framed that grace as being mediated through the institutional church and its sacraments , making salvation appear less personal and more procedural. 4. The Reformation: Salvation by Grace Alone The Protestant Reformation (16th century) marked a return to the biblical doctrine of salvation , especially the idea of justification by faith alone . Reformers like Martin Luther and John Calvin criticized the medieval Catholic Church’s emphasis on works and sacraments as means of obtaining salvation. Luther’s 95 Theses (1517) began a theological movement that emphasized that salvation is a gift of grace and cannot be earned by human effort. Luther’s doctrine of justification by faith alone was rooted in his reading of Romans 3:28 (“For we maintain that a person is justified by faith apart from the works of the law”). He emphasized that salvation is not a matter of human achievement but of divine action in Christ. In the solas of the Reformation, sola fide (faith alone), sola scriptura (Scripture alone), sola gratia (grace alone), and solus Christus (Christ alone), the church 20

title: Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption
source_file: /Users/macuser/Downloads/Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption.pdf
text: returned to the biblical teaching that salvation is through Christ alone and cannot be obtained through works. John Calvin further developed the doctrine of salvation with his Doctrines of Predestination and the Ordo Salutis (Order of Salvation), emphasizing the sovereignty of God in salvation and the centrality of God’s grace in bringing the elect to faith. For Calvin, salvation was a complete work of God , initiated in eternity and accomplished through the work of Christ and the Holy Spirit . 5. The Modern Era: New Challenges and Insights In the modern era, salvation has continued to be a focus of theological reflection, but also of debate . The rise of liberal theology and social gospel movements challenged the traditional, doctrinal understanding of salvation by focusing on social justice and humanitarianism as integral parts of the salvation process. Some even proposed that salvation could be understood more in terms of human progress and moral improvement rather than divine intervention. At the same time, the rise of Evangelicalism in the 19th and 20th centuries re-emphasized the traditional doctrine of salvation. The Evangelical movement, especially in its Reformed and Wesleyan branches, continues to highlight salvation as a personal, transformative experience through faith in Christ. These movements also insist that salvation is available to all , yet remains an act of divine grace, and not a human achievement. In recent times, the postmodern era has raised challenges to the universalistic claims of salvation. While many argue for a more inclusive 21

title: Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption
source_file: /Users/macuser/Downloads/Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption.pdf
text: approach to salvation, the traditional Christian view holds that salvation is through Christ alone , and faith in Christ is essential for salvation. From the Old Testament to the modern era, the weight of salvation has consistently pointed to God’s sovereign work in the lives of His people. While the way salvation has been understood and articulated has evolved, the central truth remains: salvation is ultimately God’s initiative , freely given to humanity through the work of Jesus Christ . This truth calls for reverence, reflection, and a renewed understanding of the weight and significance of salvation in both historical and personal terms. The Danger of Doing Christology Exclusively from Below One of the major theological missteps in modern Christianity is the tendency to study Christology “from below” exclusively, focusing only on the humanity of Jesus. While it is true that Jesus walked the earth as a man, experiencing hunger, pain, and emotion, an overemphasis on His humanity without a corresponding appreciation of His divinity leads to an imbalanced theology. A Christology that focuses exclusively on Jesus’ earthly ministry risks portraying Him as merely a moral teacher, a revolutionary leader, or an exemplary figure rather than the incarnate God. Such an approach diminishes the salvific work of Christ, reducing it to a human endeavor rather than a divine intervention. For instance, the Gospels narrate Jesus’ acts of compassion, suffering, and even His death in human terms, but they also affirm His divine authority. text: Chapter 2: The Eternal Purpose of Salvation Salvation as God’s Eternal Intention Salvation is not a reactive measure taken by God in response to human sin, nor is it a hurried afterthought. text: Chapter 3: The Virgin Birth and Its Necessity for Salvation “Behold, a virgin shall conceive, and bear a son, and shall call his name Immanuel.” - Isaiah 7:14 (KJV) Introduction: The Glory and Gravity of the Virgin Birth The doctrine of the Virgin Birth is not a peripheral or ornamental detail in the Christian faith. It is a fundamental pillar that upholds the entire edifice of Christian doctrine. Without the Virgin Birth, the entire doctrine of the incarnation, and by extension, salvation itself, collapses. The Virgin Birth is not merely a miraculous event but a divine act of intentionality, involving Trinitarian agency that reflects the unity of God’s purpose in salvation. As J. Gresham Machen asserts, “ The virgin birth is a guarantee of the uniqueness of Jesus Christ and an essential element of the supernaturalism of our faith. ” By affirming the Virgin Birth, we safeguard the integrity of the incarnation and preserve the salvific plan as instituted by God. In a world where the naturalistic view of life seeks to strip away the supernatural, the Virgin Birth is an unequivocal statement that God’s redemption of humanity enters history not through human effort, but through divine initiative. Theologians throughout history, such as Athanasius, have emphasized that the Virgin Birth is not just a miraculous event; it is a metaphysical gateway to the understanding of the person and work of Jesus Christ. This chapter will explore the theological, soteriological, and pastoral significance of the Virgin Birth, with a scholarly yet devotional reflection on its necessity for salvation. 31

text: 1. The Necessity of the Virgin Birth for Salvation To understand why the Virgin Birth is essential for salvation, we must first comprehend the fundamental problem it resolves. Humanity’s fallenness, inherited from Adam, renders every person spiritually dead. As Paul writes in Romans 5:12, “Wherefore, as by one man sin entered into the world, and death by sin... so death passed upon all men, for that all have sinned.” This sin is not merely a moral failure but a deep ontological condition, where humanity’s very nature is corrupted by sin. Had Jesus been born of natural generation, by the seed of a human father, He would have inherited this fallen nature. This is a critical point: the salvation of mankind requires that the Savior be sinless not only by conduct but also by nature. As Richard Bauckham notes, “ The sinlessness of Christ is not merely an ethical characteristic but an essential condition for His mediatorial work .” By the Holy Spirit’s work in the Virgin Birth, Jesus is not subject to the curse of original sin. His conception bypasses the natural line of Adam. He is “holy” from conception, as the angel announces in Luke 1:35: “The Holy Ghost shall come upon thee, and the power of the Highest shall overshadow thee: therefore also that holy thing which shall be born of thee shall be called the Son of God.” The term “holy thing” (Greek: to hagion ) speaks to the intrinsic holiness of Jesus. He is not made holy by any act of human effort, but His holiness is inherent in His very nature from the moment of His conception. 2. The Incarnation: Safeguarding Jesus’ Full Humanity and Full Divinity 32

text: The Virgin Birth is not just a physical or biological event; it is the theological door through which the Word became flesh. Without the Virgin Birth, the hypostatic union, the union of full divinity and full humanity in the person of Jesus, would be impossible. As the Nicene Creed (AD 381) confesses, “For us men and for our salvation, He came down from heaven, and was incarnate by the Holy Spirit of the Virgin Mary, and was made man.” The doctrine of the Incarnation, which is foundational to Christian belief, asserts that Jesus is truly God and truly man , without confusion, change, division, or separation. This theological paradox is made possible by the Virgin Birth. Jesus’ humanity is genuine, for He is born of a woman (Galatians 4:4). Yet, He is not born of a human father, which safeguards His divine nature. In Christ, the divine and the human coexist in perfect harmony. As Athanasius famously said, “He became what we are that He might make us what He is.” The Virgin Birth does not merely make Jesus a human being; it makes Him the perfect Mediator between God and man. Jesus is not God pretending to be human, nor is He a mere man elevated to divinity. He is fully and simultaneously both, one person with two natures. As the Chalcedonian Definition (AD 451) affirms, “ Christ is to be acknowledged in two natures, inconfusedly, unchangeably, indivisibly, inseparably. ” This precise formulation safeguards the integrity of the Virgin Birth and ensures that Jesus is qualified to serve as the perfect Savior, both fully divine to redeem humanity and fully human to represent humanity before God. 33

text: PART TWO: CHRIST AND HIS WORK OF SALVATION Chapter 4: The Kingdom and the Cross The gospel message proclaimed by Jesus Christ was never a simplistic religious slogan, nor was it merely a message of personal improvement. Central to His message was the declaration that the Kingdom of God had arrived in Him (Mark 1:15). However, this proclamation cannot be rightly understood without the shadow of the cross looming over it. The Kingdom and the Cross are not antithetical; rather, they are intrinsically intertwined, revealing the paradox of a King whose coronation came through crucifixion. To truly grasp the gospel, one must reckon with this tension, how Jesus is both the reigning King and the Suffering Servant, how the Kingdom is both already and not yet, and how the cross is not an unfortunate detour but a central pillar of Christ’s redemptive work. Jesus’ Proclamation of the Kingdom and the Necessity of the Cross From the outset of His public ministry, Jesus preached, “The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand; repent, and believe in the gospel” (Mark 1:15, KJV). This announcement was pregnant with Old Testament expectations, of deliverance, of the reign of the Messiah, and of the restoration of Israel. Yet, the method through which this Kingdom would be inaugurated was unexpected. Instead of military conquest or political revolution, Jesus’ path to enthronement would lead through betrayal, suffering, and death. Many of Jesus’ contemporaries anticipated a Messiah who would deliver them from Roman oppression and reestablish the Davidic monarchy in political terms. Jesus redefined those expectations. As N.T. Wright observes, “Jesus’ death was not a defeat but the means by which God’s kingdom was established. The cross was the moment when the love of God overcame the powers of darkness and sin” (Wright, The Day the Revolution Began , 2016). text: Chapter 5: Redemption - Understanding Christ’s Death The death of Jesus Christ occupies the central place in Christian theology. It is the event upon which the entire edifice of the Christian faith rests. His death is the pivotal moment in human history, where the eternal plan of God for the salvation of mankind is fulfilled. However, comprehending the nature and purpose of Christ’s death requires a deep and reflective engagement with the various models and theories that attempt to explain what transpired at the cross. Throughout history, scholars and theologians have formulated multiple theories to explain the purpose of Jesus’ death. Some of these models have focused on the penal and judicial aspects of the redemption, while others have emphasized its cosmic and victorious dimensions. However, it is not sufficient to consider only one aspect of the redemption. To fully grasp the redemptive work of Christ, one must examine the various perspectives that together present a more complete and comprehensive understanding of His sacrifice. In this chapter, we will engage in a thorough examination of the nature and purpose of Christ’s death, considering various theories of redemption and their implications. We will argue that the most coherent and comprehensive understanding of the redemption emerges from a synthesis of two primary theories: Penal Substitution and Christus Victor . These models, when combined, provide a profound and holistic understanding of what Christ achieved through His death on the cross. 42

text: Redemption - The Great Work of Christ Before delving into the various theories, it is important to clarify the term that we will use to describe what Christ accomplished on the cross: redemption . It is imperative to note that atonement is not a term primarily used in the New Testament to describe the work of Christ. The word “atonement” in the Old Testament (Kippur) means “to cover,” which relates to the temporary covering of sins in the sacrificial system. However, the New Testament consistently speaks of redemption and remission as the key concepts to describe what Christ accomplished. In Greek, the word most often associated with the work of Christ is ἀπολύτρωσις ( apolytrōsis ), which means “redemption” or “deliverance,” and it signifies the release of someone from bondage through the payment of a ransom. In this context, Jesus’ death is understood as the payment for the ransom that was required to set humanity free from the dominion of sin, death, and Satan. Additionally, the concept of remission is also crucial in understanding the redemptive work of Christ. Remission (Greek: ἄφεσις aphesis ) means “forgiveness” or “cancellation of debt,” highlighting that Jesus’ death also accomplished the complete forgiveness of sin and the cancellation of the penalty for transgressions. Thus, when we speak of Christ’s death, we are referring to redemption and remission , not merely the covering of sins. text: In order to understand the redemptive work of Christ, we must explore the various models and theories that theologians have proposed to explain the nature of Christ’s death. These models emphasize different facets of the redemptive work but, when integrated, offer a more comprehensive understanding of what occurred on the cross. The New Testament describes salvation using various metaphors, legal, sacrificial, relational, and economic. Among these, the idea of ransom stands out, especially in statements like Jesus’ own words: “The Son of Man came… to give his life a ransom for many” (Mark 10:45). This concept has led to rich, though sometimes controversial, theological exploration throughout church history. What exactly was the ransom? Who was it paid to? And how does this connect with Jesus’ declaration on the cross, “It is finished” (John 19:30)? 1. The Ransom Theory: A Historical Lens The Ransom Theory is one of the earliest ways the church explained what Jesus did to save us. It teaches that humanity was in bondage to sin, death, and Satan, and that Jesus gave His life as a ransom to set us free. Early church leaders like Origen and Gregory of Nyssa believed this ransom was paid to Satan, who they thought held people captive. They saw Christ’s death as a kind of deal that secured our release. Bible verses like Mark 10:45 (“to give his life a ransom for many”) and 1 Timothy 2:6 (“who gave himself a ransom for all”) were used to support this idea. While the theory highlights Jesus’ victory over evil, it has some problems. It suggests that Satan had a rightful claim over people and that God had to bargain with 44

text: him, something the Bible doesn’t clearly teach. So, while the Ransom Theory helped early Christians understand freedom through Christ, it doesn’t fully line up with the whole message of Scripture. Who Was the Ransom Paid To? To resolve the tension found in earlier atonement theories, theologians like Anselm of Canterbury offered a significant corrective during the medieval period. In his seminal work Cur Deus Homo (“Why God Became Man”), Anselm argued that the issue was not Satan’s claim over humanity but rather the justice and honor of God that had been violated by human sin. Sin, he taught, is an offense against the holiness of God, and the resulting “debt” is owed to God’s righteousness, not to the devil. As Scripture affirms, “The wages of sin is death…” (Romans 6:23). This understanding laid the groundwork for what became known as the Satisfaction Theory , where Christ satisfies the honor of God. Later Reformers built on this foundation to articulate Penal Substitution , emphasizing that Christ bore the punishment we deserved, in our place. The essential message remains: sin is a moral and legal offense against a holy God, and Christ “pays” the debt by offering Himself as a perfect and willing substitute. “Paid in Full”: The Declaration of Victory Jesus’ final words on the cross, “It is finished” (John 19:30), come from the Greek word tetelestai, a commercial term that means “paid in full.” This would have been stamped on receipts in ancient times, showing that a debt had been completely settled. So, when Jesus said “It is finished,” He wasn’t just saying “It’s over” He was declaring that nothing more is needed for salvation. The debt was 45

He bore our punishment, fulfilling Isaiah 53:5, which says, “He was wounded for our transgressions, he was bruised for our iniquities.” And in satisfying divine justice, He became the atoning sacrifice for our sins, as 1 John 2:2 reveals. text: A Complete Redemption While the early Ransom Theory rightly emphasized the liberation Christ secured for us, it erred in suggesting that a payment was made to Satan. The consistent witness of Scripture points instead to a debt owed to God’s justice, not to the devil. Christ’s death was the all-sufficient payment, once and for all, meeting the righteous demands of a holy God. His resurrection stands as the divine confirmation that the payment was accepted, the debt fully satisfied. When Jesus declared, “It is finished,” He wasn’t speaking metaphorically; He meant that the work of redemption was completely accomplished for all who believe. 2. Satisfaction Theory The Satisfaction Theory was developed by Anselm of Canterbury in the 11th century. This model suggests that Christ’s death was necessary to restore God’s honor, which had been offended by human sin. According to Anselm, sin is not just a violation of moral law but an offense against God’s infinite dignity. The satisfaction of God’s justice is required to address this offense, and since humanity cannot make the necessary atonement, Christ, as both fully God and fully human, provides the perfect satisfaction by offering His life as a sacrifice. The strength of the Satisfaction Theory lies in its emphasis on God’s justice and the honor of His name. It stresses that sin must be addressed with the utmost seriousness, as it is an offense against the Creator. Christ, being both divine and human, is the only one capable of satisfying the demands of divine justice. 47

text: Critics of the Satisfaction Theory argue that it can portray God as requiring a form of “payment” for His honor, which seems inconsistent with the biblical understanding of grace and love. Moreover, some have suggested that this theory neglects the relational aspect of God’s redemptive work, focusing too much on the judicial aspect. Nevertheless, it remains an important contribution to the broader understanding of redemption, emphasizing that Christ’s death was necessary to satisfy God’s justice and restore His honor. 3. Penal Substitution The Penal Substitution theory is one of the most widely accepted models of redemption in contemporary evangelical theology. It argues that Christ’s death was a substitutionary sacrifice for the penalty of sin. This model asserts that because sin incurs a penalty namely, death Christ took upon Himself the punishment that humanity deserved. Penal Substitution is rooted in Scriptures such as Isaiah 53:5, which states, “But He was pierced for our transgressions; He was crushed for our iniquities; the punishment that brought us peace was upon Him,” and 2 Corinthians 5:21, which declares, “God made Him who had no sin to be sin for us, so that in Him we might become the righteousness of God.” These passages highlight the reality that Jesus, in His sacrificial death, took upon Himself the full penalty for human sin, thereby satisfying the demands of God’s justice. Critics of Penal Substitution argue that it presents an overly legalistic and impersonal view of salvation, where God seems to be portrayed as wrathful and punitive. They assert that this theory, if misunderstood, may obscure 48

Christus Victor Christus Victor: The Paradox of Victory Through Sacrifice The Christus Victor model of redemption emphasizes Christ’s triumph over the powers of sin, death, and Satan. It highlights the cosmic dimension of redemption, portraying Jesus’ death on the cross as the decisive victory in the spiritual battle between good and evil. In this view, Christ does not simply provide forgiveness; He breaks the enslaving grip of the powers that once held humanity captive. The Scriptural Foundation This theme of triumph is deeply rooted in Scripture: ● Colossians 2:15 – “Having disarmed principalities and powers, He made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them in it [the cross].” ● 1 John 3:8 – “For this purpose the Son of God was manifested, that he might destroy the works of the devil.” These verses, among others, affirm that the cross was not just a payment or penalty, it was a cosmic victory , where Christ overcame the kingdom of darkness and set captives free (cf. Luke 4:18-19). Complementing, Not Replacing Penal Substitution 49

text: While Christus Victor captures the grandeur of Christ’s victory, it has been critiqued for potentially neglecting the need for redemption in the form of penal substitution. Without addressing divine justice and the penalty for sin, the model may seem incomplete. For this reason, many theologians propose that Christus Victor and Penal Substitution are complementary rather than mutually exclusive: the victory was achieved through the sacrificial and substitutionary death of Christ. The Lamb wins the war by laying down His life. The Paradox of Victory: The Lion and the Lamb At the heart of Christus Victor theology lies a divine paradox: Christ conquers not by force, but by sacrifice. His apparent weakness becomes His greatest strength, and His death becomes His decisive victory. This mystery is beautifully captured in Revelation 5:5–6 , where John hears of the Lion of Judah but sees a Lamb that was slain. Jesus is both Lion and Lamb, powerful yet meek, sovereign yet sacrificial. 1. The Victorious Victim Christ is not a conqueror by aggression, but by becoming the ultimate victim , the Lamb slain for the sins of the world. What looked like defeat on the cross was, in reality, heaven’s greatest triumph . 2. The Divine Irony of Redemption God’s redemptive plan subverts human expectations. The Messiah does not conquer by sword but by suffering . As Paul puts it, “God’s strength is made perfect in weakness” (2 Corinthians 12:9). This divine irony, victory through vulnerability is the essence of the Gospel. 50

text: Chapter 6: The Resurrection and the Necessity for Salvation Introduction The resurrection of Jesus Christ is the bedrock of the Christian faith. It is not simply an event in history but a reality that changes everything about how we understand salvation, eternal life, and our relationship with God. Without the resurrection, the gospel itself would be rendered meaningless. As Paul writes in 1 Corinthians 15:17, “If Christ be not raised, your faith is vain; ye are yet in your sins.” But the resurrection is not merely a victory over death; it is a pivotal act that affirms Christ’s priestly work and provides a lasting assurance of the believer’s justification and eternal life. In this chapter, we will explore two major aspects of the resurrection: its doctrinal necessity for salvation and its connection to Christ’s ongoing priestly ministry in heaven. These two themes are deeply intertwined, and understanding both is essential to grasp the full meaning of Christ’s resurrection. I. The Resurrection: A Doctrinal Necessity for Salvation The resurrection of Christ is not a secondary or peripheral doctrine in the Christian faith, it is central to the entire message of the gospel. Without the resurrection, there is no salvation, no justification, and no victory over sin and death. The New Testament consistently ties the resurrection to the doctrine of salvation, and it is in this context that we must first understand its theological importance. 1. The Resurrection as Validation of the Redemption Sacrifice One of the key functions of the resurrection is that it serves as the divine validation of Christ’s sacrificial death. In Romans 4:25, Paul makes this connection clear when he writes: “Who was delivered for our offences, and was raised again for our justification.” Christ’s death alone is not enough for our salvation unless God confirms that it was accepted as the payment for sin. The resurrection is the seal of approval from God the Father. By raising Jesus from the dead, God declares that the sacrifice of Christ was acceptable, and therefore, we are justified before Him. Without the resurrection, we would have no assurance that Jesus’ death truly secured our salvation. The resurrection proves that Christ’s death was not a tragic failure but a victorious act in which He paid the full penalty for sin. As Paul writes in 1 Corinthians 15:20, Christ is the “firstfruits” of those who have died, meaning that His resurrection guarantees that those who believe in Him will also be raised. 2. The Resurrection and Victory Over Sin and Death Another crucial aspect of the resurrection is that it signifies victory over sin and death . In the Old Testament, the high priest’s role was to offer sacrifices for the people and intercede on their behalf. Christ’s resurrection signifies that He is now the eternal High Priest , not only because He was raised but because His resurrection inaugurated His priestly work . 1. The Consecration of Christ’s Priesthood In the Old Testament, the consecration of a priest was a detailed and ceremonial process. The priest had to undergo a period of purification and consecration before he could enter the Holy of Holies and offer sacrifices for the sins of the people. This same principle applies to Christ’s priesthood. When Jesus rose from the dead, He was not merely resuscitated to live again; He was consecrated as the eternal High Priest . In John 20:17, after His resurrection, Jesus tells Mary Magdalene, “Touch me not; for I am not yet ascended to my Father.” This statement is often overlooked, but it carries profound significance. The reason Jesus forbade Mary from touching Him was not because He was not physically real, but because He was in the process of being consecrated . His ascension to the Father was the act that would complete His consecration as the High Priest in the heavenly tabernacle (Hebrews 9:11). 58

text: Identification with Christ in His Resurrection While Christ’s death is often emphasized for its substitutionary role (bearing the punishment of sin), the resurrection is equally vital because it signifies the new life believers receive when they are united with Him in His resurrection. ● Romans 6:4 : “Therefore we are buried with Him by baptism into death: that like as Christ was raised up from the dead by the glory of the Father, even so we also should walk in newness of life.” ● Ephesians 2:6 : “And hath raised us up together, and made us sit together in heavenly places in Christ Jesus.” Identification with Christ’s resurrection is not limited to a spiritual experience of being made alive in Christ (Ephesians 2:5), but also involves being spiritually united with Him to share in His victory over sin, death, and the grave. Resurrection is the validation of salvation and the promise of eternal life (Romans 6:5, 1 Peter 1:3-4). Theological implications of identification with Christ’s resurrection: ● New Life : As Christ rose from the dead, so believers are raised to newness of life (Romans 6:4). This is not just a spiritual renewal but the beginning of the eternal life that will culminate in the resurrection of the body (1 Corinthians 15:20-23). ● Victory over Death : Christ’s resurrection signifies His victory over death, and by extension, believers share in this victory (1 Corinthians 15:54-57). The resurrection guarantees that believers will one day be physically resurrected and transformed. 64

text: ● Ongoing Transformation : While the resurrection is a past historical event, its ongoing implications in the life of the believer are vital. The believer is to live out the reality of being united with Christ in His resurrection by walking in the newness of life (Romans 6:4). Conclusion The resurrection of Jesus Christ is the linchpin of the Christian faith. It validates Christ’s sacrifice, secures the believer’s justification, and guarantees eternal life. Without the resurrection, the entire narrative of salvation collapses, as Paul asserts, “If Christ be not raised, your faith is vain; ye are yet in your sins” (1 Corinthians 15:17 KJV). The resurrection is the ultimate evidence that Christ’s work was successful and that it is applicable to all who place their faith in Him. Through union with Christ, believers share in His death and resurrection, experiencing both spiritual regeneration and the sure hope of future glorification. Thus, the resurrection is not only the culmination of Christ’s redemptive work but also the foundational guarantee of salvation, transforming both the present and future reality of all who believe. 65

text: PART THREE: THE GOSPEL AND ITS WORKINGS Chapter 7: Saved by the Gospel Romans 1:16-17 - The Gospel as the Power of God unto Salvation The gospel, as presented in the Apostle Paul’s epistle to the Romans, is not merely a set of moral instructions or a philosophy of life, but the dynamic and transformative power of God for salvation. This power, accessible to all who believe, forms the foundation for the Christian life and its eternal consequences. In this chapter, we will explore the multifaceted nature of the gospel as it is revealed in Romans 1:16-17, focusing particularly on its potency in salvation, its distinction from mere good advice, and its relationship to faith, which is both a gift and a response. 1. The Gospel: More than Just Good Advice Paul begins Romans 1:16 with the emphatic declaration: “For I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ.” This phrase marks a significant distinction between the gospel and mere advice. Advice is often a collection of suggestions or counsel that one may or may not choose to follow based on personal preference, knowledge, or experience. Good advice can be beneficial in a worldly sense, offering guidance for success, well-being, or happiness. However, it is ultimately human-centered, relying on the wisdom of man and the common experiences of life. In stark contrast, the gospel is not the product of human ingenuity but divine revelation. It is the “good news” (Greek euangelion ) of God’s action in history, of what He has done for humanity through the person and work of Jesus Christ. The gospel is the announcement of God’s redemptive intervention in a world lost to sin and death. It is the revelation of God’s 66

title: Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption
source_file: /Users/macuser/Downloads/Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption.pdf
text: plan for salvation, how He has acted to restore fallen humanity to Himself. Unlike advice, the gospel is not optional; it is the only means by which salvation is attained. Paul’s assertion that he is “not ashamed” of the gospel speaks to the fact that it is, at its core, a divine truth that demands response, not a mere philosophical option to consider. For the gospel is both an invitation and a proclamation. It confronts the listener with the reality of their need for a Savior and calls them to faith in Jesus Christ, who alone is the way, the truth, and the life (John 14:6). It is the ultimate message that humanity’s hope lies in Christ alone, His person, His atoning death, and His victorious resurrection. Thus, the gospel is not just good advice, it is the good news that changes everything. It calls people to respond in faith, not merely to consider options. Its truth and impact are undeniable, and it is this gospel that has the power to save. 2. The Power of the Gospel for Salvation Paul further elaborates the nature of the gospel by declaring it to be “the power of God unto salvation” (Romans 1:16). Here, Paul is underscoring a vital theological concept, the gospel is not just a message of hope, but it is imbued with the very power of God to bring about salvation. This is no ordinary message; it is a divine, supernatural force that transcends human wisdom and ability. It is God’s appointed means for saving humanity. The word “power” ( dunamis in Greek) conveys the idea of inherent strength, potency, and capacity. It is not simply the power to inform or to inspire, but the power to effect change at the deepest level, spiritually, 67

text: eternally, and comprehensively. This power is not only capable of changing a person’s life, but it can radically alter their eternal destiny. It is through the gospel that God’s redemptive plan is unfolded, leading to salvation. Salvation is not something humanity can achieve through self-effort or religious rituals. It is a work of God from beginning to end. From the very first proclamation of the gospel, it has the power to regenerate hearts, awaken consciences, and bring sinners from death to life. This regeneration, known as the new birth, is a supernatural act of God in which He imparts spiritual life where there was once spiritual death. This is precisely what Jesus spoke of in John 3 when He conversed with Nicodemus about being “born again” by the Holy Spirit. The gospel is the channel through which the Spirit works, applying the redemptive benefits of Christ’s finished work to the heart of the believer. The gospel, as the power of God, extends to all who believe, transcending human limitations of race, status, and moral history. It is “unto salvation to everyone that believeth” (Romans 1:16). The message of salvation is not exclusive or discriminatory, but it is accessible to all who respond in faith. From the Jew first and also to the Greek, it spans the entirety of humanity. This inclusivity is essential in understanding the universal scope of salvation: it is a gift of grace that is open to every person who hears the gospel and responds with belief. 3. Faith: Not Demanded but Imparted Through the Hearing of the Gospel Paul continues in Romans 1:17 with the statement, “For therein is the righteousness of God revealed from faith to faith.” This key verse 68

Faith, therefore, is not something that we generate from within, but something that is received from God as we hear His word proclaimed. In this sense, the gospel is profoundly relational. God communicates His truth through the gospel, and it is through this communication that faith arises in the hearts of those who hear. The Righteousness of God Revealed Romans 1:17 declares that “the righteousness of God is revealed from faith to faith.” This righteousness refers not only to God’s perfect holiness and justice, but also to His unwavering faithfulness to fulfill His covenant promises. Throughout the Old Covenant, God made promises of redemption, of a new heart, of a new creation that would reflect His own righteousness. In the gospel, we see those promises fulfilled not because of human merit, but because of the finished work of Christ. The righteousness of God is thus revealed in two beautiful ways: first, in His faithfulness to the covenant keeping His word to redeem humanity through the Seed of Abraham; and second, in the righteousness that He imparts to those who believe. This righteousness is not earned by works, but is credited to us by faith, just as it was with Abraham (Romans 4:3). Through Christ’s sacrificial death and triumphant resurrection, God’s justice is satisfied and His mercy is made known. As the gospel is preached, we behold both the faithfulness and the righteousness of God, He has made a way for us to become the righteousness of God in Him (2 Corinthians 5:21). 5. From Faith to Faith: The Ongoing Life of Faith The phrase “from faith to faith” in Romans 1:17 captures a profound theological transition. It can be understood as the unfolding of God’s redemptive plan moving from the faith exemplified under the Old Covenant, often described as “faith toward God” (Hebrews 6:1), to the 70

This is not merely a shift in content, but in covenantal reality: in the former, men believed God’s promises from afar, awaiting fulfillment; in the latter, we believe in the finished work of Christ, who is both the object and the embodiment of faith. Thus, the believer’s life is not only initiated by faith but is sustained and matured by the continual appropriation of Christ’s own faithfulness. It is the divine message that reveals the righteousness of God and is the means by which faith is imparted to the believer. The gospel is more than good advice, it is the ultimate good news that brings life and salvation to all who believe. Through the gospel, the righteousness of God is revealed, and faith is not demanded but imparted. As we receive the gospel by faith, we are transformed, not only in our standing before God but in our everyday lives, as we continue to live by faith in the one who has saved us. May we never forget that the gospel is not merely a message to be heard, but the power of God to change lives, redeem hearts, and save souls for all eternity. It is the gospel of Jesus Christ that brings us from death to life, from darkness to light, and from sin to righteousness. And it is by faith, in response to this gospel, that we are saved. Let us, then, with Paul, boldly declare, “I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ, for it is the power of God unto salvation to everyone that believeth” (Romans 1:16). 71

text: Chapter 8: Redemptive History - The Plan for the Gospel Order of Salvation (Ordo Salutis) - How Salvation Unfolds in Time and Experience The term “ordo salutis” (the order of salvation) refers to the divinely orchestrated sequence of events through which God applies the benefits of redemption to individuals. This process is neither random nor accidental; it is a corporate and Christocentric plan unfolding according to God’s eternal purpose. From the very foundation of the world, God predetermined the salvation of His people in Christ , whose work secures the believer’s position before God. The ordo salutis is not a mere theological abstraction; it is the unfolding of God’s sovereign will throughout history, brought to fruition by the work of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit . This salvation process begins with God’s eternal plan of predestination (chosen in Christ ) and culminates in glorification , where believers are fully conformed to the image of His Son. The classical understanding of the ordo salutis includes predestination, calling, justification, sanctification, and glorification. These stages are not isolated events but represent the coherent outworking of God’s redemptive plan in the lives of His people, all accomplished in Christ . Each step reveals God’s infinite wisdom, grace, and love in the salvation of sinners, and each is integral to the whole of God’s purpose to make a people holy, without blame, and conformed to Christ. In this chapter, we will examine these stages in detail, reflecting on how they are grounded in Scripture, particularly in the writings of the Apostle 72

title: Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption
source_file: /Users/macuser/Downloads/Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption.pdf
text: Paul, and how they demonstrate the unity and coherence of God’s redemptive plan. We will also highlight how salvation is experienced in time and history, while remaining firmly anchored in God’s eternal purpose, a purpose that already secures the believer in Christ and will yet be fully consummated at His return. 1. Predestination: God’s Eternal Decree The doctrine of predestination is one of the most profound and frequently debated themes in Christian theology. For centuries, believers have wrestled with the question of how God’s sovereignty interacts with human will, particularly in the matter of salvation. While the deterministic Calvinistic model interprets predestination to mean that God, by His sovereign will alone, unconditionally selected a fixed number of individuals to be saved before the foundation of the world, I align with a different theological perspective, the corporate or Christocentric election view . In this Christ-centered framework, Christ is the Elect One , and all who are united with Him by faith share in that election. The focus is not on individual pre-selection for salvation, but on God’s eternal purpose in Christ to create a new humanity those who would be holy, blameless, and conformed to His image. Ephesians 1:4–5 expresses this beautifully: “According as he hath chosen us in him before the foundation of the world, that we should be holy and without blame before him in love: Having predestinated us unto the adoption of children by Jesus Christ to himself, according to the good pleasure of his will.” (KJV) 73

text: Notice that Paul says, “chosen us in Him” . The election is in Christ , not outside of Him. It is not that God arbitrarily picked a group of individuals to be saved while ignoring the rest; rather, He determined that whoever is in Christ through faith would partake of His life, righteousness, and destiny. This is corporate election : God chose a body, a people, a new creation in Christ, and we become part of that elect people when we believe the gospel. This view aligns seamlessly with other scriptural affirmations of God’s universal salvific will: “Who will have all men to be saved, and to come unto the knowledge of the truth.” - 1 Timothy 2:4 “For the grace of God that bringeth salvation hath appeared to all men.” - Titus 2:11 These verses do not portray a selective grace, but a universal invitation . God’s grace has been made manifest to all humanity, and His desire is that every person come to saving knowledge through Christ. Therefore, when Scripture uses terms like “some” or “many” , it is often representative language pointing to categories or groups rather than restrictive language meant to exclude others from God’s salvific plan. Romans 8:29–30 further reinforces this Christocentric view when rightly understood: “For whom he did foreknow, he also did predestinate to be conformed to the image of his Son...” (KJV) This foreknowledge is not about deterministic causation, but about God’s loving awareness and relational knowledge of those who would believe. To be clear, I do not align with the deterministic Calvinistic model of predestination, which suggests that God’s election is unrelated to human response or foreseen faith. Rather, I believe that God’s redemptive plan in Christ is extended to all , and those who receive Christ by faith become partakers of that predestined purpose, to be adopted, sanctified, and ultimately glorified. In sum, predestination is not a doctrine of exclusion but of inclusion through Christ . It highlights God’s faithfulness to His redemptive promise and His eternal intent to bring many sons to glory through the work of His Son. It is a message of hope, not fear; of purpose, not fatalism. It is a holy, gracious summons to partake in the life and purpose of the risen Christ. text: The New Testament does affirm that the call of God is powerful and transformative. It is through the preaching of the gospel and the work of the Holy Spirit that hearts are awakened, minds are enlightened, and people are enabled to respond in faith. This is why Paul could say in Romans 10:17: “So then faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the word of God.” (KJV) While Reformed theologians like Louis Berkhof argue that the “effectual call... brings about the salvation of those whom He calls,” this position presupposes a view of individual, unconditional election that, as noted earlier, does not align with the Christocentric framework. Rather, the calling of God should be viewed in light of Titus 2:11 , which declares: “For the grace of God that bringeth salvation hath appeared to all men.” Thus, the call of God is both universal and particular . It is universal in that the gospel goes out to all; it is particular in that those who respond in faith enter into the benefits of God’s eternal purpose. The Spirit of God is always at work drawing, revealing, and convicting, but the effectiveness of the call is not based on an irresistible decree; it is based on faith in response to grace . In this view, the call is indeed “effectual,” but its efficacy is not predetermined it becomes effectual when the hearer believes . As such, the calling is not about an inevitable response, but an invitational grace that makes response possible. God takes the initiative, and we are responsible to respond. 77

Justification: The Declaration of Righteousness Justification stands as a vital pillar in the ordo salutis , the sequence of redemptive acts through which God’s eternal plan is realized in time. In the context of God’s corporate election in Christ , justification is the legal and gracious act by which God declares the believer righteous, not based on personal merit, but solely on the redemptive work of Jesus Christ. It is God’s answer to the universal human problem of guilt, and it rests entirely upon His faithfulness to His promise of salvation revealed in Christ. Paul powerfully articulates this truth in Romans 3:24 : “Being justified freely by his grace through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus.” (KJV) This verse emphasizes that justification is a free gift , grounded in grace , and mediated through the redemptive work of Christ. The term “freely” (Greek: dorean ) signifies “without cause” or “without merit”, reminding us that nothing in the sinner compels God to justify; rather, it is a sovereign act of mercy rooted in His eternal purpose in Christ. This is not a process, but a once-for-all legal declaration . The believer is not only forgiven , but reckoned righteous , accepted before a holy God and granted peace (shalom) that reconciles the broken relationship between Creator and creature. text: 4. Sanctification: The Ongoing Work of God in the Believer Sanctification, as presented in Scripture, is both a definitive act and an ongoing process , a dynamic that reflects the believer’s positional identity in Christ and their progressive growth in holiness . It is essential to view sanctification not merely as moral improvement, but as participation in Christ’s holiness through union with Him. From the moment of salvation, the believer is already sanctified. This positional sanctification is grounded in the believer’s inclusion in the body of Christ, the Elect One. As Hebrews 2:11 declares: “For both he that sanctifieth and they who are sanctified are all of one: for which cause he is not ashamed to call them brethren.” (KJV) Similarly, Jude begins his epistle by addressing those: “...that are sanctified by God the Father, and preserved in Jesus Christ, and called.” (Jude 1:1, KJV) In these passages, sanctification is a completed reality , accomplished by God through Christ and affirmed by the Spirit. The believer is set apart , marked as holy, not by their own efforts, but by virtue of their identification with the Risen Christ. This sanctification is rooted in the corporate reality of being “in Christ,” the sanctified One. Yet, sanctification is also progressive , a continual transformation by which the believer becomes increasingly conformed to the image of Christ. This is what Jesus prayed for in John 17:17: “Sanctify them through thy truth: thy word is truth.” (KJV) 81

text: Here we see that sanctification is not static; it involves the believer being continually formed by the truth, which is God’s Word, and ultimately Christ Himself (John 14:6). This transformation is not a self-driven moral endeavor but is accomplished by the indwelling Holy Spirit , who works to manifest Christ’s life through the believer. As Paul writes in 1 Thessalonians 4:3 : “For this is the will of God, even your sanctification...” (KJV) This will of God is not ambiguous, it is that His people grow in practical holiness. While sanctification begins as a positional act at salvation, it progresses through the believer’s Spirit-empowered cooperation with God’s grace. This includes a deliberate yielding to the Spirit, a disciplined life in the Word, prayer, fellowship, and obedience to the truth. Theologian J.I. Packer captures this well in Knowing God : “Sanctification is the work of God’s Spirit in us, enabling us to walk in His ways and bringing our lives into conformity with the holiness of God.” (Packer, Knowing God , 215) From a Christocentric perspective , sanctification is the believer’s increasing participation in the sanctified life of Christ , not a striving to become something apart from Him. It is Christ in us who is our sanctification (1 Corinthians 1:30), and we are invited to work out what God has already worked in (Philippians 2:12-13). Thus, sanctification is not just about sin management or spiritual performance, it is the outworking of a new identity, lived in fellowship with Christ and His body. It is the journey of becoming in experience what we already are in position: holy, beloved, and set apart in Christ . 82

text: 5. Glorification: The Final Fulfillment of Salvation Glorification is the climactic point in the ordo salutis , the believer’s salvation journey, representing the ultimate transformation into the full likeness of Christ . It speaks to both a present spiritual reality and a future bodily consummation . In Christ, the believer is already glorified; yet, in the resurrection, that glory will be fully revealed. Jesus Himself declared this shared glory in John 17:22 : “And the glory which thou gavest me I have given them; that they may be one, even as we are one.” (KJV) This verse affirms a present possession: by virtue of union with Christ , believers already partake of His glory. This is not a distant promise alone, but a current reality grounded in our identification with the glorified Son. Glorification begins now , as believers live out their new identity in Christ, bearing the Spirit of glory and being transformed from glory to glory (2 Corinthians 3:18). Yet, glorification is also a future hope , a consummated reality that will be unveiled at Christ’s return. text: Chapter 9: Images of Salvation - The Result of the Gospel Salvation, as presented in the New Testament, is a multifaceted reality with both profound theological depth and personal, experiential significance. Throughout Scripture, various metaphors and images are employed to help believers understand and appreciate the full scope of what God has accomplished in Christ. These images are not merely figurative but are deeply theological in nature, reflecting the multifaceted work of God in redemption. As we explore these biblical metaphors, justification, redemption, reconciliation, regeneration, and adoption, we will draw on both scriptural texts and the insights of scholars and theologians to enrich our understanding of salvation’s result. 1. Justification: The Legal Metaphor One of the most central and foundational metaphors in the New Testament is that of justification. Justification refers to the legal act by which God declares the sinner righteous on the basis of the righteousness of Jesus Christ. This declaration is not based on the individual’s merit but on the imputed righteousness of Christ. In Romans 3:24, Paul writes: “Being justified freely by his grace through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus.” (Romans 3:24, KJV) Justification is a judicial term, rooted in the legal courts of the ancient world. In this metaphor, the believer stands as a defendant before the bar of God’s justice. The charge of sin is read, but instead of condemnation, the believer is declared righteous because of Christ’s finished work. This declaration is not temporary but permanent, as Paul writes in Romans 5:1: 87

John Murray, in his Collected Writings , explains that justification is not merely the forgiveness of sins but the positive imputation of Christ’s righteousness to the believer: “Justification is a judicial act of God in which He declares the sinner righteous on the basis of the righteousness of Christ” (Murray, Collected Writings , 1.246). Thus, justification establishes peace with God and assures the believer of a restored relationship with their Creator. You may refer to my book, Justification: An Apologia Series on Christian Doctrine , for a deeper understanding. 2. Redemption in the Bible is rooted in the concept of slavery, particularly the liberation of a slave through the payment of a ransom. In the Old Testament, the Israelites’ deliverance from Egyptian slavery was seen as a typological foreshadowing of the greater redemption to come in Christ. In the New Testament, this metaphor is used to describe how Christ has freed believers from the slavery of sin. In Galatians 4:4-5, Paul writes: “But when the fullness of the time was come, God sent forth his Son, made of a woman, made under the law, To redeem them that were under the law, that we might receive the adoption of sons.” (Galatians 4:4-5, KJV) 88

text: Chapter 10: The Scope and Security of Salvation Salvation, as revealed in the Scriptures, is a profound and all-encompassing reality that transcends human understanding. It extends beyond mere forgiveness of sins; it is a comprehensive restoration of the believer’s relationship with God, involving every facet of the believer’s existence. In this chapter, we will explore the scope and security of salvation from a theological and devotional perspective, addressing fundamental questions such as: How wide is salvation? Who is it for? What is the relationship between the universal offer of salvation and its particular application? Can a believer lose their salvation? And how do we understand the perseverance of the saints in light of potential apostasy? 1. The Scope of Salvation: How Wide Is Salvation? The scope of salvation is vast, and the Scriptures present a salvation that is both universal in its offer and particular in its application. On one hand, salvation is offered to all people, regardless of race, ethnicity, or background. The New Testament repeatedly affirms that Christ’s death was for the sins of the world. John 3:16 expresses this universal scope clearly: “For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life.” (John 3:16, KJV) This verse underscores the inclusive nature of the gospel, declaring that salvation is offered universally. God’s love is not confined to any one group but is extended to “the world,” and the opportunity for eternal life is open to whosoever believes in Christ. This universal offer is echoed throughout the Scriptures, as seen in 1 Timothy 2:4: 94

title: Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption
source_file: /Users/macuser/Downloads/Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption.pdf
text: “Who will have all men to be saved, and to come unto the knowledge of the truth.” (1 Timothy 2:4, KJV) John Stott, in his The Cross of Christ , writes that “the universal scope of God’s love is the foundation of the gospel’s invitation to all” (Stott, The Cross of Christ , 48). It is not God’s will that any should perish (2 Peter 3:9), and the gospel calls all men everywhere to repent (Acts 17:30). Yet, while salvation is offered universally, it is only applied particularly to those who respond in faith. The gospel is an offer, but its benefits are appropriated by individuals through personal faith in Christ. As Jesus Himself says in Matthew 7:13-14: “Enter ye in at the strait gate: for wide is the gate, and broad is the way, that leadeth to destruction, and many there be which go in thereat: because strait is the gate, and narrow is the way, which leadeth unto life, and few there be that find it.” (Matthew 7:13-14, KJV) This suggests that while salvation is universally available, it is not universally received. The way to salvation is narrow, and it is only those who accept Christ in faith who will experience the full benefits of salvation. In the words of C.S. Lewis in Mere Christianity , “The Christian doctrine of salvation is that it is a universal offer to all people, but it is applied to individuals who choose to receive it” (Lewis, Mere Christianity , 146). Thus, while salvation is universally offered, it is applied to those who respond to God’s call in faith. 95

text: 2. The Universal Offer vs. the Particular Application The tension between the universal offer of salvation and the particular application of salvation is often a point of theological discussion. God’s offer of salvation in Christ is indeed universal, but its application is particular in that it only becomes effective in the life of the individual who believes in Jesus Christ. The doctrine of the universal offer of salvation is sometimes called “unlimited atonement,” the belief that Christ’s death was for all people, not just for a select few. This view is grounded in verses like 1 John 2:2: “And he is the propitiation for our sins: and not for ours only, but also for the sins of the whole world.” (1 John 2:2, KJV) The apostle John emphasizes here that Christ’s redemption work is sufficient for the entire world, and God’s desire is that all people be saved. However, this does not imply universalism, the belief that all people will be saved regardless of their response to the gospel. Rather, it means that Christ’s death has made salvation available to all who will believe. Yet, as John Calvin and other Reformed scholars have argued, the benefits of Christ’s death are applied only to those who are elected by God and come to faith in Christ. Calvin, in his Institutes of the Christian Religion , explains that while Christ’s sacrifice is sufficient for all, it is efficient only for the elect, those whom God has chosen for salvation (Calvin, Institutes , 3.24.15). This distinction between the universal offer and the particular application of salvation is crucial to understanding the scope of God’s redemptive plan. 96

text: 3. Can a Believer Lose Salvation? Addressing Eternal Security and Assurance The question of whether a believer can lose their salvation has been a point of theological contention throughout church history. On one hand, the doctrine of eternal security suggests that once a person is genuinely saved, they cannot lose their salvation. Advocates of this doctrine often refer to passages such as John 10:28-29, where Jesus assures His followers: “And I give unto them eternal life; and they shall never perish, neither shall any man pluck them out of my hand. My Father, which gave them me, is greater than all; and no man is able to pluck them out of my Father’s hand.” (John 10:28-29, KJV) This declaration points to the security of the believer, asserting that salvation is sustained not by human effort but by the power of God. Similarly, in Romans 8:38-39, Paul emphasizes that nothing, whether death, life, or any other force, can separate believers from the love of God in Christ: “For I am persuaded, that neither death, nor life, nor angels, nor principalities, nor powers, nor things present, nor things to come, nor height, nor depth, nor any other creature, shall be able to separate us from the love of God, which is in Christ Jesus our Lord.” (Romans 8:38-39, KJV) These verses serve to reassure believers of their eternal security, underscoring that nothing in creation can sever the relationship between the redeemed and God. 97

source_file: /Users/macuser/Downloads/Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption.pdf
text: However, there are other scriptural passages that seem to present a different perspective. The book of Hebrews, particularly Hebrews 6:4-6, warns of the possibility of apostasy, the willful rejection of the faith: “For it is impossible for those who were once enlightened, and have tasted of the heavenly gift, and were made partakers of the Holy Ghost, And have tasted the good word of God, and the powers of the world to come, If they shall fall away, to renew them again unto repentance; seeing they crucify to themselves the Son of God afresh, and put him to an open shame.” (Hebrews 6:4-6, KJV) This passage is often cited by those who advocate for conditional security , which holds that salvation is secure as long as one continues in faith but can be forfeited if the individual consciously and willfully turns away from Christ. In line with this, N.T. Wright, among others, suggests that while genuine believers are eternally secure in Christ, it is possible for a mature believer to intentionally and knowingly depart from the faith, thereby forfeiting their salvation. Wright, in The Resurrection of the Son of God , asserts that the possibility of apostasy remains real for those who have fully experienced the transforming power of the gospel but later choose to renounce Christ by their own volition (Wright, The Resurrection of the Son of God , 538). This tension between the assurances of eternal security and the warnings against apostasy requires a careful and balanced interpretation of Scripture. While eternal security assures believers that nothing can separate them 98

My Position: Assurance and the Believer’s Security In light of these theological considerations, I hold the position that a believer, as long as they remain in Christ, is eternally secure. This assurance is based not on the believer’s ability to maintain their salvation but on the unwavering work of Christ. As Jesus declared: “My sheep listen to My voice, and I know them, and they follow Me. I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of My hand.” (John 10:27-28, KJV) The security of the believer is rooted in the sovereignty of Christ, who holds them firmly in His grasp. If salvation depended on human effort, no one would be able to endure. It is by God’s grace, through Christ, that the believer remains secure. As Paul writes in Romans 8:33-34, no one can condemn the believer because God has justified them through Christ’s finished work. Additionally, Scriptures like Ephesians 1:13, Ecclesiastes 3:14, and 1 John 5:11-13 assure us that once the believer is sealed by the Holy Spirit, their eternal life is secured in Christ: “In whom ye also trusted, after that ye heard the word of truth, the gospel of your salvation: in whom also after that ye believed, ye were sealed with that holy Spirit of promise.” (Ephesians 1:13, KJV) However, I also hold that apostasy remains a real and serious possibility. While the believer’s eternal security is assured as long as they remain in Christ, the Bible does not shy away from the warning that a person can 99

text: willfully reject Christ after having known Him and experienced His saving grace. Such a decision is an act of the will and, according to Hebrews 6:4-6, results in the forfeiture of salvation. The key to understanding this tension is that the believer’s perseverance in the faith is not due to human strength but to God’s preserving grace. J.I. Packer, in Evangelism and the Sovereignty of God , explains that while believers are not immune to spiritual failure, they will not ultimately fall away. God’s grace preserves the believer, ensuring their final glorification. 4. The Perseverance of the Saints vs. Apostasy The doctrine of the perseverance of the saints teaches that those who are truly saved will persevere in faith to the end. It is the believer’s certainty of salvation, not based on their own ability, but on God’s faithfulness to keep them. Philippians 1:6 reflects this confidence: “Being confident of this very thing, that he which hath begun a good work in you will perform it until the day of Jesus Christ.” (Philippians 1:6, KJV) The perseverance of the saints is not a passive, effortless continuation in the faith but involves active participation in the means of grace, prayer, Scripture reading, the sacraments, and the fellowship of the church. Thomas Schreiner, in his Romans Commentary , emphasizes that while the perseverance of the saints is a promise, it is also a condition of those who are truly saved (Schreiner, Romans , 442). The believer’s ultimate perseverance is ensured by God’s power and promise, but it involves ongoing faith and obedience. 100

text: On the other hand, apostasy refers to the intentional abandonment of faith. Apostasy is a real danger, especially for those who have tasted the goodness of God but later reject it. Hebrews 10:38-39 warns: “Now the just shall live by faith: but if any man draw back, my soul shall have no pleasure in him. But we are not of them who draw back unto perdition; but of them that believe to the saving of the soul.” (Hebrews 10:38-39, KJV) Apostasy is a grave matter, and the Scriptures urge believers to remain steadfast in their commitment to Christ. The warnings against falling away are not meant to cause unnecessary fear but to encourage vigilance and faithfulness in the Christian life. Conclusion: The scope and security of salvation are inseparable themes in the Christian faith. Salvation is universally offered, and it is God’s will that all come to repentance. Yet, salvation is particularly applied to those who respond in faith, and its security rests in God’s sovereign grace. While Scripture assures believers of the eternal security of their salvation, it also warns of the possibility of apostasy and urges perseverance in the faith. Ultimately, the believer’s assurance of salvation rests in the promises of God, who has declared that nothing can separate us from His love. As we live out our salvation, we are called to remain faithful, knowing that God will preserve His own to the end. The scope of salvation is vast, but its security is rooted in the unchanging character of God, who is faithful to complete the good work He has begun in us. 101

text: PART FOUR: SALVATION AND CHRISTOLOGY Chapter 11: The Deity and Humanity of Christ in Salvation The doctrine of the person of Jesus Christ stands as one of the central pillars of Christian theology. The nature of Christ, His simultaneous deity and humanity, is not merely a theoretical abstraction but is intimately tied to the salvation of mankind. It is a doctrine that has profound implications for the work of salvation. How we understand the dual nature of Christ directly affects how we comprehend His role as the Savior. In this chapter, we will explore the relationship between the deity and humanity of Christ in the context of salvation, examine the potential theological dangers of overemphasizing one nature over the other, and reflect on the historical heresies, such as Adoptionism, that have arisen from misinterpreting the person of Christ. The proper balance of Christ’s two natures is essential not only for orthodox theology but also for the practical outworking of our salvation in Christ. 1. Is It Possible to Overemphasize the Deity of Christ at the Expense of His Humanity? The balance between the deity and humanity of Christ is a theological issue of significant import. In the early centuries of the Church, the doctrine of the nature of Christ was debated vigorously, leading to the development of creeds that sought to safeguard the truth of Scripture. The Nicene Creed (325 A.D.), in its affirmation of Jesus Christ as “true God from true God,” was a response to various heresies that diminished the divinity of Christ. Similarly, the Chalcedonian Definition (451 A.D.) solidified the 103

title: Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption
source_file: /Users/macuser/Downloads/Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption.pdf
text: understanding that Jesus Christ is both fully God and fully man, “without confusion, without change, without division, without separation.” While these creeds have formed the backbone of orthodox Christology, the question arises: can the deity of Christ be overemphasized at the expense of His humanity? Historically, one might say that the church has sometimes tilted toward emphasizing Christ’s divinity, especially in the face of heresies such as Arianism, which denied Christ’s full divinity. This can be seen in the theological tendency to speak of Jesus primarily in terms of His eternal, divine nature, sometimes at the expense of His human experiences. In doing so, there is a danger of reducing Jesus’ humanity to an illusion, thus losing the profound significance of His identification with human beings. As Michael Horton remarks in The Christian Faith , “Jesus did not simply appear to be human, nor did He merely wear a human body like a garment; He was truly and fully human” (Horton, The Christian Faith , 419). To speak of Jesus’ divinity in isolation from His humanity distorts the nature of the Incarnation. The Gospel writers, particularly the apostle John, emphasize both aspects of Christ’s nature. John 1:14 states: “And the Word was made flesh, and dwelt among us, (and we beheld his glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father,) full of grace and truth.” (John 1:14, KJV) In this verse, John emphasizes the reality of the Word becoming flesh, His humanity, and His full deity. If we were to overemphasize Christ’s deity, we would miss the significance of His identification with us in our human condition. 104

title: Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption

text: 2. How a Proper Christology Balances Both Natures A proper Christology must maintain a balance between Christ’s full deity and full humanity. The mystery of the Incarnation is that Jesus is both “very God and very man,” as articulated in the Nicene Creed. He is not a hybrid, nor does His divinity diminish His humanity, nor vice versa. The hypostatic union, the union of the divine and human natures in the one Person of Christ, is a central doctrine of Christian theology. This balance is crucial for understanding the redemptive work of Christ. If Jesus were only divine, He could not have experienced the full range of human experiences, suffering, temptation, and death, necessary for the salvation of humanity. Conversely, if Jesus were merely human, He would not have had the divine power necessary to accomplish salvation. As John Owen argues in The Death of Death in the Death of Christ , “If Christ had not been fully human, He could not have died for us. If He had not been fully divine, His death would not have been efficacious” (Owen, The Death of Death in the Death of Christ , 23). Hebrews 2:17-18 encapsulates the necessity of both natures: “Wherefore in all things it behoved him to be made like unto his brethren, that he might be a merciful and faithful high priest in things pertaining to God, to make reconciliation for the sins of the people. For in that he himself hath suffered being tempted, he is able to succour them that are tempted.” (Hebrews 2:17-18, KJV) Christ’s identification with humanity in His suffering and temptation makes Him a merciful and faithful high priest. Yet, His divine nature 105

text: ensures that His sacrifice is sufficient to make remission for the sins of the world. Both natures are essential for His mediatorial role. 3. The Errors of Adoptionism - Did Jesus Become the Son at Baptism, or Was He Always Divine? One of the historical errors in understanding the person of Christ is Adoptionism. This heresy, which can be traced back to the 2nd century, posited that Jesus was born a mere man and that He was “adopted” as the Son of God at His baptism. In this view, the divine Sonship of Jesus was not inherent but was conferred upon Him at a certain point in His life. Adoptionism was a significant heretical position because it undermined the eternal nature of Christ’s deity and the full significance of His Incarnation. The early Church condemned Adoptionism at the First Council of Nicaea in 325 A.D., affirming that Jesus was eternally the Son of God. The Nicene Creed states that Jesus is “begotten, not made, being of one substance with the Father.” This clear declaration emphasizes that Jesus was not a mere man who became divine, but the eternal Son of God who, in the fullness of time, took on human flesh. The Apostle Paul refutes Adoptionism in Philippians 2:6-7: “Who, being in the form of God, thought it not robbery to be equal with God: But made himself of no reputation, and took upon him the form of a servant, and was made in the likeness of men.” (Philippians 2:6-7, KJV) The phrase “being in the form of God” points to Christ’s eternal, divine nature before His Incarnation. The adoptionist view fails to recognize the eternal Sonship of Christ and misrepresents the nature of the Incarnation. 106
