text: 4. Why Understanding Christ’s Dual Nature Is Essential to Salvation The dual nature of Christ is not merely an academic issue, it is vital to the salvation of humanity. Christ’s dual nature ensures that He is the perfect mediator between God and man. Only as fully God could He bear the weight of the world’s sins; only as fully man could He represent humanity before God. The early Church Father Athanasius emphasized this in his famous work On the Incarnation : “For the Son of God became man so that we might become God. And He was made man that we might be made divine.” (Athanasius, On the Incarnation , 54) Athanasius understood that the salvation of humanity required both the divinity and the humanity of Christ. Theologians from the Reformation, such as Martin Luther and John Calvin, also affirmed the importance of Christ’s dual nature. Calvin, in Institutes of the Christian Religion , argues that “Christ’s two natures must be distinguished, for He is both true God and true man. But these two natures, though distinct, are perfectly united in one person” (Calvin, Institutes , 2.13.4). The doctrine of the hypostatic union is crucial because it ensures that Christ’s work on the cross was sufficient and applicable for humanity. Christ’s humanity allows Him to represent us in His suffering and death, while His divinity makes His sacrifice efficacious for our salvation. As the Apostle Paul writes in 1 Timothy 2:5: “For there is one God, and one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus.” (1 Timothy 2:5, KJV) 107

title: Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption

text: Christ’s dual nature, both fully God and fully man, establishes Him as the unique and perfect mediator between God and humanity. Without this balance, salvation would not be possible. Conclusion The deity and humanity of Christ are indispensable to the Christian doctrine of salvation. It is not possible to overemphasize Christ’s divinity at the expense of His humanity, nor vice versa. A proper Christology must balance both natures, recognizing the significance of each for the work of salvation. The errors of Adoptionism, which diminish Christ’s eternal divinity, serve as a cautionary tale, reminding us that Jesus was always the Son of God, not merely adopted as such at a certain point in history. Finally, understanding Christ’s dual nature is essential to salvation because only in His full deity and full humanity can Christ mediate between God and man, reconciling us to the Father and securing our redemption. This is the mystery of the Incarnation, the heart of the Christian gospel, and the foundation of our salvation. 108

text: Chapter 12: The Everlasting Covenant and Salvation The concept of covenant occupies a central role in biblical theology and serves as the framework for understanding God’s relationship with humanity throughout history. It is through covenant that God reveals His plans for salvation and enacts His promises to His people. From the covenant with Adam to the New Covenant in Christ, God’s unchanging nature and His faithfulness to His promises are the bedrock of salvation. The covenantal framework not only informs us of the historical unfolding of God’s salvific work but also shapes how we live as recipients of His grace. In this chapter, we will explore the theological significance of the everlasting covenant, as revealed in Scripture, particularly through the prophecies of Isaiah. We will also reflect on how this covenant shapes our understanding of salvation, God’s promises, and the way we, as believers, are called to reflect His glory in our daily lives. 1. Isaiah 59:21 - The Covenant of the Spirit and the Word Isaiah 59:21 speaks of a covenant between God, His people, and the Spirit: “As for me, this is my covenant with them, says the Lord: My Spirit that is upon you, and my words that I have put in your mouth, shall not depart out of your mouth, or out of the mouth of your offspring, or out of the mouth of your children’s offspring, says the Lord, from this time forth and forevermore.” (Isaiah 59:21, ESV) This covenant is unique in that it highlights the role of both the Spirit and the Word in God’s salvation plan. text: Conclusion: Soteria as Our Great Gain As we reflect on the profound realities of salvation, or Soteria , we are drawn into a deep and transformative understanding of God’s eternal purpose and His gracious provision for humanity. The eternal nature of salvation, its dual aspect as both a gift and a calling, and our subsequent responsibility to live in the fullness of Soteria, all converge into a call for us to recognize the treasure we have been given and the mission we are entrusted with. The heart of the gospel rests on the notion that we have received freely what we could never have earned, and in turn, we are compelled to freely give to others, extending the invitation of salvation to a world in desperate need. The Eternal Nature of Salvation One of the most astounding truths about salvation is its eternal nature. It is not a transient, fleeting moment; it is a gift that lasts for eternity. The Apostle Paul writes in Ephesians 1:4-5: “Even as he chose us in him before the foundation of the world, that we should be holy and blameless before him. In love he predestined us for adoption to himself as sons through Jesus Christ, according to the purpose of his will.” (Ephesians 1:4-5, ESV) Salvation, as part of God’s eternal plan, was not a mere afterthought but something intricately woven into the fabric of His divine will from before time began. The eternal nature of salvation speaks to its unchanging, irreversible quality. text: Closing Charge: Theosis - The Ultimate Purpose of Salvation Salvation is far more than an escape from the consequences of sin, it is an entrance into divine participation. The work of redemption was not merely about what we were saved from but, more importantly, about what we were saved for . The ultimate aim of salvation is not just forgiveness, but divine affiliation, union with God Himself. This is the doctrine of Theosis: the mystery of our transformation into partakers of the divine nature (2 Peter 1:4). Salvation as God’s Family Project From the beginning, salvation has always been a family project. It was never just about individual deliverance; it was about divine adoption. God did not simply redeem us as forgiven sinners; He brought us into sonship. This is why Paul declares in Romans 8:15-17, “For ye have not received the spirit of bondage again to fear; but ye have received the Spirit of adoption, whereby we cry, Abba, Father. The Spirit itself beareth witness with our spirit, that we are the children of God: And if children, then heirs; heirs of God, and joint-heirs with Christ…” Notice the progression: we are children , and if children, then we are heirs . Sonship and heirship are inseparable in God’s redemptive plan. We were not merely rescued, we were brought into inheritance. And this inheritance is not merely material or external blessings, it is God Himself. The highest thing God could ever give man is not wealth, health, or power, but Himself. 121

text: He Took What Was Ours, That We Might Take What Is His The work of Christ is an exchange of identities. In order for us to become what He is, He first had to become what we were. This is what 2 Corinthians 5:21 declares: “For he hath made him to be sin for us, who knew no sin; that we might be made the righteousness of God in him.” This is the divine transaction: He took our sin so that we might take His righteousness. He took on mortality so that we might receive immortality. He took on servanthood so that we might enter into sonship. This is not merely salvation, it is transformation. This is captured in the Greek word sumklēronomos, joint-heirs with Christ. The meaning is powerful: a co-inheritor, one who receives all that belongs to another not as a subordinate, but as an equal sharer in the inheritance. What belongs to Christ automatically belongs to us because we are now in Him . God Cannot Create Another God, But He Calls Us to Partake of His Divine Nature One of the profound mysteries of salvation is that even God cannot create another God, for divinity is uncreated. But what God can do, and has done, is call us into participation with Himself. We were created in His image, but in salvation, we go beyond mere reflection, we enter into union. This is why Peter says in 2 Peter 1:4, 122

Theosis does not mean we become gods in the sense of independent deities, but that we are engrafted into the very life of God Himself. What He has, we now share. What He is, we now partake in. Agape - The Love That Leads to Rest While Agapē is not linguistically a combination of “ago” (to lead) and “pausis” (rest), it beautifully illustrates a profound truth: God's love leads us into His rest. Agapē is the kind of love that bore our burden so we could enter His inheritance. It is the love that did not merely redeem us but re-formed us into sons. The work of salvation was never just about saving us from hell, it was about bringing us into eternal rest in God. Jesus did not merely come to erase our past, but to establish our future. Watch out for Volume 2 of this book: SOTERIA AND REST for more in-depth studies on salvation and the divine rest we have in Christ. This is the True Meaning of Salvation The scandal of the cross was that God died so that we might live. The paradox of the gospel is that the Lion conquered by becoming the Lamb. And the mystery of redemption is that He took what was ours, making it His, so that what was His could become ours. So, as we conclude this journey through the depth of salvation, we must recognize this unshakable truth: we were not merely saved from wrath, we were saved for union with God. text: Glossary of Theological Terms Soteria Derived from the Greek word ’sōtēria’, it denotes salvation, deliverance, preservation, and safety. In Christian theology, Soteria encapsulates the comprehensive work of God in rescuing humanity from the bondage of sin and restoring them into right relationship with Himself. It involves both a present experience of grace and a future hope of eternal glory. Salvation The divine initiative and process by which God delivers human beings from sin, death, and separation from Himself, and grants them eternal life through faith in Jesus Christ. Salvation encompasses justification, sanctification, and glorification, and is rooted in God’s mercy, grace, and eternal purpose. Redemption The action by which God, in His mercy and grace, delivers humanity from the consequences of sin. While sin entered the world through human choice, God, in His sovereignty, was able and willing to intervene with a plan of salvation, offering forgiveness and restoration through the sacrifice of Jesus Christ. Justification A forensic act of God in which He declares the sinner to be righteous on the basis of faith in Jesus Christ. Justification is not a process but a once-for-all legal declaration, grounded in the imputed righteousness of Christ. Sanctification 163

title: Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption
source_file: /Users/macuser/Downloads/Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption.pdf
text: The progressive work of the Holy Spirit in the life of the believer that makes them more free from sin and more like Christ in their actual life. It is both positional, having been set apart in Christ, and experiential, involving a lifelong process of transformation. Glorification The final phase of the Christian salvation process wherein believers are given resurrected, imperishable bodies and are fully conformed to the image of Christ. This state is free from sin, death, and suffering, and it consummates eternal life with God. Eternal Life The unending life with God that believers receive through faith in Jesus Christ. Eternal life is not just a future promise but a present reality that begins at the moment of salvation, as believers enter into fellowship with God, experiencing His love, peace, and joy. The Fall The event in which Adam and Eve, through their disobedience to God, introduced sin into the world. While God allowed this to happen according to His sovereign will , He did not actively cause it. The fall was part of God’s plan in that He, in His foreknowledge, knew it would occur, but He also had a provision in place to redeem humanity through Jesus Christ. Atonement In the Old Testament, atonement refers to the covering or cleansing of sin through sacrificial rituals, temporarily restoring fellowship between God and His people. In the New Testament, atonement signifies the complete 164

title: Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption
source_file: /Users/macuser/Downloads/Soteria_ The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption.pdf
text: removal of sin through the death of Jesus Christ, fully reconciling humanity to God. Infralapsarianism This view teaches that God’s decree to save came after His decree to allow the Fall. It affirms that: ● God decreed to create humanity. ● God permitted the Fall (but did not cause it). ● God then decreed to save human. ● God provided salvation through Christ. Supralapsarianism: A theological view regarding the order of God’s decrees, asserting that God’s decision to save or condemn was made before the decree to allow the fall of humanity. In this view, the fall of man is seen as part of God’s eternal plan to display both His justice and mercy. Supralapsarianism emphasizes that the plan of salvation was not a reaction to the fall but part of God’s deliberate and preordained plan. Theological Anthropology: The study of humanity from a biblical perspective, focusing on the nature of mankind, including the fall, sin, and the restoration of humanity through Christ. This field addresses the relationship between humanity and God, highlighting both human dignity and human depravity. The Kingdom of God: The reign of God over His creation, both now and in the future. The Kingdom of God is both a present reality, as believers experience God’s 165

text: rule in their lives, and a future hope, as God’s kingdom will be fully established at Christ’s return. Grace: The unmerited favor of God, extended to humanity in the form of forgiveness, salvation, and spiritual empowerment. Grace is foundational to the Christian understanding of salvation, as it is by grace that people are saved, not by their works (Ephesians 2:8-9). Mercy: God’s loving-kindness toward humanity, particularly in withholding judgment and offering forgiveness. While grace gives us what we do not deserve (salvation), mercy withholds from us what we do deserve (eternal separation from God). Sovereignty of God: The belief that God has ultimate authority over all things, not necessarily by controlling every action, but by being fully aware of all events and having the power and will to intervene as He chooses. In the context of the fall of man, God’s sovereignty means that while He was not the direct cause of the fall, He knew it would occur and, in His sovereign wisdom, already had a plan of redemption in place. God’s sovereignty ensures that despite the presence of sin, He is able and more than able to deliver humanity from the fall and its consequences. Foreknowledge of God: God’s perfect and eternal knowledge of all things before they happen. This includes His knowing that humanity would fall into sin. While God did not directly cause the fall, He knew it would happen and prepared a 166

text: About The Author The author, Dr. Daniel Folarin is a theologian, pastor, and professional leadership coach with a strong passion for equipping believers with the truth which is God’s Word. He serves as the Founder/Senior Pastor of Great Haven Assembly , a thriving church with expressions in Calgary and Regina, Canada, as well as Ikeja, Nigeria . He is also the President of Logos Institute , a theological training platform with a solid presence in India , where he has been instrumental in training hundreds of pastors . Dr. Folarin holds a Doctorate from Damascus Road Bible Institute and a Master of Divinity , bringing a wealth of scholarly insight and practical wisdom to his teachings. With years of experience in leadership development, he is committed to raising a generation of spiritually and intellectually sound believers. Beyond his ministerial work, he is a devoted husband to Tolulade Folarin and a loving father to Charis, Eirene, and Doxa . He and his family currently reside in Calgary, Alberta, Canada . Dr. Folarin is a sought-after speaker, writer, and mentor, known for his ability to bridge theological depth with practical Christian living. His mission is to see believers walk in the fullness of Soteria , the eternal salvation that God has freely given through Christ. 168

text: ACKNOWLEDGEMENT This work is born out of deep gratitude for the faithful labors of those who have gone before, men and women of whom the world was not worthy. I give thanks to God for the saints; pastors, teachers, mentors, and friends, who have devoted themselves to the sacred task of teaching the Word of Truth with clarity, courage, and compassion. Their unwavering commitment to the Gospel has not only shaped my understanding but has kindled in me a fire to carry this message further. I also stand on the shoulders of the fathers of the faith; apostolic voices, early church fathers, Reformers, Puritans, and modern theologians who, though separated by time and language, have proclaimed with one voice the supremacy of Christ, the victory of His cross, and the hope of His return. Their writings and sacrifices have laid the theological and spiritual foundation upon which this work seeks humbly to build. To the unnamed saints who contended for this truth in prayer, in preaching, in exile, and in martyrdom, your labor is not in vain. This book is, in part, your legacy. May the truths proclaimed in these pages advance the Gospel of the Kingdom and prepare the Church for her full inheritance in Christ. Soli Deo Gloria. 3

text: FOREWARD The book Soteria & Victorious Eschatology: Living in Christ’s Victory Today and Forever presents a fresh scriptural perspective to the subject of the all-encompassing nature of salvation. Dr. Daniel, like a wise master builder (1 Corinthians 3:10), has skillfully explored the themes on salvation with a present truth mindset of a victorious eschatological perspective. I agree with Dr. Folarin: “ If God has not given us the spirit of fear ” (2 Timothy 1), then we cannot have a fear-based eschatology. Our view of God’s redemptive work must be laced in hope and victory. The work may rattle religious minds, but that’s exactly what Jesus came to do. The religious order of His day could not comprehend what Jesus was teaching, not because it was not the truth, but because they were fixated on their interpretation of the Holy Scriptures and were not willing to see what Jesus was saying. Yet these scriptures testify of Him (John 5:39). What this book offers is pointing us back to scriptures. Christ's work on the cross, the holistic and dynamic effect of salvation was thoroughly explained in this book. Expounding on Paul’s theology of salvation, this work elucidates the correlation between Christ and the cosmos bringing a clear perspective to Colossians 1:20 & Romans 8:19-20. Furthermore, key concepts that would frame our worldview are adequately explained in this volume: Matthew 24, the role of Israel, “shortly and at hand”, the “New Heaven and the New Earth.” The inability to understand these terms in the light of the finished work of Christ and their biblical context has hindered us from walking in the triumphant redemptive plan of God.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: PREFACE Two years ago, in 2023, I began a journey of teaching about Victorious Eschatology. What started as a deep dive into the hope-filled, victorious view of the Kingdom of God soon turned into a significant turning point in my ministry. The series was long, thorough, and, I believe, enlightening for many. It shed light on perspectives of God’s eternal plan that many had not previously considered, offering fresh insights into the way we should understand and engage with the Scriptures, especially in relation to the eschatological promises of God. However, the journey was not without its challenges. While many in the congregation embraced the teachings with an open heart, seeking to understand the truth of God’s victory over all things, others found these new insights to be offensive. The change in perspective was unsettling for some, particularly those who had adhered to more traditional interpretations of prophecy. In fact, this teaching led to a difficult season in the church, as some members, shaken by the new ideas, chose to leave. It was a hard season, one that tested my resolve and stretched me in ways I had not anticipated. The emotional toll of losing members who had been deeply involved in the ministry was felt deeply, and I found myself questioning not just the direction of the church but my own calling as a pastor. Yet, in the midst of this turmoil, I made a firm resolution: I would not retreat from the truth that God had revealed. In fact, I became more committed than ever to amplifying the message of Victorious Eschatology, because I believe this truth is essential for the body of Christ today.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: made a firm resolution: I would not retreat from the truth that God had revealed. In fact, I became more committed than ever to amplifying the message of Victorious Eschatology, because I believe this truth is essential for the body of Christ today. The dispensationalist view, which has dominated much of the church world in recent times, has, in my view, caused significant damage, fostering fear, division, and an unbiblical sense of helplessness among believers. It presents a view of the end times that undermines the victorious reality that Scripture points us to. 7

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: What I’ve come to realize is that Victorious Eschatology, the belief that Christ has already secured victory over sin, death, and the powers of evil, is not a new or novel idea. In fact, it is deeply rooted in church history. The early church fathers upheld this view, and even great preachers like Charles Spurgeon maintained a sense of victory in their eschatological outlook. This message of the already-but-not-yet reign of Christ, where His victory is both certain and present, is not foreign to our Christian heritage. In writing this book, I am offering my contribution to the body of Christ, adding my voice to a growing chorus of those who are calling the church back to a biblical and victorious understanding of God’s Kingdom. My heart is to see the body of Christ living in the fullness of what has already been accomplished through the finished work of Christ, not waiting in fear and defeat, but standing in boldness, reigning with Christ now, and walking in the power of salvation. While this journey has been really personal for me, I trust that it is also part of a larger movement within the Church, one that will see believers stepping into their God-given authority, embracing the hope of the Gospel, and proclaiming the victorious reign of Jesus Christ in the world today. This book is my humble offering, a voice among many who are committed to restoring the victorious vision of God’s Kingdom. I invite you to read this with an open heart and a willingness to explore the Scriptures in a fresh light. May this message challenge you, encourage you, and ultimately transform the way you view not only the future but also the present reign of Christ in your life. Amen! Grace and Peace! Dr. Daniel Folarin 8

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: INTRODUCTION Welcome to Volume 3 of the Soteria series. If you’ve been with me through the first two volumes, Soteria: The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption and Soteria and Rest: The Story of Redemption from Eden to Eternity , you’ve already begun to explore the profound depth of God’s redemptive plan and the salvation He offers. Now, in this third volume, we’re stepping into a new and exciting chapter: Victorious Eschatology . It’s not just a theological exploration, but an invitation to live in the victory that Christ has already secured for us. I want to be transparent: over the years, I’ve come to realize that prophecy, especially when discussed in church, can often feel distant or mysterious. But it doesn’t have to be that way. In the biblical sense, prophecy is not just about predicting the future; it is the divine unveiling of God’s redemptive plan, ultimately fulfilled in Jesus Christ. In this book, I invite you to draw near to that unfolding plan and to behold Jesus, the Savior who reigns in glory. This is a call to walk boldly in the transformative power of Soteria , the great salvation of God. This isn’t just about theology; it’s a personal invitation. As believers, we are at the crossroads of promises once foretold and their ongoing fulfillment in Christ. What was once promised in the scriptures has already been accomplished through His finished work. What we now live in is not just a future hope, but a present reality that we get to experience today. In these pages, I will walk with you through the beauty of Victorious Eschatology , God’s plan for the fulfillment of salvation that transcends time, sin, and even death.

And as we do, we find that His victory over sin, death, and time is meant to empower us to live as more than conquerors. As you read, I pray that you will discover not just the theological richness of salvation, but also a roadmap for how to live it out. This book is for anyone who is ready to challenge old ways of thinking, to embrace a deeper understanding of God’s eternal purposes, and to allow the truth of God’s Word to shape their lives. For those who long to know Jesus more intimately and to live empowered by the work He has already completed, I offer you this invitation: let’s reign with boldness, walk in the power of salvation, and hold firmly to the hope that will never disappoint. 10

text: PART ONE: The Eternal Blueprint of Salvation 14

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Chapter 1 Chosen Before Time Began Introduction The narrative of salvation, as revealed in Scripture, does not emerge as a reaction to humanity’s fall into sin but as the grand expression of God’s eternal purpose, which was established before the foundations of the world. This foundational understanding is essential for a victorious eschatology, for when we perceive salvation as an eternal plan, we embrace a vision of God’s redemptive work that is not contingent on human failure but is rooted in His sovereign will and preordained love. As we explore this concept in detail, we will examine the theological depth of God’s eternal choice in Christ and how this influences not only our understanding of salvation but also our expectations of the end times. God’s Eternal Purpose in Christ (Ephesians 1:4–5) In Ephesians 1:4-5, Paul writes, "According as he hath chosen us in him before the foundation of the world, that we should be holy and without blame before him in love: having predestinated us unto the adoption of children by Jesus Christ to himself, according to the good pleasure of his will." This verse is not just a theological statement; it is the cornerstone of our understanding of salvation. Paul’s words affirm that salvation is not an afterthought of God but a plan designed in the depths of eternity. From the very beginning, before time as we know it even began, God’s intention was to choose us in Christ, to make us holy, and to adopt us as sons and daughters through Jesus. The apostle clearly states that God’s eternal purpose was executed through Christ, establishing a relationship with humanity based on adoption. 15

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: The Eternal Choice : God’s election is a profound mystery, why He chose us is not found in any merit of our own but in His divine will and purpose. This concept of divine election, as proposed by Paul, isn’t merely about individuals but speaks to God’s redemptive plan for all of creation. It highlights that salvation was designed to be a process of being "conformed to the image of His Son" (Romans 8:29). The choice made before time began was always in Christ, meaning that Jesus Christ was not only the means of salvation but also the central figure through whom this eternal plan would unfold. In this context, the doctrine of election is not about individual predestination in the narrow sense but about God’s overarching intention to call a people to Himself through Jesus Christ. It is a holistic vision, God, in His infinite wisdom, predestined a people who would become holy and blameless, achieving this not by their own effort but through Christ’s finished work. Therefore, salvation is not an event that suddenly came about in the New Testament but was always a part of God’s eternal design. Salvation Not as Reaction, but Preordained (2 Timothy 1:9) In 2 Timothy 1:9, Paul writes, "Who hath saved us, and called us with an holy calling, not according to our works, but according to his own purpose and grace, which was given us in Christ Jesus before the world began." Here, Paul underscores the proactive nature of God’s salvation plan. Salvation is not a reactive response to sin but a gracious gift given "before the world began." This preordained purpose, rooted in God’s sovereign will, transcends time and space. It is not contingent on human failure or sin but is grounded in the eternal grace of God.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_

God’s calling of the believer is not an afterthought but is anchored in the purpose He had for us before time began. The phrase "before the world began" echoes the 16

text: same truth we find in Ephesians 1:4-5, affirming that the salvation plan was not only in God’s heart but was fully realized in Christ, even before creation. The scope of salvation, therefore, is not just about saving individuals from sin, but about rescuing and restoring God’s creation to its intended purpose. Paul’s words in 2 Timothy give insight into the deep, eternal grace that was given to us in Christ Jesus. This grace is not contingent on our actions or deeds but is based on God’s eternal decision to call us into fellowship with Him. It is a calling that transcends human action, providing the believer with the confidence that God’s salvation is not based on our performance but on His perfect will and purpose. Therefore, when we understand salvation as preordained, we are better equipped to grasp the significance of the fulfillment of God’s eternal plan. Our salvation is part of a larger narrative, one that was written in eternity, long before we ever existed or sinned. Christ Crucified from the Foundation of the World (Revelation 13:8) In Revelation 13:8, John writes, "And all that dwell upon the earth shall worship him, whose names are not written in the book of life of the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world." This passage is particularly striking because it presents the Lamb of God, Jesus Christ, not as a contingency plan after the fall of humanity but as the preordained Savior whose sacrifice was established before the foundation of the world. In this verse, the "Lamb slain" points to the eternal nature of Christ’s redemptive work. It underscores the fact that God’s plan of salvation through the death of His Son was not a reaction to sin but was always God’s intention, even before creation itself.

The Cross as the Eternal Purpose : The crucifixion of Jesus, the Lamb of God, was not an event that God had to improvise in response to the fall of humanity. Instead, it was a deliberate 17

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: and preordained act that was always part of God’s eternal plan. Before sin entered the world, God had already decided that the way to redeem humanity would be through the sacrificial death of Jesus. This concept helps us see that the cross is not a divine afterthought but the fulfillment of God’s eternal purpose. The idea that Christ was “slain from the foundation of the world” also teaches us that the entire creation narrative, including the fall and redemption, was within God’s sovereign plan. The cross was never meant to be a mere reaction to sin; it was the fulfillment of the story God had been telling since before the creation of the world. This foundational truth reshapes our understanding of the end times, for the culmination of all things is not a random or unexpected event, but a final unveiling of God’s predetermined plan, which began with Christ’s sacrifice and will culminate in His glorious return. Implications for Eschatology When we consider the eternal nature of salvation and Christ’s preordained sacrifice, we are led to a deeper understanding of eschatology. Rather than viewing the end times as a fearful and uncertain event, we can approach them with confidence, knowing that God’s purpose has always been to bring about the full restoration of His creation. The victory of Christ, secured at the cross, is the foundation for our eschatological hope. This victorious eschatology is rooted in the fact that the salvation we experience in the present is a foretaste of the full consummation of God’s kingdom. The victory of Christ on the cross, which has already been achieved, will one day be fully realized when Christ returns.

The victory of Christ on the cross, which has already been achieved, will one day be fully realized when Christ returns. In this sense, our understanding of the end times is not one of defeat or waiting for something uncertain but is a forward-looking hope rooted in the certainty of God’s eternal plan. As believers, we live in the tension of the “already but not yet,” with the victory of Christ already secured but awaiting its ultimate fulfillment in the new heavens and new earth. 18

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: As we meditate on the eternal nature of God’s salvation, let us take time to reflect on the profound significance of being chosen in Christ before the foundation of the world. This truth should strengthen our gratitude and awe before our Creator. The fact that God, in His infinite wisdom, chose to redeem us through Christ before time began means that His love for us is not reactive but proactive, eternal, and unchanging. As we live in light of this eternal purpose, we are reminded that our lives are part of a divine narrative far greater than ourselves. Our salvation is not just a personal event but a cosmic reality that spans across time and eternity. It is a salvation that secures us not only for the present but for the future, giving us confidence as we await the return of the One who was slain from the foundation of the world. In light of these truths, let us live as those who have been chosen for an eternal purpose, called to participate in the unfolding of God’s kingdom, and empowered to live victoriously in the present, knowing that the end is already determined in Christ. Conclusion The doctrine of being “chosen before time began” is foundational to understanding the eternal scope of salvation. It reminds us that salvation is not merely a response to human failure but an eternal plan, enacted in Christ, that spans both time and eternity. As we move forward in exploring the victorious eschatology that flows from this truth, we can live with confidence, knowing that our salvation is anchored in the eternal will of God. Christ, the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world, has already secured the victory, and we, as His redeemed people, live in the fullness of that victory, awaiting its ultimate realization in the age to come. 19

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Chapter 2 Redemption Is Cosmic Introduction The Christian doctrine of redemption has traditionally been understood primarily in terms of the salvation of individual souls. However, a fuller and more robust understanding of redemption reveals that it encompasses not only the salvation of humanity but also the reconciliation of the entire cosmos. From the very beginning, God’s intention was not simply to save people but to restore and renew all of creation. This cosmic scope of redemption finds its center in Christ, who is not only the Savior of humanity but the one through whom the entire universe is being reconciled to God. This chapter explores the cosmic dimensions of redemption as revealed in Scripture, focusing on Christ’s work of reconciliation, the groaning of creation, and the dawning of a new creation through the resurrection. Christ Reconciling All Things (Colossians 1:20) In Colossians 1:19-20, Paul writes, “For it pleased the Father that in him should all fullness dwell; and, having made peace through the blood of his cross, by him to reconcile all things unto himself; by him, I say, whether they be things in earth, or things in heaven.” Paul’s language here is striking. The apostle is emphatically declaring that through Christ’s redeeming death on the cross, God has worked to reconcile “all things” to Himself. This “all things” is a broad and expansive term that encompasses not only humanity but all of creation. The peace achieved through the cross is not limited to human souls but extends to the entire cosmos, making Christ’s redemptive work the pivotal moment in God’s plan to restore and renew all of creation. 20

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: The Reconciliation of the Cosmos : The idea that Christ is reconciling "all things" to Himself challenges us to think beyond individual salvation to a much larger and more profound cosmic restoration. According to the apostle, the work of Christ was never meant to be solely about human beings. In fact, the redemption Christ accomplished on the cross extends beyond human salvation to include the whole of creation, as a reconciled creation is part of God’s original and ongoing plan. Renowned theologian N.T. Wright, in his work The Day the Revolution Began , argues that the cross of Christ is the focal point not only of the salvation of individual believers but also of the entire creation’s redemption. Wright emphasizes that, in Christ’s death, God’s restoration project is set in motion. This echoes the thought of Irenaeus, one of the early church fathers, who saw the incarnation of Christ as an event that touched all of creation, not just humanity. According to Irenaeus, through the incarnation, Christ united all things in Himself, making possible the cosmic reconciliation that Paul speaks of in Colossians 1:20. The work of reconciliation is not only spiritual but also physical. Christ’s death on the cross was not just a spiritual act for human souls but also an act that initiates the renewal of the material world. As theologian Richard Bauckham states in The Theology of the Book of Revelation , Christ’s redemptive work is cosmic in nature because it impacts the created order, trees, stars, mountains, rivers, and animals all stand to benefit from the reconciliation Christ secures. This broader vision of redemption compels us to see Christ not merely as the Savior of individual souls but as the ultimate restorer of creation.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_

This broader vision of redemption compels us to see Christ not merely as the Savior of individual souls but as the ultimate restorer of creation. When Christ returns, He will complete what He began on the cross, bringing about the full renewal of all things, making the world what it was always meant to be. Creation’s Groan and Glory (Romans 8:19–23) 21

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: In Romans 8:19-23, Paul reflects on the current state of creation, writing, For the earnest expectation of the creature waiteth for the manifestation of the sons of God. For the creature was made subject to vanity, not willingly, but by reason of him who hath subjected the same in hope, because the creature itself also shall be delivered from the bondage of corruption into the glorious liberty of the children of God. For we know that the whole creation groaneth and travaileth in pain together until now. And not only they, but ourselves also, which have the firstfruits of the Spirit, even we ourselves groan within ourselves, waiting for the adoption, to wit, the redemption of our body. Here, Paul poetically describes creation’s deep longing for redemption. The "creature" (or creation) is portrayed as being in a state of longing and suffering, awaiting the “manifestation of the sons of God.” Creation’s groaning is a response to its subjection to corruption and decay, a consequence of the Fall. Yet Paul also emphasizes that this groaning is not without hope, for creation looks forward to a future glory when it will be “delivered from the bondage of corruption into the glorious liberty of the children of God.” The Groaning of Creation : The groaning of creation is a metaphor for the dissonance and disorder that pervade the world because of sin. The brokenness of creation, as evidenced in natural disasters, environmental degradation, and the suffering of animals, is a testimony to the impact of humanity’s fall. Creation itself is not indifferent to sin; it suffers in tandem with humanity. However, as Paul points out, creation’s groaning is not a hopeless cry. It is a cry of expectation.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: suffering of animals, is a testimony to the impact of humanity’s fall. Creation itself is not indifferent to sin; it suffers in tandem with humanity. However, as Paul points out, creation’s groaning is not a hopeless cry. It is a cry of expectation. Creation, along with the believer, awaits the redemption that will come with the return of Christ. This is a vital component of a biblical eschatology: the renewal of creation. As Wolfhart Pannenberg writes in Systematic Theology , “the renewal of creation is 22

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: integral to God’s redemptive plan. God’s redemption is not a disembodied salvation that only affects human souls but a holistic transformation that will include the entire material world.” The Glory to Come : Just as creation groans, it also eagerly anticipates its eventual glory. The redemption of creation is not just an escape from suffering but a transformation into the "glorious liberty of the children of God." The future glory of creation is bound up with the future glory of the believer. As believers are transformed and glorified at Christ’s return, so too will creation be transformed. This is the cosmic hope of redemption. Creation and the New Creation : Romans 8:19-23 also emphasizes that this redemption will not be a vague or abstract change but a concrete transformation. Paul compares the redemption of creation to the redemption of the human body. Just as our bodies will be resurrected and glorified, so too will creation be resurrected and glorified. This speaks to the material, physical nature of God’s redemptive work. The final hope is not a disembodied existence but the redemption of both humanity and creation in their entirety. The cosmic nature of redemption implies that God’s plan for salvation extends far beyond humanity. The resurrection of Christ is the "first fruits" of a new creation, and the future resurrection of the dead, along with the ultimate renewal of the earth, is the completion of this new creation. This new creation is the fulfillment of God’s original design, where heaven and earth will be united in a new, glorious reality. The Resurrection as the Dawning of a New Creation In 1 Corinthians 15:20-22, Paul writes, “But now is Christ risen from the dead, and become the firstfruits of them that slept.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: design, where heaven and earth will be united in a new, glorious reality. The Resurrection as the Dawning of a New Creation In 1 Corinthians 15:20-22, Paul writes, “But now is Christ risen from the dead, and become the firstfruits of them that slept. For since by man came death, by man came also the resurrection of the dead. For as in Adam all die, even so in Christ shall all be made alive.” 23

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: The resurrection of Jesus Christ is the foundational event in the history of redemption. It marks the moment when God began the work of restoring and renewing all things. As the "firstfruits" of those who will be resurrected, Christ’s resurrection is a promise of the future resurrection of believers and the renewal of creation itself. Christ’s resurrection is not just an isolated event for the salvation of humanity but a cosmic event that signals the breaking in of a new age, the dawning of a new creation. The Firstfruits of the New Creation : Christ’s resurrection is described as the "firstfruits" of the new creation. In agricultural terms, firstfruits are the initial harvest that signals the full harvest to come. Similarly, Christ’s resurrection is the firstfruit of the renewal of all things. It is a demonstration of what is to come when the entire cosmos will be made new. This new creation will not only include the physical resurrection of believers but also the physical renewal of the created order. The resurrection is the turning point in history where God’s plan to restore creation begins in earnest. Practical Implications for the Christian Life : For the Christian, this understanding of redemption as cosmic and creation-wide is transformative. It changes how we view our relationship with the environment, with our bodies, and with the world around us. If redemption is cosmic, then our actions in the world have eternal significance. Caring for creation, loving our neighbors, and living justly are all part of participating in God’s redemptive work in the world. Additionally, the resurrection of Jesus gives the believer hope. Just as Christ was raised from the dead, we too will be raised, and creation will be renewed.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: ors, and living justly are all part of participating in God’s redemptive work in the world. Additionally, the resurrection of Jesus gives the believer hope. Just as Christ was raised from the dead, we too will be raised, and creation will be renewed. This hope enables us to live with endurance and perseverance, knowing that suffering and decay will not have the final word. The resurrection reminds us that the present world, though marred by sin and suffering, is not the end of the story. A new world is coming, and we are already participants in that world through our union with Christ. 24

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Conclusion The redemption that Christ secured is cosmic in scope, encompassing not only the salvation of individuals but also the reconciliation of all things to God. The cosmic redemption described in Scripture is not a vague theological concept but a practical reality that affects the way we live in the world. Christ’s death, resurrection, and the anticipated renewal of all things offer hope and direction to every believer, empowering us to live in light of the coming new creation. In the end, redemption is not just about escaping this world but about the full restoration of creation, heaven and earth united, all things made new, and God glorified in the fullness of His redemptive plan. 25

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Chapter 3 Israel, Covenant, and the Story of Soteria Introduction The story of salvation, as revealed in Scripture, is not a disjointed collection of isolated events but a unified narrative that unfolds over time, culminating in the person and work of Jesus Christ. This grand narrative is grounded in the covenantal relationship between God and Israel. Understanding the law, the prophets, and Israel’s role in God’s redemptive plan is essential for grasping the full scope of salvation and how it reaches its fulfillment in Christ. This chapter explores how Israel, the covenant, and the promises of God all point to Christ, who is the fulfillment of every promise and the ultimate answer to God’s redemptive plan for humanity and creation. Through this lens, we also understand the integral connection between the Old Testament and the New Testament, as well as how the story of salvation is not merely a personal salvation narrative but a story with a cosmic and historical scope. The Law and the Prophets Pointing to Christ In Luke 24:44, after His resurrection, Jesus told His disciples: “These are the words which I spake unto you, while I was yet with you, that all things must be fulfilled, which were written in the law of Moses, and in the prophets, and in the psalms, concerning me.” Here, Jesus explicitly connects the entirety of the Old Testament to Himself, showing that the law and the prophets are not stand-alone elements but are part of a larger story pointing forward to the Messiah. The Role of the Law : The law, especially as outlined in the Torah (the first five books of the Bible), was given by God to Israel not only to guide them in their relationship with God but also to serve as a foreshadowing of the coming Messiah.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: siah. The Role of the Law : The law, especially as outlined in the Torah (the first five books of the Bible), was given by God to Israel not only to guide them in their relationship with God but also to serve as a foreshadowing of the coming Messiah. The sacrificial system, the laws of purification, and the moral 26

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: commandments were not mere historical or cultural artifacts; they were designed by God to point to the ultimate work of salvation that would be accomplished in Christ. The law was never intended as an end in itself but as a temporary and preparatory step toward the greater revelation of Christ. The sacrificial system in particular has strong Christological significance. In Leviticus, the law requires the offering of animals to atone for sin, but Hebrews 10:4 makes it clear that "it is not possible that the blood of bulls and of goats should take away sins." These sacrifices were symbolic, pointing to the ultimate sacrifice of Christ on the cross. Jesus Himself, as the Lamb of God, fulfills this sacrificial system. As N.T. Wright suggests, “the entire Jewish law, with its ceremonies and rituals, points beyond itself to the arrival of a new covenant, a new means of reconciliation with God that would be brought about through Jesus Christ.” The Prophets’ Foretelling of Christ : In addition to the law, the prophets played a crucial role in revealing God’s redemptive plan. From the earliest prophecies of Genesis 3:15, where God promises that the seed of the woman will crush the head of the serpent, to the messianic prophecies in Isaiah, Jeremiah, and Ezekiel, the Old Testament prophets consistently pointed to a coming Savior. The promise of a coming "Messiah" who would redeem Israel and the nations is a theme woven throughout the prophetic books. Lynn Hiles, in his teachings, underscores the centrality of these prophecies in the unfolding drama of redemption. For example, in Isaiah 9:6-7, the prophet speaks of a child who will be born to us, a son who will be given to us, and whose government will be upon His shoulders.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: , underscores the centrality of these prophecies in the unfolding drama of redemption. For example, in Isaiah 9:6-7, the prophet speaks of a child who will be born to us, a son who will be given to us, and whose government will be upon His shoulders. This prophecy is directly fulfilled in the person of Jesus Christ. In this way, the prophetic writings serve as a divine road map, guiding God’s people toward the ultimate revelation of salvation in Christ. Furthermore, the prophet Jeremiah speaks of a new covenant that God will make with His people (Jeremiah 31:31-34), a covenant that will be written 27

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: on their hearts rather than on tablets of stone. This prophecy is fulfilled in the institution of the Lord’s Supper in the New Testament, where Jesus declares that the cup represents His blood, "which is shed for many for the remission of sins" (Matthew 26:28). This is the new covenant promised by God, and Jesus is the fulfillment of that promise. Israel’s Place in Redemptive History Israel’s unique role in God’s redemptive history cannot be overstated. It was through Israel that God revealed His character, His laws, and His promises. From the call of Abraham in Genesis 12, Israel was set apart by God as a people through whom He would bring blessing to the nations. Israel’s covenant relationship with God was a central theme in the Old Testament, and this covenant was not simply for Israel’s benefit but was meant to be a blessing to the world. As God promised Abraham, “In you, all the families of the earth shall be blessed” (Genesis 12:3). However, Israel’s relationship with God was not without difficulty. Time and again, Israel faltered in their faithfulness to God, resulting in judgment and exile. But even in their failure, God remained faithful to His covenant. Israel’s history is one of anticipation and longing for the fulfillment of God’s promises, and it is in this context that Christ’s coming must be understood. Jesus is the fulfillment of everything Israel was meant to be and do. Israel as a Type of Christ : Israel itself, in many ways, serves as a type or foreshadowing of Christ. Consider, for example, how Israel went down into Egypt and came out as God’s redeemed people, much as Jesus would go down into Egypt as a child and later come out to fulfill His mission.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: elf, in many ways, serves as a type or foreshadowing of Christ. Consider, for example, how Israel went down into Egypt and came out as God’s redeemed people, much as Jesus would go down into Egypt as a child and later come out to fulfill His mission. Israel’s history of suffering, exile, and restoration mirrors the life of Jesus, who would suffer, die, and be resurrected to bring restoration to all of creation. 28

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: This typological connection is not merely an academic exercise but is deeply spiritual. It helps believers see how God’s plan of salvation unfolded in real time and history, and how the events of the Old Testament point to their fulfillment in Christ. As Wright emphasizes, “Jesus was the true Israel, the one who perfectly embodied the purposes of God for Israel and, by extension, for the world. Israel’s story is the backdrop against which Christ’s life and mission are cast.” Israel’s Failure and Christ’s Success : One of the significant aspects of Israel’s history is its repeated failure to live up to its covenantal responsibilities. Israel’s failure culminates in the exile, the destruction of Jerusalem, and the suffering of the people. But in Christ, we see the fulfillment of the covenant promises. He is the true Israelite, living perfectly in accordance with God’s will. Where Israel failed, Jesus succeeded. In this way, Christ is not merely the fulfillment of Israel’s promises; He is the one who, by His perfect obedience, accomplishes what Israel was unable to do. In Romans 11, Paul discusses Israel’s rejection of Christ and how this rejection has opened the door for the Gentiles to be grafted into the promises of God. However, he also assures the reader that God has not cast away His people forever, and there is a future for Israel in God’s redemptive plan. This underscores the continuing significance of Israel in the story of salvation.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: into the promises of God. However, he also assures the reader that God has not cast away His people forever, and there is a future for Israel in God’s redemptive plan. This underscores the continuing significance of Israel in the story of salvation. Christ as the Fulfillment of Every Promise (2 Corinthians 1:20) Paul writes in 2 Corinthians 1:20, “For all the promises of God in him are yea, and in him Amen, unto the glory of God by us.” This powerful statement encapsulates the truth that every promise God made, from the earliest covenant with Abraham to the prophetic promises of a coming Messiah, finds its “Yes” and “Amen” in Jesus Christ. 29

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Christ as the Fulfillment of Old Testament Promises : In this verse, Paul is affirming that Christ is the fulfillment of the entire narrative of Scripture. The promises made to Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Moses, David, and the prophets are all ultimately fulfilled in the person of Jesus. This includes promises of a land, a people, and a king. As Eberle suggests in his writings, “the entirety of Scripture points to Christ, and He is the answer to every promise God made to Israel and to the world.” The promises of God in the Old Testament were not given as isolated predictions but as part of a larger covenantal framework that pointed to Christ’s coming. Jesus’ life, death, and resurrection are the realization of these promises. Therefore, understanding Christ as the fulfillment of all promises shifts the focus from a future expectation of fulfillment to a present reality. The promises are not just awaiting their future fulfillment in some distant eschaton; they are already fulfilled in Christ. Practical Implications for the Christian Life For the Christian, understanding the role of Israel in redemptive history and Christ as the fulfillment of God’s promises brings several practical implications. First, it gives us a deeper understanding of Scripture. As we read the Old Testament, we no longer see it as an isolated book of history and law, but as the foundation of God’s redemptive plan that reaches its fulfillment in Christ. This enhances our appreciation of the continuity of Scripture and the unity of God’s redemptive work across the ages. Second, understanding the cosmic scope of redemption reminds us that salvation is not just about individual souls but about the restoration of all creation.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: iation of the continuity of Scripture and the unity of God’s redemptive work across the ages. Second, understanding the cosmic scope of redemption reminds us that salvation is not just about individual souls but about the restoration of all creation. The work of Christ was not just to save individual people but to fulfill God’s promises to Israel and to bring healing and restoration to the entire created order. This shapes our worldview and how we engage with the world. We are called to participate in God’s work of redemption not just in our personal lives but also in our engagement with the world around us. 30

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Lastly, Christ as the fulfillment of every promise gives us confidence in God’s faithfulness. God has always kept His promises, and He will continue to do so. The story of Israel’s failure and Christ’s success assures us that, even when we falter, God remains faithful to His covenant. In Christ, we have the ultimate "Yes" and "Amen" to all of God’s promises. Conclusion The story of salvation, as revealed in the law, the prophets, and the history of Israel, finds its fulfillment in the person of Jesus Christ. He is the fulfillment of every promise made to Israel and the world. Israel’s role in redemptive history, while unique, ultimately points to Christ as the fulfillment of God’s eternal plan of salvation. By understanding Christ as the fulfillment of the law, the prophets, and the covenant, we gain a deeper understanding of the story of redemption and how it shapes our lives today. As we look back to Israel’s story and forward to Christ’s return, we are reminded that the promises of God are sure and that in Christ, all things are made new. 31

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_

text: Chapter 4 What Really Ended? What Truly Began? Introduction The question of what "ended" and what "began" with the arrival of Jesus Christ is a profound and pivotal one in understanding the Christian faith and the transformative work of the gospel. In the New Testament, the coming of Christ is presented not merely as the start of a new chapter but as the climax of God’s redemptive plan for humanity, one that affects the very structure of salvation history. This chapter seeks to explore the implications of the Old Covenant’s dissolution and the establishment of a New Covenant based on grace and eternal life. We will examine the pivotal role of Jesus Christ as the hinge of history, the transition from the Old Covenant to the New, and what it means for us today as believers in Christ. The Old Covenant Dissolved (Hebrews 8:13) The Letter to the Hebrews presents a clear and profound theological insight into the transition from the Old Covenant to the New. In Hebrews 8:13, the author writes, “In that he saith, A new covenant, he hath made the first old. Now that which decayeth and waxeth old is ready to vanish away.” This statement marks a decisive break from the Old Covenant, which was based on the law, sacrifices, and rituals that governed Israel’s relationship with God. The Old Covenant was never intended to be the final and ultimate solution to the problem of sin.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: statement marks a decisive break from the Old Covenant, which was based on the law, sacrifices, and rituals that governed Israel’s relationship with God. The Old Covenant was never intended to be the final and ultimate solution to the problem of sin. It was always temporary, a shadow pointing forward to the coming of Christ, as Paul writes in Colossians 2:17, “which are a shadow of things to come; but the body is of Christ.” The Old Covenant, with its rituals, sacrifices, and priesthood, was necessary for a time, but it was pointing to a greater reality - Jesus Christ, who would establish a New Covenant based on grace, mercy, and a permanent relationship with God. 33

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: N.T. Wright, in his scholarly work on the New Testament, often emphasizes that the Old Covenant served a specific purpose in God’s redemptive plan. It was not a failure but a means to prepare Israel and the world for the revelation of God’s true salvation in Christ. The sacrifices of the Old Covenant were insufficient to remove sin permanently, but they pointed to the ultimate sacrifice of Christ on the cross. In the Old Covenant, God’s people were required to continually offer sacrifices, but the New Covenant, inaugurated by Jesus’ sacrificial death, is once and for all. As Hebrews 9:12 declares, “Neither by the blood of goats and calves, but by his own blood he entered in once into the holy place, having obtained eternal redemption for us.” The Old Covenant is dissolved in the sense that its ceremonial laws, its sacrificial system, and its priesthood are no longer required. The righteous requirements of the law are fulfilled in Christ, and believers are now under the grace of the New Covenant. This does not mean the Old Testament is irrelevant; rather, it means that the Old Covenant has been fulfilled and superseded by the New. As Harold Eberle points out, “The Old Covenant was a means to an end, and that end has arrived in Christ.” A New Covenant of Grace and Eternal Life (Hebrews 13:20) In stark contrast to the Old Covenant, which was based on law, rituals, and sacrifices, the New Covenant is a covenant of grace and eternal life.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: to an end, and that end has arrived in Christ.” A New Covenant of Grace and Eternal Life (Hebrews 13:20) In stark contrast to the Old Covenant, which was based on law, rituals, and sacrifices, the New Covenant is a covenant of grace and eternal life. Hebrews 13:20-21 reveals the heart of this new covenantal relationship: “Now the God of peace, that brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus, that great shepherd of the sheep, through the blood of the everlasting covenant, make you perfect in every good work to do his will, working in you that which is wellpleasing in his sight, through Jesus Christ; to whom be glory forever and ever. Amen.” The New Covenant, as initiated by Christ, is rooted in His sacrificial death and His resurrection. The promise of the New Covenant is the guarantee of 34

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: eternal life through faith in Jesus Christ. It is a covenant of grace, meaning it is not based on human effort or merit, but on the unmerited favor of God through the work of Jesus Christ. Unlike the Old Covenant, which required continuous sacrifices and ritualistic observances, the New Covenant provides a permanent solution for sin and guarantees access to the presence of God. The New Covenant is not merely a new set of rules or a reformation of the Old Covenant, but a completely new way of relating to God. It is based on Jesus’ work, not our own, and is freely given to all who believe. The New Covenant offers something far beyond what the Old Covenant could provide, it offers a living, active relationship with God that is mediated by Jesus, the great High Priest, and is marked by the indwelling presence of the Holy Spirit. Paul emphasizes in Romans 8:1-2 that in the New Covenant, “there is therefore now no condemnation to them which are in Christ Jesus, who walk not after the flesh, but after the Spirit. For the law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus hath made me free from the law of sin and death.” This freedom is a key aspect of the New Covenant, freedom from the condemnation of sin, freedom to live in the Spirit, and the freedom to walk in the righteousness of Christ. For the Christian, this means that the New Covenant brings not only forgiveness but also the power of God to live according to His will. The grace of God, as revealed in Christ, empowers believers to do what is pleasing in God’s sight. The New Covenant is a covenant of eternal life, a life that begins in the present through the indwelling presence of the Holy Spirit and finds its ultimate fulfillment in the resurrection of the dead at the return of Christ.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: pleasing in God’s sight. The New Covenant is a covenant of eternal life, a life that begins in the present through the indwelling presence of the Holy Spirit and finds its ultimate fulfillment in the resurrection of the dead at the return of Christ. The New Covenant is centered on Jesus, who is the mediator of this new relationship between God and humanity. This covenant is not merely an abstract theological concept but a concrete reality that shapes the believer’s 35

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: life and experience. In Christ, believers are reconciled to God, made partakers of His divine nature, and called to live out the values of the kingdom of God in the world. Jesus as the Pivot of the Ages (Galatians 4:4-5) In Galatians 4:4-5, Paul writes, “But when the fullness of the time was come, God sent forth his Son, made of a woman, made under the law, to redeem them that were under the law, that we might receive the adoption of sons.” Here, Paul underscores the pivotal role of Jesus Christ in the history of salvation. The coming of Jesus marks the “fullness of time,” the culmination of God’s plan for redemption that was set in motion from the very beginning of creation. Jesus is not simply a teacher or a prophet; He is the fulfillment of Israel’s hope and the pivotal moment in the history of the world. Jesus’ life, death, and resurrection mark the turning point in the unfolding drama of salvation. Wright writes that “Jesus is not just a figure in the story; he is the one who changes the whole course of the story.” In Christ, the old age, marked by the law and the shadow of death, comes to an end, and the new age of grace, life, and reconciliation with God begins. Jesus is the pivot of the ages because His coming brings the fulfillment of God’s promises, the end of the Old Covenant, and the inauguration of the New Covenant. This is not just a shift in the timeline of history; it is the breaking in of God’s kingdom on earth. The death and resurrection of Jesus are the decisive moments that bring about the new creation and the ultimate restoration of all things. In practical terms, this means that the believer’s life is now oriented around the reality of Christ’s finished work.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: ath and resurrection of Jesus are the decisive moments that bring about the new creation and the ultimate restoration of all things. In practical terms, this means that the believer’s life is now oriented around the reality of Christ’s finished work. We no longer live according to the principles of the Old Covenant, which were bound by law and sacrifice. Instead, we live by the principles of the New Covenant, which are based on grace, faith, and the transformative power of the Holy Spirit. This pivot, 36

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: marked by the coming of Christ, calls Christians to live in the present with the hope of the future and the power to overcome sin in the present. What Really Ended? What truly ended with the coming of Christ was not merely a historical period, but the system of religious observances and rituals that were a means of dealing with sin under the Old Covenant. The sacrificial system, the priesthood, the temple worship, all these things were ended because Christ, the ultimate sacrifice, the perfect High Priest, and the true Temple, had come to fulfill what these types and shadows represented. As Hebrews 10:9-10 says, “Then said he, Lo, I come to do thy will, O God. He taketh away the first, that he may establish the second. By the which will we are sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all.” The Old Covenant, with all its laws and rituals, was abolished because it was never meant to be permanent. Its purpose was to prepare for the coming of Christ, the One who would do what the law could not do, deal with sin once and for all. As Paul writes in Galatians 3:24, “Wherefore the law was our schoolmaster to bring us unto Christ, that we might be justified by faith.” What Truly Began? What truly began with the coming of Christ was a new way of relating to God, based on grace, forgiveness, and the indwelling presence of the Holy Spirit. The New Covenant, inaugurated by Jesus, is a covenant of eternal life, secured by the finished work of Christ on the cross. In this new covenant, believers are reconciled to God, receive the Holy Spirit, and are empowered to live according to God’s will. It is a covenant that reaches beyond Israel to include the Gentiles, offering salvation to all who believe. 37

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Conclusion The arrival of Jesus Christ marked the definitive end of the Old Covenant and the beginning of the New Covenant. Through His life, death, and resurrection, Jesus brought about the fulfillment of God’s redemptive plan, providing a new means of salvation for humanity. As believers, we are called to live in the reality of the New Covenant, a covenant of grace, mercy, and eternal life. The coming of Christ is not only the pivot of salvation history; it is the pivot of our lives, transforming how we relate to God and to one another. In Christ, the Old has passed away, and the New has come. We are called to live in the light of this new reality, empowered by the Holy Spirit to walk in obedience and to fulfill the purposes of God in the world. 38

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Chapter 5 Rethinking the Olivet Discourse Introduction The Olivet Discourse, found in Matthew 24, Mark 13, and Luke 21, has long been a focal point for eschatological debates within the Christian tradition. This passage, where Jesus discusses the signs of the end of the age, has often been interpreted as predicting a distant, cataclysmic end of the world. However, when read carefully and in context, we can see that Jesus’ words were not solely directed toward the ultimate end of the world, but rather the impending judgment upon Israel and the destruction of the Jerusalem Temple in AD 70. This chapter seeks to reframe the Olivet Discourse, understanding its message as primarily concerned with the fall of Jerusalem and the end of temple Judaism, which marked the conclusion of an era in God’s redemptive history. We will explore how a proper contextual reading of Matthew 24 and Luke 21 reveals the fulfillment of Jesus’ prophecies in the first century and what this means for our understanding of biblical prophecy and the end times. Reading Matthew 24 and Luke 21 in Context The key to understanding the Olivet Discourse lies in reading the text within its immediate historical and cultural context. Jesus was not speaking abstractly about the end of the physical world but was responding to questions from His disciples about the future of Israel, the temple, and the kingdom of God. The questions asked by the disciples in Matthew 24:3 and Luke 21:7 reveal that they were concerned with the destruction of the temple and the signs of the coming of the Messiah and the establishment of His kingdom.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: the temple, and the kingdom of God. The questions asked by the disciples in Matthew 24:3 and Luke 21:7 reveal that they were concerned with the destruction of the temple and the signs of the coming of the Messiah and the establishment of His kingdom. Matthew 24:3 says, “As he sat upon the mount of Olives, the disciples came unto him privately, saying, Tell us, when shall these things be? and what shall be the sign of thy coming, and of the end of the world?” In this 39

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: context, “these things” refer to Jesus’ prediction in Matthew 23:38 that the temple would be left desolate. The disciples were asking for a sign that would indicate when these events would take place and when the Kingdom of God would be fully revealed. It is crucial to note that when the disciples asked about the end of the world, the phrase “end of the world” (Greek aion ) is more accurately understood as “end of the age” or the conclusion of the current era of Israel’s covenant relationship with God, symbolized by the temple system. N.T. Wright argues that the “end of the age” is not the end of the physical world, but the end of an old era, the end of the temple-based sacrificial system and the establishment of a new era in Christ. Wright writes, “Jesus was not concerned with the end of the world; He was speaking about the end of the age - the end of the old covenant world, which was now passing away.” In Luke 21, Jesus gives a similar discourse, describing the signs that would precede the fall of Jerusalem. The events He outlines, such as wars, earthquakes, and famines, are often interpreted as signs of the end of the world, but in context, they were describing the turmoil leading up to the destruction of Jerusalem in AD 70. Jesus’ warnings of persecution and betrayal, found in Luke 21:12-19, were directed to the first-century church, preparing them for the tribulations they would face before the fall of the temple. The disciples’ question in both Matthew 24 and Luke 21 is specific to their immediate context, asking about the end of the temple and the coming of the Messiah’s Kingdom. To apply these passages exclusively to a distant end-of-the-world scenario distorts their meaning and ignores the historical and cultural realities of the first century. 40

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: “This Generation” and the Fall of Jerusalem (AD 70) One of the most debated phrases in the Olivet Discourse is “this generation”. Jesus says in Matthew 24:34, “Verily I say unto you, This generation shall not pass, till all these things be fulfilled.” The common interpretation of this phrase has often been that Jesus is referring to the generation alive at the time of His second coming, but such an interpretation fails to account for the immediate context of Jesus’ discourse and the historical events that followed His death. The phrase “this generation” (Greek genea ) refers to the people living in Jesus’ day, the generation that would witness the destruction of the temple and the fulfillment of the events He described. N.T. Wright contends that “Jesus was speaking to the generation that would live through the destruction of the temple in AD 70, the generation that would see the end of Israel’s temple-based way of life and the coming of the new age inaugurated by His death and resurrection.” Wright further emphasizes that Jesus’ prophecy was not about a far-off future but about events that would unfold within the lifetime of those listening to Him. The fall of Jerusalem in AD 70, when the Roman army, led by Titus 1 , destroyed the city and the temple, is one of the most significant events in Jewish and Christian history. This event marked the end of temple Judaism and the destruction of the central symbol of Israel’s covenant with God. Jesus’ prophecy of the temple’s destruction in Matthew 24:2 was fulfilled within a generation, as He had predicted. The historical reality of this event confirms the accuracy of Jesus’ words and the near fulfillment of His prophetic discourse.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_

The historical reality of this event confirms the accuracy of Jesus’ words and the near fulfillment of His prophetic discourse. 1 Titus, the son of Emperor Vespasian, led the Roman legions during the siege and eventual destruction of Jerusalem in AD 70. This event marked the climax of the First Jewish–Roman War (AD 66–73). Josephus, a Jewish historian and eyewitness to the events, provides a detailed account in The Jewish War , particularly noting Titus's role in both military leadership and diplomatic efforts to avoid destruction before ultimately breaching the city walls and destroying the Second Temple. See Flavius Josephus, The Jewish War , trans. G.A. Williamson (London: Penguin Books, 1981), Books V–VI. 41
