text: Harold Eberle, in his theological writings on the end times, affirms this interpretation, noting that “Jesus’ predictions of the fall of Jerusalem and the temple were fulfilled with precise accuracy within the generation of those who heard Him speak. The destruction of the temple in AD 70 was the fulfillment of the prophecy that Jesus gave.” The fall of Jerusalem was a judgment on Israel for rejecting their Messiah and a turning point in God’s redemptive plan. The End of Temple Judaism, Not the World It is essential to understand that the Olivet Discourse is not about the end of the physical world, but the end of the temple system and the old covenant age. The temple in Jerusalem was the focal point of Israel’s religious and social life. Its destruction in AD 70 symbolized the end of the sacrificial system, which had been a shadow of the ultimate sacrifice of Jesus Christ. The end of temple Judaism marked a profound shift in God’s redemptive purposes, as the old covenant, with its rituals and sacrifices, was superseded by the New Covenant in Christ. In Matthew 24:2, Jesus says, “See ye not all these things? verily I say unto you, There shall not be left here one stone upon another, that shall not be thrown down.” This prophecy was fulfilled when the Romans destroyed the temple 2 , leaving no trace of its grandeur. The physical temple, which had been the center of Jewish worship and identity, was dismantled, and with it, the old covenant system that had governed Israel’s relationship with God. 2 The temple destroyed in AD 70 was not Solomon’s original temple, which had been destroyed by the Babylonians in 586 BC.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: ish worship and identity, was dismantled, and with it, the old covenant system that had governed Israel’s relationship with God. 2 The temple destroyed in AD 70 was not Solomon’s original temple, which had been destroyed by the Babylonians in 586 BC. Rather, it was the Second Temple, initially rebuilt by Jewish exiles under Zerubbabel around 516 BC, and later extensively renovated and expanded by Herod the Great beginning in 20–19 BC. Though not a new temple, Herod’s reconstruction was so grand that it became known as Herod’s Temple . It was this version that stood during Jesus’ earthly ministry and was ultimately destroyed by the Roman army under Titus. 42

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Lynn Hiles notes that “the destruction of the temple in AD 70 was not just the collapse of a building but the end of an entire system. It was the end of temple Judaism and the old covenant age. Jesus, in His death and resurrection, brought an end to the need for sacrifices and rituals, and in doing so, He ushered in the age of grace and the indwelling of the Holy Spirit.” The destruction of the temple symbolizes the end of the old way of relating to God and the beginning of the new way, inaugurated by Christ. Jesus’ death on the cross tore the veil of the temple (Matthew 27:51), signifying that the barrier between God and humanity had been removed. No longer would God dwell in a physical temple; now, His presence would dwell in the hearts of believers (1 Corinthians 3:16). The destruction of the temple in AD 70 marked the end of an era, but it also pointed forward to the establishment of a new temple, the body of Christ, the church (Ephesians 2:19-22). What Does This Mean for Us Today? For the modern Christian, understanding the Olivet Discourse in its historical context has significant implications. First, it reminds us that Jesus’ words were not just predictive of distant future events but were also focused on the fulfillment of God’s promises in the first century. The fall of Jerusalem and the destruction of the temple were central to the establishment of the New Covenant and the proclamation of the gospel to the world. Second, the message of the Olivet Discourse calls us to examine the present reality of the Kingdom of God. While the physical temple no longer stands, the presence of God is alive and active in the world through the Church, the body of Christ.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: world. Second, the message of the Olivet Discourse calls us to examine the present reality of the Kingdom of God. While the physical temple no longer stands, the presence of God is alive and active in the world through the Church, the body of Christ. Christians today are called to live as members of this new temple, empowered by the Holy Spirit to carry out God’s redemptive mission in the world. 43

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Third, the destruction of the temple in AD 70 serves as a reminder that God’s judgment is real and that we must be faithful in our witness to Christ. Just as Israel faced judgment for rejecting their Messiah, so too must the world today reckon with the reality of Christ’s second coming and the fulfillment of God’s eternal purposes. Finally, the Olivet Discourse encourages us to live with a sense of urgency. The coming of the Kingdom of God was inaugurated in Christ, and we are called to be participants in that Kingdom, sharing the message of salvation and living as representatives of Christ’s rule on earth. We live in a time when the final fulfillment of God’s redemptive plan is still to come, but we do so with the assurance that Christ has already secured the victory. Conclusion The Olivet Discourse, when properly understood in its historical context, provides us with a profound insight into the transition from the old covenant to the new, marked by the destruction of the temple and the establishment of the Kingdom of God in Christ. Jesus’ words were not a prediction of the end of the world but the end of an era, an era that centered on the temple and the old covenant system. The destruction of the temple in AD 70 marked the fulfillment of Jesus’ prophecy and the inauguration of a new era in salvation history. As believers, we are called to live in the light of this new reality, understanding that the Kingdom of God is now present in Christ and that we are part of the ongoing mission to bring the good news of the gospel to the world. 44

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Chapter 6 Revelation Revisited - Triumph, Not Terror Introduction The Book of Revelation has long been a subject of intense fascination, speculation, and, at times, fear within the Christian community. Throughout history, various interpretations of this prophetic work have presented it as a cryptic and terrifying vision of the end of the world, filled with apocalyptic imagery of wars, plagues, and divine wrath. This interpretation often casts the believer in a state of anxious anticipation, awaiting the fulfillment of terrible events. However, such an understanding neglects the foundational message of the book: the triumph of Christ, the victory of the Lamb, and the ultimate hope of the Church. When read properly, Revelation is not primarily about terror but about the triumph of God’s kingdom over the forces of evil, the vindication of the righteous, and the establishment of the New Heaven and New Earth. In this chapter, we will explore Revelation’s time indicators, its use of symbolism, and its central theme of victory through the Lamb. We will also reflect on how these truths can deeply impact our lives today, removing any unnecessary fear while strengthening our hope and confidence in God’s eternal plan. Time Indicators: “Shortly,” “At Hand” (Rev. 1:1; 22:6) One of the first critical points in interpreting the Book of Revelation is understanding its time indicators. Throughout Revelation, words such as “shortly” (Greek tachos ) and “at hand” (Greek engys ) are used to describe the fulfillment of the visions. In Revelation 1:1, it says, “The revelation of Jesus Christ, which God gave him to show his servants the things that must soon take place.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: as “shortly” (Greek tachos ) and “at hand” (Greek engys ) are used to describe the fulfillment of the visions. In Revelation 1:1, it says, “The revelation of Jesus Christ, which God gave him to show his servants the things that must soon take place. He made it known by sending his angel to his servant John.” Similarly, Revelation 22:6 states, “And he said to me, ’These words are trustworthy and true. And the Lord, the God of the spirits of the 45

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: prophets, has sent his angel to show his servants what must soon take place.’” The consistent use of these terms in the opening and closing chapters of the book strongly indicates that the events being revealed were imminent and would take place shortly. This is significant because many interpreters have mistakenly assumed that Revelation’s predictions concern distant future events. For example, when John writes that these things will “shortly take place” (Rev. 1:1), or when Christ Himself declares that these things will happen “at hand” (Rev. 22:6), the most natural reading is that John expected the fulfillment of these prophecies to occur within the first century. N.T. Wright comments, “The language of ’at hand’ is not language of a distant future, but of an expectation of immediate fulfillment. The vision is of something that is happening now, even if its full realization may take some time to unfold.” To read Revelation as if it speaks solely to events that will unfold millennia after its writing distorts its original context and message. The early Church, to whom the Book of Revelation was primarily addressed, was living in anticipation of these events coming to pass within their own lifetimes. Jesus had already established the Kingdom of God, and the Book of Revelation was meant to encourage Christians who were suffering persecution, reminding them that their trials were temporary and that ultimate victory belonged to Christ. Harold Eberle highlights that “Revelation was written to encourage believers in the first century, not to frighten future generations.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: uffering persecution, reminding them that their trials were temporary and that ultimate victory belonged to Christ. Harold Eberle highlights that “Revelation was written to encourage believers in the first century, not to frighten future generations. Its primary purpose was to show that, despite present tribulation, Christ reigns, and the final victory is assured for those who overcome.” Revelation’s time indicators suggest an immediate expectation of these events in the early church’s context, not a vague, distant future that believers today should fear. 46

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Symbolism, Not Literalism A major obstacle to properly understanding Revelation is the tendency to read its vivid imagery in a strictly literal sense. Revelation is full of highly symbolic language, and interpreting it literally often leads to confusion and misinterpretation. The genre of Revelation is apocalyptic, which is rich in symbolic expression, drawing heavily from Old Testament imagery and Jewish traditions of prophecy. John, the author of Revelation, was not seeking to provide a precise blueprint for the future but rather to communicate a spiritual truth through powerful, symbolic visions. For example, the “seven seals” (Rev. 6), “seven trumpets” (Rev. 8–9), and “seven bowls of wrath” (Rev. 16) are not to be understood as literal events that will unfold in a sequential manner in the future. Instead, these symbols represent the ongoing spiritual battle between the forces of good and evil, the judgment of sin, and the ultimate victory of Christ. N.T. Wright emphasizes that “Revelation uses vivid and dramatic imagery to convey spiritual truths, not to provide a linear prediction of future history. To try to force Revelation into a literalistic interpretation is to miss the point of the book, which is to encourage and strengthen believers in the midst of their struggles.” John’s vision of the four horsemen of the apocalypse (Rev. 6:1–8) is another example of the symbolic nature of the book. Rather than referring to four literal horsemen that will appear in the future, these horsemen symbolize various aspects of human suffering and the destructive forces at work in the world, war, famine, death, and pestilence.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: mbolic nature of the book. Rather than referring to four literal horsemen that will appear in the future, these horsemen symbolize various aspects of human suffering and the destructive forces at work in the world, war, famine, death, and pestilence. These are not future events to be feared but ongoing realities of the human condition, experienced throughout history and particularly in times of persecution. Lynn Hiles notes that “the symbolism in Revelation serves as a way of communicating the realities of the spiritual realm, which often cannot be captured by literal descriptions. To reduce Revelation to mere literalism is to rob it of its deeper, spiritual meaning.” The use of symbols such as the 47

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: “beast” (Rev. 13), the “dragon” (Rev. 12), and the “new Jerusalem” (Rev. 21) are all meant to communicate profound truths about the nature of evil, the triumph of Christ, and the ultimate restoration of God’s people. These symbols point to realities that transcend the physical world and call believers to understand the cosmic dimensions of Christ’s victory. The book of Revelation is not a prophetic timeline to be decoded but a spiritual work meant to inspire hope and faith in believers. The symbols used throughout the text remind us that God’s redemptive work is not confined to earthly, temporal events but is a cosmic, eternal reality that cannot be fully captured in human language. Revelation speaks to the victory of Christ, not through literal events, but through the symbolic portrayal of spiritual truths. The Victorious Lamb and the Overcoming Church At the heart of Revelation is the central figure of the Lamb , Jesus Christ, who was slain but is now alive and victorious. The Lamb represents Christ’s sacrifice, His triumph over sin, and His victory over death. Revelation’s message is clear: Christ has already secured the victory, and the Church is called to live in the light of that victory, knowing that they will ultimately overcome, just as Christ overcame. In Revelation 5, John sees a vision of a “Lamb standing, as though it had been slain” (Rev. 5:6), who is worthy to open the scroll and reveal God’s ultimate plan for the world. This image of the Lamb is a powerful reminder that Christ’s victory was won through His sacrificial death on the cross, which now guarantees the triumph of God’s kingdom. This victory, however, is not merely future but is something that has already been accomplished.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Lamb is a powerful reminder that Christ’s victory was won through His sacrificial death on the cross, which now guarantees the triumph of God’s kingdom. This victory, however, is not merely future but is something that has already been accomplished. The triumph of the Lamb is seen throughout the book, especially in the imagery of the “multitude that no one could number” (Rev. 7:9), who have come through the great tribulation and now stand victorious before the 48

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: throne of God. This multitude represents the Church, the people of God, who have overcome the forces of evil through the blood of the Lamb (Rev. 12:11). For the early Church, facing intense persecution under the Roman Empire, Revelation was a powerful message of hope. It assured them that their suffering was not in vain, for the victory had already been secured in Christ. The call to the Church is not one of passive waiting but of active perseverance, overcoming through faith in the victorious Lamb. “To him who overcomes, I will grant to sit with Me on My throne” (Rev. 3:21). This promise encourages believers to remain faithful, even in the face of suffering, because the ultimate victory belongs to Christ and, through Him, to those who overcome. Harold Eberle writes, “The victory of the Lamb is not just a future event; it is a present reality. The Church must understand that, though we may face trials and tribulation, we are already partakers in Christ’s victory. We are not waiting for victory; we are living in the victory of the Lamb right now.” Implications for the Christian Life Today The message of Revelation is not one of terror but of triumph. Believers today can take comfort in knowing that Christ has already overcome the world and that, in Him, they share in that victory. There is no need for fear of future cataclysmic events, for Christ’s triumph over sin and death ensures that God’s ultimate purposes for His creation will come to pass. For the Christian living in today’s world, the message of Revelation calls for a bold, confident faith. Despite the difficulties and persecutions that may arise, believers are reminded that their hope is secure in Christ, the victorious Lamb.

text: Chapter 7 The Kingdom Has Come and Keeps Coming Introduction The Kingdom of God is one of the central themes in the Bible, particularly in the teachings of Jesus Christ. Yet, it is often misunderstood or misapplied when viewed through an overly simplistic lens. To fully grasp the significance of the Kingdom, it is crucial to recognize that it is both already present and not yet fully realized. This tension between the “already” and the “not yet” of the Kingdom encapsulates the dynamic nature of God’s redemptive work in the world and provides believers with a deeper understanding of the nature of salvation and the mission of the Church. Jesus’ proclamation of the Kingdom was not just an announcement of a future event but also the arrival of God’s reign in the present age. This tension is seen throughout the Gospels, particularly in Jesus’ parables, which describe the Kingdom’s current manifestation and its future consummation. The psalms, too, particularly Psalm 110, provide prophetic insight into the reign of the Messiah in the midst of His enemies. In this chapter, we will explore the already-not-yet nature of the Kingdom of God, delving into how Jesus’ parables reveal the present power of the Kingdom, and how Psalm 110 offers a vision of the Messiah reigning in the midst of His enemies. We will also reflect on the theological implications of this reality for Christians today, encouraging a mindset of active engagement with the Kingdom while awaiting its full consummation. The Already and Not Yet of the Kingdom One of the most important theological concepts that emerges from Jesus’ teaching is the idea that the Kingdom of God has already come in a 51

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: significant way but is not yet fully realized. This tension is central to understanding the biblical narrative and how believers are to live in the present age. When Jesus began His ministry, He proclaimed that “the Kingdom of God has come near” (Mark 1:15). In this declaration, He was announcing that the reign of God had broken into human history in a new way through His own person and ministry. This is what theologians often refer to as the “already” aspect of the Kingdom. Through Jesus’ life, death, and resurrection, the power of the Kingdom has been inaugurated. The Kingdom is here in the person of Jesus Christ, and those who follow Him are already participating in its reality. While the resurrection of Jesus inaugurated the Kingdom and fulfilled the will of God in a decisive way, Jesus also pointed to a future dimension of that Kingdom, one that has not yet been fully revealed. In Matthew 6:10, where He teaches His disciples to pray, “Your kingdom come, Your will be done on earth as it is in heaven,” He affirms this forward-looking hope. Though Christ reigns even now, this prayer expresses the longing for the full manifestation of God’s rule, when His will is perfectly done on earth as in heaven, and all things are made new. This is the 'not yet' aspect of the Kingdom: not a contradiction of His present reign, but the future consummation of what was inaugurated through His death and resurrection. N.T. Wright has written extensively on this concept of the “already but not yet” nature of the Kingdom. He explains, “The Kingdom of God is not simply something that will come in the future; it is already here in the person of Jesus.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: nd resurrection. N.T. Wright has written extensively on this concept of the “already but not yet” nature of the Kingdom. He explains, “The Kingdom of God is not simply something that will come in the future; it is already here in the person of Jesus. But it is also something that will come fully in the future when God’s reign is manifest in all its fullness.” This tension means that while we experience the blessings of the Kingdom now, we live in a world that is still marked by suffering, sin, and rebellion. The Kingdom has begun, but its final fulfillment awaits the return of Christ. 52

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: For Christians, this understanding shapes our daily lives. We are called to live as citizens of the Kingdom in a world that is still under the influence of the powers of darkness. We are called to work for justice, peace, and righteousness, knowing that the Kingdom has already come in part but will one day be fully realized. The “already” of the Kingdom provides hope and purpose in the present, while the “not yet” gives us a longing for the future and motivates us to continue advancing the cause of Christ. Parables of Present Power (Matt. 13) In Matthew 13, Jesus uses a series of parables to explain the nature of the Kingdom of God. These parables emphasize the present power of the Kingdom and its ongoing growth, even in the face of opposition. While the full reality of the Kingdom is still to come, Jesus teaches that the Kingdom has already begun to take root and is having a powerful, transformative effect on the world. In the Parable of the Sower (Matt. 13:3-9), Jesus describes how the word of the Kingdom is spread throughout the world, with some people receiving it and bearing fruit, while others reject it. The power of the Kingdom is seen in the way that God’s Word transforms lives, even in a world filled with obstacles and distractions. The Kingdom does not come with force or coercion but through the quiet yet powerful work of God’s Word in the hearts of those who hear it. The Parable of the Mustard Seed (Matt. 13:31-32) and the Parable of the Leaven (Matt. 13:33) further illustrate the idea that the Kingdom of God begins small but has a profound impact on the world. The mustard seed, though tiny, grows into a large tree, symbolizing the way the Kingdom begins humbly but grows into something that influences the entire world.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: ate the idea that the Kingdom of God begins small but has a profound impact on the world. The mustard seed, though tiny, grows into a large tree, symbolizing the way the Kingdom begins humbly but grows into something that influences the entire world. Similarly, the leaven, which works its way through the whole lump of dough, symbolizes the transformative power of the Kingdom as it spreads throughout creation. 53

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Lynn Hiles writes, “The Kingdom of God is not just about grand, visible displays of power. It is about the subtle, unseen work of God transforming the world from the inside out. It starts small, like a mustard seed or leaven, but it changes everything.” These parables remind us that, though the Kingdom may not always appear in dramatic ways, its power is real and present. Even when it seems that evil is winning or that the world is unchanged, the Kingdom is advancing, and its power is gradually but surely transforming the world. For Christians today, this means that we must be patient and persistent. The work of the Kingdom often does not yield immediate results, but the power of God is at work in ways that are unseen and often unnoticed. The call is to remain faithful in spreading the Gospel, knowing that the seed we sow will one day bear fruit, and the Kingdom will continue to grow until its full manifestation. Reigning in the Midst of Enemies (Ps. 110) Psalm 110 is a pivotal messianic psalm that reveals the nature of Christ’s reign as King. It is quoted repeatedly in the New Testament, particularly in relation to the ascension of Jesus and His present reign at the right hand of the Father. In Psalm 110:1, David prophesies, “The LORD says to my Lord: ’Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet.’” This psalm presents a picture of the Messiah, seated at God’s right hand, ruling in the midst of His enemies. While this may seem paradoxical, how can a king reign in the midst of enemies?, it actually describes the nature of Christ’s reign in the present age. Jesus is not a king who rules by military might or political power; He reigns as the victorious Lamb, having already defeated the forces of sin and death.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: n the midst of enemies?, it actually describes the nature of Christ’s reign in the present age. Jesus is not a king who rules by military might or political power; He reigns as the victorious Lamb, having already defeated the forces of sin and death. Yet, His enemies are still active, and the full manifestation of His reign is yet to come. The presence of enemies does not diminish His sovereignty but highlights the tension between the present reality of His Kingdom and the future consummation of His reign. 54

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: N.T. Wright explains that “Jesus’ reign is not one of coercion or violence but of love, justice, and peace. He reigns in the midst of opposition, and yet His Kingdom advances, even as His enemies remain active.” The reign of Jesus in the present age is a reign of patience, as He waits for the final defeat of His enemies, but it is also a reign of power, as He continues to work through His Church to bring about His purposes. For the believer, Psalm 110 offers both a comfort and a challenge. We are assured that Christ is reigning, even in the midst of opposition and hostility. His reign is not contingent on the absence of enemies; rather, it is evidenced precisely in His ability to rule in the midst of them. For Christians today, this means that we, too, are called to reign with Christ in the midst of a world that is often hostile to the Gospel. We are not promised an easy life free from opposition, but we are assured that, as we live faithfully in the power of the Holy Spirit, the Kingdom of God is advancing and will one day be fully realized. Implications for the Christian Life Today Understanding that the Kingdom of God is both already present and not yet fully realized has profound implications for the Christian life. It shapes our perspective on suffering, mission, and the way we engage with the world. First, it helps us to endure in the face of trials. While we know that the Kingdom has come, we also know that we live in a world that is still broken, and we will face difficulties and opposition. The not yet of the Kingdom means that suffering is a reality for the believer, but it is not the final word.
