text: PART THREE: Salvation in the Now 57

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Chapter 8 We Live From Victory, Not For It Introduction The Christian life is often framed as a struggle. We hear much about spiritual warfare, about the need to overcome the world, the flesh, and the devil. These are important realities. The apostle Paul himself speaks of “fighting the good fight of faith” (1 Timothy 6:12) and of being “more than conquerors” through Christ who loved us (Romans 8:37). Yet, there is an essential truth that often gets overshadowed in these depictions: we live from victory, not for it. This concept is foundational to the Christian faith. In fact, it defines how we are to approach every aspect of our spiritual lives. Many believers spend their days striving to secure a victory they think they still need to win. However, the victory has already been won. We are not striving for victory; we are walking in the victory that Christ has already achieved. This chapter seeks to explore this truth through key biblical teachings: righteousness as our position (Romans 5:1), freedom from condemnation (Romans 8:1), and our identity as overcomers (1 John 5:4). Understanding these theological concepts will not only deepen our doctrinal foundations but will also transform how we live out our Christian walk in the everyday moments of life. Righteousness as Our Position (Romans 5:1) One of the most transformative truths in Christianity is that righteousness is our position before God, and it is not earned through our actions or good works. In Romans 5:1, the apostle Paul declares, “Therefore, having been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ.” This justification is the basis of our relationship with God. To be justified means to be declared righteous, acquitted of all sin, and to be placed in a 58

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: right standing with God. This is not something we achieve by our efforts, but it is a position we receive by faith in the finished work of Jesus Christ. Theologians have long emphasized the radical nature of justification. It is not a process of gradual moral improvement or the accumulation of righteous acts; it is an instantaneous declaration by God that we are righteous because of Christ’s work on the cross. N.T. Wright writes, “Justification is the act of God in declaring the believer to be in the right, based on the work of Jesus Christ.” This declaration is final and irreversible. We are not in a state of being constantly judged or striving to be righteous. Rather, we have already been made righteous in God’s sight through Christ. The significance of this truth cannot be overstated. In the world’s religious systems, righteousness is something that is earned. People must prove themselves worthy of God’s approval through their behavior, their obedience to laws, or their rituals. But in the Christian gospel, righteousness is given freely. It is not based on our performance but on Christ’s perfect performance. Our righteousness is a gift from God, and we do not need to live in fear of losing it. It is not a performance-based standing but a position we have in Christ. This free gift of righteousness radically changes how we live. It liberates us from the exhausting cycle of trying to measure up. Instead of living under the constant pressure to be perfect, we can live in the peace of knowing that we are already perfect in Christ. This truth should embolden us to live confidently and boldly, not out of a sense of guilt or fear, but from a position of security in our standing before God.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: e to be perfect, we can live in the peace of knowing that we are already perfect in Christ. This truth should embolden us to live confidently and boldly, not out of a sense of guilt or fear, but from a position of security in our standing before God. Freedom from Condemnation (Romans 8:1) One of the greatest burdens that many Christians carry is the weight of condemnation. Guilt over past sins, shame from mistakes, and fear of judgment can create a constant sense of unease. Yet, the gospel provides a 59

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: liberating truth: there is no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus. Paul’s declaration in Romans 8:1 is not just a theological statement but a life-changing truth for the believer: “There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus.” Condemnation, as the New Testament teaches, is the result of sin, which brings death (Romans 6:23). It is the rightful consequence of rebellion against God. However, when Jesus took our place on the cross, He bore the full weight of that condemnation. Christ did not just die for our sins; He bore our condemnation. He took upon Himself the punishment we deserved so that we might be free from it forever. This freedom is not just theoretical. It has practical implications for our lives. Many Christians live under the shadow of past failures, replaying mistakes in their minds and wondering whether God has truly forgiven them. But Romans 8:1 assures us that those who are in Christ are free from condemnation. The sins that we have confessed are no longer held against us. God does not see us as He once did, guilty and condemned, but as He now sees us, righteous and blameless in Christ. The practical application of this truth is profound. It means that when we experience feelings of guilt, shame, or fear, we can remind ourselves that these emotions are not reflective of God’s view of us. We are accepted, forgiven, and free. This doesn’t mean we live carelessly or without regard for sin; rather, it empowers us to live holy lives out of gratitude and love for God, not out of fear of punishment. When we understand that we are forgiven, we can extend that forgiveness to others. The victory that Christ has won for us is the same victory we can offer to others.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: to live holy lives out of gratitude and love for God, not out of fear of punishment. When we understand that we are forgiven, we can extend that forgiveness to others. The victory that Christ has won for us is the same victory we can offer to others. This freedom from condemnation should lead to a life of grace, forgiveness, and peace. Instead of being trapped by past mistakes, we are called to live in the liberty of the gospel, knowing that we are fully accepted in Christ. 60

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Our Identity as Overcomers (1 John 5:4) A key aspect of our victorious life in Christ is understanding our identity as overcomers. In 1 John 5:4, John writes, “For everyone born of God overcomes the world. This is the victory that has overcome the world, even our faith.” The world, in this context, refers to everything that opposes God’s kingdom: sin, worldly values, the flesh, and the powers of darkness. To “overcome the world” means to live in victory over these forces, not by our own strength, but through faith in the victorious work of Christ. John’s statement is not just an abstract theological point; it is a practical reality. We are not passive participants in the world’s struggles. We are called to actively live out the victory that Christ has already secured for us. The idea of being an overcomer is central to the Christian identity. It does not mean that we will never face trials or difficulties, but it means that we are guaranteed ultimate victory. As N.T. Wright explains, “The victory over the world is not a victory to come, it is the victory already secured in the present by the death and resurrection of Jesus.” This truth transforms the way we view our daily struggles. When we face challenges, whether they are personal, relational, financial, or spiritual, we do not do so from a place of weakness. We do so from a place of strength. We are more than conquerors through Christ (Romans 8:37). We are not waiting for victory; we are living from it. Our challenges are opportunities to manifest the victory that Christ has already won for us. The victory of Christ over sin, death, and the devil is a cosmic victory. It affects all creation and every aspect of life.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: r victory; we are living from it. Our challenges are opportunities to manifest the victory that Christ has already won for us. The victory of Christ over sin, death, and the devil is a cosmic victory. It affects all creation and every aspect of life. Therefore, living as overcomers means that we confront everything in life, from personal struggles to global issues, with the confidence that Christ’s victory is sufficient. It means that we do not have to succumb to fear, worry, or defeat. Instead, we press forward in the power of Christ’s victory, knowing that He who is in us is greater than he who is in the world (1 John 4:4). 61

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Living from victory, not for it, is more than just a theological concept, it is a lifestyle. It has profound implications for how we approach every day of our lives. Here are some practical ways to apply this truth: 1. Rest in your position of righteousness. Understanding that righteousness is a gift, not a goal to be attained, changes how we live. Instead of striving to earn God’s favor, we rest in the assurance that we are already accepted in Christ. This frees us from the burden of perfectionism and allows us to live in peace with God. 2. Live in freedom from condemnation. When guilt or shame rises up, remind yourself of the truth that there is no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus. The past is forgiven, the present is secured, and the future is full of hope. This frees us to live confidently, knowing that God is not holding our failures against us. 3. Embrace your identity as an overcomer. Every challenge is an opportunity to manifest the victory of Christ. Whether it is in temptation, hardship, or spiritual battle, you can approach life with the confidence that Christ’s victory has already been secured. You are more than a conqueror. 4. Walk in the power of the Holy Spirit. The Holy Spirit empowers us to live out the victory of Christ. It is through His strength that we overcome the world, not by our own efforts. Depend on the Spirit daily to guide and empower you. Conclusion: Victory Is Ours to Live The Christian life is not about striving for a victory we have not yet obtained. It is about walking in the victory that Christ has already won. We live from victory, not for it. This victory is rooted in our righteousness in Christ, the freedom from condemnation, and our identity as overcomers.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: victory we have not yet obtained. It is about walking in the victory that Christ has already won. We live from victory, not for it. This victory is rooted in our righteousness in Christ, the freedom from condemnation, and our identity as overcomers. As we embrace these truths, we can live with confidence, peace, and 62

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: boldness, knowing that we are more than conquerors through Him who loved us. In every area of our lives, whether in our personal walk with God, our relationships with others, or our engagement with the world, we are called to live in the power of Christ’s finished work. The victory is already ours. We do not fight for it, but we live it out each day. And that is the life of victory that God has called us to. 63

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Chapter 9 New Heavens and New Earth – Now and Future Introduction: The Kingdom Come and the World to Come The biblical vision of the “new heavens and new earth” is one of the most profound and mysterious aspects of Christian eschatology. Many Christians envision this concept as a distant future reality, where God will restore creation after the culmination of human history. Yet, there is more to the “new heavens and new earth” than simply a futuristic hope. The new heavens and new earth are not only a future promise but a present reality that the church is called to live in and manifest. This chapter will explore the heaven and earth imagery as covenantal language, the role of the church as the New Jerusalem, and how seed-form reality is growing toward its fullness, blending both the now and the not-yet of the Kingdom of God. Heaven and Earth as Covenant Language The Bible opens with the creation of heaven and earth (Genesis 1:1). It is in this creation that God’s covenant with humanity begins to take shape. In the Old Testament, heaven and earth often symbolize God’s sovereign rule and covenantal relationship with His people. N.T. Wright notes that in the biblical narrative, "heaven" is not merely a distant realm of divine dwelling but, from the earliest parts of Genesis, refers to God’s realm of rule, where His will is fully carried out. Earth is the stage for God’s covenant with humanity, where God’s purposes are worked out in real-time. Throughout the Old Testament, heaven and earth stand in relation to one another as the backdrop to God’s redemptive plan. For example, when God made the covenant with Israel at Sinai, He used language of heaven and earth to underline the gravity of the covenant.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: he Old Testament, heaven and earth stand in relation to one another as the backdrop to God’s redemptive plan. For example, when God made the covenant with Israel at Sinai, He used language of heaven and earth to underline the gravity of the covenant. In Deuteronomy 30:19, Moses calls heaven and earth to witness the covenant made with Israel: “I 64

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: call heaven and earth to witness against you today, that I have set before you life and death, blessings and curses.” This covenant is not merely between God and Israel but has cosmic significance, encompassing both heaven (God’s divine realm) and earth (the earthly realm where humans live). The language of heaven and earth is not static but dynamic, it speaks of God’s intention to bring transformation and renewal to all of creation. Thus, when we speak of the “new heavens and new earth” in the Christian context, we are talking about the renewal of all things through God’s covenant with His people. Lynn Hiles notes that “heaven and earth are two realms of God’s creative work, and they are both included in the redemption narrative. God has not discarded the earth in favor of heaven; He is restoring both realms.” The promise of the new heavens and new earth is thus the fulfillment of God’s original intention for creation: a harmonious, flourishing world under the reign of God, where heaven and earth are no longer separated, but are united in the fullness of God’s presence. Revelation 21:1 promises that “the first heaven and the first earth had passed away” and that “there was no more sea,” signaling the complete renewal of creation. The Church as the New Jerusalem (Hebrews 12:22) One of the most profound implications of the new heavens and new earth is the role of the church in this future reality. In Hebrews 12:22, the author declares that the church has already come to “Mount Zion, to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem.” The New Jerusalem is a central motif in Revelation 21, where it is described as coming down from heaven, adorned like a bride for her husband.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: es that the church has already come to “Mount Zion, to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem.” The New Jerusalem is a central motif in Revelation 21, where it is described as coming down from heaven, adorned like a bride for her husband. But what is the New Jerusalem? Is it a literal city, or is it a symbolic representation of something deeper? Theologically, the New Jerusalem is not merely a future city that will descend from heaven; it is also a present reality that is experienced now in 65

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: the life of the church. N.T. Wright affirms this by noting that “the New Jerusalem is the community of God’s people, gathered around the throne of God. It is a symbol of God’s reign on earth, now manifest in the lives of those who belong to Him.” The church, as the body of Christ, is the New Jerusalem in seed-form. It is the firstfruits of the new creation, the community where the reign of God is already breaking in. In the present, the church is called to embody the values and realities of the New Jerusalem. This means that the church is a place where God’s reign is visibly manifest through love, justice, peace, and righteousness. The apostle John describes this new city as having “no need of the sun or moon to shine in it, for the glory of God gives it light, and the Lamb is its lamp” (Revelation 21:23). This is a picture of the church, as the body of Christ, living in the light of God’s glory, even in the present age. The church is not merely waiting for the New Jerusalem; it is already a manifestation of it in the world. As Harold Eberle explains, “The church is the New Jerusalem in its early stages. We are the people who are supposed to bring the Kingdom of God to the earth, and our role is to live out the realities of the new creation in our communities.” The church, as the New Jerusalem, lives in tension between the “already” and the “not yet” of God’s kingdom. We experience the new creation in part, while waiting for the fullness of that reality to come. But this should not diminish the importance of the present reality. As the church, we are called to live in a way that reflects the values of the coming Kingdom, demonstrating the presence of heaven on earth through our lives and our witness.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: o come. But this should not diminish the importance of the present reality. As the church, we are called to live in a way that reflects the values of the coming Kingdom, demonstrating the presence of heaven on earth through our lives and our witness. Seed-Form Reality Growing Toward Fullness The new heavens and new earth are both already here and yet to come. This dual reality is the key to understanding the unfolding of God’s redemptive plan. The “seed-form” reality of the new creation is already present in the world through the work of Christ, but it is still growing and 66

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: maturing toward its ultimate fulfillment. Jesus, in His parables, often spoke of the Kingdom of God as something that begins small but grows into something great. In Matthew 13, He likens the Kingdom to a mustard seed that, though small, grows into a large tree where the birds of the air come to rest. Similarly, the new heavens and new earth begin in seed-form with the death, resurrection, and ascension of Jesus Christ. This concept is encapsulated in the “already and not yet” tension. Christ’s death and resurrection inaugurated the Kingdom of God, but its fullness will not be realized until His return. The Apostle Paul writes in Romans 8:19-22 that “the creation waits in eager expectation for the children of God to be revealed. For the creation was subjected to frustration, not by its own choice, but by the will of the one who subjected it, in hope that the creation itself will be liberated from its bondage to decay and brought into the freedom and glory of the children of God.” This passage reveals the profound truth that the new heavens and new earth are not just about individual salvation but about the restoration of all creation. Creation itself is groaning and waiting for the day when it will be set free from its bondage to decay. The world is waiting for the full revelation of the children of God, who will live in perfect harmony with God’s original purpose for creation. Lynn Hiles reflects on this idea by saying, “The new heavens and new earth are not just a future hope for individuals; they are the destiny of all creation, a complete restoration of God’s original intent for the cosmos.” The seed-form reality of the new creation can be seen in the church’s mission today.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: vens and new earth are not just a future hope for individuals; they are the destiny of all creation, a complete restoration of God’s original intent for the cosmos.” The seed-form reality of the new creation can be seen in the church’s mission today. As the body of Christ, the church is called to manifest the Kingdom of God on earth through its life and witness. The church is the firstfruits of the new creation (Romans 8:23), and as such, it is to live in a way that reflects the values and realities of the new heavens and new earth. This means that we are called to live as citizens of the New Jerusalem, demonstrating justice, peace, mercy, and righteousness in the world. We are called to be a foretaste of the fullness that is to come. 67

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Practical and Devotional Application 1. Live with the End in Mind : The reality of the new heavens and new earth should shape how we live today. While we await the future fulfillment of this promise, we are called to live as though the new creation is already here. This means living in hope, peace, and joy, knowing that God is bringing about the restoration of all things. It also means living with purpose, knowing that our actions today are part of God’s unfolding redemptive plan. 2. Embody the New Jerusalem : As the church, we are the New Jerusalem in seed-form. We are called to live out the values of the Kingdom in our communities. This means being agents of reconciliation, justice, and mercy in a broken world. The church should be a place where God’s glory is visibly manifested and where the heavenly Jerusalem can be seen on earth. 3. Participate in the Renewal of Creation : Our understanding of the new heavens and new earth should lead us to be stewards of God’s creation. Creation, as we know it, is groaning for redemption, and we are called to participate in its renewal. This means caring for the environment, promoting justice for the oppressed, and seeking the well-being of the world. 4. Live in the Tension : We live in the tension between the “already” and the “not yet.” The new heavens and new earth have already begun to break into the world, but they will not be fully realized until Christ’s return. We are called to live with anticipation and expectation, knowing that the best is yet to come. Conclusion The promise of the new heavens and new earth is both a present reality and a future hope. It is the unfolding of God’s covenantal plan to restore creation and make all things new. The church, as the New Jerusalem, is the 68

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: embodiment of this new creation in seed-form, and it is our privilege and responsibility to live in a way that reflects the realities of the Kingdom. We are living in the age of restoration, and we are called to participate in God’s great work of renewal. As we live in this tension, we can be confident that the future glory will far outweigh the present suffering, and we can live in the hope of the new heavens and new earth that are coming. 69

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Chapter 10 The Church and the Age to Come Introduction: The Church’s Role in the Present Age The Church, as the body of Christ, has a critical role to play in both the present age and in the unfolding of God’s Kingdom. From the outset of Jesus’ earthly ministry, His call to His followers was not merely to wait passively for the coming Kingdom but to actively participate in bringing God’s Kingdom to earth. This call has been passed down through the generations, and it remains as relevant today as it was two thousand years ago. This chapter will examine the cultural mandates given to the Church, the apostolic mission of Kingdom expansion, and the Church’s role as the present steward of divine order, all within the context of the age to come, that is, the new creation and the Kingdom of God that is both already here and not yet fully realized. Salt and Light as Cultural Mandates Jesus, in His famous Sermon on the Mount, called His followers to be the salt of the earth and the light of the world (Matthew 5:13-16). These metaphors speak volumes about the Church’s responsibility in the world, both in terms of its character and mission. Salt in the ancient world had multiple uses, most notably as a preservative and a flavor enhancer. Similarly, the Church is called to preserve the goodness of God’s creation and to bring the flavor of the Kingdom into the world, counteracting the forces of corruption, decay, and sin. The Church, as salt, is not meant to withdraw from the world but to engage it, working to preserve and enhance God’s original order in all areas of life. The metaphor of light similarly carries profound implications. In a world filled with darkness, the Church is called to be a beacon of truth, hope, and 70

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: righteousness. The light of the Church should not be hidden but placed on a lampstand for all to see. It should shine brightly, revealing the goodness, truth, and love of God, and illuminating the path to righteousness for those who are lost in spiritual darkness. N.T. Wright emphasizes that the Church’s role as salt and light is not merely a passive witness to the world but an active force for transformation, calling the world to align with God’s order. Lynn Hiles reinforces this by noting that “salt and light are about influencing and shaping the world, not about retreating from it.” Christians are not called to live in isolation but to engage with culture, bringing the transformative power of the Gospel into all sectors of society. Whether it be through social justice, business, education, the arts, or politics, the Church’s role is to serve as God’s instruments for cultural renewal. As salt and light, Christians are to bring heaven to earth, embodying the values of the Kingdom and demonstrating them in practical ways to the world around them. Apostolic Mission and Kingdom Expansion At the heart of the Church’s mission in the world is its apostolic calling. The term "apostle" comes from the Greek word apostolos , meaning "one who is sent." Jesus sent His apostles into the world with the commission to make disciples of all nations (Matthew 28:18-20). This mandate has not changed. The mission of the Church is one of Kingdom expansion, advancing the rule and reign of Christ in every sphere of life. Harold Eberle highlights that “the mission of the Church is not just to convert individuals, but to expand the dominion of the Kingdom of God in every area of human life.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: gdom expansion, advancing the rule and reign of Christ in every sphere of life. Harold Eberle highlights that “the mission of the Church is not just to convert individuals, but to expand the dominion of the Kingdom of God in every area of human life. It is a mission that affects society, culture, and history itself.” In other words, the Church is not simply a spiritual community that cares only about the eternal destiny of individuals. The Church’s mission is deeply earthly as well, it is about transforming the 71

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: world through the message of the Gospel, restoring God’s order in every aspect of human existence. In the early Church, the apostles were instrumental in spreading the message of Jesus and planting new communities of believers that would continue the mission long after they were gone. N.T. Wright underscores that the apostolic mission was never just about preaching in the synagogues and gathering converts; it was also about challenging the cosmic powers that opposed God’s purposes in the world. The apostles were establishing kingdom outposts wherever they went, teaching that Christ is Lord, and His Kingdom is breaking into human history. Today, the mission of the Church continues through its apostolic witness to the world. This mission is often seen in terms of church planting, evangelism, and discipleship. However, as we consider the Church’s role in the age to come, it is vital to recognize that the mission of the Church is about more than just bringing individuals into the Kingdom, it is about the expansion of God’s rule and reign over all creation. This includes addressing issues of social justice, environmental stewardship, economic integrity, and political righteousness, to name a few. As the Church carries out its apostolic mission, it is advancing the Kingdom of God in all of creation. The already-but-not-yet nature of the Kingdom is particularly important here. While we see glimpses of God’s Kingdom breaking into history, we also recognize that the fullness of His Kingdom will only come when Jesus returns. The apostolic mission of the Church is not only about preparing people for heaven but also about transforming the earth to reflect the glory of God’s coming Kingdom.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: e also recognize that the fullness of His Kingdom will only come when Jesus returns. The apostolic mission of the Church is not only about preparing people for heaven but also about transforming the earth to reflect the glory of God’s coming Kingdom. As we engage in mission, we are not just preaching to prepare individuals for the next life; we are also working to bring God’s justice, peace, and righteousness to bear on the present world. 72

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: The Church as the Present Steward of Divine Order The Church, as the body of Christ, is also the present steward of God’s divine order in the world. This stewardship is not a passive responsibility; it is an active, dynamic calling to bring God’s rule into every area of human existence. In the beginning, God entrusted humanity with the care and governance of creation (Genesis 1:28). This mandate was never revoked; it is still active today, and the Church is called to be the stewards of divine order, working to restore God’s original intent for the world. N.T. Wright emphasizes that “the Church is called to act as the custodian of God’s order in the world, working to bring about God’s intended purposes for creation.” The Church, as God’s appointed agent on earth, is entrusted with the task of working in partnership with the Holy Spirit to bring creation into alignment with God’s purposes. This includes everything from social justice and caring for the poor to ethical business practices and environmental sustainability. The Church is called to uphold the moral and spiritual order established by God in the world. The Church’s role as the steward of divine order also means that it has a prophetic voice in the world, speaking truth to power and calling society to repent and return to God’s righteous ways. This can be seen throughout the prophetic tradition of the Old Testament, where prophets called Israel to repentance and obedience to God’s commandments. The New Testament continues this prophetic tradition, with the Church being the voice of God’s Kingdom in the present age, confronting injustice and unrighteousness wherever it is found. Stewardship is not just about taking care of resources; it is about bringing all things under the rule and reign of Christ.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: he Church being the voice of God’s Kingdom in the present age, confronting injustice and unrighteousness wherever it is found. Stewardship is not just about taking care of resources; it is about bringing all things under the rule and reign of Christ. This divine stewardship requires that the Church understand its responsibility to God and the world in a holistic way. The Church must not only address spiritual concerns but also engage with the pressing issues of justice, equality, and human flourishing in the world. 73

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Practical and Devotional Application 1. Live as Agents of Transformation : As salt and light, we are called to transform the culture around us. We should never settle for merely retreating from the world or leaving it to decay. We are called to bring the Kingdom of God into every corner of society. This means living with integrity, working toward justice, and seeking to make the world a better place in the name of Christ. Wherever we are, whether in the workplace, at school, or in our communities, we are agents of change, bringing the transformative power of the Gospel to bear on the world. 2. Embrace the Apostolic Mandate : The apostolic mission is not just for a select few but for all believers. We are all called to be part of Kingdom expansion, whether that means sharing the Gospel with our neighbors or engaging in social activism that promotes peace, justice, and righteousness. The mission of the Church is both global and local, and we are all invited to participate in it. Whether we are planting new churches or engaging in acts of mercy and service, we are fulfilling the apostolic mandate to bring the Kingdom of God to the world. 3. Practice Divine Stewardship : As the stewards of divine order, we are responsible for taking care of God’s creation and ensuring that His justice, peace, and righteousness are established in the world. This means advocating for the poor, protecting the environment, and working for social justice. We are also called to model the values of the Kingdom in every area of life. This requires us to live with a sense of purpose and responsibility, understanding that everything we do is part of God’s redemptive work in the world. 74

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Conclusion The Church has a crucial role to play in both the present age and the age to come. As salt and light, the Church is called to bring the reality of God’s Kingdom into every area of human life. As apostolic witnesses, we are called to expand the dominion of Christ on earth, making disciples and transforming society. And as stewards of divine order, we are called to align our lives and the world around us with God’s purposes, working to restore creation to its intended glory. The age to come has already broken into the present, and the Church is the visible sign of that coming Kingdom. As we live out our calling, we participate in God’s eternal work of redemption and restoration, and we eagerly await the fullness of His Kingdom when Christ returns to establish the new heavens and new earth. 75

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: PART FOUR: Glorification and the Ongoing Hope 76

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Chapter 11 Resurrection – Fulfilled Yet Unfolding Introduction The doctrine of resurrection stands at the very heart of Christian hope. From the earliest proclamations of the apostles to the present-day reflections on life after death, the resurrection of the dead has been viewed not merely as a doctrinal truth but as the fulfillment of God’s ultimate plan for creation. Yet, while the resurrection has been fulfilled in Christ’s bodily resurrection, it remains a mystery that continues to unfold in the lives of believers. This chapter will explore the biblical understanding of resurrection as both a present reality and a future hope. It will examine the significance of the resurrection as Firstfruits, the immortality granted at Christ’s appearing, and the mystery of change that will take place at the end of time. Firstfruits and the Bodily Hope: 1 Corinthians 15:20–23 The resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead is the cornerstone of Christian faith. The apostle Paul writes in 1 Corinthians 15:20–23 that “Christ has indeed been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep”. Paul uses the imagery of “firstfruits” to explain that Jesus’ resurrection is not an isolated event but the beginning of a larger resurrection that will include all who are in Christ. The concept of firstfruits in the Old Testament referred to the first portion of the harvest, which was set aside and offered to God as a sign of the full harvest to come (Exodus 23:19, Leviticus 23:10-11). In this way, the resurrection of Jesus is both a guarantee and a promise of the future resurrection of all believers. Jesus is the firstfruits, the pioneer of the resurrection that will be experienced by those who belong to Him. 77

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: N.T. Wright emphasizes that “the resurrection of Jesus is the launching of a new creation, the beginning of God’s restoration project for the world.” It is not merely a return to life as it was before but the beginning of something new, something transformative. Jesus’ bodily resurrection is the first step in the renewal of all things, signaling the victory of God over death, sin, and the powers of darkness. As the firstfruits, Jesus’ resurrection proclaims the promise that all who are in Christ will one day experience the same kind of transformation. The bodily nature of the resurrection is also crucial in Paul’s teaching. In 1 Corinthians 15:22–23, Paul continues: “For as in Adam all die, so in Christ all will be made alive. But each in turn: Christ, the firstfruits; then, when he comes, those who belong to him.” The resurrection is not simply a spiritual renewal but a bodily hope. The same bodies that are sown in dishonor and weakness will be raised in glory and power (1 Corinthians 15:42-44). This bodily hope points to the full redemption of the material world, a world that will be restored and perfected at the second coming of Christ. This bodily hope has significant implications for our faith and practice. As Christians, we live in the tension between what has been accomplished in Christ’s resurrection and what is yet to come in our own resurrection. It is not a matter of escaping the physical world but of God’s redemption of that world.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: and practice. As Christians, we live in the tension between what has been accomplished in Christ’s resurrection and what is yet to come in our own resurrection. It is not a matter of escaping the physical world but of God’s redemption of that world. Lynn Hiles echoes this sentiment, writing that “resurrection does not signal an escape from this world, but a restoration of the world to God’s original intention.” The resurrection is the ultimate affirmation of the goodness of creation, and it is in the body, our bodies, that we will experience the fullness of God’s redemptive work. Immortality at Christ’s Appearing: Philippians 3:20–21 In Philippians 3:20–21, Paul writes: “But our citizenship is in heaven. And we eagerly await a Savior from there, the Lord Jesus Christ, who, by the power that enables him to bring everything under his control, will 78

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: transform our lowly bodies so that they will be like his glorious body.” Here, Paul speaks of the immortality that awaits believers when Christ returns. The resurrection hope is not merely a promise of life after death, but it is a transformation of the body, a transformation that will take place at Christ’s appearing. At that moment, our bodies, which are subject to decay, weakness, and mortality, will be transformed to be like Christ’s glorious body, made imperishable and immortal. This promise of immortality is foundational to the Christian hope. It is not simply that believers will be spiritually renewed, but that they will experience the fullness of life in a transformed body. N.T. Wright has written extensively on the idea that resurrection is not a mere disembodied existence but a bodily existence in a renewed creation. The resurrection body will be like Christ’s body, which was physical, glorious, and imperishable. It will be the culmination of the redemption of the whole person, spirit, soul, and body. In practical terms, the hope of immortality is a powerful motivator for Christian living. The knowledge that our bodies will be transformed at Christ’s coming should encourage us to live in ways that reflect the eternal reality of our resurrection. We are called to live with an eternal perspective, understanding that our present suffering, decay, and mortality are temporary and will one day be replaced by the immortality promised by Christ. This reality gives Christians hope and endurance in the midst of the challenges of life, knowing that the best is yet to come.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: r present suffering, decay, and mortality are temporary and will one day be replaced by the immortality promised by Christ. This reality gives Christians hope and endurance in the midst of the challenges of life, knowing that the best is yet to come. Harold Eberle notes that “the promise of immortality is not just a doctrine to be debated, but a reality to be lived in, as it shapes our attitudes, values, and priorities in the present age.” As we live with the hope of immortality, we are called to be faithful stewards of our bodies, recognizing that they are temples of the Holy Spirit (1 Corinthians 6:19). The resurrection of the body is the ultimate affirmation of God’s goodness in creating us as embodied beings, and we are to honor God with our physical lives as we await the transformation that will come with Christ’s return. 79

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: The Mystery of Change: 1 Corinthians 15:50–54 One of the most profound aspects of the resurrection is the mystery of the change that will occur in our bodies. Paul speaks of this mystery in 1 Corinthians 15:50–54, where he writes: “I declare to you, brothers and sisters, that flesh and blood cannot inherit the kingdom of God, nor does the perishable inherit the imperishable. Listen, I tell you a mystery: We will not all sleep, but we will all be changed, in a flash, in the twinkling of an eye, at the last trumpet. For the trumpet will sound, the dead will be raised imperishable, and we will be changed.” This passage describes the mystery of the transformation that will take place at the return of Christ. Those who are alive when Christ returns will be changed in an instant, and the dead will be raised in imperishable bodies. The mystery of change is one of the most significant aspects of the resurrection doctrine. It is a change that is both sudden and profound, a transformation of the physical body that cannot be fully understood in human terms. This change is not merely a resuscitation of the old body, but a glorious transformation that makes the body fit for eternal life in God’s presence. The resurrection body will be imperishable, immortal, and glorious, unlike the frail and decaying bodies we now inhabit. N.T. Wright affirms that the resurrection body will be “real” but will possess new qualities that make it fit for the new creation, where there will be no more death, decay, or sorrow. This mystery of change is also a reminder that our present understanding of life, death, and resurrection is limited. We only see in part, but when Christ returns, we will know fully.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: new creation, where there will be no more death, decay, or sorrow. This mystery of change is also a reminder that our present understanding of life, death, and resurrection is limited. We only see in part, but when Christ returns, we will know fully. In this light, Paul encourages believers to hold fast to the hope of the resurrection, even in the midst of suffering and uncertainty. The reality of the resurrection shapes how we live now, giving 80

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: us the strength to endure trials and the courage to face death, knowing that the day will come when we will be changed and made immortal. Practical and Devotional Application 1. Live in the Light of Resurrection Hope : The resurrection is not just a doctrinal concept but a living hope that transforms how we live. The knowledge that Christ has been raised and that we too will one day be raised gives us the strength to endure trials, the courage to face death, and the confidence to live in a way that reflects God’s Kingdom. The resurrection gives meaning to every aspect of our lives, from how we view suffering to how we approach relationships, work, and the world around us. 2. Honor God with Your Body : As we await the transformation of our bodies, we are called to be faithful stewards of them in the present. Our bodies are temples of the Holy Spirit, and we are to honor God with them. This includes caring for our health, living with integrity, and using our bodies in service to others. The hope of resurrection should motivate us to live with a high view of the body, recognizing that it is part of God’s redemptive plan for creation. 3. Embrace the Mystery of Change : The resurrection teaches us that change is a mystery, one that we cannot fully comprehend in this life. But we can trust that God is at work, bringing about a transformation that will make us fit for eternal life with Him. This gives us the freedom to embrace mystery and live by faith, knowing that the ultimate answer to our questions will come when Christ returns. 4. Find Strength in the Firstfruits : The resurrection of Christ is the guarantee that our resurrection is coming. As we face the difficulties of life, we can find strength in the reality of the 81

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: firstfruits, the knowledge that Christ has already triumphed over death and that we too will be raised to eternal life. Conclusion The resurrection is the definitive answer to the problem of death and decay. It is the pivotal event in history, the moment when God’s victory over sin, death, and the grave was publicly declared in the resurrection of Jesus. Yet, the resurrection remains a mystery, unfolding in our lives as we await the fullness of the resurrection at Christ’s return. As we live in the tension between what has been accomplished and what is yet to come, we are called to live with the hope of resurrection in the present, understanding that our bodies, our lives, and the world are part of God’s redemptive plan. 82

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Chapter 12 Death Defeated Introduction From the dawn of human history, death has cast a long shadow over creation. It is the inevitable consequence of the fall, the grim reminder of sin’s intrusion into the world. But the good news of the Gospel is that death is not the final word. Through the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ, death has been defeated once and for all. As Paul writes in 1 Corinthians 15:55, “O death, where is thy sting? O grave, where is thy victory?” The victory of Christ over death is not just a future hope but a present reality that transforms how believers live and die. In this chapter, we will explore the significance of Christ’s final blow to death, the believer’s triumph in Christ’s victory, and the transformative understanding of death as a door to eternal life, rather than a final defeat. The Scriptures teach us that Christ’s work on the cross has radically redefined death, rendering it powerless and opening the way for believers to live with an entirely new perspective on life, death, and eternity. Christ’s Final Blow to Death: Hebrews 2:14-15 In Hebrews 2:14–15, the writer speaks of Christ’s decisive victory over death, stating: “Since the children have flesh and blood, he too shared in their humanity so that by his death he might break the power of him who holds the power of death, that is, the devil, and free those who all their lives were held in slavery by their fear of death.” The author of Hebrews reveals that Christ’s incarnation and subsequent death were necessary to destroy the power of death and to set humanity free from its fear. The devil is described as the one who holds the power of death, but Christ’s sacrifice on the cross shattered this power, rendering death powerless over those who are in Him. 83

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: This passage underscores the profound unity between Christ and humanity. By taking on flesh, Jesus fully identified with the human condition and took on the penalty of sin, which is death (Romans 6:23). His death was not just an act of substitutionary sacrifice but a confrontation with death itself. Christ’s victory over death was cosmic in scope, as it was not only a victory for humanity but for all of creation. Theologian N.T. Wright writes that “Christ’s death and resurrection were not merely a victory for individuals, but for the whole world, a victory that will eventually bring new creation.” Jesus did not simply escape death; He conquered it on behalf of His people. Lynn Hiles further emphasizes that “Jesus didn’t just die for us; He died as us, to destroy the power of death that holds us in bondage. His death was the means by which He broke the grip of death, freeing us from fear and giving us the gift of eternal life.” The death of Jesus, therefore, represents not only a substitution but also a transformative victory that extends to all believers. It is a victory over the devil, who has held humanity captive to the fear of death, and it is this victory that believers now share in Christ. Christ’s victory is not merely a theoretical or doctrinal concept but a practical reality that transforms the lives of believers. By His death, Jesus has freed us from the fear of death, allowing us to live in the confidence that death no longer has the final say. Christians are no longer bound by the terror of the grave; instead, they can live with the assurance that death is merely a gateway to eternal life.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: from the fear of death, allowing us to live in the confidence that death no longer has the final say. Christians are no longer bound by the terror of the grave; instead, they can live with the assurance that death is merely a gateway to eternal life. Our Triumph in His Victory: 1 Corinthians 15:54–57 In 1 Corinthians 15:54–57, Paul celebrates the triumph of believers in Christ’s victory over death: “When the perishable has been clothed with the imperishable, and the mortal with immortality, then the saying that is written will come true: ’Death has been swallowed up in victory.’ ’Where, O 84

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: death, is your victory? Where, O death, is your sting?’ The sting of death is sin, and the power of sin is the law. But thanks be to God! He gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ.” Paul’s triumphant declaration is not just a future hope, but a present reality for believers. In Christ, death has been swallowed up in victory, and believers share in this victory. Paul teaches that the sting of death, sin, has been removed through the cross of Christ, and the power of sin, which brings death, has been defeated by the grace of God. This victory is a gift that believers receive through faith in Christ, and it is something that empowers them to live differently in the present world. The importance of understanding this victory not only as a doctrinal truth but as an experiential reality: The victory over death is not a distant future event, but a present-day reality for believers. We have been given victory over sin and death right now, and that victory is meant to affect the way we live, think, and engage with the world around us. This victory is the basis for Christian hope, not a vague wish for the future but a certainty grounded in the historical event of Christ’s resurrection. It is a victory that has already been won and is now being applied to the lives of believers through the indwelling Holy Spirit. This victory is already but not yet: While we do not yet experience the fullness of our resurrection bodies, the victory over death is already secured in Christ. We live in the tension between what has already been accomplished and what is still to come, but we are assured that death’s grip has been broken and that eternal life awaits us. As a result, Christians can live fearlessly, knowing that death is no longer the end.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: tension between what has already been accomplished and what is still to come, but we are assured that death’s grip has been broken and that eternal life awaits us. As a result, Christians can live fearlessly, knowing that death is no longer the end. The hope of resurrection gives believers the courage to face death with the confidence that it is not the end of their story. As N.T. Wright writes, “Death has been undone, and the promise of eternal life in Christ remains secure.” This hope leads to a radical transformation in how 85

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Christians view their lives and their eventual death. The victory over death is a living hope that sustains believers through the trials of life. Death as a Door, Not a Defeat In light of Christ’s victory, death is no longer to be feared as the ultimate defeat, but rather it is to be seen as a door, a passage into eternal life. Jesus Himself, in John 14:19, said, “Because I live, you also will live.” His resurrection assures us that death is not the final separation but rather the entryway into a new life with God. This shift in understanding radically alters the way Christians approach death. It is no longer an enemy, but a transition to the fullness of life in the presence of God. Death is not the enemy it once was. It is simply a passage from one phase of life to the next, as we move from the earthly realm into the eternal realm. Death has lost its power to terrorize, and the fear of death that once enslaved humanity has been broken by the power of Christ’s death and resurrection. Christians, therefore, can approach death not with fear or despair, but with hope and anticipation. This transformation of how we view death is deeply practical for the Christian life. When we understand that death is a door and not a defeat, it changes how we live. We no longer live in fear of the end, but we live boldly in the light of the hope of eternal life. This affects everything, from how we face suffering and hardship to how we interact with others who are facing death. It gives us a perspective of eternity, helping us to live with an eternal mindset, focusing on things that matter beyond this life. In the face of death, Christians are not called to deny the pain or sorrow it brings but to embrace the hope that death is not the end.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: spective of eternity, helping us to live with an eternal mindset, focusing on things that matter beyond this life. In the face of death, Christians are not called to deny the pain or sorrow it brings but to embrace the hope that death is not the end. In Christ, death has been transformed from a curse into a blessing, from the final defeat to the gateway to eternal victory. This is not a denial of the reality of loss, but a radical redefinition of death’s power over us. 86

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Practical and Devotional Applications 1. Live Fearlessly : Understanding that Christ has defeated death gives us the freedom to live boldly. We no longer need to fear death, because we know that in Christ, we have eternal life. This allows us to take risks in our service to God, to live with courage, and to embrace the fullness of life in Christ, knowing that death no longer has dominion over us. 2. Live with an Eternal Perspective : The victory over death invites us to live with an eternal perspective. Our values, priorities, and decisions should be shaped by the reality that we are eternal beings, and our life on earth is temporary. This perspective transforms how we approach our relationships, work, and mission, as we recognize that our ultimate hope is in eternal life with Christ. 3. Comfort the Grieving : As believers, we have the privilege of comforting those who grieve, reminding them that death is not the end. While we mourn the loss of loved ones, we do so with the hope that they have passed from this life into the presence of Christ. We can offer comfort to others because we know that Christ’s victory has transformed death from a final separation into a hopeful transition. Conclusion Christ’s defeat of death on the cross is the central act of redemption in the Christian faith. Through His death and resurrection, He has destroyed the power of death and has opened the way for believers to live in the freedom of eternal life. This victory is not just a future hope but a present reality that empowers Christians to live without the fear of death. We now see death not as a defeat but as a door to the eternal life we have in Christ.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: ers to live in the freedom of eternal life. This victory is not just a future hope but a present reality that empowers Christians to live without the fear of death. We now see death not as a defeat but as a door to the eternal life we have in Christ. The victory over death is a definitive and transforming truth that gives us hope, courage, and purpose as we live in the light of Christ’s resurrection. 87

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Chapter 13 The Restoration of All Things Introduction The concept of restoration is deeply embedded in the heart of the Christian faith, stretching from the beginning of creation to the ultimate future hope. The Bible presents a grand narrative in which all things are being restored, a restoration that reaches beyond the redemption of humanity to the reconciliation of all creation to God’s original plan. This restoration is not only a future promise but is already being enacted in the present through the work of Christ and the empowering of the Holy Spirit. In this chapter, we will explore the theological, biblical, and practical aspects of the restoration of all things. Specifically, we will reflect on the promise of restoration found in Acts 3:21, the restoration of the image of God in humanity, and the promise of God dwelling with man forever as revealed in Revelation 21:3. Through this journey, we will gain a deeper understanding of God’s redemptive work in history and how this hope of restoration impacts the way we live as Christians today. Acts 3:21: The Promise of Covenantal Restoration In Acts 3:21, Peter speaks to the crowd, proclaiming the restoration that has been promised by God: “Heaven must receive him [Jesus] until the time comes for God to restore everything, as he promised long ago through his holy prophets.” This statement encapsulates the eschatological hope of the early church: that God is in the process of restoring all things. This restoration is not just about individual salvation but is the renewal and reconciliation of creation itself. This passage speaks to the covenantal nature of God’s promises. From the Abrahamic covenant to the Davidic covenant, God has promised not only to save individuals but also to redeem His entire creation.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: renewal and reconciliation of creation itself. This passage speaks to the covenantal nature of God’s promises. From the Abrahamic covenant to the Davidic covenant, God has promised not only to save individuals but also to redeem His entire creation. The restoration 88

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: of all things refers to the fulfillment of God’s promises made to Israel, and more broadly, to all of creation. The long-awaited Messiah, Jesus Christ, has inaugurated this restoration by His death, resurrection, and ascension. The resurrection of Jesus is not merely the launching of a new hope for individuals, but the beginning of the restoration of the world itself. It is the ’first fruits’ of the new creation. Jesus’ work is both redemptive for humanity and restorative for the entire cosmos. The kingdom of God is not just about saving people from their sins; it’s about the restoration of the creation itself. Through Jesus, God is bringing all things back into harmony with His original purpose.” The work of restoration is cosmic in scope. It includes the healing of the earth, the rebuilding of broken relationships, and the restoration of God’s image in human beings. This restoration is being realized in the present age through the work of Christ but will be fully realized in the age to come, when Christ returns to establish His eternal reign. The Image of God Fully Restored Central to the restoration of all things is the restoration of the image of God in humanity. At the beginning of creation, humanity was made in the image of God (Genesis 1:26–27). This image, however, was marred by the fall when Adam and Eve sinned. The perfect relationship between humanity and God was severed, and the image of God in humanity was distorted. As a result, human beings became enslaved to sin, and the effects of sin permeated every part of creation. However, through the work of Christ, this image is being restored. The Gospel teaches that those who are in Christ are being renewed to reflect God’s image more fully (2 Corinthians 3:18).

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: sin, and the effects of sin permeated every part of creation. However, through the work of Christ, this image is being restored. The Gospel teaches that those who are in Christ are being renewed to reflect God’s image more fully (2 Corinthians 3:18). The life, death, and resurrection of Jesus are the means by which humanity’s broken relationship with God is restored and the image of God in humanity is being gradually reformed. Christ is not only the model of the perfect human, but He is also the agent of our restoration to that image. 89

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: In Romans 8:29, Paul writes that Christians are being “conformed to the image of his Son”, a process that is initiated at salvation but will be brought to completion when Christ returns. The full restoration of the image of God in us will only be realized in the age to come, when we are raised with new bodies and perfected in holiness. This will be the culmination of the restoration process, as humanity is restored to its intended purpose, to live in perfect fellowship with God, reflect His character, and rule over creation as His vice-regents. The restoration of the image of God in humanity is not simply a return to pre-fall innocence but a transformation into something even greater: humanity as it was always meant to be, fully redeemed, fully conformed to the likeness of Christ, and fully restored to fellowship with the Creator. Harold Eberle writes, “The restoration of the image of God is not just a moral transformation but a complete spiritual renewal that affects every aspect of the human being.” This restoration is a gradual process in the present age, but it will be consummated in the future when we are made like Christ in His glory. God Dwelling with Man Forever: Revelation 21:3 The ultimate goal of restoration is not just the renewal of creation or the restoration of humanity; it is the restoration of the presence of God with His people. In Revelation 21:3, we read, “And I heard a loud voice from the throne saying, ’Look! God’s dwelling place is now among the people, and he will dwell with them.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: he restoration of humanity; it is the restoration of the presence of God with His people. In Revelation 21:3, we read, “And I heard a loud voice from the throne saying, ’Look! God’s dwelling place is now among the people, and he will dwell with them. They will be his people, and God himself will be with them and be their God.’” This passage reveals the culmination of God’s redemptive work: the restoration of intimate fellowship between God and humanity, a fellowship that was lost in the garden of Eden but will be perfected in the new heavens and new earth. This verse points to the full realization of God’s covenant promise to be with His people (Exodus 29:45). The physical presence of God will dwell with His people in the new creation, and they will live in perfect harmony 90

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: with Him forever. The paradise lost in Genesis will be restored in Revelation, but in an even more glorious way. This is not merely a return to the Garden of Eden but the establishment of an eternal kingdom in which God’s presence is unmediated, and His people will enjoy an unbroken fellowship with Him. This is the end goal of salvation, God’s presence with His people in a restored creation. In this new creation, there will be no more sin, no more pain, no more tears. Everything will be perfect, and God’s glory will fill the earth as the waters cover the sea (Habakkuk 2:14). N.T. Wright writes, “The restoration of all things is about the fulfillment of God’s original intention for creation. It’s not a return to some idyllic past but the fulfillment of God’s purposes for a redeemed world and a redeemed people.” This promise points to the consummation of the kingdom of God, when all things will be made new, and God will dwell with His people in eternal joy and peace. Practical and Devotional Applications 1. Hope for the Present : The promise of the restoration of all things gives Christians hope in the midst of suffering and brokenness. In a world marked by sin, pain, and injustice, the hope of restoration is the anchor for the soul. Christians can live with joy and peace in the present, knowing that God is at work, restoring all things and that one day, He will make all things new. 2. Living as Ambassadors of Restoration : As believers, we are called to be agents of restoration in the world. This means actively working for justice, peace, and reconciliation in our communities and the world. As ambassadors of Christ, we participate in God’s restorative work by sharing the Gospel, engaging in social action, and living lives that reflect God’s reconciling love. 91

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: 3. Embrace the Process of Personal Transformation : The restoration of all things includes the restoration of the image of God in us. This means that Christians are to be actively engaged in the process of becoming more like Christ in every area of life. This involves both sanctification, the gradual renewal of our minds and hearts, and glorification, the final transformation we will experience when Christ returns. 4. Live in the Light of Eternity : The promise of God dwelling with us forever calls Christians to live with an eternal perspective. The eternal future of perfect fellowship with God should shape the way we live today. We are to live with hope and anticipation of the day when we will experience the fullness of God’s presence in the new heavens and new earth. Conclusion The restoration of all things is a grand narrative that stretches from creation to consummation. God’s plan is to restore His creation, to renew humanity in His image, and to dwell with His people forever. As we await the final fulfillment of this restoration, we live as people who are already restored in part and who live in the hope of the complete restoration to come. This is the gospel, the good news that God is making all things new. May we live in the light of this hope, actively participating in God’s restorative work in the world and anticipating the glorious future that awaits us. 92

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: PART FIVE: The Implications of a Victorious Eschatology 93

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Chapter 14 Discern the Times, Don’t Fear Them Introduction The current age is marked by a clash of narratives, as different worldviews compete for our attention, shaping our perspectives on history, humanity, and the future. For Christians, this presents a unique challenge: to live wisely and faithfully in a world that is constantly changing and increasingly hostile to biblical values. One of the most important aspects of this challenge is learning to discern the times in which we live, understanding the signs of the times from a biblical perspective, and avoiding the temptation to either retreat from the world in fear or succumb to apocalyptic panic. The need for discernment is especially crucial in light of the increasing prominence of prophetic ministry in the church today. While prophetic words can be a source of great encouragement and guidance, they can also be misused or misunderstood, leading to confusion or unnecessary fear. Therefore, the call in this chapter is to develop sound prophetic discernment so that we may understand the times we are in, but not be overwhelmed by them. Instead of reacting with escapism or panic, we are called to engage with our world in a way that reflects the hope and faith we have in Christ. Escaping Escapism: A Call to Action One of the most prevalent tendencies among Christians in times of difficulty or uncertainty is to adopt a mindset of escapism. This mindset often manifests as an overwhelming desire to withdraw from the world, either physically or emotionally, in an attempt to escape the pressures, trials, or challenges that we face. Some Christians may even look for escape routes in the form of prophetic predictions or theological 94

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: interpretations that promise an imminent escape from the world in the form of the Rapture or other forms of divine intervention. However, this tendency to escape contradicts the teachings of Scripture. Throughout the New Testament, the apostles and early church leaders urged believers to remain steadfast in the faith, not to retreat from the world, but to engage with it faithfully and fruitfully. Jesus’ high priestly prayer in John 17:15 demonstrates this attitude: “My prayer is not that you take them out of the world but that you protect them from the evil one.” Jesus did not call His followers to escape the world, but to be preserved in it as they carry out their mission. The call of the Christian is not to flee from the challenges of life but to faithfully engage with the world, discerning the times and understanding the unique opportunities that arise in each generation to fulfill the Great Commission. We are to be like salt and light (Matthew 5:13–16), agents of change, transformation, and hope in a world that desperately needs it. Escapism undermines the incarnational nature of the gospel. Jesus did not withdraw from the world, He entered into it. His incarnation was the ultimate act of engagement with the brokenness of humanity, and we are called to follow His example by being present in the world, while also maintaining our distinctiveness as His people. Escapism robs us of the opportunity to experience the fullness of our purpose in God. When we seek to escape the troubles of this world, we miss the opportunities God gives us to shine His light in the midst of darkness.

text: Sound Prophetic Discernment: Testing the Spirits The Bible is clear that prophecy has an important role to play in the life of the church, as it serves to build up (1 Corinthians 14:3), encourage (Acts 15:32), and edify (1 Corinthians 14:4). However, Scripture also warns about the misuse of prophecy, cautioning believers to test what is said and to exercise discernment when receiving prophetic words. In 1 Thessalonians 5:20–21, Paul writes: “Do not treat prophecies with contempt, but test them all; hold on to what is good.” The implication here is clear: while prophecy can be a powerful tool for spiritual growth and encouragement, it must be tested and weighed carefully against the teachings of Scripture. N.T. Wright highlights that “prophetic discernment is not just about hearing a word, it’s about interpreting it faithfully in light of God’s character and plan.” This requires not only a knowledge of Scripture but also a sensitivity to the Holy Spirit, who guides us into all truth (John 16:13). Prophecies should never be treated as absolute predictions about the future but should always be evaluated in light of God’s Word and God’s character. Prophecy that causes confusion, fear, or panic is not a reflection of God’s character, which is one of peace (Philippians 4:7), hope (Romans 15:13), and love (1 John 4:18). Prophecy is not meant to drive us into panic or uncertainty but to reveal the heart of God and to encourage us in our mission. The prophetic word should always lead us to greater faith, not fear. The prophetic ministry is meant to be a gift of encouragement, to build up the church and to help believers navigate difficult times with hope and faith, not to lead them into unnecessary alarm or escapism.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_

This means being open to hearing prophetic words, but also being committed to testing those words against the truth of Scripture and the witness of the Holy 96

Prophecy for Edification, Not Panic A central theme in Scripture is that prophecy is intended for the edification and building up of the body of Christ, not for instilling fear or causing division. In 1 Corinthians 14:3, Paul writes: “But the one who prophesies speaks to people for their strengthening, encouraging and comfort.” This is the key purpose of prophecy: to strengthen believers, encourage them in their faith, and provide comfort in times of difficulty. Prophetic words are meant to offer hope and clarity, not to plunge the church into despair or panic. This is especially crucial when considering the eschatological teachings found in Scripture, which can often be a source of confusion or fear for many Christians. While Scripture does indeed teach that the end times will be marked by tribulation, persecution, and cosmic upheaval, it also assures believers that God will triumph, and His kingdom will be established forever. The focus of prophetic words about the end times should be on encouraging Christians to remain faithful and hopeful, rather than stirring up fear about impending disaster. The end times are not a time of uncertainty for believers but a time of hope and assurance. The future is not something to fear for Christians, but something to eagerly anticipate. The return of Christ is the fulfillment of all God’s promises, not the beginning of chaos. Christians are called to live in the light of this hope, steadfast in faith and ready for action, rather than anxious about what might come.
