title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Chapter 15 Hope, Mission, and Dominion Introduction Christianity is not merely about an individual’s personal salvation but about the restoration of the world, the restoration of all things in Christ. Central to this restorative mission is the Great Commission, which calls the Church to actively engage in God’s plan for the world. This mission is far broader than merely saving souls, it encompasses the redemption of creation, the transformation of societies, and the advancement of God’s Kingdom in every realm of life. This chapter aims to explore the relationship between hope, mission, and dominion. We will examine the Great Commission within its fulfilled context, how the Church is called to engage culture as part of its Kingdom strategy, and the proper understanding of dominion as part of God’s redemptive plan, without falling into the pitfalls of dominionism, which has often been associated with power-seeking and oppression. The Bible presents an expansive vision for the Church’s role in the world, a vision rooted in hope for the restoration of creation, an active mission to bring about that restoration, and the dominion given to humanity in Christ to steward creation in righteousness. The Church’s mission, then, is not merely escape from the world, but engagement with it in a way that reflects God’s Kingdom and purposes. This understanding redefines what it means to be a follower of Christ in a world filled with challenges, injustice, and suffering. The Great Commission in Fulfilled Context The Great Commission, found in Matthew 28:18-20, is often understood in the context of evangelism, go into all the world and make disciples. This is, of course, true, but the commission extends beyond simply calling 100

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: individuals to personal salvation; it is about shaping societies through the establishment of God’s Kingdom. When we read the Great Commission through the lens of the fulfilled Kingdom of God, we see that it is not a futuristic event awaiting fulfillment but one that is being unfolded in the present age. The resurrection of Jesus Christ marked the beginning of the in-breaking of God’s Kingdom. As Jesus declared, "All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me" (Matthew 28:18). This declaration of cosmic authority is a declaration of dominion, not just over the hearts of individuals, but over all creation. The Great Commission, therefore, is not only about personal salvation but also about discipleship in the Kingdom. It is the process of bringing every nation, every people, and every sphere of life under the lordship of Christ. N.T. Wright points out that the Great Commission is an invitation to participate in God’s redemptive work in the world, not to merely wait for the end of the world. It is about the restoration of all things and making disciples in every corner of society, from government to education, from arts and culture to business and science. In a fulfilled context, the Great Commission is about the ongoing work of God’s people to expand the reach of God’s Kingdom in every area of life. This includes personal salvation but also cultural transformation. The Kingdom of God is already inaugurated and continues to expand through the faithfulness of the Church in fulfilling its mission to disciple the nations. Engaging Culture as Kingdom Strategy One of the most critical aspects of understanding the mission of the Church is its role in engaging culture. It is not enough for Christians to withdraw from society in an attempt to avoid its corruption.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: nations. Engaging Culture as Kingdom Strategy One of the most critical aspects of understanding the mission of the Church is its role in engaging culture. It is not enough for Christians to withdraw from society in an attempt to avoid its corruption. Instead, we are called to engage with culture in a way that reflects the Kingdom of God. 101

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Jesus’ command in Matthew 5:13-16 to be the salt of the earth and the light of the world implies a direct engagement with the world around us. The salt and light metaphor teaches us that we are not to remain isolated from culture, but to influence it by living out Kingdom principles in our daily lives. This can be seen in the context of the Church’s mission: we are called to be ambassadors of the Kingdom, acting as agents of transformation in every area of society. Christians must recognize that God is not only concerned with the salvation of individuals but with the redemption of creation. In Romans 8:19-21, Paul writes that the whole creation is groaning and waiting for the revelation of the sons of God, and that creation itself will be liberated from its bondage to decay and brought into the glorious freedom of the children of God. This passage points to the larger scope of God’s mission, the renewal of the entire cosmos. The Church, as the body of Christ, is called to be a participant in this work. Harold Eberle emphasizes that Kingdom strategy involves Christians bringing the principles of God’s Kingdom into every area of life, government, education, business, healthcare, and every other sphere of influence. The Church is called to redeem culture by living according to Kingdom values: justice, mercy, peace, and truth. This engagement is not about taking over or imposing a Christian theocracy, but about being salt and light, living as agents of transformation, and offering a compelling vision of what life can be like when lived according to God’s purposes. Dominion Without Dominionism: A Christocentric Understanding of Dominion At the heart of the biblical vision for human existence is the call to dominion over creation.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: compelling vision of what life can be like when lived according to God’s purposes. Dominion Without Dominionism: A Christocentric Understanding of Dominion At the heart of the biblical vision for human existence is the call to dominion over creation. This was given to Adam and Eve in the Garden of Eden (Genesis 1:28), where God commanded them to fill the earth and subdue it, and to have dominion over all living things. This call to 102

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: dominion was not a call to oppression, exploitation, or authoritarian control but to faithful stewardship of God’s creation. When we consider the concept of dominion within the context of the Kingdom of God, we must ensure that our understanding does not devolve into a distorted version of dominionism, the idea that Christians are called to dominate and rule over others in a worldly sense. Dominionism, in its more extreme forms, has been associated with authoritarianism and has been used to justify the coercion of individuals and nations to conform to a particular interpretation of Christianity. Dominion is rooted in servant leadership, where those who have been entrusted with authority are called to serve rather than exploit. In Christ, dominion is exercised in humility, self-sacrifice, and faithfulness, not through force or oppression. Jesus, the King of the Kingdom, demonstrated this through His humble servanthood, and Christians are called to follow His example in their exercise of dominion. Dominion is the responsibility of Christians to bring order and peace to the world around them, to restore creation to its original beauty, and to care for the earth as God intended. This is dominion without dominionism, a dominion that is Christ-centered and focused on service rather than control. Dominion is not about personal gain or earthly power; it is about reflecting God’s love and righteousness in the world. This understanding of dominion allows for the transformation of culture without the need for oppressive or coercive measures. Christians are called to demonstrate dominion through their actions, showing the world what God’s Kingdom looks like in practice, through acts of mercy, justice, compassion, and truth. Christians are called to expand God’s Kingdom by teaching others to live according to His ways and by transforming society through faithfulness to the Gospel. 3. Christians are called to exercise dominion by reflecting Christ’s example of self-sacrificial love, not through coercive or controlling measures. Conclusion The mission of the Church is grand and expansive. It is not merely about individual salvation but about the restoration of creation, the advancement of God’s Kingdom in every sphere of life. Christians are called to engage culture, disciple nations, and exercise dominion as faithful stewards of God’s creation. This mission is rooted in hope, hope in the fulfillment of God’s promises and in the coming of His Kingdom in its fullness. 104

May we live in hope, faithfully fulfill our mission, and exercise dominion as Christ has modeled for us. 105

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Chapter 16 Living Forward - Anchored in Peace, Moving in Power Introduction The Christian journey is a life lived between the already and the not yet, where believers are partakers of the fulfilled promises of God while simultaneously anticipating the fullness of His redemptive plan. As we engage in the mission of God, we live in the tension of what has been accomplished in Christ’s first coming and what is still to come in His second coming. The question is not only about our destination, but also about how we are to live forward in this present age while firmly anchored in peace and moving in the power of the Holy Spirit. This chapter explores the concepts of fulfilled prophecy, divine timeline, and eternal perspective as key elements for believers to embrace in order to live with purpose and confidence. These elements, when understood, provide a framework for a believer to confidently occupy the present with hope, peace, and power, while keeping an eternal perspective on God’s unfolding plan. The Joy of Understanding Fulfilled Prophecy One of the most profound aspects of Christian faith is the belief that God’s promises are sure and that His prophetic word will always be fulfilled. From the promises to Abraham in the Old Testament to the fulfillment of Christ’s coming, the prophetic Scriptures form the foundation of our confidence in God’s sovereign plan. Prophecy is not a mere prediction but a divine declaration of what is to come, rooted in God’s eternal plan. Prophecy in the Bible is not just about the future, it is deeply tied to the present and the past, revealing God’s character and His intentions for creation. 106

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Fulfilled prophecy is not merely something to be studied for historical or theological interest; it is a source of joy for the believer. The birth, life, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ were prophesied long before His advent. The anticipation of Christ’s first coming created hope and expectation among Israel and the nations. For the believer today, Jesus’ first coming provides us with confidence in God’s faithfulness to fulfill His promises. Jesus Himself taught His disciples that the prophecies about Him had been fulfilled through His life and ministry (Luke 24:44). His fulfillment of these prophecies serves as a signpost to God’s ongoing work in the world. The believer who understands the fulfillment of prophecy is then able to rejoice in the surety of God’s plan. Prophecy is not foretelling alone but a means of calling believers to action. The fulfillment of prophecy strengthens our faith and assures us that what God has begun in Christ will be completed in His return. The joy of understanding fulfilled prophecy should also deepen our worship, knowing that the God who declared the end from the beginning is the same God who is working in us and through us for His eternal purposes. It anchors our hope in the certainty that God is at work, faithful to His promises, and will bring all things to fulfillment in His perfect timing. The Believer’s Confidence in Divine Timeline Understanding the divine timeline is crucial for every believer. As we look around, it can often seem as though the world is spinning out of control. Wars, natural disasters, societal upheaval, and personal struggles may cause us to question God’s plan. However, for the Christian, the divine timeline provides a perspective of confidence in God’s sovereign control over history and the future.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: trol. Wars, natural disasters, societal upheaval, and personal struggles may cause us to question God’s plan. However, for the Christian, the divine timeline provides a perspective of confidence in God’s sovereign control over history and the future. 107

text: In 1 Thessalonians 5:1-2, Paul reassures the believers that the day of the Lord will come like a thief in the night, but they are not in darkness. They are children of the light and the day, equipped with the knowledge that God’s plan is unfolding with precision and purpose. The believer’s confidence lies not in knowing all the details of the future but in knowing that God is in control and that His timeline is perfect. For many Christians, it is easy to become preoccupied with the uncertainty of the future, but the divine timeline reminds us that God has already secured the final victory through Jesus Christ. The timeline of God is not defined by human time but by His eternal purpose, which will not fail. The believer, anchored in the confidence of this divine timeline, can live each day in light of the fact that the best is yet to come. This perspective fosters peace because it provides assurance that God is always at work. The divine timeline also serves as a reminder that God’s purposes are unfolding, and we, as believers, have a part to play. We are not mere spectators of a cosmic drama; we are active participants in the unfolding of God’s redemptive plan. Our lives are woven into God’s grand narrative, and He has called us to live in the now with hope and purpose, while we await the future fulfillment of His promises. This confidence in the divine timeline should give every believer a deep sense of peace that transcends the chaos of the world.

"Occupy Till I Come" with Eternal Perspective In Luke 19:13, Jesus gives a command that echoes throughout the New Testament: “Occupy till I come.” This command, often associated with the parable of the ten minas, is an exhortation to faithful stewardship of the resources, opportunities, and responsibilities entrusted to believers. It is a call to engage the present with an eternal perspective, knowing that Jesus will return and that His Kingdom will come in fullness. 108

Wright writes, “The Kingdom is not something to escape to; it’s something we are called to bring into the present age.” The eternal perspective gives believers the ability to look at the world around them, not with despair, but with the certainty that God’s redemptive plan will prevail, and we have a role in that unfolding story. In practical terms, to “occupy” means to actively live out the Kingdom in every sphere of lif, whether in the workplace, in relationships, or in societal engagement. But all of this is done with the understanding that our ultimate hope is not in this world, but in the future fulfillment of God’s promises. To live with an eternal perspective means to prioritize the things of God in light of the coming age. The way we live now, our choices, our priorities, and our missions, should be influenced by the knowledge that this present age is not the final reality. As Harold Eberle emphasizes, living with eternity in view should shape how we approach our work, our relationships, and our service to others. Every act of obedience to God’s Word, every act of justice, every prayer for healing, every effort to bring light into darkness is a foretaste of the Kingdom to come. text: Bonus Appendix: Clarifying the Controversies 112

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Appendix Rapture: History, Hype, and Hope Introduction Among the many eschatological (end-times) controversies within the Christian church, the concept of the Rapture has certainly garnered significant attention and stirred intense debate. The question of when and how the Church will be taken from the earth has led to various interpretations, fueling disagreement among Christians, theologians, and scholars for centuries. Is the Rapture a sudden, dramatic event? Is it pre-, mid-, or post-tribulational? Is it an actual, historical event or more of a symbolic reality representing God’s ultimate deliverance? This appendix aims to clarify the history, examine the hype, and ultimately reveal the hope inherent in the Rapture, drawing upon both biblical scholarship and the theological traditions that have shaped this doctrine. In doing so, we will navigate through historical developments, assess the role of the Rapture in modern evangelicalism, and present a biblically-informed understanding of hope in the return of Christ. 1. The Historical Development of the Rapture Doctrine The doctrine of the Rapture is often associated with John Nelson Darby and the Plymouth Brethren in the 19th century, though some argue that aspects of the idea were present in the early church. Darby’s dispensational theology introduced the notion of a pre-tribulation Rapture, the belief that Christians will be secretly taken away before the period of tribulation described in Revelation. According to this view, the Rapture will occur prior to the great tribulation, which will be followed by Christ’s second coming to establish His Kingdom on earth. However, it is essential to note that the idea of a Rapture was not a widespread belief in the early church. N.T. Wright, one of the foremost 113

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: scholars in New Testament studies, notes that early Christian eschatology was primarily concerned with the second coming of Christ and the resurrection of the dead, not the sudden removal of believers. In his book, Surprised by Hope , Wright suggests that the Rapture is often a misinterpretation of biblical texts that were meant to encourage Christians in their faith and perseverance, rather than to instill fear or confusion about the future. The specific idea of the Rapture being an event where believers are taken up to meet Christ in the air (1 Thessalonians 4:16-17) was not widely developed in Protestant theology until the 19th century. Darby’s teachings were popularized by the Schofield Reference Bible, which contributed to the spread of dispensationalism in evangelical circles, particularly in the United States. Many church historians argue that while elements of premillennialism (the belief that Christ will return before a literal 1,000-year reign) existed in early Christian writings, the modern pre-tribulation Rapture was largely absent. While it is helpful to understand the historical development of the doctrine, the biblical message is what ultimately should define our eschatological views. 2. Hype: The Rapture in Popular Culture In the 20th century, the concept of the Rapture gained immense popularity through works of fiction and films, particularly with the publication of the Left Behind series by Tim LaHaye and Jerry B. Jenkins. These books, along with their movie adaptations, depicted a dramatic and apocalyptic scenario where millions of Christians are mysteriously taken away in an event known as the Rapture, leaving behind a chaotic world. This portrayal has profoundly influenced many Christians’ perceptions of the Rapture.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: icted a dramatic and apocalyptic scenario where millions of Christians are mysteriously taken away in an event known as the Rapture, leaving behind a chaotic world. This portrayal has profoundly influenced many Christians’ perceptions of the Rapture. While the Left Behind series popularized the pre-tribulation Rapture view, it also fed into sensationalism and fear. The focus was often on the 114

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: catastrophic events of the tribulation period, overshadowing the biblical focus on hope and faithfulness. This hype around the Rapture tends to emphasize escape from tribulation rather than encouraging believers to remain faithful in the midst of suffering and tribulation. It’s essential to recognize that while prophecy certainly plays an important role in Christian eschatology, the biblical message is not one of fearful speculation about the future, but one of hope and perseverance. As N.T. Wright and John Stott argue, Jesus’ return is not intended to be a catastrophic escape plan, but a glorious and redemptive event for the whole creation. Wright notes that the resurrection of the dead and the renewal of all things will be at the center of God’s eternal plan, and it is this hope that Christians should focus on, rather than speculative scenarios about how and when the end will come. In light of this, Christians should approach the Rapture with a sense of balance, acknowledging that the Scriptures speak of hope in Christ’s return, not fear or speculation. 3. Hope: The Rapture and the Ultimate Hope of Christ’s Return Despite the controversies surrounding the specifics of the Rapture, the ultimate hope for every believer is that Christ will return and make all things new. Whether Christians hold to a pre-tribulation, mid-tribulation, or post-tribulation view, the central point is that Christ will return and redeem His people. As Harold Eberle argues, the Rapture should not be reduced to a theological gimmick or speculative fantasy, but rather seen as part of the biblical hope that Christ’s return is certain and will result in the final victory over evil.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: edeem His people. As Harold Eberle argues, the Rapture should not be reduced to a theological gimmick or speculative fantasy, but rather seen as part of the biblical hope that Christ’s return is certain and will result in the final victory over evil. For the believer, the Raptureregardless of when it occurs, should be understood in the broader context of Christ’s triumphant return to earth, where He will set all things right, wipe away every tear, and establish His eternal Kingdom. The Apostle Paul’s words in 1 Thessalonians 4:16-18 115

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: comfort believers, stating, “For the Lord Himself will come down from heaven, with a loud command, with the voice of the archangel and with the trumpet call of God, and the dead in Christ will rise first. After that, we who are still alive and are left will be caught up together with them in the clouds to meet the Lord in the air.” The true hope of the Rapture is not an escape from suffering but an affirmation that God will one day restore all things and bring His Kingdom to earth in fullness. The eternal perspective is that Christ will return to gather His people, resurrect them, and inaugurate the new heavens and new earth (Revelation 21:1-5). This hope is grounded in the certainty of God’s promises and His sovereignty over all history. The Rapture, therefore, is a promise of comfort, as it assures believers that in the midst of suffering and hardship, God will deliver them in His perfect timing. The hope of Christ’s return is transformational for how believers engage in the world today. Living with hope in the return of Christ compels Christians to act justly, love mercy, and walk humbly with their God (Micah 6:8), anticipating the fullness of God’s Kingdom, where justice, peace, and righteousness will reign. 4. Practical Reflections on the Rapture ● Endurance in Faith : Regardless of when the Rapture occurs, the primary focus for the believer is faithfulness in the present age. As Jesus taught in Matthew 25:1-13 in the parable of the ten virgins, we are called to be prepared and vigilant, living in light of Christ’s return with hope and obedience. ● Hope and Peace : The Rapture serves as a reminder that our ultimate hope is in Christ’s return. This hope gives us peace in the midst of trials, knowing that God will restore all things and make all things new. 116

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: ● Living with Purpose : The anticipation of the Rapture should spur us on to greater mission and kingdom work. Christians are called to live with purpose, knowing that the eternal Kingdom is coming. This perspective shifts the way we engage with culture, relationships, and our work, as we labor in hope for the world to come. Conclusion: Beyond the Hype-A Hope that Transcends While the Rapture has been the subject of much debate, speculation, and sensationalism, it ultimately remains an important doctrine within Christian eschatology. The historical development, hype, and misinterpretations should not detract from the core Christian hope: that Christ will return to gather His people and to establish God’s eternal Kingdom. Whether or not Christians adhere to a pre-tribulation Rapture or a different view is secondary to the essential truth that God is in control of history, and Christ’s return is our blessed hope. In the end, the Rapture is not merely a theological event; it is a comforting assurance for every believer that we are part of a larger story, a story where God triumphs over evil, and where we, as His people, are brought into His eternal presence. May this hope anchor us, empower us, and motivate us to live faithfully, with the understanding that the best is yet to come. 117

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Appendix The Antichrist: One Man or a System? Introduction The concept of the Antichrist has long been a subject of fascination and speculation within Christian eschatology. For many, the term evokes images of a single, future world ruler who will rise to power during the end times, leading a global rebellion against God. However, as scholars such as N.T. Wright, Lynn Hiles, and Harold Eberle point out, a more nuanced biblical understanding of the Antichrist reveals that this figure might not be a single individual but a spirit or system at work in the world. This exploration seeks to examine the concept of the Antichrist in light of biblical passages and the theological perspectives of respected scholars, ultimately proposing that the Antichrist is not just an individual but also a spiritual or systemic force in opposition to Christ and His Kingdom. 1. The Antichrist in 1 John: A Systemic View The Apostle John provides significant insight into the nature of the Antichrist in his epistles, particularly in 1 John 2:18, 22, 1 John 4:3, and 2 John 7. John’s writings present a very different perspective from the traditional one-man Antichrist theory. In 1 John 2:18, John writes “Children, it is the last hour; and as you have heard that the Antichrist is coming, even now many Antichrists have come, by which we know that it is the last hour.” Here, John’s statement indicates that Antichrist is not just a future figure but many individuals or forces that oppose Christ. The use of "many Antichrists" suggests a broader, systemic manifestation of Antichrist rather than a single, future individual. In 1 John 2:22, John continues: 118

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: “Who is a liar but he who denies that Jesus is the Christ? He is Antichrist who denies the Father and the Son.” Here, Antichrist is not described as a world ruler, but as anyone who denies the core truth of the Incarnation: that Jesus is the Christ. This denial is seen as the antagonistic spirit of the Antichrist, which works systematically to undermine the fundamental doctrines of the faith. N.T. Wright in his book The New Testament and the People of God affirms that the Antichrist spirit is not limited to one individual but is seen in a broader cultural and ideological framework that consistently opposes Christ’s redemptive work. Similarly, in 1 John 4:3, John writes: “And every spirit that does not confess that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh is not of God. And this is the spirit of the Antichrist, which you have heard was coming, and is now already in the world.” This passage again reveals that the Antichrist is present in the world, operating through spirits and ideologies that deny the truth of Jesus’ divinity and humanity. The Antichrist spirit is not bound to one individual but can manifest in a variety of ways in false teachings, deceptive philosophies, and cultural movements that seek to undermine Christ’s role as the Savior. Here, John emphasizes that the Antichrist is already at work in the present age. Finally, in 2 John 7, he states: “For many deceivers have gone out into the world who do not confess Jesus Christ as coming in the flesh. This is a deceiver and an antichrist.” John’s warnings are systemic in nature, highlighting that the Antichrist spirit is not confined to one person but is embodied in any movement or teaching that denies the central truth of Christ’s Incarnation. This is not just 119

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: a future event but something that Christians need to recognize and resist in their present context. 2. The Antichrist and Its Systemic Nature According to N.T. Wright, the Antichrist cannot be understood simply as an isolated figure at the end of time. Rather, it represents a larger systemic force in the world, an ideological and spiritual anti-Christ that works through various systems, both religious and secular, to oppose God’s Kingdom. Wright argues that many forms of false religion, idolatry, and worldly systems can embody the spirit of the Antichrist, making it a pervasive force throughout history, rather than something only to be expected in the future. Lynn Hiles echoes this perspective, emphasizing that the Antichrist is not limited to a single figure but rather manifests as a systemic force that seeks to lead people away from the truth of Christ. He states that the Antichrist spirit works in every cultural, political, and religious sphere that seeks to replace Christ as the center of human life and history. This anti-Christ spirit operates in all systems of power, governance, and belief systems that seek to substitute Christ with human-made ideologies. Harold Eberle similarly teaches that the Antichrist should be seen as a principle of opposition to the Kingdom of God rather than a single, identifiable figure. Eberle suggests that the Antichrist spirit works through the cultural systems of the world to oppose the message of the Kingdom, making it essential for Christians to remain vigilant and discerning in their engagement with the world. 3. Theological Implications: Antichrist as a Spirit and a System The Antichrist spirit, therefore, is not merely a future event but something that has already taken root in the world.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: remain vigilant and discerning in their engagement with the world. 3. Theological Implications: Antichrist as a Spirit and a System The Antichrist spirit, therefore, is not merely a future event but something that has already taken root in the world. The biblical writers were deeply concerned with how worldly systems, false religions, and even secular ideologies work to deny the authority of Christ and undermine His reign. 120

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: This makes the Antichrist more than just a political figure or a distant prophecy; it is a real force at work today, often manifested in the subtle influences of culture, politics, and even personal ideologies. For example, secular humanism, which denies God’s sovereignty and promotes a self-centered worldview, could be seen as one such manifestation of the Antichrist system. The growing trend of moral relativism and cultural shifts that seek to define truth apart from Christ’s revelation are also evidence of the Antichrist spirit at work in the world. In this context, 1 John 2:18-22 becomes particularly relevant, as John’s warnings serve as a call to discernment and faithfulness in a world filled with antagonistic ideologies. 4. Practical Implications for the Believer Understanding the Antichrist as a system rather than merely an individual has significant implications for the Christian life: ● Discernment : Christians must cultivate discernment in order to recognize the Antichrist spirit in the world. As N.T. Wright points out, this requires an understanding of Scripture and the ability to identify false ideologies that contradict the truth of Christ’s Lordship. ● Cultural Engagement : Recognizing the Antichrist system calls Christians to engage culture not with fear or retreat but with a clear vision of Christ’s supremacy over all things. This means that Christians must actively work to bring God’s Kingdom to bear in their communities, addressing the idolatry and injustice prevalent in society. ● Spiritual Warfare : As Lynn Hiles emphasizes, engaging with the Antichrist spirit requires Christians to embrace the full armor of God and fight against the forces of darkness. This includes standing firm in the truth of the Gospel, resisting false teachings, and living 121

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: out the truth in a world that constantly challenges the authority of Christ. ● Living in Hope : Finally, understanding the Antichrist spirit helps believers to live with hope and confidence in Christ’s ultimate victory. As Harold Eberle reminds us, the Kingdom of God will ultimately triumph over every Antichrist system, and believers can live in anticipation of Christ’s return, when He will fully establish His reign. Conclusion The Antichrist is not simply a future individual or a one-time event; it is a systemic force that is already at work in the world. By recognizing the Antichrist spirit as a pervasive influence in culture, politics, and religion, Christians can develop a biblical understanding of how to engage with the world in ways that honor Christ’s authority and advance His Kingdom. As N.T. Wright, Lynn Hiles, and Harold Eberle all teach, the Antichrist system is not to be feared, but resisted through faithfulness, discernment, and hope in the coming of Christ’s eternal Kingdom. 122

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Appendix: 666 and Nero Caesar: Contextual Arguments The number 666, often associated with the Mark of the Beast in Revelation 13:18, has been a subject of much speculation and interpretation throughout history. While the traditional view tends to identify this number with a future, singular Antichrist figure, a growing body of scholars, especially within the frameworks of preterism and historical interpretation, proposes that 666 is most directly linked to Nero Caesar, the infamous Roman emperor who reigned from 54 to 68 A.D. This appendix explores contextual arguments that suggest Nero is the immediate fulfillment of the 666 prophecy, grounding this interpretation within the first-century historical context of the book of Revelation. 1. The Historical Context of Revelation and Nero Caesar The Book of Revelation, written by the apostle John during the reign of Domitian (around 95-96 A.D.), is often understood as a prophetic critique of Roman imperialism and its persecution of early Christians. The book is apocalyptic in genre, using vivid imagery, symbols, and numbers to convey its message. The number 666 appears specifically in Revelation 13:18, where it is described as the number of a man, a cryptic reference that many scholars believe points to a specific historical figure: Nero Caesar. 2. Numerical Gematria: The Key to Understanding 666 The use of numbers as symbols is a hallmark of Jewish apocalyptic literature, and the number 666 is commonly understood as a form of gematria, a system of assigning numerical values to letters in a word. In the ancient world, this was a common practice for encoding secret meanings. In Hebrew and Greek (the original languages of much of the Bible), the letters of "Nero Caesar" can be numerically calculated to total 666.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: al values to letters in a word. In the ancient world, this was a common practice for encoding secret meanings. In Hebrew and Greek (the original languages of much of the Bible), the letters of "Nero Caesar" can be numerically calculated to total 666. Here’s how the gematria works: 123

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: ● Nero Caesar in Hebrew is written as נרו קסר Neron Kesar). ● When these letters are assigned numerical values according to the system of gematria, the sum of the letters adds up to 666. This provides a strong contextual argument that 666 refers directly to Nero Caesar, an emperor who was both despised and feared by early Christians for his brutal persecution of believers. He is often remembered for his role in the great fire of Rome in 64 A.D. and for later blaming Christians for the fire, leading to widespread persecution and martyrdom. 3. Nero and the Beast In Revelation 13, the beast is described as having a blasphemous mouth and a persecuting role against God’s people. This imagery strongly parallels Nero’s reign, during which he persecuted Christians, forced them into public spectacles, and even used them as human torches to light his garden at night. Nero’s connection to the beast also aligns with the description of the beast as being both a political figure and one associated with the Roman Empire’s oppressive power. In the context of early Christianity, the Roman Empire itself was often portrayed as the agent of evil in the world, and Nero, as one of its most notorious emperors, became the embodiment of imperial oppression. 4. The Early Christian Interpretation of 666 Early Christian writings, including the works of Irenaeus, Hippolytus, and Tertullian, also make connections between the Antichrist and Nero Caesar. ● Irenaeus (c. 130-202 A.D.) is one of the earliest Christian writers who explicitly identified the number 666 with Nero. In his work Against Heresies , he writes that the name of the beast, when calculated in Greek, comes to 666. His writings reflect the 124

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: post-tribulational view of the Book of Revelation, where the prophetic imagery has immediate relevance to Christians experiencing persecution under Nero and other Roman emperors. ● Hippolytus (c. 170-235 A.D.), another early Christian scholar, also made similar associations, interpreting the beast of Revelation as symbolic of Nero. His writings suggest that Nero was viewed as a prefiguration of the final Antichrist who would rise at the end of the age. These early Church Fathers understood the Apocalypse not as a distant prophecy about the end of the world, but rather as a timely message for their generation, a call to persevere in the face of Roman oppression. This perspective is in line with a preterist reading of Revelation, which suggests that much of the book’s content was fulfilled in the first century, during the fall of Jerusalem and the Roman persecutions of Christians. 5. The Symbolic Meaning of 666 While 666 is often taken as a literal number, it is also important to consider its symbolic significance within the context of Jewish numerology. In the Jewish tradition, the number 6 is seen as the number of imperfection (since 7 is considered the number of completion or perfection). The number 666 thus becomes a symbol of complete imperfection or human failure, a fitting description of Nero, whose reign was marked by cruelty, tyranny, and corruption. This interpretation finds further support in the Revelation’s depiction of the beast as a corrupt, human-driven empire that ultimately falls. 666 becomes the number of the fallen world system, which, while powerful for a time, is destined to be replaced by the perfect reign of Christ. 125

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: 6. The Legacy of Nero and the Antichrist While Nero was certainly the primary fulfillment of the 666 prophecy in the first century, many interpreters also see in his reign a pattern that will be repeated in later figures. The spirit of Nero (symbolized by 666) is not limited to him alone but is present throughout history in tyrants and systems that persecute God’s people. For instance, many see the rise of totalitarian regimes in the 20th century, such as those of Hitler and Stalin, as manifestations of the same Antichrist spirit that was seen in Nero. In this sense, the 666 number is not merely a historical reference to one man but also serves as a timeless symbol for the ongoing struggle between the kingdom of God and the forces of evil that work through human systems. 7. Implications for Christians Today Understanding 666 in relation to Nero Caesar provides valuable context for interpreting the Book of Revelation and biblical prophecy as a whole. It challenges Christians to recognize that persecution and evil systems are not just future concerns but are ongoing realities in the present. The beast system is not confined to one future Antichrist figure but is alive and well in the world today, often manifesting in unjust political systems, false ideologies, and persecutory regimes. For believers today, this understanding of 666 as Nero serves as both a warning and a hope. It is a warning that evil systems will continue to rise against God’s people, but it is also a message of hope, because Christ’s victory over Nero and imperial oppression was fully realized in the first century, and believers can trust in the ultimate triumph of Christ over all forces of evil. 126

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Conclusion The connection between 666 and Nero Caesar provides a historical, contextual understanding of Revelation’s apocalyptic message. By grounding this interpretation in the first-century context of Roman persecution, we gain a deeper appreciation for the timeless relevance of the message of Revelation. The number 666 not only symbolizes the fallen world systems that oppose Christ but also serves as a reminder of God’s sovereignty, His triumph over evil, and the assurance of His eventual victory. The Antichrist system, whether in Nero’s day or in our own, will ultimately be overcome by the Kingdom of God. 127

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Appendix Understanding the Millennium Clarifying the Controversy for Everyday Believers Few passages in the Bible generate as much conversation, and confusion, as Revelation 20, where we read about a “thousand-year reign.” This mysterious reference, often called the Millennium, has become a centerpiece for different end-time interpretations. What does it really mean? Should we take it literally? Is it still ahead of us, or are we living in it now? This appendix offers a clear, non-technical guide to the major perspectives Christians have held, why they differ, and what we can be sure of, even when details vary. Why Does the Millennium Matter? The Millennium isn’t just a trivia topic for prophecy enthusiasts. It touches on how we see the present reign of Christ, the mission of the Church, and the hope of the world. Some believe it describes a future golden age of peace. Others see it as symbolic of the age we live in now, a time when Jesus reigns through His people. Understanding the range of views can help believers stay focused on what matters most: living in the victory of Christ, both now and forever. 128

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Four Major Views at a Glance: So... What’s the Thousand Years About? Revelation 20 speaks of a “thousand years”, a number often taken literally. But in apocalyptic literature (like Revelation), numbers are usually symbolic. The number 1,000 in Scripture suggests fullness, completion, and divine purpose, not necessarily a calendar span. 129 View Key Idea When Does Jesus Return? What Is the Millennium? Dispensational Premillennialism Jesus will return before a literal 1,000-year reign on earth. Before the Millennium A future, physical kingdom on earth after Jesus returns. Historic Premillennialism Similar to the above but without the “rapture” idea. Before the Millennium A future reign of Christ on earth, following a time of tribulation. Postmillennialism The Gospel will bring a period of peace and righteousness across the earth before Jesus returns. After the Millennium A golden age of Gospel influence in history. Amillennialism The “1,000 years” is a symbolic number for the present reign of Christ through the Church. After a symbolic Millennium A spiritual, heavenly reign already in progress.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Just as the beast and the dragon in Revelation aren’t literal creatures, the “thousand years” isn’t necessarily a countdown clock. Instead, it pictures a complete period of Christ’s victorious reign. Some Christians believe this reign began with Christ’s resurrection and continues until the final judgment. Others expect it to begin after Jesus returns. Either way, the emphasis is not on how long it lasts, but who is reigning, and how His reign transforms the world. Is the Millennium Infinite or Temporary? Another question that often arises: “If Christ’s Kingdom has no end (Isaiah 9:7), how can it last just 1,000 years?” This is where it’s helpful to remember: the Bible often uses symbolic language to describe spiritual truths. The Millennium in Revelation 20 likely represents a definite, God-ordained season, not because Jesus’ reign is limited, but to emphasize a divinely measured period of victory, restraint of evil, and authority. Ultimately, Scripture teaches that Christ’s reign is eternal. The “1,000 years” may simply mark a chapter within that eternal rule, a chapter that reveals His justice, vindicates His people, and prepares for the final resurrection and judgment. Voices that Help Us See Clearly Several modern Christian thinkers have helped believers reclaim a hopeful and victorious vision of the end: ● N.T. Wright emphasizes that the New Testament isn’t focused on the end of the world, but on God’s renewal of all things through the risen Christ. For Wright, Jesus is already reigning, and His resurrection launched a new creation. 130

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: ● Lynn Hiles highlights the reality of the New Covenant, not as something we await, but as a present spiritual reality that fulfills what the Old Covenant pointed toward. For him, the “Millennium” is not a pause between events, but an unfolding of Christ’s triumph in us. ● Harold Eberle teaches that much of Revelation describes events that have already happened, especially in the first century. He presents a compelling case that we are not waiting for Christ’s Kingdom to begin, we’re already living in it. The Heart of the Matter Whatever one’s millennial view, all Bible-believing Christians affirm that: ● Jesus reigns as Lord and King. ● His victory over sin and death is complete and eternal. ● His return will bring final judgment and full renewal. ● Until then, we are called to live in His victory and spread His Kingdom in word and deed. Whether the Millennium is symbolic of the Church age or a future season, the message remains the same: “Of the increase of His government and peace there shall be no end…” - Isaiah 9:7 Final Thought: Keep the Main Thing, the Main Thing The Millennium is not just about prophecy, it’s about perspective. Do we live like Jesus is reigning now? Are we aware of His present victory, or are we waiting for something He has already accomplished? 131

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Let this truth anchor your heart: Jesus is not coming to take sides, He’s coming to take over. And in many ways, He already has. 132

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Appendix Understanding the "End Times" - Exposition of Matthew 24 Matthew 24:1-2 begins with Jesus leaving the temple and predicting its destruction. His disciples point out the grandeur of the buildings, but Jesus declares, "There shall not be left here one stone upon another, that shall not be thrown down." This prophecy was fulfilled in AD 70 when the Romans, under General Titus, destroyed Jerusalem and the temple. Josephus, a first-century Jewish historian, records the extensive destruction, aligning with Jesus' words. This was not merely a physical event but carried theological significance. The destruction symbolized the end of the Old Covenant age and the judgment upon Israel for rejecting their Messiah, echoing themes found in Matthew 23. Verses 3-14 contain the disciples' question on the Mount of Olives: "Tell us, when shall these things be? and what shall be the sign of thy coming, and of the end of the world?" In context, "end of the world" (Greek: aion) is better understood as "end of the age." Jesus answers their questions with signs that would take place before the end of the Jewish age and the destruction of the temple: deception by false Christs, wars and rumors of wars, famines, pestilences, earthquakes, persecution of believers, apostasy, and the global proclamation of the gospel. These were all fulfilled in the generation leading up to AD 70. Eusebius, the early Church historian, and other records corroborate that Christians in Judea experienced severe persecution, and historical accounts by Tacitus and Josephus document the famines and natural disasters of that period. Verses 15-22 speak of the "abomination of desolation" spoken of by Daniel. In first-century context, this referred to the desecration of the temple by Roman armies.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: ts by Tacitus and Josephus document the famines and natural disasters of that period. Verses 15-22 speak of the "abomination of desolation" spoken of by Daniel. In first-century context, this referred to the desecration of the temple by Roman armies. Luke’s parallel passage (Luke 21:20–21) identifies the sign more clearly: "When ye shall see Jerusalem compassed with armies, then know that the desolation thereof is nigh." Early Christians heeded this warning and fled to Pella, a city in the Decapolis, 133

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: avoiding the destruction. The phrase "let those who are in Judea flee to the mountains" underscores that this was a localized judgment, not a global catastrophe. The urgency reflects the severe tribulation that befell Jerusalem, as detailed by Josephus in The Wars of the Jews . Verses 23-28 warn against false messiahs and prophets performing signs to deceive, if possible, the elect. These deceptions occurred during the Jewish-Roman War, when various leaders claimed to be deliverers. The imagery of lightning in verse 27 emphasizes the swiftness and visibility of Christ’s coming in judgment upon Jerusalem. It was not a secret event, but an unmistakable act of divine retribution. Verses 29-31 use apocalyptic language typical of Old Testament prophetic texts. The sun being darkened, the moon not giving its light, and stars falling from heaven symbolize the collapse of political and religious powers, not literal cosmic events. This language mirrors Isaiah 13, Ezekiel 32, and Joel 2. Verse 30's "sign of the Son of man in heaven" likely refers to the vindication of Christ’s authority. The phrase "coming in the clouds" is a theophanic image drawn from Daniel 7:13–14, where the Son of Man comes to the Ancient of Days to receive dominion. Verse 31’s trumpet call and gathering of the elect signifies the worldwide spread of the gospel and the inclusion of the Gentiles into the covenant community, echoing Isaiah 27:13. Verses 32-35 contain the parable of the fig tree, emphasizing that the generation then living would witness these things. The phrase "this generation shall not pass, till all these things be fulfilled" is central to a preterist understanding.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: 27:13. Verses 32-35 contain the parable of the fig tree, emphasizing that the generation then living would witness these things. The phrase "this generation shall not pass, till all these things be fulfilled" is central to a preterist understanding. Jesus was assuring His disciples that the events, including the destruction of the temple and Jerusalem, would happen within their lifetime, which they did. Verses 36-41 speak of the unexpectedness of the coming judgment. "But of that day and hour knoweth no man" refers to the specific timing of the destruction. The comparison to the days of Noah underscores the 134

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: suddenness and the unpreparedness of many. The images of "two in the field" and "two grinding at the mill" being separated refer to judgment and deliverance, not a secret rapture. Verses 42-51 exhort vigilance and faithful stewardship. Jesus warns that His coming in judgment would be sudden, and His followers must be watchful and faithful. The faithful servant is contrasted with the evil servant who delays the master’s return and mistreats others. The judgment he receives reflects the consequences of unfaithfulness. Throughout Matthew 24, Jesus uses prophetic language to describe the imminent judgment on Jerusalem and the transition from the Old Covenant to the New. Early Christian writers such as Eusebius interpreted the passage as fulfilled in the events of AD 70. Modern scholars such as R. C. Sproul, N. T. Wright, and Kenneth Gentry support this preterist or partial-preterist view, which sees the passage not as about the end of the physical world, but as the end of the Jewish age and the vindication of Jesus as the true Messiah and Judge. This interpretation preserves the immediacy and relevance of Jesus' words to His original audience and frames eschatology not as a distant, fear-inducing event, but as the unfolding of God's redemptive history, already inaugurated in Christ's resurrection and continuing through His reign in the Church. 135

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Appendix Exposition of 2 Peter 3 2 Peter 3 is a rich eschatological passage that must be read in its immediate literary context and broader biblical theology. The chapter deals with the coming of the Lord and the end of the age, centering on God’s faithfulness, the scoffing of unbelievers, divine patience, and the ultimate renewal of creation. A Christ-centered exposition reveals its harmony with other New Testament teachings on judgment, redemption, and cosmic renewal. Verses 1–2: Peter opens by reminding his readers of his earlier epistle, written to stir up their sincere minds. He urges them to remember the words of the prophets and the commandment of the apostles, thus establishing continuity between the Old and New Testament witness. The emphasis on remembrance is a pastoral call to remain grounded in the Scriptures amid rising skepticism and doctrinal error. Verses 3–7: Peter then introduces scoffers who mock the promise of Christ’s coming. These scoffers argue from an apparent uniformity in nature, saying, “All things continue as they were from the beginning” (v. 4). Peter refutes them by pointing to God's past judgment by water in the flood (v. 6), showing that divine intervention in history is not only possible but has precedent. He then speaks of a coming judgment by fire (v. 7). The reference to fire here must be read in light of prophetic-apocalyptic language. Fire consistently symbolizes purifying judgment (cf. Malachi 3:2-3; Isaiah 4:4), not annihilation. Church Fathers such as Origen and Gregory of Nyssa often viewed such fire as purgative and transformational, aligned with God's redemptive character. Verses 8–9: Peter now explains the perceived delay in Christ’s coming. Quoting Psalm 136

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: 90:4, he affirms God’s transcendence over time. The delay is not a sign of slackness but of mercy: “not willing that any should perish but that all should come to repentance.” This is a strong affirmation of the divine desire for redemption over destruction. Verses 10–13: This section contains some of the most debated phrases. Verse 10 states, “The day of the Lord will come as a thief... the heavens shall pass away... and the elements shall melt with fervent heat.” The Greek word stoicheia ("elements") has sparked interpretive controversy. While it can mean physical components of the cosmos, it is also used in Pauline literature (Galatians 4:3, 9; Colossians 2:8, 20) to refer to elemental spiritual principles or the rudimentary religious systems of the old covenant world. Many scholars, including N.T. Wright and R.T. France, note that stoicheia in 2 Peter is best understood in continuity with its Pauline usage: referring not to atoms and molecules, but to the foundational structures of the old age,religious, political, and spiritual systems now obsolete in light of the new covenant. Hebrews 8:13 supports this: “In that he saith, A new covenant, he hath made the first old. Now that which decayeth and waxeth old is ready to vanish away.” Furthermore, Peter's allusion to the days of Noah is crucial. Just as the world in Noah’s day was judged and the ungodly were taken away (cf. Matthew 24:38–39), so also shall it be in the day of the Lord,not the destruction of the physical cosmos, but the removal of the ungodly and the systems aligned against God’s kingdom. Verse 13 balances the imagery with hope: “We, according to his promise, look for new heavens and a new earth, wherein dwelleth righteousness.” This echoes Isaiah 65:17 and Revelation 21:1.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: of the ungodly and the systems aligned against God’s kingdom. Verse 13 balances the imagery with hope: “We, according to his promise, look for new heavens and a new earth, wherein dwelleth righteousness.” This echoes Isaiah 65:17 and Revelation 21:1. The new creation here is not replacement but renewal, palingenesia (Matthew 19:28),the regeneration of creation itself. Early theologians like Irenaeus and Justin Martyr taught that 137

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: the new earth would be a renewed, restored creation, not a disembodied heaven or annihilated cosmos. Verses 14–16: Peter exhorts believers to live holy and blameless lives in light of the coming day. He also refers to Paul's writings as Scripture, though he acknowledges that some parts are “hard to be understood.” This remark confirms both the apostolic unity and the theological depth of eschatological teachings. Verses 17–18: The epistle concludes with a pastoral exhortation: beware of error, and grow in the grace and knowledge of Jesus Christ. For Peter, eschatology is not about speculative timelines but transformed lives. The expectation of Christ’s return fuels holiness, not fear. Summary: 2 Peter 3 does not predict the annihilation of the physical universe but proclaims the judgment and renewal of creation through God's righteous intervention. The elements (stoicheia) are not merely physical particles but the elemental structures of the old covenant world, now being dismantled in the light of Christ’s kingdom. As in the days of Noah, it is not creation that is destroyed, but the ungodly who are taken away. The “new heavens and new earth” speak of a creation redeemed and inhabited by righteousness, reflecting the triumph of God’s covenantal faithfulness through Jesus Christ. 138

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Appendix Exposition of 1 Thessalonians 4:13-18 "Caught Up Together" - A Victorious Eschatology In 1 Thessalonians 4:13-18, Paul provides profound insight into the future hope of believers, particularly concerning the second coming of Christ. The Thessalonians, deeply concerned about their fellow believers who had died, questioned whether these departed Christians would miss out on Christ’s return. Paul addresses their fears by teaching them about the resurrection of the dead and the glorious transformation of the living believers at the return of Christ. Context of 1 Thessalonians 4:13-18 The Thessalonian believers, eager to understand the nature of the resurrection and the return of Christ, had several concerns. They feared that their loved ones who had already passed away in Christ might be left behind when Christ returned. Paul’s response, recorded in this passage, is not merely an explanation but an assurance of victory over death and a comforting hope for the church. Exposition of 1 Thessalonians 4:13-18 Verses 13-14: The Hope of Resurrection Paul begins by clarifying that the Christian’s hope is rooted in the resurrection of Jesus Christ (v. 14). Resurrection is not merely an abstract theological concept but a living hope made possible by Christ’s victory over death. As Jesus was raised from the dead, so too will those who have died in Christ be raised to life. The resurrection is the key to understanding our eternal destiny, as the early church father Irenaeus wrote: "The resurrection of the dead is the hope which has been taught to the faithful from the beginning" (Against Heresies, Book V). This resurrection ensures that those who have died in Christ will not be left behind when Christ 139

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: returns, but rather will be the first to experience the newness of life in His presence. Verses 15-17: The Parousia and the "Caught Up" Event Paul then describes the parousia, the glorious and visible return of Christ to earth. The word “parousia” in Greek refers to the arrival of a king, and it was a familiar term in the ancient world, used to describe the visit of a dignitary or ruler. When the king arrived at the city’s border, his people would go out to meet him and escort him back into the city in a procession. Similarly, Christ’s return will not be a secret but a public and victorious event, and believers will meet Him at the border of His kingdom,in the air,to welcome Him. The phrase “caught up” (Greek: harpagēsometha ) in verse 17 refers not to believers being taken away to another realm but to their transformation in the presence of Christ as they are united with Him and one another in a glorious procession of victory. Augustine of Hippo commented that “the saints are caught up not to escape from earth but to join the procession in honor of Christ’s victory” (City of God, Book XX). This meeting with Christ in the air is not an escape but a moment of spiritual transformation and victorious unity with the glorified Christ. The “twinkling of an eye” moment described in 1 Corinthians 15:52 further illustrates this instantaneous transformation of believers, where we will be changed and caught up to meet Christ in a moment of glorious anticipation. The “caught up” event marks the believers’ complete and full transformation, as they are made fit for eternity and united with Christ in the final stages of the kingdom of God on earth.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: d caught up to meet Christ in a moment of glorious anticipation. The “caught up” event marks the believers’ complete and full transformation, as they are made fit for eternity and united with Christ in the final stages of the kingdom of God on earth. The Parousia as the Triumph of Christ The essence of Paul’s teaching in these verses is not about the believers being removed from the earth but about their participation in Christ's triumph over death, sin, and the world. This interpretation aligns with the understanding of Christ’s second coming as a victorious event, not one of 140

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: fear or retreat. The idea of Christ's return as a victory procession has roots in early Christian thought, such as the writings of Tertullian, who emphasized that the Parousia is a triumphal procession where believers march in victory with Christ into the eternal kingdom (Apology, Chapter 50). The return of Christ, therefore, represents the final realization of God’s eternal purpose, where believers are caught up together to welcome the King, not to be removed from the earth but to join in His reign as joint-heirs. The meeting in the air is the moment of final victory, where death is swallowed up in life, and Christ establishes His kingdom fully. Verse 18: Comfort and Encouragement The final verse of this passage encourages the believers to comfort one another with these words. The hope of Christ's return is a source of great comfort for believers facing persecution, suffering, or death. The return of Christ brings the assurance of ultimate restoration, where believers will experience not only resurrection but the glory of reigning with Christ forever. As the church father Cyprian wrote: “Let us take comfort in the promise of the resurrection, for through it we shall be victorious with Christ, our King” (Treatises, 6). This comfort is not merely theoretical but practical: It calls believers to live with the hope of Christ’s return, trusting that His victory is certain and that we will share in that victory. Christ-Centered Implications of This Passage 1. Christ as the Central Figure in the Hope of Resurrection ○ The hope of resurrection is tied to Christ’s own resurrection. Just as He was raised from the dead, so too will believers be raised to share in His victory over death.

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: ions of This Passage 1. Christ as the Central Figure in the Hope of Resurrection ○ The hope of resurrection is tied to Christ’s own resurrection. Just as He was raised from the dead, so too will believers be raised to share in His victory over death. Christ is the firstfruits of the resurrection, and through Him, we inherit eternal life (1 Corinthians 15:20). His victory over death 141

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: ensures that believers will not be defeated by death but will share in His triumph. 2. The Role of the Church in Christ’s Return ○ The Church is called to participate in Christ's triumph. The moment of being caught up to meet Christ in the air is a celebration of the believers’ victory in Christ. The church is not waiting to escape the world but to witness Christ’s triumph and usher Him into His eternal reign. This aligns with the teaching of John Chrysostom, who noted that Christ’s return is a victory procession where believers join in celebrating His kingdom’s final establishment (Homilies on 1 Thessalonians, 8). 3. The Comfort of the Believer ○ The ultimate comfort in this passage is the certainty of Christ's return and the fullness of His kingdom. This comfort is not based on escaping suffering but on the promise of victory. As theologian N.T. Wright points out, the Christian hope is not an escape from the world but the transformation of the world through the victory of Christ’s kingdom (Surprised by Hope). Conclusion In conclusion, 1 Thessalonians 4:13-18 provides believers with a hope that is centered on the victorious return of Christ. The moment when believers are “caught up” to meet the Lord is not about escaping the earth but about participating in Christ’s triumph as He returns to establish His eternal reign. This event marks the consummation of the kingdom of God, where believers will be transformed and united with Christ forever. The comfort we derive from this passage is not only in the future promise of resurrection but also in the present reality of participating in Christ’s 142

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: victory. This hope compels us to live boldly in light of the coming kingdom, trusting that the King will return in glory and victory. 143

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Appendix Joint Heirs with Christ A Christ-Centered View of Our Inheritance The doctrine of being "joint-heirs with Christ" (Romans 8:17) is a profound theological truth that speaks to the believer’s union with Christ in His reign, glory, and inheritance. Unlike the idea of co-heirs, which implies divided portions, joint-heirs signifies shared and equal participation in the full inheritance that belongs to Christ. "And if children, then heirs; heirs of God, and joint-heirs with Christ; if so be that we suffer with him, that we may be also glorified together."- Romans 8:17 (KJV) This glorious union reveals that the believer is not merely an adopted member of God’s household but is positioned to share equally in the Son’s inheritance. As the Bride of Christ, the Church is invited into full participation in the Kingdom, not as servants receiving wages, but as sons and daughters receiving a legacy. Theological and Historical Insights 1. John Calvin (Reformer) “Christ has nothing distinct from His people, for they are made one with Him. All that He has, He shares with them… not as a portion, but as a joint possession.” - Commentary on Romans 8:17 2. Matthew Henry (Puritan commentator) “They are joint-heirs with Christ, not merely co-heirs receiving different lots, but inheriting with Him, in Him, and through Him; for He is the heir of all things.” 144

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: 3. N.T. Wright (Contemporary New Testament scholar) “To be a joint-heir with Christ is to share in His vocation as the true Son of God, to take up the renewed image-bearing role of Adam, now fulfilled in Christ.” - Paul and the Faithfulness of God 4. Origen of Alexandria (Church Father, 3rd Century) “He who is united with Christ becomes as one with Him, not in essence, but in dignity and participation in the divine inheritance.” 5. Augustine of Hippo (Church Father) "He is the heir because He is the Son by nature; we are heirs because we are sons by adoption. Yet we inherit not less than He because we are made one with Him." 6. John Piper (Reformed Pastor and Author) “Being a joint-heir means that the inheritance God gives to His Son, everything, is shared with us. It is not a pie that gets cut into pieces; we all inherit the whole.” Scriptural Foundation ● Hebrews 1:2 – "...whom he hath appointed heir of all things, by whom also he made the worlds." ● Psalm 2:8 – "Ask of me, and I shall give thee the heathen for thine inheritance, and the uttermost parts of the earth for thy possession." ● Romans 8:17 – "And if children, then heirs; heirs of God, and joint-heirs with Christ..." 145

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: ● Ephesians 1:11 – "In whom also we have obtained an inheritance…" ● Revelation 3:21 – "To him that overcometh will I grant to sit with me in my throne…" ● 1 Corinthians 3:21-23 – “All things are yours… and ye are Christ’s; and Christ is God’s.” Joint Heirs vs. Co-Heirs ● Co-heirs suggests a division of inheritance, like siblings dividing property. ● Joint-heirs (Greek: synklēronomoi ) indicates shared ownership and equal access . The entire inheritance belongs fully to Christ and fully to us in Him ,without diminishing either party’s possession. Illustration: If Christ receives the crown, we don’t get a piece of it,we get to wear the crown with Him (Rev. 3:21). His throne becomes our throne because we are His Body. In Roman law, to be a joint-heir ( heres in solidum ) meant the entire inheritance was entrusted to each party fully. Likewise, our inheritance in Christ is not subdivided, but whole and complete, given to each believer in union with the glorified Christ. Kingdom Implications As joint-heirs: ● We share in His suffering now (Romans 8:17) and will share in His glory later. ● We will reign with Him (2 Timothy 2:12; Revelation 5:10). ● We partake in the new creation (Romans 8:19-21). 146

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: ● We inherit eternal life , righteousness , and the world to come . Our sonship grants us legal standing; our union with Christ grants us relational intimacy and royal participation. The earth was given to Christ as His possession (Psalm 2:8), and now, in Christ, it becomes our shared inheritance, not to exploit but to steward and rule under His Lordship. Conclusion Being joint-heirs with Christ means we share in all that He has received from the Father. This includes authority, glory, inheritance, and ultimately, dominion over the new creation. It is a staggering expression of grace and union with Christ. Our inheritance is secure, complete, and eternal because it is rooted in Christ Himself, the rightful Heir of all things. This doctrine is more than encouragement, it is empowerment. It shapes how we live, suffer, serve, and reign. We are heirs not of temporal things only, but of eternal realities. Let us therefore live with confidence, humility, and purpose, knowing we are heirs of a Kingdom that cannot be shaken. 147

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: Closing Exhortation Beholding the Lamb, Reigning with Boldness, Walking in the Power of Soteria We have not believed cleverly devised myths. We have not gathered around fables of fear or timelines of terror. We have beheld the Lamb. Christ Jesus , crucified, risen, ascended, and enthroned, is not waiting to be crowned. He reigns now, and we are seated with Him. He is not trying to secure victory; He has already triumphed. SOTERIA, this great salvation, is more than our rescue; it is our restoration, our inheritance, and our participation in divine purpose. Once foretold by prophets, Now fulfilled in Christ, And forever to be glorified in the saints. So let us walk worthy of this salvation, not with hesitation, but with boldness, with hearts purified by truth and feet prepared by peace. Let us declare with our lives what our doctrine proclaims: The Kingdom has come, is coming, and shall have no end. Reject the paralysis of waiting for what has already begun. Refuse the fear that keeps you bound to shadows. Receive the invitation to reign in life through One, Jesus Christ (Romans 5:17). Look again to the Lamb, not just as the One who died, but the One who lives, who leads, and who loves through us. And as we behold Him, we become like Him, full of grace, full of glory, and fearless in the face of every age. 148

title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: “So great salvation” (Hebrews 2:3) is not behind us or ahead of us only, It is within us, upon us, and working through us. This is our hope. This is our heritage. This is the hour to walk in it. Amen. Come, Lord Jesus, in fullness, in power, in us. 149
