title: Soteria & Victorious Eschatology_
text: About the Book Soteria & Victorious Eschatology: Living in Christ’s Victory Today and Forever is the third volume in the Soteria series, a journey that began with the wonder of our sonship and redemption, moved through the beauty of rest and divine rhythm, and now arrives at the glorious horizon of our salvation’s consummation. This volume is deeply personal for me. It was born out of both a deep study of Scripture and a season of pastoral shaking, when teaching about victorious eschatology brought clarity to some and conflict to others. Yet, in the midst of the tension, something unshakable was forged in me: a commitment to proclaim a Christ-centered, hope-filled view of the end, one that magnifies Jesus, not fear. In these pages, I invite you to rethink what you’ve been taught about “the last days.” This is not a book of gloom and escape but of glory and engagement. We will explore how the great salvation, Soteria , is not only personal but cosmic, not only past and future, but powerfully present. This book is for anyone who: ● Has wrestled with confusing or fear-based end-time teachings, ● Desires to unlearn limiting narratives and rediscover the victorious gospel, ● Longs to live confidently in the now-and-forever triumph of Christ. Whether you're a theologian, a pastor, or a passionate believer hungry for more, my hope is that this book will strengthen your heart, sharpen your understanding, and stir your affections for Jesus, the Savior, the King, and the fulfillment of all things. This isn’t just theology. It’s a call to live the fulfilled life, in Christ’s victory, now and forever. 153

title: Statement of Faith
text: Statement of Faith and Theological Convictions About Pastor Daniel Folarin Pastor Daniel Folarin is a Christ-centered pastor , teacher , author , and theologian committed to helping people see the beauty of Jesus Christ and experience the transforming power of union with Him. His ministry is rooted in the conviction that the gospel is not merely about escaping judgment, but about participating in the life of God through Christ. He believes that the Christian life is the life of Christ expressed in and through believers by the Holy Spirit. The Authority of Scripture We believe that the Holy Scriptures are inspired by God and are the supreme authority for faith, doctrine, and Christian living. The Bible reveals God's redemptive purpose in Christ and finds its fulfillment in Jesus. Scripture is best understood through a Christ-centered lens, recognizing that all of God's promises find their fulfillment in Him. The Triune God We believe in one God, eternally existing as Father , Son, and Holy Spirit. The Father is the source of all things. The Son is the eternal Word made flesh, the perfect revelation of God. The Holy Spirit indwells, empowers, transforms, and leads believers into the life of Christ. Jesus Christ We believe Jesus Christ is fully God and fully man. Through His incarnation, life, death, resurrection, ascension, and present reign, He has accomplished the complete work of redemption. Jesus is the center of all Scripture, the image of the invisible God, and the true revelation of humanity as God intended it to be. Salvation We believe salvation is a gift of God's grace received through faith in Jesus Christ. Through Christ's finished work, believers are forgiven, justified, reconciled to God, and adopted into His family. 1

title: Statement of Faith
text: Salvation is not earned by human effort but received through faith in the gospel. We believe the believer's security rests in the faithfulness of Christ and not in human performance. Union With Christ We believe the central reality of the Christian life is union with Christ. Through the Holy Spirit, believers participate in the life of Christ and are called to grow into the fullness of His likeness. The Christian life is not merely imitation of Christ but participation in Christ. The Holy Spirit We believe the Holy Spirit dwells within every believer and empowers them for holy living, spiritual growth, service, and witness. The Holy Spirit illuminates Scripture, produces the fruit of Christlike character , distributes spiritual gifts, and leads believers into maturity. We affirm the present-day ministry and operation of the gifts of the Spirit. The Church We believe the Church is the body of Christ and the family of God on earth. The Church exists to worship God, make disciples, proclaim the gospel, care for one another , and participate in God's mission in the world. Every believer is called to meaningful participation in the life of the local church. Spiritual Formation and Discipleship We believe that discipleship is the lifelong process of being transformed into the image of Jesus Christ. This transformation occurs through Scripture, prayer , worship, fellowship, service, obedience, and the ongoing work of the Holy Spirit. The goal of discipleship is Christlikeness. Prayer We believe prayer is communion with God and participation in His purposes. 2

title: Statement of Faith
text: Prayer is not merely a religious duty but a relationship of trust, dependence, worship, and partnership with God. The Kingdom of God We believe the Kingdom of God has been inaugurated through the life, death, resurrection, and reign of Jesus Christ. Believers are called to live as citizens of God's Kingdom, demonstrating the character , justice, mercy, and love of Christ in the world. New Creation We believe that through Christ, God is making all things new. The resurrection of Jesus is the beginning of God's new creation, and believers are invited to participate in that new creation life here and now. Eschatology We believe Jesus Christ reigns as Lord and King. We approach biblical prophecy through a Christ-centered and victorious framework, emphasizing the triumph of Christ, the advancement of His Kingdom, and the hope of the new creation. Our focus is not fear but faith, not speculation but discipleship, and not escapism but faithful participation in God's mission. Mission We believe every believer is called to participate in God's mission. The gospel is good news for every person, culture, and nation. The Church exists not merely to gather but to go, making disciples and embodying the love of Christ in the world. Our Desire Our desire is to help people know Christ deeply, live from their identity in Him, grow in spiritual maturity, participate in the life of the Spirit, and become faithful witnesses of God's Kingdom for the glory of God and the good of the world. 3

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The fear of God is a phrase often spoken but rarely understood. It has inspired awe, provoked confusion, stirred deep devotion, and at times, even been used to weaponize obedience. But what does it mean genuinely and biblically to fear the Lord? Is it a paralyzing terror in the face of divine judgment? Is it the anxiety of standing before a wrathful Deity? Or is it something deeper, richer, and holier, a sacred reverence, a trembling joy, a conscious orientation of the heart toward the majesty and mercy of God? This book is a journey into the Scriptures, seeking to unearth and systematize a doctrine that has long been fragmented across sermons, misquoted in moral tales, and misunderstood in practice. The fear of God is not merely a peripheral concept tucked into the shadows of Hebrew poetry or the thunder of prophetic oracles; it is a theme that stretches across redemptive history, woven into the Law, highlighted in the Prophets, embodied in Wisdom literature, and fulfilled in the life and ministry of Jesus Christ. Indeed, a proper understanding of this fear is essential to spiritual maturity. As the Scriptures declare, “The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom” (Proverbs 9:10). It is both a foundation and a fountain, laying the 8

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: groundwork for wise living and springing forth in sanctified action. And yet, we must ask: what is this fear? Is it noun or verb, disposition or action, dread or devotion? To answer these questions, I have drawn not only from the biblical text but also from the original languages in which the Scriptures were given. The Old Testament (written primarily in Hebrew) uses the root word yareʾ to describe the fear of God, a word that encapsulates reverence, awe, respect, and yes, even trembling. But it is not a fear that drives us from God; rather, it is a fear that draws us to Him with eyes wide open and knees bent low. In the New Testament (written in Greek), phobos and eulabeia extend this meaning, not merely in the context of servile fear, but as a response to God’s holiness, justice, and glory. It is a sanctified response to God, who is both consuming fire and perfect love. Throughout the Scriptures, the fear of God is never reduced to a generic religious feeling or a vague emotional impulse. It is covenantal. It is relational. It flows from knowing who God is, His character, His majesty, His justice, and His mercy. As we follow the biblical thread, we see that the fear of God is cultivated when people encounter Him personally: when Abraham obeys without knowing where he’s going; when Moses removes his sandals at the burning bush; when Israel trembles at Sinai yet draws near through blood; when David sings of the holiness of Yahweh; when 9

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Isaiah cries “Woe is me!” ; when Jesus, the embodiment of divine reverence, teaches that only God is to be feared; and when the early Church “walked in the fear of the Lord and in the comfort of the Holy Ghost” (Acts 9:31). Theologically, the fear of God is eschatological, it points us forward. It is not simply a response to God’s present greatness; it is shaped by the knowledge that we must all stand before Him, and that our lives will be weighed in the light of eternity. It teaches us to live purposefully, humbly, and dependently. Not in the dread of condemnation, for the one who is in Christ Jesus has passed from death to life, but in the gravity of stewardship and the glory of sonship. As John the Beloved wrote, “Herein is our love made perfect, that we may have boldness in the day of judgment... There is no fear in love; but perfect love casteth out fear: because fear hath torment” (1 John 4:17-18). Yet this same John also fell as though dead at the feet of the risen Lord in Revelation. He understood the paradox well, perfect love does not cancel holy reverence; it purifies it. We will investigate how the fear of the Lord is cultivated, lost, restored, and fulfilled in the believer’s life. From Adam’s hiding in the garden to the trembling of the saints before the throne in glory, this book will explore how God initiates, deepens, and redeems the fear that is due to His name. We will examine the prophetic tradition where the lack of the fear of God 10

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: is both a diagnosis of national backsliding and a personal invitation to repentance. We will study how Jesus redefines greatness not in terms of religious status but in intimacy with the Father, childlike humility, and awe-filled obedience. And we will see how the apostles, particularly Paul and Peter, exhorted the Church to walk in holy fear, not out of terror, but out of the weight of love, grace, and divine calling. My prayer is that as you read, a deep longing will be awakened in you, not merely to know about the fear of God, but to walk in it. That your heart will be stirred to worship, your knees drawn to prayer, and your life conformed to the pattern of those who feared the Lord and walked with Him. For the fear of the Lord is not a theoretical posture; it is the atmosphere in which a life of holiness, humility, and heartfelt obedience is cultivated. It is not the enemy of intimacy with God, it is the gateway to it. We live in a time when much of modern Christianity has lost the vocabulary of reverence. God is spoken of as friend, helper, life coach, and even business partner, but rarely as holy . Rarely as King . Rarely as Judge . And rarer still as the One before whom angels veil their faces and elders cast their crowns. If ever there was a time when the Church needs to rediscover the fear of God, it is now. So, let us come with open hearts and attentive spirits. Let us think deeply, pray earnestly, and read reverently. This book does not claim to exhaust the 11

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: subject, but it does aim to serve as a theological map and a devotional guide. I have consulted the Hebrew and Greek texts where necessary, employed biblical typology and redemptive themes, and submitted all to the Word of Truth rightly divided. I ask and receive for you the spirit of wisdom and revelation in the knowledge of Him as you read and consider this writing. May your life be marked by the fear that frees, the fear that teaches, the fear that purifies, and the fear that draws us nearer to the One who is altogether holy and altogether loving. 12

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The journey that led to the creation of this book began with a conversation, a moment that profoundly altered the course of my thinking and deepened my understanding of one of the most foundational aspects of the Christian faith: the fear of the Lord. It was in a casual yet deeply impactful discussion with my dear friend, Apostle Bobby Udosen, that the subject was brought up, almost as if it had never truly crossed my mind before. As he spoke about the fear of God, something in that moment resonated within me, striking my heart with the force of a fresh revelation. His words seemed to leap out of the conversation, grabbing my attention in a way that was impossible to ignore. It felt as though I had never truly understood the depth, the beauty, and the necessity of this godly fear. I found myself captivated by the idea, and from that point onward, I was compelled to embark on a personal study of what it means to walk in the fear of the Lord, and how this reverence should be reflected in the life of every believer. The more I studied, the more I realized how profoundly this theme is woven throughout Scripture, from the patriarchs of the faith to the teachings of Jesus Christ Himself. It became apparent that the fear of the 13

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Lord is not a fleeting or peripheral concept, but a core principle that informs every facet of our relationship with God and with one another. I felt an urgency to not only study this theme for my own personal growth but also to share it with the congregation of Great Haven Assembly, where I serve as pastor. As I began teaching on the fear of the Lord, I saw transformation in the hearts of those who listened. The church was being drawn into a deeper, more genuine relationship with God, where reverence and awe were no longer abstract concepts but lived realities. And thus, this book was born. The pages of this book are not merely an academic exploration of a theological concept. They are an invitation to experience the life-changing power of the fear of the Lord in a way that transforms our hearts, our homes, and our churches. As you read, you will be invited into a journey of reflection, one that will challenge your perspectives, deepen your reverence, and inspire a fresh pursuit of holiness and obedience. This book is not simply for those who seek knowledge, but for those who long to walk in the fullness of God’s presence and to live lives that honor Him in all things. Throughout the chapters, you will encounter the lives of biblical figures, Abraham, Moses, Joseph, and others, whose walk in the fear of the Lord shaped the course of history. You will also be confronted with practical 14

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: applications of how the fear of God should influence our speech, choices, priorities, and relationships. You will see how the fear of the Lord is not something to be dreaded, but a holy reverence that fuels wisdom, purity, and transformation in every believer’s life. As you journey through this book, I pray that you will be awakened to the depth of God’s holiness, the beauty of His presence, and the joy of living in awe and reverence before Him. My hope is that this book will be more than a source of knowledge for you. I pray that it will stir a new desire in your heart to live in the fear of the Lord, to honor God with your life, and to walk in the fullness of His grace. May you encounter the transforming power of the fear of the Lord in ways that lead you to a deeper, more intimate relationship with God. May it inspire you to live with wisdom, humility, and integrity, and may it equip you to lead others into a life of reverence, obedience, and joy before our Heavenly Father. Thank you for joining me on this journey. I trust that as you read, the fear of the Lord will become not just a doctrine to understand, but a reality to experience, a reality that will guide you, shape you, and bring you into the fullness of God’s calling for your life. Dr. Daniel Folarin 15

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: . SECTION ONE: FOUNDATIONS OF THE FEAR OF THE LORD 16

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: What Is the Fear of God? The concept of the "fear of God" is one that appears repeatedly throughout Scripture, yet it often carries with it a great deal of misunderstanding. What does it mean to fear God? Why is it so central to wisdom, yet seemingly contradictory to our understanding of God as a loving Father? These questions must be addressed if we are to grasp the true essence of the fear of the Lord, its power, and its significance for the believer. The Essence of the Fear of God The fear of God, often considered an abstract idea or concept, has been widely misunderstood in the Christian community. On one hand, the Bible seems to say "do not fear" (Isaiah 41:10), yet on the other hand, it clearly states that "the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom" (Proverbs 9:10). The key to understanding the fear of the Lord lies in discerning the different types of fear presented in Scripture. As humans, we tend to view fear as an inherently negative or destructive emotion, something to avoid. However, the Bible shows that the fear of the 17

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Lord is not just an emotion but a foundational principle for understanding God and living wisely. If the Bible simultaneously encourages us to fear and to not fear, we must learn the context in which each type of fear applies. What, then, is the right kind of fear that leads to wisdom and life? Exploring the Hebrew Word for "Fear" In the Old Testament, the primary Hebrew word used for "fear" is yirah ( יָרֵא .) This word encompasses a broad spectrum of meanings, ranging from simple fear or terror to reverence, awe, and reverential worship. Yirah is often translated as "fear" or "to be afraid," but it can also mean "to revere" or "to be in awe of" someone or something. This distinction is crucial because it shows that fear in the biblical sense can be both a positive and a negative experience depending on its object. The most well-known verses that discuss the fear of the Lord are: ● Proverbs 14:27 : "The fear of the Lord is a fountain of life, to depart from the snares of death." ● Psalm 111:10 : "The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom; a good understanding have all they that do His commandments: His praise endures forever." 18

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: ● 1 John 4:18 : "There is no fear in love; but perfect love casts out fear: because fear has torment. He that feareth is not made perfect in love." These passages present the fear of the Lord in a very positive light, showing that it is something to be pursued rather than avoided. But at the same time, 1 John 4:18 tells us that perfect love casts out fear, which seems to create a paradox. To understand this, we must first recognize that the "fear" spoken of in 1 John 4:18 is different from the "fear of the Lord" spoken of in the Old Testament. The fear that is driven out by perfect love is the kind of fear that torments us, the fear that arises from uncertainty, anxiety, and dread. But the fear of the Lord is not tormenting; rather, it brings wisdom, life, and understanding. Debunking Misconceptions About the Fear of the Lord One of the greatest misconceptions about the fear of the Lord is that it is a kind of terror or dread, like the fear we feel when we are in danger or face an uncertain threat. This idea is rooted in the human understanding of fear, something negative and incapacitating. However, the Bible does not present the fear of the Lord as something that causes paralyzing terror. 19

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Rather, it is a reverential awe that leads to a deeper relationship, wisdom, and obedience to God. Misconception #1: Fear of the Lord Means Being Afraid of God Some people may approach the fear of the Lord from a perspective of fear as terror, believing that it means to be afraid of God in the sense of fearing His anger or punishment. While Scripture acknowledges that God is a righteous judge (Hebrews 10:31), the fear of the Lord is not merely about fearing God’s wrath. It is about recognizing His greatness, holiness, and authority. When we fear God, we are not cowering in terror, but rather standing in awe of who He is and what He has done. Misconception #2: The Fear of the Lord Is Only About Obeying Commandments Another misconception is that the fear of the Lord is simply about obeying a set of rules or commandments. While obedience is an important part of living in reverence to God, the fear of the Lord goes deeper. It is not just about outward compliance; it is about cultivating a heart of awe and reverence before God. This fear leads to a willingness to submit to 20

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: God’s will because we recognize that He is worthy of our trust and obedience. Misconception #3: The Fear of the Lord Is a Negative Emotion As we have already seen, fear is often considered a negative emotion. However, the fear of the Lord is not a negative force but a positive one. It is a gift that enables us to live in harmony with God’s will and to walk in wisdom. When we fear the Lord, we are aligning ourselves with His purposes, seeking to live in accordance with His Word, and embracing His holiness. The Hebrew Pictographs of "Yirah" and Their Meaning To gain a deeper understanding of the fear of the Lord, it is helpful to explore the Hebrew word yirah through the lens of ancient Hebrew pictographs. Ancient Hebrew was a language rich in symbolism, and each letter had a picture that conveyed deeper meanings. The word yirah is spelled yod ( י ,) resh ( ר ,) aleph ( א ,) and hey ( ה .) Let’s explore the pictographs for each of these letters: ● Yod (י) : The first letter in yirah is yod , which is often depicted as a hand or arm. It symbolizes action, work, or a mighty deed. This suggests that the fear of the Lord is not just a passive emotion; it is a response to the mighty works and deeds of God. 21

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: ● Resh (ר) : The second letter is resh , which represents the head or leader. It symbolizes authority, leadership, and mastery. This signifies that the fear of the Lord is rooted in recognizing God’s ultimate authority and sovereignty over our lives. ● Aleph (א) : The third letter is aleph , which is represented by an ox. It symbolizes strength and leadership, particularly the strong leader or head of the family. This represents God as our strong and mighty Father, the one to whom we owe our reverence and fear. ● Hey (ה) : The final letter is hey , which is often depicted as a man with uplifted hands, symbolizing revelation or a call to pay attention. This signifies that the fear of the Lord comes from a revelation of His greatness and holiness. When we behold His works, we are moved to respond with awe and reverence. Together, these letters form the word yirah , which speaks of a mighty work by the prince for the Father that causes us to look and behold, leading us to respond in awe and reverence. The fear of the Lord is not just an emotional reaction; it is a response to the revelation of God’s greatness, power, and love. 22

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The Fear of the Lord as a Gift from God As we delve deeper into the meaning of the fear of the Lord, we must recognize that it is not something that we can conjure up on our own. The fear of the Lord is a gift from God. It is something that He imparts to us as we behold His works and come to know Him more intimately. In the Hebrew language, when the word yirah is combined with the name of the Lord, it becomes yirat ( יִרְאַת .) This form signifies that the fear of the Lord is not something we initiate; it is something that belongs to God and is given to us. The fear of the Lord is His fear, a fear that flows from His revelation of Himself to us. The transformation of yirah to yirat is important because it indicates that the fear of the Lord is not simply a human response but a divine impartation. Just as the presence of the Lord is His presence and the love of God is His love, so the fear of God is His fear. We receive it as a gift, not as a self-generated response. 23

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The Cross and the Fear of the Lord To truly understand the fear of the Lord, we must look to the cross. The mighty work of the Prince (Jesus Christ) for the Father was accomplished on the cross. In this act, God revealed His heart and His intentions for humanity. The fear of the Lord, then, is tied to our response to the revelation of God’s work on the cross. When we behold the cross, we see the ultimate expression of God’s holiness, justice, mercy, and love. The fear of the Lord is a natural response to the magnitude of what Christ accomplished on our behalf. As we reflect on the cross, we are moved to awe and reverence for the Father who gave His Son and for the Son who willingly laid down His life for us. Conclusion In conclusion, the fear of the Lord is not something to be feared or avoided. It is a blessing, a gift that leads to wisdom, understanding, and life. The fear of the Lord is a reverential awe that comes from beholding the works of God, particularly the work of Christ on the cross. It is not a terror that paralyzes us but a response that empowers us to live in accordance with God’s will. As we continue our journey of faith, let us seek to understand the fear of the Lord and to cultivate it in our hearts. May we grow in wisdom, 24

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: reverence, and love for our God, responding to His mighty works with awe and obedience. As we do, we will experience the fullness of life and wisdom that comes from the fear of the Lord. The Fear of the Lord Is Not Terror (Understanding Reverence, Awe, and Relational Fear as Seen in Scripture) In contemporary Christianity, the notion of the "fear of the Lord" is often misunderstood. In many theological circles, the fear of the Lord is confused with a terrifying dread that compels the believer to shrink back in horror from God’s presence. However, a proper theological understanding, grounded in the sacred texts of the Bible, offers a much richer and more nuanced perspective of this concept. Far from representing mere terror or dread, the fear of the Lord is a profound reverence, awe, and relational respect that shapes our relationship with God and influences our spiritual life. 25

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: A Biblical Foundation To grasp the biblical understanding of the fear of the Lord , we must first explore its depiction in Scripture. One of the earliest mentions of this concept appears in the book of Proverbs, where it is described as the beginning of wisdom: "The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom: and the knowledge of the holy is understanding" (Proverbs 9:10, KJV). Here, the fear of the Lord is connected to wisdom and understanding. It is not the kind of fear that leads one to shrink back in terror, but rather the kind that opens up the heart to deeper knowledge of God. The Hebrew word for fear used in this context is yirah , which conveys a sense of awe, reverence, and deep respect. In its most robust sense, it encompasses a fear that leads to a proper understanding of God’s holiness, justice, and mercy. It is a response to God’s power, beauty, and goodness, not a crippling terror of His wrath. The Bible frequently links yirah to a relational understanding of God. In Deuteronomy 6:13, we are told, "Thou shalt fear the Lord thy God, and serve Him, and shalt swear by His name." This is not a fear that causes us to run away from God, but one that compels us to draw near in awe and worship. It is a fear that encourages us to approach God with reverence, knowing that He is both transcendent and immanent, infinitely holy and yet intimately personal. 26

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: As we examine further passages such as Psalm 111:10, we see that the fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom , not as a result of terror, but as a profound acknowledgment of God’s holiness and greatness. In this light, the fear of the Lord serves as the foundation for a life of worship, obedience, and godly living. It is not a destructive fear, but a constructive and transforming one. Terror vs. Reverence A common misconception about the fear of the Lord is that it is synonymous with terror, that God’s presence induces a frightful, paralyzing fear. This error arises, in part, from a misunderstanding of divine justice and holiness. While it is true that God’s holiness and righteousness stand in sharp contrast to our sinfulness, the fear of the Lord does not suggest that believers should live in constant fear of God’s wrath or punishment. Rather, it invites us into a deeper understanding of who God is and how He relates to us as His children. Terror is a fear that leads one to hide, to avoid the object of fear. Consider the response of Adam and Eve after their fall in the Garden of Eden. Their initial encounter with God after sin was one of terror. They "heard the voice of the Lord God walking in the garden in the cool of the day: and 27

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Adam and his wife hid themselves from the presence of the Lord God amongst the trees of the garden" (Genesis 3:8, KJV). Their fear was a reaction to their disobedience. It was a fear driven by guilt, shame, and separation from God. This terror-filled fear is the antithesis of the fear of the Lord that believers are called to embrace. While terror stems from sin and guilt, the fear of the Lord is rooted in a proper understanding of God’s holiness, righteousness, and majesty. It is a fear that invites us to draw near to God, not away from Him. Throughout the Old and New Testaments, we see that the fear of God, when rightly understood, is not an inhibiting terror but a loving response to the divine presence. It is akin to the awe a child feels for a loving father who is both authoritative and protective. This fatherly fear is not driven by anxiety about punishment but by an understanding of the father’s strength, wisdom, and goodness. The Meaning of ‘Fear’ in the Context of Relationship To understand the fear of the Lord fully, we must consider the relational aspect of this fear. The fear of God is not an abstract emotion, but one deeply embedded in the personal relationship between God and His people. At its core, the fear of the Lord is a relational fear, one that emerges from a 28

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: deep and growing awareness of God’s attributes and our appropriate response to them. Jesus Christ, the perfect embodiment of reverential fear, modeled this fear in His earthly life. When He prayed in the Garden of Gethsemane, He demonstrated a deep submission to the will of the Father, even as He experienced the agony of approaching the cross. In His prayer, He said, "Father, if thou be willing, remove this cup from me: nevertheless not my will, but thine, be done" (Luke 22:42, KJV). Jesus’ submission was not born of terror or dread but of an intimate fear rooted in His love for the Father and His deep awareness of the Father’s holiness and authority. Likewise, the Apostle Paul emphasizes that this fear is not servile but is a voluntary submission to God’s sovereignty. In 2 Corinthians 7:1, Paul urges believers to “perfect holiness in the fear of God.” This is not a fear that cripples or paralyzes, but one that compels believers to pursue holiness and godliness as a natural outflow of their reverence for God. Theologically speaking, this relational fear corresponds to a proper understanding of God’s transcendence (His holiness, righteousness, and power) and immanence (His nearness, goodness, and love). Both aspects of God’s nature must be held in tension to understand the fullness of the fear 29

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: of the Lord . We fear Him because He is holy, powerful, and just, yet we also love Him because He is merciful, loving, and close. Our fear, then, is a response to the totality of God’s character, which includes both His holiness and His love. The Transformative Power of the Fear of the Lord The fear of the Lord is not merely an intellectual exercise or an emotional response. It is transformative, it affects the way we live, think, and relate to others. Proverbs 14:27 tells us, “The fear of the Lord is a fountain of life, to depart from the snares of death.” This "fountain of life" symbolizes the life-giving power that comes from a deep reverence for God. It protects us from the snares of sin and keeps our hearts aligned with God’s will. When we live in the fear of the Lord, we acknowledge that God’s commands are not burdensome but life-giving. We seek to align our will with His because we trust that His ways are best. The fear of the Lord leads us to seek purity, righteousness, and integrity in our lives. As the psalmist writes in Psalm 34:9, “O fear the Lord, ye His saints: for there is no want to them that fear Him.” This verse reminds us that when we live in the fear of the Lord, we are under His protection and provision. Furthermore, the fear of the Lord shapes how we interact with others. It fosters humility, compassion, and a heart for service. In a world where 30

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: pride and self-centeredness dominate, the fear of the Lord calls us to live with a different posture. We recognize that every interaction with others is an opportunity to reflect God’s character, His love, justice, mercy, and holiness. The fear of the Lord impels us to honor others, to seek peace, and to live in unity, knowing that all people are made in God’s image. Shepherding in the Fear of the Lord We have a solemn responsibility to teach the fear of the Lord . However, our teaching must be clear, rooted in Scripture and sensitive to the nuances of human experience. Every believer must understand that the fear of the Lord is not about terror, but about reverence, awe, and relational intimacy with God. Pastoral care requires that we walk alongside our people as they grow in their understanding of God’s holiness. We must help them recognize that true fear of the Lord leads to life, peace, and spiritual vitality. It is a fear that encourages trust and obedience, not out of dread of punishment but out of reverence for the greatness of God. When preaching about the fear of the Lord, we must emphasize its transformative power. This fear should not lead to a life of dread or anxiety 31

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: but to one of worship, service, and faithful obedience. It is a fear that motivates believers to live in accordance with God’s will and to honor Him in every aspect of their lives. Conclusion In conclusion, the fear of the Lord is not terror but reverence, awe, and relational submission. It is the beginning of wisdom, the gateway to understanding, and the path to spiritual vitality. As we grow in our knowledge of God’s nature, His holiness, justice, and love, we are invited to live in the fear of the Lord, a fear that leads us closer to God and transforms our lives. May we, as a church, learn to walk in the fear of the Lord, not out of a desire to escape judgment but out of awe and reverence for the One who is holy, just, and loving. As we do so, we will experience His peace, His protection, and His provision, and we will grow in wisdom, holiness, and Christlikeness. In the next chapter, we will explore how the fear of the Lord brings about genuine transformation in the believer’s character and relationships. We will examine how this reverential fear shapes our behavior, our values, and our interactions with others in the body of Christ. 32

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: A Covenant of Fear and Comfort (Exploring Malachi 2:4-6 and the Divine Pairing of Fear with Intimacy) The intersection of divine fear and intimate comfort is one of the profound mysteries embedded within the heart of the covenantal relationship between God and His people. In Malachi 2:4-6, we encounter a pivotal text that sheds light on how God, through the prophet Malachi, speaks to the people of Israel about their covenant with Him, a covenant marked by both fear and comfort. These verses not only offer an Old Testament perspective on the complex relationship between God and His people but also serve as an enduring revelation of the harmonious interplay between God’s holiness (which provokes a reverential fear) and His mercy (which offers comfort). In this chapter, we will explore Malachi 2:4-6 through a theological, scholarly, and practical lens, unpacking the covenantal principles contained within the passage. We will examine the dual nature of the fear of the Lord and how it is intertwined with divine intimacy. Through a detailed theological reflection on these verses, we will uncover the profound meaning of the covenant of fear and comfort that undergirds the 34

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: relationship between God and His people, and how these ancient truths remain relevant and applicable in our contemporary walk with God. The Covenant Context in Malachi 2:4-6 Before diving into the specifics of Malachi 2:4-6, it is essential to establish the broader covenantal framework in which these verses are situated. The book of Malachi is the last prophetic book of the Old Testament and serves as a concluding message to the Israelites. Malachi's prophecies were given after the return of the Jewish exiles from Babylonian captivity, during a time when the post-exilic community was struggling with spiritual apathy, complacency, and a failure to fully honor their covenant with God. Malachi's message addresses several critical issues, such as the priests' failure to offer pure sacrifices, the people's disobedience, and their disillusionment with God’s justice. Yet, at the heart of this book lies a reminder of God’s covenantal faithfulness. In Malachi 2:4-6, the prophet speaks about the priesthood and its role in preserving the fear of God and maintaining the covenantal relationship with Him. 35

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The text begins with an imperative: “And ye shall know that I have sent this commandment unto you, that my covenant might be with Levi, saith the Lord of hosts.” The covenant in question is God’s special covenant with the tribe of Levi, the priestly tribe, whose role was to mediate God’s presence to the people. This covenant is foundational because it establishes the priesthood as a conduit through which the people would experience the fear of the Lord and the comfort of His presence. The priesthood was entrusted with the responsibility of teaching the fear of God, preserving the integrity of the covenant, and fostering a culture of holiness among the people. The Fear of the Lord as a Covenant Marker Malachi 2:5-6 introduces the idea that the fear of the Lord is not just a personal response to God’s majesty but a covenantal marker, a defining characteristic of the relationship between God and His people. In verse 5, God describes His covenant with Levi as one of life and peace: “My covenant was with him of life and peace; and I gave them to him for the fear wherewith he feared me, and was afraid before my name.” This statement reflects the theological truth that 36

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: the fear of God is not a passive, abstract concept but a dynamic, life-shaping principle that sustains the believer's relationship with God. The fear of the Lord was to be at the very core of the Levitical priesthood, the foundation of their covenant with God, and the key to the preservation of life and peace in Israel. The phrase “the fear wherewith he feared me” in verse 5 is particularly striking. The repetition of the term "fear" here is meant to emphasize the nature of this fear, it is a deep, awe-filled reverence that not only acknowledges God’s holiness but also recognizes the responsibility of maintaining the covenant. This fear is both a safeguard for the individual priest and the community at large, ensuring that the people would live in accordance with God’s will, recognizing that their covenantal relationship with God is not to be taken lightly. This fear of the Lord is tied to the concept of holiness. In the Old Testament, the fear of God and holiness are inseparable. The priesthood’s role was to reflect God’s holiness, ensuring that the people were constantly reminded of His sanctity. This covenant of fear, then, is not merely about submitting to God in a passive sense but actively engaging in a life that reflects God’s purity and glory. The fear of the Lord, then, is a gracious gift from God that enables His people to live in harmony with His will. This fear serves both as a 37

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: boundary marker (keeping the people from straying into sin) and as a motivating force that draws them closer to the holiness of God. It is a fear that leads to life, peace, and joy, not terror, but reverence that springs from an understanding of God’s transcendent holiness and immanent mercy. The Comfort of the Covenantcy It is important to note that the fear of the Lord, as presented in Malachi 2:4-6, is not isolated from the relational aspect of the covenant. The fear of God in this context is not merely a form of dread but a fear that is balanced with comfort, peace, and intimacy. In verse 5, God’s covenant is described as one of “life and peace,” which implies that the fear of the Lord, when rightly understood, brings comfort, not despair. The coupling of fear with life and peace is deeply significant because it emphasizes that the fear of God does not lead to alienation or distance from Him. Rather, it fosters a deeper intimacy with God, a closeness that is characterized by reverence, awe, and trust. This is the essence of what the Apostle John would later call "perfect love" that “perfect love casteth out fear” (1 John 4:18), not because fear is eradicated but because it is transformed by the love and mercy of God. 38

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The fear of the Lord, when experienced in the covenantal relationship, draws the believer nearer to God. It is a relational fear that expresses not a flight from God’s wrath but a desire to remain in right standing with Him. It is a fear that cultivates an awe-filled reverence for God’s holiness, while simultaneously fostering a comforting sense of His presence and His fatherly care. In the priestly context, this dual experience of fear and comfort was essential for maintaining spiritual vitality within the community. The Levitical priests were called to walk in both reverence before God and in the comfort of His covenantal promises. They were to lead the people not only by teaching the law but by exemplifying the fear of the Lord that brought about peace and life. This concept can be practically applied to the life of the modern believer. When we live in the fear of the Lord, we acknowledge God’s holiness and righteousness, but we do so in a way that draws us closer to Him. The fear of the Lord does not make God seem distant or unapproachable. Instead, it makes us more aware of the intimacy that we can experience with Him through the covenant of grace. 39

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The Covenant of Fear and Comfort Today As we look at the church today, the principle of the covenant of fear and comfort remains profoundly relevant. In our contemporary context, the balance between the fear of God and the comfort of His presence is vital for spiritual growth and maturity. In many church circles, there is a tendency to focus either too heavily on the comfort of God’s love or on the terror of His judgment. However, a true understanding of the covenant relationship calls for both elements to be held in tension. From a pastoral standpoint, it is critical to teach that the fear of the Lord is not a negative or paralyzing fear but a reverent awe that leads to peace and intimacy with God. Pastors must help believers understand that their relationship with God is one of both reverence and love. It is a relationship where the fear of God does not drive them away from His presence but draws them closer to Him, knowing that His holiness and mercy go hand in hand. This teaching has practical implications for the way believers approach God in worship, prayer, and everyday living. When we approach God in prayer, for example, we should do so with a sense of awe and reverence, recognizing His majesty and power, but we should also do so with the 40

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: comfort of knowing that He is our loving Father, who welcomes us into His presence. This balance fosters a deeper relationship with God, one that is both respectful and intimate. Moreover, this covenant of fear and comfort should inform our relationships with one another in the body of Christ. As believers, we are called to live in the fear of the Lord, which leads us to honor God in our interactions with others. At the same time, we must extend the comfort of God’s love and grace to those around us, understanding that the same fear of the Lord that draws us to reverence also compels us to offer comfort and encouragement to our brothers and sisters in Christ. A Living Covenant of Fear and Comfort In conclusion, Malachi 2:4-6 presents a profound picture of the covenantal relationship between God and His people, a relationship marked by both fear and comfort. The fear of the Lord is not an immobilizing terror, but a reverence that leads to life, peace, and intimacy with God. It is a fear that draws us closer to God, not further away, and it compels us to live in holiness and to worship Him with all reverence. As we embrace the covenant of fear and comfort , we are called to live lives of awe and intimacy with our Creator, recognizing that His holiness and love are not at odds but are perfectly balanced in the covenant He has made 41

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: with us. This understanding transforms the way we relate to God, to one another, and to the world, as we seek to live in a way that honors both His majesty and His mercy. In the next chapter, we will explore how the fear of the Lord shapes our worship and influences the way we approach God in spirit and truth. We will examine the biblical practices of worship and how they reflect a healthy balance of fear and comfort in our relationship with the Almighty. 42

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The Fear of God and Company (Wisdom, Knowledge, and Understanding as Visible Signs of True Fear) "The fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge, but fools despise wisdom and instruction." Proverbs 1:7 "The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom, and the knowledge of the Holy One is understanding." Proverbs 9:10 "And unto man He said, Behold, the fear of the LORD, that is wisdom; and to depart from evil is understanding." Job 28:28 "To fear the LORD is to hate evil; I hate pride and arrogance, evil behavior and perverse speech." Proverbs 8:13 Introduction The fear of God is not merely a solitary experience; it is a divine force that brings with it a community of virtues that shape the life of the believer. These virtues wisdom, knowledge, and understanding are not only the 43

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: evidences of the fear of God in a person’s life but also the very tools by which God reveals His will to humanity. In the Scriptures, fear and knowledge are intricately connected, with the fear of God acting as the foundational starting point for wisdom, knowledge, and understanding. It is important to recognize that wisdom, knowledge, and understanding are not mere abstract concepts or academic pursuits. They are relational, practical, and transformative. They are not the kinds of wisdom and knowledge that the world values but are given by God to those who truly fear Him. Therefore, understanding the connection between the fear of God and these three virtues is not only critical to our spiritual growth but also to our daily walk in the world. This chapter aims to explore the deep theological significance of these virtues, grounded in the fear of God, and their practical implications for Christians. The Fear of God as the Foundation The first step to unlocking wisdom, knowledge, and understanding lies in recognizing that the fear of God is the foundation from which these virtues flow. The Proverbs highlighted above establish a clear principle: the fear of God is the beginning of wisdom and knowledge. The phrase "the 44

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: beginning" signifies that all true wisdom, knowledge, and understanding emanate from this initial fear. In understanding the fear of God, it is important to note that it is not simply an emotional reaction or an act of terror. Instead, it is a profound reverence, awe, and submission to the holiness and majesty of God. To fear the LORD means to recognize His authority, to live in awe of His presence, and to walk in humble submission to His will. The Scriptures do not merely describe fear as a feeling but as a guiding principle for living. Proverbs 1:7 implies that the fear of God is the entry point into knowledge, and Proverbs 9:10 deepens this understanding by connecting the fear of God with wisdom. Job 28:28 further enhances this by stating that the fear of God is wisdom itself. Thus, the fear of God is not only foundational but also a living force that sustains a Christian's pursuit of truth, righteousness, and divine understanding. Wisdom: The Skillful Application of Divine Knowledge The first companion of the fear of God is wisdom. In the Hebrew Scriptures, the word for wisdom is "hokmah," and it encompasses much more than mere knowledge. "Hokmah" refers to an intrinsic skill or ability that comes from God, enabling a person to make sound decisions that align 45

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: with His will. It is not just intellectual understanding but practical skill, the ability to navigate life according to God’s standards. Scholars such as Derek Kidner, in his commentary on Proverbs, note that wisdom in the biblical sense is “skill in living”. It is the ability to make decisions and act in ways that honor God and reflect His character. It is, in essence, the application of divine knowledge in every aspect of life, whether in personal conduct, work, relationships, or spiritual practice. The fear of God, then, is intrinsically tied to wisdom because the wisdom that comes from fearing God is practical and has far-reaching consequences in the world. It is not confined to theological discussions or debates but manifests in everyday decisions. Whether it is a leader making just decisions, a parent training children, or a Christian navigating the complexities of the world, the fear of God equips them with the wisdom to act in alignment with divine purposes. The practical nature of this wisdom can be seen in the lives of individuals throughout Scripture. In the building of the tabernacle, the craftsmen who were filled with the "spirit of wisdom" (Exodus 28:3) demonstrated that God’s wisdom was to be expressed through skillful and beautiful work. This wisdom was not theoretical but tangible and relevant to the needs of the people and the work of God. Thus, when we witness wisdom being applied in the world, it is a testimony to the fear of God at work. 46

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Knowledge: Intimacy with God and Revelation The second companion of the fear of God is knowledge . In the Hebrew Scriptures, the term "da'at" is used for knowledge, and it refers not simply to factual information but to an intimate, relational knowledge of God. Knowledge in this context is not theoretical or detached but is relational and transformative. In the Western world, knowledge is often reduced to intellectual capacity or the accumulation of facts. However, in the Hebrew tradition, knowing God is deeply personal and relational. As we see in Genesis 4:1, the verb "yada," translated as "to know," is used to describe the intimate relationship between Adam and Eve. This kind of knowledge is not about mere awareness but involves a deep connection and communion between the individual and God. The fear of God, therefore, fosters an intimate relationship with God where the believer comes to know Him not just through scripture but through direct experience and revelation. The fear of God makes one receptive to the wisdom that comes from being in the presence of God. This intimate knowledge allows believers to live in accordance with God’s will and reflect His glory in the world. 47

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Scholars such as John Piper have highlighted that the knowledge of God is “not just about knowing facts about God but about knowing God personally and experientially.” This kind of knowledge is only available to those who fear God because it is through fear, through reverence and submission to God’s authority, that we are drawn into a deeper relationship with Him. In practical terms, knowledge as a result of the fear of God manifests in how we perceive the world and how we approach life’s decisions. It enables us to see beyond the surface and to discern God’s purposes in all things. The fear of God opens our hearts to greater intimacy with the Creator, allowing us to perceive His will for our lives. Understanding: Discerning the Will of God The third companion of the fear of God is understanding. The Hebrew term for understanding is "biynah," which refers to insight or the ability to discern the deeper meanings and purposes behind situations, circumstances, and divine revelation. It is the capacity to understand the will of God in specific contexts, often in ways that transcend human logic or worldly wisdom. 48

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: In Proverbs 2:6, we are told that "the LORD gives wisdom; from His mouth come knowledge and understanding." This understanding is not merely intellectual but is spiritual insight that enables a believer to interpret life through the lens of divine revelation. It is what enables Joseph to interpret Pharaoh’s dreams (Genesis 41) and what allowed Daniel to understand the mysteries of the vision given to him (Daniel 9:22-23). Understanding, as a result of the fear of God, allows the believer to see beyond the natural world and recognize God’s hand in every circumstance. It provides the wisdom needed to act in alignment with God’s will and purposes. Furthermore, understanding helps Christians distinguish between good and evil, right and wrong, and the best course of action in any given situation. Theologian R.C. Sproul asserts that understanding, in biblical terms, involves “seeing the world as God sees it.” This perspective is given to those who walk in the fear of the Lord and are willing to listen to His voice. Understanding, then, is a critical sign of the fear of God, as it manifests in a believer’s ability to perceive and respond to the will of God. The Fear of God as a Visible Reality As we consider the companions of the fear of God, wisdom, knowledge, and understanding, it becomes clear that they are not abstract virtues but 49

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: practical, visible signs of true fear. These virtues manifest in the life of the believer in very real and tangible ways, shaping their thoughts, actions, and relationships with others. In a world that often values worldly wisdom, knowledge, and understanding, Christians are called to a higher standard, one rooted in the fear of God. The fear of God leads to a wisdom that is applicable in every area of life, a knowledge that transforms relationships, and an understanding that aligns with God’s will. Thus, the fear of God is not a passive or inactive force but a transformative and active power that works within the believer. It is the starting point from which all other virtues flow, and it is the lens through which we understand the world and our place in it. As believers, we are called to cultivate this fear, knowing that it is the key to a fruitful and fulfilling life in God’s kingdom. 50

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Fear, Wisdom & the Heart of a Craftsman (Unpacking “Hokmah” - Divine Skill and Practical Godliness in Action ) In our previous explorations, we delved into the intrinsic relationship between the fear of the Lord, wisdom, knowledge, and understanding. In this chapter, we are invited into a deeper examination of one of the most profound expressions of wisdom: hokmah . In particular, we will explore how hokmah , the divine skill, is intimately connected to the fear of God and how it manifests itself practically in the life of the believer. The Foundation of Hokmah: A Divine Gift The Hebrew word hokmah is often translated as "wisdom," but it is much more than a mere intellectual understanding. It is an intricate, practical skill, a divine capability that reflects the heart of God. The word hokmah appears extensively in the Old Testament, particularly in the wisdom literature (such as Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, and Job), and it is used to describe an attribute of God that is bestowed upon human beings who fear Him. 51

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: To truly grasp the significance of hokmah , we must recognize that it is a gift that originates from God. As Job 28:23-28 declares: “God understands the way to it [wisdom], and He knows its place... And to man He said, ‘The fear of the Lord, that is wisdom; and to depart from evil is understanding.’” Here, hokmah is not simply intellectual insight; it is the skill and competence to live according to God’s will and purposes. Hokmah embodies more than the ability to make wise decisions; it is the profound skill that aligns a person’s actions, thoughts, and desires with God’s eternal purposes. It is functional, practical, and deeply connected to living out the divine design for human life. Wisdom in this sense is not passive knowledge but active godliness, expressed in everything from moral decisions to practical work. Hokmah and the Fear of the Lord The connection between hokmah and the fear of the Lord is critical to understanding how divine wisdom operates in the believer’s life. The fear of the Lord, as we have seen, is the foundational principle from which wisdom flows. It is not a fear of punishment, but rather a reverent awe and a deep respect for God’s authority, holiness, and righteousness. It is this 52

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: deep respect for God that opens the door to the wisdom that God desires to impart to His people. Proverbs 9:10 says, "The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom, and the knowledge of the Holy One is understanding." Hokmah is thus inextricably linked to the fear of God because it is God Himself who grants wisdom to those who revere Him. This reverence produces not just an awareness of God’s greatness but a willingness to submit every aspect of life to His guidance and instruction. Wisdom, in this context, is the practical outworking of that submission. The hokmah that flows from the fear of God is not restricted to theological or philosophical wisdom but encompasses every aspect of life. As we will see, it extends to the very act of working, creating, and living as God intends. The fear of God imparts to the believer the divine skill to act justly, to discern rightly, and to perform their daily tasks with excellence. The Heart of a Craftsman: Wisdom in Action The hokmah imparted by the fear of God is exemplified in the lives of skilled craftsmen in Scripture. One of the clearest examples of this is found in the construction of the Tabernacle in Exodus. In Exodus 31:1-6, we 53

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: encounter Bezalel and Oholiab, two craftsmen whom God specifically endowed with the hokmah necessary to design and build the Tabernacle: "Then the Lord said to Moses, ‘See, I have chosen Bezalel son of Uri, the son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah, and I have filled him with the Spirit of God, with wisdom, with understanding, with knowledge, and with all kinds of skills, to make artistic designs for work in gold, silver, and bronze, to cut and set stones, to work in wood, and to engage in all kinds of crafts.’" (Exodus 31:2-5) This passage reveals a powerful truth: divine wisdom ( hokmah ) is not only concerned with abstract theological concepts but is also deeply rooted in practical action. God gave Bezalel and Oholiab the ability to work with their hands, to create beauty, and to fulfill His purposes through craftsmanship. Their work was an act of worship, a visible expression of the fear of God in action. Bezalel’s hokmah was not just a natural talent or skill, but a divine gift. His craftsmanship was the manifestation of his relationship with God. As he worked, he was fulfilling God’s will, using the wisdom imparted by God to 54

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: create something holy and sacred. In this way, his labor was both spiritual and practical. It was an embodiment of what it means to have the fear of God guiding every aspect of life. Similarly, in the New Testament, the apostle Paul speaks of the believer’s work as an offering to God. Colossians 3:23-24 reminds us that "Whatever you do, work heartily, as for the Lord and not for men, knowing that from the Lord you will receive the inheritance as your reward. You are serving the Lord Christ." This is a continuation of the principle that work, whether physical or intellectual, is an opportunity to express the fear of God through skill, diligence, and godliness. Divine Skill and Practical Godliness The practical outworking of hokmah in the life of the Christian can be seen in several ways, ranging from the way we approach our professional lives to how we relate to others. The fear of God, as expressed through wisdom, is not just a theoretical or doctrinal belief; it is a practical guide for living. 1. In the Workplace: Whether a person is a teacher, engineer, nurse, artist, or homemaker, the hokmah of God can be applied in every profession. The person who fears God works with excellence, integrity, and a sense of purpose. This is not simply about performing well for the sake of career advancement but about 55

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: honoring God through the work itself. The fear of God compels the believer to perform their tasks with diligence and excellence because their work is seen as an offering to the Lord. 2. In Relationships: The wisdom that flows from the fear of God is also evident in how we relate to others. Proverbs 4:7 reminds us, "The beginning of wisdom is this: Get wisdom. Though it cost all you have, get understanding." The hokmah of God enables us to navigate relationships with grace, humility, and discernment. It helps us to choose our words carefully, to exercise patience, and to resolve conflicts with wisdom. In relationships, hokmah is not merely intellectual knowledge but practical godliness that guides us in how to love and serve others well. 3. In Our Spiritual Walk: The fear of God produces wisdom that manifests in our personal spiritual life. It leads us to seek God through prayer, meditation on His Word, and a commitment to obedience. The person who fears God is not content with superficial religious rituals; they seek a deeper, more intimate relationship with the Lord. This wisdom drives the believer to discern God’s will for their life and to act accordingly. 56

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The Role of the Holy Spirit in Imparting Hokmah The Spirit of God is the one who imparts hokmah to the believer. Just as Bezalel and Oholiab were filled with the Spirit of God to craft the Tabernacle, so too does the Holy Spirit empower the believer to live wisely and skillfully in every aspect of life. The wisdom of God is not something that can be gained through human effort alone; it is a gift that must be received through the Holy Spirit. In 1 Corinthians 2:12-13, Paul writes: "Now we have received not the spirit of the world, but the Spirit who is from God, that we might understand the things freely given us by God. And we impart this in words not taught by human wisdom but taught by the Spirit, interpreting spiritual truths to those who are spiritual." Here, Paul emphasizes that true wisdom, the kind that aligns with God’s will and purposes, comes from the Spirit. The believer, therefore, is dependent on the Holy Spirit to reveal God’s wisdom and to empower them to live it out in practical ways. 57

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Living Out Hokmah in a Fallen World While hokmah is a divine gift, its expression is not without challenge. Living in a fallen world means that the wisdom imparted by God is often tested through adversity, temptation, and sin. The wisdom of God calls the believer to make decisions that align with His will, even when the world’s wisdom seems contrary or countercultural. However, the fear of God empowers the believer to stand firm in the face of temptation, knowing that the wisdom of God is superior to the wisdom of the world. James 3:17 gives us a beautiful description of this wisdom: "But the wisdom from above is first pure, then peaceable, gentle, open to reason, full of mercy and good fruits, impartial and sincere." In a world that is often chaotic and hostile, the wisdom that comes from the fear of God enables the believer to navigate life’s challenges with a heart of peace, mercy, and integrity. The world may offer its own version of wisdom, but the believer is called to embrace the wisdom that comes from the Lord, which transcends human understanding and leads to godly living. 58

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The Fear of the Lord as Wisdom in Action In this chapter, we have unpacked the concept of hokmah , the divine skill and practical godliness that is the outworking of the fear of God. Wisdom, knowledge, and understanding are not merely abstract principles but are actively lived out in the believer’s life, whether in the workplace, in relationships, or in their spiritual walk. The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom, and it is through the fear of God that we are empowered to live out the divine wisdom He has imparted to us. The heart of a craftsman is a heart of wisdom, skillfully living out the purposes of God in every area of life. Just as Bezalel’s work in the Tabernacle was a manifestation of divine wisdom, so too can our work be a manifestation of God’s wisdom, bringing glory to Him in all that we do. Let us, therefore, embrace the fear of the Lord, seek after His wisdom, and live lives that reflect the godliness and skill of a true craftsman in the kingdom of God. 59

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Devotion as Knowledge: The Relational Intelligence of the Fearful (“Da’at” - Not Information, but Intimacy with God) In the pursuit of spiritual maturity, the Christian life is often marked by a quest for deeper knowledge. The Bible speaks frequently of knowledge and understanding, with many scriptures extolling their importance in the believer’s journey. However, there is a kind of knowledge that transcends mere intellectual understanding, a kind of knowledge that is deeply relational and intimate. This knowledge is not based on facts or data, but rather on a close, personal relationship with God. It is the knowledge that is born of devotion, not simply study, and it is referred to in Hebrew as da’at . In this chapter, we will explore da’at , not as information, but as a relational intelligence that emerges from the fear of God and devotion to Him. We will examine the theological depth of this concept, its implications for the believer’s life, and its practical outworking in everyday relationships with God and others. Ultimately, we will see that da’at is the kind of knowledge 60

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: that transforms a Christian’s life from the inside out, as it flows from the heart of one who fears God and desires to walk closely with Him. What is Da’at ? A Theological Foundation The Hebrew word da’at is often translated as "knowledge," but it is a much richer and deeper term than the simple acquisition of facts. While the English term “knowledge” can denote both factual information and relational intimacy, da’at in the Old Testament predominantly refers to a type of knowing that involves closeness, understanding, and experiential familiarity. It is knowledge that is not merely intellectual or theoretical but one that is derived from relationship and experience. One of the clearest illustrations of da’at can be found in the book of Genesis. In Genesis 4:1, the text says, “Now Adam knew Eve, and she conceived and bore Cain.” The word “knew” here is yada , which is closely related to da’at , and it refers to an intimate, relational knowing, a deep, personal connection that is not just physical but emotional, spiritual, and intellectual. Similarly, in the context of our relationship with God, da’at represents a knowing that goes far beyond facts about God to a personal and relational knowledge of His character, His heart, and His will. This relational intelligence is something that every believer is invited to cultivate. It is not something that can be acquired through mere study or 61

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: the accumulation of theological knowledge. Instead, it is birthed in the fertile soil of devotion, worship, and intimacy with God. As Proverbs 2:5 declares, “Then you will understand the fear of the Lord and find the knowledge of God” ( da’at Elohim ). This verse makes it clear that da’at is not merely a pursuit of intellectual knowledge but a deepening relationship with God that begins with the fear of the Lord. The Fear of the Lord as the Gateway to Da’at At the heart of da’at lies the fear of the Lord. As we explored in previous chapters, the fear of the Lord is not about being terrified of God, but about holding Him in the highest regard, honoring His majesty, revering His holiness, and submitting to His authority. The fear of the Lord is the foundation for all true wisdom and knowledge. Proverbs 9:10 reminds us that “The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom, and the knowledge of the Holy One is understanding.” Here, we see that the fear of the Lord opens the door to da’at , a deeper, more intimate knowledge of God. The more we fear God, the more we are drawn into a deeper relationship with Him, and the more we experience the transformative power of knowing Him personally. 62

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The fear of God is what allows us to approach Him with awe and reverence. It is through this posture that we begin to enter into the kind of intimate knowledge that da’at describes. Just as in a human relationship, we do not come to know someone deeply without giving them the honor and respect they deserve, so too do we come to know God in a deeper way through a humble and reverent approach to Him. The fear of the Lord, then, is the relational intelligence that prepares the heart to receive da’at , knowledge that is experiential, personal, and transformative. Da’at in the Context of Relationship The Bible makes it clear that da’at is not just about knowing things about God, but about knowing God Himself. In Jeremiah 9:23-24, God declares: “Let not the wise man boast of his wisdom or the strong man boast of his strength or the rich man boast of his riches, but let him who boasts boast about this: that he understands and knows me, that I am the Lord, who exercises kindness, justice, and righteousness on earth, for in these I delight, declares the Lord.” Here, God makes a stark distinction between worldly wisdom, strength, and wealth and the true knowledge that He desires, intimacy with Him. In this context, da’at is not about accumulating facts or theology for the sake 63

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: of personal pride. It is about understanding and knowing God, His character, His will, and His ways. It is relational intelligence that enables us to see the world through God’s eyes, to know His heart for us, and to walk in the fullness of His purpose for our lives. Jesus also highlights this relational dimension of knowledge in His high priestly prayer in John 17:3: “Now this is eternal life: that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom you have sent.” Eternal life, according to Jesus, is not simply about a future reality in heaven but about a present relational experience. Eternal life is da’at , a knowing of God that begins now and continues into eternity. In this sense, da’at is not static or theoretical knowledge. It is dynamic and relational. It is the knowledge that grows and deepens as we walk with God. It is the knowledge that is cultivated through prayer, worship, obedience, and communion with God. It is the kind of knowledge that cannot be gained through books alone but through a living, active relationship with the Creator. 64

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Devotion as the Source of Da’at Devotion is at the heart of da’at . True knowledge of God does not come through intellectual curiosity alone but through a life of devotion, devotion to God’s Word, to prayer, to worship, and to obedience. Devotion is the cultivation of the heart that longs for deeper intimacy with God and seeks to know Him more fully. In the life of Jesus, we see the perfect example of devotion leading to da’at . Jesus, in His earthly ministry, was constantly in communion with the Father. He withdrew to solitary places to pray, He meditated on God’s Word, and He lived in complete obedience to the Father’s will. It was through this devoted life that Jesus demonstrated the ultimate relational intelligence, a perfect knowledge of the Father, which He then shared with His disciples. Jesus said in John 5:19, “Very truly I tell you, the Son can do nothing by himself; he can do only what he sees his Father doing, because whatever the Father does the Son also does.” As followers of Jesus, we are invited into this same relational intelligence. Devotion to God, through prayer, worship, and obedience, opens our hearts to a deeper, more intimate knowledge of God. The more we devote ourselves to Him, the more we come to know Him. And the more we know Him, the more we are transformed into His likeness. 65

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The Practical Outworking of Da’at in the Christian Life The practical implications of da’at are far-reaching. When we cultivate relational intelligence with God, it affects every area of our lives. Let’s explore how da’at shapes our day-to-day existence. 1. In Our Prayer Life: The knowledge that comes from devotion leads to a richer, more intimate prayer life. As we grow in relational intelligence with God, our prayers move beyond mere requests and become a deeper communion with Him. Prayer becomes an opportunity to listen to God, to express our hearts to Him, and to align ourselves with His will. Through this relationship, our prayers are shaped by the intimacy we share with our Father. 2. In Our Relationships with Others: The more we know God, the more we are able to love others with the same love that God shows us. Da’at informs our relationships, teaching us how to be merciful, kind, and patient. As we grow in intimacy with God, we gain His heart for others, which enables us to relate to them with grace and understanding. 66

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: 3. In Our Work and Service: Da’at transforms our approach to work and service. When we know God intimately, we begin to see our work not just as a task or responsibility but as a means of honoring God. Whether we are in ministry, business, or the home, our work becomes an expression of our devotion to God, and we approach it with excellence and dedication. 4. In Our Decision-Making: The relational intelligence that comes from da’at empowers us to make decisions that align with God’s will. As we grow in our knowledge of God’s heart and character, we develop the discernment to make choices that reflect His desires for our lives. The Transforming Power of Da’at In this chapter, we have explored the concept of da’at , a knowledge of God that is born of devotion and relational intelligence. It is not simply intellectual understanding, but an intimate, personal knowledge that is cultivated through the fear of the Lord and a life of devotion. This knowledge transforms every aspect of the believer’s life, enabling them to live in alignment with God’s will and to experience His presence more fully. 67

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: As we seek to know God more intimately, we are invited to cultivate da’at , a relational intelligence that leads us into deeper communion with our Creator. Let us, therefore, devote ourselves to knowing God, not as an academic exercise but as a life-transforming relationship that shapes everything we do. In doing so, we will experience the fullness of life that God intends for us, a life marked by wisdom, love, and intimacy with the One who created us and calls us His own. 68

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Understanding: The Insight of the Reverent (“Biynah” - Discernment Through Devotion and Divine Relationship) In the Christian life, the pursuit of understanding is much more than the acquisition of information. It is the quest for wisdom and insight that is rooted in the reverence of God, a heart of devotion, and an intimate relationship with the Creator. In the Hebrew Scriptures, the term biynah is often used to describe the type of understanding that is not solely intellectual but deeply spiritual, relational, and transformative. It is a discernment that flows from a heart that is deeply connected to God, recognizing that true insight comes not from human wisdom but from divine revelation. In this chapter, we will explore the theological and practical implications of biynah , understanding it as the divine insight that emerges from a life lived in reverence to God. We will explore how biynah leads to discernment, shaping the believer’s ability to make decisions, engage in relationships, and serve in ministry.
