title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: John Stott comments: "The death of Ananias and Sapphira was a sign that God still acts in judgment under the new covenant. The church must be holy if it is to be powerful." ( The Message of Acts , 1990) This incident underscores a critical point: revival without reverence becomes reckless . The early church was entrusted with extraordinary power because they walked in extraordinary fear. They did not merely fear the consequences of sin; they feared the thought of grieving the Spirit or misrepresenting the holiness of God. Acts 9: The Church Multiplied in Fear and Comfort "Then the churches throughout all Judea, Galilee, and Samaria had peace and were edified. And walking in the fear of the Lord and in the comfort of the Holy Spirit, they were multiplied." (Acts 9:31) This verse offers a remarkable synthesis: the fear of the Lord and the comfort of the Holy Spirit are not opposites but complements. The church walked , that is, conducted their entire life, in an atmosphere charged with both holy fear and divine encouragement. This passage affirms that true fear of the Lord does not stifle joy , does not hinder growth , and does not limit supernatural power . Instead, it provides the environment in which all of these thrive. The Greek term for "walking" ( peripateō ) implies an ongoing lifestyle, not a fleeting emotion. 146

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Puritan writer John Owen once wrote, "When the fear of God rules in the heart, it will be seen in the life." The early church’s walk in fear was not theoretical, it was ethical, relational, and missional. They governed their decisions, their preaching, their giving, and their worship in alignment with the honor of Christ. Fear as Ecclesial Culture Theologian Craig Keener notes in his Acts: An Exegetical Commentary that the fear of the Lord in Acts is part of a broader "temple motif" wherein God dwells among His people, and such proximity necessitates purity and reverence (Keener, 2012). In essence, the early church saw itself not as a casual gathering of believers but as a dwelling place for God . This required a radical reorientation of how they treated one another, handled resources, and welcomed outsiders. In many ways, their ecclesiology was rooted not just in community but in consecration. Reclaiming the Fear of the Lord Today The modern church often suffers from familiarity without reverence , power without purity, and community without consecration. The early 147

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: church’s example invites us to recover a healthy, Spirit-inspired fear of the Lord that: ● Drives us to truthful speech and transparency in community (Acts 5) ● Compels us to prioritize God’s presence over performance (Acts 2) ● Calls us to honor God in governance, worship, and mission (Acts 9) To walk in the fear of the Lord today is to live with the conscious awareness that God is in our midst , not just as a doctrine, but as a holy reality. It is to consider every decision, private or public, in the light of His glory. Practical Implications 1. Church Leadership : Leaders must model and teach the fear of the Lord, not through manipulation or legalism, but through example, prayer, and scriptural fidelity. 2. Worship : Worship should be awe-filled and Christ-centered, not merely emotional or entertaining. 148

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: 3. Discipleship : Discipleship must include spiritual formation in reverence, teaching believers to live lives that honor Christ in all things. 4. Evangelism : The fear of the Lord was attractive, not repulsive. Authentic reverence draws seekers who long for what is real and holy. 5. Community Life : Truth-telling, generosity, and holiness should be marks of our communal existence, not afterthoughts. Conclusion The fear of the Lord was not a relic of the Old Testament but a revival force in the New. The early church knew that to walk with God in power required walking before Him in reverence. Their story is not merely descriptive but prescriptive. We, too, must recover the awe of a God who dwells among us. As we do, we will find ourselves, like the early church, purified, empowered, and multiplied. "Let us have grace, by which we may serve God acceptably with reverence and godly fear. For our God is a consuming fire." (Hebrews 12:28b-29, KJV) 149

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Perfect Love Casts Out Torment, Not Reverence (Understanding 1 John 4:18 in the Context of Healthy, Holy Fear) Introduction The apostle John, often referred to as the apostle of love, penned one of the most theologically rich yet misunderstood statements about fear in the New Testament: "There is no fear in love; but perfect love casteth out fear: because fear hath torment. He that feareth is not made perfect in love" (1 John 4:18, KJV). At first glance, this verse seems to suggest that fear, in all its forms, is antithetical to love and should be entirely cast off by the mature Christian. However, a closer reading of the text, in light of the broader canon of Scripture, reveals that what is being expelled is not holy reverence but rather the torment of dread. The Nature of the Fear Addressed in 1 John 4:18 The Greek word translated as "fear" in this passage is phobos , a term that can denote either reverential awe or a crippling terror. Contextually, John is 150

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: writing about judgment and assurance. The preceding verse reads: "Herein is our love made perfect, that we may have boldness in the day of judgment: because as he is, so are we in this world" (1 John 4:17, KJV). Clearly, the "fear" in question is not the reverential fear of God that leads to obedience and worship, but the dread of punishment and alienation from God. The torment ( kolasis ) John refers to is related to the expectation of judgment. As scholar D.A. Carson writes, “The fear that has to do with punishment is not the same as reverential awe. It is the fear of a slave who dreads the lash, not the son who delights in his father’s approval.” Perfect love, then, does not annihilate reverence, it dispels the kind of fear that sees God as an angry tyrant instead of a gracious Father. Fear in the Broader Biblical Witness This distinction is consistent throughout Scripture. Proverbs tells us that "the fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom" (Prov. 9:10), and Isaiah 11:2-3 prophetically speaks of the Messiah as one who will "delight in the fear of the Lord." Clearly, the Bible holds up a kind of fear that is desirable and even Christlike. How then do we reconcile this with John’s assertion? 151

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: We must understand that Scripture presents multiple facets of fear. The Old Testament uses terms like yirah and pachad , which cover a spectrum from dread to reverence. The New Testament does the same with phobos . The believer’s fear is not fear of eternal condemnation but the awe-inspired posture of one who recognizes God’s holiness and majesty. Jesus and the Right Kind of Fear Jesus himself exemplified this holy fear. Hebrews 5:7 tells us, "Who in the days of his flesh, when he had offered up prayers and supplications with strong crying and tears unto him that was able to save him from death, and was heard in that he feared." The word translated "feared" here is eulabeia , meaning godly reverence. This fear led Jesus to absolute submission to the Father’s will, even unto death. Far from contradicting John’s message, Jesus’ example affirms it. The fear cast out by perfect love is not reverence but terror. What remains, and what is even deepened, is a heart-level reverence that anchors the soul in obedience. Apostolic Interpretation and Church History In Acts 9:31, we read: "Then had the churches rest... and walking in the fear of the Lord, and in the comfort of the Holy Ghost, were multiplied." The early Church grew in power and purity, not despite the fear of the Lord 152

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: but because of it. Church fathers like Augustine and Chrysostom saw the fear of God as a necessary virtue. Augustine wrote, “Let us fear the loss of Him whom we love.” This fear was not a paralyzing dread but a motivating reverence. Reformer John Calvin distinguished between servile fear and filial fear, the former being the dread of a slave, the latter the loving reverence of a child. Calvin wrote, “The fear of God, which believers have, is not that which produces anxiety, which shakes the soul, and renders it discouraged... it is that which holds believers in obedience and love.” Devotional Implications for Today’s Believer Understanding the difference between tormenting fear and reverent fear is essential for spiritual maturity. Many Christians live under the weight of anxiety, afraid that God will reject them for their failures. But 1 John 4:18 reassures us that those who are in Christ can approach God with confidence. Yet, this confidence does not lead to arrogance. True love leads us into deeper awe, not less. The Christian who understands this distinction will: 153

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: 1. Walk in assurance, not anxiety - Knowing that God’s love is perfect and complete drives out the dread of punishment. 2. Maintain reverence, not recklessness - The absence of terror does not mean the absence of honor. Reverence still governs the believer’s thoughts, words, and actions. 3. Pursue holiness, not complacency - Love fuels obedience. As Paul writes in 2 Corinthians 7:1, "perfecting holiness in the fear of God." 4. Model reverence in relationships - In parenting, discipleship, and leadership, we model a fear of God that inspires love, not shame. Conclusion 1 John 4:18 does not denounce all fear; it discerns between torment and reverence. Perfect love, the kind revealed through Christ’s atoning work, casts out the fear of rejection and eternal punishment. But it births within us a holy reverence that transforms how we live, worship, and relate to God. As John Owen wrote, “The greatest sorrow and burden you can lay on the Father, the greatest unkindness you can do to Him, is not to believe that He 154

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: loves you.” And when you believe that, truly and deeply, the torment of fear will vanish, but the reverence will flourish. Let us therefore press into the love of God, confident and secure, and let our love be matured by reverence, as we delight in the One who both loves us perfectly and is worthy of all awe. 155

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The Fear of the Lord and Apostolic Counsel (How Paul and Peter Instructed the Church to Walk in Godly Fear) Introduction The apostolic writings are filled with practical and theological exhortations on the fear of the Lord. Far from being a concept relegated to the Old Testament or to the Sinai experience alone, the apostles, especially Paul and Peter, recognized the fear of the Lord as an indispensable part of the Christian life. In their pastoral counsel and epistolary teaching, they demonstrated that godly fear was not antithetical to grace but in fact reinforced a life shaped by the Gospel. In this chapter, we will explore how Paul and Peter understood and taught the fear of the Lord in the context of the New Covenant, emphasizing its formative power in spiritual growth, ecclesial purity, personal holiness, and divine mission. 156

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: 1. Paul’s Counsel on the Fear of the Lord Paul is sometimes mischaracterized as a theologian who focused purely on grace and not on reverence. Yet the Pauline epistles are rich with references to fear, particularly in a redemptive and sanctifying sense. a. 2 Corinthians 5:10-11 "For we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ... Therefore, knowing the fear of the Lord, we persuade others." Here, Paul ties the fear of the Lord directly to the final judgment. The Greek word translated as "fear" here is phobos , which often carries the nuance of reverence, awe, and recognition of authority. Paul’s point is that awareness of future accountability before Christ fuels both his own integrity and his evangelistic passion. This godly fear motivates mission and ministry. Scholar D. A. Carson notes, "Paul’s evangelism is not just driven by love, but also by accountability to the One who will judge. Godly fear and gospel urgency are not mutually exclusive." (Carson, The Cross and Christian Ministry , 2003) 157

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: b. Philippians 2:12-13 "Work out your own salvation with fear and trembling, for it is God who works in you..." Paul calls for an ongoing posture of reverence in our sanctification. This is not fear of being rejected by God but a profound awareness of His holiness and the weight of divine presence at work within us. Paul is affirming that believers are to take their spiritual growth seriously because God Himself is at work in them. John Stott comments, “This fear is not servile but filial. It is not fear of judgment, but of disappointing the God who now lives and works within us.” ( The Message of Philippians , 1984) c. Romans 11:20-22 "Do not become proud, but fear. For if God did not spare the natural branches, neither will He spare you. Note then the kindness and the severity of God." Paul here warns Gentile believers not to take their inclusion in God’s covenant lightly. This passage highlights the balance between divine mercy 158

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: and divine severity. The fear he calls for is one that acknowledges the majesty and justice of God. 2. Peter’s Counsel on the Fear of the Lord Peter also speaks extensively of fear, both in his apostolic witness and in his pastoral exhortation. a. 1 Peter 1:17 "And if you call on Him as Father who judges impartially according to each one’s deeds, conduct yourselves with fear throughout the time of your exile." Peter roots godly fear in our relationship with God as both Father and Judge. It’s a holy tension: we are sons, but we are also pilgrims accountable to a holy God. This verse links divine judgment with filial reverence. b. 1 Peter 2:17 "Honor everyone. Love the brotherhood. Fear God. Honor the emperor." 159

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: In this simple string of imperatives, Peter exhorts believers to maintain vertical reverence while living responsibly in a hostile society. “Fear God” is a distinguishing mark of those who live under divine Lordship. c. 1 Peter 3:15 "But sanctify the Lord God in your hearts, and always be ready to give a defense... with meekness and fear." Peter connects reverence for God with the tone and approach of Christian witness. Evangelism must be done in fear, not fear of men, but fear of God. It’s a posture of humility, not arrogance. Scholar Karen Jobes writes, “The fear Peter advocates is not dread, but sanctifying God in our hearts as holy. It is the awe that informs both our boldness and our humility.” ( 1 Peter , BECNT, 2005) 3. The Practical Outworking of Apostolic Fear Both Paul and Peter saw the fear of the Lord as: a. A Deterrent to Sin: Godly fear keeps us from presumption. It anchors us in holiness. Paul warns in 2 Corinthians 7:1: "Let us cleanse ourselves from every defilement... bringing holiness to completion in the fear of God." 160

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: b. A Catalyst for Mission: The apostles did not separate reverence from responsibility. As we’ve seen in 2 Corinthians 5:11, fear motivated persuasion. Paul and Peter taught that fear sharpens our vision for souls. c. A Marker of Maturity: Spiritual maturity does not outgrow fear. Instead, it deepens in reverence. The apostles walk a narrow road between joy in salvation and awe at the majesty of God. The more you grow, the more reverence becomes instinctive. d. A Preserver of Unity and Purity: In Acts 9:31, the early church is described as “walking in the fear of the Lord and in the comfort of the Holy Spirit.” This fear wasn’t oppressive; it was stabilizing and sanctifying. Godly Fear in Today’s Church To many believers, “fear” still sounds too archaic or severe. Yet the apostolic counsel teaches us that fear, rightly understood, is: ● A heart that trembles at God’s Word (Isaiah 66:2) ● A life that honors His commands (John 14:15) ● A posture that keeps pride in check (Romans 11:20) ● A tone of reverence in our words and actions (1 Peter 3:15) 161

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The fear of the Lord is not bondage, it’s our safeguard against self-centeredness, self-reliance, and sin. Let the words of Psalm 130:4 ring true: “But there is forgiveness with You, that You may be feared.” The grace that saves is the grace that teaches reverence (Titus 2:11-12). Apostolic counsel is clear: we are not to be flippant or casual with divine things. As recipients of God’s mercy, we walk humbly before Him, with godly fear as the fragrance of our devotion. Conclusion The early Church did not grow merely by charisma or organization, it grew because it walked in the fear of the Lord. Paul and Peter call us to this same pathway. As we embrace reverence and awe, our witness will sharpen, our hearts will purify, and our churches will testify to the holy presence of God. May we, like our apostolic fathers, walk in reverence, serve in humility, and glorify God in holy fear. Amen. 162

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: SECTION FIVE: LIVING IN THE FEAR OF THE LORD 163

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Walking in the Fear of the Lord Daily (Practical examples of how fear influences speech, choices, priorities) Introduction The biblical call to walk in the fear of the Lord is not an abstract religious idea but a dynamic, day-to-day lifestyle rooted in devotion, awe, and reverence. It is a manner of living that reflects the weight of God’s glory in our decisions, the holiness of His presence in our speech, and the gravity of His love in our priorities. The Scripture declares, "Blessed is every one that feareth the Lord; that walketh in his ways" (Psalm 128:1, KJV). Walking in the fear of the Lord is an intentional commitment to God-consciousness that affects every facet of our lives. Daily Fear In the Hebrew Bible, the word often used for "fear" is yirah , which connotes both awe and reverence. This fear is not the terror of a tyrant, but the trembling honor due to a transcendent, holy, and loving God. In the New Testament, phobos is the Greek term often rendered as "fear," and it 164

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: retains the same sense of reverential awe, especially as applied to believers in their relationship with God. Dr. John Murray noted that "the fear of God in which godliness consists is the fear which constrains adoration and love. It is the fear which consists in awe, reverence, honor and worship, and all of these on the highest level of exercise." (Murray, Principles of Conduct , 1957). Thus, the fear of the Lord is a sustaining principle that informs both theological understanding and ethical behavior. Influence on Speech The fear of the Lord sanctifies the tongue. Proverbs 10:19 warns, “In the multitude of words there wanteth not sin: but he that refraineth his lips is wise.” A reverent heart leads to restrained speech. It causes believers to avoid gossip, slander, lies, and corrupt talk, and instead choose words that edify and glorify God. R.C. Sproul rightly observed, “The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom, but it is not the end. It is the beginning point that directs the heart 165

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: into deeper reverence, commitment, and obedience.” ( The Holiness of God , 1985). Fear becomes the soil in which mature Christian virtues grow. Without it, love becomes sentimental, grace becomes cheap, and obedience becomes optional. Dietrich Bonhoeffer, in The Cost of Discipleship , warns of a Christianity without reverence: “Cheap grace is grace without discipleship, grace without the cross, grace without Jesus Christ, living and incarnate.” Walking in the fear of the Lord is walking with the weight of God’s holiness, understanding the cost of discipleship. To walk in the fear of the Lord is to live with continual awareness that our lives are lived coram Deo , before the face of God. This brings a sanctifying presence to the mundane. Washing dishes, driving a car, attending a board meeting, all can be acts of reverent service when done unto the Lord. Psalm 34:11-14 gives a clear devotional blueprint: “Come, ye children, hearken unto me: I will teach you the fear of the Lord... Depart from evil, and do good; seek peace, and pursue it.” Walking in the fear of the Lord involves both putting off sinful habits and cultivating virtue in thought, word, and deed. 166

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Daily prayers like, “Lord, help me walk in holy fear today,” or the spiritual discipline of pausing before decisions to ask, “Does this honor God?” can keep believers sensitive to God’s presence. Practical Examples ● Time management : Believers prioritize prayer, Word-study, and service over endless scrolling or entertainment. ● Speech : Instead of sarcasm or crude jokes, they speak life into others with their words. ● Finances : Tithing and generosity are non-negotiables because their money belongs to God. ● Relationships : They avoid gossip, honor elders, and seek peace, not conflict. ● Integrity : Even in anonymity, they act with integrity, knowing God sees in secret (Matt. 6:4). ● Parenting : Parents train children in godliness, modeling reverence and trust in God. 167

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Conclusion Walking in the fear of the Lord daily is both possible and essential. It is not legalism but liberty, a freedom to live in joyful awe of the One who loved us first. This fear does not drive us away from God, but draws us closer, keeping us anchored in humility and dependence. It transforms the ordinary into the sacred and makes every moment a canvas for worship. As we walk in reverence, our lives become testimonies of God’s glory, wisdom, and love. Let us then make it our daily aim to live in the fear of the Lord, not merely on Sunday mornings, but in boardrooms, living rooms, and quiet places of prayer. For "the Lord taketh pleasure in them that fear him, in those that hope in his mercy" (Psalm 147:11). 168

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Fearing God in a Culture That Fears Nothing (A Call to Courage, Holiness, and Conviction in a Godless Age) Introduction We live in a generation that prizes autonomy, exalts personal freedom, and dismisses moral absolutes. Cultural voices cry out, "Do what feels right," "Live your truth," and "No one can judge you." In such a climate, the biblical call to fear the Lord sounds foreign, if not offensive. But while the world has unlearned reverence, the people of God are being summoned afresh to rediscover a holy, transformative fear , the fear of the Lord that leads to wisdom (Proverbs 9:10), purity (2 Corinthians 7:1), and perseverance (Philippians 2:12). This chapter explores the relevance and necessity of godly fear in a time when fear of man, fear of missing out, and fear of rejection dominate , while the fear of God is mocked or neglected. Herein lies both our challenge and our charge. 169

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Fearless Yet Fearful Though modern culture touts itself as fearless, it is actually saturated with fear , fear of death, insignificance, rejection, and pain. But rather than turning to God, society builds idols: wealth, success, fame, and pleasure. Dr. Os Guinness notes, “We modern people have ‘dethroned’ God and enthroned the self. But self as sovereign is a cruel and weak king.” This dethronement of divine authority breeds moral relativism and spiritual apathy. Romans 3:18 captures this clearly: "There is no fear of God before their eyes." This absence is not benign; it results in a society unanchored by eternal truths. The moral decay and confusion we see around us are not random , they are symptoms of a God-forgetting world. Fear God, Not Man Throughout Scripture, fearing the Lord is not optional , it is foundational. Jesus Himself said: “Do not fear those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul. But rather fear Him who is able to destroy both soul and body in hell” (Matthew 10:28). 170

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The apostles echoed this. Peter, writing to believers suffering under persecution, exhorted them to “honor all people, love the brotherhood, fear God, honor the king” (1 Peter 2:17). Paul reminded the Corinthians that “knowing the fear of the Lord, we persuade men” (2 Corinthians 5:11). This fear is not terror or dread in the punitive sense but a deep, reverential awe that produces humility, obedience, and godly resolve. As John Murray famously wrote, “The fear of God is the soul of godliness.” Holy Fear in an Unholy Culture When the culture celebrates sin and scorns virtue, the call to fear God becomes counter-cultural. Holiness, once esteemed, is now seen as judgmental. Yet, Scripture declares, “Without holiness no one will see the Lord” (Hebrews 12:14). Holiness is not external legalism but inward reverence. It is living before God with sincerity, aligning one’s speech, sexuality, and stewardship with His Word. This is where the fear of God shines , in quiet conviction, in ethical business decisions, in sexual integrity, in forgiving when it’s hard, and in speaking truth in love. As theologian R.C. Sproul said, “We are not sinners because we sin; we sin because we are sinners. The fear of the Lord is that disposition in the heart 171

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: that acknowledges our sinful unworthiness before the majestic holiness of God.” Courage in the Fear of God It is ironic, but true: fearing God makes us fearless before men. Those who revere God will not tremble before human threats. Consider the Hebrew midwives in Exodus 1:17: “But the midwives feared God, and did not do as the king of Egypt commanded them, but saved the male children alive.” Their fear of God gave them the courage to defy Pharaoh. Daniel refused to stop praying, knowing the lion’s den awaited. Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego would not bow, even under threat of the furnace. Why? They feared God more than death. The book of Acts echoes this repeatedly: "We must obey God rather than men" (Acts 5:29). In a time when many are canceling truth to avoid cancellation themselves, fearing God restores our backbone. Conviction: The Bedrock of Godly Fear Conviction is not stubbornness; it is the fruit of a soul anchored in God’s Word and awed by His majesty. It means we have settled in our hearts that God is holy, His Word is true, and His standards are not suggestions. 172

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Conviction causes a believer to: ● Tell the truth when lying is convenient. ● Reject bribes when no one would find out. ● Refuse to laugh at sin when it’s normalized. ● Honor their marriage when the culture doesn’t. ● Train their children in godliness, not just success. Paul instructs us to “work out [our] own salvation with fear and trembling” (Philippians 2:12). Why? Because salvation is not a casual hobby. It’s a holy calling. Raising a God-Fearing Generation What we fear shapes what we pass on. If we fear man, we’ll raise children obsessed with peer approval. If we fear failure, we’ll raise anxious achievers. But if we fear God, we’ll raise sons and daughters who walk in truth and courage. Psalm 103:17-18 declares: “The mercy of the Lord is from everlasting to everlasting on those who fear Him, and His righteousness to children’s children, to such as keep His covenant.” Fearing God is a generational gift. 173

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The Church’s Prophetic Witness The Church must not imitate the culture , it must confront it. But this confrontation is not in arrogance or bitterness. It is in holy love, rooted in reverence. Acts 9:31 says, “Then the churches throughout all Judea, Galilee, and Samaria had peace and were edified. And walking in the fear of the Lord and in the comfort of the Holy Spirit, they were multiplied.” Notice: the fear of God was not an impediment to growth; it was a catalyst. If the Church will regain its awe, it will regain its voice. Practical Steps to Walk in Godly Fear 1. Daily Devotion: Begin each day in awe of God’s Word and presence. 2. Confession and Repentance: Keep your heart tender before Him. 3. Scripture Meditation: Read passages that highlight God’s holiness (e.g., Isaiah 6, Revelation 4). 4. Accountability: Surround yourself with people who revere God and pursue holiness. 174

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: 5. Obedience: Fear is perfected in obedience. Don’t delay doing what God asks. Conclusion Even in a culture that fears nothing, God is preserving a remnant who fear His name (Malachi 3:16-18). These are not perfect people, but reverent ones. They may be mocked, but they are immovable. And to them, God gives this promise: “To you who fear My name the Sun of Righteousness shall arise with healing in His wings” (Malachi 4:2). Let us be among them. Let us reclaim reverence. Let us walk in holy awe. Let us fear the Lord, and fear nothing else. 175

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The Reward of Those Who Fear the Lord (Everlasting Joy, Wisdom, Stability, and Divine Friendship) Introduction The concept of "fearing the Lord" is foundational to the Christian walk, woven throughout Scripture and intimately tied to the believer’s relationship with God. It is not merely an abstract idea or a theological concept, but a living, dynamic principle that shapes the heart, character, and life of the one who chooses to revere and honor God. For those who fear the Lord, the rewards are profound, far-reaching, and eternal. These rewards, ranging from joy and wisdom to stability and divine friendship, are manifestations of God’s faithfulness to His covenant people. In this chapter, we will explore how the fear of the Lord opens the door to these divine rewards and the profound impact they have on the believer's life. This exploration will take us deep into the theological significance of fearing the Lord, the biblical promises attached to it, and the practical outworking of these promises in the lives of ordinary Christians. We will delve into how Scripture portrays the fear of the Lord as a key to both 176

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: spiritual and practical benefits, providing a balance of scholarly insight, devotional reflection, and practical guidance for everyday living. The Fear of the Lord as the Foundation of True Blessing At the heart of the Old Testament teachings about the fear of the Lord lies the idea that those who revere God will experience His favor in ways that transcend earthly measures of success. The book of Proverbs, for instance, repeatedly underscores that the fear of the Lord is the "beginning of wisdom" (Proverbs 1:7, 9:10). This means that the proper attitude toward God, an awe-filled reverence, is the foundation from which all wisdom and understanding flow. To "fear" God is to acknowledge His supreme authority and to align oneself with His will, a posture that opens the heart to receive divine wisdom. Theologically, the fear of the Lord signifies a relationship based on trust and reverence for God’s holiness, power, and justice. The fear of God does not exist in isolation but is tied to other divine qualities such as mercy, love, and truth. These divine attributes come together in such a way that the believer, by revering God, experiences the fullness of life as it was meant to be lived. Indeed, fearing God is directly connected to walking in the fullness of His blessing. 177

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The Reward of Everlasting Joy One of the key rewards of fearing the Lord is everlasting joy. This joy is not contingent on external circumstances but is deeply rooted in the believer’s relationship with God. Psalm 112:1-2 declares, “Praise the Lord! Blessed is the man who fears the Lord, who greatly delights in His commandments! His offspring will be mighty in the land; the generation of the upright will be blessed.” The "blessed" individual here is not one who experiences fleeting happiness or momentary pleasure, but someone whose joy is anchored in the knowledge of God’s favor, presence, and promises. The joy that comes from fearing God is transformative and enduring. It is the kind of joy that remains steady through trials, suffering, and hardship, because it is not based on circumstances but on the unchanging nature of God. Jesus Himself spoke of this joy when He promised His disciples, "These things I have spoken to you, that My joy may remain in you, and that your joy may be full" (John 15:11). The joy of the Lord is a deep-seated peace that stems from the believer’s union with Christ, who is the ultimate embodiment of God’s holiness, goodness, and love. To fear God is to be positioned to experience His joy in a way that leads to contentment, fulfillment, and spiritual vitality. 178

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The Reward of Wisdom Scripture repeatedly asserts that wisdom is the natural byproduct of a life lived in reverence to God. Proverbs 2:6 says, “For the Lord gives wisdom; from His mouth come knowledge and understanding.” The fear of the Lord opens the door to divine wisdom, which is more valuable than silver, gold, or precious gems (Proverbs 3:13-15). This wisdom is not merely intellectual knowledge or practical insight, but a life-transforming understanding of God’s will and ways. It is the ability to see life from God’s perspective and to live in accordance with His divine order. Theologically speaking, wisdom, as described in the Bible, is not just an abstract concept but a person, Christ Himself. Paul writes in 1 Corinthians 1:24 that Christ is "the power of God and the wisdom of God." As Christians walk in the fear of the Lord, they are drawn into a deeper understanding of who Christ is, which in turn equips them with wisdom to navigate the complexities of life. In practical terms, this means that the believer who fears God is able to make choices that reflect divine insight, understanding, and discernment. 179

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The Reward of Stability Another profound reward of fearing the Lord is stability, spiritual, emotional, and relational stability. Proverbs 10:27 says, "The fear of the Lord prolongs days, but the years of the wicked will be shortened." This verse speaks not only to the idea of longevity but also to the sense of peace and steadfastness that accompanies a life devoted to reverence for God. Stability here is about more than avoiding the chaos of life; it is about being grounded in a secure foundation, knowing that one’s life is firmly established in God’s promises. The believer who fears the Lord experiences stability in their relationships, finances, and personal character. This is because the fear of the Lord leads to uprightness in all areas of life. Those who revere God are less likely to be swayed by worldly influences, unstable emotions, or fleeting desires. They are rooted and built up in Christ, the solid rock of their salvation. As such, they live lives marked by peace, integrity, and steadfastness in the face of adversity. The Reward of Divine Friendship Perhaps one of the most profound rewards of fearing the Lord is the gift of divine friendship. Psalm 25:14 expresses this beautifully: “The secret of the Lord is with those who fear Him, and He will show them His 180

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: covenant.” To fear God is to enter into a deep and intimate relationship with the Creator. It is to be drawn near to God in such a way that He reveals Himself to His faithful ones. The “secret” of the Lord refers to the intimate knowledge of God that is reserved for those who live in reverence before Him. This relationship is characterized by love, trust, and mutual respect. The one who fears the Lord is not a servant who stands far off but a friend who enjoys the nearness and friendship of God. Jesus affirmed this when He said to His disciples, “No longer do I call you servants, for the servant does not know what his master is doing; but I have called you friends, for all that I have heard from My Father I have made known to you” (John 15:15). The friendship that is promised to those who fear the Lord is not a distant or formal relationship; it is an active, living partnership with the Creator of the universe. Walking in the Fear of the Lord Today For the average Christian, the call to walk in the fear of the Lord is not an abstract theological concept but a practical invitation to live according to God’s wisdom, joy, and stability. What does this look like in everyday life? 1. Choosing Holiness Over Convenience: The fear of the Lord compels us to make choices that honor God, even when it is 181

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: inconvenient or unpopular. Whether in our workplaces, families, or communities, we are called to live according to God’s standards of righteousness, not the world’s. 2. Pursuing Wisdom Through Scripture: If wisdom is a reward of fearing the Lord, then we must actively seek it through the study of God’s Word. The believer who reveres God will delight in His commandments, finding in them the wisdom to navigate life’s complexities. 3. Cultivating a Spirit of Gratitude: The joy that flows from fearing the Lord is rooted in gratitude for God’s grace and faithfulness. When we reflect on all that God has done for us, we are moved to praise and thanksgiving, which leads to a deeper experience of His joy. 4. Seeking Divine Fellowship: Walking in the fear of the Lord means cultivating intimacy with God. We must approach Him not as distant servants but as beloved friends, desiring to know His heart and align ourselves with His will. 182

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Conclusion The fear of the Lord is not a transient or superficial concept but a deep, life-shaping principle that yields eternal rewards. From everlasting joy and divine wisdom to stability and intimate friendship with God, the benefits of fearing the Lord far surpass any earthly gain. The one who reveres God is marked by a heart that seeks His presence, values His wisdom, and walks in the light of His truth. As we fear the Lord, we are drawn into a deeper understanding of His character and promises, and we are transformed into the likeness of Christ. May we, as the people of God, continue to cultivate this holy fear in our lives, knowing that as we do, we will experience the fullness of the joy, wisdom, and stability that God promises to those who revere Him. The fear of the Lord is, indeed, the path to true blessing and eternal rewards. 183

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The Enduring Message: Fear God and Keep His Commandments (Ecclesiastes 12:13 , The Final Word on What Truly Matters) Introduction satisfaction. Solomon’s final conclusion, then, is that the essence of human life, what we are made for, lies in fearing God and keeping His commandments. The fear of the Lord, as described in Ecclesiastes 12:13, is not a mere emotional reaction to God’s power or authority; it is a comprehensive, reverential awe that encompasses the entirety of human life and existence. It is a fear that is not driven by terror or dread but by a deep respect, reverence, and recognition of God’s sovereignty, holiness, and wisdom. The fear of the Lord is the foundation upon which true wisdom, understanding, and purpose are built. In Proverbs 9:10, Solomon himself writes, “The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom, and the knowledge of the Holy One is understanding.” This declaration resonates with Ecclesiastes, where the 184

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: preacher repeatedly states that all of humanity’s pursuits are "vanity" unless they are centered on the fear of God. To fear the Lord is to acknowledge His ultimate authority over all creation, to submit our lives to His will, and to recognize that He alone is worthy of our worship and obedience. In theological terms, the fear of the Lord can be seen as a form of covenantal relationship. The fear that Scripture speaks of is not a reaction to arbitrary power but a recognition of God’s covenant faithfulness and the righteous demands He places upon His people. This fear, therefore, is tied to a life that is shaped by covenant obedience. As God’s people, we are called to live in a manner that reflects the holiness, justice, and mercy of the One we reverently fear. To fear God, then, is to be rightly aligned with His character, His will, and His purpose for our lives. Theological Implications of Keeping God’s Commandments The second part of Ecclesiastes 12:13 connects the fear of the Lord with obedience: “and keep His commandments.” To fear God is inherently tied to keeping His commandments, which are His revealed will for humanity. 185

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: The fear of God is not passive or abstract; it is an active, life-shaping reverence that compels obedience. Throughout the Bible, obedience to God is consistently linked with blessing, favor, and covenantal relationship. Jesus Himself affirmed this when He said, “If you love Me, keep My commandments” (John 14:15). The fear of the Lord and the keeping of God’s commandments are inseparable in the biblical narrative. Our reverence for God is demonstrated through our willingness to obey Him. In the Old Testament, God’s commandments were given as a means of preserving the holiness of His people and maintaining a righteous order in society. In the New Testament, Jesus reaffirms the commandments, particularly the two greatest commandments: to love God with all our heart, soul, mind, and strength, and to love our neighbor as ourselves (Matthew 22:37-39). These commandments are not burdensome but are the expression of God’s perfect love for humanity. They are the framework through which we can live in harmony with God’s will. For the Christian, keeping God’s commandments is not about legalistic adherence to rules but about living a life of gratitude, worship, and obedience out of love for Christ. The Apostle John makes this connection clear when he writes, “For this is the love of God, that we keep His commandments; and His commandments are not burdensome” (1 John 186

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: 5:3). Obedience, in this sense, becomes a joyful response to the fear of the Lord, not a duty or a chore. It is a natural outflow of a heart that reveres and loves God. Practical Application: Living Out the Fear of the Lord and Obedience in Everyday Life While Ecclesiastes 12:13 provides a profound theological insight into the meaning of life, it also offers practical guidance for the Christian walk. The fear of the Lord and obedience to His commands are not abstract principles but are lived out in the day-to-day choices, actions, and relationships of the believer. 1. In Our Speech: The fear of the Lord shapes the way we speak. James 3:10-12 reminds us that our words have the power to bless or curse, and the one who fears God will use their words to build up rather than tear down. Speaking in a way that reflects the reverence we have for God, whether in private or public, is an important sign of living in the fear of the Lord. 2. In Our Relationships: Those who fear God are called to love and serve others. Obeying the command to love our neighbors as ourselves is a direct outworking of our fear and reverence for God. 187

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: In our marriages, friendships, workplaces, and churches, we are called to honor God by loving and serving others in His name. 3. In Our Work and Daily Decisions: The fear of the Lord extends to every area of life, including our work and daily decisions. Proverbs 16:3 exhorts us to commit our works to the Lord, and they will be established. This means that whether we are at work, at home, or in any other setting, we are to live in a way that reflects God’s holiness, justice, and mercy. Our decisions should be guided by God’s Word, and our actions should be a reflection of His character. 4. In Our Worship: True worship flows from a heart that fears God. The fear of the Lord brings us into His presence with awe, reverence, and humility. Worship is not merely a ritual or an act of duty but a response to the greatness and majesty of God. When we approach God in worship, we do so with a heart that recognizes His holiness and sovereignty. The Enduring Relevance of Ecclesiastes 12:13 Ecclesiastes 12:13 remains as relevant today as it was when it was first written. In a world that often seeks fulfillment in fleeting pleasures, material success, or personal achievement, the preacher’s message stands 188

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: in stark contrast. It calls us to remember that true meaning in life is found not in our accomplishments or possessions but in our relationship with God. The fear of the Lord and obedience to His commands are the foundations of a life well-lived. In a time of cultural relativism, where truth is often seen as subjective, the call to fear God and keep His commandments is a radical declaration. It challenges us to live with conviction, to align our lives with God’s unchanging truth, and to submit to His authority in every area of life. Conclusion: The Whole Duty of Man Ecclesiastes 12:13 concludes with the reminder that fearing God and keeping His commandments is "the whole duty of man." This is the essence of what it means to be human, to live in reverence and obedience to our Creator. It is not an optional calling but the very purpose for which we were made. As we fear God and keep His commandments, we align ourselves with His divine order, experience His blessings, and fulfill the highest purpose of our lives. May we, like Solomon, reflect on the fleeting nature of the world’s pursuits and find our ultimate joy, peace, and fulfillment in the fear of the Lord and the obedience to His Word. This is the end of the matter, the final word on 189

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: what truly matters: “Fear God and keep His commandments, for this is the whole duty of man.” 190

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Pictorial Representation of Yirah The fear and fearfulness are usually each Yirah which is spelt " Yood Reysh Aleph Hey ." Ancient Language Modern Hebrew Yood is the picture of the hand or arm and means "to work or a mighty work or deed." Reysh is the picture of the head and means "the leader, the master, or the prince." 191

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Aleph is the picture of the ox and means "the strong leader, the head of the family, or God the Father." Hey is the picture of the man with uplifted arms and means "to behold, to pay attention to what follows, or the Holy Spirit as the Revelator." 192

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: 1. The Fear of the Lord A reverent awe, respect, and honor of God, which is expressed through obedience, worship, and a conscious awareness of His holiness and majesty. It is a central biblical concept that influences all aspects of the believer's life and relationship with God. 2. Reverence A deep respect and veneration for God, acknowledging His supreme authority and sovereignty. It is a heart attitude that recognizes God’s holiness and leads to awe and humility in His presence. 3. Wisdom The ability to discern and apply knowledge in a godly manner. In the context of the fear of the Lord, wisdom is not only intellectual understanding but practical living that aligns with God’s will. 4. Purity The state of being morally and spiritually clean, free from sin, and set apart for God’s purposes. The fear of the Lord leads to purity by 193

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: guiding believers to avoid sin and live according to God’s standards. 5. Holiness The quality of being set apart for God’s purposes, reflecting His perfect moral nature. Living in the fear of the Lord compels believers to pursue holiness in their thoughts, actions, and relationships. 6. Obedience The act of following God’s commands and instructions. True obedience is a natural outflow of the fear of the Lord, as reverence for God results in a life lived according to His Word. 7. Godly Fear A term used interchangeably with the fear of the Lord, referring to a fear that is rooted in reverence, respect, and awe of God’s holiness and authority. It is not a fear of punishment, but one of love and reverence. 8. Divine Wisdom Wisdom that comes from God and is rooted in a deep understanding of His Word, His ways, and His will. Divine wisdom 194

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: is granted to those who fear the Lord and seek to live in accordance with His truth. 9. The Spirit of the Fear of the Lord The Holy Spirit’s role in cultivating a reverence for God in the hearts of believers. In Isaiah 11:2-3, the Spirit of the Lord is said to rest upon the Messiah, equipping Him with a deep fear of the Lord, which He exemplified in His life. 10. Reverential Fear A kind of fear characterized by deep respect and reverence rather than dread or terror. It acknowledges God’s greatness and supremacy, leading to a life of devotion and submission. 11. Wisdom Literature A category of biblical texts, particularly in the Old Testament, that focuses on the principles of wise living, godliness, and moral conduct. Books like Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, and Job are considered wisdom literature, emphasizing the importance of the fear of the Lord as the foundation of wisdom. 12. Covenant A sacred agreement between God and His people, often characterized by promises, laws, and expectations. The fear of the 195

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Lord is integral to the covenant relationship, as it signifies the proper attitude of reverence and obedience to God’s commands. 13. Awe A feeling of wonder, amazement, and deep respect, especially in the presence of something majestic or holy. The fear of the Lord involves a sense of awe before God’s greatness and sovereignty. 14. Obedience to God’s Commands The action of aligning one’s life with God’s directives, as expressed in Scripture. The fear of the Lord leads believers to willingly and joyfully obey God’s Word. 15. Divine Friendship The intimate relationship between believers and God, characterized by mutual respect, trust, and love. The fear of the Lord deepens this friendship, as it nurtures reverence and devotion. 16. Godly Living A lifestyle marked by obedience to God’s Word, moral purity, and a desire to reflect His character. The fear of the Lord provides the foundation for godly living. 17. Fear of God’s Judgment A sense of reverence that recognizes God as the ultimate Judge, 196

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: who will hold every person accountable for their actions. While this fear is not driven by dread, it encourages a healthy respect for God’s righteousness and justice. 18. The Holiness of God God’s nature of being absolutely pure, morally perfect, and set apart from all sin. Understanding the holiness of God fuels the believer’s fear of the Lord, compelling them to live holy lives. 19. Faithful Servant A term used to describe believers who diligently serve God with reverence and obedience. The fear of the Lord inspires a heart of service that seeks to please God in all things. 20. Divine Fear Another term for the fear of the Lord, emphasizing its origin as a gift from God that enables believers to live in reverence and obedience to Him. 21. Spiritual Authority The power and responsibility entrusted to leaders in the Church to guide and teach others. The fear of the Lord is crucial for those in spiritual authority, as it ensures they lead with integrity, wisdom, and reverence. 197

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: 22. Reverence for God’s Word A deep respect and honor for Scripture as the inspired, authoritative Word of God. Those who fear the Lord hold God’s Word in the highest regard, allowing it to shape their beliefs and actions. 23. Eternal Rewards The blessings and rewards that await believers who faithfully walk in the fear of the Lord. These rewards include eternal life, divine wisdom, and a deeper relationship with God. 24. Sanctification The process of being made holy, set apart for God’s purposes. The fear of the Lord is central to sanctification, as it guides believers in the pursuit of purity and righteousness. 25. Divine Discipline The loving correction from God intended to refine believers and guide them back to righteousness. The fear of the Lord helps believers respond to God’s discipline with humility and a desire for growth. 26. Kingdom of God The reign of God in the lives of believers and in the world. The 198

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: fear of the Lord is foundational to entering and living in God’s Kingdom, as it aligns believers with His rule and authority. 27. The Fear of the Lord in Relationships The impact of reverence for God on interpersonal relationships. The fear of the Lord influences how believers interact with one another, fostering love, respect, and integrity in relationships. 28. The Wisdom of God God’s perfect understanding and ability to make the right decisions in every circumstance. The fear of the Lord is the key to accessing this divine wisdom, as it opens the heart to God’s guidance. 29. The Glory of God The manifested splendor and greatness of God, displayed in His creation, His acts, and His character. The fear of the Lord magnifies the glory of God in the life of the believer, leading them to honor and glorify Him in all things. 30. Fear of the Lord as Protection A biblical concept where walking in the fear of the Lord serves as a shield, providing spiritual protection and guidance in times of trouble. Those who fear the Lord are promised His care and deliverance. 199

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: 31. Eternal Life The gift of life with God that extends beyond death. The fear of the Lord leads to eternal life, as it cultivates a relationship with God that transcends the temporal world and prepares believers for the life to come. 32. Reverence in Worship A heart attitude that honors and respects God during times of corporate and private worship. The fear of the Lord informs how we approach God in worship, ensuring that it is done with humility, awe, and sincerity. 33. The Heart of the Fear of the Lord The inner disposition of a believer’s heart that recognizes God’s greatness and submits to His authority. This heart attitude is what ultimately leads to godly living, wise decisions, and deep reverence for God’s holiness. 34. Accountability to God The recognition that every individual will give an account to God for their life, actions, and decisions. The fear of the Lord encourages believers to live with a sense of responsibility and reverence for their Creator. 200

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: 35. The Fear of the Lord in the Church The corporate reverence and respect for God that should permeate the life of the Church. This fear guides the Church in purity, accountability, and faithful service, ensuring that it remains a place of holiness and truth. 36. Spiritual Growth The process of maturing in one’s relationship with God and becoming more like Christ. The fear of the Lord is integral to spiritual growth, as it leads believers to submit to God’s guidance and discipline. 201

text: ABOUT THE AUTHOR Pastor (Dr.) Daniel Folarin, fondly known as PDF , is the Lead Pastor of Great Haven Assembly, a thriving church with branches in Canada and Nigeria. He is very passionate about raising believers who are grounded in sound doctrine, filled with the Spirit, and aligned with God’s purpose, standing perfect and complete in all the will of God. Honored with a Doctor of Biblical Studies (Hon. D.B.S.) in recognition of his impactful work in Christian leadership and ministry, Dr. Folarin brings a compelling blend of theological depth, pastoral warmth, and strategic insight. He is committed to helping people not only believe, but truly become , to rediscover Jesus, reconstruct their faith, and live with spiritual vibrancy in a broken world. He is the author of several impactful books, both doctrinal and devotional, including Justification: An Apologia Series on Christian Doctrine and The Fear of God - What Does It Really Mean? He lives in Calgary, Alberta, with his beloved wife, Tolulade, and their three beautiful children: Charis, Eirene, and Doxa. 207

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: About the Book The Fear of the Lord is a profound exploration of one of the most pivotal yet often overlooked aspects of the Christian faith, reverence for God. Originally written in 2021, this book sought to lay the foundation for understanding the role of the fear of the Lord in the life of a believer. Now, with the release of this second edition, the book has been expanded, enriched, and restructured to provide a more robust and fuller treatment of the subject. In this edition, readers are taken on a transformative journey through Scripture, theology, and practical application, gaining deep insights into how godly fear shapes our relationship with God, influences our daily choices, and establishes a foundation for spiritual growth and maturity. The fear of the Lord is explored not just as a concept but as a living, active force that empowers believers to live holy lives, make wise decisions, and engage with the world in ways that honor God. Through detailed studies of key Biblical passages, theological insights, and powerful examples from church history and contemporary Christian life, this book invites readers to reconsider how they understand reverence for God. Far from being a term rooted in dread or torment, the fear of the Lord is shown to be a source of life, wisdom, stability, and joy. This edition dives deeper into the rich theological foundation behind the fear of the 208

title: THE FEAR OF GOD - 2nd edition.docx
text: Lord, linking it to holiness, justice, mercy, and the very nature of God’s character. This second edition of The Fear of the Lord is not only a theological treatise but also a devotional call to live in awe and reverence before our Creator. It is a guide to walking in the wisdom and understanding that come from recognizing the greatness of God and responding with a heart full of love, humility, and worship. Whether you are a seasoned believer seeking to deepen your walk with God, or a new Christian exploring the depths of Scripture, this book will challenge, inspire, and transform your understanding of what it means to fear the Lord in a way that leads to lasting change and a closer relationship with the Father. 209

title: THE GOOD SHEPHERD
text: “Ken Bailey is the consummate expert on the cultural context of first-century life. And here he applies these skills to one motif—the shepherd—illustrating how it is em- ployed in a variety of biblical stories and parables. His interpretations use a variety of tools unknown to many of us: ancient versions, original languages, cultural anthro- pology, ancient Middle Eastern writers who shared this ancient culture, and his own personal career embedded in a crosscultural life in the Middle East. Always insightful, always fresh, consistently surprising, Bailey has produced yet another book that will get many of us rethinking beloved passages of Scripture in completely new ways. ” Gary Burge, professor of New Testament, Wheaton College “‘The Lord is my shepherd. ’ There is no more beloved picture of God’s relationship with his people. Jesus’ declaration that he is ‘the good shepherd’ has comforted Christians for centuries. Using his impressive knowledge of Scripture and his expe- rience living for many years in the Middle East, Bailey deepens and enriches our understanding of what it means to be members of God’s flock. ” Tremper Longman III, Robert H. Gundry Professor of Biblical Studies, Westmont College “Kenneth Bailey refreshes the souls of readers with deep textual insights and helpful contextual background to safely shepherd readers through the thousand-year story of Psalm 23. He makes several stops along the way in the Prophets, before settling into the Gospels where he deftly introduces us to a fresh understanding of the Good Shepherd. Anyone who loves the 23rd Psalm will love this book. ” David Lamb, associate professor of Old Testament, Biblical Theological Seminary; author of God Behaving Badly “‘Y ou prepare a table before me . . . my cup overflows.

title: THE GOOD SHEPHERD
text: anding of the Good Shepherd. Anyone who loves the 23rd Psalm will love this book. ” David Lamb, associate professor of Old Testament, Biblical Theological Seminary; author of God Behaving Badly “‘Y ou prepare a table before me . . . my cup overflows. ’ What a feast Ken Bailey has prepared for us in this book, and what an overflowing cupful of insights and illumi- nation. If you ever thought there was nothing more you could ever learn or preach about the biblical image of the good shepherd, this book has an abundance of sur - prises. Again and again I thought, ‘Why have I never seen that before?’—as Ken draws on his rich lifetime’s experience of Middle Eastern culture to explore biblical texts, and particularly when he shows eye-opening connections between Old and New Testament texts around the shepherd theme. This is a book that simultaneously brings the Bible to life, and exalts the Lord Jesus Christ by deeply enriching our understanding of his mission and claims as the Good Shepherd. ” Christopher J. H. Wright, International Ministries Director, Langham Partnership “Deftly wielding the tools of biblical scholarship, story-telling, and cultural exegesis, Ken Bailey has restored to its full color and brilliance one of the most magnificent images of Scripture—the character and work of the Good Shepherd. title: THE GOOD SHEPHERD
text: PREFACE My journey with the theme of this book began with seven years of my childhood in the late 1930s in the south of Egypt, where my family lived in the village of Edmu in the province of Minya. After some years of absence, that journey continued from the mid 1950s and into the 1960s when I served for ten years with the rural churches of the Egyptian Evan- gelical Church in the same district. This was followed by twenty years in Lebanon, and finally by a further ten years living and teaching at the edge of Bethlehem in Israel/Palestine at the Tantur Ecumenical Institute, where shepherds grazed their flocks around us. Thus, for nearly fifty years, Middle Eastern shepherds with their flocks were a part of the larger context in which I grew up and then lived and taught the New Testament. It was my privilege to have laymen and clergy in three coun- tries as my students who had herded sheep for extended periods in the Eastern Mediterranean. Sheep-herding in the Middle East falls into two overlapping categories. First, there are the seminomadic sheep herders who often have large herds and spend months away from their villages following seasonal rains and grazing the open pasture land. Second, there are settled village families, each of whom owns a few sheep. It is customary for a number of families to combine their animals, and for one young man (or two young women) to form them into a herd and take them out each day to graze. This second pattern is widespread and more universal than the first. Some villagers de- velop large herds and still return to the village each night. Countless books and articles have been written on the texts selected for Good Shepherd_FIXED.indd 13 10/8/14 10:38 AM

title: THE GOOD SHEPHERD
text: 14 The Good Shepherd this work. Of particular interest are the brief monographs written by Middle Easterners who in their earlier years themselves herded sheep. Each of these shepherds emigrated to the West, and at some point in their lives, using their own personal experience as shepherds, published their perceptions of Psalm 23. I have chosen five such accounts in which these two patterns are ob - servable. The five are as follows: Rev. M. P . Krikorian grew up in the late nineteenth century in the moun- tains behind the cities of Tarsus and Adana, which are now in Turkey. While he was in high school his family acquired a herd of a hundred sheep, and his father selected him to herd them. Initially the young man “wept bitterly at the suggestion” because it meant that he was obliged to drop out of school.1 Y et some years later after becoming a pastor in the Middle East and then surviving the Armenian Genocide, he emigrated to the West and wrote a book on the Twenty-Third Psalm, which he dedicated to “My beloved father who gave me that first chance to become a shepherd. ”2 The family had a long tradition of herding sheep, but for Krikorian it was a new experience. He acquired shepherding skills from the family, from other shepherds and from the sheep themselves. In his case, he returned each night to his family home in the village. His book is titled The Spirit of the Shepherd: An Interpretation of the Psalm Immortal.3 Rev. Faddoul Moghabghab was born in the city of Ain-Zehalta in the mountains of Lebanon. As a young man he herded the family’s small flock. As he notes, “My father owned a number of sheep and lambs; it is customary for all the people of Mount Lebanon to keep a few sheep.

title: THE GOOD SHEPHERD
text: Preface 15 deacon of the Syrian Orthodox Church, Dr. Lamsa grew up in a community of seminomadic sheep herders in Syria. He writes, My ancestors for untold generations were sheep raising people. My father and my mother loved and tended sheep. I was raised in a sheep camp. We lived in a tent made of the hair of goats just as Abraham and Isaac did. Like other boys, I was taught and disciplined by the shepherds. Since my father was chief shepherd, I was taught thru [sic] his wisdom.5 Lamsa became a Syriac scholar and translated the fourth-century Syriac Bible (the Peshitta) into English. He also published many other books re- cording his studies of and reflections on that same Syriac Bible. Along with these numerous works he published a short study titled The Shepherd of All: The Twenty-Third Psalm. Stephen A. Haboush grew up in Galilee. In his youth he became the shepherd of the family flock of sheep. Later in life he immigrated to America and wrote a book titled My Shepherd Life in Galilee: With an Exegesis of the Shepherd Psalm.6 His short work is authentic and helpful. Abrahim Mitri Rahbany was not a shepherd, but he grew up in the moun- tains of what is now Lebanon, surrounded by farmers and shepherds. He observed those shepherds with their sheep on a daily basis for all of his early years. Later in life he wrote a book that covers many aspects of Eastern life and the Gospels. This book, titled The Syrian Christ, includes a helpful chapter on sheep and shepherds.7 In addition to these five Middle Easterners we can note two Westerners, one American and the other British. William Thompson lived and served in Lebanon for twenty-five years as a missionary of the American Presby- terian Church.

text: 16 The Good Shepherd years and authored Jesus of Palestine and Prophets of Palestine, which have valuable information about the biblical text and the world of Middle Eastern shepherds.9 Of more limited value are recent accounts by Westerners who were at various times in their lives exposed to shepherds and their sheep. Leslie D. Weatherhead of England gathered data regarding sheep from as far away as India,10 and Phillip Keller owned and operated a sheep ranch in East Africa and was involved with sheep in the American West. 11 These authors apply experience from sheep-herding in Africa, Asia and America to Psalm 23. Occasionally it fits and is useful. In addition to these English-language sources, this study has relied heavily on the Arabic writings on the good shepherd texts that are available to us. Much has been lost across the centuries, but a few treasures, ancient and modern, have survived. I have had the following available to me. Arabic Language Commentaries Ibn al-Salibi, Dionesius. Kitab al-Durr al-Farid fi Tafsir al-‘ Ahd al-Jadid [The Book of Precious Pearls in the Interpretation of the New Testament]. 2 vols. Cairo: ‘ Abd al-Masih Dawlayani, 1914. Ibn al-Salibi was bishop of Amad (Diyarbakr). He wrote in Syriac and died in a.d. 1164. This commentary was translated into Arabic in the Monastery of Za‘farani (Southeast Turkey) in 1728. Ibn al-Tayyib. Tafsir al-Mashriqi [A Commentary on the Four Gospels]. 2 vols. Cairo: Tawfiq Press, 1910. Ibn al-Tayyib was a part of the Church of the East. He was a brilliant scholar, a medical doctor, a translator, an author and secretary to the patriarch of his church. He wrote books on medicine and theology. He produced philosophical works along with a number of high-quality commentaries. These two volumes are among them.

The Gospel According to Saint Matthew: Studies, Interpretation and Explanations [Arabic]. Cairo: Monastery of Saint Maqar, 1999. Father Matta al-Miskin (d. 2009) was a monk at the Coptic Orthodox Monastery of St. Makar in Wadi Natron (between Cairo and Alex- andria). He spent most of his life writing extensive commentaries on the Gospels, which were published by his monastery. His work is little known outside of the Arabic-speaking Christian world. His monastic spirituality is deep, and these volumes will no doubt in time be trans- lated into English and bless the worldwide church for many generations to come. Sa‘id, Ibrahim. Sharh Bisharit Luqa [Commentary on the Gospel of Luke]. Cairo: Middle East Council of Churches, 1980. ———. Sharh Bisharit Yuhanna [Interpretation of the Gospel of John]. Cairo: Dar al-Thaqafa, n.d. Rev. Dr. Ibrahim Sa‘id was a pastor and scholar of the Egyptian Evan- gelical Church in the mid-twentieth century. He was a brilliant preacher in the finest Classical Arabic, and under his leadership the Qasr al- Dubara church on the famous Maydan al-Tahrir in central Cairo was built. He authored extensive commentaries on Luke and John. Arabic Translations of the Bible Translation is always commentary. The translators must try to understand the text, and only then can they present it in the receptor language. This study has made use of a collection of twenty versions of the New Testament into Arabic and four versions of the Old Testament. I have managed to ac- quire copies of all the printed Arabic New Testaments that began to appear at the very end of the sixteenth century.

I have also relied on information I was privileged to glean over two de- cades from several of my students at the Near East School of Theology, Beirut, Lebanon, who herded sheep in their early years. From their living experiences I have gained many insights. Finally, it was my privilege on numerous occasions to observe and question shepherds with their sheep in the south of Egypt (ten years), the mountains of Lebanon (twenty years) and in the West Bank, Israel/Palestine, near Bethlehem (ten years). While writing parts of this book I was able to observe numerous shepherds leading their flocks among the terraced hills around Bethlehem. My deepest gratitude must be extended to countless friends over a sixty- year period that stretch from the south of Egypt to Armenia who have done their best to keep me on “the paths of righteousness” in my interpretation of this great biblical theme. Without the help, guidance and support my editor, Andrew Le Peau of InterVarsity Press, this work would never have been written. His wisdom and friendship have sustained me in all stages of the composition of this book. I wish also to express my unending gratitude to my personal secretary and copyeditor, Sara B.
