title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: Truth and Freedom One of the most powerful aspects of truth in Christianity is that truth leads to freedom . In a world where many see truth as a tool for oppression or control, Christianity offers a radically different vision. Truth, as embodied in Christ, is not about dominating or manipulating others, it is about setting us free. Jesus Himself said, “And you will know the truth, and the truth will set you free” (John 8:32). This freedom is not the freedom to live as we please, but the freedom to live as we were created to live. It is freedom from sin, freedom from falsehood, and freedom from the slavery of selfish desires. The truth of the gospel frees us from the lies and deceit that bind us, and it gives us the power to live in accordance with God’s will. Truth is the foundation of true liberty. Without truth, there can be no true freedom. In a society that prizes individual autonomy, we often forget that true freedom is found in the revelation of God’s truth. To know the truth is to be set free from the lies that enslave us, whether those lies are about our identity, our purpose, or our moral choices. In Christ, we find the ultimate liberation, a freedom that is not dictated by external forces, but by an internal transformation that comes from knowing the truth and living by it. The Power of Truth to Heal and Restore In the end, truth is not just an abstract concept; it is a healing power . The truth of the gospel, what God has done through Jesus Christ, has the power to heal the brokenness in our lives. It heals our broken hearts, restores our fractured relationships, and transforms our minds. In a world filled with confusion and deception, truth brings clarity, hope, and peace.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: hrough Jesus Christ, has the power to heal the brokenness in our lives. It heals our broken hearts, restores our fractured relationships, and transforms our minds. In a world filled with confusion and deception, truth brings clarity, hope, and peace. As we embrace the truth of God’s Word and live according to it, we are not merely gaining knowledge. We are being transformed . The truth changes 24

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: us from the inside out. It renews our minds, strengthens our will, and molds our character into the image of Christ. Truth is the foundation of true healing, and through it, God’s redemptive work continues to unfold in our lives and in the world. The Call to Embrace the Truth In a world where truth is often seen as relative or negotiable, Christianity calls us to a higher standard. Truth exists , and it is revealed to us in the person of Jesus Christ. Truth is not just about knowing facts, it is about understanding reality as God sees it, and living in accordance with His will. Christianity offers us a framework for knowing the truth and living by it, one that leads to freedom, healing, and fulfillment. In a world of confusion and contradiction, the truth found in Christ offers clarity. It offers peace. And ultimately, it offers the path to life, true, abundant, and eternal life. A FAITH THAT ENGAGES THE REAL WORLD Christianity, at its core, is not a theoretical or abstract set of ideas; it is a living, breathing faith that engages deeply with the complexities and challenges of the real world. Unlike some worldviews that retreat from life's hardest questions or offer simplistic answers, Christianity confronts them directly, with honesty, clarity, and compassion. In a world full of suffering, confusion, and brokenness, Christianity doesn’t offer a "pie-in-the-sky" idealistic view of life. It acknowledges the harshness of reality. It doesn’t pretend that life here and now will be free from pain, sorrow, or struggle. Jesus Himself, the very heart of the Christian faith, did not come to earth to shield Himself from human suffering but to walk through it alongside us . He was acquainted with grief and suffering (Isaiah 53:3), and He bore the weight of humanity’s 25

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: pain on the cross. Christianity acknowledges suffering but offers a radically different perspective. It assures us that suffering does not have the final word , and it is not meaningless. Christians do not escape the difficulties of this world, but they face them with the assurance that the world is not all there is . Christianity offers a redeemed life, not a perfect one. It offers hope, not based on the fleeting circumstances of this life, but based on eternal promises rooted in the gospel. In Christ, the ultimate answers to life's deepest questions, questions about identity, suffering, purpose, and truth, are found. But more than just answers, Christianity offers a relationship with the One who is the answer to it all. A Faith That Sees the World As It Is Christianity doesn't ignore the complexities of human existence; it sees them clearly and without denial. Christianity doesn’t offer an easy escape from life’s harsh realities but gives us the spiritual resources to face them. The Christian faith gives us a framework that allows us to engage the world as it truly is, not as we wish it to be. This view of the world is grounded in God’s creation and His ultimate purpose for it. The goodness of creation, though marred by sin, still reflects God’s glory. Every person, every culture, every story is woven into this grand narrative, and each is valuable because it comes from God. While humanity has fallen short, God, through Jesus Christ, has begun the work of redemption, a work that will be fully realized in the new heavens and new earth (Revelation 21:1-5). Christianity holds this tension in perfect balance: the world is broken, yet it is redeemable in Christ. The Christian faith teaches that we are in the world, but not of the world (John 17:14-16).

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: in the new heavens and new earth (Revelation 21:1-5). Christianity holds this tension in perfect balance: the world is broken, yet it is redeemable in Christ. The Christian faith teaches that we are in the world, but not of the world (John 17:14-16). This means that while we engage with the world’s struggles, we do so with the understanding that our ultimate allegiance is to 26

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: a higher kingdom. We do not place our hope in the transient things of this world, but in the eternal promises of God that transcend earthly suffering. A Faith That Demonstrates Love and Compassion While Christianity provides intellectual answers to life’s profound questions, it also demonstrates those answers in the way believers live. The gospel is not just a theological statement; it is a call to action. Jesus, who is the embodiment of truth, did not merely teach truth, He lived it. His life was marked by compassion, mercy, and sacrificial love. His ministry was characterized by reaching out to the marginalized, the broken, and the hurting, and He continues to call His followers to do the same. Christianity is a faith that transforms hearts, but it doesn’t stop there, it transforms lives and communities. When the love of Christ takes root in a person’s heart, it compels them to reach out to others in love. This is not a love based on convenience or self-interest but a selfless, sacrificial love that seeks the welfare of others. It is a love that serves, sacrifices, and stands in solidarity with the oppressed. At its core, Christianity calls us to live out our faith by loving God and loving our neighbors (Matthew 22:37-39). This love isn’t abstract or theoretical, it’s deeply practical. Christianity challenges its followers to engage with the world, to address its needs, and to embody the love of Christ in real, tangible ways. Whether it’s through acts of kindness, standing for justice, or offering a word of encouragement, the Christian faith calls us to actively engage in the world around us. A Faith That Does Not Shy Away From the Difficult Questions Christianity does not shy away from the difficult questions. In fact, it invites them.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: fering a word of encouragement, the Christian faith calls us to actively engage in the world around us. A Faith That Does Not Shy Away From the Difficult Questions Christianity does not shy away from the difficult questions. In fact, it invites them. Why is there suffering? What happens after death? What is the purpose of life? Christianity doesn’t provide pat answers that 27

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: dismiss these struggles; rather, it engages them head-on, offering profound insights and eternal hope. For example, the question of suffering is deeply personal and painful. Many people ask, “Why does God allow suffering?” Christianity does not provide a simple answer, but it does give us the truth of God’s presence in suffering. God entered our suffering through Jesus Christ, and He is not distant from us in our pain. He stands with us, offering hope in the midst of despair. Christianity teaches that suffering is not the end of the story, it is part of the larger narrative of redemption that God is unfolding in history. Similarly, the question of death, and what happens after it, is a question that many fear but few are brave enough to face. Christianity does not ignore death; it confronts it directly. Jesus’ death and resurrection are the ultimate answers to the fear of death. Through His sacrifice, believers are assured of the promise of eternal life (John 14:19). Death, while still an enemy, has been defeated in Christ, and those who are united with Him have the hope of eternal life beyond the grave. Why I Am a Christian: Encountering the One Who Is the Answer At the heart of the Christian faith is not just a set of doctrines, but a person , Jesus Christ, who is the answer to all of life’s deepest questions. The reason I am a Christian is not merely because the gospel provides logical answers or philosophical satisfaction to these profound questions, but because in Jesus , in His life, death, and resurrection, I have encountered the One who is the answer. Through Him, I have found purpose, hope, and peace. I have discovered that life is not meaningless or accidental, but is deeply anchored in God’s eternal plan.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN

I have found that truth is not a matter of subjective opinion, but is embodied in the very person of Jesus. 28

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN

Conclusion Christianity is not a faith that retreats from life’s toughest challenges, it meets them head-on with honesty, clarity, and compassion. It does not promise a life free from struggle, but it promises a redeemed life anchored in truth and filled with grace. Through the gospel, we encounter the One who is the ultimate answer to the questions of who we are, why we suffer, and what our purpose is. And this is why I am a Christian, because in Jesus Christ, I have found the answer to everything that truly matters. 29

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: CHAPTER 2 CREATED IN THE IMAGE OF GOD The Divine Signature in Every Soul In the opening chapters of Genesis, the Bible reveals a profound and foundational truth: humanity is uniquely created in the image of God. Genesis 1:26-27 declares, "Then God said, 'Let us make man in our image, after our likeness... So God created man in his own image, in the image of God he created him; male and female he created them." This statement lays the groundwork for understanding not only our identity as human beings but also our purpose in the world. It speaks to our dignity, value, and the special role we play in God's creation. The concept of the Imago Dei , the Image of God, suggests that humans, unlike the rest of creation, reflect certain divine attributes that mirror God's nature. These attributes include rationality, creativity, morality, and relationality. Through these qualities, humans have the capacity to reason, create, choose between right and wrong, and engage in meaningful relationships. They are equipped to be stewards of the earth, making ethical decisions that honor God and care for creation. Moreover, being made in the image of God means that humanity is entrusted with a unique role: to represent God's presence on earth. Humans are not mere accidents or insignificant creatures; they are ambassadors, called to reflect God's nature in the world and to steward His creation with care and responsibility. Unlike other creatures, who exist in the world but do not bear God's image in the same way, humans are set apart as those who can know, relate to, and reflect God in ways that are meaningful. This divine signature marks every individual, regardless of their status, race, or background. Every person has inherent dignity and worth because 30

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: they are created in the image of God. This truth compels us to honor the sacredness of human life, treat others with respect, and strive to live in accordance with the values embedded in God's image. When we understand the Imago Dei , we begin to see the world, and those around us, through the lens of divine significance, recognizing that every human life has intrinsic value, and that every person is created with purpose. In this sense, the Imago Dei calls us to live lives that reflect the nature of God. It invites us to reflect His creativity, His morality, and His relationality through our actions, decisions, and relationships. Whether in our work, our homes, or our communities, we are tasked with embodying the values of our Creator in all that we do. This understanding also calls us to recognize the sacredness of life and to treat one another with the dignity that God has bestowed upon us. Human Dignity and the Imago Dei The doctrine of the Imago Dei (Image of God) is central to understanding human dignity in Christian theology. It asserts that every individual, regardless of their achievements, status, or abilities, possesses intrinsic value and dignity simply because they are created by God in His image. This perspective challenges many of the societal norms that often reduce human worth to what one can produce or accomplish. In contrast, Christianity teaches that human dignity is not contingent upon performance, wealth, or social standing. It is an inherent gift bestowed by God upon every person. In a world where worth is frequently tied to success, power, or material gain, the Imago Dei provides a radical alternative.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN

In a world where worth is frequently tied to success, power, or material gain, the Imago Dei provides a radical alternative. It affirms that each person, regardless of their circumstances, whether they are rich or poor, healthy or ill, young or old, capable or disabled, has inherent value. This dignity is not earned or bestowed by society, but is rooted in the very act of creation. Every human being, from the moment of conception, carries the 31

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: divine signature of their Creator. As the Catechism of the Catholic Church beautifully states, "The dignity of the human person is rooted in his or her creation in the image and likeness of God." This teaching has profound implications for how Christians are called to relate to one another. If every person is made in the image of God, then every person is deserving of respect, honor, and love. This belief compels believers to treat others with compassion, kindness, and justice. It challenges us to reject prejudice, discrimination, and dehumanization, recognizing that the person in front of us, regardless of their background, appearance, or beliefs, is a reflection of God's image. The Imago Dei also calls Christians to engage in acts of social justice and to stand against systems that exploit, oppress, or devalue individuals. If all people are made in God’s image, then all people must be treated with the dignity that flows from that truth. This includes advocating for the rights of the marginalized, the poor, the oppressed, and the vulnerable. Whether in our personal relationships, in our churches, or in our communities, Christians are called to embody a culture of respect and justice for all people, especially those who are most often overlooked or mistreated. This vision of human dignity also invites believers to look beyond the surface, to see each individual not merely as a product of their circumstances or accomplishments, but as a unique creation, fashioned by the hands of a loving God. In doing so, Christians are empowered to affirm the inherent worth of every person, recognizing that the ultimate value of a human life is found in its divine origin.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: Living Out the Imago Dei Understanding that we are made in God’s image is not merely an abstract theological concept, it has profound, practical implications for how we live, interact with others, and engage with the world around us. The doctrine of the Imago Dei challenges us to reflect God’s nature in our everyday actions, relationships, and decisions. It calls us to live in ways that honor the dignity of every human being and to fulfill our role as responsible stewards of God’s creation. Here are several ways the Imago Dei shapes our actions and worldview: Value Life Recognizing that all human beings are made in the image of God compels us to affirm the sanctity of life at all stages. From the unborn child to the elderly, every human life has intrinsic value. This belief calls us to advocate for the most vulnerable, those who cannot speak for themselves, such as the unborn, the disabled, the elderly, and the oppressed. Christians are called to stand against practices that devalue or destroy life, whether through abortion, euthanasia, or neglect. This respect for life is also reflected in the way we treat others, seeing in each person a unique creation made in God’s image, worthy of dignity and honor. Pursue Justice The Imago Dei calls Christians to pursue justice, working toward a society where every person is treated equitably and with respect. The inherent dignity bestowed upon all people by God demands that we address systemic injustices such as racism, sexism, poverty, and inequality. Christians are called to advocate for the marginalized, to support fair policies, and to engage in the work of reconciliation. The biblical call to “love your neighbor as yourself” (Matthew 22:39) extends to our collective 33

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: responsibility to create a just society where all individuals have the opportunity to thrive. Justice is not merely a social or political concern; it is a deeply spiritual one. In Micah 6:8, God reveals His desire for His people to act justly, love mercy, and walk humbly with Him. By actively pursuing justice, we reflect God’s own character, who is perfectly just and merciful. Every act of advocacy for justice is an expression of our commitment to honor the dignity of those created in God’s image. Practice Compassion As those who are made in God’s image, we are called to reflect His love through our actions and words. God, in His mercy and compassion, has demonstrated a profound care for humanity, and as His image-bearers, we are called to do the same. Compassion involves more than just feeling pity for someone’s suffering; it involves actively engaging in ways that alleviate pain, offer comfort, and bring healing. The Imago Dei compels us to show empathy and kindness, not just to those who are like us, but to all people, especially those in need. Jesus’ teachings, especially the parable of the Good Samaritan (Luke 10:25-37), show us that our compassion should transcend boundaries of race, class, and culture. It is not enough to love only those who love us; we are called to love even our enemies, to show grace and forgiveness, and to be peacemakers in a world full of strife. Steward Creation Human beings, made in the image of God, are entrusted with the care of creation. In Genesis 1:28, God commands humanity to “fill the earth and subdue it,” and to have dominion over the fish, birds, and every living thing. This dominion is not one of exploitation, but of stewardship. As 34

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: image-bearers, humans are called to care for the environment, protect resources, and preserve the earth for future generations. Living out the Imago Dei means acknowledging our responsibility as caretakers of God’s creation. This includes not only environmental conservation, but also a broader understanding of sustainability, ensuring that we use resources wisely and with respect for the world God has entrusted to us. It means recognizing the interconnectedness of all life and advocating for policies and practices that protect the natural world from harm. In our everyday choices, whether it’s reducing waste, conserving energy, or supporting ethical practices, we are reflecting God’s love for the earth and His desire for creation to flourish. Work and Vocation The Imago Dei also informs our understanding of work and vocation. Since humans are created to reflect God’s creativity, purpose, and order, our daily tasks are not just mundane chores, but opportunities to honor Him. Whether in the home, workplace, or community, every action can be an expression of our divine calling. The Apostle Paul writes in Colossians 3:23, “Whatever you do, work heartily, as for the Lord and not for men.” This means that work is not merely about earning a living or achieving personal success, it is an avenue for serving God and others. Whether one is in ministry, education, business, healthcare, or any other field, work becomes an act of worship when done in service to God. The Imago Dei teaches that each person’s work has intrinsic value because it is part of God’s grand design for creation. Even in the most seemingly mundane tasks, we reflect God’s image by bringing order, beauty, and goodness into the world.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN

Even in the most seemingly mundane tasks, we reflect God’s image by bringing order, beauty, and goodness into the world. In summary, living out the Imago Dei is about aligning our lives with God’s purposes. It is a call to recognize the sacred worth of all people, to 35

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: pursue justice and compassion, to steward creation, and to see our work as an opportunity to glorify God. Every day, in every interaction, we are called to reflect God’s image to a world in need of hope, love, and redemption. A Call to Reflect God's Image Being made in God’s image is both a profound privilege and a sacred responsibility. The concept of the Imago Dei challenges us to live in ways that honor our Creator, reflecting His character and purpose in every area of life. It is not merely about an abstract theological truth; it is about how we live, interact, and engage with the world around us. We are called to mirror the love, justice, and holiness of God, which involves intentional efforts to live with purpose, authenticity, and integrity. Here are several key aspects of what it means to reflect God’s image: Cultivate Relationships At the heart of God’s nature is relationality. From the very beginning, God existed in perfect community within the Trinity, Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. The act of creating humanity in His image reflects a divine desire for relationship, not only with Himself but also with others. God made us to be in communion, both with Him and with one another. To reflect God’s image means to engage in meaningful relationships, grounded in love, respect, and mutual care. As humans, we are created for connection. The Imago Dei calls us to build strong, healthy relationships, whether in our families, friendships, or broader communities. These relationships reflect God’s love and grace in tangible ways. We are to foster environments where people feel valued, understood, and accepted, just as God invites us into His love and fellowship. 36

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: Seek Truth and Wisdom God is the ultimate source of truth and wisdom. In the beginning, He spoke creation into existence through His Word, and that Word continues to reveal His nature and His will for our lives. To reflect the Imago Dei is to align our minds and hearts with the truth that comes from God. As we grow in our understanding of who God is, we are called to pursue knowledge and wisdom that leads us closer to Him and His purposes. Seeking truth is not limited to intellectual pursuits alone; it encompasses our daily decisions and actions. It means striving to align our thoughts, attitudes, and behaviors with God's will. In a world full of conflicting ideas and ideologies, the Christian faith provides a firm foundation for discernment. As we seek wisdom from God's Word, we can make decisions that reflect His heart and His holiness, serving as ambassadors of His truth in a world that desperately needs it. Live with Integrity Integrity is the alignment of our inner character with our outward actions. To live with integrity is to embody truth in every area of our lives, whether in our personal conduct, work ethic, relationships, or spiritual commitments. This reflects the holiness of God, whose nature is entirely pure and unblemished. As God’s image-bearers, we are called to uphold moral principles, acting in ways that honor both God and others. Living with integrity means being honest, reliable, and just in all our dealings. It involves making choices that reflect the character of Christ, even when no one is watching. The Imago Dei compels us to be people of our word, who live consistently with the values of the kingdom of God, demonstrating the transformative power of the gospel in every aspect of our lives. 37

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: A Life that Mirrors God's Love, Justice, and Holiness To be made in God’s image is to be called into a life that mirrors His love, justice, and holiness. These divine attributes are foundational to understanding who God is and how He interacts with the world. As we reflect God’s image, we are to be agents of love, justice, and holiness in the world around us. ● Love : God’s love is unconditional, sacrificial, and eternal. As image-bearers, we are called to love others in the same way, offering grace and compassion even when it is difficult. This love is not self-centered but other-centered, seeking the well-being of others above our own. It is the foundation of our relationships and interactions. ● Justice : God is a just God, and He calls us to pursue justice in our world. This means standing up for the oppressed, seeking fairness in all situations, and working toward a society that reflects God’s heart for justice. As God’s image-bearers, we are called to advocate for the marginalized and work toward healing broken systems. ● Holiness : God is holy, and He calls us to be holy as well (1 Peter 1:16). Holiness is a reflection of God’s purity, His moral excellence, and His separation from sin. As we live out the Imago Dei , we are called to embody holiness by rejecting sin and striving to live in a way that reflects God’s perfect character. An Invitation to Live Authentically, Honorably, and Purposefully In essence, to be made in the image of God is an invitation to live authentically, honorably, and purposefully. It is an invitation to live in such a way that others see God’s character reflected in our lives. Our daily 38

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: actions, choices, and relationships should all point back to the One who created us and gave us value and purpose. The Imago Dei calls us to live with intentionality. Our lives are not random or without purpose; we have been created for a divine purpose that reflects God’s love, justice, and holiness. Living out the Imago Dei means that every moment, every decision, and every relationship matters. It means living in such a way that we honor God and reflect His character to the world. In conclusion, the call to reflect God’s image is a high calling, one that invites us into a life of purpose and meaning. It challenges us to engage in relationships, pursue truth, and live with integrity, all while embodying the divine attributes of love, justice, and holiness. As we reflect God’s image, we fulfill our created purpose and glorify the One who made us. 39

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: CHAPTER 3 THE PROBLEM OF EVIL: A REAL DIAGNOSIS Sin, Rebellion, and the Fracture of the World Few questions cut as deeply into the human experience as the problem of evil. From the anguish of personal loss to the horrors of war, genocide, injustice, and systemic oppression, evil remains a pressing and persistent reality. Humanity across cultures and centuries has asked: Why does suffering exist? What is the origin of evil? How can a good and all-powerful God allow such brokenness to endure? This cluster of questions, often referred to as the problem of evil , has occupied the minds of theologians, philosophers, and seekers of truth throughout history. While various worldviews have attempted to answer these questions, some seeing evil as an illusion (as in some strands of Eastern philosophy), others attributing it solely to ignorance, societal structure, or cosmic necessity, Christianity offers a unique and deeply coherent explanation. It neither diminishes the reality of evil nor offers detached philosophical abstractions. Instead, it diagnoses evil with theological precision and responds to it with divine compassion and hope. According to the Christian narrative, evil is not intrinsic to creation, nor is it the product of a malevolent creator. Scripture affirms that God created the world “very good” (Genesis 1:31), filled with order, purpose, and harmony. Evil entered not as a creation of God but as a corruption of what was good, through the willful rebellion of rational creatures endowed with freedom. The Genesis account (Genesis 3) narrates how Adam and Eve, representing humanity, distrusted God's goodness and chose autonomy 40

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: over obedience. In grasping to "be as gods" (Genesis 3:5), they fractured the covenantal relationship between humanity and the Creator. This primal rebellion, what Christian theology identifies as original sin , was not merely an isolated act of disobedience, but a rupture with cosmic consequences. Romans 5:12 articulates this succinctly: "Wherefore, as by one man sin entered into the world, and death by sin; and so death passed upon all men, for that all have sinned." The fallout from this rebellion affected every dimension of creation: human nature became disordered, relationships were corrupted, work became toilsome, and death, both physical and spiritual, entered human history. Theologians like Augustine and later Anselm and Calvin argued that evil is not a substance but a privation ( privatio boni ), a distortion or absence of the good. In other words, evil does not have ontological weight on its own; it is parasitic on the good, twisting it out of alignment with God’s will. Human sin, then, is not simply the violation of an abstract moral code but a profound rupture in communion with God, resulting in alienation, guilt, and decay. Furthermore, this fracture is not merely personal but systemic. As individuals chose sin, institutions, cultures, and societies began to reflect this rebellion, embedding injustice, greed, and oppression into the very structures of civilization. Yet, at its core, the problem of evil is not fundamentally structural, it is theological. It is the result of a creature turning away from the Creator. Until the heart is restored to its proper orientation, Godward, no structural reform or moral effort can fully mend what is broken. Christianity, therefore, does not offer a naïve optimism that evil can be eliminated through human effort alone.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: ator. Until the heart is restored to its proper orientation, Godward, no structural reform or moral effort can fully mend what is broken. Christianity, therefore, does not offer a naïve optimism that evil can be eliminated through human effort alone. Nor does it dismiss evil as an illusion to be transcended. Instead, it names evil for what it is, an intrusion into God’s good world, and then anchors hope not in human progress but in 41

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: divine redemption. The incarnation, suffering, and resurrection of Jesus Christ represent God’s response to evil, not merely to explain it, but to defeat it from within. In sum, the problem of evil is best understood through the lens of human sin and rebellion, a real historical and spiritual condition that has fractured the world. Yet even in diagnosing the depth of this problem, Christianity holds forth the promise of a restored creation, where God will make all things new (Revelation 21:5). This dual emphasis on the gravity of sin and the grandeur of redemption forms the theological backbone of the Christian response to evil: sober-minded, deeply realistic, and ultimately hopeful. THE REALITY OF SIN AND REBELLION The Bible provides a clear diagnosis for the problem of evil: it all stems from sin, humanity’s rebellion against God. In Genesis 3, we see the first act of disobedience when Adam and Eve choose to defy God’s command in the Garden of Eden. Their desire to be like God, to define good and evil on their own terms, introduced sin into the world. This rebellion was not just a mistake or an isolated act of disobedience; it was a deliberate act of defiance against the Creator. Romans 5:12 states, "Therefore, just as sin came into the world through one man, and death through sin, and so death spread to all men because all sinned." The impact of Adam and Eve’s rebellion was not confined to them alone. It fractured the very fabric of creation, introducing a brokenness that affects every part of the world. Sin did not just corrupt human hearts; it marred the entire created order, leading to physical death, spiritual separation from God, and the moral decay we see in society. The rebellion of humanity against its Creator is what theologians call the Fall .

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: did not just corrupt human hearts; it marred the entire created order, leading to physical death, spiritual separation from God, and the moral decay we see in society. The rebellion of humanity against its Creator is what theologians call the Fall . It is the cause of all the suffering, injustice, and evil we experience. The world is not as it should be. Human beings were created for good, to 42

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: live in harmony with God, one another, and creation. But through sin, humanity has brought disorder, suffering, and corruption into the world. The Bible is clear that sin is not just an abstract concept but a force that leads to real consequences: broken relationships, violence, illness, and ultimately, death. The problem of evil cannot be divorced from the problem of human sin. It is the root cause that explains why we see a world filled with violence, pain, and injustice. The Fracture of Creation The consequences of sin are not only spiritual but also physical and societal. As humans rebelled against God, they also began to rebel against one another and the created order. The relationship between humanity and the rest of creation was disrupted, leading to environmental decay, natural disasters, and a general dissonance in how we relate to the world around us. In Romans 8:20-22, Paul writes, "For the creation was subjected to futility, not willingly, but because of him who subjected it, in hope that the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to corruption and obtain the freedom of the glory of the children of God. For we know that the whole creation has been groaning together in the pains of childbirth until now." Here, Paul speaks of the groaning of creation itself, as if the world is crying out for redemption. The disorder we experience in nature, the decay, the suffering, the natural disasters, these are not just random occurrences; they are the effects of sin’s presence in the world. Creation itself is affected by humanity’s rebellion against God. The original harmony between humanity and creation has been broken. The world we live in is fractured, not as it was intended to be, but as it has become through human sin. 43

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: This is why we often experience a sense of longing for something better, something that is not tainted by suffering, death, or injustice. It is a longing for the restoration of all things, a restoration that, according to Scripture, will be fully realized only when God redeems both humanity and creation. Beyond Systemic or Illusory Explanations Many secular and philosophical approaches to the problem of evil attempt to explain it away by attributing it to systemic forces, psychological factors, or even the nature of existence itself. Some might argue that suffering is merely an illusion, a consequence of human perception, or simply the result of arbitrary cosmic forces. Others may claim that systemic structures, like economic or social systems, are the root causes of evil and must be reformed in order to create a just society. While these perspectives do offer some insight into the human experience, they often fail to address the deeper, spiritual realities behind evil. They do not confront the problem of evil in its fullness. Christianity, however, acknowledges the reality of evil, not as an illusion, but as a pervasive force that has entered human history through sin. It does not try to sweep suffering under the rug or offer hollow explanations. Instead, it gives us an honest account of the problem of evil and provides a way forward. Christianity teaches that evil is not just a matter of systems or human psychology; it is a spiritual reality that stems from humanity’s rebellion against God. Systems of injustice, corruption, and inequality are indeed a consequence of the brokenness of human nature, but they are ultimately rooted in a rejection of God’s design for the world.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: ritual reality that stems from humanity’s rebellion against God. Systems of injustice, corruption, and inequality are indeed a consequence of the brokenness of human nature, but they are ultimately rooted in a rejection of God’s design for the world. Until the human heart is transformed by grace, no system, philosophy, or human effort can eradicate the evil that exists in the world. Furthermore, the Christian worldview offers a way to confront evil with hope. The Bible does not shy away from the reality of suffering and evil, 44

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: but it also provides a vision for the future, a future where evil will be eradicated and the world will be restored to its original goodness. This is not a naive optimism or wishful thinking, but a hope grounded in the promise of God’s redemption. The Redemptive Response to Evil While the problem of evil remains one of the most formidable objections to faith, Christianity does not stop at intellectual theodicy. Instead, it offers something deeper: not just an explanation, but a solution . The Christian faith responds to evil not only with philosophical reasoning but with a historical act of divine love, the Incarnation . God did not remain distant or detached from the suffering of His creation. Rather, He stepped into the very world marred by sin, pain, and injustice through the person of Jesus Christ. Jesus of Nazareth, the eternal Son of God, became fully human without ceasing to be fully divine. In doing so, He willingly subjected Himself to the full brunt of human suffering and evil. He was betrayed by friends, unjustly condemned by religious and political powers, and executed by crucifixion, the most brutal form of punishment in the Roman world. Yet, in that moment of deepest darkness, something extraordinary occurred: the cross became the site of cosmic redemption. Scripture affirms that Christ’s suffering was not accidental, but purposeful. Isaiah prophesied of the Suffering Servant who would be "wounded for our transgressions" and "bruised for our iniquities" (Isaiah 53:5). On the cross, Jesus bore the weight of sin, not just individual moral failures, but the full burden of humanity's rebellion and the brokenness it unleashed.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: who would be "wounded for our transgressions" and "bruised for our iniquities" (Isaiah 53:5). On the cross, Jesus bore the weight of sin, not just individual moral failures, but the full burden of humanity's rebellion and the brokenness it unleashed. His sacrifice was both substitutionary and victorious: He bore judgment in our place, and in doing so, broke the power of sin and death (Romans 6:9-10; 1 Corinthians 15:55-57). 45

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: The resurrection is the decisive vindication of Christ's victory. By rising from the dead, Jesus triumphed over evil, inaugurating a new creation. The cross and empty tomb are thus not just the heart of Christian hope, they are God’s definitive answer to evil. Rather than merely removing suffering, God, through Christ, transforms it. As New Testament scholar N.T. Wright puts it, “The cross is what love looks like when it takes on evil and wins.” This redemptive work continues today. Through the gospel, individuals are offered forgiveness, healing, and reconciliation with God. The Holy Spirit works in believers to renew hearts, reshape communities, and restore broken systems. Even though evil still exists, it no longer reigns. Christ has disarmed it (Colossians 2:15), and His kingdom is advancing, often quietly, like leaven in dough (Matthew 13:33), yet powerfully and irreversibly. The Christian hope is not escapism, but restoration. Revelation 21:4 holds out the promise that one day, God will wipe away every tear, and “there shall be no more death, neither sorrow, nor crying, neither shall there be any more pain.” Evil will not have the final word; God will. In the face of evil, then, Christianity offers not only a diagnosis but a Savior. Not merely an explanation, but a cross. Not only a future hope, but a present grace. And through it all, we are invited to trust, not in a God who stands afar, but in One who has entered our world, borne our pain, and is making all things new. Conclusion The problem of evil is a real and difficult issue that touches every aspect of human life. It is not an illusion or simply a consequence of systemic failures; it is a result of sin, the rebellion of humanity against its Creator.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: hings new. Conclusion The problem of evil is a real and difficult issue that touches every aspect of human life. It is not an illusion or simply a consequence of systemic failures; it is a result of sin, the rebellion of humanity against its Creator. The fracture caused by sin affects both humanity and the world around us, leading to suffering, injustice, and death. However, Christianity does not leave us without hope. Through the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus 46

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: Christ, we are offered redemption from sin and the promise of a restored world where evil will be no more. While we still experience the effects of evil, we have the assurance that God is working to make all things new and that one day, He will restore what has been lost. 47

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: PART II: THE CHRISTIAN CLAIM 48

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: CHAPTER 4 THE GOD WHO SPEAKS AND ACTS Revelation: God is Not Silent One of the foundational affirmations of the Christian faith is that God speaks . This belief is neither metaphorical nor merely inspirational; it is theological and historical. The Christian God is not a distant deity who wound up the universe like a clock and then withdrew into silence. He is a personal, relational Being who reveals Himself to His creation, not out of necessity, but out of love and purpose. From the very beginning of Scripture, God's nature as a communicative being is evident. “And God said, ‘Let there be light’” (Genesis 1:3). His voice brings order from chaos and calls life into existence. The entire cosmos responds to the divine Word. This is not incidental. It establishes a key theological truth: God is a God who speaks, and by His Word, He reveals His will, His ways, and His very self. Christian theology identifies two primary modes of divine revelation: general and special . General Revelation General revelation refers to the knowledge of God that is universally accessible to all people through the created world, human reason, and moral intuition. As the Apostle Paul writes in Romans 1:20, “For the invisible things of Him from the creation of the world are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made, even His eternal power and Godhead.” In other words, creation is not mute. The beauty of a sunrise, the complexity of biological systems, the vastness of the cosmos, all echo 49

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: the voice of the Creator. Even human conscience, with its innate sense of right and wrong, bears witness to a moral lawgiver. However, while general revelation renders all humanity “without excuse” (Rom. 1:20), it is not sufficient for salvation . It reveals that there is a God, powerful and purposeful, but it does not disclose the specifics of His redemptive plan. Special Revelation This leads us to special revelation , through which God reveals Himself more specifically and personally. Special revelation includes dreams, visions, angelic visitations, prophetic words, but supremely, it comes through Scripture and culminates in Jesus Christ . The Bible is not merely a collection of ancient religious texts; it is a divinely inspired narrative, a record of God’s self-disclosure across history, speaking through real events, real people, and real words. In this sense, the Bible is not primarily a book about humanity seeking God; it is the record of God seeking humanity . The biblical God does not wait for human beings to ascend toward knowledge of the divine. Instead, He stoops to reveal Himself, progressively, patiently, and purposefully. Hebrews 1:1-2 encapsulates this beautifully: “God, who at sundry times and in divers manners spake in time past unto the fathers by the prophets, hath in these last days spoken unto us by His Son...” Jesus Christ, the eternal Word made flesh (John 1:14), is not merely a messenger from God, He is God’s message in person. In Him, special revelation finds its climax. Jesus is the full and final Word, not just a revelation about God, but God revealed . 50

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: The Nature of Revelation Revelation, then, is not something humans achieve; it is something God gives. It is grace , not discovery. Human reason, while valuable, is not sufficient to arrive at the fullness of divine truth. We know God because He chooses to be known. As the Swiss theologian Karl Barth famously stated, “God is not dumb. He speaks.” Barth emphasized that revelation is not a static deposit of facts, but a living event, God personally making Himself known. It is relational, dynamic, and authoritative. This has profound implications. If God speaks, then listening becomes a moral and spiritual responsibility. If God reveals Himself, then revelation becomes the lens through which we rightly understand ourselves, our world, and our purpose. SCRIPTURE AND THE COHERENCE OF THE BIBLICAL NARRATIVE The Bible is not simply a compilation of sacred writings; it is a unified, divinely inspired narrative that reveals God’s unfolding plan for creation, redemption, and consummation. Written by over 40 human authors across more than a millennium, in multiple cultures, languages, and literary genres, Scripture nevertheless exhibits a striking coherence that testifies to its divine origin and theological depth. The Bible’s unity lies not in uniformity, but in its thematic and covenantal coherence . It tells one overarching story: God’s redemptive work in the world , culminating in the person and work of Jesus Christ.` A Covenant Story 51

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: At the heart of Scripture is the concept of covenant , God’s solemn commitment to His people and His purpose to dwell among them. Beginning with God’s promises to Abraham (Genesis 12), moving through the covenant at Sinai (Exodus 19-24), the Davidic covenant (2 Samuel 7), and the prophetic anticipation of a new covenant (Jeremiah 31:31-34), the Old Testament progressively unveils God’s gracious and redemptive engagement with humanity. These covenants are not isolated religious contracts. They form a framework through which God reveals His character, His expectations, and His desire for relationship. Each covenant builds upon the former, driving forward the narrative of hope, judgment, restoration, and promise. The Christological Fulfillment The New Testament identifies Jesus Christ as the fulfillment of all that the Old Testament anticipated. He is not merely a teacher or prophet; He is the embodiment of the divine Word (John 1:1, 14). In Him, the covenants find their fulfillment, and the fragmented hopes of the Old Testament are brought to resolution. As the author of Hebrews puts it: “God… hath in these last days spoken unto us by His Son, whom He hath appointed heir of all things…” (Hebrews 1:1-2). Jesus is not just the messenger; He is the message. The Word spoken at creation now stands among creation in flesh and blood. This understanding is affirmed in Jesus’ post-resurrection conversation on the road to Emmaus, where He interprets “in all the Scriptures the things concerning Himself” (Luke 24:27). The Old Testament was never meant to stand on its own; it is messianically charged , anticipating the arrival of the Redeemer who would bear the weight of human sin and inaugurate the kingdom of God. 52

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: A Thematic Tapestry Key biblical themes such as creation, fall, covenant, law, kingdom, exile, sacrifice, redemption, and restoration are not disconnected motifs; they are interwoven strands that form a theological tapestry. This narrative coherence gives the Bible its unique authority and beauty. It is not merely a book about God, it is a book through which God speaks , forming and reforming a people for Himself. Scripture, then, is both historical and theological. It is grounded in real events, people, places, and timelines, but it interprets those events through a divine lens. It is not neutral reportage; it is divine disclosure , shaping the reader as much as it informs them. The Church and the Story For the Christian, reading Scripture is not just an intellectual exercise. It is participation in a living story . The Church stands within the trajectory of this narrative, called to live out its implications in real time. As followers of Christ, believers are not just recipients of the Word, they are actors in the ongoing drama of redemption , bearing witness to the One in whom all things hold together (Colossians 1:17). Why This Matters That God speaks is not a peripheral doctrine, it is a profound declaration of His relational nature and intentional engagement with creation. He is not a distant deity who watches from afar but the Covenant-Maker who reveals Himself in words and actions, inviting humanity into meaningful relationship. Revelation, therefore, is not just a transfer of information but an act of divine hospitality. In speaking, God draws near. The authority of Scripture derives not merely from its antiquity or religious influence, but from its divine authorship and its Christ-centered 53

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: coherence . The Bible is not a random anthology of moral insights or inspirational sayings. It is a theologically unified and historically grounded narrative that presents a comprehensive worldview , one that addresses the deepest questions of existence: Who is God? Who are we? What went wrong? What is the remedy? No other book answers these questions with such redemptive depth and theological consistency. In a fragmented cultural landscape filled with noise, confusion, and competing claims to truth, the Scriptures provide an unshakable foundation. The Word of God is a lamp in the darkness (Psalm 119:105), a mirror that reveals (James 1:23-25), a sword that discerns (Hebrews 4:12), and a seed that transforms (1 Peter 1:23). It shapes not only our doctrines but our desires, not just our thinking but our living. To read the Bible, then, is not a passive act of religious duty, it is an encounter with the living God . In its pages, we hear the same voice that called light out of darkness, now calling us out of confusion, sin, and despair into the marvelous light of His redemption. Scripture is both the record of God’s acts and the means by which He continues to act, forming, convicting, comforting, and commissioning His people. This is why the doctrine of revelation matters. It reminds us that God is not silent , and because He speaks, we can live with confidence, purpose, and hope. 54

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: CHAPTER 5 JESUS - THE CRUX OF HISTORY AND HOPE Historical Credibility of Jesus’ Life, Death, and Resurrection Christianity stands or falls on the person of Jesus Christ. Unlike many religious systems that begin with abstract principles or philosophical ideals, Christianity is fundamentally rooted in history , in events that occurred in space and time, witnessed by real people, and recorded in a coherent narrative. At the center of that history is Jesus of Nazareth, a figure whose life, teachings, death, and resurrection are not mere matters of private belief, but of public truth . Jesus in the Historical Record The historical existence of Jesus is one of the most well-supported facts of ancient history. The overwhelming majority of scholars, whether religious or secular, affirm that Jesus of Nazareth lived in first-century Palestine, gathered a following, was crucified under the Roman prefect Pontius Pilate, and left behind a movement that became the early Church. Beyond the New Testament, non-Christian sources from antiquity also testify to His existence. Tacitus, a Roman historian writing around AD 116, mentions “Christus,” who suffered under Pontius Pilate during the reign of Tiberius. Josephus, a first-century Jewish historian, makes reference to Jesus in two passages, one of which refers to Him as a wise teacher and the other to His brother James. Pliny the Younger, a Roman governor, also documents early Christians worshiping Christ “as to a god.” These references do not prove the divine claims of Jesus, but they corroborate the basic historical framework presented in the New Testament: that Jesus lived, taught, was crucified, and was central to a rapidly expanding religious movement. 55

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: The New Testament as Historical Witness The New Testament, especially the four Gospels, serves as the primary source for understanding the life of Jesus. Though often dismissed as “religious texts,” the Gospels are historical-biographical documents, composed within living memory of the events they narrate, by authors either eyewitnesses themselves or closely associated with eyewitnesses (cf. Luke 1:1-4; John 19:35). Scholars such as Richard Bauckham have argued persuasively for the presence of eyewitness testimony embedded in the Gospels. The level of detail, geographical accuracy, use of proper names, and the internal consistency among the Gospel accounts all indicate that they are not late legends or mythological embellishments, but historical reportage shaped by theological conviction. The apostle Paul’s writings, dated to within 15-25 years of Jesus’ death, further confirm the early and widespread belief in Jesus’ resurrection. In 1 Corinthians 15:3-8, Paul recounts an early Christian creed, likely received within a few years of the resurrection, that affirms Christ’s death, burial, and resurrection, and lists multiple eyewitnesses, including Peter, James, the Twelve, and over 500 brethren. The Crucifixion: A Scandalous Certainty That Jesus was crucified under Roman authority is one of the most secure facts in the historical record. Crucifixion was a brutal and shameful form of execution, reserved for the worst offenders, traitors, rebels, and slaves. The early Christian proclamation that their Messiah had been crucified was not a strategic choice for religious propaganda; it was counterintuitive, even scandalous. 56

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: As Paul writes, “We preach Christ crucified, unto the Jews a stumblingblock, and unto the Greeks foolishness” (1 Corinthians 1:23). No Jewish group expected a crucified messiah, and no Gentile would naturally revere a man executed in such a degrading manner. That the earliest Christians nonetheless proclaimed Jesus’ crucifixion as central to God’s saving purpose suggests they were compelled by reality, not convenience. The Resurrection: Event and Evidence If Jesus had merely died, His movement would likely have faded, as many others had. What transformed a group of fearful disciples into bold witnesses was their unwavering conviction that Jesus had risen bodily from the dead. This claim was not based on vague spiritual sentiment or metaphorical reinterpretation, it was grounded in historical experience and objective claims. The resurrection is supported by multiple lines of evidence: ● The Empty Tomb : All four Gospels report that the tomb was found empty by women, an unlikely detail to invent in a male-dominated culture where female testimony was not legally reliable. The Jewish authorities never produced a body, and even hostile explanations (e.g., the disciples stole the body) presuppose the tomb was empty. ● Eyewitness Testimony : The New Testament contains multiple accounts of post-resurrection appearances of Jesus, physical, bodily, and often in groups. These appearances were diverse in time, place, and context (e.g., indoors, outdoors, in Jerusalem, in Galilee), and involved physical interaction (e.g., Luke 24:39; John 20:27). 57

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: ● Transformation of the Disciples : The apostles went from hiding in fear to boldly proclaiming the risen Christ in the very city where He had been executed. Many of them endured persecution and martyrdom, not for a philosophical ideal, but for their unshakable conviction that they had seen the risen Lord. ● The Conversion of Skeptics : Paul was a zealous Pharisee and persecutor of the Church. James, the brother of Jesus, was not a believer during Jesus’ earthly ministry. Yet both became convinced of Jesus’ resurrection, Paul through a personal encounter (Acts 9), and James through a post-resurrection appearance (1 Corinthians 15:7). ● The Birth of the Church and Lord’s Day Worship : The earliest Christians, many of whom were Jewish monotheists, began worshiping Jesus as Lord and gathering on the first day of the week (Sunday) in honor of His resurrection. Such radical shifts in religious practice demand an extraordinary cause. A Faith Anchored in History The Christian faith does not ask people to take a blind leap into the dark. It is a reasonable trust grounded in credible historical testimony. As New Testament scholar N.T. Wright has argued, the best explanation for the rise of early Christianity, the boldness of its witnesses, and the content of their proclamation is that Jesus truly rose from the dead. The resurrection is not merely a doctrine, it is a historical event with theological significance. It vindicates Jesus as the Son of God, confirms the truth of His teachings, and inaugurates the new creation. As Paul affirms, “Christ is risen from the dead, and become the firstfruits of them that slept” (1 Corinthians 15:20). 58

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: In a world accustomed to death and decay, the resurrection offers hope that is both future-oriented and presently empowering. It assures believers that the God who raised Jesus will also raise them, and that the story of history is headed not toward despair, but toward renewal and glory. WHY THE RESURRECTION CHANGES EVERYTHING The resurrection of Jesus Christ is not a peripheral doctrine or optional belief, it is the pivotal event of the Christian faith. It is not simply the happy resolution of Jesus’ suffering, nor is it a spiritual metaphor for hope or renewal. It is the historic, bodily triumph of God over sin, death, and all the powers of evil. If it is true, it alters everything, from the way we understand history to the way we live and die. The Resurrection Validates Jesus’ Identity and Mission Throughout His ministry, Jesus made profound claims about Himself, He spoke as one with divine authority, forgave sins, claimed unity with the Father, and foretold His own death and resurrection. These were not the claims of a mere moral teacher. As C.S. Lewis famously observed, Jesus must either be a liar, a lunatic, or Lord. The resurrection is God’s vindication of Jesus. Paul calls it the moment when Jesus was “declared to be the Son of God with power... by the resurrection from the dead” (Romans 1:4). It was God’s public affirmation that Jesus’ claims were true, His mission fulfilled, and His sacrifice accepted. It demonstrated that Jesus was not a failed messianic figure, but the Risen Lord, the Christ, the Son of the living God. The Resurrection Is the Cornerstone of the Gospel Without the resurrection, the gospel collapses. The apostle Paul makes this clear in 1 Corinthians 15:17-19: “If Christ be not raised, your faith is vain; ye are yet in your sins...

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: of the living God. The Resurrection Is the Cornerstone of the Gospel Without the resurrection, the gospel collapses. The apostle Paul makes this clear in 1 Corinthians 15:17-19: “If Christ be not raised, your faith is vain; ye are yet in your sins... we are of all men most miserable.” In other words, 59

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: no resurrection means no salvation. The resurrection is the divine “receipt”, proof that the debt of sin has been paid in full and that death has been defeated. Jesus did not just die to take away sin, He rose to give life. His resurrection is the down payment of a new humanity and the guarantee that all who trust in Him will likewise rise. “Because I live,” Jesus says, “ye shall live also” (John 14:19). The Resurrection Launches the New Creation The resurrection is not only an event that happened in the past, it is the beginning of the future . In raising Jesus, God has inaugurated the new creation. What Israel longed for, what the prophets foresaw, has now begun. As Paul describes it, Christ is “the firstfruits of them that slept” (1 Corinthians 15:20). His resurrection body is the prototype of what awaits all believers. This has profound implications. It means that history is not cycling aimlessly, nor is it heading toward annihilation. It is moving toward redemption, renewal, and resurrection . The physical world matters. The human body matters. Justice matters. Why? Because the resurrection affirms that God’s purposes are not to abandon creation but to restore it in glory. The Resurrection Grounds Christian Ethics and Mission Far from being a mere private belief or future hope, the resurrection radically reorients present life. Paul’s conclusion after explaining the resurrection in 1 Corinthians 15 is striking: “Therefore, my beloved brethren, be ye steadfast, unmovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord...” (v. 58). The resurrection motivates perseverance, purpose, and sacrificial service. 60

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: Because Jesus is alive, Christians are not just waiting for heaven, they are witnesses of the kingdom, called to live out resurrection life here and now. This includes forgiving enemies, confronting injustice, loving sacrificially, and caring for creation. Resurrection people live with courage in the face of suffering, because death no longer has the final word. The Resurrection Confronts the Powers and Offers Ultimate Hope In a world marred by violence, oppression, disease, and despair, the resurrection announces that evil will not have the last say. It is the cosmic sign that God’s justice will prevail, that death is a defeated enemy, and that history will end not in ruin, but in restoration. Every tear, every wound, every injustice will be answered. The risen Christ, now exalted, will return to judge the living and the dead, to right all wrongs, and to usher in the fullness of God’s reign. This is not a vague optimism, it is the Christian hope, grounded in an event as real as any in history. In summary, the resurrection of Jesus is the epicenter of Christian faith and the anchor of Christian hope. It is the divine reversal of sin’s curse, the proof of Christ’s victory, the pattern of new life, and the pledge of coming glory. Because He is risen, believers can live with confidence, courage, and compassion, knowing that the tomb is empty, the throne is occupied, and the future is secure. Conclusion Jesus is not merely a religious figure or a moral teacher. He is the crux, the pivotal point, of history and of hope. In Him, the promises of Scripture find fulfillment, the brokenness of the world finds healing, and the deepest longings of the human heart find satisfaction. His resurrection is the guarantee that evil will not have the last word, that suffering will be 61

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: redeemed, and that the story of the world is headed not toward despair, but toward glory. 62

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: CHAPTER 6 THE GOSPEL: GOOD NEWS IN A NOISY WORLD In a world increasingly saturated with voices, opinions, and ideologies, the message of the gospel stands out as a beacon of clarity, truth, and hope . It is the Good News that, though lost in sin and brokenness, humanity can be reconciled to God through the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ. But what does the gospel actually mean, and why does it still matter today, especially in a world where so many other messages compete for attention? THE GOSPEL: WHAT IT MEANS At its most fundamental level, the gospel is the proclamation of God’s redemptive work through Jesus Christ. It is neither a moralistic self-help message nor a series of philosophical principles about how to live a virtuous life. The gospel, in its simplest form, is the declaration that God has accomplished, in Jesus, what humanity could never achieve on its own. Jesus: The Divine Redeemer Jesus, the Son of God, entered human history as the incarnate Word (John 1:14), living a life without sin (Hebrews 4:15), but ultimately sacrificing Himself on the cross to remit humanity's sins (Romans 5:8). His death was not an accident or a mere moral example; it was the preordained plan of God to redeem sinners, providing forgiveness for those who place their faith in Him (1 Peter 1:20). But His death was not the end of the story. Jesus was raised from the dead, triumphing over sin, death, and the powers of darkness, securing eternal life for those who believe in Him (Romans 6:4). This act of redemption was not because of anything humanity did or deserved, but entirely because of God's grace and unfailing love. The 63

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: gospel reminds us that salvation is a gift that can never be earned through human effort, religious observance, or moral striving. Jesus did not merely come to instruct people on how to live better lives; He came to make us new creations (2 Corinthians 5:17), to reconcile us to God, and to restore the brokenness caused by sin (Colossians 1:20). The Good News: Answering Life's Fundamental Questions The gospel answers the most profound questions that human beings face: 1. Who is God? God is the Creator and Redeemer of all things. He is both the origin of the universe and the active agent of its restoration. Through His work in Christ, God reveals Himself as a deeply loving, merciful, and just Father who is committed to redeeming the world He created, rescuing humanity from sin, and ultimately renewing all things (Genesis 1:1; John 3:16; Revelation 21:5). 2. What is our problem? Humanity's problem is sin, a fundamental separation from God. This alienation from the Creator is both the cause of all human suffering and the reason why we live in a fractured world. Sin distorts our relationships, corrupts our motives, and leads to both spiritual death and eternal separation from God (Romans 3:23; Isaiah 59:2). We are unable to save ourselves from this condition, no matter how hard we try. 3. What has God done about it? In response to our brokenness, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to die in our place. Jesus' sacrificial death on the cross was the atonement for sin, and His resurrection was the guarantee of victory over death (1 Peter 3:18). Through Jesus, God provides the means of reconciliation and restoration for anyone who believes in Him 64

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: (Romans 5:10). This act of divine intervention was not a random occurrence; it was the culmination of God's eternal plan for the redemption of humanity (Ephesians 1:4-5). 4. How do we respond? The gospel calls for a response of faith. To believe in Jesus Christ as Lord and Savior is to accept the free gift of salvation that God offers. Faith in Christ is the means by which we are reconciled to God and restored to the relationship for which we were created. This faith is not merely intellectual assent but involves a personal trust in Jesus’ work on the cross, acknowledging our need for His forgiveness and relying on His grace to transform us (John 14:6; Ephesians 2:8-9). The Gospel: A Declaration of Freedom The gospel is not simply an information packet about Jesus or a set of moral teachings to follow. It is the announcement of something already accomplished by God for humanity: freedom from the tyranny of sin and death . Jesus' life, death, and resurrection signal the end of the reign of sin and the opening of the way to eternal life with God. This announcement is an invitation for all people to embrace the good news of God's provision in Christ and to trust in His redemptive work on their behalf. It is the call to enter into a new reality : one where forgiveness is available, where death has been defeated, and where life, abundant and eternal, is offered to all who will receive it. This is the heart of the gospel: it is God’s initiative to save humanity, and it is available to all who respond in faith. 65

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: WHY THE GOSPEL STILL MATTERS The gospel remains as relevant today as it ever was because it proclaims timeless truth that transcends culture, time, and circumstance. In a world that is continually evolving, whether through rapid technological advancements, shifting political landscapes, or ever-changing cultural norms, the gospel remains steadfast. It is not subject to the whims of human innovation or the tides of societal change; it is anchored in the eternal nature of God and the unchanging significance of Jesus Christ’s work of redemption. In an age where everything seems to be in constant flux, the gospel offers something enduring, eternal hope . While the world may promise quick fixes or temporary solutions to life’s challenges, the gospel addresses the deepest needs of the human heart : the need for forgiveness, purpose, meaning, and lasting hope. These are not mere psychological needs; they are spiritual realities that shape the very core of who we are as human beings. The gospel doesn’t simply modify our behavior or improve our external circumstances; it transforms our identity and eternal destiny . The Gospel: A Call to the Lost In a time where knowledge is more accessible than ever before, the gospel’s message stands in bold contrast to the overwhelming noise of self-help, motivational speakers, and wellness movements. Despite the endless flow of information and advice, the gospel boldly asserts that humanity remains fundamentally lost apart from God. It calls us to recognize that, no matter how much we progress as a society or improve ourselves through modern techniques, we are still, in our essence, broken and in need of divine restoration (Romans 3:23).

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: emains fundamentally lost apart from God. It calls us to recognize that, no matter how much we progress as a society or improve ourselves through modern techniques, we are still, in our essence, broken and in need of divine restoration (Romans 3:23). The world’s methods may offer temporary fixes, psychological relief, emotional healing, or a boost to one’s self-image, but these are only 66

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: surface-level remedies to the deeper spiritual wounds we carry. Motivational speakers can encourage us to become our “best selves,” but they cannot heal the alienation from God that lies at the heart of human suffering. Philosophies and ideologies may attempt to guide us, but they fall short when faced with the eternal questions of life, death, and the meaning of existence. The Gospel: An Unshakable Foundation What makes the gospel so profound and enduring is its ability to speak to the deepest longings of the human soul and provide an unshakable foundation for life. Unlike the promises of temporary fixes, the gospel’s message is rooted in the grace of God, a grace that does not depend on our own efforts, circumstances, or understanding. The grace of God is a constant, steady force that has provided salvation through Christ, and it is this grace that sustains believers in both the present and the eternity to come (Ephesians 2:8-9). The gospel offers an unshakable foundation because it is grounded in objective reality, the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ. This is not a myth or an abstract concept, but a historical reality that affects every aspect of human life. And as such, the gospel provides true peace in a world of chaos, true hope in a world of despair, and true purpose in a world that often feels aimless. The Gospel in a Fractured World The gospel’s timeless relevance does not lie in its ability to adapt to every trend or cultural moment; rather, it lies in its eternal truth that meets the deepest needs of humanity. In a fractured and fast-paced world, where people are constantly searching for meaning, the gospel stands as the eternal answer to the human condition: a message of redemption, transformation, and hope in Jesus Christ. 67

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: This is why the gospel still matters today, because it speaks to the heart of humanity’s deepest needs, offering a solution that transcends time and culture. It is the one truth that remains steadfast amid all the changes and challenges of life. SALVATION, NOT SELF-HELP A significant misunderstanding in contemporary culture is the idea that Christianity, and specifically the gospel, is primarily about self-help : a program or set of principles to help us become better versions of ourselves. While the Bible certainly teaches ethical living and transformation, the gospel is not a self-improvement plan. It is salvation from sin and its consequences . In the self-help mentality, people are often encouraged to look within themselves to find the strength, wisdom, and motivation to improve their lives. This can lead to positive outcomes, but ultimately, it fails to address humanity’s deepest problem, our separation from God . No matter how hard we try, no matter how many self-help strategies we implement, we cannot solve the problem of sin on our own. The gospel, on the other hand, directs our attention not to our own efforts, but to God’s grace . It is the story of what God has done for us , not what we must do for ourselves. In Jesus Christ, God has already accomplished what we could never do: He lived the perfect life we could not live, died the death we deserve, and rose again to give us new life. This does not mean that Christians do not change or grow, it means that the power for change comes not from self-discipline or positive thinking, but from the Holy Spirit at work in our lives. As Paul writes in Philippians 2:12-13, “Work out your own salvation with fear and trembling, for it is God who works in you both to will and to do of His good pleasure.” The 68

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: change that the gospel brings is not about making us “better” by our own efforts, but about making us new by God’s power. The gospel is therefore not self-help , but divine help , the help we need in order to be reconciled to God, transformed from the inside out, and empowered to live according to God’s purposes. The Gospel’s Invitation: Rest for the Weary In the noisy, crowded world of competing voices and relentless self-promotion, the gospel comes as a welcome invitation . It is an invitation to rest , not in the sense of inactivity, but in the sense of trusting in the finished work of Jesus Christ. Jesus says in Matthew 11:28-30: “Come unto me, all ye that labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you, and learn of me; for I am meek and lowly in heart: and ye shall find rest unto your souls. For my yoke is easy, and my burden is light.” The gospel invites us to stop striving for approval or success in our own strength, and to instead rest in the sufficiency of Christ’s work. The rest that Jesus offers is not an escape from life’s responsibilities, but a deep peace rooted in the assurance that our salvation is secure, our future is certain, and our identity is in Christ. This is the gospel, the Good News that, in a world full of noise, confusion, and self-effort, there is a better way. There is a way of grace, of peace, of salvation. The gospel calls us to stop trying to “fix” ourselves and to instead receive the help that only God can give. 69

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: PART III: THE CHRISTIAN LIFE 70

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: CHAPTER 7 LIVING WITH PURPOSE AND PEACE Designed to Know and Glorify God (Isaiah 43:7) In the fast-paced and often chaotic nature of modern life, it is easy to lose sight of the most fundamental aspect of our existence: the fact that we were created with purpose. In a world filled with noise, distractions, and the constant pursuit of success, it is not uncommon for people to question the meaning of their lives. Yet, Scripture clearly communicates that our existence is not the result of random chance or a cosmic accident. We are purposefully designed by a Creator who has a profound and intentional plan for each of us. Isaiah 43:7 encapsulates this profound truth, stating: "Everyone who is called by My name, whom I created for My glory, whom I formed and made." Purpose at the Core of Creation From the very beginning of humanity's creation, God's intent for us has been clear: to know Him and to bring Him glory. This purpose transcends the transient and fleeting goals that often dominate our lives, such as personal achievements, material gain, or individual pleasure. Instead, the biblical narrative invites us into a much higher calling, one that connects our individual lives to the eternal story of God’s glory. The recognition that our lives are not just about the pursuit of personal desires but about participating in the divine purpose established by God before the foundation of the world transforms the way we view our existence. This purpose is not confined to the spiritual realm alone; it impacts every aspect of our lives, including our relationships, work, and daily choices. We are called to live in alignment with God’s design, embracing the fact that we are His image-bearers in the world. 71

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: The Nature of God's Glory and Our Role in It To understand the purpose of our lives, we must first consider the nature of the glory of God.
