God’s glory refers to His supreme worth, majesty, and beauty that is on display through His character and actions. It is the visible manifestation of God’s infinite greatness and His eternal goodness. Everything in creation reflects His glory, and as humanity is the crown of His creation, we are meant to reflect His glory in a unique and profound way. To “bring Him glory” means that we live in such a way that our lives reflect His goodness, holiness, and truth. We are called to embody His character in the world, serving as witnesses to the divine and making visible the unseen realities of God’s love, justice, and mercy. This is not merely about public acts of worship or religious observance, but it involves every moment of our existence, our thoughts, words, and actions are to be aligned with God’s will. Glorifying God is about reflecting His likeness and proclaiming His excellency through how we live in the world He has made. Life as a Divine Calling Isaiah 43:7 also highlights that humanity was formed and made by God for this specific purpose. These terms, “formed” and “made”, suggest a careful, intentional design. Just as an artist meticulously crafts a masterpiece, God has carefully and intentionally shaped us to fulfill this purpose. This purpose is not a distant ideal but is intricately woven into the very fabric of who we are as human beings. Our ability to reason, create, love, and reflect divine attributes all point toward the deeper purpose for which we were created, to know God and to bring Him glory. In practical terms, this means that our lives are not our own .

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: are as human beings. Our ability to reason, create, love, and reflect divine attributes all point toward the deeper purpose for which we were created, to know God and to bring Him glory. In practical terms, this means that our lives are not our own . We do not exist merely to fulfill our desires or to build our kingdoms. Rather, our 72

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: lives belong to the Creator, and we exist to serve His purposes in the world. This understanding of life as a divine calling has profound implications for how we approach our everyday existence. Whether in our professional work, family life, or social interactions, we are called to reflect God’s glory and to serve Him in every context. The True Peace Found in Purpose Living with this understanding that our lives are designed with purpose is the key to experiencing true peace. When we realize that our existence is not accidental or arbitrary, but that we are part of a grand, divine narrative, we begin to experience peace in our hearts. This peace comes from knowing that we are fulfilling the very reason we were created. It is the peace that comes from aligning ourselves with God’s will and recognizing that our lives have eternal significance in His grand plan. Moreover, this peace is not merely the absence of conflict or discomfort but the profound contentment that arises from knowing we are participating in the eternal purposes of God. It is the peace that transcends the fleeting satisfaction of worldly success and affirms that our lives are part of something far greater than ourselves. When we understand that we were created to know and glorify God, our priorities shift. We are no longer consumed by the pursuit of self-centered goals, but we are driven by a higher calling, to live for God’s glory and to experience the joy and fulfillment that comes from fulfilling our divine purpose. The Greater Story of God’s Glory To live in light of God’s purpose is to live with a sense of eternal perspective . Our lives are part of a much larger story, God’s story of creation, redemption, and glory.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: t comes from fulfilling our divine purpose. The Greater Story of God’s Glory To live in light of God’s purpose is to live with a sense of eternal perspective . Our lives are part of a much larger story, God’s story of creation, redemption, and glory. We are not isolated, random beings; we are integral parts of God’s plan to bring glory to Himself through all of creation. Understanding this purpose is foundational to living a life of 73

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: meaning and peace. It allows us to see beyond the distractions and struggles of daily life and focus on what truly matters, knowing God and making Him known. As we embrace this purpose, we are invited into the profound joy and peace that comes from living for the glory of the One who created us and who calls us to be a reflection of His glory in the world. Living with Purpose: The Call to Know God The first aspect of our purpose is to know God . As the Creator, God desires a relationship with each one of us. The very reason we were created is to experience His presence and to walk with Him in fellowship. In knowing God, we discover the true meaning of life, and this understanding gives our lives the direction and significance they were meant to have. Knowing God is not just about intellectual knowledge, it’s about experiencing Him personally, allowing His truth to shape our hearts and actions. In the book of Philippians, Paul expresses this desire to know God deeply: “That I may know Him and the power of His resurrection, and the fellowship of His sufferings, being conformed to His death” (Philippians 3:10). This pursuit of knowing God is the foundation of a life lived with true purpose and peace. It is in knowing God intimately that we find the peace that surpasses understanding and the purpose that gives life its depth. Living with Purpose: Glorifying God The second aspect of our purpose is to glorify God . Isaiah 43:7 not only tells us that we were created to know God but also that we were created to glorify Him. To glorify God means to honor Him with our lives, to reflect His character and will in everything we do. This is a purpose that transcends personal ambition or the pursuit of fleeting happiness; it is 74

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: about living in such a way that others see God’s goodness, greatness, and glory in our lives. Jesus Himself modeled this perfectly. In John 17:4, He prays to the Father, “I have glorified You on the earth. I have finished the work which You have given Me to do.” His life was marked by a single aim, to glorify the Father through obedience, sacrifice, and love. As His followers, we are called to the same. Glorifying God encompasses all aspects of life: our work, relationships, choices, and even our attitudes. Whether we eat, drink, or work, everything we do should be for God’s glory (1 Corinthians 10:31). When we live with this focus, we not only experience peace but also a deep fulfillment. There is no greater purpose than to live for the glory of the One who created us. 75

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: THE CONNECTION BETWEEN PURPOSE AND PEACE In a world that constantly bombards us with messages about finding peace through external circumstances, it is easy to fall into the trap of believing that true peace is found in things like wealth, relationships, success, or comfort. These pursuits, while often enjoyable or fulfilling in the short term, can only offer fleeting moments of relief. They are inherently temporary, dependent on shifting circumstances or personal achievements. The reality is, true peace is not found in the ever-changing conditions of our lives but in something far deeper and more enduring. The Source of True Peace The true peace that the Bible speaks of comes when we align our lives with the purpose for which we were created. We were designed to know God and to glorify Him . This is the fundamental reason for our existence, and when we live in accordance with this divine purpose, we experience a peace that is not dependent on external circumstances but is anchored in God's eternal, unchanging nature . This peace does not hinge on the temporary state of our finances, relationships, or social standing. It is not influenced by the ups and downs of life. Instead, it comes from the assurance that our lives have eternal significance and that they are aligned with the greater purposes of God . When we understand that our lives matter to God, no matter what our external circumstances may be, we can experience peace that transcends all understanding (Philippians 4:7). 76

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: Peace Through Alignment with God’s Will When we live in harmony with God’s will, our hearts and minds are at peace because we know that we are in the right place, doing the right thing. This peace is not just a psychological state but a spiritual assurance that we are fulfilling the purpose for which we were created. It is a peace that flows from living in the center of God’s will, knowing that He is sovereign and in control of all things. It is often easy to feel anxious or unsettled when we are chasing after things that are temporary or disconnected from God’s purposes. However, when we orient our lives around knowing Him and making Him known , our hearts find rest. We no longer have to search endlessly for peace in things that cannot satisfy because we have found it in the one true source : God Himself. Living Out Our Purpose Brings Inner Peace As we grow in our relationship with God and embrace the purpose He has for us, we are able to reflect His character to the world. We are agents of peace , bringing a peace that is rooted in God’s truth and love to those around us. Living with purpose means that every aspect of our lives, our work, our relationships, our choices, becomes an opportunity to glorify God . This not only brings peace to our hearts but also contributes to peace in the world around us. Moreover, living out our purpose helps us to better navigate life’s inevitable challenges. In times of trial or uncertainty, the peace that comes from knowing we are fulfilling God’s plan for our lives enables us to stand firm in faith, trusting that He is with us through every circumstance. The peace we experience is not the absence of trouble, but the presence of God in the midst of it, assuring us that our lives are meaningful and our futures secure. 77

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: A Peace Beyond Understanding Ultimately, true peace is the fruit of living in alignment with God’s purpose . It is the peace that comes when we understand that our lives are not accidents, nor are they merely driven by the quest for personal gain. We were created for a higher purpose: to know God, to reflect His glory, and to live in harmony with His will. This peace is not temporary or circumstantial; it is rooted in the eternal truth that our lives matter to God, and that He is in control of all things. In this peace, we find the strength to face the challenges of life, knowing that we are fulfilling the very purpose for which we were created. CONCLUSION: LIVING WITH PURPOSE AND PEACE In a world that often feels chaotic and uncertain, understanding that our lives are not accidents but are created with purpose can bring profound peace. We were designed to know God and to glorify Him , and in doing so, we find both purpose and peace. When we focus on these eternal truths, our perspective shifts from the temporary and superficial to the eternal and meaningful. We can rest in the peace that comes from knowing that our lives are part of a divine story, one where we are called to know the Creator and to make His glory known to the world. Let us embrace this purpose fully, living lives that honor God, knowing that this is the path to true peace. 78

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: CHAPTER 8 ANCHORED MORALITY IN A DRIFTING CULTURE In a world that constantly shifts in its values, beliefs, and practices, the question of morality, what is right and wrong, has become increasingly complex. In many ways, our culture seems to be drifting, with morality becoming subjective and changeable, shaped by popular opinion rather than timeless truth. In such an environment, the concept of absolute morality can feel out of place or even irrelevant. Yet, despite this shifting tide, the need for a firm, unchanging moral foundation remains critical. God’s character provides the unshakable anchor for our morality, ensuring that we have a standard for justice, love, and right living that transcends cultural trends and human opinions. Why Moral Relativism Doesn’t Work Moral relativism, the belief that moral principles are not universal but are shaped by cultural, personal, or societal contexts, offers a view of ethics where the standards of right and wrong are not fixed but depend on individual preferences, societal norms, or situational factors. In this framework, moral truth becomes subjective, varying from one person or culture to another, and there are no objective standards by which actions can be universally judged. While this perspective may seem appealing in an increasingly diverse and pluralistic world, it fails to provide a stable or coherent moral framework. Several fundamental problems arise from the adoption of moral relativism: 79

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: 1. Inconsistent Standards One of the most immediate consequences of moral relativism is the creation of inconsistent standards of morality. If what is right or wrong is relative to the individual or culture, this opens the door to contradictions between different societies or even individuals within the same society. For instance, one culture might accept slavery as morally permissible, while another would condemn it as a grave moral wrong. Similarly, some societies may embrace practices such as genocide or dishonesty, while others reject them outright. This inconsistency presents a significant problem, as it becomes impossible to establish a universally applicable framework for moral decision-making. Without common standards, the capacity to affirm universal human rights, justice, and dignity is weakened, leading to moral confusion and ethical relativism that hampers meaningful cross-cultural dialogue and global cooperation. 2. The Erosion of Accountability Moral relativism undermines the concept of accountability , a cornerstone of justice. If there are no absolute moral truths, then no one can be held definitively accountable for their actions. When morality is subjective, individuals can justify harmful or immoral actions based on personal belief or cultural context. For instance, acts such as theft, lying, or even violence may be seen as acceptable within certain personal or cultural frameworks, rendering any claim of wrongdoing highly subjective. This erosion of accountability means that the very foundation of justice, the recognition that certain actions are always wrong, regardless of context, is compromised.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: sonal or cultural frameworks, rendering any claim of wrongdoing highly subjective. This erosion of accountability means that the very foundation of justice, the recognition that certain actions are always wrong, regardless of context, is compromised. Without a universally recognized moral standard, individuals and societies are left without reliable means of judging right and wrong, and perpetrators of injustice can evade consequences by merely 80

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: appealing to their own beliefs or societal norms. As a result, the rule of law and social justice mechanisms lose their moral weight, which is essential for upholding societal order. 3. The Failure to Address True Injustice Moral relativism is fundamentally inadequate in addressing true injustice . If there are no objective moral truths, concepts like human rights, equality, and justice become mere social constructs or cultural preferences, rather than universal principles. This lack of objective morality means that severe injustices, such as genocide, oppression, or exploitation, cannot be adequately condemned as universally wrong. Instead, they can be justified or dismissed as mere cultural differences or subjective opinions. The failure to recognize universal principles of justice leads to the normalization of immoral practices. Without an authoritative moral standard to appeal to, individuals or groups who suffer from oppression or exploitation have little recourse to challenge systemic evil. For example, a relativistic approach to morality might allow for human trafficking or discrimination to persist, as long as these practices are seen as acceptable by a particular culture or group, undermining the very concept of human dignity . 4. Lack of Meaningful Morality At its core, moral relativism offers no meaningful morality . While individuals may choose to live according to specific personal or cultural values, the absence of absolute moral truths means there is no solid grounding for deciding what is truly right or wrong, good or evil. This results in a moral vacuum, where each individual or society determines right and wrong according to subjective feelings or cultural influences. 81

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: In a world governed by moral relativism, moral choices are often reduced to mere preferences or opinions, akin to choosing a flavor of ice cream, without any lasting moral weight. This lack of a firm moral compass contributes to a moral chaos where individuals and societies drift, unsure of how to make ethical decisions or how to define justice in a meaningful way. In such an environment, people may struggle to make moral decisions that align with truth, reason, or justice, leading to ethical confusion and a lack of societal cohesion. 5. The Undermining of the Moral Order The inherent instability of moral relativism undermines the moral order required for flourishing societies. If moral truth is left to the whims of personal or cultural preference, then there is no anchor by which people can evaluate the long-term consequences of their choices. Communities become fragmented, as different groups may operate under completely different ethical frameworks. Moreover, without a shared commitment to certain moral absolutes, such as the inherent dignity of human beings or the sanctity of life, societal cohesion is threatened. Ethical relativism often leads to the breakdown of trust and unity, as it fosters division over what is considered right and wrong. The absence of a common moral law that applies to all undermines the possibility of universal human flourishing and social harmony. The Case for Objective Morality While moral relativism may seem attractive in a pluralistic society that values diversity of thought and experience, its failure to provide consistent, meaningful, and universally applicable standards for justice and love is problematic.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: ive Morality While moral relativism may seem attractive in a pluralistic society that values diversity of thought and experience, its failure to provide consistent, meaningful, and universally applicable standards for justice and love is problematic. The lack of objective moral truths results in ethical inconsistency, a lack of accountability , and an inability to address true injustice . Moreover, it offers no solid foundation for determining what is 82

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: right and wrong, leaving individuals and societies without a reliable moral compass. In contrast, an objective moral framework , grounded in universal principles of justice, love, and human dignity, provides the stability and consistency necessary for a flourishing society. By recognizing a higher moral law rooted in God’s character and nature, we can build a society that stands on principles of justice and compassion, capable of addressing the true needs of the human heart and establishing lasting peace. GOD’S CHARACTER AS THE STANDARD FOR JUSTICE AND LOVE In stark contrast to the fluid and shifting nature of moral relativism, God’s character provides an unshakable, objective foundation for morality. The nature of God, as revealed through Scripture, is not only the source of moral principles but also the standard by which we can measure justice, love, and righteousness. As God is eternal and unchanging, so too is the moral order that flows from His perfect nature. This makes God's character the ultimate and reliable benchmark for discerning right from wrong, good from evil. 1. God is Just At the heart of God’s moral nature is His perfect justice . Unlike human justice, which is often tainted by bias, error, or subjective interpretation, God’s justice is perfectly righteous, fair, and impartial. It does not fluctuate with changing times or opinions, nor does it bend in the face of cultural trends or power dynamics. In Scripture, God’s justice is presented as the foundation of His throne, symbolizing the ultimate authority by which all things are judged. 83

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: Psalm 89:14 declares, “Righteousness and justice are the foundation of Your throne.” This verse highlights that God’s justice is not arbitrary but rooted in His unwavering commitment to truth and fairness. His justice is always in harmony with His other attributes, ensuring that His decisions are not only fair but also wise and loving. God’s justice is not only concerned with punishment for wrongdoing but with upholding truth, maintaining order, and ensuring that righteousness prevails. It is through His justice that moral wrongs are confronted, and through it, society can find a clear standard for what is right. 2. God is Loving Equally foundational to God’s nature is His love . The Bible makes it abundantly clear that God’s love is not just a sentiment or emotional response, but a selfless, sacrificial, and intentional commitment to the well-being of His creation. In 1 John 4:8, we are told, “God is love,” meaning that love is intrinsic to who God is. It is through God’s love that the world finds its true meaning, and it is the divine love that forms the foundation of all human relationships. God’s love is not contingent on human merit or circumstances; it is steadfast and unchanging. This love compels us to love others, not just in words but in actions. The command to love our neighbors as ourselves (Matthew 22:39) reflects God’s own love for humanity and provides a moral framework for how we should treat others. True love, as modeled by God, seeks the well-being of others, promotes their flourishing, and is willing to make sacrifices for their good. This standard of love transcends personal preferences and cultural definitions of love, offering a divine model of how we should live in relationship with others. 84

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: 3. God is Holy God’s holiness further defines the boundaries of moral right and wrong. Holiness refers to God’s absolute purity, His separateness from sin, and His perfection. The Bible states that God is "holy, holy, holy" (Isaiah 6:3), which emphasizes the absolute nature of His purity. Because God is holy, He cannot tolerate sin or evil in His presence. His holiness sets Him apart from all creation and establishes a moral boundary between good and evil. God’s holiness is not only a description of His nature but also serves as the standard for the moral law. The law reflects God’s character, what is right is defined by His purity and righteousness. God’s holiness compels Him to act justly in the face of sin, as sin is contrary to His nature. Therefore, when we speak of morality in God’s terms, it is always in alignment with His holiness, which demands purity, righteousness, and truth in every aspect of life. 4. God’s Commands Reflect His Nature God’s commands are not arbitrary or capricious; rather, they reflect His character and nature. When God commands us to act justly, love mercy, and walk humbly with Him (Micah 6:8), He is inviting us to live in harmony with His nature. These commands are expressions of His own moral attributes, justice, mercy, and humility. They are not external rules imposed for no reason; rather, they are intended to guide us in reflecting His character in the world. For example, when God calls us to love our enemies and to forgive those who wrong us, He is not simply giving a moral directive for social harmony; He is inviting us to model the selfless love that He has shown us through Christ. When He calls us to live justly, He is inviting us to uphold the integrity and fairness that reflect His own justice. To live according to 85

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: God’s moral law is to embody His nature and participate in His work of bringing justice, love, and peace to the world. 5. God’s Moral Law Brings Stability and Order Unlike the ever-changing and subjective nature of moral relativism, God’s moral law provides a stable and timeless framework for ethical living. It is a law that transcends cultural boundaries, human opinions, and societal trends, remaining consistent throughout history. God’s moral law offers clarity and order in a world that is often filled with confusion and moral ambiguity. By adhering to God’s commands, we find a path to righteousness that promotes personal flourishing and societal well-being. God's law is not burdensome or restrictive; rather, it is a guide to living in alignment with His will and in a manner that reflects His character. In a world where morality can feel relative and shifting, the constancy of God's moral law offers a firm foundation upon which individuals and societies can build a just, loving, and peaceful life. The Unshakable Standard of God’s Character God’s character, His justice, love, and holiness, forms the basis for a moral framework that is both stable and universally applicable. Unlike the fluctuating nature of moral relativism, which leaves people without a clear moral compass, God’s nature provides an unwavering standard for how we should live, relate to others, and engage with the world. His justice ensures fairness and righteousness, His love compels us to serve others sacrificially, and His holiness sets the boundaries for what is right and wrong. Living in accordance with God’s moral law is not only the key to personal peace and fulfillment but also to the flourishing of society as a whole. As 86

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: we reflect God’s nature in our lives, we bring about justice, mercy, and love in the world, creating a stable and harmonious moral order. In this way, God's character remains the eternal and unshakable foundation for all that is just, loving, and good. THE IMMUTABILITY OF GOD’S MORAL LAW One of the greatest strengths of God’s character as the basis for morality is its immutability . God is the same yesterday, today, and forever (Hebrews 13:8). His moral standards do not change with time, culture, or circumstances. This provides a reliable and stable moral anchor in a world that is constantly shifting. In contrast, moral relativism is susceptible to the changing whims of human opinions, cultural trends, and political movements. These changes are often influenced by power dynamics, convenience, or self-interest, leading to injustice and inequality. God’s moral law, however, is rooted in His eternal character, and it is this immutability that provides a stable foundation for justice and love . In a world where it can be difficult to know what is right, God’s truth remains an anchor for those who seek to live according to His will. The Need for Anchored Morality As our culture drifts further into moral relativism, the need for an anchored, objective moral standard becomes ever more urgent. Without an unchanging foundation, individuals and societies are left adrift, unable to make consistent or just moral decisions. God’s character , His justice, love, and holiness, provides that unshakable moral foundation. His commands are not simply rules to follow, but expressions of His eternal and perfect nature, offering a clear path to true justice and love. When we align our lives with God's moral standard, we 87

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: participate in a world where right is distinguished from wrong, where justice is pursued, and where love truly flourishes. In the midst of a drifting culture, God's truth remains our anchor , offering the stability and clarity needed to navigate a complex and often confusing world. 88

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: CHAPTER 9 HOPE IN A WORLD OF SUFFERING One of the most profound questions people face in life is the question of suffering. Why do we endure pain, loss, and hardship? Why do innocent people suffer, and why does it seem that sometimes suffering is inexplicable or even unjust? These are questions that have perplexed humanity for centuries. In a world where suffering is a reality, where pain seems inevitable, and where despair can feel overwhelming, it is essential to explore what Scripture has to say about suffering, pain, and hope. In the midst of suffering, the Bible provides a message of hope, rooted in the truth of God's redemptive plan and the promise of ultimate restoration. While pain is a part of the human experience, it is not the final word. Through the lens of faith, we can see that suffering, though painful, is not without purpose, and it is not the end of the story. God, in His infinite wisdom and love, has a plan for ultimate healing and restoration that transcends the pain of the present moment. The Problem of Pain Pain and suffering, in their multifaceted forms, are undeniably integral to the human experience. From the agony of losing loved ones to the physical suffering caused by illness or injury, from emotional distress and mental anguish to existential crises, pain is a pervasive reality in every person's life. For many, it becomes one of the most difficult and perplexing challenges, particularly when viewed through the lens of Christian theology. If God is good, loving, and all-powerful, then why does He allow suffering to exist in the world He created? 89

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: This profound question, often asked in the midst of personal or collective hardship, strikes at the heart of both the human experience and the Christian faith. Theologians, philosophers, and ordinary believers have wrestled with the problem of pain for centuries. It is a question that does not merely concern abstract theological concepts, but touches deeply on the emotional and spiritual realities of living in a fallen world. THEOLOGICAL CONTEXT OF PAIN: THE FALL AND ITS CONSEQUENCES The Bible does not shy away from the reality of suffering but provides a clear, foundational explanation for its existence. According to Scripture, when God created the world in the beginning, it was “very good” (Genesis 1:31). In the pristine creation of God, there was no pain, no suffering, no death, everything functioned in perfect harmony. God's original intent was for humanity to live in peaceful communion with Him and with one another, with an abundance of life and flourishing. However, this perfect state was disrupted by the fall of humanity, as narrated in the book of Genesis. Adam and Eve's decision to disobey God by eating from the Tree of the Knowledge of Good and Evil brought sin into the world (Genesis 3:6). In this moment of rebellion, sin, defined as the violation of God's perfect order, entered human existence, leading to spiritual and physical death (Romans 5:12). The consequences of this fall were far-reaching: pain, suffering, illness, and death became pervasive in the human condition, affecting not just humanity but all of creation.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: red human existence, leading to spiritual and physical death (Romans 5:12). The consequences of this fall were far-reaching: pain, suffering, illness, and death became pervasive in the human condition, affecting not just humanity but all of creation. Romans 8:20-22 tells us that "the creation was subjected to futility, not willingly, but because of him who subjected it, in hope that the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to corruption." The fall of humanity did not only affect human beings but also the natural world, which now bears the marks of the brokenness introduced by sin. The curse of sin brought physical decay, suffering, and death into the very fabric of the 90

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: universe. Thus, pain and suffering are not arbitrary or random occurrences, but consequences of a fallen world, where sin has marred the perfect creation of God. Personal and Existential Dimensions of Suffering While the Bible offers a theological explanation for the origin of pain, this does not fully resolve the tension that many people feel when confronted with suffering in their own lives or the lives of others. The personal and existential dimensions of suffering make it far more than just an abstract theological problem; it is a deeply felt reality. Pain comes in various forms: the loss of a loved one, the suffering from illness, the ache of loneliness, the despair of failure, the anguish of guilt or shame. Each person experiences suffering differently, yet all are united in the experience of pain’s undeniable presence in their lives. What makes the problem of pain even more complex is the fact that it does not always seem to align with our sense of justice or fairness. Why do the innocent suffer? Why do good people experience such profound pain while others appear to escape hardship altogether? These questions challenge our understanding of God’s goodness and justice. The Bible does not offer simplistic or formulaic answers to these questions. Instead, it invites believers into a deeper faith journey, where suffering is acknowledged, confronted, and ultimately seen in light of God’s redemptive plan. Faith, Perseverance, and Hope: A Response to Pain In confronting the reality of pain, Scripture consistently encourages a response of faith , perseverance , and hope .

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: s acknowledged, confronted, and ultimately seen in light of God’s redemptive plan. Faith, Perseverance, and Hope: A Response to Pain In confronting the reality of pain, Scripture consistently encourages a response of faith , perseverance , and hope . While there are no easy or simplistic answers to the problem of pain, the Bible offers a profound message of hope that is grounded in the reality of a suffering God who has entered into the pain of the world. 91

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: In the person of Jesus Christ, God Himself took on human flesh and entered the world of suffering. The Incarnation is not merely a theological concept but a radical statement that God is not distant from our pain but has chosen to suffer with and for us. Jesus’ life was marked by suffering, He was misunderstood, betrayed, and ultimately crucified in one of the most excruciating ways imaginable. Through His suffering, Christ demonstrated that God fully understands the depths of human pain and that He is actively present in our suffering. The apostle Paul, in his letters, speaks often of suffering but also emphasizes the hope that comes from faith in Christ. In Romans 8:18, Paul writes, "I consider that our present sufferings are not worth comparing with the glory that will be revealed in us." For the believer, suffering is not the end of the story. It is, rather, the pathway through which God's purposes are often fulfilled, even if we cannot fully comprehend how. Pain becomes, in the Christian narrative, a tool that God uses to shape, refine, and transform His people. It is not that suffering is good in itself, but rather that God, in His sovereignty, can bring about good even through suffering (Romans 8:28). Moreover, suffering brings about perseverance. James 1:2-4 speaks of the “testing of our faith” that produces perseverance, which leads to spiritual maturity. The Christian faith does not promise a life free of suffering, but it offers a framework through which suffering can be seen as a means of growth and transformation. Perseverance through trials strengthens faith and deepens trust in God's goodness. Finally, the Bible offers a hope that transcends the pain of the present.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: fers a framework through which suffering can be seen as a means of growth and transformation. Perseverance through trials strengthens faith and deepens trust in God's goodness. Finally, the Bible offers a hope that transcends the pain of the present. This hope is rooted in the promise of redemption and restoration , the assurance that God will make all things new. The book of Revelation offers a vision of a future where pain, death, and suffering are no more (Revelation 21:4). The hope of eternal life and ultimate restoration in the 92

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: new heavens and new earth provides Christians with the strength to endure present suffering, knowing that it is temporary and that God has already secured the victory over sin and death through Jesus Christ. The problem of pain is one that cannot be fully explained in human terms, but Scripture offers a comprehensive framework for understanding it. Pain and suffering entered the world as a result of sin, and they are a reality that all human beings experience in various forms. While the Bible acknowledges the existence of pain, it also presents a hope that is not confined to this world. Through the suffering of Jesus Christ, God entered into the pain of humanity and provided a way for redemption. For Christians, suffering is not the final word; rather, it is part of a larger story that ends in restoration, where pain and death will be eradicated once and for all. In the face of suffering, the Bible calls us to respond with faith, perseverance, and hope. Although we may not fully understand why we suffer, we can trust in God's sovereignty and goodness, knowing that He is present with us in our pain and that He is working through it for our ultimate good. As we endure, we cling to the hope of a future where suffering will be no more, and all things will be made new. Redemption and the Promise of Restoration While the Bible acknowledges the reality of suffering, it also provides a powerful message of hope through redemption and the promise of restoration . The central message of the Christian faith is that Jesus Christ, through His life, death, and resurrection, has provided a way for humanity to be redeemed from the effects of sin and suffering.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: hope through redemption and the promise of restoration . The central message of the Christian faith is that Jesus Christ, through His life, death, and resurrection, has provided a way for humanity to be redeemed from the effects of sin and suffering. In the midst of pain, there is hope because Christ has entered into human suffering, taking upon Himself the burden of sin and death. 93

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: The apostle Paul reminds us in Romans 8:18-22 that “the sufferings of this present time are not worth comparing with the glory that is to be revealed to us.” This powerful statement helps us keep suffering in perspective. Yes, we experience pain now, but there is coming a day when God's glory will be fully revealed, and all that is broken in the world will be made right. Through Christ's redemption, we are assured that our suffering is not meaningless or in vain. Christ’s suffering on the cross was the ultimate act of love and sacrifice, and through His death and resurrection, He secured our victory over sin and death. Jesus bore the ultimate suffering on our behalf, taking the penalty for our sins so that we could experience eternal life and the hope of future restoration. This act of redemption is the basis for Christian hope in the face of suffering. The Hope of Restoration The hope of restoration is rooted in the promise that God will make all things new. The world as we know it is marked by suffering, but this is not the final reality. God’s plan for restoration is made clear in passages like Revelation 21:4, where it is written, "He will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore, for the former things have passed away." This is the glorious future that awaits believers, a world where suffering, pain, and death have been eradicated, and where God's people will experience eternal peace and joy in His presence. This promise of restoration is not just about the future; it also offers a present hope.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: at awaits believers, a world where suffering, pain, and death have been eradicated, and where God's people will experience eternal peace and joy in His presence. This promise of restoration is not just about the future; it also offers a present hope. While we live in the "already but not yet", where we experience the firstfruits of redemption in Christ but have not yet fully realized the new heavens and new earth, we are still assured that God is with us in our suffering. His presence provides comfort and peace in the midst of pain, and His Holy Spirit helps us endure and persevere. 94

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: Furthermore, redemption and restoration are not limited to the afterlife but are actively at work in the world today. God is constantly working to redeem individuals, communities, and even the world itself through the ministry of reconciliation that He has entrusted to His followers (2 Corinthians 5:18). As we partner with God in His redemptive work, we participate in the process of bringing His healing and restoration to a broken world. Living with Hope in the Midst of Suffering The reality of suffering does not negate the hope that we have in Christ. Rather, it is the very presence of suffering that highlights the depth of God’s redemptive love. As Christians, we are called to live in the tension between the "already" of redemption and the "not yet" of full restoration. This means that while we wait for the future promise of God’s complete restoration, we can live with hope and confidence that our pain is not the final word. In the midst of suffering, we are called to remember that God is present with us. Jesus Himself is called "Immanuel," which means "God with us" (Matthew 1:23). No matter what pain we face, we are not alone. The promise of God’s presence and the hope of ultimate restoration provide the strength to endure and persevere. As we live in light of the redemption Christ has already secured, we are empowered to face suffering with a different perspective, one that sees pain not as the end, but as a part of the story that points to a greater hope. We can endure suffering because we know that it is not meaningless; it is part of God’s redemptive plan. We can endure suffering because we know that, in the end, restoration is coming. 95

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: CONCLUSION: A HOPE THAT TRANSCENDS SUFFERING Suffering is an unavoidable aspect of the human condition, and while it can often feel overwhelming and without purpose, it is not the final chapter in the story of our lives. The cross of Christ offers us an unshakable hope that transcends the deepest of pains and the most difficult of trials. Through His death and resurrection, Jesus has secured redemption for all who trust in Him, guaranteeing that suffering does not have the final say. We may never fully grasp the reasons behind our suffering or why certain hardships are allowed to touch our lives, but we can be certain that, in the grand narrative of God’s redemptive plan, suffering is never without purpose. It is part of the journey toward restoration, where every tear will be wiped away, and pain will be no more. Even in our pain, we are not abandoned or left to face it alone. The presence of God is with us, and His redemptive work continues to unfold, transforming us through our suffering. This hope is not simply a distant, future promise that we must wait for passively. It is a present reality that sustains us in our daily lives. As we face trials, we are reminded that Christ is with us, actively working in and through us, using our suffering to shape and refine us. We endure with the assurance that suffering does not define us, but rather God’s work in us and through us is what truly defines our lives. Ultimately, our hope is anchored in the promise of a future where all things are made new. In the new heavens and new earth, there will be no more pain, no more sorrow, no more death (Revelation 21:4). This hope of restoration fuels our perseverance through life’s difficulties.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: ored in the promise of a future where all things are made new. In the new heavens and new earth, there will be no more pain, no more sorrow, no more death (Revelation 21:4). This hope of restoration fuels our perseverance through life’s difficulties. It gives us the strength to endure, knowing that the present suffering is temporary, and the glorious future we await is eternal. 96

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: Thus, the hope of the Christian faith is not merely a hopeful wish; it is a steadfast assurance grounded in the finished work of Christ. In the midst of suffering, we cling to this hope, for it is the very thing that sustains us and carries us forward, no matter what trials we may face. In Christ, we have a hope that transcends suffering, a hope that one day, all things will be made right. 97

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: PART IV: CHRISTIANITY AND CULTURE 98

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: CHAPTER 10 UNITY IN DIVERSITY One Race, One Savior (Galatians 3:28) In a world filled with divisions based on race, nationality, and culture, the gospel of Jesus Christ stands as a powerful declaration of unity. The Apostle Paul, in his letter to the Galatians, writes, "There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is neither male nor female; for you are all one in Christ Jesus" (Galatians 3:28, NKJV). This profound statement challenges the natural tendency of humanity to categorize and separate based on external distinctions. In Christ, all human barriers, whether racial, social, or gender-based, are abolished. The gospel declares that salvation is available to all, regardless of background, ethnicity, or status. This message, so counter-cultural in its time, continues to speak powerfully to the world today, where divisions often dominate our social structures. Paul’s declaration in Galatians 3:28 highlights a profound spiritual reality: all believers are equal in Christ. There is no hierarchy or exclusion within the family of God. Everyone, regardless of race or background, is welcomed into the fold of Christ. The cross of Jesus removes the distinctions that often cause division among humanity, uniting believers in a common faith and a shared identity as children of God. This unity, however, does not mean that cultural and ethnic differences are erased. Rather, it means that these differences, when viewed through the lens of the gospel, become opportunities for the beauty of diversity to enrich the body of Christ. The gospel does not call for uniformity but for 99

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: unity in diversity, where people from all walks of life come together to worship one Savior. THE CHURCH AS A FAMILY FOR ALL NATIONS The vision of the church as a family transcends earthly distinctions and reflects God’s divine plan for humanity, a plan that extends beyond the confines of any single nation, culture, or ethnicity. From its inception, the church was meant to embody the unifying power of the gospel, drawing people from every corner of the world to find their ultimate identity in Christ. In Matthew 28:19-20, Jesus commissions His disciples to go into all the world and make disciples of all nations, emphasizing the global scope of the gospel message. This call is not merely a future hope but a present reality that is being worked out in the life of the church today. The church is meant to reflect the diversity of the nations while also manifesting a profound unity in Christ. This unity is not a superficial harmony achieved through human effort or conformity but a deeper, spiritual unity that is rooted in the shared experience of redemption through Jesus Christ. The Apostle Paul writes in Ephesians 2:19, "Now therefore you are no longer strangers and foreigners, but fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God." In Christ, all believers, whether Jew or Gentile, rich or poor, male or female, are adopted into God’s family and become part of a new, unified people. This unity does not erase cultural differences or diminish the uniqueness of individuals but, instead, affirms and celebrates them. Each person, regardless of background, brings a unique perspective and cultural richness that enhances the whole.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: ple. This unity does not erase cultural differences or diminish the uniqueness of individuals but, instead, affirms and celebrates them. Each person, regardless of background, brings a unique perspective and cultural richness that enhances the whole. The church, as the body of Christ, is composed of many members, each with different functions, yet all working together in harmony (1 Corinthians 12:12-27). In this way, the church is called to be a family, one that spans across national boundaries, racial distinctions, and cultural differences. 100

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: A Vision of Global Unity in Revelation 7:9 The Apostle John’s vision in Revelation 7:9 offers a powerful picture of this global unity in Christ. “After these things I looked, and behold, a great multitude which no one could number, of all nations, tribes, peoples, and tongues, standing before the throne and before the Lamb.” This vision is not merely a description of a future event; it is a theological declaration of what the church is meant to be in the present. The worship of God in heaven will be a glorious, diverse gathering of people from every tribe, tongue, and nation, all united in their adoration of the Lamb. This vision challenges the church today to reflect the same unity in diversity that is depicted in this heavenly scene. The church on earth is meant to be a foretaste of this future reality, a community where people from every nation and culture come together to worship the one true God. Just as there will be no division between races or nations in heaven, so too, the church must strive to overcome the divisions that often exist in the world. The Church as a Family Beyond National and Cultural Boundaries The church, as the family of God, is fundamentally a community of belonging that transcends all earthly distinctions. Through the gospel, God has torn down the walls that once divided humanity. In Christ, there is no longer Jew or Gentile, slave or free, male or female, such distinctions are irrelevant to one’s standing before God. What matters is one’s identity in Christ, who brings people together from all walks of life into the family of God. In this family, members are no longer strangers or foreigners but fellow citizens with the saints (Ephesians 2:19).

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: anding before God. What matters is one’s identity in Christ, who brings people together from all walks of life into the family of God. In this family, members are no longer strangers or foreigners but fellow citizens with the saints (Ephesians 2:19). The church, therefore, is not merely an institution or an organization, but a living, dynamic family that spans across ethnicities, cultures, and nationalities. It is a family that finds 101

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: its identity in Christ and is bound together by a common faith, shared values, and a commitment to live according to God’s will. The church’s family-like structure is based on mutual love, respect, and care. The relationships within the body of Christ are characterized by a deep sense of belonging, not just to a local congregation but to the universal family of God. This unity is not a forced conformity but a natural outgrowth of the gospel’s power to reconcile and transform. The Practical Outworking of Unity in Diversity While the theological truth of unity in diversity is clear, it must also be lived out in practical terms within the church. This requires intentionality and commitment from all believers to welcome and embrace those who are different from themselves. It calls for a willingness to extend grace to those with whom we may not share common experiences, cultures, or perspectives. The church must create spaces where people from diverse backgrounds feel valued and included. This goes beyond simply tolerating differences; it requires actively celebrating the richness of the various cultures and traditions represented within the body of Christ. The early church, for example, was comprised of Jews and Gentiles, each with their own distinct cultural practices, yet they were called to live in unity through their shared faith in Christ. The apostle Paul, writing to the church in Galatia, reminds believers that in Christ, there is no division based on ethnic or cultural identity. "There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is neither male nor female; for you are all one in Christ Jesus" (Galatians 3:28). This verse underscores the radical inclusivity of the gospel.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: d on ethnic or cultural identity. "There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is neither male nor female; for you are all one in Christ Jesus" (Galatians 3:28). This verse underscores the radical inclusivity of the gospel. It calls the church to reject divisions based on race, class, gender, and nationality, affirming that all believers are united in Christ. 102

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: Moreover, this unity in diversity is not just a theological concept but a practical responsibility. It means taking the time to understand and appreciate the cultural expressions and experiences of others. It means engaging in meaningful relationships with people from different backgrounds and working together for the common good of the kingdom of God. The church must be a place where every believer, regardless of their ethnicity or cultural background, can flourish in their unique identity while being united in Christ. Living Out the Vision of a Family for All Nations The vision of the church as a family for all nations calls us to action. It challenges us to actively work toward building a community that reflects the diversity of the kingdom of God. This involves not only celebrating the differences among believers but also striving for unity in the midst of those differences. It requires an ongoing commitment to love one another, to forgive one another, and to work together for the common mission of the church. The church is called to be a beacon of hope in a divided world. In a society where racial, ethnic, and cultural divisions are often emphasized, the church stands as a powerful testimony to the unifying power of the gospel. By embracing and celebrating diversity, the church not only reflects the character of God but also becomes a witness to the world of the transformative power of Christ. As believers, we are called to live in light of this vision, actively working to build a community that is truly a family for all nations. This means rejecting all forms of prejudice and division, welcoming people from every background, and living out the love of Christ in every aspect of our lives. A Unified Family, A Global Witness 103

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: The church, as the family of God, is a unique community that transcends all earthly distinctions. United in Christ, believers from every nation, ethnicity, and culture are called to live together in harmony, reflecting the unity that will one day be fully realized in heaven. The gospel calls the church to be a family for all nations, a place where unity in diversity is not only possible but expected. As the body of Christ, we are to live out this vision in our everyday lives, actively embracing and celebrating the diversity that reflects the heart of God. The church, as a unified family, is a powerful witness to the world of the reconciling power of the gospel, and it is through this unity that the church can fulfill its mission of making disciples of all nations. CELEBRATING DIVERSITY IN THE CHURCH A healthy church embraces diversity, not as a mere concept but as a lived experience. It is not enough to acknowledge diversity; the church must actively celebrate it. This means creating spaces where different cultures, languages, and backgrounds are valued and incorporated into the life of the church. It is about fostering an environment where people feel seen, heard, and loved, no matter where they come from. Unity in diversity also involves recognizing that different cultural expressions of faith enrich the worship and life of the church. Whether it is through music, art, or fellowship, the varied cultural practices within the body of Christ can serve as powerful expressions of the manifold wisdom of God (Ephesians 3:10). When the church gathers, it should reflect the beauty of God’s creation in all its diversity, pointing to the truth that in Christ, all are one. The gospel calls the church to be a beacon of hope in a divided world.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: ld wisdom of God (Ephesians 3:10). When the church gathers, it should reflect the beauty of God’s creation in all its diversity, pointing to the truth that in Christ, all are one. The gospel calls the church to be a beacon of hope in a divided world. It invites people from all nations to come together in the love of Christ, breaking down barriers that separate and dividing walls that isolate. As the church lives out its calling to unity in diversity, it becomes a witness to the 104

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: world of the power of the gospel to transform hearts, break down walls, and bring about reconciliation and peace. A Kingdom Without Borders The gospel is not bound by cultural, racial, or national lines. God’s kingdom is vast and includes people from every corner of the earth. The church’s mission is to proclaim the good news to every nation, bringing people from every tribe, tongue, and culture into the family of God. The diversity found within the church is a glimpse of what heaven will look like, a diverse and united people worshiping the Lamb who was slain. As believers, we are called to live out the reality of this unity in our everyday lives. This means loving our neighbors, regardless of their background, and actively seeking reconciliation where there is division. It also means embracing the beauty of diversity within the body of Christ, understanding that our differences are a reflection of God’s creativity and grace. The message of the gospel is clear: all people are invited to come to Christ, and in Him, we are one. The church is a family for all nations, a place where unity is found in the love and grace of Jesus Christ. And in this family, there is no room for prejudice, exclusion, or division. Instead, there is only room for love, acceptance, and the celebration of the diversity that reflects the heart of God. Conclusion In Christ, there is neither Jew nor Greek, neither slave nor free, neither male nor female. The gospel transcends all boundaries and unites believers from all walks of life. As the church, we are called to live as a family for all nations, embracing our diversity while maintaining our unity in Christ. This unity in diversity is a powerful testimony to the world of the 105

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: transforming power of the gospel, and it is a picture of the heavenly reality that awaits us. In the church, there is no room for division; only love, unity, and the joy of being one family in Christ. 106

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: CHAPTER 11 ENGAGING A POSTMODERN WORLD WITH ANCIENT TRUTH In the postmodern era, society has embraced a worldview that is often characterized by skepticism toward absolute truths, relativism, and deconstruction of traditional beliefs and values. The postmodern thinker rejects the certainty of grand narratives and embraces a multiplicity of perspectives, with the conviction that truth is subjective, fluid, and often culturally constructed. This shift has deeply influenced how people engage with truth, authority, and meaning. In a world that questions everything, Christianity is sometimes seen as outdated, irrelevant, or incompatible with modern thought. However, this perspective overlooks the enduring relevance and depth of the ancient truths of the Christian faith. Christianity, at its core, is built on eternal, unchanging truths that transcend time, culture, and philosophical trends. The gospel of Jesus Christ, which forms the foundation of the Christian faith, is not merely a cultural artifact from a bygone era; it is the truth of God’s eternal plan for humanity, revealed in the person of Jesus Christ and grounded in the Scriptures. The message of the Bible is not static or time-bound but is ever relevant, offering wisdom for living, answers to human suffering, and hope for the future. To engage a postmodern world with ancient truth, the church must communicate the gospel in a way that is thoughtful, relevant, and intellectually honest. This means not retreating into simplistic or dogmatic answers but engaging deeply with the questions that the postmodern world raises.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: ent truth, the church must communicate the gospel in a way that is thoughtful, relevant, and intellectually honest. This means not retreating into simplistic or dogmatic answers but engaging deeply with the questions that the postmodern world raises. Christians are called to be faithful stewards of the gospel, translating it into language and concepts that are meaningful and accessible to a world that is questioning, deconstructing, and seeking truth. 107

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: Faith in the Age of Deconstruction Deconstruction, a concept that has grown increasingly prevalent in contemporary discourse, particularly in relation to faith, involves the critical examination and often dismantling of previously held beliefs and structures. Originating from postmodern thought, deconstruction seeks to unravel and question the foundational assumptions upon which ideologies, religious systems, and even personal identities are built. For many, particularly within the realm of Christianity, deconstruction is seen as a necessary process in the journey toward truth, a means of freeing oneself from inherited dogmas that may no longer align with personal experiences or intellectual integrity. In its most constructive form, deconstruction is not the rejection of faith but an invitation to deeper understanding. It involves interrogating the frameworks that shape religious belief, testing them against lived experience, reason, and personal convictions. This intellectual and emotional process often leads individuals to discard certain theological constructs that seem untenable, outdated, or incompatible with their current understanding of the world. This journey, while painful, can open doors to a more authentic faith, one that is not simply inherited or dictated but personally embraced. However, the process of deconstruction can also lead some to a place of doubt, uncertainty, and even the complete abandonment of their faith. This is particularly true when the questions raised are met with silence or rejection, when the church fails to provide a safe space for wrestling with difficult issues.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: a place of doubt, uncertainty, and even the complete abandonment of their faith. This is particularly true when the questions raised are met with silence or rejection, when the church fails to provide a safe space for wrestling with difficult issues. In such cases, deconstruction can become a path not to a refined or renewed faith but to spiritual disillusionment, where the very act of questioning leads to a complete rejection of previously held convictions. As the church engages with this postmodern era marked by widespread deconstruction, it must adopt a posture of both empathy and truth. 108

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: Empathy is not merely a therapeutic response but a profound theological stance rooted in the love of Christ. When individuals embark on the painful journey of deconstructing their faith, it often stems from a place of deep hurt, confusion, or disillusionment. These feelings may arise from experiences of spiritual abuse, hypocrisy, or intellectual dishonesty within religious communities. As such, the church is called to become a place of refuge, an environment where doubts are not silenced but are welcomed, where questions are not seen as a threat but as an opportunity for growth. It is essential, however, to recognize that empathy alone is insufficient. While the church must offer a compassionate space for doubt and deconstruction, it must also be equipped to provide a robust foundation for faith. Christianity, when fully understood and embraced, offers compelling answers to the profound questions that often underlie the process of deconstruction: questions of meaning, purpose, suffering, and identity. The Christian faith is not merely a set of abstract theological propositions but a lived reality that offers existential hope. It provides a framework for understanding the complexities of human existence and the world in which we live. The Bible, as the revealed Word of God, speaks with depth and honesty about human frailty, doubt, and suffering. It challenges its readers to confront the complexities of life, offering both comfort and challenge, mystery and clarity. Moreover, the gospel offers something that is deeply essential in the postmodern age: a sense of ultimate meaning and purpose. In a world that is increasingly fragmented, where the quest for truth often feels like an endless and hopeless pursuit, the Christian faith provides an anchor.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: g that is deeply essential in the postmodern age: a sense of ultimate meaning and purpose. In a world that is increasingly fragmented, where the quest for truth often feels like an endless and hopeless pursuit, the Christian faith provides an anchor. It affirms that ultimate truth is not relative or arbitrary but is found in the person of Jesus Christ, who is "the way, the truth, and the life" (John 14:6). Christianity teaches that the search for meaning does not lead to an impasse but to a Person, Jesus Christ, who embodies ultimate truth and offers it freely to all who seek Him. 109

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: Engaging a postmodern world through the lens of ancient truth requires humility, patience, and an unwavering commitment to the gospel. The church must be prepared to meet people where they are, whether they are in the midst of deconstructing their beliefs, struggling with doubt, or longing for a more authentic and meaningful faith. The journey of deconstruction is not a linear path and does not follow a prescribed formula. Some may emerge from it with a deeper, more nuanced faith, while others may feel lost or disillusioned. Yet, through it all, the church must be present, not as a gatekeeper of rigid doctrine but as a community that journeys alongside others, offering wisdom, encouragement, and the enduring hope of the gospel. As Christians, we are called to model a faith that is intellectually honest, emotionally real, and deeply rooted in the timeless truths of Scripture. This does not mean offering simplistic or superficial answers to deep questions but embracing the complexity of the Christian faith. Christianity is not a faith that demands blind adherence to doctrines; rather, it calls individuals to wrestle with truth in the context of relationship with God. It invites us to engage our minds, our emotions, and our experiences with a view toward a deeper understanding of God's truth. In a world marked by skepticism, relativism, and deconstruction, the church must demonstrate that faith is not incompatible with critical thought or intellectual engagement. It is not enough to simply reject postmodernism or deconstruction outright. Instead, we must invite those who are deconstructing to engage with the deeper truths of the Christian faith, to explore the gospel with both their minds and hearts, and to find in it the answers to life's deepest questions.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: deconstruction outright. Instead, we must invite those who are deconstructing to engage with the deeper truths of the Christian faith, to explore the gospel with both their minds and hearts, and to find in it the answers to life's deepest questions. Through such engagement, the church can offer a faith that is both intellectually rigorous and profoundly transformative, one that transcends the doubts and complexities of the modern world and offers the timeless truth of God's love, grace, and redemption in Christ. 110

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: In doing so, the church fulfills its calling as a beacon of hope in a world that is searching for meaning, purpose, and truth. It offers a faith that is not afraid of questions but that stands firm in the face of doubt, grounded in the unshakable truth of God’s revelation in Jesus Christ. Christianity Is Not Anti-Intellectual In a world that increasingly values intellectual inquiry, reason, and critical thinking, it is essential to affirm that Christianity is not inherently anti-intellectual. Rather, Christianity has a rich intellectual tradition that has engaged with the deepest questions of life, existence, ethics, and knowledge for centuries. Throughout history, Christian thinkers, from Augustine to Aquinas, from Kierkegaard to C.S. Lewis, have made significant contributions to philosophy, theology, and the humanities, demonstrating that the Christian faith is deeply intertwined with intellectual thought. The Bible itself is not a simple, anti-intellectual text; it is a profound work that invites its readers into a deep, reflective engagement with truth. Scripture challenges believers to think critically, engage with complex theological concepts, and wrestle with the mysteries of the divine. The apostle Paul, in 2 Corinthians 10:5, urges believers to "take every thought captive to obey Christ," underscoring the role of the mind in the life of faith. Christianity, therefore, does not oppose intellectual pursuits but encourages them, recognizing that the search for truth is integral to the human calling to reflect the image of God. Jesus, in His earthly ministry, exemplified this intellectual engagement.

The fact that Jesus engaged so thoughtfully with the ideas of His contemporaries indicates that intellectual pursuit and spiritual development are not mutually exclusive in Christianity. His 111

In the context of a postmodern world, where skepticism and relativism dominate, it is vital for Christians to demonstrate that their faith is not incompatible with intellectual exploration. The church must cultivate an environment where intellectual engagement is valued, where difficult questions are welcomed, and where doubt is not seen as a threat but an opportunity for growth. In doing so, the church can create a space where intellectual rigor is held in balance with spiritual depth, fostering a thoughtful faith that engages both the mind and the heart. Christianity encourages believers to engage deeply with the world’s ideas, whether in philosophy, science, history, or culture. The integration of these disciplines with faith allows Christians to form a more comprehensive worldview that addresses the ultimate questions of existence, meaning, and purpose. Jesus commands His followers to love God with all their heart, soul, mind, and strength (Mark 12:30). This love for God is expressed not only through emotion and devotion but also through intellectual engagement. Christians are called to cultivate a love for truth and knowledge in every sphere of life, acknowledging that all truth is God’s truth. In the postmodern age, where truth is often seen as relative and fragmented, Christians are uniquely positioned to offer a coherent and compelling vision of reality. Christianity provides answers to some of the deepest existential questions, offering a robust worldview that can stand up to the intellectual challenges posed by contemporary culture.
