Thus, the church must encourage its members to engage in intellectual pursuits, not only as a way to strengthen their own faith but also as a means of offering a credible and thoughtful witness to the world. Christianity does not require the abandonment of reason; it invites believers into a journey of faith that is intellectually rigorous and spiritually fulfilling. The ancient truths of Christianity offer a solid foundation for understanding the complexities of the modern world, showing that faith and intellect are not in opposition but are integral components of a faithful and holistic Christian life. 113

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: CHAPTER 12 A UNIQUE WORLDVIEW: COHERENCE, BEAUTY, AND TRANSFORMATIVE POWER Christianity stands apart from other worldviews not merely because of its longevity or cultural influence but because of its unique coherence, beauty, and transformative power. In a world filled with competing ideologies and religious claims, Christianity offers a worldview that is intellectually satisfying, existentially meaningful, morally compelling, and spiritually life-giving. Coherence: A Worldview That Holds Together One of Christianity’s distinguishing features is its coherent narrative, its ability to address life’s deepest questions with intellectual and existential integrity. It speaks meaningfully to questions such as: Where did we come from? What has gone wrong with the world? Is there hope for restoration? What lies beyond this life? These questions are answered through a grand, unifying storyline composed of four movements: Creation , Fall , Redemption , and Consummation . From Genesis to Revelation, the Bible offers a unified framework that integrates theology, human experience, and historical reality. This coherence is not forced or artificial, it emerges from a diverse collection of writings penned by multiple authors over centuries, yet with a consistent redemptive arc centered on the person and work of Jesus Christ. Christianity does not offer isolated dogmas; it offers a worldview, a lens through which all of life can be interpreted with meaning and hope. In contrast, many other worldviews struggle to make sense of the fullness of human experience. Materialism , for instance, may offer scientific 114

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: explanations for the physical world, but it falters when asked to account for consciousness, morality, purpose, or the human longing for transcendence. Eastern pantheistic systems may affirm spiritual reality but often dissolve distinctions of personal identity and moral responsibility, making human suffering either illusory or irrelevant. Christianity, however, does not shy away from complexity. It embraces the spiritual and the material, the personal and communal, the rational and the mysterious. It acknowledges the reality of evil and suffering, yet offers not mere explanation, but redemption, through a God who steps into time, bears human pain, and offers healing from within. Rather than offering simplistic answers, it presents a suffering Savior who embodies truth, justice, and mercy. This coherence is not merely theoretical. It is practical and livable. It allows Christians to hold together intellect and emotion, truth and grace, faith and reason. It gives a story large enough to make sense of the brokenness we see, and hopeful enough to anchor our lives in love, purpose, and eternal promise. Beauty: A Vision That Captivates the Heart Christianity is not only intellectually compelling, it is aesthetically and existentially beautiful. Its beauty lies not merely in abstract doctrine, but in the narrative it tells: the story of a holy and loving God who enters into the brokenness of His creation to redeem and restore. The incarnation of Christ, His self-giving death on the cross, and His victorious resurrection are not cold theological propositions, they are the luminous expression of divine love breaking into history.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: enness of His creation to redeem and restore. The incarnation of Christ, His self-giving death on the cross, and His victorious resurrection are not cold theological propositions, they are the luminous expression of divine love breaking into history. This story of grace, that God would pursue the undeserving, forgive the guilty, and heal the wounded, resonates with a deep longing in the human soul. It speaks to our desire not just for truth, but for meaning, belonging, 115

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: and restoration. It is a beauty that satisfies the mind, yes, but also captures the imagination and touches the heart. The beauty of Christianity is also reflected in its enduring cultural legacy. The grandeur of European cathedrals, the stirring compositions of Johann Sebastian Bach, the writings of Dante, Dostoevsky, and Lewis, all testify to a vision of life animated by Christian hope. These works are not merely religious, they are human, because they flow from a view of the world where every person is made in the image of God and every moment can be imbued with divine significance. Yet the most powerful expression of Christian beauty is not confined to stained glass or sonatas. It is revealed in the transformation of lives. When the addict is liberated, the wounded find healing, the marginalized are embraced, and enemies are reconciled, the beauty of the gospel is made visible. These living testimonies proclaim that Christianity is not just a system of belief, but an encounter with grace that renews and re-creates. The Christian vision of beauty is not superficial or sentimental. It is a beauty that weeps with those who suffer, that bears scars, and that shines most brightly in the face of despair. In a world often marked by ugliness, fragmentation, and cynicism, the beauty of Christ stands as a radiant hope, a vision that not only explains the world, but re-enchants it. Transformative Power: A Faith That Changes Lives What most profoundly distinguishes Christianity is not merely the coherence of its worldview or the beauty of its narrative, it is the transformative power at its heart. Christianity is not a philosophy to be admired or a moral system to be followed; it is the power of God breaking into human history to redeem, renew, and restore.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: worldview or the beauty of its narrative, it is the transformative power at its heart. Christianity is not a philosophy to be admired or a moral system to be followed; it is the power of God breaking into human history to redeem, renew, and restore. As the apostle Paul writes, “I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ: for it is the power of 116

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: God unto salvation to every one that believeth” (Romans 1:16, KJV). This power is not theoretical; it is experiential and evidential. The gospel announces not what humanity must do to reach God, but what God has done in Christ to reach humanity. It is the proclamation of divine initiative, of God entering the human condition, bearing its sin, and inaugurating a new creation through the resurrection of Jesus. Those who believe are not simply given new information; they are given new life. Through union with Christ, they are justified, regenerated, indwelt by the Holy Spirit, and adopted into the family of God. This is no mere change of status, it is a change of nature. Such transformation reshapes the entire person. It renews the mind (Romans 12:2), heals emotional wounds, redirects the will, and reorients desires. It empowers individuals to live not by compulsion or fear, but by love and grace. Christianity offers more than behavioral modification, it offers spiritual resurrection. It does not promise perfection in this life, but it does promise a real and ongoing transformation marked by growth in holiness, wholeness, and Christlikeness. Moreover, this transformation is not confined to individuals. It spills over into communities. The church becomes a living testimony to the reconciling power of the gospel, a community that transcends racial, social, economic, and cultural divides. In a fractured world, the church offers a vision of unity through Christ that is as radical as it is needed. Where other ideologies may call for self-discovery, personal enlightenment, or moral striving, Christianity calls for and enables nothing less than new creation (2 Corinthians 5:17). It is not the self that is elevated, but the soul that is remade.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: . Where other ideologies may call for self-discovery, personal enlightenment, or moral striving, Christianity calls for and enables nothing less than new creation (2 Corinthians 5:17). It is not the self that is elevated, but the soul that is remade. It is not by ascending through effort that we are changed, but by receiving grace freely given. In this, Christianity is utterly unique: it is a faith whose truths do not merely inform, they transform. 117

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: PART V: BECOMING AND BELONGING 118

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: CHAPTER 13 HOW TO BECOME A CHRISTIAN Responding to the Gospel: What It Means to Believe and Follow Jesus Christianity is more than a set of beliefs, it is a relationship with the living God through Jesus Christ. While the gospel is rich in theological depth and historical grounding, it is also deeply personal. The message of Christianity calls for a response. It invites every person not merely to acknowledge the truth intellectually, but to embrace it with the heart, entrust oneself to it, and walk in its reality. THE HEART OF THE GOSPEL At the center of the Christian faith lies the gospel, the “good news” that God, in His infinite love and wisdom, has acted decisively in history to rescue humanity from sin and restore us to Himself. This good news is succinctly summarized by the Apostle Paul: “Christ died for our sins according to the scriptures; and that he was buried, and that he rose again the third day according to the scriptures” (1 Corinthians 15:3-4, KJV). These events are not merely historical facts but redemptive acts that form the foundation of Christian salvation. Theologically speaking, the gospel addresses the most fundamental human problem: our alienation from God due to sin. According to Scripture, all have sinned and fall short of God’s glory (Romans 3:23). Sin is not only the violation of divine commands; it is a condition of the heart, a rebellion against the Creator’s rightful rule. This rebellion incurs guilt, results in spiritual death, and separates us from the source of life and meaning. But the gospel proclaims that God did not abandon His creation. Motivated by divine love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, fully God and fully man, to live the life we could not live and to die the death we deserved. On the 119

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: cross, Jesus bore the penalty of sin in our place, satisfying the justice of God while simultaneously displaying His mercy. His resurrection from the dead was not only a vindication of His identity as the Son of God (Romans 1:4) but also a demonstration that sin, death, and the powers of darkness have been defeated. This salvation is not achieved by human effort, moral striving, or religious observance. Paul emphasizes in Ephesians 2:8-9 (KJV), “For by grace are ye saved through faith; and that not of yourselves: it is the gift of God: not of works, lest any man should boast.” Grace is unmerited favor, God’s initiative in reaching out to lost humanity. Faith, in turn, is the means by which we receive this gift. It is not merely intellectual assent or emotional sentiment, but a personal trust in Christ, entrusting our lives to Him and relying wholly on His finished work. To believe the gospel is to respond to God’s invitation with repentance and faith. Repentance involves a turning away from sin and self-rule, a change of mind and direction, and faith involves turning toward Christ, resting in His sufficiency for our salvation. Together, repentance and faith form the human response to the divine initiative of grace. Ultimately, the heart of the gospel is not a philosophy or a moral code, but a person, Jesus Christ. To embrace the gospel is to embrace Him: His cross, His resurrection, His lordship. It is to enter into a relationship with the living God, to be adopted into His family, and to begin a lifelong journey of transformation marked by grace, truth, and hope. WHAT IT MEANS TO BELIEVE Belief, in the Christian sense, is not merely intellectual assent to a set of doctrines or historical facts.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: , to be adopted into His family, and to begin a lifelong journey of transformation marked by grace, truth, and hope. WHAT IT MEANS TO BELIEVE Belief, in the Christian sense, is not merely intellectual assent to a set of doctrines or historical facts. While it certainly includes the affirmation that Jesus is who He claimed to be, fully God and fully man, crucified and risen, it goes far deeper. To believe in Jesus is to place one’s personal trust 120

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: in Him as both Savior and Lord. It is to rest one’s entire confidence not in personal merit, religious activity, or moral achievement, but in the sufficiency of Christ’s finished work on the cross and the power of His resurrection. In the New Testament, the Greek word often translated as "believe" ( pisteuō ) implies a relational trust, a full-hearted commitment. It is the kind of belief that results in allegiance, surrender, and transformation. As such, saving faith is not static or abstract, it is dynamic, personal, and life-altering. Jesus began His public ministry with a call to response: “The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand: repent ye, and believe the gospel” (Mark 1:15, KJV). This summons reveals two inseparable components of biblical faith: repentance and belief . Repentance is a change of mind that leads to a change of life, a turning from sin and self-rule to God’s mercy and reign. It is the recognition that we are not autonomous beings but creatures accountable to a holy Creator, and that our self-sufficiency must give way to divine grace. Believing the gospel, therefore, is not a vague optimism or religious sentimentality. It is a decisive act of the will and heart. It is the acknowledgment that we cannot save ourselves, and that Jesus Christ is both able and willing to save us completely. It involves entrusting our past, present, and future to Him, accepting His forgiveness, submitting to His lordship, and beginning a new life by the Spirit. Faith, then, is both receptive and responsive . It receives the gift of grace and responds in love and obedience. The believer is not only declared righteous before God (justification) but is also set on a path of ongoing renewal (sanctification), empowered by the Spirit and guided by the Word. 121

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: To believe in Christ is to be united with Him, His death becomes our death to sin, and His resurrection becomes our new life (Romans 6:4-5). It is the doorway into a new identity and a new destiny, one that begins now and extends into eternity. WHAT IT MEANS TO FOLLOW JESUS Faith in Christ begins with belief, but it matures and manifests in a life of discipleship, a sustained, lifelong journey of learning from and walking with Jesus. In the words of our Lord Himself: “If any man will come after me, let him deny himself, and take up his cross, and follow me” (Matthew 16:24, KJV). These words are not a call to mere admiration of Christ, but to active participation in His life and mission. To follow Jesus is to reorder one’s life under His lordship and to embrace the costly but rewarding path of transformation. Discipleship, at its core, is about union and communion , being united with Christ by faith and living in communion with Him daily. This new life, as Paul puts it, is one of “walking in newness of life” (Romans 6:4). The believer, having died to sin and been raised with Christ, now embarks on a journey marked by spiritual renewal, moral reorientation, and growing conformity to Christ’s likeness (Romans 8:29). To follow Jesus is to enter His school, to become His apprentice in the way of the kingdom. This involves learning from His Word, submitting to His teachings, imitating His character, and embodying His love in the world. It means loving what He loves, serving those He served, and investing one's life in what truly matters: the glory of God and the good of others. This path of discipleship is not without difficulty. Jesus did not promise a life of ease but one marked by sacrifice, perseverance, and at times, suffering.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: ed, and investing one's life in what truly matters: the glory of God and the good of others. This path of discipleship is not without difficulty. Jesus did not promise a life of ease but one marked by sacrifice, perseverance, and at times, suffering. Yet it is in these very challenges that the believer experiences the sustaining grace of God and discovers the deeper joys of walking with 122

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: Christ. The cross is not only a symbol of salvation, it is the shape of Christian living. As Dietrich Bonhoeffer wrote, “When Christ calls a man, he bids him come and die”, to self-will, to worldly ambitions, and to superficial living. But this death leads to resurrection life, a life infused with purpose, peace, and the abiding presence of God. Moreover, to follow Jesus is to become part of His people. Discipleship is not a solitary endeavor; it unfolds within the context of the church, where believers encourage one another, bear one another’s burdens, and spur one another on to love and good works (Hebrews 10:24-25). It is in community that we learn the rhythms of grace, experience mutual accountability, and grow together into spiritual maturity. Ultimately, following Jesus is not about achieving moral perfection or performing religious duties. It is about abiding in a Person, remaining connected to the vine, as Jesus described in John 15. From that living union flows the fruit of righteousness, the joy of obedience, and the strength to endure. It is a journey of becoming who we were always meant to be, image-bearers of God, redeemed and restored by grace, walking in step with the Savior who calls us friend. A Prayer of Faith There are no magic words or formulas for becoming a Christian. What matters is the posture of the heart. But if you are ready to respond to the gospel and begin a relationship with Jesus, you may express that decision in a simple prayer, like this: “Lord Jesus, I confess that I am a sinner in need of Your mercy. I believe that You died for my sins and rose again. I turn from my sin and place my trust in You. I receive You as my Savior and surrender to You as my Lord. Make me new, and help me follow You all the days of my life. Amen.” 123

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: If you’ve prayed that sincerely, welcome. You’ve begun the most important and life-changing journey you will ever take, the journey of knowing, loving, and following Jesus Christ. 124

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: CHAPTER 14 WHY I AM STILL A CHRISTIAN A Personal and Universal Question The question of why one remains a Christian is both deeply personal and broadly relevant. In a world marked by pluralism, secularism, and skepticism, the persistence of faith can appear not only irrational but even naïve. Yet for many, including myself, remaining a Christian is not a clinging to outdated traditions or emotional crutches, it is the outcome of wrestling honestly with doubt, meaning, history, beauty, and truth. This chapter explores the reasons, intellectual, spiritual, historical, and existential, why I remain committed to Jesus Christ and His gospel. CHRISTIANITY AS INTELLECTUALLY COHERENT One of the most compelling reasons I remain a Christian is the intellectual coherence of the Christian worldview. Christianity does not ask me to abandon reason; rather, it invites me to think deeply, rigorously, and redemptively about reality. It offers a meta-narrative, a grand story, that not only explains the world around me but also illuminates the world within me. It addresses the fundamental questions of life: Where did we come from? What went wrong? What can be done about it? And where is history headed? At its core, the Christian account of reality is structured around four major movements: Creation, Fall, Redemption, and New Creation . These are not merely theological categories; they provide a philosophical and existential framework for understanding the human condition and the cosmos itself. Christianity teaches that the universe is not a random accident, but the purposeful creation of a personal and moral God (Genesis 125

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: 1:1). It explains the presence of moral evil and natural disorder as the result of human rebellion against God, a rupture that affects not only individuals but the whole created order (Romans 5:12; Romans 8:20-22). But Christianity does not stop at diagnosis. It offers a remedy in the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ, the eternal Son who entered into history not only to teach us truth but to bear the full weight of human sin and reconcile us to God (2 Corinthians 5:19-21). Finally, it holds forth the promise of ultimate restoration, of a renewed heaven and earth in which justice dwells, where God’s original intent is finally and fully realized (Revelation 21:1-5). From a philosophical perspective, the Christian faith demonstrates an elegant balance between truths often held in tension: transcendence and immanence, justice and mercy, freedom and sovereignty, individuality and community. The doctrine of the Trinity, one God in three Persons, is not a contradiction, but a profound resolution to the philosophical dilemma of unity and diversity. Only a triune God can be eternally loving within Himself, and thus not dependent on creation to express love. This provides the ontological basis for both relationality and morality, as love is not a mere function of creation but flows from the very nature of divine being (1 John 4:8). Furthermore, Christianity affirms both human dignity and human depravity, another balance many worldviews fail to hold. On the one hand, humans are made in the image of God (imago Dei), possessing intrinsic worth, rationality, and moral responsibility. On the other hand, we are fallen creatures, corrupted by sin, and unable to save ourselves. This dual affirmation accounts for both the glory and tragedy of human experience.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: go Dei), possessing intrinsic worth, rationality, and moral responsibility. On the other hand, we are fallen creatures, corrupted by sin, and unable to save ourselves. This dual affirmation accounts for both the glory and tragedy of human experience. The resurrection of Jesus Christ stands at the center of this intellectual and historical coherence. It is not a mythological invention or a symbolic fable; it is presented in the New Testament as a real, bodily event attested by 126

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: multiple eyewitnesses (1 Corinthians 15:3-8), many of whom were martyred for refusing to deny what they saw. The early Christian movement did not flourish on the basis of abstract principles but on the explosive claim that a man who had died publicly and shamefully rose physically and victoriously. This event was not merely a private spiritual experience but a public historical claim, open to investigation and embedded in the timeline of Roman history (Luke 3:1-2). The resurrection provides the cornerstone for Christian epistemology and ethics, it affirms that truth is embodied, that death is defeated, and that history has meaning. In every age, from the early Church Fathers to medieval scholastics, from Reformation thinkers to modern philosophers, Christianity has welcomed the contributions of rigorous thinkers. Church luminaries like Augustine of Hippo wrestled with the integration of faith and philosophy. Anselm’s “faith seeking understanding,” Aquinas’s synthesis of reason and revelation, Pascal’s wager and critique of human arrogance, Lewis’s moral argument for God, and Alvin Plantinga’s defense of the rationality of belief in God, all demonstrate that Christianity thrives not in the absence of reason but in its sanctification. To be clear, Christian faith is not reducible to logic or empirical proofs. It is relational and spiritual, requiring the illumination of the Holy Spirit. Yet it is never irrational or anti-intellectual. As Paul wrote, believers are not called to abandon the mind but to renew it (Romans 12:2), to bring every thought captive to Christ (2 Corinthians 10:5). The Christian life is one of “loving the Lord your God with all your heart, soul, mind, and strength” (Mark 12:30).

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN

The Christian life is one of “loving the Lord your God with all your heart, soul, mind, and strength” (Mark 12:30). In a world that often embraces relativism, scientism, or nihilism, I find in Christianity a worldview that neither ignores the mind nor idolizes it. Instead, it brings the intellect under the Lordship of Christ and sends it 127

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: forth into the world to pursue wisdom, truth, and beauty for the glory of God and the good of others. CHRISTIANITY AS HISTORICALLY RELIABLE In an age where skepticism is often seen as a virtue and historical revisionism runs deep, many have walked away from Christianity due to the moral failures of its adherents or the perception that its origins are clouded by myth and manipulation. I do not deny that the Church has often faltered, its complicity in colonial exploitation, systemic injustices, abuse, and schism cannot be excused or dismissed. These failures are tragic betrayals of the very gospel the Church proclaims. Yet, I remain a Christian not because the Church has always been faithful, but because Christ is . There is a critical distinction between the visible Church, composed of flawed human beings, and the perfect Christ who stands at its center. The failures of Christians, while lamentable, do not invalidate the truth of Christianity any more than the hypocrisy of a scientist invalidates the laws of physics. Jesus anticipated such moral failures and warned against wolves in sheep’s clothing (Matthew 7:15). His gospel, rather than being disproven by sin within the Church, ironically confirms its message: that humanity is deeply broken and in need of grace. What gives me enduring confidence is not ecclesiastical performance, but historical grounding . Christianity is not a collection of abstract moral teachings or mystical speculations. It is a faith rooted in real events that took place in real places involving real people. The life, death, and resurrection of Jesus of Nazareth stand as the historical bedrock of Christian belief. 128

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: The Reliability of the New Testament The New Testament documents are the most well-attested texts from the ancient world by a significant margin. With over 5,800 Greek manuscripts , thousands more in Latin and other languages, and a chain of patristic citations that allow for near-total reconstruction of the New Testament by the fourth century, we possess a textual foundation unparalleled in ancient literature. By comparison, works like Homer’s Iliad or Caesar’s Gallic Wars survive in far fewer and much later copies. The process by which the New Testament canon was recognized was not a haphazard vote or political conspiracy, as some popular narratives (e.g., The Da Vinci Code ) have suggested. Rather, early Christians affirmed texts that were apostolic in origin, orthodox in doctrine, and consistently used in worship across diverse geographical regions. The Muratorian Fragment (c. AD 170), the writings of Church Fathers like Irenaeus, and the deliberations of church councils reflect the discernment of a community already guided by the Spirit and united by the gospel’s core content. Moreover, the content of the New Testament arose far too early to be legendary accretion. Scholars across the spectrum acknowledge that key traditions about Jesus, His death, burial, resurrection, and post-resurrection appearances, were circulating within years, if not months, of the crucifixion. For example, the creed cited by Paul in 1 Corinthians 15:3-8, which he “delivered” but also “received,” is widely dated to within 5 years of Jesus’ death. This creed contains the same essential proclamation that the Church has maintained for two millennia: Christ died for our sins, was buried, and rose again on the third day.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: ered” but also “received,” is widely dated to within 5 years of Jesus’ death. This creed contains the same essential proclamation that the Church has maintained for two millennia: Christ died for our sins, was buried, and rose again on the third day. This early testimony, combined with the transformation of cowardly disciples into bold witnesses (Acts 4:13), the conversion of skeptics like James the brother of Jesus and Saul of Tarsus (later Paul), and the growth of a persecuted movement in the face of Roman hostility, strongly affirms 129

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: that the resurrection was not a later theological embellishment but the catalytic event of Christian faith. The Role of the Creeds and Councils The early creeds , such as the Apostles’ Creed and the Nicene Creed, were not political tools for control but theological summaries intended to safeguard the apostolic witness in the face of heresy and cultural pressure. These formulations arose not from ivory towers but from communities under persecution, seeking to faithfully confess what they had received. The ecumenical councils of the early Church (e.g., Nicaea, Chalcedon) engaged in deep theological reflection to articulate the mystery of the Incarnation and the Trinity, not to invent new doctrine but to clarify what the Scriptures taught and what the apostles handed down. Far from undermining the gospel, these historic moments reflect the Church's profound commitment to intellectual rigor and fidelity to truth . CHRISTIANITY AS PUBLIC TRUTH Unlike mystical traditions or esoteric philosophies, Christianity is a faith open to public verification. Its foundational claims, especially the resurrection, invite historical inquiry. The Gospels provide geographical, political, and personal details that anchor the narrative in history. Luke begins his Gospel by stating that he sought to compile “an orderly account” based on eyewitness testimony (Luke 1:1-4). Acts records speeches and events that correlate with Roman legal, political, and cultural realities. Paul's letters assume the public nature of Christian claims, even appealing to the knowledge of his readers (“this was not done in a corner,” Acts 26:26). In other words, Christianity does not fear the light of scrutiny. Its claims are testable, not in a laboratory, but in the realm of historical reasoning. 130

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: While faith is required, indeed, without faith it is impossible to please God (Hebrews 11:6), that faith is not blind. It is grounded in credible testimony, coherent witness, and an unbroken tradition that proclaims Christ crucified, risen, and reigning CHRISTIANITY AS EXISTENTIALLY SATISFYING There are times in life when syllogisms and systematic theologies seem to falter under the weight of suffering. In the raw moments of grief, betrayal, loss, and longing, the intellect seeks refuge not merely in arguments, but in presence, hope, and meaning. It is in these deeply human experiences, where pain seems to eclipse reason, that I have found Christianity to speak with unmatched power and tenderness. Far from offering a sanitized or escapist account of human life, Christianity confronts suffering head-on. At its center is not a distant deity, nor an impersonal force, but a crucified God . In Jesus Christ, God enters into the human condition not as an observer, but as a participant. The cross stands as the great paradox of divine love and divine justice: the innocent bearing the penalty of the guilty, not as a philosophical abstraction but as a historical event soaked in blood and agony. This is what makes Christianity existentially satisfying, it does not explain away suffering with trite slogans or reductive karma. Rather, it asserts that God has stepped into suffering and redeemed it from within . As the prophet Isaiah declared centuries before Christ, He would be “a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief” (Isaiah 53:3). This prophetic vision was realized in Jesus, who wept at Lazarus’ tomb, groaned in Gethsemane, and cried out on the cross, “My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?” (Mark 15:34, quoting Psalm 22:1).

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: orrows and acquainted with grief” (Isaiah 53:3). This prophetic vision was realized in Jesus, who wept at Lazarus’ tomb, groaned in Gethsemane, and cried out on the cross, “My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?” (Mark 15:34, quoting Psalm 22:1). In doing so, He gave permission for lament and gave context to our own cries. 131

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: In my own life, especially in seasons of profound personal loss and disappointment, it has not been theological constructs alone that sustained me, but the person of Jesus . The doctrine of the Incarnation means that God knows what it is to be tired, to be misunderstood, to be forsaken by friends. The Resurrection assures me that evil and death do not have the final word, that beyond the grave, there is life. The Ascension tells me that Jesus, now exalted, intercedes for His people and governs history with sovereign mercy. And Pentecost , the outpouring of the Holy Spirit, reminds me that God is not only above me or for me, but with me , comforting, sanctifying, and empowering. The Christian story does not pretend that all is well in the world. It teaches that we live in a creation that groans under the weight of sin and futility (Romans 8:20-22), yet it also insists that this groaning is not the end, but the precursor to new birth. The longings we carry, for justice, for beauty, for permanence, are not delusions, but evidences of the Imago Dei in us. Ecclesiastes 3:11 says God has set “eternity in our hearts,” and Romans 8 portrays our yearning as a form of spiritual pregnancy, waiting for the full redemption of our bodies and the restoration of all things. In an existential sense, Christianity offers a story that I can live inside. It gives me a vocabulary for pain, a framework for hope, and a community of fellow pilgrims. When I confront my mortality, Christianity does not deny it, it promises resurrection. When I face injustice, it does not dismiss my anger, it assures me of ultimate judgment and divine vindication. When I am overwhelmed by beauty, whether in art, nature, or love, it tells me that these are foretastes, not accidents.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: ises resurrection. When I face injustice, it does not dismiss my anger, it assures me of ultimate judgment and divine vindication. When I am overwhelmed by beauty, whether in art, nature, or love, it tells me that these are foretastes, not accidents. Unlike nihilism, which says there is no meaning, or Eastern mysticism, which says suffering is illusion, Christianity says suffering is real, but it is not ultimate. Jesus did not bypass suffering; He conquered it by passing through it. He absorbed its poison so that it would no longer have 132

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: dominion over those who trust Him. This is not merely intellectually affirming, it is existentially liberating . I am still a Christian because Christianity does not shrink my soul with empty optimism or crush it with despair. Instead, it expands it, filling it with a hope that is as real as the empty tomb and as sure as the returning King. CHRISTIANITY AS MORALLY TRANSFORMATIVE One of the most compelling reasons I remain a Christian is because of the moral power of the gospel, not merely as an abstract ethical code, but as a dynamic, transformational reality. Christianity does not simply tell people to be good; it makes them new. The message of Jesus Christ has not only reoriented individual lives but has altered the moral landscape of entire civilizations. From a historical perspective, the transformative influence of Christianity is well documented. The early church, despite persecution and marginalization, cultivated communities marked by radical love, economic sharing, racial reconciliation, and compassion for the vulnerable. As Rodney Stark and Tom Holland have argued, Christianity uniquely challenged the moral assumptions of the Greco-Roman world by insisting on the equal worth of all people, slave and free, male and female, Jew and Gentile. Practices such as infanticide, gladiatorial combat, and casual exploitation were gradually undermined by the Christian ethic of neighbor-love, rooted in the Imago Dei, the belief that every human is made in the image of God (Genesis 1:27). The moral fruit of Christianity is not limited to ancient history. The abolitionist movements in the UK and the U.S. were led by Christians like William Wilberforce and Frederick Douglass, who saw slavery as incompatible with the gospel. The founding of hospitals, orphanages, and 133

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: universities in the West, especially during the Middle Ages and Reformation, was deeply informed by Christian convictions about human dignity, stewardship, and the love of neighbor. In more recent times, civil rights leaders like Martin Luther King Jr. drew directly from the teachings of Jesus and the Hebrew prophets to advocate for justice and nonviolence. Yet for all the social impact, what moves me most is the moral transformation I have witnessed, and experienced, on the personal level. The gospel is not a behavioral improvement plan. It is the power of God unto salvation (Romans 1:16), a divine intervention that reorders the human heart. As Paul declares, “If any man be in Christ, he is a new creature: old things are passed away; behold, all things are become new” (2 Corinthians 5:17). The transformation begins with justification, the declaration of righteousness by faith, and continues through sanctification, the Spirit-empowered process of becoming more like Christ. Unlike moralism, which merely reforms behavior, the gospel renews identity. In Christ, the sinner is not simply modified but reborn (John 3:3). The Holy Spirit does not dwell in the morally qualified; He makes His dwelling in the unworthy and qualifies them by grace (Titus 3:4-7). This grace does not excuse sin but empowers obedience. As Paul writes in Titus 2:11-12, “For the grace of God that bringeth salvation hath appeared to all men, teaching us that, denying ungodliness… we should live soberly, righteously, and godly.” In my years of pastoral and personal life, I have seen the moral transformation of the gospel firsthand. I have witnessed addicts overcome their bondage not by willpower alone, but by the indwelling power of the Spirit.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: erly, righteously, and godly.” In my years of pastoral and personal life, I have seen the moral transformation of the gospel firsthand. I have witnessed addicts overcome their bondage not by willpower alone, but by the indwelling power of the Spirit. I have seen hardened cynics melt in the presence of divine mercy. I have watched broken marriages restored through the humility and forgiveness that flow from Christ’s example. I have seen young men and 134

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: women discover purpose, dignity, and moral courage because they came to know they were deeply loved and called by God. Importantly, this transformation is not always instantaneous, nor is it perfect. Sanctification is a lifelong journey, often marked by struggle, setbacks, and grace upon grace. But it is real. The gospel does not merely command the moral life, it creates it. It does not simply demand love, it produces it by pouring God’s love into our hearts through the Holy Spirit (Romans 5:5). In a world that swings between moral relativism and harsh legalism, Christianity offers something better: redemptive transformation. It grounds morality not in shifting cultural trends or coercive external pressure, but in an inner renewal brought about by union with the Risen Christ. The result is not only outward change but inward peace, a life that reflects the love and holiness of God. I am still a Christian because I have experienced this transformation. I have been forgiven, changed, redirected, and empowered, not because I am strong, but because God is gracious. The gospel has not only explained the world to me; it has remade me. CHRISTIANITY AS A STORY THAT MAKES SENSE OF ALL STORIES Human beings are storied creatures. We live, breathe, and interpret our existence through narrative. Whether consciously or unconsciously, every person is shaped by a framework, a worldview, that tells a story about who we are, where we came from, what is wrong with the world, and how it can be made right. Some stories are inherited through culture; others are crafted through personal experience. But all of us, at some level, are asking: What is the story I’m living in, and does it make sense of my life and the world? 135

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: Christianity has endured across centuries and civilizations because it tells a story that is at once theologically profound, historically grounded, and emotionally resonant. It is not a fable, not a mere system of morality, and not a religious philosophy detached from human experience. It is a comprehensive narrative arc, Creation, Fall, Redemption, and Restoration, that uniquely speaks to the human condition and the human hope. Creation affirms the intrinsic dignity and worth of every human being. We are not the result of cosmic accident, as materialism would suggest, nor are we simply expressions of an impersonal divine essence, as in pantheism. Rather, Christianity proclaims that we are imago Dei , made in the image of a personal, relational, and holy God (Genesis 1:26-27). This grounds our longing for love, creativity, and significance in something more than evolutionary instinct; it roots them in the very nature of our Creator. The Fall explains the undeniable brokenness of our world. While some worldviews deny the existence of sin, or reduce evil to social conditioning, Christianity names the fracture: rebellion against God. This rebellion not only separated humanity from God but introduced disorder into every level of creation, moral, relational, spiritual, and ecological (Romans 8:20-22). The doctrine of the Fall is not simply a theological abstraction; it maps accurately onto our lived experience. We sense that things are not as they should be, internally and externally. Redemption is the heart of the Christian narrative. Unlike myths of self-salvation or karma, Christianity insists that the solution to human brokenness is not found in self-improvement or detachment, but in divine intervention. God does not remain aloof.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: mption is the heart of the Christian narrative. Unlike myths of self-salvation or karma, Christianity insists that the solution to human brokenness is not found in self-improvement or detachment, but in divine intervention. God does not remain aloof. In the Incarnation, He steps into the story, into flesh and time and space. Jesus Christ, the Word made flesh (John 1:14), bears our sin, defeats death, and inaugurates the Kingdom of God. The cross and the resurrection are not merely symbols; they are 136

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: cosmic events that changed the trajectory of history and the lives of those who believe. Restoration offers a future hope that is both cosmic and personal. Christianity does not teach escape from the world, but the renewal of it. The resurrection of Jesus is the “firstfruits” of a coming new creation, when death will be no more and all things will be made new (Revelation 21:5; 1 Corinthians 15:20-26). This eschatological vision is not escapism; it empowers present engagement. It calls believers to live as agents of renewal now, in justice, in mercy, in truth, and in love. In contrast, many competing worldviews leave critical questions unresolved. Secularism preaches human progress through reason and science, yet offers no transcendent grounding for human rights, no coherent answer to suffering, and no ultimate hope in the face of death. Eastern philosophies often prescribe detachment from desire and individuality, which can feel foreign to the human yearning for personal love and embodied redemption. Materialism reduces us to atoms and instincts, yet cannot account for consciousness, moral obligation, or the transcendent joy of beauty and art. Christianity does not deny the ache, the questions, or the tensions of life, it embraces them and weaves them into a coherent whole. It explains why we are drawn to stories of sacrificial love, why we protest injustice even when we are powerless to fix it, why we long for permanence in a perishing world. C.S. Lewis once wrote, “I believe in Christianity as I believe that the sun has risen, not only because I see it, but because by it I see everything else.” That rings true for me. Christianity is the light by which all other stories are illuminated and judged.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: once wrote, “I believe in Christianity as I believe that the sun has risen, not only because I see it, but because by it I see everything else.” That rings true for me. Christianity is the light by which all other stories are illuminated and judged. I remain a Christian because this story is not only intellectually credible and historically rooted, but existentially rich . It is a story that has claimed me, and that continues to shape my understanding of God, myself, and the 137

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: world. When I encounter beauty, when I grieve loss, when I strive for justice, when I fail and need grace, it is this story that makes sense of it all. It is the true myth, as Tolkien called it: the story that fulfills all our longings because it is real . FAITH, DOUBT, AND THE GRACE THAT KEEPS ME I do not remain a Christian because I have never doubted. Quite the opposite. My journey of faith has often passed through the valley of shadows, shadows of intellectual questions, emotional disorientation, and spiritual fatigue. There have been days when prayer felt like silence, when doctrine felt distant, and when the very foundations of what I believe trembled under the weight of life’s unrelenting questions. And yet, I am still here. Not because of the strength of my grasp on Christ, but because of His unyielding grip on me. Doubt, contrary to what some may assume, is not the enemy of faith. Indifference is. Doubt, when brought honestly before God, can become the doorway to deeper faith. The Psalms are replete with cries of confusion, protest, and even despair. Job questioned, Jeremiah wept, John the Baptist doubted from prison, and Thomas needed proof. Scripture does not sanitize human struggle; it sanctifies it. Faith, then, is not the absence of doubt but the decision to trust amidst uncertainty. In seasons of questioning, I have returned again and again to the character of God revealed in Jesus. If Jesus truly is the image of the invisible God (Colossians 1:15), then I am not dealing with a distant abstraction but with a Savior who knows what it means to weep, to be betrayed, to cry out, “My God, why have You forsaken Me?” (Matthew 27:46). In the garden of Gethsemane, we see not a stoic deity but a trembling Son who understands anguish.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: distant abstraction but with a Savior who knows what it means to weep, to be betrayed, to cry out, “My God, why have You forsaken Me?” (Matthew 27:46). In the garden of Gethsemane, we see not a stoic deity but a trembling Son who understands anguish. And in the resurrection, we see that even the grave is not beyond the reach of God’s power. 138

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: But more than all this, I am sustained by grace, grace that holds me even when I cannot hold back. The doctrine of perseverance is not rooted in my ability to endure, but in God's unchanging resolve to finish what He started. “Being confident of this very thing, that He which hath begun a good work in you will perform it until the day of Jesus Christ” (Philippians 1:6). That verse has anchored me more times than I can count. My salvation is not a fragile thing, dependent on my performance or emotional constancy. It is a covenant, sealed by the blood of Christ and secured by the Spirit of God. When my faith is frail, His faithfulness is firm. When my love grows cold, His remains steadfast. I have come to realize that I do not carry my faith like a torch that I must keep lit in the storms of life. Rather, it is God who carries me, who kindles the flame, who breathes life into my weary soul. The gospel is not about how tightly I cling to God, but about how completely He has committed Himself to me in Christ. In moments of clarity, I see this truth: my faith has never been self-sustained, it has always been grace-sustained. “For by grace are ye saved through faith; and that not of yourselves: it is the gift of God” (Ephesians 2:8). Even the faith I exercise is itself a work of grace. So yes, I have doubted. I may doubt again. But I know now that my doubts do not disqualify me from grace, they drive me deeper into it. And when the storm rages and all I can do is whisper a halting “Lord, I believe; help thou mine unbelief” (Mark 9:24), I find that He is already there, faithful, present, and sufficient.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: do not disqualify me from grace, they drive me deeper into it. And when the storm rages and all I can do is whisper a halting “Lord, I believe; help thou mine unbelief” (Mark 9:24), I find that He is already there, faithful, present, and sufficient. CONCLUSION: A LIVING HOPE In the end, I am still a Christian not because I have figured everything out, but because I have encountered the One who holds all things together (Colossians 1:17). Jesus Christ is not merely a figure of history, a moral 139

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: teacher, or the founder of a religion, He is the living Lord, risen from the dead, reigning in glory, and returning in hope. My continued allegiance to Him is not sentimental loyalty but a deeply reasoned conviction formed by Scripture, shaped by experience, and sustained by grace. Christianity has not always made my life easier. At times, it has made it more costly, demanding forgiveness when revenge would feel sweeter, calling for surrender when control is more comfortable, asking for faith when certainty seems elusive. And yet, I find in it not just a code to live by, but a life to live into, a life that is abundant, eternal, and rooted in the love of God. There is no other worldview that more thoroughly addresses the human condition in its complexity, our beauty and brokenness, our longings and limits. There is no other narrative that speaks more compellingly to creation and corruption, justice and mercy, suffering and glory. And there is no other person who meets my deepest fears and hopes with such truth, tenderness, and transforming power as Jesus Christ. I believe in Christianity not because it offers all the answers, but because it offers the truest answer: a crucified and risen Savior. This faith is not built on the shifting sands of feeling or cultural approval, but on the unshakable foundation of the gospel, a gospel that is intellectually credible, historically reliable, existentially compelling, morally transformative, and spiritually sustaining. Like Peter, I have come to the same sobering, liberating conclusion: “Lord, to whom shall we go? thou hast the words of eternal life” (John 6:68).

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: e, historically reliable, existentially compelling, morally transformative, and spiritually sustaining. Like Peter, I have come to the same sobering, liberating conclusion: “Lord, to whom shall we go? thou hast the words of eternal life” (John 6:68). In a world of noise and novelty, shifting values and passing trends, I remain a Christian because I have found in Christ a Living Hope, one that is incorruptible, undefiled, and that fadeth not away (1 Peter 1:3-4). 140

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: To follow Jesus is not to escape the storm but to have an anchor within it. It is to know that even when I walk through the valley of doubt, disillusionment, or despair, I do not walk alone. I am held by the One who conquered death, who bears scars of love, and who is faithful to the end. That is why I am still a Christian. 141

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: CHAPTER 15: WELCOME TO THE FAMILY What It Means To Belong To A Church The Christian faith is not intended to be lived in isolation. From the very beginning, God designed His people to be a community, a family united by faith in Jesus Christ. The church is not a building or an institution, but the living, breathing body of believers. In the words of the Apostle Paul, we are “members one of another” (Romans 12:5). Belonging to a church means more than simply attending services or participating in activities, it means being a part of a community where we love one another, serve one another, and grow together. Belonging to a church is about identity. The church is the place where we are reminded of who we are in Christ. Through worship, fellowship, and the preaching of God’s Word, we are reminded of our shared calling and our common hope. We are not merely individuals trying to live out our faith in isolation; we are a part of something much larger, a global family of believers who span generations, cultures, and nations. Jesus Himself modeled this communal life with His disciples. He didn’t call people to follow Him individually, but to follow Him together as a group. The early church in Acts was marked by unity, shared meals, prayer, and care for one another (Acts 2:42-47). This is the kind of community to which we are called, a community that supports, encourages, and holds each other accountable. Belonging to a church also means taking responsibility. It’s not enough to simply show up; we are called to actively contribute. This can take many forms: using our spiritual gifts to serve, offering encouragement, giving 142

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: financially, or praying for others. A healthy church is one where every member plays a vital role, and the whole body is built up as each part does its work (Ephesians 4:16). The church thrives when everyone is engaged, not just in receiving, but also in giving. How We Can Help You Grow One of the central purposes of the church is to help believers grow in their faith and become more like Christ. The Christian journey is one of continual transformation, and the church is the community where that transformation happens most profoundly. There are several ways the church can help you grow: 1. Teaching and Preaching the Word of God The primary way that the church helps believers grow is through the preaching and teaching of Scripture. The Word of God is the foundation for everything we do as Christians. As Paul writes to Timothy, “All Scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness” (2 Timothy 3:16). Through the faithful preaching and study of God’s Word, we are equipped to grow in our understanding of God and His will for our lives. 2. Worshiping Together Worship is not just an individual experience, it is something we do as a community. When we gather to worship together, we are reminded of God’s greatness, His goodness, and His grace. Through singing, prayer, and the sacraments, we declare our allegiance to Christ and are reminded of His work in our lives. Worship also helps us align our hearts and minds with God’s truth, which deepens our relationship with Him. 143

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: 3. Fellowship and Accountability The church is a place where we come together not just to hear a sermon but to build meaningful relationships with one another. Fellowship is an essential part of our growth as believers. In the context of Christian community, we find encouragement, support, and accountability. Proverbs 27:17 says, “Iron sharpeneth iron; so a man sharpeneth the countenance of his friend.” Being part of a church gives us the opportunity to surround ourselves with others who can help us grow spiritually, challenge us when needed, and celebrate with us in times of joy. 4. Serving and Using Spiritual Gifts Growth in Christ also involves using the gifts God has given us to serve others. Every believer has a unique role to play in the body of Christ (1 Corinthians 12:4-7). When we serve others, we are not only helping them but also growing ourselves. Serving stretches us, teaches us humility, and gives us a greater sense of purpose. It is through serving that we truly experience the joy of living out our faith. 5. Prayer and Intercession Prayer is one of the most powerful means of growth in the Christian life. The church provides opportunities for corporate prayer, where we come together to pray for one another, for the community, and for the world. Through prayer, we deepen our relationship with God, experience His presence, and learn to depend on Him in every circumstance. Jesus Himself modeled the importance of prayer (Mark 1:35), and as His followers, we are called to pray continually. 6. Discipleship and Mentoring In addition to corporate growth, the church provides an 144

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: environment where believers can be personally mentored and discipled. Discipleship is the process of growing in our understanding of Christ and learning to live out His teachings in our daily lives. This is often done through one-on-one relationships, small groups, or Bible studies, where more mature believers help newer believers navigate their spiritual journey. In these relationships, we are encouraged to ask tough questions, face our doubts, and take steps toward spiritual maturity. CONCLUSION Belonging to a church is not just a matter of attending services or being a part of a social group. It is a commitment to being a part of a living, breathing community of believers, where we grow together in the grace and knowledge of Jesus Christ. As we gather for worship, study God’s Word, serve one another, and pray together, we become more like Christ and are equipped to fulfill His mission in the world. If you are new to the church, welcome! You are now a part of the family. Our prayer is that you will find a place of belonging, grow in your faith, and discover the joy of serving others. If you have been here for some time, remember that the church is not just a place to receive but also a place to give. Together, we can grow in Christ and become a powerful witness of His love and grace to the world. 145

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: APPENDICES FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS (FAQ) Here are some frequently asked questions to help you better understand what it means to belong to a church and how you can grow in your faith: 1. Why should I join a church? While it’s possible to be a Christian without formally belonging to a church, Scripture encourages believers to be part of a community. The church is a place where we can grow spiritually, serve others, and be encouraged in our walk with Christ. The New Testament speaks of believers as members of the body of Christ, where each part is essential (1 Corinthians 12:12-27). Joining a church allows you to experience the benefits of fellowship, teaching, accountability, and service to others. 2. What is the church's role in my spiritual growth? The church provides various means of growth: ● Teaching and Preaching : The Word of God is foundational to our growth. Through the Bible’s teachings, we learn God’s will, His promises, and His expectations for our lives. ● Fellowship : Being with other believers encourages spiritual growth. You are not walking your faith journey alone. ● Worship : Corporate worship helps align our hearts with God’s truth and reminds us of His greatness and goodness. 146

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: ● Service : Engaging in service helps believers use their spiritual gifts for the benefit of others, which in turn helps develop Christ-like character. 3. Can I grow spiritually without going to church? While personal devotion and private worship are vital, the Bible teaches that the Christian faith is lived out within community. The early church was characterized by communal worship, shared meals, teaching, and fellowship (Acts 2:42). Spiritual growth in isolation can be limited, as we need the encouragement, accountability, and support of other believers to mature in our faith. 4. What should I expect when I visit a church for the first time? When you visit a church, expect to be welcomed warmly and treated as a valued guest. Most churches will have friendly greeters who can help you get settled and answer any questions you may have. During the service, you will likely experience worship through singing, prayer, and a sermon that teaches from the Bible. There may also be opportunities for fellowship after the service, such as a coffee hour or small groups, where you can meet others. 5. How can I get involved in the church community? Getting involved in the church is one of the best ways to grow spiritually and build meaningful relationships. Start by attending a service regularly. You can also participate in small groups, Bible studies, volunteer for church events, or join a ministry team. Most churches have ministries for different age groups, interests, and skill sets, where you can serve and make an impact. 6. How can I grow in my relationship with God outside of church? 147

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: Spiritual growth is a lifelong process, and much of it happens outside of church services. To grow in your relationship with God, engage in daily personal Bible reading and prayer. Spend time reflecting on God’s Word, and allow the Holy Spirit to speak to your heart. Surround yourself with like-minded believers who can encourage you and hold you accountable. Also, remember that living out your faith through acts of love, kindness, and service to others is an essential part of your spiritual growth. 7. How does the church support me when I face challenges or trials? The church is a community where you can find support during difficult times. In moments of trial, the church provides prayer, encouragement, and a place to process your struggles. Members of the church are available to walk alongside you, whether through pastoral counseling, prayer groups, or practical help. As the body of Christ, the church is called to bear one another’s burdens (Galatians 6:2), and you do not have to face challenges alone. 8. How do I know if this church is the right one for me? Finding the right church involves prayer, reflection, and discerning whether the church’s teaching aligns with Scripture and whether its culture supports spiritual growth. Consider whether the church’s mission and values align with your own beliefs and whether you feel welcomed and supported. It is also helpful to look at the church’s community involvement, programs, and opportunities for service, as these can provide insight into whether this is a place where you can contribute and grow. 9. How can I get involved in ministry or service at the church? Most churches have various ministries that need volunteers. Consider your spiritual gifts, interests, and passions.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: nsight into whether this is a place where you can contribute and grow. 9. How can I get involved in ministry or service at the church? Most churches have various ministries that need volunteers. Consider your spiritual gifts, interests, and passions. Whether you have a heart for children, hospitality, prayer, music, or outreach, there is a place for you to 148

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: serve. Speak with a pastor or ministry leader about where you might get involved. Serving is one of the best ways to grow spiritually and make a meaningful impact in the lives of others. 10. What does membership in the church entail? Membership in the church typically involves committing to the church’s teachings, fellowship, and mission. It means actively participating in the life of the church and taking responsibility for your spiritual growth as well as the well-being of the church community. Membership may also include baptism if you haven’t been baptized yet. It’s important to ask questions about what the church expects from its members and what you can expect from them. 11. How can I handle doubts or questions about my faith? Doubt is a natural part of the faith journey. The church is a safe place to ask questions and explore difficult issues. It is helpful to engage with trusted mentors, pastors, or small groups who can help you process your doubts and guide you to Scripture for answers. Christianity is not afraid of questions, and exploring doubt can lead to a deeper, more robust faith. These FAQs are intended to give you a better understanding of what it means to be part of a church community and how it can help you grow spiritually. If you have further questions or would like more information, don’t hesitate to ask. We are here to support you on your journey of faith! 149

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: FURTHER READING AND RESOURCES For those seeking to deepen their understanding of Christian doctrine, theology, and the spiritual journey, I am pleased to offer the following resources. These works have been crafted to provide both scholarly insights and practical applications of the Christian faith. Each book in this collection builds upon the core themes of redemption, justification, victorious living in Christ, and the awe-inspiring truth of God’s eternal plan for His people. Whether you are exploring foundational truths, grappling with theological questions, or desiring to grow in your walk with God, these volumes will offer you wisdom, guidance, and a deeper understanding of the unshakeable hope found in Christ. Books Available: ● Soteria: The Unbreakable Gift of Sonship and Redemption (Volume 1) This volume explores the profound reality of salvation and the unbreakable nature of our sonship in Christ. It lays the foundation for understanding the eternal gift of redemption, unpacking the richness of God's grace and the unshakable security we have in Christ's finished work. ● Soteria and Rest: The Story of Redemption from Eden to Eternity (Volume 2) Tracing the story of God’s redemption throughout the entire Bible, from the Garden of Eden to the eternal new creation, this volume provides an in-depth look at God's redemptive plan. It offers a biblical framework for understanding salvation as a continuous story unfolding across time and cultures. 150

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: ● Soteria & Victorious Eschatology: Living in Christ's Victory Today and Forever (Volume 3) This volume takes a victorious approach to eschatology, showing how believers can live in the power of Christ’s victory over sin, death, and the grave. It offers insights into living out our faith confidently in the present while anticipating the fullness of Christ’s Kingdom in the future. ● Justification: An Apologia Series on Christian Doctrine (Volume One) A thorough examination of the doctrine of justification, this book delves into its biblical foundation, historical development, and ongoing importance for Christian life. It presents a clear defense of justification by faith alone and its implications for salvation and Christian living. ● The Fear of God: What It Really Means and Why It Still Matters This book addresses the often misunderstood concept of the fear of God. It unpacks its biblical significance and explores why this reverence for God is still vital for today’s believers, providing a rich theological and practical understanding of this key aspect of the Christian faith. ● Christ: The Desire of Nations: Tracing the Eternal Christ Through Time, Cultures, and Prophecy A scholarly exploration of the person and work of Christ, this volume traces His presence through the pages of Scripture, across different cultures, and through the fulfillment of prophecy. It demonstrates how Christ is the central figure in the unfolding story of God’s redemptive plan for humanity. 151

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: ● Logos Believers' Class A foundational guide for believers, this book serves as a comprehensive resource for understanding key doctrines and practical Christian living. It is designed to equip believers to live out their faith with knowledge and confidence, grounded in the truth of Scripture. These works are available on Amazon and are intended to serve as comprehensive resources for your personal study, teaching, or ministry. Each volume presents a unique angle on the rich depths of God’s Word and offers valuable insights for both the intellectual and spiritual growth of the believer. 152

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: ABOUT THE BOOK Why I Am a Christian is more than just a response to the intellectual or emotional struggles many face with faith; it is an invitation to embark on a journey of rediscovery. Written for those who are grappling with doubt, deconstructing their beliefs, or simply seeking a deeper understanding of why Christianity matters, this book aims to offer a fresh perspective on the timeless truths of the Christian faith. In a world where faith is often questioned and religious institutions have faced scrutiny for their failures, this book serves as a reminder that the heart of Christianity is not found in human imperfection but in the perfect Savior, Jesus Christ. It is not about offering polished answers or simple formulas; instead, it acknowledges the complexities and struggles inherent in the human experience. This book is for those who have felt the weight of life’s hardships, the sting of disappointment, or the pull of skepticism but are still searching for a deeper meaning and a reason to believe. Why I Am a Christian is a personal reflection on the foundational aspects of the Christian faith, its historical reliability, existential power, transformative impact, and the story it tells about the world and our place in it. It is a guide for those who find themselves questioning the significance of Christianity, offering both intellectual insight and heartfelt encouragement. The book recognizes the pain of doubt but also underscores the enduring hope and peace found in Jesus Christ. The journey through this book will help you explore the heart of Christianity, not as a set of rules or rituals, but as a living faith rooted in the truth of Jesus Christ, alive, relevant, and transformative.

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: ing hope and peace found in Jesus Christ. The journey through this book will help you explore the heart of Christianity, not as a set of rules or rituals, but as a living faith rooted in the truth of Jesus Christ, alive, relevant, and transformative. Whether you are in the midst of a faith crisis or simply seeking to understand why Christianity has endured for over two millennia, Why I Am a Christian will serve as a companion to guide you toward a renewed sense of purpose, belonging, and spiritual vitality. 157

title: WHY I AM A CHRISTIAN
text: Ultimately, this book is about inviting you to see beyond your doubts and struggles to experience a deeper relationship with Jesus Christ, the Savior who holds the answers to the questions that matter most. 158
